docs.title: 3.5 Manually uploading pump data to CareLink via the blue CareLink USB
docs.text:
To manually upload the MiniMed 780G insulin pump to CareLink, follow these steps:

  • Log into your CareLink account and click on the Upload button in CareLink to initiate the upload process.

  • If this is not done yet, you will be prompted to install the uploader software on your computer.

  • Follow the software's instructions, including plugging in the blue CareLink USB, selecting Paired Devices on the pump, and linking to CareLink.

  • Keep the pump near the connected blue USB during data transfer.

  • The uploading process may take 5-10 minutes, so it's recommended to perform this at home to avoid delays during consultations.

docs.title: 3.6 MiniMed Mobile app >\< CareLink
docs.text:
Linking the MiniMed Mobile app to CareLink is very easy

  • The MiniMed Mobile app automatically syncs data with CareLink by default when you link the app to your CareLink account during setup.

  • Note that the data transmission occurs via Wi-Fi or your data plan, and additional charges may apply based on your subscription.

  • The data is transferred to your CareLink account every 5 minutes, but it may take up to 24 hours for the latest information to appear in your CareLink reports. To have the most up-to-date data in your reports, you can select the "Upload Now" option in the MiniMed Mobile app.

docs.title: 3.7 CareLink Connect app >\< CareLink
docs.text:
Finally, you can also connect a caregiver or family member to a personal CareLink account via the CareLink Connect app.

As a caregiver you can download the CareLink Connect app and follow the prompts. Create your account. After you read through the terms, you have to enter your login details. Enter your information and verify your email. When the enrollment is complete, the app is ready to be used. Press proceed to login and send a follow request by entering the CareLink personal account user name. Once the request is approved, the CareLink Connect app serves as a mirror app. You will also be able to view time in range and history data.

By following these steps, you can successfully establish the necessary links between the components of the MiniMed 780G automated insulin delivery system, enabling seamless communication and data transmission for effective diabetes management.

docs.title: 4. How the MiniMed 780G works (7.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on the functionality of the MiniMed 780G insulin pump.

In this video, we will explore the usage of the MiniMed 780G insulin pump in both auto mode and manual mode. Additionally, we'll cover the utilization of the MiniMed Mobile app, which functions as a view app.

docs.title: Starting SmartGuard Auto Mode
docs.text:
To enable Auto Mode or the "SmartGuard function" in the MiniMed 780G, go to the shield icon in the menu, and turn the SmartGuard on. Before using the SmartGuard function, the pump must be in manual mode for a minimum of 48 hours starting at midnight, allowing it to consider the total daily insulin dose from the past 2-6 days.

Once the SmartGuard function is active, a blue shield will be displayed on the screen, showing sensor glucose readings and active insulin at the bottom right. If there are issues turning on the SmartGuard function, you can refer to the SmartGuard checklist for troubleshooting.

In the sensor chart, you'll find a 3-hour representation with purple bars indicating auto basal and light blue bars representing autocorrection boluses. Use the back arrow to return to the previous screen.

Keep in mind that while you can't see the exact amount of basal insulin, you can get an idea from the purple bars on the sensor graph and the insulin on board displayed on your home screen.

docs.title: Using Temporary Target
docs.text: In the SmartGuard menu, you can select the Temp Target of 150 mg/dl or 8.3 mmol/l if you’re planning to do exercise. You can activate it after you select a duration. The home screen will display a green bar indicating how long the Temp Target will be active.

docs.title: Adjusting parameters
docs.text:
In the SmartGuard Settings, you can change the target value to 100, 110, or 120 mg/dl or 5.6, 6.1 or 6.7 mmol/l. You can also choose whether to enable or disable autocorrections.

To modify the carbohydrate ratio and active insulin time, follow these steps:

  • Access the Delivery Settings on your MiniMed 780G pump via the Insulin or Settings menu.

  • In the Bolus Wizard Setup, you will be able to click on Carb Ratio and Active Insulin Time to change it.

  • Don’t forget to change the settings if you change something.

docs.title: Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
In Auto Mode, bolusing is performed similarly to Manual Mode.

However, delivering a manual bolus, overriding the bolus recommendation, or delivering an extended bolus is not possible.

You can access the bolus screen via the Insulin menu or use the downward arrow as a shortcut to the bolus screen.

To give a meal bolus, you have to enter the grams of carbs you are going to eat.

If the Safe Meal Bolus feature prevents you from administering insulin for the carbohydrates you've entered due to low glucose levels, you should monitor your glucose trend. Once an upward trend is detected, retry entering the carbs to receive a suggested insulin bolus.

To give a correction bolus, you can enter 0 grams of carbohydrates.

docs.title: Suspending insulin
docs.text:
To temporarily stop the insulin, such as when disconnecting for showering or swimming, follow the same process as in manual mode. Select "Suspend all delivery" in the Insulin menu or in the Status screen which will appear if you click on the upward arrow.

To restart the basal insulin delivery, you can follow the same process.

docs.title: 4.2 Manual Mode & Suspend-before-low
docs.text:
In Manual Mode, you can set basal insulin rates, as well as configure temporary basal rates as needed. Furthermore, the pump allows for manual bolus administration and boluses using the bolus calculator, including both regular and extended boluses. For detailed instructions on using Manual Mode effectively, please refer to the module of the MiniMed 780G insulin pump.

Besides Auto Mode, the MiniMed 780G pump also has a built in Suspend-Before-Low Mode.

  • This suspend-before-low feature halts basal insulin delivery if the sensor glucose is below 70 mg/dl or 3,9 mmol/l above the low limit, and the algorithm predicts that it will drop below 20 mg/dl or 1,1 mmol/l above the lower limit within the next 30 minutes.

  • After 30 minutes, insulin resumes if the sensor glucose is 20 mg/dl or 1.1 mmol/l above the low limit and the algorithm predicts it to rise more then 40mg/dl or 2.2 mmol/l above the low limit within the subsequent 30 minutes.

  • Insulin delivery will always resume after two hours and it can always manually be resumed. After a suspend-before-low episode, the function becomes unavailable for 30 minutes to avoid prolonged periods without insulin.

To activate the Suspend-Before-Low Mode, go to the Alert Settings and choose Low Alert. Set the desired low limit, enable “Suspend before low” and save the changes you made.

It's important to note that the suspend-before-low function can only be used in Manual Mode, and it's recommended to activate it as a safer way to deliver insulin when exiting Auto Mode.

docs.title: 4.3 MiniMed Mobile app
docs.text:
The MiniMed Mobile app allows users to track sensor and insulin data but does not provide the capability to adjust insulin dosages.

The app's home screen features a hamburger menu in the top-left corner. The status area displays sensor glucose readings and active insulin on board. The sensor graph and insulin delivery, as seen on the pump, are also displayed. Swiping to the left reveals your Time In Range of the last 24 hours.

Via the menu you can turn on notifications. This is necessary to receive notifications from your pump. The notifications will appear as a color coded banner on your phone. Red is for alarms, orange for alerts, grey for reminders and blue for messages.

Via the menu you can also check your synchronisation with CareLink and manage your care partners.

By familiarizing yourself with the different modes and features of the MiniMed 780G system, you will be able to efficiently navigate and enhance its performance. Remember, if something is not clear, there's no need to worry. You can always refer to the manual for clarification in using the MiniMed 780G system. This manual is conveniently accessible online and can also be downloaded under this video, for comprehensive guidance and detailed information on utilizing the system effectively.

docs.title: 5. Creating and Interpreting Reports (7.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video on creating and interpreting reports using the CareLink software for the MiniMed 780G system.

In previous videos, we have discussed creating a CareLink account and uploading pump data.

In this video, we will cover:

  • How to create reports in CareLink,

  • The types of reports available,

  • Tips for interpreting the reports.

docs.title: 5.1 Creating reports
docs.text:
To create reports:

  • Access CareLink Personal or go to a specific patient in CareLink Clinic.

  • Go to the Reports section.

  • For the Summary Reports, you can set the desired reporting period, typically 2 weeks, and select the reports you want. You can choose your favorite reports, and you can select the date range you'd like to use for comparison in the Assessment and Progress Report.

  • For the Daily Reports, you can select the specific days for which you want to see detailed reports. Selecting the "max" option will generate a report for each day within the selected period.

  • Select Generate Reports to create a PDF, which can be printed if needed for discussion during consultations.

The most important summary report is the "assessment and progress" report.

  • It provides an easy comparison between two periods, showing trends in hypo and hyperglycemia and the set carbohydrate ratio.

  • Time In Range and Time Below Range are displayed using familiar colored bars.

  • Auto Mode issues are highlighted, with sensor problems being the main cause.

  • Key statistics include the percentage of time spent in auto mode, sensor usage, estimated HbA1c (or GMI, this requires a minimum report duration of 14 days), coefficient of variation, total daily insulin dose, set and reservoir change, meal entries, carbohydrate intake, active insulin time and the 24 hour manual basal dose. Set change refers to the number of times the tubing has been filled, while reservoir change indicates the number of times the pump has rewound the piston. If a user changes only the infusion set without replacing the reservoir or tubing, it will not be reflected in these statistics.

The MiniMed 780G system offers additional reports such as the Weekly Review, Meal Summary, Adherence Report, and Device Settings and Daily Reports.

  • The Weekly Review provides a clear day-by-day breakdown. Grey shading indicates periods when the user was in manual mode, while orange shading indicates manual mode during stop-for-low events.

  • The Meal Summary shows the impact of the set carbohydrate ratio and provides insight into the timing of insulin boluses. If the glucose rises before the insulin line, it suggests the bolus was administered too late.

  • The Adherence Report provides information on the frequency of insulin reservoir and tubing changes, as well as the instances when the cannula was filled.

  • Device Settings allow you to access information such as the SmartGuard target value, basal insulin dose for manual mode, configured alarms and reminders, and the status of the suspend-before-low feature.

docs.title: 5.2 Interpreting reports
docs.text:
Interpreting reports on the MiniMed 780G system is generally straightforward due to the limited number of adjustable parameters. These parameters include the target value, carbohydrate ratio, and active insulin time. However, it is important to remember that optimizing the user's interaction with the pump is always a possibility for further improvement.

Interpreting reports from the MiniMed 780G system can be done effectively by following a structured approach. Here are the recommended steps:

1. Assess Glycemic Information: Begin by evaluating the Time In Range and Time Below Range over the past 2 weeks on the Assessment and Progress Report. Ensure that treatment goals are being met, with Time In Range above 70% and Time Below Range below 4%. Pay attention to the frequency of meal bolusing, which should be at least 3 times a day.

2. Optimize Automated Insulin Delivery Settings: Analyze the Ambulatory Glucose Profile on the Assessment and Progress Report to identify trends of hypo- or hyperglycemia. Take note of the additional information provided below the AGP graph and check if these trends are related to meal boluses on the daily reports.

  • If you notice trends in post-meal glucose levels, it's important to assess various factors such as timing, accurate carbohydrate counting, and the activation of the Safe Meal Bolus feature. To check if the Safe Meal Bolus feature is being used, manually divide the amount of carbohydrates by the carbohydrate ratio and verify if the given insulin corresponds to this number. It's also essential to assess if the patient is entering false carbohydrates, which is generally not recommended. If you observe consistent entries of the same grams of carbohydrates in response to high glucose levels, inquire with the user if these were fake carbs. Encourage them to avoid this behavior and develop trust in the system.

  • For trends outside of meals, consider adjusting the active insulin time if you observe trends after autocorrection boluses. Similarly, if you notice trends outside of any boluses, consider changing the target value. Additionally, explore possible correlations with factors such as exercise, alcohol consumption, or hypoglycemia.

3. Guide Behavioral Recommendations: Ensure proper usage of the automated insulin delivery system by checking sensor wear, activation of auto mode, and accurate carb entries in the Assessment and Progress Report. Evaluate the adherence to pre-meal bolusing in the Meal Bolus Wizard Report and the regularity of infusion set changes in the Adherence Report. Review daily reports for overcorrection of hypo- or hyperglycemia, correct use of temporary target values, and the frequency of alarms. Assess the appropriateness of alarm settings and reminders in the Device Settings.

4. Review Pump Settings and Emergency Plans:

  • Verify that the preset basal insulin rate matches the actual basal insulin in auto mode. If there is a discrepancy, adjust the basal rate by 10% in each time block.

  • Document all pump settings and establish an emergency plan, including insulin pen usage, in case of a pump malfunction. Ensure access to insulin pens.

By following these steps, users, caregivers, and healthcare providers can effectively interpret reports from the MiniMed 780G system and make informed decisions to optimize its performance. If something is not clear on the reports, you can always look at the legend, where the meaning of each icon and color on the graph is explained. Although the MiniMed 780G does not use the regular AGP reporting system, interpreting the reports is generally straightforward in this context.

For mobile access to your CareLink data, we suggest registering for the Medtronic WeCare platform and app if this is available in your country. WeCare offers insights into CareLink data, personalized diabetes management content, and access to support services. Good luck!

6. Managing special situations

Welcome to this lesson on managing special situations with the Minimed 780G system.

In this video, we will provide specific tips on managing hypo- and hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, sports, illness, and travel with the MiniMed 780G system.

These recommendations complement the general guidelines for managing similar situations with automated insulin delivery systems. Please refer to the general module on automated insulin delivery systems for further information.

docs.title: 6.1 Hypoglycemia
docs.text:
With an automated insulin delivery system, you require fewer carbohydrates to treat or prevent hypoglycemia compared to non-automated insulin delivery systems, especially if you have little insulin on board. With the MiniMed 780G you can check your insulin on board on the home screen or on the sensor graph.

If you consume less than 10 grams of carbohydrates to address a hypo, there is no need to enter this in the pump.

To prevent hypoglycemic events, you can activate the temporary target.

docs.title: 6.2 Hyperglycemia
docs.text:
In case of mild hyperglycemia, it is generally recommended to trust the MiniMed 780G system and avoid entering fake carbs. However, you can give a correction bolus by entering 0 grams of carbohydrates in the bolus calculator.

In cases of prolonged severe hyperglycemia for example more than 270 mg/dl or 15 mmol/l with no decreasing trend or no 50 mg/dl or 2.7 mmol/l decrease within one hour after an insulin bolus, consider potential infusion set problems. If in doubt, change it out. You can administer an extra insulin bolus with your pump in Manual Mode or by entering fake carbs. If this doesn’t work or if there is an infusion set problem, use an insulin pen.

Carry sufficient equipment to change your infusion set and give insulin via an insulin pen when needed.

To avoid infusion set problems, replace the infusion set as recommended and set a reminder through the Device Settings on your pump or on your phone.

docs.title: 6.3 High-fat meals
docs.text:
It is recommended to enter 70% of the carbohydrates during a high-fat meal, allowing the automated insulin delivery system to handle the remaining portion.

If necessary, consider splitting the bolus by giving 50% of the carbohydrates before the meal and the remaining 50% two hours later.

Recent studies suggest that simply entering 100% of the carbohydrates usually does not lead to hypo- or hyperglycemia when using the MiniMed 780G.

docs.title: 6.4 Exercise
docs.text:
If you exercise with the MiniMed 780G, use the temporary target and meal bolus reduction as recommended in the general module on automated insulin delivery systems.

For light prolonged activity, you can also choose to use the normal targets and disable autocorrections.

If you use the MiniMed Mobile app, consider using the native Apple Watch connection or open-source apps to transfer your sensordata on your smartwatch or cycling computer for easier monitoring.

docs.title: 6.5 Illness
docs.text:
Most of the time, you will be able to remain in Auto Mode during illness with the MiniMed 780G system. However, there are exceptions to this rule. If you experience elevated ketones or prolonged hyperglycemia, which can be caused by factors such as high doses of corticosteroids, it may be necessary to temporarily exit Auto Mode and take additional measures to manage your glucose levels effectively. In such cases, it is important to consult with your healthcare team for specific guidance and adjustments to your treatment plan.

Note that higher doses of paracetamol may cause false high values on the Guardian 4 and Simplera sensor. In such cases, perform additional finger prick tests. If uncertain about the sensor's integrity, it is advisable to exit Auto Mode.

docs.title: 6.6 Alcohol
docs.text:
While the risk of false high values on the Guardian 4 sensor due to alcohol consumption is rare, it has been mentioned.

It is essential to be aware of a higher risk of hypoglycemia after consuming alcohol.

Eating more carbohydrates does not help prevent alcohol-related hypoglycemia. Instead, consider using the Temp Target preventively for a few hours after drinking high amounts of alcohol.

docs.title: 6.7 Travel
docs.text:
When traveling with the MiniMed 780G pump, make sure to have an adequate supply of essential items. Don't forget to bring some insulin pens with rapid-acting insulin to cover any emergencies that may arise.

In the event of pump failure, a new emergency replacement insulin pump will be sent to you within 24 hours, except for remote locations like islands. If you are traveling to such areas, you can request a loaner pump through the WeCare app or platform, but ensure to do so at least 3 weeks in advance of your trip.

If you’re using the Guardian 4 sensor, take good care of your transmitters, especially while traveling, as they are not rushed for replacements. Consider bringing a new transmitter if the current one is close to expiration.

Remember to pack AA batteries for your pump and AAA batteries for your transmitter charger if you’re using the Guardian 4 sensor.

Additionally, don't forget to adjust the time on your MiniMed 780G insulin pump when arriving in a new time zone.

In conclusion, managing special occasions with the MiniMed 780G requires individualized approaches and a willingness to adapt and learn through trial and error. While we have provided starting point recommendations for various situations, it's important to remember that everyone's diabetes management needs are unique. Working closely with your healthcare team is crucial in fine-tuning your insulin dosages and strategies to suit your specific circumstances. Additionally, seeking support from online communities, such as Facebook groups, can provide valuable insights and a sense of solidarity, as you connect with others who may have faced similar challenges. Remember, this information on managing special occasions is meant to complement the general recommendations from the overall video on AID systems.

docs.title: 7. Case report (1.5 min)
docs.text:
As we conclude this module on the MiniMed 780G system, we want to share a personal and detailed review that highlights the practical aspects of using this advanced diabetes management technology. We invite you to watch a video by Diabetic Danica, where she shares her experiences after using the MiniMed 780G system with the Guardian 4 CGM in SmartGuard mode for a month.

You can watch her review by clicking on the link below this video:

My Opinions: Medtronic MiniMed 780G Insulin Pump with Guardian 4 CGM in SmartGuard!

Congratulations on completing this course and gaining valuable insights into the MiniMed 780G system. Danica's candid review provides a real-world perspective on the pros and cons of the system, helping you understand what it might be like to use this technology in your own diabetes management journey.

To explore further support and connect with a vibrant community, we encourage you to join relevant Facebook groups where you can exchange experiences, seek advice, and find encouragement from others who understand the challenges and triumphs of managing type 1 diabetes.

Embrace the possibilities that lie ahead, armed with knowledge, advanced technology, and the support of a community that stands beside you. Your journey with the MiniMed 780G system is just beginning, and we wish you success, empowerment, and a life filled with freedom and joy.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Diabetes Technology Expert Program

Mylife Loop

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Mylife Loop according to the CARES paradigm
  • 3. Linking components together
  • 4. How mylife Loop works
  • 5. Creating and interpreting reports
  • 6. Managing special situations
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Welcome to this module about mylife Loop automated insulin delivery system. This relatively new automated insulin delivery system combination from YpsoMed uses the CamAPS FX algorithm that has been developed in the UK, and is the first automated insulin delivery system with the FreeStyle Libre 3 sensor.

The mylife Loop AID system consists of three main components:

  • the Dexcom G6 or FreeStyle Libre 3 or 3 Plus sensor,

  • the CamAPS FX algorithm as an app on your phone.

  • and the mylife YpsoPump insulin pump.

The mylife CamAPS FX app is available for Android, with an iOS version coming soon. It allows generating and exporting reports as PDFs from the app. Additionally, users can connect the app to the Glooko platform for sharing data with healthcare professionals. Family and caregivers can track the user's progress through the Companion app, and SMS-based remote monitoring is also available.

In the previous general videos on automated insulin delivery systems, you learned about comparing automated insulin delivery systems, selecting the right system for you, interpreting reports, and managing specific scenarios such as hypo- and hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, exercise, illness, alcohol, and traveling.

In the upcoming videos, we will dive deeper into the mylife Loop AID system, exploring its features, functionality, and step-by-step operation. This comprehensive education will provide you with the necessary knowledge to confidently start and manage mylife Loop.

Please note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your diabetes therapy.

The videos were created by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content has been reviewed by Daniëla Struijk, a diabetes educator from the Netherlands who uses the mylife Loop AID system herself, and by Tom Vesely from Germany, an individual living with type 1 diabetes who reviewed mylife Loop on his Type One Talks YouTube channel. Their expertise and personal experience contribute to the comprehensive and reliable information provided in the videos.

By the end of this video series, you will have a comprehensive understanding of:

  • How mylife Loop aligns with the CARES paradigm for automated insulin delivery systems

  • The practical functionality of the mylife CamAPS FX app

  • Creating and interpreting reports using the Glooko platform

  • And managing specific scenarios such as hypo and hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, exercise, illness, alcohol, and traveling with mylife Loop.

As a healthcare provider, you will be well-equipped to support individuals using mylife Loop, and as a user, you will have the knowledge and tools to ensure a successful start-up and make the most of mylife Loop. You will also gain valuable tips to simplify your experience with the system.

We wish you the best of luck as you embark on this educational journey with mylife Loop.

docs.title: 2. mylife Loop according to the CARES paradigm (12.5 min)
2. Mylife Loop according to the CARES paradigm
docs.text:
Welcome to this video where we will dive into mylife Loop, examining its functionality based on the CARES paradigm.

We will discuss how mylife Loop calculates and adjusts the automated insulin delivery, when it reverts back to manual manual, specific education tips and sensor issues, and how it shares data. After that we will go over mylife Loop’s official indications and software updates.

docs.title: 1) Mylife Loop according to the CARES paradigm
docs.text:
#1 Calculate

Mylife Loop operates by providing extended insulin boluses every 8 to 12 minutes, based on the predicted glucose levels for the next 2.5 to 4 hours, along with the user's total daily insulin dose and weight.

The system incorporates a learning algorithm through three approaches:

  • Overall learning: Analyzing daily data with recent data given higher weightage.

  • Diurnal learning: Assessing insulin requirements at specific times of the day.

  • Post-meal learning: Observing insulin requirements after meals.

The target value can be adjusted within 30-minute time blocks, ranging from 80 to 200 mg/dl or 4.4 to 11 mmol/l.

docs.title: #2 Adjust
docs.text:
Mylife Loop allows for adjustments in the following parameters:

  • Target Value: this is adjustable within 30-minute time blocks between 80 and 200 mg/dl or 4.4 and 11 mmol/l.

  • Temporary Target: Mylife Loop offers a higher and lower temporary target via its Ease-Off and Boost function

  • and the carb ratio.

Ease-Off: With the optional Ease-Off feature, users can temporarily receive reduced insulin delivery. It allows for an adjustable duration of up to 24 hours. During Ease-Off, the target value increases by 45 mg/dl (or 2.5 mmol/l), insulin sensitivity increases by 50%, and insulin delivery stops when glycemia reaches less than 126 mg/dl (or 7 mmol/l).

Boost: The Boost function provides a temporary increase in insulin delivery when glycemia exceeds the target value. It can be adjusted for a duration of up to 13 hours. Insulin delivery is enhanced by 35% only when glycemia is higher than the target value.

During the initial setup of mylife Loop, you can enter your total insulin dose and weight, with the option to adjust the weight later on.

In mylife Loop, the basal insulin rate, insulin sensitivity, and duration of insulin action are not adjustable. These are determined by the system.

In mylife Loop, meals can be managed effectively through two methods: the bolus calculator and the "add meal" function.

  • Bolus Calculator: Via the regular bolus screen, users can input grams of carbs to calculate the appropriate insulin dose using the bolus calculator. It accurately determines the insulin needed based on the carbohydrates consumed.

  • Add Meal Function: Alternatively, the "add meal" function allows insulin administration only if glucose levels rise above a specific threshold after the meal. Users can choose options such as meal and snacks, hypoglycemia treatment, or slowly absorbed meals.

This additional meal bolus method offers flexibility and precise insulin administration for optimal post-meal glucose control.

docs.title: #3 Revert
docs.text:
Mylife Loop reverts to Manual Mode under various circumstances:

  • If CGM data are unavailable, including during sensor warmup

  • If there is a loss of connection with the insulin pump

  • If the pump insulin delivery is suspended.

  • Or if Bluetooth is turned off on the pump or the smartphone

When there is no connection to the pump or sensor, the app will first enter an “Auto Mode Attempting" status. By clicking on the i-icon in the bottom left, you can access information about why Auto Mode is not possible. If the issue persists for 30 minutes, the system will switch to the pre-programmed basal rate on your pump or “Manual Mode”.

In Manual Mode:

  • A suspend-for-low function is not available.

  • The basal insulin settings on your pump will be used, and you can only check and adjust them on the pump.

  • However, if the mylife CamAPS FX app is still connected, you can still administer a bolus.

Once the connection to the pump and transmitter is restored, Auto Mode will automatically restart.

In theory, the connection between the mobile phone and the mylife YpsoPump and sensor should be maintained within a range of 6 meters (20 feet). However, in practice, it is recommended to keep your mobile phone as close to you as possible for optimal connectivity. For children, using a backpack to carry the mobile phone can be a convenient solution.

docs.title: #4 Educate
docs.text:
Proper education is crucial when starting an AID system. For general education points, please refer to the generic module on AID systems.

The general education points covered were:

  • Using fewer carbohydrates to correct hypoglycemia.

  • Considering infusion set problems if blood glucose levels remain high without an apparent reason.

  • Trusting the automated insulin delivery system and avoiding unnecessary manual interventions.

  • Timing meal boluses 15 minutes before meals.

  • Utilizing Exercise Mode 1 to 2 hours before activity.

  • Stopping insulin delivery if the pump is disconnected for more than 15 minutes.

  • Responding to alarms and being mindful of automatic updates on mobile phones.

  • Seeking peer support for guidance and sharing experiences.

Specific education points for mylife Loop include:

  1. Checking the compatibility of your Android mobile phone with both the mylife CamAPS FX app and the sensor (Dexcom G6 or FreeStyle Libre 3) before starting the mylife Loop AID system.

  2. Allowing a two-week adaptation period for the system to adjust to your body before making parameter adjustments.

  3. Using the Ease-off and Boost functions conservatively during the initial stages.

  4. Avoiding correction boluses when giving meal boluses using the bolus calculator in Auto Mode, as the algorithm often accounts for them. Correction boluses are more appropriate in Manual Mode.

  5. A high amount of basal insulin after a meal suggests that there might not have been enough meal bolus insulin administered, while a very low or even no basal rate after a meal suggests that there might have been too much meal bolus insulin administered.

  6. Entering carbohydrates through the "add meal" function in case you want insulin administered only if glucose levels rise. This function can be used for carbs as treatment for hypoglycemia, for big or fatty meals and for snacks.

  7. Lowering the target value during the night if necessary.

  8. When there is no basal rate, the system will give very small insulin boluses or patency pulses to keep the tubing open. These patency pulses are an indication of a very low insulin on board, which might lead to a hyperglycemia later on.

  9. Utilizing the free e-learning program offered by CamDiab for patients and healthcare providers, allowing users to obtain a certificate and gain comprehensive system knowledge. The e-learning program typically takes about 3 hours to complete. While it may not be easy, it is highly recommended as it provides essential knowledge. To activate the app, you will need a training code. However, you can install the mylife CamAPS FX app beforehand and test it using a virtual pump and sensor.

  10. Keeping the mobile phone, which hosts the algorithm, nearby at all times.

  11. And finally, consider having a spare mobile phone for emergencies.

docs.title: #5 Sensor
docs.text:
Mylife Loop can be used with the Dexcom G6 or the FreeStyle Libre 3 or 3 Plus sensor. You can easily switch between the 2 sensors.

If you have doubts about the accuracy of your sensor reading or do not feel it reflects your condition, perform a finger prick test to confirm.

If a significant discrepancy, such as a more than 20% difference, exists between the sensor and finger prick readings, even when the blood glucose is stable within the target range, it is advisable to discontinue Auto Mode and promptly replace the sensor.

The Dexcom G6 sensor can be calibrated if a significant discrepancy exists between the sensor reading and finger prick results, assuming stable glucose levels. It's important to note that if you calibrate the sensor, it will need one calibration each day until a new sensor is applied.

docs.title: #6 Share
docs.text:
The mylife Loop system offers the ability to share insulin and glucose data. Here's how it works:

  • You can connect the mylife Loop app to a Glooko Personal account, which allows you to access your stats and generate PDF reports. This Glooko Personal account can be linked to a professional account, enabling real-time follow-up. It's important to note that manual upload of pump data into Glooko is not supported.

  • Family members can monitor the user's glucose values in real-time using the Companion mode of the mylife CamAPS FX app. While this feature allows for monitoring, it does not provide remote control of insulin delivery.

By sharing insulin and glucose data, mylife Loop promotes collaboration and support among healthcare providers, family members, and the user for better diabetes management.

docs.title: 2) Indications
docs.text:
The mylife CamAPS FX app is CE-labeled for people with type 1 diabetes aged one year and over, including pregnant women. The mylife Loop automated insulin delivery system is approved for people with type 1 diabetes aged 2 years and over when used in combination with Dexcom G6 or FreeStyle Libre 3 Plus, and aged 4 years and over when used in combination with FreeStyle Libre 3.

It is suitable for individuals with a daily insulin requirement ranging from 5 to 350 units per day and a weight between 10 and 300 kg (or 22-661 pounds).

The system is compatible with Novorapid or Fiasp PumpCarts, or any other rapid-acting U100 insulin which can be manually filled into empty pump reservoirs.

Please note that the CamAPS FX algorithm does not have an FDA label at this time, and it is not approved for use during dialysis.

docs.title: 3) Software updates
docs.text:
After installing the mylife CamAPS FX app, it is recommended to regularly check the online portal for updates and install them when available. Before performing an update, it is advised to turn off Auto Mode and then restart it after the update is completed. This ensures a smooth and successful update process for the app.

During your journey with mylife Loop, if you encounter any challenges or have questions, remember that help is just a call away. You can always reach out to the customer service teams of Ypsomed, Dexcom or Abbott for assistance and guidance. They are dedicated to providing support and addressing any concerns you may have. Don't hesitate to reach out to them and take advantage of the resources and expertise they offer. Together with the support of your healthcare team, you can navigate any obstacles and continue on the path to successful diabetes management.

docs.title: 3. Linking components together (3.5 min)
docs.text:
In this video, we will walk you through the process of linking the different components of mylife Loop and enabling data sharing capabilities.

Mylife Loop operates in the following way:

  • Glycemia is measured every 1 to 5 minutes by either the Dexcom G6 or FreeStyle Libre 3 or 3 Plus sensor.

  • The Dexcom G6 transmitter or the FreeStyle Libre 3 transmitter sends the glycemia readings to the mylife CamAPS FX app on your mobile phone.

  • The mylife CamAPS FX app calculates the insulin dose based on the received data and sends it to the mylife YpsoPump.

  • The mylife YpsoPump delivers insulin boluses every 8 to 12 minutes, guided by the information from the mylife CamAPS FX app.

  • Data from the mylife CamAPS FX app is sent to the CamAPS Cloud, which also stores insulin settings for future use in case of mobile phone switches.

  • The mylife CamAPS FXapp can transmit data to Glooko for further analysis and reporting.

  • Additionally, the mylife CamAPS FX app can be connected to the mylife CamAPS FX app in Companion mode, allowing caregivers to access real-time information.

In this video, we will cover the step-by-step process of establishing the following connections:

  • Linking the Dexcom G6 or FreeStyle Libre 3 or 3 Plus sensor to the mylife CamAPS FX app.

  • Connecting the mylife YpsoPump to the mylife CamAPS FX app.

  • Establishing the link between the mylife CamAPS FX app and the CamAPS Cloud.

  • Creating a Glooko account.

  • Linking the mylife CamAPS FX app to Glooko Personal.

  • Connecting the mylife CamAPS Fx app in Companion mode to the mylife CamAPS FX app.

By successfully creating these links, you can ensure seamless integration of the mylife Loop system's components and enable effective sharing of data.

docs.title: 3.1 Linking the sensor to the mylife CamAPS FX app
docs.text:
When launching the mylife CamAPS FX app, you will have the option to select either the Dexcom G6 or the FreeStyle Libre 3 or 3 Plus sensor, depending on your country. You can switch between the 2 sensors at any time. If you go to the sensor status screen via the menu, you can always choose which type of sensor you want to use.

  • Follow these steps for the Dexcom G6 sensor:

  • If you haven't already done so, you can apply the Dexcom G6 sensor and transmitter on your skin.

  • If you are currently using the Dexcom G6 transmitter with the Dexcom G6 app, you need to disconnect it first: make a note of the serial number of the transmitter, delete the Dexcom G6 app, and remove the transmitter from your mobile phone's Bluetooth connections. It’s important not to stop the sensor in the app!

  • Enter the serial number of the Dexcom G6 transmitter in the mylife CamAPS FX app. The app will display a 30-minute counter, but the connection process usually takes only a few minutes.

  • Once the pairing is successful, you will see the "start sensor" option on the home screen. Tap on it and enter the 4-digit sensor code to initiate the 2-hour warm-up period for the sensor. If the sensor has already been started, the screen will show the sensor's glycemia, indicating that the installation is complete.

  • After 10 days, the sensor session will automatically stop, and the "start sensor" option will reappear on the home screen. If needed, you can manually stop the sensor earlier by selecting "stop sensor" from the hamburger menu at the bottom.

  • The FreeStyle Libre 3 has no serial number for the transmitter or sensor code for the sensor. So start-up will be just a little smoother. Once you select the FreeStyle Libre 3 in the app, you can simply scan the sensor with your phone to start it. After starting, you’ll see a warm-up message with a 60-minute countdown. Once that’s complete, your sensor glucose reading will be displayed.

docs.title: 3.2 Linking the mylife YpsoPump to the mylife CamAPS FX app
docs.text:
To link the mylife YpsoPump to the mylife CamAPS FX app, follow these steps:

  1. In the mylife CamAPS FX app, navigate to the settings section and select "insulin pump." Choose Ypsopump as the pump type.

  2. Ensure that Bluetooth is enabled on your mylife YpsoPump. Access the pump settings through the main menu and look for the Bluetooth icon. If there is a cross on the icon, tap on it to turn on Bluetooth.

  3. Tap on the serial number displayed in the app and select your country from the options provided.

  4. Put your mylife YpsoPump into Pairing Mode. This can be done by selecting the icon with the two Bluetooth logos in the pump settings.

  5. The pump will display a code that you need to enter into the mylife CamAPS FX app to establish the link. Enter the code as prompted.

If the pairing process is successful, you will see the serial number of your mylife YpsoPump, along with information about the insulin on board and the number of days since the insulin cartridge was replaced, in the menu.

Please note that when changing the infusion set, you will need to perform this action on the pump itself. To access the pump menu, you will temporarily have to disconnect from the mylife CamAPS FX app.

docs.title: 3.3 Linking the mylife CamAPS FX app to the CamAPS cloud
docs.text:
When you first open the mylife CamAPS FX app, you will be prompted to create an account that will link your information to the CamAPS cloud. It is important to remember your username and password for future reference, especially if you ever switch mobile phones to ensure you don't lose your settings.

In the event that your mobile device breaks, gets lost, or if you decide to switch to a different mobile, you can transfer the previously learned information to your new device. Simply reinstall the mylife CamAPS FX app on your new device and log in using your existing username and password. However, please note that if there are significant differences in your body weight and total daily insulin dose compared to what was previously used, the mylife CamAPS FX app will not download the learned information into the new app.

In such cases, the algorithm will restart its learning process from the beginning and require approximately two weeks to optimize itself again.

docs.title: 3.4 Creating a Glooko account
docs.text:
The pump and sensor data can be shared with your healthcare provider via Glooko.

We will describe how to create a Glooko account as a user via Glooko Personal, and as a healthcare professional via Glooko for Professional Use.

#1 Glooko Personal

  • As a user, you can visit my.glooko.com and choose "Create Account."

  • At a certain moment you will be able to enter a "proconnect code" that connects your account to your hospital.

  • After completing the registration, you will receive an email with further instructions. It is also possible to receive this email directly from your healthcare provider.

  • Create a password for your account and keep it accessible for future logins, especially when you need to upload data from your pump.

#2 Glooko for Healthcare Providers:

  1. Healthcare providers or institutions can purchase a professional Glooko account directly from Glooko. You can then log in via my.glooko.com using the username and password provided by your hospital.

  2. Upon logging in, you will find an overview of the latest data uploads and a separate tab displaying an overview of your patients. On the top right corner you will find the "proconnect code" of your hospital.

  3. You can customize the patient list by sorting it according to your preferences or using flags to identify patients with high or low glycemic levels easily. You can add a new patient via the button in the top right corner. and you can add their email address to invite them to Glooko.

docs.title: 3.5 Linking the mylife CamAPS FX app to Glooko Personal
docs.text:
Within the mylife CamAPS FX app, you can access the Sharing feature from the menu. For User 1, you have the option to enter your email address associated with your Glooko Personal account as the username, along with the corresponding password for your Glooko account.

Please note that it may take up to 48 hours for your CamAPS data to be fully synchronized with Glooko.

By enabling the connection, the mylife CamAPS FX app will transmit its values to your Glooko Personal account at regular intervals of every 5 to 10 minutes. This allows for seamless transfer and monitoring of your data within the Glooko platform.

docs.title: 3.6 Linking the mylife CamAS FX app in Companion mode to the mylife CamAPS FX app
docs.text:
Via the mylife CamAPS FX app in Companion mode , you can allow up to 10 caregivers to follow your progress in real-time. Follow these two simple steps:

Step 1: From the menu of your mylife CamAPS FX app, navigate to the Share section. Enter the email address and username of the person you wish to grant access to. Once entered, click on the "Invite" button. This action will send an email to the designated person with detailed instructions.

Step 2: The recipient of the invitation will be prompted to install the mylife CamAPS FX app and create a new account on their CamAPS FX app.

Once they have done this, they can link their app to the companion, and select the person who has invited them. After a little while, the glucose and insulin data will start appearing. The CamAPS companion mirrors the information on the person's own CamAPS FX app. The glucose and insulin data as well as alerts will be shared in real time. The CamAPS companion can even have different alerts to the one set on the person's own CamAPS FX app if that is appropriate.

The functions that change insulin delivery such as the bolus calculator, Boost and Ease-Off are disabled for safety reasons. There may be times that the companion is not needed for example a teacher when the child is not at school. The companion can be made inactive in the child's CamAPS FX's share menu.

People can use both CamAPS companion and SMS alerts for remote monitoring purposes. Users will need a SIM card and a suitable Mobile plan to send sms's.

By successfully creating these links, you can ensure seamless integration of the mylife Loop system's components and enable effective sharing of data. Stay determined and follow the instructions provided to make the most of this innovative diabetes management solution. Together, you can effectively navigate the challenges and achieve better control over your health.

docs.title: 4. How mylife Loop works (12.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on the functionality of mylife Loop. Controlling mylife Loop is done through the mylife CamAPS FX app on your mobile phone.

In this video, we will explore how to use the mylife CamAPS FX app in general, and how to use it in both Auto Mode and Manual Mode.

docs.title: 4.1 mylife CamAPS FX app
docs.text:
The mylife CamAPS FX app is currently available for Android and iOS phones. It's essential to verify if your phone is compatible with the sensor you intend to use, such as the Dexcom G6 or the FreeStyle Libre 3 or 3 Plus sensor.

To check compatibility, you can try downloading the CamAPS FX app and the respective Dexcom G6 or FreeStyle Libre 3 app from the Google Play store or App store.

During installation, the mylife CamAPS FX app will request permission to access your contacts and location, and it will also prompt you to disable energy-saving battery mode. In order to utilize the bolus calculator, you must set a PIN or a similar function on your mobile phone.

Let's explore the different screens and buttons of the app and learn how to adjust the settings.

The home screen presents several distinct elements:

  • Located at the top left is the menu, which we will delve into in detail later.

  • Below the menu are three tabs: the home screen, the bolus calculator and the calibration screen.

  • At the center of the home screen, you will find the sensor's blood glucose level, trend arrow, and a graph displaying the sensor's readings from the past few hours. You can adjust the time frame displayed on the graph.

  • Tapping the i-icon reveals additional information, including active insulin, current basal insulin rate, remaining insulin in the reservoir, pump battery status, last insulin bolus, last connection time to the pump and transmitter, target blood glucose level, total insulin dose, and average blood glucose levels for today and yesterday.

  • By using the rotation screen icon, you can switch to landscape mode.

  • Below the sensor graph, you will find the Boost and Ease-off functions, enabling you to increase or decrease the automatic basal insulin delivery.

  • At the bottom, there is a button to toggle Auto Mode on and off.

Pressing the menu at the top left reveals several options:

  • Add meal

  • Notifications

  • Share

  • Statistics

  • Information about the mylife YpsoPump and sensor, including the option to pair the pump or transmitter

  • Settings

  • Information about the app and your account

  • And an option to stop your sensor.

Within the notifications section, you can customize your alarms according to your personal preferences. This primarily involves sensor alarms such as high and low alarm, urgent-low alarm, rise-and-fall alarm, and signal-loss alarm. Additionally, you can set a reminder for pump refill, typically set to three days to prompt infusion set replacement. You have the choice to configure vibration or select a specific sound for notifications, with the sound level determined by your mobile phone's volume settings. Please note that you cannot disable the urgent low alarm at 56 mg/dl or 3.1 mmol/l, even if the sound on your mobile phone is turned off. Sleep mode is available to temporarily silence notifications for 10 minutes to 2 hours.

Under the "Statistics" section, you can view your data for the past day, week, month, or three months. This includes average glucose, estimated HbA1c, coefficient of variation, time in range, time below range, sensor glucose availability, total daily insulin dose, and time spent in Auto Mode. Furthermore, you can generate a PDF report to share with your healthcare provider.

In the settings, you'll find additional options such as:

  • Changing your weight setting.

  • Setting a lock to prevent unauthorized access or unintended data entry.

  • Adjusting your personal glucose target. By default, it is set to 104 mg/dl or 5.8 mmol/l, but it can be modified within the range of 80 to 200 mg/dl or 4.4 to 11.1 mmol/l for different time periods.

  • Configuring settings for the bolus wizard. Please note that in Auto Mode, only the carbohydrate ratio is relevant, while the other parameters are used solely in Manual Mode.

  • Modifying thresholds for statistics, which can be useful for specific situations such as pregnancy.

  • Defining default portion sizes for meal calculations.

  • Setting the carbohydrate unit.

  • Revealing hidden messages.

  • And enabling notifications from the mylife CamAPS FX app.

docs.title: Starting Auto Mode
docs.text:
To activate Auto Mode in the mylife CamAPS FX app, simply tap the Auto Mode button located at the bottom of the home screen. If Auto Mode is successfully enabled, the app screen will transition from a gray to a green color, indicating that the system is in Auto Mode. When the app is attempting to start Auto Mode, the background becomes orange. If you're unsure why Auto Mode remains orange instead of turning green, you can tap the i-icon in the lower left beside of auto mode for more information.

In landscape mode, the mylife CamAPS FX app displays the automatic basal insulin as a blue graph below, while the insulin boluses and carbohydrate entries are shown on top. The gray bar on top indicates when Auto Mode is Enabled or Attempting to activate. Additionally, a red bar positioned in the middle signifies the usage of the Boost function, while a yellow bar represents the Ease-Off function.

In Auto Mode, you won't have the ability to modify any settings on your mylife YpsoPump. Instead, you will only be able to observe the delivery of extended boluses on your mylife YpsoPump.

docs.title: Using Ease-off and Boost
docs.text:
In Auto Mode, you have the option to temporarily adjust the amount of insulin you receive by activating the ease-Off or Boost function.

  • The "Ease-off" function is designed to reduce insulin delivery.

  • It can be utilized to prevent hypoglycemia during exercise, as well as to mitigate the risk of low blood sugar in hot weather, after consuming alcohol, or following episodes of hypoglycemia.

  • When activating "Ease-off," you will be prompted to set a time duration of up to 23 hours.

  • You can choose whether the change should take effect immediately or at a later time. If you select a later time, you can specify the exact timing.

  • A blue status bar labeled "Scheduled reduction" will appear below the graph, indicating that the Ease-Off Mode will commence at the designated time.

  • On the other hand, the "Boost" function increases the algorithm's insulin delivery.

  • It can also be used to treat hyperglycemia resulting from factors such as errors in carbohydrate counting during a meal, late or missed boluses, non-functioning infusion sets, or mild illness without the presence of ketones.

  • Additionally, Boost can be used to prevent hyperglycemia in situations involving temporary insulin resistance, such as the days preceding menstruation or when using corticosteroids.

  • By activating the "Boost" function, you can set a time duration of up to 13 hours.

docs.title: Adjusting parameters
docs.text:
Mylife Loop allows for adjustments in the target value or the carb ratio.

  • The target value can be found in the settings as the personal glucose target

  • And the carb ratio can be found in the configuration options of the bolus calculator.

When you launch the app for the first time, it will prompt you to provide your average total insulin dose over the last 5 days and your current weight. The weight setting can be changed afterwards via the settings.

docs.title: Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
To administer a bolus in Auto Mode, you can utilize the meal bolus tab within the app.

  • Upon tapping the meal bolus tab, the app will establish a connection with your insulin pump. Please note that there might be a slight delay during this process.

  • You can then input your carbohydrate intake in various ways:

  • Choose a preset meal size.

  • Utilize a slider to select the number of grams of carbohydrates.

  • Or enter the exact number of grams manually.

  • This is how you give an insulin bolus for a specific amount of carbohydrates in Auto Mode. The bolus can be canceled at any time. In landscape mode, the bolus and carbohydrate values are prominently displayed at the top of the graph.

It's important to note that if you are in Auto Mode and use the bolus calculator, the only thing you have to do is initiate the suggested bolus.

  • If you do an extra step and tap on the glucose value and confirm it, the app will calculate and add a correction bolus based on the current sensor glucose reading, which is not necessary. If Auto Mode is active, the algorithm has already accounted for out-of-range glucose levels. Administering an additional insulin correction bolus in this manner often leads to hypoglycemia 1-3 hours later.

  • On the other hand, if Auto Mode is disabled, it is useful to tap on the glucose value and confirm it, to calculate a correction using the bolus calculator. If Auto Mode is disabled the glucose will be corrected automatically in the bolus calculator. The insulin needed for correction is shown to the right of the glucose. The insulin on board will be subtracted.

  • Additionally, if you are in Auto Mode and you want to give a manual insulin bolus, for example because you suspect an infusion set blockage, it might also make sense to calculate an additional correction bolus by tapping on and confirming your glucose.

There are situations where it is beneficial not to enter carbohydrates in the bolus calculator, but instead utilize the additional "add meal" option. By doing so, the app will administer insulin only if necessary. Within this option, there are three choices that correspond to specific scenarios:

  • Carbs as a meal or snack: This is useful when consuming a small snack between meals but only requiring insulin if hyperglycemia occurs. It can also be employed to split the carbohydrates from larger meals, entering a portion here and the remainder via the bolus calculator.

  • Carbohydrates to treat hypoglycemia: Use this option when carbohydrates are consumed to address low blood sugar, and insulin administration is not required.

  • Slowly absorbed meal: If carbohydrates are entered here, the insulin for these carbohydrates will be administered gradually over the next 3 to 4 hours, but only if blood glucose rises.

docs.title: Suspending insulin
docs.text: If you need to temporarily stop the pump, such as when disconnecting it for showering or swimming, you can only do so directly on the pump itself via the main menu. The mylife CamAPS FX app will then send notifications every 30 minutes to remind you to reactivate the pump.

docs.title: 4.3 Manual Mode
docs.text:
If there is no connection with the sensor or no sensor data available, such as during sensor warm-up, mylife Loop will revert to Manual Mode.

If the mylife YpsoPump is still connected to the mylife CamAPS FX app, you can utilize the app to administer a bolus using the integrated bolus calculator.

However, it's important to remember that you cannot adjust the basal insulin settings of Manual Mode within the app itself; these adjustments can only be made directly on the pump. In Manual Mode, there is no suspend-before-low function available.

In the event that you forget your mobile phone or its battery is depleted, you can still control your pump using the pump itself. Detailed instructions on how to do this can be found in the mylife YpsoPump module.

On the mylife YpsoPump, you have the ability to change the basal insulin settings and set temporary basal rates if necessary. Additionally, you can administer a manual bolus, choosing between a regular or extended bolus. Please note that the mylife YpsoPump itself does not have a bolus calculator feature.

Mastering mylife Loop opens up a new realm of possibilities for diabetes management. This comprehensive guide has covered the ins and outs of operating the system, from understanding the mylife CamAPS FX app to navigating Auto Mode and Manual Mode. With mylife Loop, you have the power to fine-tune your insulin delivery and optimize your blood glucose control.

Remember, mastering any new technology may take time and practice. If you encounter any challenges along the way, don't hesitate to consult the manual for detailed instructions or reach out to the supportive community in the CamAPS FX or YpsoPump Facebook group. The app itself is designed to be user-friendly, with intuitive features and a range of adjustable settings. Stay patient, explore the various options available, and soon you'll be harnessing the full potential of mylife Loop for personalized diabetes management.

docs.title: 5. Creating and interpreting reports (16 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this informative video on creating and interpreting reports using mylife Loop. In order to access and analyze these reports, we will be utilizing the Glooko readout software.

Before we delve into the details, please make sure you have followed the instructions from the previous videos to create a Glooko account and connect it to your mylife CamAPS FX app. This ensures that your information is regularly transmitted to Glooko every 5-10 minutes.

In this video, we will cover the following key points:

  • Overview of the different types of reports available in Glooko.

  • And step-by-step guidance on how to interpret these reports effectively.

By the end of this video, you will have a comprehensive understanding of how to generate and make the most out of the reports provided by Glooko. Let's dive in and explore this essential aspect of mylife Loop.

docs.title: 5.1 Types of reports
docs.text:
In Glooko, you can find the key reports under the tabs Summary, Graphs, Insights and Devices.

The summary tab is the first tab within any patient profile on Glooko. Here you have the patient's name, date of birth and their type of diabetes. You can also create pdf reports.

Below that we have the time selector. Here, using the drop down, you can select between a variety of time periods, from one day to 90 days, and custom time ranges. You can select between blood glucose data and CGM data using the selector on the right hand side. Using the arrows below you can navigate between different time periods in the increments selected, in the time drop down box above.

As we continue to scroll, you can see that we have the glucose data divided into time in range, time below range and time above range.

We can also see other metrics such as the glucose management indicator, the averages, as well as the highest and lowest blood glucose readings within this time period. As we continue to scroll, you'll see that we are presented with an AGP graph.

Here you can see the dark line indicating the median and the various shaded areas showing the statistical distribution.

As we look towards the right hand side of the summary tab you can see insulin data. Here we have the average total daily basal dose and bolus dose and also this percentage split between each. We can see the total daily dose on average as well as the percentage of overrides that this patient is doing using in this case their insulin pump. Overrides will not always be available as will some other metrics metrics will be displayed based on the availability of the data in the devices that that patient is using.

For example, for users of the CamAPS FX algorithm, the Glooko summary report will also show these System Details, indicating the amount of time in Auto Mode On and Off, the time in Auto Mode Attempting, and the usage of the Boost and Ease-Off function.

As we continue to scroll you can find dietary information for average carbs per day and the number of entries and below that we finally have the history section here we have all the events listed for the time period selected including food, blood glucose results, and any insulin use.

The overview tab within the graph section of a patient's profile is particularly flexible and allows for deep insights into the data that has been uploaded. First we can select the time range that we would like to review from the drop down including custom time ranges. After doing this we can review the graph below where we see glucose results color coded for those that are above within and below the target range including a black trend line. Below that we have carbohydrates which are represented as proportionally sized bars on the chart representing the amount of carbohydrates that are recorded for each day. Likewise we have proportionally sized bars for insulin which indicate the amount of insulin that's been administered on each day.

The great thing about the charts within Glooko is that they can be interacted with. So when we highlight a particular day, we can see the median glucose for that day. We can see the total carbs as well as the total units of insulin over the course of that day. If we want to see this information in a more detailed view, you can actually click on that specific day. That will then bring up a much more detailed chart, which will show the sensor results but this time they will be presented alongside the individual blood glucose values that were recorded. Beneath that we have the carbohydrate values that were recorded and when we hover on them we have the exact time that they were recorded. We have bolus insulin which is presented with proportional bars and when we hover over these we get more information such as this one here.

Beneath that we have the basal ray where you see the dotted line represents a temporary basal the solid line represents a scheduled basal and we might get gaps in that line such as we see here on the right hand side which is signified by the red dots which show that the basal was suspended during this time.

For users of automated insulin delivery systems, you can also see the automated basal insulin delivery and the System details.

For patients that are using insulin pumps the Insights tab can be particularly useful as it allows clinicians and patients to see how the events and features that are unique to an insulin pump are impacting the diabetes management. Here you can see that we start on the set and site change tab. Here we can see how frequently a patient is changing the set or site their pump is located. You can see for instance that this patient has changed their pump set after two days, they have changed it five times after three days, and they have changed it three times after four days. This allows the clinician and patient to see if the patient is sticking within the recommended guidelines and perhaps here where the patient changed it after two days there was an issue with the pump that they would then want to discuss with their clinician further.

As we scroll down you can see the date and time of each one of these events and the time since the last set or site change. You'll see on the right hand side we have each of the events lined up here down the black line. We can see glucose control for 12 hours prior to the event and for six hours after the event. This allows us to see if how the event has actually been handled has impacted glucose management. For instance inserting a cannula into a hard fatty lump. This may impact how insulin is absorbed by the body and might be something that clinician would want to explore further with a patient and offer an education opportunity.

The device tab within a patient's profile shows all of the devices that are contributing data to the report seen within the profile. On the left hand side, you can find all the devices listed, along with the serial number and the time the device was last synced with Glooko. If we select a device such as an insulin pump you'll see we have device specific information including the basal profiles. These are represented in a text format and graphically, showing the units per hour the insulin will be delivered and how this varies over a 24 hour period. As we continue to scroll you'll find other information such as the carb ratios, insulin sensitivity and the target blood glucose ranges, which the device uses to provide guidance for both correction and bolus doses.

As we scroll back to the top of the screen we can access device settings. Here we have general information such as the active insulin time, the units that the device is reporting in, as well as parameters such as the maximum bolus and maximum basal rate. Please note that you cannot change settings from here, but they will be recorded. Which is really useful, because if you decide to change settings in order to benefit patient outcomes, but this does not have the desired effect, and you wish to revert to settings that have previously been used, you can use the drop down box on the right hand side, to view settings from previous uploads. You'll see on the left we have an icon of a clock and again that is shown at the top of the screen. That indicates that the time and date on this device do not align with the time and date of the glucose server. In this case you can see by the text on the right hand side that this device deviates by three minutes. It's important that all the devices that a patient is using are synchronized in order to ensure that the reports are of the highest quality and you can gain the best clinical insights from them for assisting a patient and informing clinical decision making.

When generating a PDF report in Glooko, you will also have access to the Weekly Overview alongside other reports.

This report allows you to compare multiple daily reports in detail, providing a faster and more efficient way to analyze the data compared to viewing them individually through the charts tab.

docs.title: 5.2 Interpreting reports
docs.text:
When using mylife Loop, the ability to adjust parameters is limited, primarily focused on the target value and carbohydrate ratio. However, understanding and effectively utilizing the system's additional features, such as Add Meal, Ease-Off and Boost, is key to maximizing its potential.

Additionally, it's important to consider the time spent in Auto Mode, as the algorithm operates on your mobile phone, which may not be in proximity of your sensor and pump at all times.

Allowing a grace period of approximately two weeks for the system to adapt to the user is highly recommended before implementing significant adjustments. After this time, it is common to fine-tune the system by setting a more aggressive carbohydrate ratio and reducing the personal glucose target value.

When analyzing the reports, it is beneficial to follow a structured approach, as emphasized in the general module on automated insulin delivery systems. Here is a roadmap to guide your interpretation:

1. Assess Glycemic Information: Begin by evaluating the TIR (Time in Range) and TBR (Time Below Range) over the past 2-4 weeks on the Summary Tab.

  • Ensure that treatment goals are being met, with TIR above 70% and TBR below 4%.

  • It is important to pay attention to the frequency of meal bolusing, which is also indicated in the Summary Tab of Glooko. On average, there should be around 3 boluses per day for meals. If you notice an unusually high number of boluses, such as 20 boluses per day, it may indicate that the user does not trust the system and is constantly interfering with manual correction boluses.

2. Optimize Automated Insulin Delivery Settings: Analyze the AGP (Ambulatory Glucose Profile) on the Summary Tab to identify trends of hypo- or hyperglycemia, and check if these trends are related to boluses on the Weekly Overview reports.

  • If you notice trends after meal boluses, it's important to assess various factors such as timing and accurate carbohydrate counting, before adjusting the carb ratio.

  • Persistent hypos after meals indicate a too strong carbohydrate ratio.

  • Persistent basal insulin administration after meals, with possible late post-meal hypo, indicates a too weak carbohydrate ratio.

  • For trends outside of meal boluses, consider adjusting the target value. Keep in mind that you have the flexibility to adjust the target value based on different time blocks. Consider lowering the target value further during overnight periods to optimize your Time in Range (TIR) and improve overall glycemic control.

  • Additionally, explore possible correlations with factors such as exercise, alcohol consumption, or hypoglycemia.

If you come across "patency pulses" in the Glooko reports, there's no need to be concerned.

  • These pulses represent small insulin boluses of 0.04 E delivered every 90 minutes when the basal insulin is turned off. Their purpose is to prevent catheter or infusion set blockage. Although they may appear as 0.2 E on the graph, they actually amount to only 0.04 E. This minimal dose of insulin has no significant clinical effect and will not cause hypoglycemia.

  • If you notice frequent occurrences of patency pulses throughout the day, it's advisable to investigate possible reasons of this temporary low insulin requirement. For very young children, patency pulses are often observed during the latter part of the night due to their lower insulin requirements.

3. Guide Behavioral Recommendations: To ensure the proper usage of the automated insulin delivery system, it is important to review various aspects in the reports available. Here are some key considerations:

  • Summary Tab: Check the sensor wear duration and time spent in Auto Mode.

  • If the time in Auto Mode is \<85%, emphasize that the mobile phone, which hosts the algorithm, should be kept nearby at all times.

  • Also in the Summary Tab: Evaluate if bolus calculator overrides are low (ideally 0%). Also, check the amount of carbs entered for meals.

  • Weekly Overview Report: Assess the adherence to pre-meal bolusing, overcorrection of hypo- and hyperglycemia, and the appropriate use of the Ease-Off and Boost function.

  • In this example, you can observe a situation where a bolus was missed, leading to elevated blood glucose levels.

  • Here, you can see an instance where a user administered a double bolus for carbohydrates, but unfortunately, it was done after the rise in blood glucose levels, resulting in delayed hypoglycemia.

  • During the initial phase, it is advised to limit the use of the Boost and Ease-Off functions. By doing so, you provide the algorithm with an opportunity to establish the optimal balance and adapt to your body's needs. It's common for individuals to be overly aggressive in correcting hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia, as illustrated in this example.

  • On the Insights tab, you can check if the infusion set replacement is done every 3 days.

  • Devices Tab: Assess the appropriateness of alarm settings and reminders in the mylife CamAPS FX app. Verify that the settings align with the user's needs and preferences.

4. Review Pump Settings and Emergency Plans:

  • It is important to update your weight during each check-up if necessary, especially during pregnancy, as body weight plays a crucial role in the post-meal insulin calculation. However, outside of pregnancy, when aiming for less stringent post-meal targets, weight updates are less critical.

  • Verify that the preset basal insulin rate on your mylife YpsoPump aligns approximately with the basal insulin of Auto Mode. You can check this in the Devices tab of the Glooko report

  • Keep a detailed record of all pump settings and create an emergency plan that includes instructions for using insulin pens in case of a pump malfunction. Ensure easy access to insulin pens for such situations.

So, now you have learned how to obtain and interpret reports from the mylife Loop automated insulin delivery system. You have gained valuable insights into optimizing your settings and making adjustments to enhance your experience.

With the knowledge and understanding you have acquired, you can feel confident in managing your diabetes effectively using the mylife Loop AID system. Remember, it's normal to take some time to fine-tune the parameters and find the right balance for your unique needs. Trust yourself and the system, and don't hesitate to seek support from healthcare professionals or the online community if needed.

You are empowered to take control of your diabetes management and achieve better outcomes with mylife Loop. Keep up the great work, and enjoy the benefits of this advanced technology!

docs.title: 6. Managing special situations (8.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module where we will discuss managing special situations with the mylife Loop automated insulin delivery system!

In this informative video, we will provide you with specific tips on managing hypo- and hyperglycemia, dealing with high-fat meals, engaging in sports activities, navigating illness, drinking alcohol and handling travel situations while using mylife Loop.

These tips are designed to complement the general guidelines for automated insulin delivery systems. If you want to learn more about managing these situations in the context of automated insulin delivery systems, refer to the general module on automated insulin delivery systems.

By following these guidelines, you'll be equipped with practical strategies to address various scenarios and ensure optimal glucose control with mylife Loop. Let's dive in and explore these valuable tips together!

docs.title: 6.1 Hypoglycemia
docs.text:
In the event of hypoglycemia, you will need fewer carbs with mylife Loop compared to traditional methods, especially when you have minimal insulin on board. You can utilize the landscape mode to check your insulin on board.

When consuming carbohydrates to treat or prevent a hypo, it is recommended to enter this information under the "Add Meal" function in the system.

To prevent recurring hypoglycemic episodes, you can take advantage of the Ease-Off function. This feature can also be used to prevent hypoglycemia during exercise, alcohol consumption, hot weather, and other relevant situations.

Additionally, mylife Loop allows you to temporarily set a higher target value. This adjustment can be beneficial in the following scenarios:

- During periods of frequent hypoglycemia

- When engaging in an active holiday or visiting warmer climates

- Or when starting a new job or returning to school or training after a break

docs.title: 6.2 Hyperglycemia
docs.text:
When dealing with mild hyperglycemia, it's important to trust the mylife Loop AID system and allow it to work autonomously. Avoid giving excessive manual correction boluses, as this often leads to subsequent hypoglycemia 1-3 hours later. In the example shown, someone gave a correction bolus for a hyperglycemia following a meal, resulting in a subsequent hypoglycemic episode. Since the algorithm already provides additional insulin when hyperglycemia occurs, it's safer to use the "Boost" function to address hyperglycemia, especially after a meal.

Using "Boost" to support the system in correcting glucose levels quickly, is preferable to adjusting the carbohydrate ratio or personal glucose target. However, especially in the beginning, it's advisable not to use the "Boost" and "Ease-off" functions too much. The system needs time to understand your patterns and preferences. When you use a lot of Boost or Ease-Off, the system's learning process is hindered. Overusing these features can be compared to driving a car with one foot on the accelerator and the other on the brake simultaneously.

In cases of prolonged severe hyperglycemia, it's important to consider the possibility of an infusion set issue. "If in doubt, change it out."
