It's recommended to have enough supplies with you, including an extra inserter for the infusion set. To prevent infusion set problems, it's advisable to replace the set every 2-3 days and set a reminder in your mylife CamAPS FX app under the "refill pump" message.

docs.title: 6.3 High-fat meals
docs.text:
For large meals with more than 60 grams of carbs, you can try to split the bolus.

  • Administer 50-75 % 15 minutes before the meal using the bolus calculator & the remaining insulin dose during or after the meal using either the bolus calculator or the "Add meal" option with the "meal or snack" setting.

For meals that are slowly absorbed due to factors such as low glycemic index, high fat and protein content, or gastroparesis, you can consider to enter the second part of the bolus as a slowly digested meal in the add meal function. The algorithm will then provide small additional insulin boluses every 30 minutes for the next 3-4 hours, depending on the glucose level.

If you have a reduced appetite or irregular eating pattern, you can enter the minimum amount of carbohydrates you reliably consume into the bolus calculator. If you end up eating more carbohydrates than initially planned, you can add the remaining amount later through a second bolus or by using the "Add meal" option, selecting "meal or snack."

docs.title: 6.4 Exercise
docs.text:
Utilize the Ease-Off function and reduce the meal bolus if prolonged exercise is anticipated within 2 hours of a meal, as recommended in the general module on automated insulin delivery systems.

If you reduce the meal bolus to prevent hypoglycemia, it is recommended to enter the full number of grams of carbohydrates but adjust the bolus recommendation accordingly. You can achieve this by tapping the suggested insulin bolus and adjusting the dose using the scroll wheel. This way, the algorithm is aware that more carbohydrates were consumed.

In addition to using Ease-Off, recent guidelines recommend raising the glucose target based on glucose trends before and during exercise. If sensor glucose is trending downward, increase the target to 120 to 150 mg/dL (or 6.7 to 8.3 mmol/L) or higher.

Please note that these recommendations serve as a starting strategy, and insulin dosage should be further adjusted based on your individual observations and patterns during exercise.

docs.title: 6.5 Illness
docs.text: During illness, hyperglycemia is common. Follow the guidelines for managing hyperglycemia in such situations.

docs.title: 6.6 Alcohol
docs.text:
If you drink alcohol, you can proactively try to prevent hypoglycemia by temporarily increasing your target value and/or using the "Ease-off" function to reduce insulin delivery.

It does NOT help to take in more carbohydrates, as the system will compensate for any increase in glucose levels by increasing the administration of insulin.

docs.title: 6.7 Travel
docs.text:
When travelling with mylife Loop, be sure to take plenty of supplies with you. Be sure not to forget your mobile phone charger and, if necessary, an appropriate travel plug for the country you are travelling to. If you’re using the Dexcom G6 sensor, it is best to take a spare Dexcom G6 transmitter with you.

Consider using the "Ease-off" function or setting a higher personal glucose target for the first few days of your holiday. This can help prevent the risk of hypoglycemia, especially if you anticipate being more active than usual or traveling to a destination with warmer weather.

The mylife CamAPS FX app automatically adjusts the time when you travel through different time zones. This ensures that your system continues to operate smoothly without any manual intervention required.

Some airlines require passengers to put their mobile phones on Airplane Mode during take-off and landing, which can temporarily interrupt Bluetooth communication with mylife Loop. If this happens, don't worry. After 30 minutes, the system will switch to the preset basal insulin delivery until Bluetooth connectivity is restored. Once you're allowed to use your phone again, remember to turn off Airplane Mode promptly to ensure Auto Mode resumes.

In this informative video, we have covered specific tips on how to handle various situations with mylife Loop. From managing hypo- and hyperglycemia to dealing with high-fat meals, sports, illness, alcohol, and travel, you now have the knowledge and strategies to navigate these challenges effectively.

Remember, you are in control of your diabetes management with mylife Loop. Embrace the flexibility, trust in the system, and have confidence in your ability to handle any situation that comes your way. You've Got This!

docs.title: 7. Case report (2.5 min)
docs.text:
In this final chapter, we have an exciting YouTube video featuring Tom from Type One Talks, who made a great review of mylife Loop. In a highly engaging video, Tom goes over all the main features and his own experience with the system, while comparing mylife Loop with other automated insulin delivery systems like Tandem Control-IQ. He tested mylife Loop with both the Dexcom G6 sensor and the FreeStyle Libre 3 sensor, and described which one he liked best.

Please click on the link below this lesson, to watch Tom’s video now. This quick recap will aid you in consolidating all the knowledge acquired in this module, ensuring that you retain the essential information about mylife Loop!

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r4nVdTaUfSo – 22 min

By completing this course, you have gained valuable knowledge and insights into mylife Loop. This knowledge empowers you to make informed decisions and take control of your diabetes management effectively. Congratulations on finishing the course!

Now, here comes the fun part. Are you up for a fun challenge within the course? Here's an exciting fact: You can put the mylife CamAPS FX app to the test right now! We dare you to try it out! Download the app on your phone, select the virtual pump, and pair it with the virtual sensor. It's time to experience mylife Loop firsthand! Now, here comes the challenge.

Give yourself a bolus and capture a picture of this achievement. Upload that picture in the next lesson and share your experience in the comments section below to inspire and motivate others on their diabetes management journey. Let's support each other and make this a fantastic learning experience together!

Remember, mylife Loop provides a revolutionary approach to insulin delivery, allowing you to lead an active and fulfilling life while maintaining optimal glucose control. Embrace the possibilities, stay connected with the community, and continue learning and growing in your diabetes management journey.

Thank you for joining us on this educational course, and we wish you all the best in your continued use of mylife Loop. Together, we can make a positive impact on diabetes management and improve the quality of life for individuals worldwide.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Omnipod 5 according to the CARES paradigm
  • 3. Linking components together
  • 4. How the Omnipod 5 system works
  • 5. Creating and interpreting reports
  • 6. Managing special situations

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this comprehensive module on the Omnipod 5 automated insulin delivery system. Designed to provide you with a thorough understanding of the system, this module will guide you through its components, functionality, and practical application. By the end of this module, you will have gained valuable knowledge on how to effectively utilize the Omnipod 5 system in your diabetes management.

The Omnipod 5 automated insulin delivery system is widely utilized in the United States and has recently made its way to Europe, with expectations of expanding to more countries in the near future. The system combines three key components:

  • A glucose sensor like the Dexcom G6, Dexcom G7 or FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus, providing accurate and continuous glucose monitoring.

  • The Omnipod 5 insulin pump, which shares similarities with the Omnipod DASH.

  • And the integrated SmartAdjust algorithm, automatically adjusting insulin delivery based on glucose data.

Reports from the system can be accessed and analyzed using the Glooko platform or the dedicated Omnipod Discover platform. Control of the pump can be managed through the Omnipod 5 Controller. In the US there is also an Omnipod 5 app available for iOS and Android phones. If you use a Dexcom sensor, the Dexcom G6 or G7 app plays a crucial role in the Omnipod 5 system, allowing family members to easily follow and monitor glucose levels through the Dexcom Follow app. When using a FreeStyle Libre sensor, glucose data can be shared with family members via LibreLinkUp, depending on regional availability.

Throughout this module, we will build upon the knowledge gained from our general videos on automated insulin delivery systems, covering topics such as comparing these AID systems using the CARES reference framework, selecting the right system for your needs, interpreting reports, and managing various scenarios such as hypo and hyperglycemia, exercise, illness, high-fat meals, alcohol, and travel.

It's crucial to remember that these videos are not intended as medical advice.It is recommended to consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

The course content has been developed by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The material has been reviewed by Gary Scheiner, a Certified Diabetes Care and Education Specialist and OmniPod user and trainer from Integrated Diabetes Services in the US, and Meg Cremin for the US, an individual with type 1 diabetes who uses the Omnipod 5 system. Their expertise and personal experience contribute to the comprehensive and reliable information provided in the videos.

By the end of this module, you will have gained a comprehensive understanding of the Omnipod 5 automated insulin delivery system, including its performance within the CARES paradigm, practical implementation using the Omnipod 5 Controller and app, report generation and interpretation using Glooko, and strategies for managing hypo and hyperglycemia, exercise, illness, high-fat meals, alcohol, and travel.

As a caregiver, you will be equipped to better support individuals utilizing the Omnipod 5 system, and as an Omnipod 5 user, you will be well-prepared for a successful start-up, armed with a wealth of tips and tricks to simplify your diabetes management with the Omnipod 5 system!

Best of luck on your journey!

docs.title: 2. Omnipod 5 according to the CARES paradigm (14 min)
2. Omnipod 5 according to the CARES paradigm (14 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video where we discuss the Omnipod 5 system according to the CARES paradigm.

We will discuss how Omnipod 5 calculates and adjusts the automated insulin delivery, when it reverts back to manual mode, specific education tips and sensor issues, and how it shares data. After that we will go over Omnipod 5’s official indications and software updates.

docs.title: #1 Calculate
docs.text:
The Omnipod 5 system operates as follows:

  • The SmartAdjust algorithm calculates the basal insulin requirement or “adaptive basal rate” every 5 minutes. It considers the predicted glucose levels for the upcoming hour, the target value and the total insulin delivered by the previous Pod. When predicted hyperglycemia is detected, the algorithm increases the insulin delivery. On the other hand, if hypoglycemia is predicted, the basal insulin delivery is reduced or paused. If the glucose level falls below 60 mg/dL (3.3 mmol/l), the basal insulin delivery is always paused to prioritize safety.

  • When initiating the Omnipod 5 system, you will have to enter starting settings including basal rates. Automated mode can be started immediately and the system will base the magnitude of basal rate adjustments on the basal rate provided, although the maximum delivery is more constrained with the first Pod for safety.

  • After 48 hours of use, and the first Pod change, the system will use the historical basal insulin delivery as a base for the automated basal insulin and further adjust to individual needs thanks to machine learning. The longer the system is worn, the better it performs, so it is recommended to wear it consistently for a few weeks to fully experience the positive impact on Time in Range. This also means that changes in health conditions or medication, such as sickness or starting a steroid medication, will have a more noticeable effect after a pod change. It’s important to note that if you switch Controllers, you will lose your insulin delivery history, and the system will need to re-learn your insulin requirements.

  • The SmartAdjust algorithm does not provide additional autocorrection boluses. It uses basal rate adjustments to prevent and correct hyperglycemia.

  • The target glucose range can be adjusted in 60-minute time blocks, allowing customization between 110 and 150 mg/dL (6.1 and 8.3 mmol/l).

  • It’s important to note that the SmartBolusCalculator of the Omnipod 5 system does not only take into account the current glucose, but also the glucose trend.

docs.title: #2 Adjust
docs.text:
With the Omnipod 5 system, users have the ability to adjust certain parameters to personalize their insulin therapy:

  • The target glucose range can be set within 60-minute time blocks, allowing customization between 110 and 150 mg/dL (6.1 and 8.3 mmol/L).

  • The bolus calculator parameters, such as the carbohydrate-to-insulin ratio, correction factor, duration of insulin action, and "Correct Above" value, can also be adjusted. It’s important to note that these bolus calculator parameters will only have an impact on the manual mealtime or correction boluses, and not on the calculated automated or “adaptive basal rate”. Of these bolus calculator parameters, the carbohydrate-to-insulin ratio is the most influential parameter in terms of clinical impact.

  • An optional Activity Feature is available, which sets a higher target glucose value of 150 mg/dL (8.3 mmol/L) and reduces insulin delivery to accommodate physical activity or times of increased risk of hypoglycemia.

docs.title: #3 Revert
docs.text:
In the Omnipod 5 system, there are situations that can trigger a "Limited mode," including a lack of sensor data for more than 20 minutes or a minimum or maximum insulin release for an extended period of time. During Limited mode, the system maintains a fixed basal insulin rate based on recent history and settings. The maximum basal insulin rate in Limited mode will never exceed the basal rate settings of Manual Mode.

A lack of sensor data can occur due to sensor warm-up or communication issues between the sensor and the Pod, or between the Pod and the Omnipod 5 app. To prevent communication problems, it is recommended to wear the sensor and the CGM within line of sight. If communication problems persist, the app will display a "No Pod Communication" message, and you should follow the on-screen instructions to resolve the issue. Bringing the Controller or mobile phone closer to the Pod, within a distance of 1.5 meters (or 5 feet), can help restore the connection between the Controller and the Pod. If connection restoration fails, you have the option to deactivate the Pod. Once the issue causing the Limited Mode is resolved, Automated Mode will automatically resume.

In addition, Limited Mode may be triggered by minimum or maximum insulin release continuously for an extended period of time, resulting in an "Automated Delivery Restriction" alarm. It is important to respond appropriately by confirming the accuracy of the sensor and reactivating Automated Mode.

When deactivating a Pod, the system automatically switches to Manual Mode. After activating a new Pod, you will be advised to restart Automated Mode again.

It's worth noting that connecting the OmniPod 5 app to the Pod is only necessary for delivering bolus insulin, activating and deactivating a Pod, and for responding to alarms. The algorithm resides on the Pod itself, meaning that even without communication between the Pod and the Omnipod 5 app, the basal insulin will be adjusted every 5 minutes based on the sensor glucose values and trends.

docs.title: #4 Educate
docs.text:
When starting an automated insulin delivery system like Omnipod 5, it's important to consider both general education points for AID systems and specific education points for Omnipod 5.

For general education, please refer to the generic module, which includes the following key points:

  • Use fewer carbohydrates to correct hypoglycemia.

  • Consider an insulin delivery/absorption problem if glucose levels remain high for several hours without an apparent reason. "If in doubt, change it out."

  • Trust the AID system.

  • Administer a meal bolus 15 minutes before meals.

  • Activate Exercise Mode 1 to 2 hours before exercising.

  • Respond to alarms promptly.

  • Disable automatic updates on your mobile phone.

  • And Seek peer support for guidance and sharing experiences.

Specific education points for Omnipod 5 are as follows:

  • Checking the compatibility of your phone with the Dexcom G6 app before starting. If in your country, you have access to the Omnipod 5 mobile app and wish to use this instead of your Controller, you should also check the compatibility of your phone with this app.

  • When using the Omnipod 5 app on your mobile phone, it is recommended to leave it open in the background and avoid closing it. This ensures continuous communication and data exchange within the Omnipod 5 system.

  • Wear the Pod and the CGM on the same side of your body within sight of each other to minimize connection problems.

  • During the initial weeks of using the Omnipod 5 system, you may encounter the "Automated Delivery Restriction" alarm more frequently due to the limited maximum insulin delivery while using the first couple of Pods. However, as you continue using the system, this occurrence will decrease. It is important to respond promptly to this alarm by confirming the accuracy of the sensor and returning to Automated Mode.

  • Press "Use CGM" to ensure that the bolus calculator considers the trend arrow.

  • Brief interruptions in basal insulin delivery may occur if glucose levels are trending downward, even if they are above the target value. This is a normal occurrence with Omnipod 5's algorithm and typically lasts only 5 to 10 minutes unless glucose continues to fall.

docs.title: #5 Sensor
docs.text:
The Omnipod 5 can be used with the Dexcom G6, G7 or the FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensor. You can easily switch between the sensor type.

If you have doubts about the accuracy of your sensor reading or do not feel it reflects your condition, perform a finger prick test to confirm.

If a significant discrepancy, such as a more than 20% difference, exists between the sensor and finger prick readings, even when the blood glucose is stable within the target range, it is advisable to switch to Manual Mode and / or replace the sensor.

The Dexcom sensors can be calibrated if a significant discrepancy exists between the sensor reading and finger prick results, assuming stable glucose levels.

docs.title: #6 Share
docs.text:
With Omnipod 5, you have the ability to share insulin and glucose data with your healthcare provider and family members. The Omnipod 5 app on your Controller or on your phone are continuously connected to your Glooko account and send data to the cloud every 5 minutes, eliminating the need for manual data downloading. Healthcare providers can access this data through their professional Glooko account.

Family members can monitor the user’s glucose sensor values in real time via the Dexcom Follow app when a Dexcom sensor is used, or via the LibreLinkUp app when the user is wearing a FreeStyle Libre sensor. However, connectivity to the LibreView and LibreLinkUp cloud is only available in certain geographic regions.

Currently, there is no dedicated follow app for Omnipod 5, and the Omnipod View and Display app is not compatible. However, there is an experimental integration with Gluroo that enables one-way synchronization of insulin activity entered on a compatible phone running the Omnipod 5 app. This allows you to see insulin doses from the Omnipod 5 on any phone using Gluroo. Please note that this app is not approved or supported by Insulet, and also doesn’t have a CE label or FDA approval. Its use is at your own risk.

docs.title: 2) Indications
docs.text:
Omnipod 5 has both a CE-label and FDA-label for use in individuals with type 1 diabetes who are at least 2 years old. In addition, it holds FDA approval for adults aged eighteen years and older with type 2 diabetes who use insulin.

It is suitable for individuals with a minimum total daily insulin dose of 5 units.

The system is approved for use with Novolog, Novorapid, Humalog or Admelog 100 U/ml insulins.

Please note that Omnipod 5 is not approved for use in pregnancy or in individuals requiring dialysis.

docs.title: 3) Software updates
docs.text:
Occasionally, Insulet may roll out updates to the Omnipod 5 App in stages. When an update is available, you'll receive a notification on your Omnipod 5 app, either on your smartphone or Controller. Before proceeding with the update, ensure your device has sufficient battery power (above 15%) and a strong Wi-Fi connection.

If you're ready to install the update, select ‘Update Now’ and follow the on-screen instructions. If you prefer to wait, you can choose ‘Not Now’ and manually update later from the notification menu (via the bell icon).

During the update, you won't be able to navigate within the Omnipod 5 App on your Controller, including the ability to bolus. However, basal insulin will continue to be delivered in the background. The update process should only take a few minutes, depending on your Wi-Fi strength.

Once the update is complete, normal use of the Omnipod 5 App will resume, and your insulin delivery settings or the adaptivity from the algorithm will remain unaffected. Please note that if you uninstall and reinstall the Omnipod 5 App on your smartphone, you will lose your insulin settings and the adaptivity from the algorithm.

New software releases often come with improved communication and overall functionality. For more details, you can visit the Omnipod website. To check your current software version, tap the three lines in the upper left corner of the app, navigate to the "About" section, and look for the app version.

In conclusion, the Omnipod 5 automated insulin delivery system offers users a convenient and efficient way to manage insulin delivery. With its integration of the Omnipod insulin pump, Dexcom or FreeStyle Libre sensor, and SmartAdjust algorithm, it provides a comprehensive solution for diabetes management.

It's important to remember that while this chapter provides valuable information, it is always recommended to consult with your healthcare provider for personalized advice and guidance. Additionally, you are not alone in your journey with the Omnipod 5 system. There is customer support available from Insulet, the manufacturer of Omnipod, who can assist with any technical issues or questions you may have. Furthermore, joining a dedicated Facebook group or online community can provide a platform for sharing experiences, gaining insights, and finding support from others who are also using the Omnipod 5 system.

Remember, managing diabetes is a journey, and with the support and resources available, you can confidently navigate and make the most of your experience with the Omnipod 5 system.

docs.title: 3. Linking components together (11 min)
docs.text:
In this video, we will guide you through the process of creating links between the various components of the Omnipod 5 system to ensure seamless communication and data sharing.

The Omnipod 5 Pods and Controller share a similar appearance to those used in the Omnipod DASH system, but they incorporate significant differences:

  • The Omnipod 5 Pods contain the SmartAdjust algorithm, which is responsible for making insulin delivery adjustments.

  • You can identify the Omnipod 5 Pods by examining the packaging at the back and the specific box they come in. Additionally, the needle cap of the Omnipod 5 Pods is clear, while the needle cap of the Omnipod DASH Pods is blue.

  • The Omnipod 5 Controller, while slightly larger than its predecessor, the Omnipod DASH PDM, offers several notable improvements. It incorporates additional software to seamlessly integrate with a Dexcom or FreeStyle Libre sensor, and it is equipped with a SIM card, ensuring continuous internet connectivity. One advantage that users have observed is the extended battery life of the Omnipod 5 Controller, surpassing that of the Omnipod DASH PDM.

The Omnipod 5 system integrates multiple components to provide seamless glucose monitoring and insulin delivery. Here's how the system operates:

  1. Glucose data is collected every 1 to 5 minutes from the Dexcom or FreeStyle Libre transmitter.

  2. The data is transmitted to the Omnipod 5 Pod, which houses the algorithm responsible for calculating insulin delivery. The Pod takes instructions from the Omnipod 5 app on a Controller or mobile phone.

  3. The Pod administers the necessary insulin and transmits all the glucose and insulin data to the Omnipod 5 app.

  4. If you’re using a Dexcom sensor, you’ll need to download the dedicated Dexcom app to start the sensor and receive glucose alerts. In this case, the Omnipod 5 app will only trigger an alarm when glucose is below 54 mg/dl or 3 mmol/l.

  5. If you’re using a FreeStyle Libre sensor, you can start the sensor with the Omnipod 5 app on your Controller or on your phone. All glucose alerts will come from the Omnipod 5 app.

  6. All the data from the Omnipod 5 app is sent to the Glooko and Omnipod Discover platform, offering a comprehensive overview of your diabetes management.

  7. Although there is no dedicated follow app for Omnipod 5 itself, family members and caregivers can still view the user’s glucose data. If a Dexcom sensor is used, up to 10 people can follow the user via the Dexcom Follow app. If a FreeStyle Libre sensor is used, up to 20 people can follow via the LibreLinkUp app.

By utilizing these components, the Omnipod 5 system provides accurate and efficient glucose monitoring and insulin delivery for improved diabetes management.

In this video, we will cover the step-by-step process of

  • linking a glucose sensor to the Omnipod 5 app on the Controller or smartphone,

  • connecting the Pod to the Omnipod 5 app on the Controller or smartphone,

  • creating a Glooko account,

  • establishing the link between the Omnipod 5 app and Glooko,

  • and connecting the Omnipod 5 app to LibreView.

Please note that the usage of the Dexcom G6, G7 and Dexcom Follow app is covered in the module about Dexcom G6 and Dexcom G7.

By following the instructions in this video, you will be able to establish the necessary links between the different components of the Omnipod 5 system, enabling seamless data transfer and ensuring effective management of your diabetes.

docs.title: 3.1 Linking a glucose sensor to the Omnipod 5 app
docs.text:
In order to establish a connection between the glucose sensor and the Omnipod 5 app, follow these steps:

  1. If you haven't already, place your Dexcom G6, G7 or FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensor on your skin according to the instructions provided.
  1. On the Omnipod 5 app on your Controller or mobile phone, you'll have the option to select your sensor type during the initial setup. The available options will vary depending on your location. To start a new sensor or to switch sensor types, you can navigate to the main menu and select Manage Sensor.

For people using a Dexcom G6 sensor, you’ll first have to connect your Pod to the Dexcom G6 transmitter. You can do this by entering the serial number of the Dexcom G6 transmitter in the Omnipod 5 app. Please note that it may take up to 20 minutes for the connection to be established. Any time you switch to a new Dexcom G6 transmitter, make sure to update the serial number in this section as well.

Please note that the activation of the Dexcom G6 sensor is done through the dedicated Dexcom G6 app. For detailed instructions on how to start the sensor, please refer to the specific module on Dexcom G6.

After successfully activating the sensor, you can expect to receive glucose readings on both the Dexcom G6 app and the Omnipod 5 app on your Controller or mobile phone after approximately two hours. It's important to be aware that there may be a slight delay in the glucose readings displayed on your Omnipod 5 app compared to the Dexcom G6 app. This delay occurs because the sensor data first transmits to the Omnipod Pod and then to the Omnipod 5 app, while the Dexcom G6 app directly receives the data from the Dexcom transmitter.

For people using a Dexcom G7 sensor, you can select Dexcom G7 as your sensor type, and connect the Dexcom G7 sensor by entering the 4-digit pairing code and the 12-digit serial number. You can do this manually or by taking a photo with your phone or Controller. To receive glucose alerts, it is also necessary to pair your Dexcom G7 sensor to the Dexcom G7 app on your phone. You will need to connect each new Dexcom G7 Sensor to both the Omnipod 5 App and Dexcom G7 app for your Pod and Sensor to stay connected.

For people using a FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensor, you can select FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus as your sensor type. First you will need to enter your preferred settings for your low glucose alert, high glucose alert and missing sensor values alert. Next, you can follow the on-screen instructions to start your sensor by scanning the inserted sensor. Tap OK if the Sensor scans successfully. Please note that it can take up to 20 minutes for the Pod and Sensor to connect. When the Pod and Sensor have connected, you will be asked to switch to Automated Mode.

If you receive a “Failed to Connect” message, try reconnecting the sensor. Make sure there are no other Pods nearby that you may have previously used or attempted to use, as other Pods within Bluetooth range (that is within approximately 9 meters or 30 feet) can interfere with the connection. Check that the Pod and sensor are in direct line of sight and worn on the same side of the body, without your body blocking communication. Also confirm that the sensor is still properly attached to your skin.

You can also try turning Airplane Mode on and then off again via the Settings Menu to re-establish the connection, and ensure that Bluetooth is turned back on afterward. After Pod activation, allow up to 20 minutes for sensor values to appear in the Omnipod 5 app.

If the issue persists, delete the sensor and add a new one in the Omnipod 5 app. Once a sensor is deleted, it cannot be reconnected and a new sensor must be started. Please note that you must be in Manual Mode to manage your sensor.

Although the FreeStyle LibreLink app or Libre app is not required to start the FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensor when using the Omnipod 5 system, you may still choose to use this app after sensor start. Scanning the sensor with the Libre app allows you to view glucose readings directly on your phone and can be useful during temporary connection issues, for example when the Controller displays a “sensor searching” message.

If you experience frequent sensor connection interruptions, you might consider disabling the Missing Sensor Values alert - as most of these sensor connection issues resolve on their own.

A mandatory alert will still be triggered if the connection is lost for more than one hour. In most cases, simply changing your body position to improve line of sight between the sensor and the Pod is sufficient to restore the connection.

docs.title: 3.2 Linking the Omnipod 5 Pod to the Omnipod 5 app
docs.text:
In order to activate an Omnipod 5 Pod and link it to the Omnipod 5 app, you can follow the same steps as you would for the Omnipod DASH:

  1. Open the Omnipod 5 app on your device and navigate to the "Pod Info" tab.

  2. Choose the option to set up a new Pod.

  3. The app will guide you through the necessary steps, which include:

o Filling the Pod with insulin

o Pairing the Pod with the Omnipod 5 app

o Placing the Pod on your body

o And inserting the cannula to initiate insulin delivery

By following these steps, you will be able to activate an Omnipod 5 Pod and establish the connection with the Omnipod 5 app for seamless control and monitoring of your insulin delivery.

docs.title: 3.3 Creating a Glooko account
docs.text:
The pump and sensor data can be shared with your healthcare provider via Glooko.

We will describe how to create a Glooko account as a user via Glooko Personal, and as a healthcare professional via Glooko for Professional Use.

docs.title: #1 Glooko Personal~~:~~
docs.text:
• As a user, you can visit my.glooko.com and choose "Create Account."

  • At a certain moment you will be able to enter a "proconnect code" that connects your account to your clinic.

  • After completing the registration, you will receive an email with further instructions. It is also possible to receive this email directly from your healthcare provider.

  • Create a password for your account and keep it accessible for future logins, especially when you need to upload data from your pump.

  • When logging into Glooko, you will be prompted to select the devices you use. For Omnipod 5, you can simply choose the option for Omnipod 5 from the list of available devices. This will ensure that the data from your Omnipod 5 system is properly synced and integrated into the Glooko platform for comprehensive data analysis and reporting.

docs.title: #2 Glooko for Healthcare Providers:
docs.text:
1. Healthcare providers or institutions can purchase a professional Glooko account directly from Glooko. You can then log in via my.glooko.com using the username and password provided by your hospital.

  2. Upon logging in, you will find an overview of the latest data uploads and a separate tab displaying an overview of your patients. On the top right corner you will find the "proconnect code" of your hospital.

  3. You can customize the patient list by sorting it according to your preferences or using flags to identify patients with high or low glycemic levels easily.

  4. You can add a new patient via the button in the top right corner and you can add their email address to invite them to Glooko.

Additionally, healthcare providers can access patient reports without a Glooko Pro account if the patient shares their login credentials.

docs.title: 3.4 Linking the Omnipod 5 app to Glooko
docs.text:
To connect the Omnipod 5 app to Glooko, follow these steps:

  1. During the setup process of the Omnipod 5 app, you will be asked to provide your email address and password associated with your Glooko account.

  2. Once you have entered your credentials, the Omnipod 5 app will automatically start sending data to your Glooko account every 5 minutes.

  3. Please note that there may be a one-hour delay in the data appearing in Glooko.

  4. To verify if your Omnipod 5 app is properly linked to Glooko, log in to your Glooko personal account, go to the settings section, and navigate to "Apps and Devices". You should see the Omnipod 5 listed as a connected device.

By successfully connecting the Omnipod 5 app to Glooko, you can ensure that your Omnipod 5 data is seamlessly transferred and available for comprehensive analysis and reporting within the Glooko platform.

docs.title: 3.5 Connecting the Omnipod 5 app to LibreView
docs.text:
In some regions, it is now possible to connect the Omnipod 5 app to LibreView when using the FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensor. To set this up, log in to your Omnipod account with your Omnipod ID on a desktop computer and then click on the LibreView link button and follow the steps. Once you are linked to LibreView, click on the Manage LibreLinkUp button to invite family and caregivers to view your glucose data with the LibreLinkUp app. Any caregivers you have previously linked to LibreLinkUp will already be able to see your sensor information.

It is important to keep your Omnipod 5 Controller and Pod within range to enable glucose values to be shared with your caregiver. If the Controller is not in range of the Pod, there may be gaps in the data available to the caregiver.

With these step-by-step instructions, you are now ready to fully integrate the Omnipod 5 system into your diabetes management and make the most of its advanced glucose monitoring and insulin delivery capabilities.

docs.title: 4. How the Omnipod 5 system works (11.5 min)
4. How the Omnipod 5 system works (11.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on the functionality of the Omnipod 5 system. Controlling the insulin delivery is done through the Omnipod 5 app on your Controller or on your mobile phone. Currently, the Omnipod 5 app for phone control is only available in the US, but broader roll-out is expected.

In this video, we will explore how to use the Omnipod 5 app in general, and how to use it in both Automated and Manual Mode.

docs.title: 4.1 Omnipod 5 App
docs.text:
The initial setup of the Omnipod 5 app needs to be completed on your Controller.

  • During this process, you will be required to provide necessary details, including your Omnipod ID and Glooko account information. To create an Omnipod ID, you can visit Insulet's website at www.omnipod.com/setup. Creating an Omnipod ID is free of charge and is necessary for accessing the app and website functionalities. Once you have obtained your Omnipod ID, you can use it to log into Insulet's website and access their online training resources.

  • When setting up the Omnipod 5 app on your Controller, you will also be prompted to confirm whether you have completed the appropriate training. After completing this step, you can choose to proceed with the setup either on the Controller or on the Omnipod 5 app installed on your mobile phone.

  • Please note that switching between the Controller and mobile phone is only possible when starting a new pod. Additionally, when you uninstall and reinstall the Omnipod 5 app on your phone, or change the Controller, all the data and history accumulated by the Pod will be lost, and the algorithm learning restarts. If necessary, you have the option to clear the data within your existing controller to restart the algorithm.

  • For the Controller, you will choose a PIN, security message, and background, while the mobile app PIN will match your phone's PIN.

  • If you choose to use the Omnipod 5 app on your mobile phone, there are specific settings that you need to consider for optimal functionality:

  • Enable Bluetooth.

  • Grant location permission to the app.

  • Allow notifications from the Omnipod 5 app.

  • Ensure that the "do not disturb" option is disabled.

  • Disable any battery-saving mode.

  • To utilize the bolus calculator, set a PIN code or a similar security measure on your mobile phone.

Using the Omnipod 5 app, as well as the Dexcom G6 or G7 app, should not significantly impact the battery life of your device. Both apps utilize Bluetooth Low Energy for communication, which is designed to be energy-efficient.

Internet connectivity is essential for receiving updates and automatically uploading data to Glooko. The Controller comes with a SIM card for internet access, while the mobile app utilizes your data plan. It's advisable to keep your Wi-Fi turned on.

The home screen of the Omnipod 5 app on the Controller or mobile phone resembles the Omnipod DASH's home screen with some differences:

  • An additional mode indicator is displayed at the top right, showing either Automated, Manual, Limited or No Pod Communication.

  • In Automated or Auto Mode, the icon and letters are purple, while in Manual Mode, they are blue.

  • The CGM curve can be accessed at the bottom right, providing insulin on board information by pressing the question mark.

docs.title: Starting Omnipod 5 Automated Mode
docs.text:
To enable Automated Mode on the Omnipod 5 app, go to the settings and tap on Switch Mode. Note that Automated Mode can only be activated if the Pod and sensor are properly paired.

If Automated Mode is functioning properly, the mode indicator on the home screen will change to "Automated," and the dominant color on the app will be purple.

In landscape mode, a pink line will appear at the bottom of the screen indicating that the basal insulin is automated based on the sensor glucose. Note that you cannot directly view the exact basal insulin delivery. If you wish to see this information, navigate to Settings, History Detail then Auto Events.

docs.title: Using Activity Feature
docs.text:
For physical activities, you can utilize the Activity Feature:

  • Access the menu and select "Activity."

  • Set a duration between 1 and 24 hours, then press "Start."

  • The middle tab will turn light green and display "Activity." Tapping the tab will show the remaining duration and allow you to cancel the activity feature.

  • Once the set time limit is reached, the system will automatically turn the Activity Feature off.

docs.title: Adjusting parameters
docs.text:
The Omnipod 5 system allows for adjustments in the target value and several parameters for when you use the bolus calculator.

  • To modify the target value, you can navigate to the menu and select "Settings," then choose the "Bolus" option. Here you can adjust your target value per 60-minute time block.

  • The bolus calculator parameters such as carb-to-insulin ratio, correction factor, duration of insulin action and “correct above value” can also be found here. It's important to note that any modifications made to these parameters will only impact the insulin doses calculated when using the bolus calculator feature. The reverse correction option is also available, which recommends a reduction to the meal dose of insulin when the glucose is lower than the target value.

Additionally, when initiating the Omnipod 5 system, you will need to enter a basal rate (or basal rates), which will be used by the algorithm to determine the adaptive basal rate for the first pod. After 48 hours of use, and the first Pod change, the system will use the historical basal insulin delivery as a base for the automated basal insulin. If you are unsure of how to determine a starting basal rate, consider settings with approximately 50% of insulin needs going to the basal rate and the other 50% for bolus insulin.

docs.title: Giving an insulin bolus
docs.text:
When administering a meal bolus, follow these steps:

  • Press the bolus button.

  • Enter the number of grams of carbohydrates you will be consuming.

  • The bolus calculator in the Omnipod 5 app automatically retrieves the sensor glucose for calculation. However, to incorporate the sensor glucose trend into the calculation, you need to tap the "Use Sensor" button. If the Omnipod 5 app does not have recent sensor glucose available, the "Use Sensor" button will be disabled, and you will need to manually enter a glucose value.

  • To view the details behind the calculation of your bolus, you can press the calculations button.

  • Extended bolus delivery is not available in Automated Mode and can only be done in Manual Mode.

  • Finally, you can administer the bolus. Once the green bar appears, you can put away your Controller or mobile phone. If necessary, you can cancel the bolus during delivery.

docs.title: Suspending insulin
docs.text:
To temporarily stop insulin delivery, such as during exercise or other brief activities, you need to switch back to Manual Mode. Here you can choose to pause insulin or set a temporary basal rate of 0 units per hour or minus 100%.

To pause insulin delivery:

  1. Access the menu by pressing the menu button.

  2. Select "Pause Insulin" from the options.

  3. Specify the duration of the pause, which can be 0.5 hours, 1 hour, 1.5 hours, or 2 hours.

  4. Confirm the pause by selecting "Pause" and then confirming the action.

  5. A yellow banner will appear on the home screen indicating that insulin delivery is paused.

Note: During the pause period, the Pod will emit beeps every 15 minutes. At the end of the pause period, insulin delivery does not automatically resume! The Pod and Omnipod 5 App will notify you every minute for 3 minutes and repeat the notification every 15 minutes until you manually restart insulin delivery.

It is essential to tap the "Start Insulin" option to resume insulin delivery after a pause period in Manual Mode. Insulin delivery does not restart automatically, and failure to restart it could lead to hyperglycemia.

Alternatively, you can use a temporary basal rate of 0 units per hour or minus 100% for a specific duration, up to 12 hours. In this case, insulin delivery will automatically resume after the set time period. The app will emit an audible beep at the beginning and every hour to indicate the temporary basal rate. This approach is particularly useful when you need to disconnect for a longer duration, as it reduces the number of alarms and interruptions. However, it is never advisable to suspend all insulin delivery for more than two hours unless you plan to take insulin by injection, as ketoacidosis can develop.

Many individuals choose to turn off their Controller using the ON/OFF button when temporarily stopping insulin delivery, as it prevents alarms from being triggered since the Controller is no longer connected to the Pod.

docs.title: 4.3 Manual Mode
docs.text:
In certain situations, it may be necessary to switch to Manual Mode in the Omnipod 5 system. This can be done to adjust insulin delivery according to specific needs, such as during illness, menstrual cycles, periods of stress, extended physical activity, or to pause insulin delivery.

In Manual Mode, the menu of the Omnipod 5 app differs from that of Automated Mode. It provides options for setting the basal insulin rate, switching basal profiles, creating temporary basal rates, and pausing insulin. Extended boluses can also be administered in Manual Mode.

For a detailed understanding of Manual Mode operation, you can refer to the module on the Omnipod DASH system, as the functionality is similar.

In conclusion, the Omnipod 5 system empowers users to take control of their insulin management. With the flexibility to switch between Automated Mode and Manual Mode, users have the ability to adapt and make adjustments according to their individual needs.

Remember, mastering any new technology may take time and practice. If you encounter any challenges along the way, don't hesitate to consult the manual for detailed instructions or reach out to your diabetes team. The app itself is designed to be user-friendly, with intuitive features and a range of adjustable settings. Stay patient, explore the various options available, and soon you'll be harnessing the full potential of the Omnipod 5 System for personalized diabetes management.

docs.title: 5. Creating and interpreting reports (15 min)
docs.text:
In this video, we will explore the process of creating and interpreting reports using the Glooko software with Omnipod 5. By connecting your Omnipod 5 app to Glooko, you can seamlessly transmit data every 5 minutes, enabling real-time access to various insightful reports.

Throughout this video, we will cover the different types of reports available in Glooko and how to effectively interpret and analyze the data presented in these reports.

By mastering the creation and interpretation of reports, you will gain valuable insights into your diabetes management, allowing for informed decisions and improved overall control. Let's dive in and discover the power of Omnipod 5 reports with Glooko!

docs.title: 5.1 Types of reports
docs.text:
In Glooko, you can find the key reports under the tabs Summary, Graphs, Insights and Devices

The summary tab is the first tab within any patient profile on Glooko. Here you have the patient's name, date of birth and their type of diabetes. You can also create pdf reports.

Below that we have the time selector. Here, using the drop down, you can select between a variety of time periods, from one day to 90 days, and custom time ranges. You can select between blood glucose data and CGM data using the selector on the right hand side. Using the arrows below you can navigate between different time periods in the increments selected, in the time drop down box above.

As we continue to scroll, you can see that we have the glucose data divided into time in range, time below range and time above range.

We can also see other metrics such as the glucose management indicator, the averages, as well as the highest and lowest blood glucose readings within this time period. As we continue to scroll, you'll see that we are presented with an AGP graph.

Here you can see the dark line indicating the median and the various shaded areas showing the statistical distribution.

As we look towards the right hand side of the summary tab you can see insulin data. Here we have the average total daily basal dose and bolus dose and also this percentage split between each. We can see the total daily dose on average as well as the percentage of overrides that this patient is doing using in this case their insulin pump. Overrides will not always be available as will some other metrics metrics will be displayed based on the availability of the data in the devices that that patient is using.

For example, for users of Omnipod 5, the Glooko summary report will also show these System Details, indicating the amount of time in Automated Mode, Limited Mode, Activity Feature and Manual Mode. It is important to note that Limited Mode may occur when there is a lack of sensor data for more than 20 minutes, such as during sensor warm-up or during prolonged periods of maximum and/or minimum insulin delivery. Understanding these modes and their durations will help in assessing the performance and functionality of the Omnipod 5 system.

As we continue to scroll you can find dietary information for average carbs per day and the number of entries and below that we finally have the history section here we have all the events listed for the time period selected including food, blood glucose results, and any insulin use.

The overview tab within the graph section of a patient's profile is particularly flexible and allows for deep insights into the data that has been uploaded. First we can select the time range that we would like to review from the drop down including custom time ranges. After doing this we can review the graph below where we see glucose results color coded for those that are above within and below the target range including a black trend line. Below that we have carbohydrates which are represented as proportionally sized bars on the chart representing the amount of carbohydrates that are recorded for each day. Likewise we have proportionally sized bars for insulin which indicate the amount of insulin that's been administered on each day.

The great thing about the charts within Glooko is that they can be interacted with. So when we highlight a particular day, we can see the median glucose for that day. We can see the total carbs as well as the total units of insulin over the course of that day. If we want to see this information in a more detailed view, you can actually click on that specific day. That will then bring up a much more detailed chart, which will show the sensor results. But this time they will be presented alongside the individual blood glucose values that were recorded. Beneath that we have the carbohydrate values that were recorded. And when we hover on them we have the exact time that they were recorded. We have bolus insulin which is presented with proportional bars and when we hover over these we get more information such as this one here.

Beneath that we have the basal ray where you see the dotted line represents a temporary basal the solid line represents a scheduled basal and we might get gaps in that line such as we see here on the right hand side which is signified by the red dots which show that the basal was suspended during this time.

For users of the Omnipod 5 system, you can also see the automated basal insulin delivery and the System details.

For patients that are using insulin pumps the Insights tab can be particularly useful as it allows clinicians and patients to see how the events and features that are unique to an insulin pump are impacting the diabetes management. Here you can see that we start on the set and site change tab. Here we can see how frequently a patient is changing the set or site their pump is located. You can see for instance that this patient has changed their pump set after two days, they have changed it five times after three days, and they have changed it three times after four days. This allows the clinician and patient to see if the patient is sticking within the recommended guidelines and perhaps here where the patient changed it after two days there was an issue with the pump that they would then want to discuss with their clinician further.

As we scroll down you can see the date and time of each one of these events and the time since the last set or site change. You'll see on the right hand side we have each of the events lined up here down the black line. We can see glucose control for 12 hours prior to the event and for six hours after the event. This allows us to see if how the event has actually been handled has impacted glucose management. For instance inserting a cannula into a hard fatty lump. This may impact how insulin is absorbed by the body and might be something that a clinician would want to explore further with a patient and offer an education opportunity.

The device tab within a patient's profile shows all of the devices that are contributing data to the report seen within the profile. On the left hand side, you can find all the devices listed, along with the serial number and the time the device was last synced with Glooko. If we select a device such as an insulin pump you'll see we have device specific information including the basal profiles. These are represented in a text format and graphically, showing the units per hour the insulin will be delivered and how this varies over a 24 hour period. As we continue to scroll you'll find other information such as the carb ratios, insulin sensitivity and the target blood glucose ranges, which the device uses to provide guidance for both correction and bolus dosis.

As we scroll back to the top of the screen we can access device settings. Here we have general information such as the active insulin time, the units that the device is reporting in, as well as parameters such as the maximum bolus and maximum basal rate. Please note that you cannot change settings from here, but they will be recorded. Which is really useful, because if you decide to change settings in order to benefit patient outcomes, but this does not have the desired effect, and you wish to revert to settings that have previously been used, you can use the drop down box on the right hand side, to view settings from previous uploads. You'll see on the left we have an icon of a clock and again that is shown at the top of the screen. That indicates that the time and date on this device do not align with the time and date of the glucose server. In this case you can see by the text on the right hand side that this device deviates by three minutes. It's important that all the devices that a patient is using are synchronized in order to ensure that the reports are of the highest quality and you can gain the best clinical insights from them for assisting a patient and informing clinical decision making.

When generating a PDF report in Glooko, you will also have access to the Weekly Overview alongside other reports.

This report allows you to compare multiple daily reports in detail, providing a faster and more efficient way to analyze the data compared to viewing them individually through the charts tab.

docs.title: 5.2 Interpreting reports
docs.text:
With Omnipod 5, the target value, correction factor, duration of insulin action and carbohydrate-to-insulin ratio are the key parameters that can be adjusted to personalize your insulin therapy. The target value represents the desired glucose level, and it can be set for each time block to accommodate different insulin needs throughout the day. Similarly, the carbohydrate-to-insulin ratio determines the amount of insulin required for a certain amount of carbohydrates consumed. Both of these parameters can be easily adjusted within the bolus settings, allowing you to fine-tune your insulin delivery based on your individual needs and preferences.

When analyzing the reports, it is beneficial to follow a structured approach, as emphasized in the general module on automated insulin delivery systems. Here is a roadmap to guide your interpretation:

1. Assess Glycemic Information: Begin by evaluating the Time in Range and Time Below Range over the past 2-4 weeks on the Summary Tab.

  • Ensure that treatment goals are being met, with Time in Range above 70% and Time Below Range below 4%.

  • It is important to pay attention to the frequency of meal bolusing, which is also indicated in the Summary Tab of Glooko. On average, there should be around 3 boluses per day for meals, with a few additional boluses for snacks. If you notice an unusually high number of boluses, such as 20 boluses per day, it may indicate that the user doesn't trust the system and is constantly interfering by delivering manual correction boluses. On the other hand, fewer than 3 boluses per day may indicate that the user is neglecting to bolus for food and mistakenly believes that the algorithm will “cover” their meals and snacks.

2. Optimize AID Settings: Analyze the AGP (Ambulatory Glucose Profile) on the Summary Tab to identify trends of hypo- or hyperglycemia, and check if these trends are related to boluses on the Weekly Overview reports.

  • If you notice trends after meal boluses, it's important to assess various factors such as bolus timing and accurate carbohydrate counting, before adjusting the carb ratio.

  • If you see a trend after manual or autocorrection boluses, you can try to adjust the correction factor, the duration of insulin action or the “Correct Above” threshold.

  • For trends outside of meal boluses, consider adjusting the target value. Keep in mind that you have the flexibility to adjust the target value based on different time blocks. If glucose levels are modestly elevated through the night, consider lowering the target value further during overnight periods to optimize your Time in Range (TIR) and improve overall glycemic control.

  • Additionally, explore possible correlations with factors such as exercise, alcohol consumption, or hypoglycemia.

3. Guide Behavioral Recommendations: To ensure the proper usage of the automated insulin delivery system, it is important to review various aspects in the reports available. Here are some key considerations:

  • Check the sensor wear duration and time spent in Automated Mode in the Summary Tab

  • The time in Automated Mode should be >90%, and the time in Limited Mode should be low. If the time in Limited Mode is high, this could be caused by a poor connection between the transmitter and Pod. You can check to ensure that the sensor and Pod are worn on the same side of the body, in sight of each other, to have fewer connection problems.

  • Another cause of excessive Limited Mode is a frequent "Automated Delivery Restriction" alarm. Assure the user that this is more common during the first few weeks of system use, and that it is important to respond to it by confirming the accuracy of the CGM and then turning Automated mode back on.

  • Also in the Summary Tab: Evaluate if bolus calculator overrides are low (ideally 0%). Also, check the amount of carbs entered for meals.

  • Weekly Overview Report: Assess the adherence to pre-meal bolusing, overcorrection of hypo- and hyperglycemia, and the appropriate use of the Activity Feature.

  • On the Insights tab, you can check if the Pod replacement is being performed as recommended (which is usually every 3 days)

  • Ask the patient if the sensor alert settings align with the user's needs and preferences. These can be adjusted in the Dexcom G6 app.

4. Review Pump Settings and Emergency Plans:

  • Devices Tab: Verify that the preset basal insulin rate on your Omnipod Pod aligns approximately with the amount of basal insulin being delivered in Automated Mode. This can be accomplished by dividing the average daily basal delivery by 24.

  • Keep a detailed record of all pump settings and create an emergency plan that includes instructions for using long-acting and short-acting insulin pens in case of a pump malfunction. Ensure easy access to insulin pens for such situations.

Finally, it is worth mentioning the valuable tools provided by the Barbara Davis Center in Colorado. Their website, Panther Diabetes, offers free downloadable resources, including two roadmaps specifically designed for monitoring individuals on Omnipod 5. These resources, available for download at the bottom of the video, offer guidance and support in optimizing diabetes management. It is essential to emphasize the significance of education in conjunction with these tools to ensure a comprehensive approach to diabetes care. By leveraging these resources and staying committed to continuous education, individuals can maximize the benefits of Omnipod 5 and enhance their overall diabetes management.

docs.title: 6. Managing special situations (8 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video on managing special situations with the Omnipod 5 automated insulin delivery system.

In this video, we will provide specific tips on managing hypo- and hyperglycemia, dealing with high-fat meals, participating in sports, managing illness, alcohol and navigating travel while using the Omnipod 5 system.

These guidelines complement the general recommendations for automated insulin delivery systems. For a comprehensive understanding, refer to the general module on automated insulin delivery systems.

docs.title: 6.1 Hypoglycemia
docs.text:
With the Omnipod 5 system, managing hypoglycemia becomes more efficient. You may require fewer carbohydrates to raise your blood sugar levels compared to when not using an automated insulin delivery system, especially if you have minimal insulin on board.

You can check the insulin on board displayed on the home screen of the Omnipod 5 app. In landscape mode, you can also see your recent insulin history, which can be helpful in assessing your insulin levels over the past few hours.

It's important to note that if you consume a small amount of carbohydrates for treating hypoglycemia, typically less than 10 grams, you don't need to enter this amount into the bolus calculator.

When planning physical activity, consider using the Activity Feature or temporarily setting a higher Target Value to prevent hypoglycemia. These adjustments help maintain stable blood sugar levels and reduce the risk of recurrent hypoglycemia. Also, consider using activity mode for several hours after particularly intense exercise in order to prevent delayed bouts of hypoglycemia.

docs.title: 6.2 Hyperglycemia
docs.text:
When it comes to mild hyperglycemia, the idea is to trust Omnipod 5 and let it work itself. However, you can bring down hyperglycemia faster if you administer a manual correction bolus via the bolus calculator. You can leave the carbs at 0 grams. Don’t forget to press the Use CGM button to also take the glucose trend into account.

When it comes to prolonged severe hyperglycemia, you should always think of an infusion set problem. When in doubt, it is best to change your pod and place it in a new site.. The motto is "If in doubt, change it out".

Make sure to carry enough equipment to do an emergency pod change.

docs.title: 6.3 High-fat meals
docs.text:
Here are some recommendations for managing a high-fat meal with Omnipod 5:

  1. It is generally advised to enter only 70% of the total carbohydrates consumed for a high-fat meal. The algorithm in the Omnipod 5 system will adjust the basal insulin delivery to handle the remaining carbohydrates and prevent post-meal hypoglycemia.

  2. Another strategy is to split the bolus dose by entering 50% of the calculated carbohydrates before the meal and the remaining 50% approximately 2 hours after the meal. This allows for a more precise insulin response to the slower digestion of high-fat meals.

  3. While Automated Mode in the Omnipod 5 does not allow for extended boluses, you have the option to switch to Manual Mode if you prefer to use an extended bolus feature. In Manual Mode, you can set an extended bolus to match the digestion rate of the high-fat meal.

By employing these strategies, you can effectively manage glucose levels when consuming high-fat meals with the Omnipod 5 system. It is always recommended to consult with your healthcare provider for personalized guidance and adjustments based on your specific needs.

6.4 Exercise

When exercising with Omnipod 5, it is recommended to turn on the Activity Feature 1-2 hours prior to the onset of activity. If exercise takes place after a meal, consider reducing the suggested meal bolus as recommended in the general module on automated insulin delivery systems.

If you’re using a Dexcom sensor, you can try to connect your Dexcom G6 or G7 app to your smartwatch or cycling computer for easier monitoring during exercise.

If you ingest small amounts of carbohydrates while exercising, you don't usually need to enter them in the Omnipod 5 app.

docs.title: 6.5 Illness
docs.text:
During illness you may notice more prolonged hyperglycemia. Consider additional manual correction boluses.

Remember that the actual total insulin dose will only be taken into account when you change your pod. To better manage blood sugar levels during illness, you may consider switching to Manual Mode and adjusting the temporary basal and/or basal insulin rate. You may also choose to set up a basal insulin profile specifically for use when you are sick.

Follow hyperglycemia guidelines for managing high blood sugar levels during illness.

docs.title: 6.6 Alcohol
docs.text:
When consuming alcohol, it is recommended to take proactive measures to prevent hypoglycemia. This can be done by temporarily increasing your target value and/or utilizing the Activity Feature to reduce insulin delivery.

It is important to note that increasing carbohydrate intake will not be effective in preventing hypoglycemia, as the system will compensate by increasing insulin administration to balance any rise in glucose levels.

docs.title: 6.7 Travel
docs.text:
When traveling with Omnipod 5, it is important to pack an ample supply of necessary equipment and accessories. Remember to bring your Controller charger and, if needed, an adapter for the country you are visiting. While a spare Controller is not available, you can consider packing an extra Dexcom G6 transmitter if you’re using this sensor type.

If you are traveling by air, you can keep the Bluetooth functionality of your handset, pump, and transmitter active. However, the SIM card's Wi-Fi and 4G capabilities will not be operational during the flight. This should not affect the functioning of the Omnipod 5 app on your Controller or mobile device, as it will continue to work seamlessly via Bluetooth. However, data transmission to Glooko will be temporarily unavailable.

When crossing different time zones, the Omnipod 5 app should automatically adjust the time. However, it is always a good idea to double-check the time settings upon arrival at your destination.

During take-off and landing, some airlines may require you to put your Controller or phone in airplane mode. You can do this via the settings. Putting your Omnipod 5 app in airplane mode will not affect your automated basal insulin delivery.

It is important to note that the Omnipod 5 Controller is not waterproof. If you plan to go swimming, you can leave it in the changing room and temporarily turn it off using the ON/OFF button to avoid triggering unnecessary alarms due to the disconnection from the Pod. Or you can use a waterproof case, which allows you screen access even if you have wet hands.

In this chapter we explored valuable tips for managing various situations with the Omnipod 5 system, including hypo- and hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, sports, illness, and travel. These tips provide practical guidance for optimizing insulin delivery and glucose control in different scenarios. The Omnipod 5 system simplifies the management of these situations, making it one of the easiest automated insulin delivery systems on the market. However, it is beneficial to stay connected with peers and colleagues who also use the Omnipod 5 system. By sharing experiences and insights, we can learn from each other and enhance our understanding and proficiency with this innovative system. Together, we can navigate the intricacies of diabetes management and achieve better outcomes with the Omnipod 5.

7. Case report

In this chapter, we will delve into a real-life example that showcases the Omnipod 5 insulin pump in action.

We invite you to click on the link below this video to watch Meg’s personal experience and journey with the Omnipod 5 system. Through her story, you will gain valuable insights into how to successfully adjust and utilize the Omnipod 5 to improve glucose management. Please learn from Meg's experience and witness the positive impact of the Omnipod 5 on her diabetes journey.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QvrGpvQY0_k – 3 min

Congratulations to everyone who has completed the module on the Omnipod 5 system! Each of you has taken an important step towards enhancing your diabetes management. By embracing this innovative technology, you have gained greater control over your health and well-being. This achievement is significant because it represents your commitment to living a fulfilling life despite the challenges of diabetes.

Remember, it is crucial to stay connected with peers and colleagues who also use the Omnipod 5 system. By sharing experiences and insights, we can learn from one another and discover new strategies for optimizing our diabetes management. Together, we can continue to support and inspire each other on this journey towards improved health and happiness.

To further solidify your understanding, we suggest trying out the Omnipod 5 Simulator App. This app allows you to explore the system’s features and practice using the interface in a virtual environment. It’s a great way to reinforce the knowledge you’ve gained and build confidence in using the Omnipod 5 system.

Keep up the great work and embrace the power of the Omnipod 5 system as you navigate the complexities of diabetes management. You have the tools and knowledge to make a positive impact on your life, and by sharing your success stories, you can inspire others to embark on their own transformative journeys with the Omnipod 5.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Tandem Control-IQ technology according to the CARES Paradigm
  • 3. Linking components together
  • 4. How the Tandem Control-IQ technology works
  • 5. Creating and interpreting reports
  • 6. Managing special situations
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on Tandem Control-IQ technology, one of the most widely used automated insulin delivery systems, particularly in the US. In this comprehensive guide, we will delve into the intricacies of Tandem Control-IQ technology, providing you with a step-by-step understanding of its functionality and how to effectively manage your diabetes with this system.

Tandem Control-IQ technology seamlessly integrates three key components:

  • a Dexcom or FreeStyle Libre sensor

  • the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump,

  • and the integrated Control-IQ technology.

The pump and sensor data can be viewed on the Tandem t:slim Mobile app, and in selected regions like the US, you can even administer an insulin bolus via the app. Reports can be accessed via Glooko or Tandem Source, Tandem’s dedicated readout software. If you’re using a Dexcom sensor, the sensor data can be sent to the Dexcom Clarity and Share platform, allowing a family member or caregiver to monitor your sensor data remotely.

In our general module on automated insulin delivery systems, you have already gained valuable knowledge on comparing automated insulin delivery systems using the CARES reference framework, selecting the right system for your needs, interpreting reports, and managing specific situations with automated insulin delivery systems in general.

In the upcoming videos, we will provide detailed insights specifically tailored to the Tandem Control-IQ technology system, ensuring you receive a comprehensive and structured education on getting started. It's essential to remember that these videos do not provide medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

The videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the general St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr. Liesbeth Van Huffel, an endocrinologist at the AZORG Hospital in Aalst in Belgium, along with Rosaline Jongeneelen and Ilse Faber, both Tandem Control-IQ system users residing in the Netherlands. Here you can see their disclosures. Their expertise and personal experiences ensure that the information presented in the videos is comprehensive and unbiased.

By the end of this video series, you will have a comprehensive understanding of:

  • How Tandem Control-IQ technology performs within the CARES paradigm,

  • Practical implementation on the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump,

  • Creating and interpreting reports using Glooko,

  • And managing specific situations such as hypo and hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, exercise, illness, alcohol and travel.

Whether you are a caregiver seeking to better support individuals using Tandem Control-IQ technology or a user of the system looking for a successful start, these videos will equip you with the knowledge, tips, and tricks to enhance your experience. Remember, your diabetes journey is unique, and Tandem Control-IQ technology offers valuable tools to navigate it effectively.

Congratulations on taking the first step towards a better understanding of Tandem Control-IQ technology. We wish you the best of luck as you embark on this educational journey, empowering you to live a fulfilling life with improved diabetes management.

docs.title: 2. Tandem Control-IQ technology according to the CARES Paradigm (12.5 min)
2. Tandem Control-IQ technology according to the CARES Paradigm (12.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson about the Tandem Control-IQ technology according to the CARES paradigm.

Tandem Control-IQ technology offers a range of features that make it an effective automated insulin delivery system for diabetes management. Let's explore some key points to further understand this system within the CARES paradigm, its indications and its software updates.

docs.title: 2.1 Tandem Control-IQ according to the CARES paradigm
#1 Calculate
docs.text:
Tandem Control-IQ technology modulates preset basal insulin rates based on the predicted glucose levels over the next 30 minutes.

Additionally, hourly autocorrection boluses are administered at 60% of the standard dose when glucose levels exceed 180 mg/dL or 10 mmol/L.

The target range, or treatment values as they are called by Tandem, are set between 112.5-160 mg/dL (6.25 and 8.8 mmol/L), with the goal of achieving optimal glycemic control during the day.

During sleep mode, the treatment values are further refined to 112.5-120 mg/dL (6.3-6.7 mmol/L), promoting stable glucose levels without autocorrected boluses.

docs.title: #2 Adjust
docs.text:
Users can modify the following settings:

  • the basal insulin rate (different profiles are possible),

  • the carbohydrate ratio,

  • and the correction factor or insulin sensitivity.

With the updated Control-IQ+ software, users can even set temporary basal rates from 0% to 250% for up to 72 hours while continuing to benefit from automatic insulin adjustments and correction boluses from the Control-IQ+ algorithm.

In addition to these adjustments, Tandem Control-IQ technology offers different glucose targets or treatment values for sleep and exercise.

  • Sleep Activity: This setting aims for narrower treatment values of 112.5-120 mg/dL (6.25-6.7 mmol/L) during nighttime to maintain stable glucose levels without using autocorrected boluses.

  • Exercise Activity: This setting is designed for periods of increased physical activity. It sets higher treatment values of 140-160 mg/dL (7.8-8.9 mmol/L) to accommodate the metabolic demands of exercise. Additionally, during exercise activity, the pump does use autocorrected boluses to help manage glucose levels effectively.

During the initial setup of Control-IQ technology, users will input their total daily insulin dose and weight. These values can be modified later to ensure accurate insulin delivery based on individual requirements.

While Tandem Control-IQ technology provides flexibility in adjusting various parameters, it's important to note that certain aspects remain fixed. The default treatment values of 112.5-160 mg/dL (6.25-8.9 mmol/L), the duration of insulin action set at 5 hours, and the target value for correction boluses fixed at 110 mg/dL (or 6.1 mmol/L).

docs.title: #3 Revert
docs.text:
In the event that no sensor data is available for more than 20 minutes, the Tandem Control-IQ technology “auto mode” will transition to “manual mode”. This can occur due to issues such as a loss of connection with the transmitter or during sensor warm-up.

If there are connectivity issues between the pump and CGM, recommend wearing the pump on the same side as the CGM and positioning the pump with the screen facing outwards. Keeping the software up to date can also help improve connectivity.

If you’re using the Dexcom G6 sensor and persistent connectivity problems arise, consider replacing the Dexcom transmitter. A functional transmitter battery is crucial for maintaining a stable connection.

While in manual mode:

  • The suspend-before-low function is not available, as it is specific to the Basal-IQ algorithm and not compatible with Control-IQ software.

  • If Control-IQ technology spontaneously reverts to manual mode, the basal insulin rate of your personal profile will be limited to 3 units per hour. To increase the insulin dose to more than 3 units per hour in manual mode, you will need to deactivate the Control-IQ technology. This 3 units/hour limit does not apply to the updated Control-IQ+, which was specifically designed for users who require higher basal insulin rates.

Once sensor values become available again, the pump will automatically switch back to Control-IQ technology, ensuring the continuous operation of the automated insulin delivery system.

docs.title: #4 Educate
docs.text:
