If you consume less than 10 grams of carbohydrates to treat or prevent a hypo, there is no need to enter this in the pump.

To prevent hypoglycemia for example during exercise, you can use the Exercise Activity from 1-2 hours before exercise, until after the exercise.

docs.title: 6.2 Hyperglycemia
docs.text:
The Tandem Mobi System is not a learning algorithm, so manual autocorrection boluses can be given in between. However, it is important to follow the bolus advice.

In cases of prolonged severe hyperglycemia, it is important to consider infusion problems.

To avoid infusion set problems, it is recommended to replace the infusion set in a timely manner and set a reminder for this on your pump. Remember to rotate your infusion sites and avoid lipodystrophy.

docs.title: 6.3 High-fat meals
docs.text:
The Tandem Mobi System allows you to use an extended bolus. For high-fat meals, it is generally recommended to deliver 50% of the bolus immediately and the remaining 50% over the next 2 hours.

This is the default setting for an extended bolus, but further individual adjustments may be needed.

docs.title: 6.4 Exercise
docs.text:
When exercising with the Tandem Mobi System, you can activate the Exercise Activity 1-2 hours prior to the activity and manually reduce the suggested meal and correction boluses as discussed in the general module on automated insulin delivery systems.

It's important to note that autocorrection boluses are still given during Activity Mode.

You can also use a separate personal profile for sports or a dedicated temporary basal during that time. This provides more options to adjust the insulin release according to your personal needs.

docs.title: 6.5 Illness
docs.text:
During illness, hyperglycemia is common. Follow the guidelines for managing hyperglycemia in such situations.

It’s recommended to preset a personal profile dedicated to illness with a higher % of basal insulin rate, and more aggressive carb ratio and correction factor.

You also have the option to set a dedicated temporary basal rate during illness, providing additional flexibility to tailor insulin delivery to your individual needs.

docs.title: 6.6 Alcohol
docs.text: Refer to the specific guidelines of the general module on automated insulin delivery systems for managing your automated insulin delivery in relation to alcohol consumption.

docs.title: 6.7 Travel
docs.text:
When traveling with the Tandem Mobi Insulin pump, you have the option to request an additional insulin "loaner pump" to accompany you during your holiday. Moreover, in case of an emergency or if your pump requires replacement, Tandem can send you a replacement insulin pump promptly.

When traveling, be sure to also bring enough sensor supplies and charging equipment to keep your pump powered during the journey. Also make sure you have access to injectable basal and bolus insulin in case of pump failure.

When traveling across time zones, you will need to manually adjust the time on your Tandem Mobi App.

By following these tips, you can effectively manage various situations while using Tandem Mobi System and maintain optimal control over your diabetes. Remember to consult with your healthcare provider for personalized guidance and support in managing specific scenarios.

docs.title: 7. Case report
docs.text:
Welcome to this final video featuring the Tandem Mobi System!

First, Congratulations on completing this module on the Tandem Mobi System! By now, you have gained valuable insights into the features, functionalities, and best practices of this innovative advanced automated insulin delivery system.

To truly grasp the impact and effectiveness of the Tandem Mobi System, we highly recommend watching an insightful YouTube interview by Justin from Diabetech.

This video has discussions with Lissy Poyner, a Diabetes Health Coach living with type 1 diabetes who used the Tandem Mobi System for 3 months. It's a long video but worth it! To access this review, simply click on the link below the video.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nYrLFBAMvbk&t=12s 40 mins

We would like to express our appreciation for your commitment to learning about the Tandem Mobi System. By equipping yourself with knowledge and understanding, you are taking an active role in managing your diabetes effectively. Tandem Mobi has the potential to significantly enhance your quality of life, providing you with greater control and peace of mind in your day-to-day journey.

Remember, always consult with your healthcare provider for personalized guidance and support on using Tandem Mobi System. By staying informed, connecting with peers, and utilizing the resources available, you can confidently navigate the intricacies of diabetes management and make the most of the advanced features offered by Tandem Mobi System.

Continue to embrace the possibilities that Tandem Mobi offers and empower yourself to lead a fulfilling life while effectively managing your diabetes. Together, we can make a positive difference in the lives of those living with diabetes.

You did great! Best wishes for your continued success on your diabetes journey!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. TouchCare Nano according to the CARES paradigm
  • 3. Linking components together
  • 4. How the TouchCare Nano System works
  • 5. Creating and Interpreting Reports
  • 6. Managing special situations
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Hi there, and welcome! In this Module, we’re going to introduce you to the Medtrum TouchCare Nano automated insulin delivery system. — a super convenient, tubeless insulin pump combined with a continuous glucose monitor. The TouchCare Nano System is designed to help people with diabetes manage their blood sugar easily, without the hassle of tubes or complicated set-ups.

The Medtrum TouchCare Nano system has three simple parts working together:

  • The TouchCare Nano Patch Pump,

  • The TouchCare Nano CGM,

  • And the APGO algorithm on your pump and transmitter that can be controlled via your phone or on your PDM.

The system allows you to manage your insulin delivery and monitor glucose levels with ease and flexibility. Through the EasyPatch app, EasyTouch app and EasyView web portal, you can access detailed reports, analyze trends, and adjust your diabetes management in collaboration with your healthcare team. Family and caregivers can follow the glucose and insulin data through the EasyFollow app.

In previous general videos on automated insulin delivery systems, you learned valuable information, including how to compare automated insulin delivery systems using the CARES reference framework, how to choose the right system for your needs, how to interpret reports, and how to handle various scenarios such as hypo and hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, exercise, illness, alcohol, swimming, and traveling.

Now, we’re going to walk you through how the Medtrum TouchCare Nano system works, step by step. We’ll show you how to set it up, use it, and make the most of its features. Our goal is to make sure you feel confident and comfortable using this technology in your daily life.

It is important to note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your diabetes therapy.

The content of these videos has been developed by Dr. Inge van Boxelaer, founder of Diabetotech and an experienced endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The material has been reviewed by Dr. Sarah Siegelaar, an endocrinologist at Amsterdam UMC in the Netherlands, as well as Davor Skeledzija from Croatia, who’s daughter with type 1 diabetes has used the TouchCare Nano system. These individuals have contributed their expertise and insights to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the information provided in these videos.

By the end of this video series, you will have a thorough understanding of the TouchCare Nano system, including its performance, daily practical usage, report interpretation, and strategies for managing specific scenarios.

Whether you are a healthcare professional seeking to expand your expertise or an individual considering or already using the Medtrum TouchCare Nano system, these videos will offer valuable knowledge, insights, and practical tips to help you optimize your diabetes management.

We wish you the best of success on your journey with the Medtrum TouchCare Nano system!

docs.title: 2. TouchCare Nano according to the CARES paradigm (8.5 min)
2. TouchCare Nano according to the CARES paradigm (8.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on the TouchCare Nano System, structured according to the CARES paradigm.

In this session, we'll delve into the system's insulin delivery calculations, adjustment capabilities, procedures for reverting to manual mode, user education essentials, sensor features, data sharing functionalities, indications for use, and software updates. Let's begin!​

docs.title: 2.1 TouchCare Nano according to the CARES paradigm
2. TouchCare Nano according to the CARES paradigm (8.5 min)
#1 Calculate
docs.text:
The TouchCare Nano system has an AI-enabled algorithm that adapts insulin delivery every two minutes. It combines two advanced control strategies—hybrid proportional-integral control and model predictive control. This allows the system not only to respond to current glucose levels but also to anticipate future trends and adjust proactively.

A key strength of the algorithm is its self-learning capability. It continuously analyzes glucose, meal, and insulin data from the past two to six days and incorporates broader patterns from up to 90 days — it learns on a rolling basis. This enables highly personalized insulin delivery that evolves over time based on the user’s individual needs.

Users can select a target glucose level of 100, 110, or 120 mg/dL or 5.6, 6.1, or 6.7 mmol/l.

For meal management, the system offers two options. The user can manually input the grams of carbohydrates using the bolus calculator, or simply announce the type of meal—such as breakfast, lunch, dinner, or a snack—via the Auto Meal Handling feature. The algorithm then calculates the appropriate insulin response.

docs.title: #2 Adjust
docs.text:
The TouchCare Nano system allows for a range of personalized settings, giving users and care teams flexibility to fine-tune therapy.

  • The target sensor glucose can be set to 100, 110, or 120 mg/dL—or 5.6, 6.1, or 6.7 mmol/L—depending on individual goals.

  • Carbohydrate ratio and insulin sensitivity settings can be adjusted within the bolus calculator. However, in Auto Mode, it is primarily the carbohydrate ratio that influences insulin delivery, while changes to insulin sensitivity have minimal effect. Users can also modify traditional settings like basal rates, and blood glucose targets, and insulin-on-board time within the bolus calculator. However, these settings do not affect insulin delivery in Auto Mode.

Only the initial basal rates serve as the starting point for Auto Basal when Auto Mode is first activated—any manual changes made afterward do not influence how Auto Basal adjusts insulin delivery.

Similarly, within the Auto Mode settings, users can enter their weight and total daily insulin dose, but these values also do not influence insulin delivery once Auto Mode is active.

For physical activity, the TouchCare Nano system includes an Exercise Activity feature. When this is activated, the system will automatically reduce insulin delivery to help lower the risk of hypoglycemia during exercise. Unlike some other closed-loop systems, the Exercise Activity in TouchCare Nano doesn’t use a specific target glucose level. Instead, it simply adjusts insulin delivery downward for the duration you’ve set, based on your activity needs.

docs.title: #3 Revert
docs.text:
In certain situations, the TouchCare Nano system may temporarily switch to manual mode.

  • This can occur if sensor glucose data is not received for three hours, or if maximum or minimum insulin delivery limits are reached and sustained.

  • When this happens, the system automatically reverts to the user’s preset basal rates, allowing the pump to operate in manual mode.

  • Once sensor communication is restored, the user must manually re-enter Auto Mode.

If the connection between the insulin pump and the PDM or phone is lost, Auto Mode will still continue as long as the CGM maintains a stable connection with the Patch Pump, since the transmitter can send sensor glucose data directly to the pump base.

docs.title: #4 Educate
docs.text:
User education is key to effective use of the TouchCare Nano System.

The general education points covered in previous modules include:

  • Using fewer carbohydrates to correct hypoglycemia.

  • Considering infusion set problems if blood glucose levels remain high without an apparent reason.

  • Trusting the automated insulin delivery system and avoiding unnecessary manual interventions.

  • Timing meal boluses 15 minutes before meals.

  • Utilizing Exercise Mode 1 to 2 hours before activity.

  • Responding to alarms and being mindful of automatic updates on mobile phones.

  • And seeking peer support for guidance and sharing experiences.

Alongside general diabetes education, it's important to approach the TouchCare Nano system with a bit of extra mindfulness. Since it's a newer technology, we don’t yet have extensive data on its long-term safety and effectiveness.

That’s why it’s a good idea to occasionally perform an additional blood glucose finger prick—such as once a day in a fasting state when using Auto Mode, and anytime the user doesn’t feel well or their symptoms don’t match the sensor readings. This simple step can help ensure ongoing safety and confidence in the system.

docs.title: #5 Sensor
docs.text:
If there are doubts about the accuracy of sensor readings—especially if they don’t match how the user is feeling—it’s important to perform a confirmatory finger prick test. If there is a significant discrepancy, such as a difference greater than 20% between the sensor and finger prick values, even when glucose is stable and within the target range, calibration of the sensor may be necessary. If the difference persists, it is best to exit Auto Mode and consider replacing the sensor.

With the TouchCare Nano system, the CGM can be calibrated using a finger prick value. However, calibration should only be done when sensor readings are stable and within range, to avoid introducing further inaccuracy.

docs.title: #6 Share
docs.text:
The TouchCare Nano system makes it simple to share glucose and insulin data with healthcare providers and family members, enabling real-time monitoring for added safety and support.

Users of the EasyPatch app benefit from automatic cloud syncing, while those using the PDM will need to download the EasyTouch app and connect it to their device to enable real-time data uploads.

For family members and caregivers, the EasyFollow app provides remote access to both glucose and pump data, helping them stay informed and respond if necessary.

Healthcare professionals can access and review data via the EasyView Pro web platform, supporting more informed clinical decisions and personalized care.

docs.title: 2.2 Indications
docs.text:
The TouchCare Nano System is indicated for individuals aged 2 years and older who require insulin therapy.

It is approved for use with fast-acting insulins—Humalog, NovoRapid, Admelog, Apidra, Fiasp and Lyumjev—in their 100 units per milliliter concentration.

Please note that the TouchCare Nano system does not have an FDA label at this time, and it is not approved for use in pregnancy and during dialysis.

It’s important to note that advanced features like Auto Mode and Auto Meal Handling can only be activated following a consultation with the healthcare provider. Once approved, a company representative can unlock these features within the app or PDM.

docs.title: 2.3 Software updates
docs.text:
Medtrum provides regular software updates to ensure the TouchCare Nano System devices remain current with the latest features and improvements. Updates can be applied via the EasyPatch or EasyTouch apps and do not interrupt the delivery of basal insulin during the process.

In conclusion, understanding how the TouchCare Nano System functions will help users confidently manage diabetes. With predictive features, adjustable settings, and strong support tools, this system is designed to simplify insulin delivery and glucose monitoring. For any questions or assistance, users are encouraged to contact Medtrum’s customer support or their healthcare provider.

docs.title: 3. Linking components together (6 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on how to establish connections between the different components of the TouchCare Nano System.

The TouchCare Nano System operates as follows:

  • Glucose levels are measured every 2 minutes by the TouchCare Nano continuous glucose monitor, or CGM. These readings are sent to either the TouchCare Nano Pump.

  • The APGO algorithm on the PDM or EasyPatch app calculates the insulin requirement based on the most recent data and communicates with the TouchCare Nano patch pump—either the 200U or 300U model—to deliver the appropriate amount of insulin.

  • Glucose data and insulin delivery can be monitored and adjusted via the PDM or the EasyPatch app on your Android or iPhone.

  • All system data can be shared in real-time with caregivers and family through the EasyFollow app, and healthcare providers can access it via the EasyView web

  • For users of the PDM, it’s important to use the EasyTouch app to collect and sync data to the cloud, ensuring access and sharing of treatment information.

In previous modules, you’ve learned how to pair the TouchCare Nano CGM with the PDM and the EasyPatch app, and how to link the patch pump to these platforms.

In this guide, we’ll focus on three additional connection steps:

  • Setting up an EasyView account

  • Uploading PDM data via the EasyTouch app

  • And enabling real-time data sharing via the EasyFollow app

Let’s get started.

docs.title: 3.1 Creating an EasyView account:
docs.text:
When setting up the TouchCare Nano System through the EasyPatch or EasyTouch app, you’ll be prompted to either create a Medtrum account or log in to an existing one. This same email and password can also be used to access your data via the EasyView platform.

To view your glucose trends, insulin delivery history, and device settings, simply visit https://easyview.medtrum.eu and log in with your Medtrum credentials.

Once logged in, a navigation bar will appear on the left side of the page. By selecting Settings, you can customize how reports and data are displayed.

If you're a healthcare provider, you can register for a professional account through EasyView Pro. After submitting your application, The Medtrum team will contact you and confirm that you are a healthcare professional administrator. After that you will receive your password in an email. You can change the password after signing in for the first time.

First, click on patients to go to the patient list page to send a request so that you can follow a patient. Click find patient. You will now need to enter the email address of the patient you wish to follow. Enter the email address and click search. The patient's details will appear on the next screen to the right of the patient's details. You will see a box which says send request. Click this box and the request to follow will be sent to the patient. Once the patient has accepted your follow request their name will appear in your patient list.

To view a patient's data you will need to click on their name. From here you will need to click the settings tab on the left of your browser page to select Data To View. If the patient is using the EasyPatch app, you will need to make sure Real-time Data is selected. If the patient is using the PDM, you will need to make sure Uploaded Data is selected.

docs.title: 3.2 Uploading your PDM data via the EasyTouch app
docs.text:
If you're using the Personal Diabetes Manager (PDM), you can upload your data to the EasyView platform using the EasyTouch app.

The EasyTouch app is available for most iPhones and Android devices. To check if your phone is compatible, visit medtrum.com/compatibility. You can find the app by searching “Medtrum” in the App Store or Google Play.

Once you open the app, it will prompt you to connect your PDM—either by scanning its QR code or manually entering the serial number.

To upload your data to the Medtrum cloud, open the main menu of the EasyTouch app, go to Settings, and select Upload Data. You’ll see four upload options: Last week, Last month, Last three months, and All data. Choose the desired time range and tap Upload Data. Once the Bluetooth connection between the PDM and the EasyTouch app is established, the data transfer will begin. When you see the “Upload Successful” message, your data will be available on the EasyView platform and on the Statistics page of the EasyTouch app.

docs.title: 3.3 Real-time sharing via EasyFollow app
docs.text:
The EasyFollow app allows caregivers and family members to monitor a user’s real-time pump and CGM data remotely—even when they’re far away.

To get started, caregivers simply download the EasyFollow app and create their own Medtrum account.

Within the app, they can tap Manage in the top right corner, then select Add Account. By entering the username associated with the user’s EasyView account, they send a follow request.

Once the request is approved, the caregiver will be able to view real-time glucose values, insulin delivery information, and overall system status—providing peace of mind and an extra layer of support.

Congratulations on completing this lesson! By now, you should have a solid understanding of how all the components of the TouchCare Nano System—CGM, PDM or EasyPatch app, patch pump, and the connected platforms EasyView, EasyTouch, and EasyFollow—work together to deliver intelligent, real-time diabetes management.

Rest assured, once you have the devices in hand, the setup process is quite intuitive and largely self-explanatory. And of course, you’ll always have customer service and your care team to support you along the way.

docs.title: 4. How the TouchCare Nano System works (3 min)
4. How the TouchCare Nano System works (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on the functionality of the TouchCare Nano System.

In this guide, we will explore how to use the TouchCare Nano System’s features in both auto mode and manual mode, both on the PDM as on the EasyPatch app.

docs.title: 4.1 Auto Mode
docs.text:
The TouchCare Nano System automatically adjusts insulin delivery in real time, based on continuous glucose readings from the CGM.

Please note: Auto Mode and Auto Meal Handling can only be activated following a discussion with your healthcare provider. Once approved, a company representative must enable these features within the system.

docs.title: Starting Auto Mode:
docs.text:
• Auto Mode will only work if your basal pattern and bolus calculator have already been set up. And keep in mind, it can’t be turned on if insulin delivery is currently paused.

  • To start Auto Mode from the PDM, go to the main menu, tap EasyLoop, then tap Auto Mode. Slide the switch to turn it on. You’ll be asked to enter your target sensor glucose, your weight, and your total daily insulin dose. Once Auto Mode is active, your Basal menu will change to an Activity menu, and a green shield icon will appear on your home screen. If the system is delivering 30% more or less insulin than your usual basal rate, you’ll see an arrow over the shield. If insulin delivery is suspended, a red line will appear across the icon. If you ever want to turn Auto Mode off again, you can do it from the same screen.

  • If you’re using the EasyPatch app, go to the main menu, tap EasyLoop, then Auto Mode. Switch it on, and again enter your target glucose, weight, and total insulin dose. You’ll see the same green shield icon once it’s active.

docs.title: Starting Auto Meal Handling:
docs.text:
• Auto Meal Handling is a feature that adjusts insulin automatically when you announce that you’re having a meal like breakfast, lunch, dinner, or a snack.

  • To turn it on from the PDM, go to the main menu, tap EasyLoop, then Auto Mode. In that screen, you’ll see an option to switch on Auto Meal Handling. You’ll need a password to activate it. Once it’s on, you can select which meal you’re having from the Activity Menu.

  • In the EasyPatch app, go to the main menu, tap EasyLoop, then Auto Mode. You can switch on Auto Meal Handling there too. When it’s active, the bolus icon at the bottom of your home screen will change into an activity icon. Just tap it to announce your meal.

docs.title: Starting Exercise Activity:
docs.text:
• You can also start an Exercise activity which will reduce your insulin delivery during exercise.

  • On the PDM, go to the main menu and tap Activity. If Auto Meal Handling is on, you’ll see options like breakfast, lunch, dinner, snack, and exercise. Tap Exercise and choose how long you want it to last—from 30 minutes up to 12 hours. Then slide to confirm.

  • On the EasyPatch app, open the main menu, tap Events, and then tap Exercise, or go to the Activity shortcut button directly from the home screen. Pick how long your activity will last and slide to announce it.

docs.title: Adjusting Parameters:
docs.text:
• The settings that can be adjusted during Auto Mode are the target sensor glucose, the carb ratio and the insulin sensitivity factor.

  • On the PDM, you can change your target glucose directly from the Auto Mode screen by going to EasyLoop in the main menu. To change your carb ratio, go to Settings, then Insulin Pump, then Bolus Setup, and finally Bolus Calculator Setup.

  • On the EasyPatch app, go to EasyLoop in the main menu to adjust your target glucose. To change your carb ratio, go to Settings, then Insulin Pump, then Bolus Calculator Setup.

docs.title: Bolus Delivery:
docs.text:
• When it’s time to give a bolus, you have two options. If you’re using carb counting, you can use the bolus calculator. On the PDM, just tap Bolus in the main menu and follow the steps. If you’re using Auto Meal Handling, go to the Activity menu and select your meal.

  • In the EasyPatch app, tap the bolus icon on the home screen to use the calculator. Or if Auto Meal Handling is on, go to the Activity shortcut button on the home screen and choose your meal type.

docs.title: Suspending Insulin:
docs.text:
• Sometimes you might want to pause insulin delivery. The system can also do this on its own if it predicts a low glucose.

  • On the PDM, tap Suspend from the main menu. You can also resume insulin from the same place.

  • In the EasyPatch app, go to Insulin Delivery from the main menu and tap Suspend. You’ll be able to set how long you want insulin paused, then slide to confirm. The circle around your glucose reading will turn red while insulin is suspended. To resume, go back to the same screen and tap Resume.

docs.title: 4.2 Manual Mode and Safety Features
docs.text:
When Auto Mode is turned off, the system works in Manual Mode. In this mode, you can set your own basal rates or use temporary basal rates, and deliver insulin boluses either manually or by using the bolus calculator.

For meals that are high in fat or eaten over a longer period of time, you can also use the extended or combination bolus options to better match insulin delivery with your food intake.

The system also includes a Predictive Low Glucose Suspend feature. If your glucose levels drop too low—or are predicted to go low within the next 30 minutes—the system will automatically suspend insulin delivery. This setting can be adjusted in the EasyLoop menu. Insulin will resume automatically when glucose levels stabilize, or after a maximum pause of two hours.

By understanding how both Manual Mode and Auto Mode work, along with the built-in safety features, you can get the most out of the TouchCare Nano System and feel more confident in your diabetes management. For detailed steps and settings, always refer to your device manual or consult with your healthcare provider.

docs.title: 5. Creating and Interpreting Reports (6 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on how to create and interpret reports using the EasyView platform for the TouchCare Nano System.

In earlier lessons, we showed you how to create an EasyView account and upload data from your device. Now, we’ll look at how to view your reports, understand what they show, and use them to support better diabetes management.

docs.title: 5.1 Creating Reports
docs.text:
To access your reports, log into EasyView Personal or, if you're a healthcare provider, open EasyView Pro and select the patient you’d like to review.

Then go to the Reports section. You will now have the option to look at the following:

  • Dashboard, which gives you an overview and averages on the patient's data.

  • Daily summary which gives you a detailed overview of a selected date.

  • Day by day overview which gives you either the last 7 or 14 days worth of data on one page.

  • Sensor overlay which gives you the last seven days worth of CGM readings all on top of each other.

  • Trend analysis which gives you details of both pump and CGM averages.

  • Events which gives you the history of every event that has been logged through the patient's phone or PDM handset.

  • Logbook which gives you details of the last 7 or 14 days worth of data in regards to BG levels, carbohydrate intake, bolus dosages along with further information.

  • Alert which gives you a history of every alert that has appeared on the mobile phone or PDM handset, as well as the pump.

  • And finally Device Settings Review which gives you details on the settings of the pump and CGM.

You can print the reports or save them as PDF. To do this you will need to click on the print tab on the left of your browser page. From here you can select what parts of the report you want to save as a PDF and also the time frame of data you want to save. Once you have done this you will need to scroll down to the bottom of the page and click on create report. The system will now open up a new tab on your web browser from here you can click on print and then save the document as a PDF.

Via Report Settings, you can adjust the glucose target of the reports and choose whether to include alerts and glucose values in the reports.

If the CGM or pump is connected via Bluetooth to the EasyPatch app, you can also view real-time data under the Monitor tab.

docs.title: 5.2 Interpreting Reports
docs.text:
Understanding your reports is key to making the most of your TouchCare Nano System.

The most useful report to start with is the Dashboard Report. This gives you a clear summary of your recent glucose control, insulin delivery, and auto mode performance. It includes:

  • Your average glucose level, estimated HbA1c, average total daily insulin dose, and average recorded carb and exercise entries.

  • An Ambulatory Glucose Profile

  • Time In Range, Time Below Range, and Time Above Range

  • Sensor usage and average insulin reservoir usage

  • Time spent in Auto Mode and the reasons for any exits

Other important reports are the Day by Day Overview and Device Settings Review.

Here’s a simple structure to follow:

  1. *Step 1: Review Glycemic Control* Start with the Dashboard Report. Look at your Time In Range and Time Below Range over the past two weeks. In most cases, Time In Range should be over 70%, and Time Below Range should be under 4%. Also check how often boluses are delivered. At least three boluses per day is a good sign of consistent mealtime insulin. If Auto Meal Handling is used, make sure meals are being correctly announced.

  2. *Step 2: Fine-Tune Insulin Delivery Settings* Use the Ambulatory Glucose Profile in the Dashboard Report to spot patterns of highs and lows. Then compare with the Day by Day Overview to see if these trends are linked to meals. If post-meal glucose is often high, check if carb counting is accurate and boluses are timed properly before adjusting the carb ratio. For patterns outside of meals, you may want to adjust the insulin sensitivity or glucose sensor target. Also consider other possible influences like physical activity, stress, alcohol, or missed meals.

  3. *Step 3: Encourage Good System Use* Check the Dashboard Report to see how much time the system is in Auto Mode, how often the CGM is being worn, and whether carbs and activities are regularly entered. Daily reports can help identify whether people are overcorrecting highs or lows, or if Exercise mode is being used properly. You can also use the Alert Report and Device Settings Review to see if any alerts are frequent or if alarm settings need adjustment.

  4. *Step 4: Confirm Safety and Backup Plans* Look at the basal rate programmed in Manual Mode and compare it with the average basal delivered in Auto Mode. If there’s a big difference, adjust the manual basal rate by about 10% per time block. Adjusting the basal rate does not affect insulin delivery in Auto Mode, but it ensures a safe basal rate is in place if the system reverts back to manual mode. Always make sure an emergency plan is in place in case the pump stops working. That includes access to insulin pens and knowing how to switch to backup insulin therapy.

By following these steps, both users and healthcare providers can use EasyView reports to get more out of the TouchCare Nano System and make informed adjustments when needed.

If anything in a report is unclear, check the legend at the bottom of the report PDFs—it explains all the symbols and color codes.

And finally, if you’d like to check your data on the go, you can also use the EasyTouch or EasyPatch app. Just open the main menu and tap on Statistics. Here, you can scroll through different reports to view your weekly, monthly or quarterly Time In Range, along with other key insights.

It’s a simple way to stay connected to your data and make informed decisions about your diabetes management—anytime, anywhere.

docs.title: 6. Managing special situations (4 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on managing special situations with the TouchCare Nano System.

In this guide, we will provide specific tips on managing hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, exercise, illness, alcohol consumption, and travel with the TouchCare Nano System. These recommendations are designed to complement general diabetes management strategies and help you make the most of the system in various scenarios.

docs.title: 6.1 Hypoglycemia
docs.text: With the TouchCare Nano System, you may require fewer carbohydrates to correct low blood sugar levels, especially if there is little insulin on board. You can monitor the insulin on board from the home screen of the PDM and EasyPatch app. For mild lows, treating with 10 grams or fewer of carbohydrates does not require entering these carbs into the system.

docs.title: 6.2 Hyperglycemia
docs.text:
For mild highs, trust the system’s automated corrections and avoid entering false carbs. If needed, you can administer a correction bolus using the bolus calculator by entering zero grams of carbohydrates. For persistent high blood sugar (over 270 mg/dl or 15 mmol/l) without improvement, check for pump issues. Change your pump if necessary and consider using an insulin pen if the problem continues. Keep spare infusion sets and insulin pens on hand.

The TouchCare Nano system will notify you if your glucose remains above 250 mg/dL (13.9 mmol/L) for more than three hours in auto mode.

docs.title: 6.3 High-fat Meals
docs.text: When eating high-fat meals without Auto Meal Handling, you can enter 100% of the carbs, or only 70% and allow the system to manage the remainder. Alternatively, you can split the bolus—half before the meal and half two hours later. The TouchCare Nano system does not offer extended or combo insulin boluses during Auto Mode.

docs.title: 6.4 Exercise
docs.text:
Before exercising, it’s recommended to activate Exercise Activity about 1 to 2 hours before your planned activity and to keep it on during the exercise itself.

If you’re not using Auto Meal Handling, and you plan to exercise within 2 hours after a meal, you might also consider reducing your mealtime bolus by 25 to 33%. This can help lower the risk of low blood glucose during physical activity.

docs.title: 6.5 Illness
docs.text: Most users can stay in Auto Mode during illness. However, if you experience prolonged hyperglycemia or elevated ketones, consider switching to manual mode and consult your healthcare team for specific adjustments. If you suspect sensor inaccuracies confirm glucose levels with finger pricks.

docs.title: 6.6 Alcohol
docs.text: Be mindful of increased hypoglycemia risk after alcohol consumption. Instead of eating additional carbs, consider using the Exercise Activity for a few hours after drinking to help maintain stable glucose levels.

docs.title: 6.7 Travel
docs.text:
When traveling with the TouchCare Nano System, carry sufficient supplies: extra Reservoir Patches, sensors, insulin pens, charger for the PDM and transmitter, and a blood glucose meter with strips. If you use the PDM, remember to adjust the time clock settings when entering a new time zone.

In conclusion, managing special situations with the TouchCare Nano System involves flexibility, ongoing learning, and working closely with your healthcare team. For additional support, consider joining diabetes communities and online support groups for example on FaceBook, where you can share experiences and gather practical advice from others using the system.

docs.title: 7. Case report
docs.text:
Congratulations on completing this course and gaining valuable insights into the TouchCare Nano System!

To wrap up, we’d like to share a personal review of the system written by Davor Skeledžija from Croatia, the father of a young girl who uses the TouchCare Nano. His story highlights the positive impact the system has had on his daughter’s glucose control.

The blog is written in Croatian, but if you open it in Google Chrome or use a translation tool like Google Translate, you can easily read it in your own language.

Davor’s experience is largely positive—he describes how the system significantly improved their daughter’s glucose levels. But he also gently reminds us that technology alone isn’t enough. As he puts it: “An insulin pump by itself will not lead to success if there is no adequate education and motivation of the user.”

You can read the full blog below this video.

https://www.nainzulinu.com/inzulinske-pumpe/novi-sad-o-maloj-heleni-i-medtrum-nano

To continue learning and feeling supported, consider joining online communities—such as Facebook groups—where users and families share tips, ask questions, and support each other. Being part of a community can make a real difference on your journey.

You’re now equipped with knowledge, advanced tools, and the confidence to move forward. Your journey with the TouchCare Nano System is just beginning—and we wish you success, empowerment, and a fulfilling experience ahead.

You've got this!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Trio according to the CARES paradigm
  • 3. Linking components together
  • 4. How Trio works
  • 5. Creating and interpreting reports
  • 6. Managing special situations
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Welcome to this comprehensive video series on Trio, an open-source automated insulin delivery system for iPhones. In these videos, we will explore the key components of Trio, including the insulin pumps and glucose sensor options, and the Oref algorithm. Additionally, we will delve into important topics such as viewing reports with Nightscout and remotely monitoring users as a caregiver.

Open-source automated insulin delivery systems are as safe and effective as commercial automated insulin delivery systems, but they have not been approved by any medical authorities. Consequently, if you choose to use it, you'll need to build and use it at your own risk. To access the manual for building the app, you can visit triodocs.org.

Additionally, you can seek assistance and support from various online communities, such as the "Trio" group on Facebook, and the "Trio" group on Discord. The Loop and Learn community also offers several explainer videos on YouTube.

Before we begin, it is essential to note that these videos are not meant as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider for personalized medical advice and therapy adjustments.

The content in these videos was created and updated by dr. Inge van Boxelaer, founder of Diabetotech and endocrinologist at the St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The material has been reviewed by Gary Scheiner, a Certified Diabetes Care and Education Specialist from Integrated Diabetes Services in the US, and by Theresa Hastings, Trio developer and co-founder also from the US. It's important to note that Trio is available for free, and therefore, no financial interests are involved, ensuring an unbiased assessment of this open-source automated insulin delivery system.

Throughout the series, you will gain valuable insights into Trio, including its performance based on the CARES paradigm, practical usage of the Trio app, creating and interpreting reports in Nightscout, and guidance for managing various situations such as hypo and hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, exercise, illness, alcohol, and travel.

By the end of these videos, you will have a comprehensive understanding of Trio and be equipped with helpful tips and tricks to enhance your experience with the system. Whether you are a caregiver supporting Trio users or an Trio user yourself, we wish you success on your journey with this powerful automated insulin delivery system. Good luck!

docs.title: 2. Trio according to the CARES paradigm (26.5 min)
2. Trio according to the CARES paradigm (26.5 min)
docs.text:
In this video, we will cover Trio according to the CARES paradigm.

We will discuss how Trio calculates and adjusts the automated insulin delivery, when it reverts back to Manual Mode, specific education tips and sensor issues, and how it shares data. After that we will go over Trio indications and software updates. Let's get started!

docs.title: 1) Trio according to the CARES paradigm
2.1 Calculate
docs.text:
In Trio, the default algorithm adjusts basal insulin only. The preset basal insulin rate is adjusted based on the predicted glycemia of the next 5 to 9 hours. Here's a breakdown of how Trio works:

Trio combines different blood glucose or "BG" predictions to calculate what the user's glucose is predicted to be at the end of the currently administered insulin's full Duration of Insulin Action or DIA:

  • Trio calculates a carb predicted BG, which is a prediction based on the carbohydrates on board,
  • an insulin predicted BG, which is a prediction based on the insulin on board,
  • an UnAnnounced Meal predicted BG, which is a prediction in case of unannounced carbohydrate consumption or other conditions resulting in an unexpected change in insulin needs,
  • and a Zero Temp predicted BG, which is the worst-case scenario, in case insulin is suddenly no longer administered and remaining entered carbohydrates are no longer absorbed.

Trio takes into account any errors in predictions, because the Eventual BG prediction is adjusted based on the deviation between the current glucose and the predicted glucose, also known as the blood glucose impact. The algorithm also considers the glycemia trend by calculating the delta, or the difference between the current and past glycemia. And it also takes into account the short-term and long-term glucose momentum, which is the amount of change between current and the glucose of 15 and 40 minutes ago.

The insulin requirement or InsulReq is calculated by subtracting the target blood glucose from the minimal predicted blood glucose, and dividing this difference by the insulin sensitivity or ISF. This insulin requirement will determine whether to increase or decrease the manually preset basal insulin.

If hypoglycemia is predicted, basal insulin will be stopped, and rescue carbohydrates may be recommended. This Minimum Safety Threshold is determined by the target glucose, but can be overridden to up to 120 mg/dl or 6.7 mmol/l.

In conclusion, the insulin delivery, including the baseline basal, will be raised or decreased based on the calculated insulin requirement. This is shown in the app as a temporary or temp basal with a specified insulin rate and duration or a microbolus, and these temp basals are recalculated every 5 minutes. A new calculation from Trio is also called a loop cycle.

In Trio, there is the option to deliver the insulin required calculation as a super micro bolus or the traditional temp basal. When given as a microbolus, the same insulin dose is delivered faster, followed by a temporary pause or reduction in basal insulin delivery. How these super micro boluses or SMBs are calculated based on the temp basals, can be adjusted within predefined safety settings.

Trio also uses an autosens feature, which increases or decreases the insulin delivery, based on a comparison of the user's insulin sensitivity from the past 8 and 24 hours.

  • It calculates a percentage that reflects how the insulin sensitivity in the past hours differs from the currently set sensitivity, and uses that % to adjust your insulin sensitivity and basal insulin rate.
  • The Autosens function can also be applied to the target value, adjusting it based on the calculated sensitivity ratio.
  • The max and min Autosens ratio are set at 120% and 70% respectively. They determine the maximum and minimum adjustment percentages based on the Autosens function. This means that the values, and thus insulin delivered, can be adjusted by no more than 120% and no less than 70% using Autosens.
  • If you activate the UnAnnounced Meal function, the algorithm will detect fast glucose increments caused by carbs, adrenaline, etc., and tries to adjust it with SMBs. This also works the opposite way: if there is a fast glucose decrease, it can stop SMBs earlier.

Additionally, Trio offers Dynamic ISF which is a more aggressive way to adjust your insulin sensitivity.

  • If you enable Dynamic ISF, it replaces the Autosens percentage calculation.
  • It adjusts your ISF, but in a dynamic way, based on your current glucose reading. There is an inverse adjustment to ISF. The higher your glucose reading, the lower your ISF, and vice versa.

  • The default formula for Dynamic ISF uses a logarithmic scale to determine the ratio for ISF adjustment.

  • This calculation uses a combination of the Total Daily Insulin from the last 2 hours and the last 10 days, current glucose reading, and accounts for the insulin type being used to determine what the current insulin sensitivity is for this loop cycle. This is considered the more physiologically accurate method for determining insulin sensitivity based on glucose readings.

  • Logarithmic Dynamic ISF must first be manually tuned at the time it is enabled. The Logarithmic Adjustment Factor should start at approximately 0.02 x your average total daily insulin dose. Adjustment Factor is a very individual value and will be different for each person.

  • Logarithmic's aggressiveness can be adjusted using the Autosens Maximum and Minimum settings. Logarithmic Adjustment Factor should not be used to change aggressiveness.

  • The second option for Dynamic ISF uses a Sigmoid formula.

  • This formula is simpler and contains fewer variables, but is less physiologically accurate. Sigmoid does not utilize your Total Daily Insulin, but relies solely on how far from target your current glucose reading is. With Sigmoid, Dynamic ISF uses the profile ISF when you are at your target glucose.

  • Sigmoid's aggressiveness can be adjusted using 3 settings: Sigmoid Adjustment Factor, Autosens Maximum, and Autosens Minimum. In Sigmoid, the Adjustment Factor should never be greater than 100% and should stay around 50% for most users. Under 50% will cause a slower response, above 50% causes a more rapid response.
  • If a user is unable to tune their Logarithmic settings, Sigmoid is the recommended option. Sigmoid is also a great option for users with rapid variability in insulin sensitivity, such as women who have variations during their menstrual cycle.

The target glucose value of Trio can be adjusted between 72 and 180 mg/dl or 4 and 10 mmol/l.

Trio places a high priority on safety and incorporates various safety settings and limits to prevent potentially dangerous insulin delivery. To ensure users configure the system correctly, the user community proposes defensive settings, and has disabled Dynamic ISF for the first 7 days of closed loop use. Over the course of several weeks, you can gradually move towards more aggressive settings.

docs.title: 2.2 Adjust
docs.text:
In Trio, you have the flexibility to adjust various parameters to personalize your treatment.

You can set a single target glucose value per time block of 30 minutes between 72 and 180 mg/dl (4 and 10 mmol/l). International guidelines suggest starting with a target value between 110 to 120 mg/dl (or 6 to 6.5 mmol/l). Many users opt for a target value of 100 mg/dl (or 5.6 mmol/l) during the day and even lower during the night - for example 90 mg/dl (or 5 mmol/l). This can be considered if this doesn't cause significant hypoglycemia.

Carbohydrate Ratio, Insulin Sensitivity, and Insulin Peak Time can be adjusted according to your specific requirement.

  • The duration of insulin action in open-source closed-loop systems is typically longer than in traditional insulin models due to the consideration of insulin's long tail effect and the impact adjusting this will have on the insulin decay model. Trio uses a Duration of Insulin Action of 10 hours as the internal formula used for insulin decay most closely matches the reported decay from insulin manufacturers.
  • Trio allows you to select the type of insulin you use. You can choose from options such as Apidra, Humalog, Novorapid or NovoLog, Fiasp and Lyumjev. Depending on the selected insulin type, Trio will provide a corresponding profile. Additionally, while Duration of Insulin Action should not be adjusted, Insulin Peak Time can vary by person and this setting can be fine tuned, if necessary.

Within Trio, there are numerous other adjustable parameters, and we will delve into the primary ones: SMB options, Recommended Bolus Percentage, Autosens options, Dynamic ISF and Delivery limits.

  • When starting to use Trio in "closed loop", it is recommended to use supermicroboluses only after consuming carbohydrates. If this works well for you, you can choose to activate SMB at other times as well. If any SMB options are enabled, UAM should be enabled as well. You can make the SMBs more or less aggressive by adjusting the Max SMB Basal Minutes.
  • In Trio, you can change how much insulin is suggested via the bolus calculator by changing the Recommended Bolus Percentage. Trio first calculates an insulin dose based on the integrated bolus calculator, which will then be multiplied by your "Recommended Bolus Percentage" to display your suggested insulin dose. Trio will deliver the remaining insulin via SMBs as the blood sugar starts to rise. You can view the details of the bolus calculation by tapping the "info icon" next to the recommendation.
  • Trio automatically adjusts your insulin sensitivity based on an Autosens feature. You can enable Autosens to also adjust your target glucose value. You can set the maximum and minimum % your ISF can be adapted. And you can allow Autosens to adjust your ISF when you set a Temporary Target.
  • Dynamic ISF can be enabled via Dynamic Settings after 7 days of successful closed looping. Adjusting the aggressiveness of Dynamic ISF is dependent upon which formula is used. After the proper Logarithmic Adjustment Factor is set, Logarithmic Dynamic ISF can be adjusted using Autosens Maximum and/or Minimum. Sigmoid Dynamic ISF can be adjusted using Sigmoid Adjustment Factor, Autosens Maximum, and/or Autosens Minimum.

  • Additionally, there are delivery limits in Trio that can be adjusted within a safe range based on the user's age and type.

  • You can set your maximum insulin on board, maximum bolus, maximum basal rate, maximum carbs on board and a minimum safety threshold. The maximum insulin on board or IOB is the total amount of basal and bolus insulin allowed to be active at once. It acts as a safety cap on how much insulin Trio can deliver beyond your scheduled basal rates. In Trio, this value defaults to 0 for safety and must be increased manually before automated insulin dosing occurs. For adults, the maximum basal rate is recommended to be set at 3 to 5 times the highest basal rate.

  • Maximum daily safety multiplier and current basal safety multiplier are additional limits for the maximum basal insulin rate, and it's generally best to leave them unchanged at the start. The maximum daily multiplier is set to 300% by default, allowing a maximum of 3 times the highest manually set basal rate. The current basal mode multiplier is set to 400%, allowing a maximum of 4 times the manually set basal rate. These can be safely increased to up to 500% if you have low basal rates compared to your overall insulin needs for carbs.

Remember that all settings and parameters should be adjusted in consultation with your healthcare provider, taking into account your individual needs and medical guidance.

docs.title: 2.3 Revert
docs.text:
In Trio, if there is no connection to the sensor or pump for more than 15 minutes, the system will revert to Manual Mode.

When the connection is lost, the pump will continue the last set temp basal. After a maximum of 120 minutes for zero temp basals or 30 minutes for increased temp basals, it will transition to Manual Mode.

In Manual Mode or Open Loop, the basal insulin delivery is set to the preset basal rate without a suspend-before-low function. Currently, Open Loop is not recommended for Trio users. Should you want to use your pump in manual mode, it is best to set Maximum Insulin on Board (Max IOB) to 0 and set Autosens Maximum and Minimum both to 100%. This will give you a suspend-before-low function without any autosens or dynamic functions.

Once the connection to the pump or sensor is reestablished, the system will automatically resume the Auto Mode, which is called closed-loop in Trio. It will take 2-3 consecutive readings after a disconnected sensor for Trio to automatically resume closed loop.

docs.title: 2.4 Educate
docs.text:
Proper education is crucial when starting an automated insulin delivery system. For general education points, please refer to the generic module.

The general education points covered were:

  • Using fewer carbohydrates to correct hypoglycemia.
  • Considering infusion set problems if blood glucose levels remain high without an apparent reason.
  • Trusting the automated insulin delivery system and avoiding unnecessary manual interventions.
  • Timing meal boluses 15 minutes before meals.
  • Utilizing an Exercise Mode 1 to 2 hours before activity.
  • Stopping insulin delivery if the pump is disconnected for more than 15 minutes.
  • Responding to alarms and being mindful of automatic updates on mobile phones. Automatic iOS updates should be disabled because new iOS releases may not immediately support Trio; compatibility should always be checked first.
  • Seeking peer support for guidance and sharing experiences.

Trio is a powerful tool for managing diabetes, but it's important to remember that it is not a fully automated system that you can "set and forget". It requires active monitoring and understanding of its actions. Here are some additional education points specific to using Trio:

  • Active Monitoring: Trio controls your insulin delivery, so it's crucial to watch it closely and understand how it works. Be attentive to any potential errors or unexpected behavior and learn how to interpret the system's actions.
  • Phone Usage: Once paired, the phone can instruct the pump to perform various actions. Your phone with Trio is to be regarded as a medical device and be handled accordingly. Avoid installing unnecessary apps or games that could introduce malware and potentially interfere with the system's operation.
  • Security Measures: Install all security updates provided by Apple to keep your device protected. This helps safeguard against potential security vulnerabilities. Do not activate "Automatic Updates" or "Beta Updates" in the Software Update settings of your iPhone. Check first the support of a specific iOS release in the Trio community prior to upgrading your iPhone operating system.
  • Proximity to Pump and CGM: Keep your mobile phone in close proximity to your insulin pump and CGM. It should be within the recommended distance (which is usually within 5 meters or 15 feet for the CGM) to maintain a reliable connection.
  • Passwords and Backups: Keep a record of all passwords used and store a backup of your app in iCloud. Regularly exporting your settings to iCloud simplifies the reinstallation process and prevents resetting your personalized parameters.
  • Spare Mobile Phone: It's advisable to have a spare mobile phone with the Trio app and CGM apps installed available for emergencies. This ensures that you have a backup device in case of any technical issues with your primary phone.

  • Check basic settings first: It is recommended to close the loop and utilize the first 7 days without access to dynamic ISF to check all your basic settings like basal insulin rate, carb ratio and insulin sensitivity.

  • The results of the Trio system depend largely on the correctness of these basic settings, so they need to be fairly accurate.

  • Next slowly raise "maximum insulin on board", to allow Trio to automatically dose insulin. This is set by default on 0 and can slowly be raised to the sum of your average meal bolus plus 3 times your highest basal rate.

  • Next you can enable multiple individual SMB options or SMBs Always.

  • Advanced Settings: Utilize advanced settings, such as Max SMB Basal Minutes and Dynamic ISF only after you have a good understanding of the basics and have achieved stable blood glucose control. Note that these features may not work optimally for everyone.

  • Healthcare Provider Access: Ensure that your healthcare provider has access to your reports generated by Nightscout or Tidepool. This enables them to stay informed about your diabetes management and make informed decisions during your medical consultations.
  • Master Insulin Pump and Sensor Use: Make sure you are able to use your insulin pump and sensor properly as well.

By following these guidelines and maintaining a proactive approach to using Trio, you can optimize the system's effectiveness and ensure a safe and reliable automated insulin delivery experience.

docs.title: 2.5 Sensor
docs.text:
Trio can be linked to the Dexcom sensors G5, G6, G7 and One Plus, European FreeStyle Libre 2 and Libre 2 Plus sensors, and Medtronic Enlite sensors via older Medtronic pumps.

Since Trio can directly connect to the European FreeStyle Libre 2 and Libre 2 Plus sensors, the LibreLink app is only needed to start a new sensor in these cases.

For Dexcom sensors, you'll need to have the associated Dexcom App running on your iPhone to allow Trio to retrieve your glucose value.

Trio also accepts additional open-source sensor apps such as Nightscout as CGM or xDrip4iOS. These apps can be used to transmit sensor data to Trio over an internet connection, although the native connections to CGM are more stable. Some people choose to use these open-source apps for better alarms and connection to other devices.

In an automated insulin delivery system, continuous and accurate sensor values are essential for proper insulin dosing. If your sensor glycemia is jumpy or noisy, it can lead to incorrect insulin dosing, resulting in high or low blood glucose levels. In such cases, it is important to disable the Auto Mode until the issue is resolved. The problem may be related to the configuration of the glucose sensor or issues with the sensor or site. If necessary, replacing the glucose sensor may be required to address the problem.

If you experience jumpy or noisy sensor values, you might try to enable the "Smooth Glucose Value" feature via the CGM Device Settings.

If your sensor does not show a number or arrow, or your readings do not match your symptoms, use your meter to make diabetes treatment decisions. When in doubt, get your meter out.

If you experience a significant difference between your sensor glucose and finger prick value, although you have a stable and in range glucose, you might try to calibrate your sensor. You can do this via the Trio Settings, via the CGM menu, or directly in the sensor app.

docs.title: 2.6 Share
docs.text:
In Trio, sharing glucose data with healthcare providers and family members is possible through Nightscout, Tidepool, Apple Health and via Dexcom Share or LibreView. Sharing glucose and insulin data is only possible via Nightscout and Tidepool, with Nightscout being the preferred option. Nightscout is the only platform that supports complete Trio data and remote commands; Tidepool does not support remote dosing and requires manual uploads.

To use Nightscout, you'll need to set up a personal Nightscout website. Trio can send the data to the Nightscout website, allowing your healthcare provider to access and review your reports in real-time.

Caregivers can access the data from the personal Nightscout website through the Loop Follow app and Nightscout. This allows them to view and track your insulin and glucose information and stay updated on your diabetes management.

Caregivers can even give an insulin bolus, enter carbs and change the insulin profile remotely, via Loop Follow. To be able to use remote care, Nightscout has to be set up to use remote commands, and the remote care has to be enabled in the Trio app.

In the Trio settings, multiple iOS shortcuts are available, but iOS Bolus shortcuts must be activated by "Allowing bolusing with Shortcuts". This could also help parents to assist their young children remotely.

Users can download reports from Nightscout and Tidepool or use tools like Nightscout Reporter to generate comprehensive reports that can be shared with healthcare providers for in-depth analysis and evaluation.

By leveraging these sharing capabilities, Trio users can facilitate effective communication and collaboration with their healthcare team and loved ones, fostering a supportive and informed diabetes management environment.

docs.title: 2.7 Indications
docs.text:
Trio is an open-source app that does not have official indications approved by the FDA or European authorities. It can be used with all rapid-acting and ultra-rapid insulin analogues.

The Trio app is not available for download from app stores. Users are required to build the app themselves at their own risk. Setting up the system requires determination and the willingness to follow directions and learn. If you don't have the technical know-how at the beginning, you will have by the end.

All the information you need can be found in the Trio documentation that is available online, and from others who have already done it – support is available via the Trio Facebook group or other forums like Discord.

Discord is mainly used for development and build topics, whereas Facebook is more used for end-user help. Both can be used in parallel, but do not repeat each problem or topic in both environments simultaneously.

Both Facebook and Discord are highly recommended platforms for interactive chat among Trio and Nightscout users, parents, and developers. It covers a wide range of subjects, from first-time user queries to more technically advanced discussions about the inner workings of Trio and the OpenAPS algorithm. The primary language is English.

Many people have successfully built Trio and are now using it entirely safely, but it is essential that Trio users:

  • Understand how their insulin pump and glucose sensor works.
  • Adjust its individual dosage algorithm with the help from his or her diabetes team.
  • Maintain and monitor the system to ensure it is working properly.
  • Build the system themselves so that they thoroughly understand how it works, or rely on a trustworthy source to download and use the Trio App from Testflight. Testflight Apps have a 90 day validity, as this iOS environment is supported by Apple for testing new Apps.

To utilize Trio, you will need a compatible iPhone that is running on iOS 17 or newer. The minimum requirements for your devices can be found in the Trio documentation.

For building, you'll need a yearly renewable Apple Developer license, which costs 99 US dollar per year. Once this license is activated you can start the building process in 2 different ways.

  • You can follow the "Build Trio with Script" procedure, using Mac computer with Xcode Trio build. For this method you will first have to put your iPhone and Apple Watch if applicable to Developer Mode. On your device, you can enable this via Settings and Privacy & Security.
  • Or you can follow the "Build Trio with GitHub" procedure, which is also called the "browser build" as you can use any computer for this. After completion of this method, you can download the Trio app on your iPhone via Testflight. The main advantage of this method is that it is an easier method to build and update your Trio app.

Some people don't build the Trio app themselves, but are invited to use it via a TestFlight invitation. Some healthcare professionals may be comfortable sharing the app. If your healthcare professional provides you with the app, you won't need an Apple Developer license.

docs.title: 2.8 Software updates
docs.text:
Regular software updates are crucial for maintaining the optimal performance and accessing the latest features of Trio. The Trio team releases new versions periodically. These updates often include bug fixes and improvements to enhance the functionality of the system.

The version of Trio is mentioned on top of the Settings screen. The most recent "main" and "dev" versions are also mentioned, to indicate "newer" version(s) available.

Once every 3 months you'll need to update your Trio app. To update Trio, follow these steps:

  • It's good practice to update your iPhone settings to iCloud. This way all your settings of the Trio will have a backup on iCloud. You can back up your iPhone via the settings.
  • When building, keep the same signing keys as the previous installation.
  • If you are using the "Build Trio with script" method, you'll use Xcode to refresh the code to the new version, while your iPhone and Apple Watch are connected and powered.
  • In the case of the "Browser build" method, upon successful execution of the build process, the new version needs to be installed via a Testflight download.

If you encounter any issues, stay calm and seek support from the Trio community and documentation. Remember that troubleshooting is a normal part of software usage, and with patience, you can overcome any challenges that arise.

In conclusion, staying informed and proactive with Trio is key to optimizing your diabetes management. By keeping up with software updates, following care guidelines, and addressing any issues promptly, you can ensure a smooth and effective experience. Prioritize safety, maintain accurate records, and utilize available resources for support. Embrace the possibilities that Trio offers to enhance your health and well-being.

docs.title: 3. Linking components together (11 min)
docs.text:
In this video tutorial, we will guide you through the process of creating the necessary links within the Trio automated insulin delivery system. These links establish connections between various components to ensure seamless communication and data exchange.

Trio operates in the following manner:

  • Glycemia is measured every 1 to 5 minutes using a glucose sensor, such as Dexcom G6, G7, Dexcom ONE Plus, FreeStyle Libre 2 or FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus.
  • After starting your European Libre 2 or 2 Plus sensor with your LibreLink app, the sensor sends the glucose readings directly to your Trio app. With Dexcom sensors, the Dexcom App needs to stay active.
  • Trio calculates the required insulin dosage and sends it to your insulin pump, such as Omnipod DASH, Medtrum Nano, Dana RS, Dana-i, Omnipod Eros or older Medtronic pumps.
  • The insulin pump delivers the necessary insulin and provides pump data back to the Trio app.
  • Trio shares the glycemia and insulin data with Nightscout, which serves as the readout program. Trio also shares this information to Apple Health and Tidepool, and the Dexcom and LibreLink app send glucose data to Dexcom Clarity and LibreView respectively.
  • Additionally, the Trio app provides an Apple Watch version being installed during the same building & installation process. This allows you to view your glucose values in real-time on your Apple Watch, give an insulin bolus, and even start and stop overrides and temp targets directly from your Apple Watch, adding convenience to your diabetes management.

In this video tutorial, we will guide you through the process of creating various links in the Trio automated insulin delivery system. Specifically, we will cover the following steps:

  • How to link your glucose sensor to your Trio app.
  • How to pair your insulin pump.
  • The process of creating a Nightscout account, which serves as the data hub for Trio.
  • How to establish a connection between the Trio app and Nightscout, ensuring the smooth transfer of glycemia and insulin data.
  • And how to configure Trio on your Apple Watch.

By following these steps, you will be able to establish the necessary links within the Trio system, enabling effective automated insulin delivery functionality for diabetes management.

docs.title: 3.1 Linking Sensor to Trio App
docs.text:
Let's delve into the process of connecting the most used sensors such as the FreeStyle Libre 2, 2 Plus, Dexcom G6, G7 and ONE Plus.

For European FreeStyle Libre 2 or 2 Plus sensors, the initial step involves starting your sensor via the LibreLink app. After the one-hour warm-up period, you can make the connection between your sensor and the Trio app. Within Trio, navigate to Settings, select Devices, CGM, and then opt for the FreeStyle Libre. Tap the configuration option to select Libre 2 Direct. Here, you can start the pairing process and scan your sensor. This enables Trio to directly receive data from your FreeStyle Libre 2 or 2 Plus sensor. Once scanned, your sensor information will populate the screen, and your current sensor glucose will be visible on the Trio homescreen. Be sure to disable bluetooth permissions in the Libre app or Trio's connection will be unstable. After 14 days, the FreeStyle Libre 2 or 2 Plus sensor will stop functioning. You can start a new sensor via the LibreLink and Trio app.

If you're using FreeStyle Libre 1 sensor with an after-market transmitter you can select Bluetooth transmitter and follow the prompts in xDrip4iOS. This sensor requires a constant internet connection and is not generally recommended.

For Dexcom G6 sensors, start the sensor using the Dexcom G6 app. In the Trio app, select Dexcom G6 in the CGM menu and enter the transmitter serial number. After a two-hour warm-up, you'll receive your results. You must keep the G6 app running in the background. Sensor changes are done in the G6 app.

For the Dexcom G7 and Dexcom ONE Plus sensor, start the sensor using the Dexcom G7 or Dexcom ONE Plus app, preferably after the warm-up period of at least 30 min, to allow overlap of sensors. Within Trio, select Dexcom G7/ONE+. When readings start in the Dexcom app, Trio will automatically read the G7/ONE+ readings.

You can opt to receive sensor alerts from either the LibreLink or Dexcom app or via the Trio app through Notifications in the Settings. Trio has 3 glucose alert options. Disabled will not provide any alerts, Always will alert you to every glucose reading, and Only Alarm Limits will alert you when glucose is above or below the limits you set. If you require more alert options, consider using your native sensor app or an open-source application like xDrip4iOS.

docs.title: 3.2 Linking Insulin Pump to Trio App
docs.text:
Most people will use Trio with an Omnipod DASH, Dana or TouchCare Nano insulin pump. If you want to use Omnipod Eros or an older Medtronic pump, you'll need to use a Riley link.

We'll show the process of linking an Omnipod DASH Pod.

  • First go to the Settings, Devices, Insulin pump and choose Omnipod DASH.
  • You will first be asked to set your expiration reminder, low reservoir reminder, and your insulin type.
  • Then you can tap Omnipod DASH to go to the pump screen and select "Pair Pod".
  • The app will take you through the different steps, similar as the Omnipod DASH PDM does. First fill your Pod with insulin, then link your Pod, remove the needle guard and place the Pod to your body. Finally continue to insert the cannula.
  • Once your Pod is paired, you'll see a dedicated DASH pop-up in the app interface. If you tap it, you go to the pump screen, where you can access information about your Omnipod DASH pump and control its functions. The buttons are intuitive and serve as replacements for the functions performed by the Omnipod's PDM. Although the PDM is not needed if you are using Trio, it is recommended to keep it as a backup device.

For users of other insulin pumps, the app interface will display different options corresponding to the specific pump type. These features ensure that you can effectively manage and control insulin delivery through your linked pump using the Trio app.

docs.title: 3.3 Creating a Nightscout Website
docs.text:
Nightscout is not a platform to log into but a website that you build or have built, which may involve some additional costs.

A Nightscout account consists of a website URL (name) and a password to access the website.

Nightscout accounts are created as individual user accounts. There is no Nightscout platform specifically for healthcare providers.

Nightscout enables real-time tracking of your sensor and insulin data, allowing you to share it with your loved ones or healthcare provider. Additionally, Nightscout is the exclusive method for generating reports from your Trio automated insulin delivery system.

Nightscout is more than just a website for Trio data; it can connect to various devices. Nightscout acts as a central hub, receiving data from sensors and distributing it to multiple devices such as mobile phones, smartwatches, computers, and other monitoring devices. It provides diverse options to access and display glucose information, including through smartwatches, desktop widgets, wall-mounted devices, and integration with voice assistants like Alexa.

There are multiple ways to create a Nightscout website.

  • Nightscout is open-source software. You can find all the information on nightscout.github.io.
  • You can set it up yourself using step-by-step plans available online.
  • Or there are firms that offer Nightscout setup services, such as T1Pal, NS10BE, Nightscout Pro, Serendipity Bio, Nightscout4u, Nightscout-easy and Opensource.clinic.
  • Nightscout and its associated services are not FDA approved, except for T1Pals' service. There is no approval from European authorities, so its use is at your own risk. Nightscout appears to be GDPR and HIPAA compliant but does not claim official compliance due to the absence of auditors.

It is crucial to handle sensor glycaemia data securely and protect your Nightscout URL with a password. If you want to share your Nightscout website with a loved one or healthcare providers, it is recommended to create a separate token for them.

By following these steps, you can create a Nightscout website easily and securely, allowing you to track and share your diabetes data effectively.

docs.title: 3.4 Linking Trio App to Nightscout
docs.text:
To connect your Trio data to your Nightscout website, just head to Settings, then Nightscout, and enter your Nightscout URL and password.

Once connected, your Trio data will show up on your Nightscout website. This gives you, your family, and your healthcare provider realtime information about how you're managing your diabetes.

If you turn on "Remote Control," trusted individuals can give you insulin, input carbs, or adjust your insulin settings using Loop Follow or their Nightscout login.

docs.title: 3.5 Trio App on Apple Watch
docs.text:
Using an Apple Watch will not only allow you to follow your sensor glucose and insulin on board on your wrist, but also allow you to add carbs, bolus and select/set/stop a profile from your watch.

Trio will automatically be downloaded on your connected Apple Watch. On your Apple Watch, you can set a Trio complication in a modular watch face.

If you tap the Trio complication on your watch face, you will see a Trio screen where you can see your glucose and loop data, your insulin on board and your carbs on board. It also lets you add carbs, do a manual bolus, and set an override or a temporary target. However, the companion app only serves as a remote control for the iPhone app; connection to the CGM and pump is still done through your iPhone, and you will have to bring it with you.

In this chapter, we explored the different connections available in Trio. From glucose sensors to insulin pumps and Nightscout, these links enable seamless communication for effective diabetes management. By establishing these connections, users can fully leverage the capabilities of Trio and take control of their diabetes.

docs.title: 4. How Trio works (9 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video on the functionality of Trio.

Controlling your insulin delivery with the Trio automated insulin delivery system is done through the Trio app which is installed on your iPhone.

The app offers two main operating modes: Closed Loop or Auto Mode and Open Loop or Manual Mode.

  • In Closed Loop, Trio automatically adjusts insulin delivery every 5 minutes based on real-time sensor glucose readings.
  • If Closed Loop is disabled, you are in Open Loop or Manual Mode, and only your scheduled basal will be administered without manual intervention.

In this video, we will delve into the functionality of the Trio app in general, and explore how you can use the Trio app in both Closed Loop and Open Loop Mode.

docs.title: 4.1 Trio app
docs.text:
When you use the Trio app for the first time, an onboarding wizard will help you learn about the app.

When you open the Trio app, you can see the Trio homescreen. It has four main sections: Status Bar, Graph & History, Adjustment Status, and Action Buttons.

Let's start with the Status Bar.

  • Here, you'll see three elements: Pump Info, Current Glucose, Current Metrics, and the Loop Indicator.
  • Pump Info shows your reservoir level and how much Pod life is left.
  • Your current glucose value is displayed together with the trend information.
