  • The Loop Indicator should normally be green. If the last loop cycle was more than five minutes ago, it turns yellow. After more than ten minutes, it turns red. And when Closed Loop is disabled, the indicator appears grey.
  • Right below that, you'll find the Eventual Glucose — this is a glucose forecast for up to 6 hours in the future. It is typically around 4-hours.
  • The second row of the Status Bar shows your Insulin on Board, Carbs on Board, and your current basal rate. Keep in mind that Insulin on Board can be negative when the algorithm has reduced the scheduled basal rate.

Next, let's look at the Graph and History section.

  • This area displays basal adjustments, current and past activity, active insulin, active carbs, and various stats. You can customize the graph scale and additional graph options in the Settings menu.
  • If you tap on "Stats," you'll see detailed statistics for glucose, insulin, looping, and meals.
  • If you tap on "Info," you'll see a full chart legend.

Below that is the Adjustment Status, which tells you whether a bolus, override or temp target is currently active. This is also where you can cancel an active bolus, override, or temp target.

Finally, at the bottom of the screen, you'll see five Action Buttons.

  • "Main" brings you back to the homescreen.
  • "History" shows your recent treatments, meals, glucose readings, and adjustments.
  • The plus icon lets you enter carbs and boluses.
  • "Adjustments" is where you can start or stop an override or temp target, and manage your presets.
  • And in "Settings," you can check the software version, enable or disable Closed Loop, access the Trio configuration, and find support and community resources.

Now, let's walk through the Trio Configuration menu in detail. This section includes six parts: Devices, Therapy, Algorithm, Features, Notifications, and Services.

  • First, Devices. Here you select your insulin pump, CGM, and smartwatch, and you can access all device-specific settings.
  • Next is Therapy Settings. In this section, you set your unit type — mg/dL or mmol/L — and define your delivery limits. You can also enter your glucose target, basal rates, carb ratios, and insulin sensitivities. All of these can be configured in 30-minute intervals if needed.
  • The third section is Algorithm. This is where you adjust settings related to Autosens, Super Micro Bolus, dynamic decisions, target behaviour, and other algorithm parameters. The additional algorithm settings normally don't need any changes. Only adjust them if you fully understand what they do. Every parameter includes a short description, and if you tap the information icon, you'll see a more detailed explanation.
  • Next, we have Features. Here you can configure the bolus calculator, meal settings, shortcuts, and remote control. You can also customize the user interface and app icon. This section also lets you enable anonymised data sharing through app diagnostics.
  • Then we move to Notifications. This is where you adjust all Trio app notifications and enable the Live Activity feature on your phone. Live Activity can appear as a dynamic island on your home screen or as a live view on your lock screen. You can also enable calendar events, which allow your glucose values to show up on your Apple Watch and in Apple CarPlay.
  • Finally, Services. Here you can connect your Trio app with Nightscout, Tidepool, and Apple Health.

By navigating through these sections, you can effectively manage your diabetes using the Trio app.

docs.title: 4.2 Closed-Loop
docs.text:
Now that we know how the Trio app works, we will discuss how to use Trio in Closed Loop mode.

Starting Trio Closed loop mode

Once you have completed the Onboarding Wizard, paired a pump and a CGM, you can enable Closed Loop via the main settings screen.

Use a preset Temporary Target or Override

In Trio, you can set a Temp Target or an Override via the Adjustments icon.

Overrides also allow temporary adjustments of your basal rate, carb ratio, ISF and SMB settings, according to your individual needs and preferences.

Adjusting parameters

In Trio, you have the flexibility to change your basal insulin rate, carb ratio, insulin sensitivity, and target glucose.

Giving an insulin bolus

You can deliver meal insulin using the plus icon.

The Bolus Calculator interface is divided into four sections: the dynamic glucose forecast, the meal entry, the dosing calculation, and the Enact Bolus button.

At the top, you'll see the dynamic forecast graph. As you enter carbs or adjust the insulin amount, this graph updates in real time. It shifts the forecast to reflect the expected impact of your upcoming meal and insulin dose.

Suspending insulin

If you need to temporarily stop the insulin delivery, you can go to your Pump screen by tapping the pump icons at the top left of the homescreen or via the settings. From there, you can choose the option to "Suspend Insulin Delivery" for a duration ranging from 30 minutes to 2 hours. This will temporarily suspend the insulin delivery from the pump. Here you can also resume insulin delivery.

docs.title: 4.3 Open Loop
docs.text:
In rare situations, such as extremely high and false glucose readings, it may be necessary to switch to Manual Mode in Trio. In these situations, you may lose the ability to activate Dynamic ISF when you resume closed loop, due to lack of looping history.

You can go to Manual Mode by disabling Closed Loop via the Settings.

In Manual Mode, Trio still has manual operations:

  • you can adjust the profile basal insulin rate, carb ratio, insulin sensitivity, and target glucose value via the Settings
  • you can set a temporary basal rate in the pump menu
  • And you can deliver a bolus via the plus icon.
  • You will not get glucose predictions or low glucose prevention when in open loop. Only use open loop if absolutely necessary.

Alternatively, if the concern is settings-related, it's advised to stay in closed-loop and set Maximum Insulin on Board to 0. This will maintain low glucose prevention without allowing Trio to automatically administer any additional insulin above your current scheduled basal rate. It's best to go through the documentation and have stable core settings before enabling more advanced settings, like Dynamic ISF.

While it may seem daunting to adjust the system yourself, it is important to remember that you are not alone in this journey. The Trio community is there to support you every step of the way. So, have confidence, seek help when needed, and refer to the extensive documentation as you work towards achieving optimal glycemic control with Trio.

docs.title: 5. Creating and interpreting reports (11 min)
docs.text:
In this video, we will explore the process of creating and interpreting reports in Nightscout, which is used in conjunction with Trio.

Previous videos have covered the steps to create a Nightscout website and connect your Trio app to Nightscout for data transmission every 5 minutes.

Now, we will delve into the features and functionality of the Nightscout interface and reports, helping you understand and leverage the valuable information they provide for managing your diabetes.

docs.title: 5.1 Nightscout Interface
docs.text:
In the Nightscout interface, the website is typically black and can be customized based on your preferences. Here are the different sections of a Nightscout website:

The title bar displays the name of your site on the left and includes buttons on the right, such as the menu button with various settings options. You may also find edit and audio icons, which are rarely used. Depending on your authentication status, you will also see a plus sign, where you can log treatments.

System information: The system information section shows the current information of all systems connected to Nightscout. It may include gray boxes or plugins, providing additional details when hovered over. In Nightscout we call these pills.

User and treatment information: The user and treatment information section displays information about you and your treatments. You'll find pills or abbreviations that will be explained further in the settings section.

Basal insulin: The basal insulin section shows your basal insulin release.

Sensor glucose and treatments: The sensor glucose and treatments section displays your sensor glucose readings, carbohydrate and/or insulin treatments, as well as predictions from Trio.

Data history: This data history section shows the last 48 hours of data.

In Nightscout, you can access various settings through the hamburger menu at the top. Here's an overview of the available options:

  • Reports: Allows you to generate reports.
  • Profile and Nutrition: Via Profile and Food Editor you can change settings, although the settings are usually managed through the Trio app rather than Nightscout.
  • Admin Tools enable you to create access tokens for sharing data with healthcare providers or family members.
  • Account settings: Account settings provide options to adjust units, time format, language, and more. You can also customize the appearance of your Nightscout account, including the display of plugins or pills. Additionally, this section explains the abbreviations used in the interface.
  • Saving settings: Changes can be saved if you enter your API password. Below that, you can see the authentication status.

By exploring these settings and making appropriate adjustments, you can personalize your Nightscout experience to suit your needs.

docs.title: 5.2 Types of reports
docs.text:
When using Trio, you can access reports through your Nightscout website via the menu in the top right.

Some of the key reports you can access include:

  • Day to day Reports: These shows how the algorithm adjusts basal insulin and provides insights into micro bolus delivery.
  • Distribution Report: This report displays Time in Range, Time Below Range, and estimated HbA1c. The Coefficient of Variation can be calculated by dividing the standard deviation by the mean glucose level and multiplying it by 100.
  • The Percentile Chart Report offers an Ambulatory Glucose Profile-like picture to analyze glucose trends.
  • The Profiles Report allows you to review and record insulin settings, such as insulin duration, carbohydrate ratio, insulin sensitivity, basal insulin rate, and target value. Algorithm settings are not saved.
  • And the Loopalyzer Report provides visualizations of average basal adjustments over a 24-hour period within a specified time frame. This can help identify patterns and trends in basal insulin adjustments made by the algorithm.

Alternatively, reports from Nightscout can be visualized using the Nightscout Reporter software. This free web-based platform provides a more user-friendly interface and allows you to select the desired reports, set the time period, and generate a PDF document to forward to your healthcare provider. Interesting reports available include analysis, profiles, glucose percentile chart, and daily reports.

There are three ways to discuss these reports with your healthcare provider:

  • You can provide your healthcare provider with your Nightscout URL and a secure token that you have created. This allows them to access and view the reports directly.
  • While you can't directly generate a PDF from Nightscout, you can save the reports as PDF files. Simply open the desired report, press Command+P on Mac (or "Ctrl+P" on Windows), and select "Save as PDF" as the printer option. This will create a PDF file that you can save and email to your healthcare provider if needed.
  • Another option is to use Nightscout Reporter to create more visually appealing reports that can be easily forwarded to your healthcare provider.

By effectively utilizing Nightscout and Nightscout Reporter, you can provide your healthcare provider with comprehensive reports that highlight the performance and trends of your diabetes management with Trio.

docs.title: 5.3 Interpreting reports
docs.text:
In Trio, the main parameters that can be adjusted are the target glucose value, basal insulin rate, carbohydrate ratio, insulin sensitivity, delivery limits like maximum basal and maximum insulin on board, and the SMB settings.

This can be done via the Settings. It is important to follow a structured approach when interpreting reports and making adjustments. Here is a detailed guide:

1. Assess Glycemic Information:

Check on the Distribution report if treatment goals, particularly Time in Range and Time Below Range, are being met over the past 2 to 4 weeks. Following international guidelines, time in range should be above 70% and time below range should be below 4%.

If you have access to the Analysis report of Nightscout Reporter, take note of the frequency of meal bolusing, which you would expect to be on average 3 times per day.

2. Optimize AID Settings:

Analyze the Ambulatory Glucose Profile to identify trends of hypo- or hyperglycemia. If you spot trends, try to find out on the Daily reports, if these are related to meal boluses.

  • For trends related to meal boluses: assess factors such as timing, carbohydrate counting, and consider adjusting the carbohydrate ratio.
  • If postprandial hyperglycemia is an issue, also consider using a lower Temp Target before meals.
  • You can also explore the option of faster-acting insulins like Fiasp or Lyumjev.
  • For trends after manual correction boluses or after hyperglycemia: change the insulin sensitivity or duration of insulin action, to adjust the aggressiveness of the algorithm.
  • For trends outside of meals or boluses: you can adjust the Target glucose value per time block or change the basal insulin rate from 1 hour before and during the corresponding period with 10-20%.
  • It's also important to explore possible correlations with missed boluses, exercise, alcohol or hypoglycemia.

In Trio, it is important to ensure that the basal insulin rate is appropriately set. If you notice a consistent pattern of increasing or decreasing basal insulin during a specific period of the day that cannot be explained by SMB or manual bolusing, for example in the Loopalyzer Report, the aim is to adjust the basal insulin rate at that time to align with the algorithm's requirements.

If you are an experienced Trio user, you can control the aggressiveness of the Trio algorithm's insulin delivery in several ways.

  • You can adjust the max basal and max IOB settings.
  • Lowering these limits can help prevent excessive insulin administration, especially in cases of frequent hypoglycemia.
  • On the other hand, if the max IOB setting is restricting the system's ability to correct high blood glucose levels effectively, it may need to be increased. This should be done with caution.
  • You can also adjust the SMBs by changing the Max SMB Basal Minutes and Max UAM Basal Minutes.
  • If you use Logarithmic Dynamic ISF, you can change your Autosens Maximum or Autosens Minimum. If it's not behaving as expected, ensure your Adjustment Factor is still 0.02 x your total daily insulin dose. Your total daily insulin dose may have changed and your Adjustment Factor may need to be changed as well.
  • If you use Sigmoid Dynamic ISF, you can change your Sigmoid Adjustment Factor, Autosens Maximum, or Autosens Minimum.

3. Guide Behavioral Recommendations: Ensure proper usage of the automated insulin delivery system in the Daily reports:

  • Sensor wear time, activation of closed loop, and adherence to pre-meal bolusing can best be checked in the Day-to-day reports.
  • You can also check the correct use of temporary targets and overrides, and evaluate for overcorrection of hypo- or hyperglycemia.
  • Also ask about the appropriateness of alarm settings and reminders, to avoid alarm fatigue.

4. Review Pump Settings and Emergency Plans:

  • Document your pump settings, including at least your basal insulin rate, carbohydrate ratio, insulin sensitivity and target glucose value.
  • Establish an emergency plan and always have insulin pens with you, in case of a pump malfunction or other emergencies.

When making changes, it is important to modify only one setting at a time. After implementing a change, closely monitor the effects over a period of 1-2 weeks. It is possible that your initial adjustment may not yield the desired results, in which case, you can explore alternative options and make further adjustments accordingly. The key is to iterate and fine-tune your settings based on the feedback provided by your Nightscout reports, ensuring optimal performance of your Trio system.

By following these guidelines and considering the provided details, you can effectively interpret reports, optimize insulin settings, and ensure proper usage of Trio for improved diabetes management.

docs.title: 6. Managing special situations (10 min)
docs.text:
In this video, we will provide specific tips and recommendations for managing common situations in diabetes management while using the Trio automated insulin delivery system.

These tips are in addition to the general guidelines covered in the general AID systems module.

We will cover strategies for handling hypo- and hyperglycemia, dealing with high-fat meals, managing diabetes during physical activities, coping with illness, alcohol and planning for travel.

By implementing these tips, you can optimize your glucose control and enhance your overall diabetes management with Trio.

docs.title: 6.1 Hypoglycemia
docs.text:
With an automated insulin delivery system like Trio, you require fewer carbohydrates to treat hypoglycemia compared to traditional diabetes management, especially when you have little insulin on board.

Trio provides the ability to see your insulin on board on the home screen. If you haven't had insulin in the last few hours, a smaller dose of carbohydrates is usually sufficient.

It is recommended not to enter the carbs you consume to treat or prevent hypoglycemia. If you consume too many carbs, you can enter the excess as a carb entry, if needed.

To prevent further episodes of hypoglycemia, you can make adjustments in the Trio system to reduce insulin delivery. This can be done by using a higher temporary target value via the Temp Target button, or via an override.

In an override, you can also choose to reduce the percentage of basal rate adjustment, inversely change your carb ratio and insulin sensitivity, and turn off SMBs, which might be a good strategy if you're experiencing hypoglycemia.

It's important to note that in some cases, the algorithm may increase basal insulin even when you are experiencing low glucose levels. This could be necessary if the predicted glucose level is higher than the target value. In the majority of cases, Trio is accurate regarding its predictions.

docs.title: 6.2 Hyperglycemia
docs.text:
In the case of mild hyperglycemia, it is generally recommended to trust Trio and allow the system to work on its own. However, you can consider giving a manual correction bolus using the bolus calculator without entering carbohydrates.

To address hyperglycemia, you also have the option to increase the percentage of the basal rate adjustment in your override or temporarily set a lower target value.

In situations of prolonged and severe hyperglycemia, it is important to consider the possibility of an infusion set problem. When in doubt, it is advisable to change your infusion set or Pod. Remember the phrase "If in doubt, change it out."

It is essential to ensure that you have enough equipment on hand to carry out all necessary substitutions and replacements. Being prepared with the required supplies is crucial for effectively managing hyperglycemia and maintaining the proper functioning of your Trio system.

docs.title: 6.3 High-fat meals
docs.text:
When consuming a high-fat meal while using Trio, there are several recommended approaches you can consider:

  1. In Trio, you have the flexibility to include carbs, fat and proteins into the bolus calculator. Via the settings you can see and adjust how these fats and proteins are taken into account in the calculation of the insulin delivery. Entering fat and protein will disable UAM functionality until those carbs are absorbed.

  2. You can also utilize the Reduced Bolus Option in the bolus calculator and SMBs. It must first be enabled in the settings, but once it is, you can select this option in the bolus calculator and it will further reduce your initial bolus recommendation. If enabled, SMBs will then address the delayed rise in glucose from high-fat or protein meals over the next 6 hours following a carb entry. Ensure either SMB Always, SMB after carbs, or SMB with Carbs On Board is enabled in the SMB settings menu.

  3. Another option is to administer your initial typical bolus, then allow UAMs to handle any delayed glucose rise from fat and protein. You'll need to have both SMBs and UAMs enabled in settings. You also may find you need to increase your SMB or UAM Basal Minutes to accommodate this approach.

It's important to note that individual responses to high-fat meals can vary, so it may be helpful to experiment with different approaches and monitor your glucose levels closely to find the most effective strategy for managing your blood sugar levels during such meals.

docs.title: 6.4 Exercise
docs.text:
When participating in physical activities with Trio, it is advisable to implement the following strategies:

  • Most people use a dedicated override in Trio specifically designed for exercise. This override typically should incorporate a higher target glucose, and potentially a lower insulin delivery percentage to accommodate the impact of physical activity.
  • You can also just set a higher target value during exercise to mitigate the risk of hypoglycemia.
  • In Trio, you have the option to disable SMBs when using a higher target value. This prevents increasing insulin on board during exercise which is the primary cause of hypoglycemia.

As discussed in the general AID module, it is also recommended to reduce your mealtime insulin if you exercise within 3 hours of your meal.

Take advantage of the integration capabilities of Trio by connecting your dedicated Trio app to an Apple Watch. This allows for convenient monitoring of glucose levels and trend data during exercise.

It's important to remember that these recommendations serve as a starting point, and adjustments may be necessary based on individual responses to exercise. Regular monitoring, self-observation, and collaboration with healthcare professionals will help in refining insulin dosing and achieving optimal blood sugar management during physical activity with Trio.

docs.title: 6.5 Illness
docs.text:
During illness, it is generally recommended to keep Trio in Closed Loop Mode unless advised otherwise by your healthcare provider. Some people use a dedicated override during illness for example with a higher insulin percentage and a lower glucose target.

Please refer to the guidelines about illness discussed in the general module on automated insulin delivery systems.

docs.title: 6.6 Alcohol
docs.text:
When consuming alcohol while using Trio, it is important to take certain precautions to prevent hypoglycemia. Here are some recommendations:

You can adjust your target value to a higher level than usual, or set a lower % of your insulin delivery on an override, when consuming alcohol. This can help prevent the system from administering excessive insulin in response to any increase in glucose levels.

It is worth noting that simply consuming more carbohydrates is not an effective strategy to prevent hypoglycemia, as the automated insulin delivery system will compensate for any rise in glucose levels by increasing insulin delivery.

Always monitor your glucose levels closely when consuming alcohol and be prepared to make further adjustments as needed.

docs.title: 6.7 Travel
docs.text:
When traveling with Trio, it's important to be prepared and consider some specific factors. Here are some considerations for travel:

  1. Ensure that you have enough diabetes supplies, including insulin, infusion sets, sensors, test strips, and backup devices, to last throughout your trip. It's always better to be prepared with extra supplies in case of any unexpected situations.

  2. When traveling by air, you may need to make some adjustments. Keep in mind the following:

  3. The Bluetooth function of your mobile phone, pump, and transmitter can typically remain on during the flight. If you're instructed to activate Airplane Mode, there's no need to be concerned, as iPhones will still maintain Bluetooth connectivity even when Airplane Mode is enabled.

  4. During the flight, the Wi-Fi, 4G and 5G connectivity of your SIM card will not be available. This should not affect the operation of the Trio app as it will continue to function perfectly via Bluetooth. However, the data will not be forwarded to Nightscout until you regain a stable internet connection.
  5. The Trio app usually detects time zone changes automatically and adjusts the time accordingly, although depending on pump and CGM setup, jumps in time can lead to problems. However, it's a good practice to double-check the time settings in the app after arriving at your destination to ensure accurate data recording.

Remember to always comply with airline regulations and guidelines regarding the use of electronic devices, and inform the airline staff that you are wearing a medical device such as an insulin pump.

By taking necessary precautions and planning ahead, you can enjoy your travel while ensuring the continued functionality of your Trio system.

In this chapter, we covered how to manage special situations such as hypo/hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, exercise, illness, alcohol and travel with Trio. We provided recommendations and strategies to address these situations effectively. You've got this! Remember, individual responses may vary, so experimentation and monitoring are key. Seeking support from the Trio community and healthcare providers is also valuable.

By leveraging Trio and connecting with peers, individuals can navigate these situations and improve their diabetes management effectively.

docs.title: 7. Case report (2 min)
docs.text:
You've reached the end of the Trio course. By completing every chapter, you now have a thorough understanding of Trio's interface, configuration options, algorithm behaviour, and practical use. You've learned how the system adapts, how to interpret its settings, and how to guide users through the transition phase that many people experience when moving from other AID systems.

To close the module, we want to offer you a deeper, lived-experience perspective from clinicians who use Trio themselves.

Below this lesson, you'll find the link to the YouTube episode "Trio Open-Sourced Insulin Delivery: The Good, The Challenging, and The Differences."

In this podcast-style discussion, four highly experienced diabetes clinicians from Integrated Diabetes Services — all of them certified diabetes professionals and loopers themselves — talk openly about:

  • the features they personally appreciate in Trio
  • why some users prefer Trio over Loop
  • how autosens, SMBs, UAM, and dynamics behave in real life
  • the transition challenges they faced
  • how they adjusted settings to reduce lows or handle insulin resistance
  • how Trio performs in day-to-day life, exercise, meals, hormones, growth spurts, and steroid bursts
  • which patient groups benefit most from the system
  • and their practical strategies for basal testing, tuning the algorithm, and navigating the learning curve

This conversation brings the theory you learned in the module into real clinical context — with honest insights about what works, what doesn't, and what makes Trio different from other AID systems.

Congratulations on completing the Trio course.

You now have the knowledge to understand, support, and troubleshoot this open-sourced dynamic AID system with confidence.

Whether you use Trio yourself or guide others professionally, the insights gained here will strengthen your ability to deliver high-quality diabetes technology care.

Well done.

docs.title: twiist AID System
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. twiist according to the CARES paradigm
  • 3. Linking components together
  • 4. How twiist works
  • 5. Creating and interpreting reports
  • 6. Managing special situations
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: Transcript
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the twiist Automated Insulin Delivery (or AID) System, the first FDA-cleared version of one of the earlier versions of DIY Loop.

In this module, we will explore how the twiist AID system works step by step, and how it helps simplify and improve daily diabetes management.

The twiist AID system combines three essential components:

  • The twiist pump -- a small, tubed insulin pump that holds 300 units of insulin.
  • A compatible glucose sensor -- currently the FreeStyle Libre 3 Plus sensor, which streams glucose readings every minute.
  • And the twiist Loop algorithm - hosted on the twiist pump and controlled via the twiist app on an iPhone and/or a compatible Apple Watch.

Reports and data sharing is possible through Tidepool, allowing you and your healthcare team to review patterns, make informed decisions, and personalize your therapy. The twiist insiight app allows family and caregivers to follow the user in real-time.

The twiist AID system is FDA-cleared and backed by Sequel Med Tech. It includes robust safety features such as occlusion detection, glucose safety limits, and customizable alerts. Unlike open-source systems, updates and customer support are provided directly by the manufacturer.

Before we begin, please note that this module is for educational purposes only and is not a substitute for medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any changes to your diabetes treatment.

The videos were produced by Dr Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the AZ St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Gary Scheiner, a Certified Diabetes Care and Education Specialist from Integrated Diabetes Services in the US.

By the end of this module, you will have a clear understanding of how the twiist AID system works, how to set it up, how to navigate the app, and how to handle real-life situations such as exercise, meals, sick days, and travel.

docs.title: 2. twiist AID System According to the CARES Paradigm (X min)
Transcript
docs.text:
In this part of the module, we will look at the twiist AID system through the CARES paradigm.

We'll discuss how twiist calculates and adjusts automated insulin delivery, what happens if the system reverts to manual mode, important education points, how the system interacts with its compatible sensor, and how data can be shared with your healthcare team.

docs.title: 2.1 twiist AID according to CARES paradigm
#1 Calculate
docs.text:
The twiist Loop algorithm adjusts the preset basal insulin rate every 5 minutes, based on the glucose prediction for up to 6 hours. These predictions are calculated using multiple factors:

  • The Carbs On Board prediction estimates the effect of consumed carbohydrates on glucose levels using a dynamic carb absorption model.
  • The Insulin On Board prediction considers the impact of insulin already in the body.
  • The Glucose Momentum prediction considers the average rate of change in glucose levels between the current reading and the reading 15 to 30 minutes ago.
  • And the Retrospective Correction prediction takes into account the difference between the predicted and actual glucose levels over the past 30 minutes.

Every 5 minutes, the twiist Loop algorithm calculates the insulin requirement by comparing the predicted glucose level with the target glucose level, and divides the difference by the insulin sensitivity factor.

In the twiist app, the target glucose value---called the Correction Range---can be customized for each 30-minute time block and set between 87 and 180 mg/dL, or 4.8 and 10 mmol/L.

If the glucose level is predicted to fall below the Glucose Safety Limit over the next 3 hours, basal insulin delivery is suspended. This safety limit can be set between 67 and 110 mg/dl or 3.7 and 6.1 mmol/l.

docs.title: #2 Adjust
docs.text:
With twiist, users and their healthcare providers can personalize a wide set of parameters, including:

  • The Correction Range -- recommended to start at 100 to 115 mg/dl or 5.6 to 6.4 mmol/l.
  • A dedicated Pre-Meal Preset and Workout Preset.
  • Basal Rates -- adjustable in 30-minute intervals.
  • Carb Ratios & Insulin Sensitivities.
  • The Glucose Safety Limit -- recommended at 75 mg/dl or 4.2 mmol/l for adults and 80 mg/dl or 4.4 mmol/l for kids.
  • The Delivery Limits -- the maximum basal is recommended to start at 3 to 4 times the highest scheduled basal rate.

The twiist AID system also offers 2 dedicated glucose targets:

  • The Workout Preset with a higher glucose target range (150 to 170 mg/dl or 8.3 to 9.4 mmol/l) to prevent hypoglycemia while exercising.
  • The Pre-Meal Preset with a lower glucose target range (80 to 100 mg/dl or 4.4 to 5.6 mmol/l) to prevent post-meal hyperglycemia.

docs.title: #3 Revert
docs.text:
Loop will turn off or "revert to Manual Mode" if there is no connection to the sensor for more than 15 minutes. The Loop icon will turn red and your basal insulin delivery will return to your scheduled basal rate.

When Loop is off or in Manual Mode:

  • Your basal insulin is delivered according to your scheduled basal rate, or you can set a temporary basal.
  • You can give an insulin bolus using the integrated bolus calculator.
  • You can use the on-pump bolus-button to give an insulin bolus if enabled.

docs.title: #4 Educate
docs.text:
General education points for AID systems:

  • Use fewer carbohydrates to correct hypoglycemia.
  • Consider infusion set problems if blood glucose levels remain high without an apparent reason.
  • Trust the AID system and avoid unnecessary manual interventions.
  • Time meal boluses before meals.
  • Start Exercise Mode 1 to 2 hours before activity.
  • Respond to alarms.
  • Seek peer support for guidance and sharing experiences.

Additional education points specific to twiist:

  • Wear your pump and CGM on the same side of your body to minimise communication errors.
  • Always keep your iPhone charged and within Bluetooth range of your pump.
  • Always keep Notifications and Critical Alerts turned on in your iPhone settings.
  • Do not close the twiist app -- leave it open in the background.
  • Take advantage of the Pre-Meal Preset before meals.
  • Do not use pump when swimming or bathing.

docs.title: #5 Sensor
docs.text:
At the moment of making this course, the twiist AID system is only compatible with the FreeStyle Libre 3 Plus sensor.

The FreeStyle Libre 3 Plus sensor can only be used with the twiist app, and not with the FreeStyle Libre 3 app or Libre 3 receiver. This means that values will not go to LibreView nor will they be shareable via LibreLinkUp.

docs.title: #6 Share
docs.text:
Data from twiist can be seamlessly uploaded to Tidepool, where you and your healthcare team can view reports on insulin delivery, glucose patterns, carbs, and settings.

Your family and loved ones can use the insiight app to follow you in real-time. However, at the moment of making this course, the insiight app does not offer alerts.

docs.title: 2.2 Indications
docs.text:
The twiist AID system is FDA-cleared for people with diabetes aged 6 years and older who require insulin. It is compatible with Humalog, Novolog or Novorapid U100 insulin.

Please note that the twiist AID system does not have a CE label at this time, and it is not indicated for use by anyone who is pregnant, on dialysis, or critically ill.

The twiist AID system is fully dependent on an iPhone (with iOS 18 or higher).

docs.title: 2.3 Software Updates
docs.text: Unlike open-source AID systems that require manual rebuilds, twiist provides updates automatically via the App Store and official firmware releases.

docs.title: Transcript
docs.text:
In this instructional video, we will guide you through the process of linking the components of the twiist AID system:

  • Linking the Libre 3 Plus sensor to the twiist app.
  • Linking the twiist pump to the twiist app.
  • Connecting your account to Tidepool for data sharing.
  • Using your Apple Watch to control the system.
  • And using the insiight app for real-time follow-up by caregivers.

docs.title: 3.1 Linking Libre 3 Plus sensor to twiist App
docs.text:
Be aware that the twiist AID system cannot use a sensor that is already in session at start up. You must start a new sensor.

On your twiist app, tap Add CGM in the CGM status or tap Settings and Add CGM, and choose Start New Sensor.

Choose a sensor site on the back of the upper arm, at least 2.5 cm or 1 inch from an insulin injection site. Start by washing your hands, wash the application site with plain soap and water, wipe dry with a clean towel, clean with an alcohol wipe, unscrew the cap of the inserter, place the loaded inserter over the prepared site, and push down firmly to apply the FreeStyle Libre 3 Plus sensor on your body.

Tap next, and then Scan new sensor. Hold the top of your iPhone very close to the sensor.

docs.title: 3.2 Linking the twiist Pump to the twiist App
docs.text: A step by step process on how to link the pump base to the twiist app is discussed in the module about the twiist pump. Be aware that you need wifi or cellular data to do this.

docs.title: 3.3 Connecting to Tidepool
docs.text:
To connect your app to Tidepool, go to the settings and select Data Sharing. Here, you can login with your Tidepool account credentials.

Once linked, your glucose, insulin delivery, and carb data are automatically uploaded to Tidepool when internet is available.

Be aware that Tidepool is HIPAA-compliant and claims GDPR compliance, but it is not officially FDA-cleared. Before using it in a clinical setting, approval from your data protection manager is recommended.

docs.title: 3.4 Using your Apple Watch to control the system
docs.text:
1. Pair your Apple Watch with your iPhone.
  2. On your iPhone, open the Watch app and install the twiist companion app.
  3. Once installed, you'll be able to view glucose, insulin status, and recent trends.
  4. From the watch, you can enter carbs, confirm bolus recommendations and activate Pre-Meal or Workout ranges.

Remember that the Apple Watch relies on your iPhone for communication with the pump. If your iPhone is out of Bluetooth range, actions requested from the watch will not take effect until the iPhone reconnects.

docs.title: 3.5 Using the insiight App
docs.text:
With the twiist insiight app you can follow multiple twiist users if the user grants you permission.

The insiight app also offers a widget that you can add to your iPhone homepage, making it possible to see your glucose value simply by swiping your lock screen to the right.

docs.title: Transcript
docs.text:
Welcome to this video on the functionality of the twiist automated insulin delivery (AID) system.

The twiist system can operate with Loop automation on or off.

  • When Loop is ON, the twiist AID system will automatically adjust your basal insulin delivery in response to your glucose as often as every 5 minutes, in an effort to bring your glucose into your Correction Range.
  • When Loop is OFF, your basal will be delivered based on your scheduled basal rates.

docs.title: Starting Loop
docs.text:
Once your sensor and pump are paired, you can turn on Loop via the settings.

If Loop is on, the twiist Status icon is a closed circle, the glucose prediction is projected on the charts and the basal insulin delivery is adjusted every 5 minutes.

docs.title: Using Workout Preset
docs.text: The twiist app offers an exercise feature, called the Workout Preset, to prevent hypoglycemia during exercise. You can activate it via a heart-shaped icon on the bottom of the home screen. Once you tap it, you can choose to keep the workout preset active for 1 hour, 2 hours or until you choose to turn it off.

docs.title: Using Pre-Meal Preset
docs.text: The twiist app also offers a more aggressive Pre-Meal Preset, to prevent hyperglycemia after meals. You can use the Pre-Meal Preset by tapping the second icon on the left on the bottom of the homescreen when Loop is on. The Pre-Meal Preset will stay active until you enter carbs.

docs.title: Bolus Delivery
docs.text:
When Loop is active, you can enter carbohydrates into the bolus calculator to receive an insulin dose recommendation. The default carb absorption time is set to 3 hours but can be adjusted to anywhere between 30 minutes and 23.5 hours.

To deliver a correction bolus without eating, tap the triangle icon on the status bar to see if a bolus is recommended.

docs.title: Suspending Insulin
docs.text: You can temporarily stop insulin delivery through the app if your infusion set is disconnected or during special situations. To do this, tap the pump status icon on the home screen and tap suspend delivery.

docs.title: 4.2 Loop Off
docs.text:
When Loop is off, the twiist Loop icon will be an open circle, there will be no glucose prediction and insulin delivery is not adjusted every 5 minutes.

In Loop Off Mode the pump delivers your scheduled basal profile, or you can choose for a temporary basal. You can still enter carbs, bolus for meals, and give corrections with the integrated bolus calculator.

docs.title: Transcript
docs.text:
Welcome to this video on creating and interpreting reports with the twiist AID system.

In this video, we will cover the various reports available in Tidepool and how to interpret these reports effectively.

docs.title: 5.1 Types of reports
docs.text: Tidepool provides a modern, web-based interface that works on both desktop and mobile devices. Once your twiist app is linked to your Tidepool account, data uploads automatically and can be reviewed anytime.

docs.title: Basics View
docs.text: The Basics view shows summary statistics including daily time in range, average blood glucose, sensor usage percentage, average daily total insulin breakdowns, average daily carbs, and GMI (glucose management indicator).

docs.title: Trends View
docs.text: The Trends view overlays blood glucose meter or CGM data over a 24-hour period to help you and your patients better understand where blood glucose trends and variability are currently occurring.

docs.title: Daily View
docs.text: The Daily view syncs up all blood glucose meter, CGM, and insulin pump data. Summary statistics include Time In Range, Average Blood Glucose, Total Insulin, Total Carbs, Standard Deviation and Coefficient of Variation.

docs.title: Print Data
docs.text: You can click the print button to generate a PDF that includes the Basics view, the last six days from the Daily view, the last 30 days from the Blood Glucose Log section and the insulin pump profile settings.

docs.title: 1. Assess Glycemic Information
docs.text: • Check Time in Range over the last 2-4 weeks. Aim for at least 70% in range and less than 4% below range.

docs.title: 2. Optimize AID Settings
docs.text:
• Review the AGP for patterns of hypoglycemia, then evaluate patterns of hyperglycemia.
  • If patterns are linked to meals, check carb counting accuracy and consider adjusting the carb ratio.
  • If patterns occur after correction boluses, review the insulin sensitivity factor.
  • Review basal insulin delivery patterns. If consistently modulating up, increase scheduled basal rate by 10-20%. If consistently modulating down, decrease by 10-20%.
  • If there is a pattern of plateau of basal insulin delivery, consider increasing Max Basal rate.

docs.title: 3. Guide Behavioral Recommendations
docs.text:
• Use the Daily Report to check pre-meal bolusing, carb entry timing, overriding the bolus recommendations and usage of Workout and Pre-Meal Presets.
  • Confirm consistent sensor wear time and time in Automated Mode.

docs.title: 4. Review Pump Settings and Emergency Plans
docs.text:
• Record the basal rates, carb ratio, insulin sensitivity, correction range, and glucose safety limit in the electronic health record.
  • Establish an emergency plan with backup insulin pens in case of pump or system failure.

docs.title: Transcript
docs.text:
In this video, we will provide practical tips for handling special situations when using the twiist AID system.

We will review strategies for managing hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, high-fat meals, exercise, illness, alcohol, and travel.

docs.title: 6.1 Hypoglycemia
docs.text:
With an AID system, fewer carbohydrates are needed to correct a low compared to traditional pump therapy. This is because the system suspends or reduces insulin earlier when it predicts an approaching low.

You can check the Insulin on Board (IOB) in the twiist app before treating a low. If you have little insulin active, smaller carb amounts are usually sufficient.

When eating carbs to treat hypoglycemia, it is recommended to add the carbs in the twiist app, so the algorithm can take these carbs into account.

docs.title: 6.2 Hyperglycemia
docs.text:
For mild hyperglycemia, it is often best to let the twiist system handle the correction automatically.

  • If needed, you can give a correction bolus from the app via the triangle icon.
  • Using the Pre-Meal target or a lower correction range can also help bring glucose down faster.
  • If you experience prolonged or severe hyperglycemia, always check for pump or infusion site issues. "If in doubt, change it out."

docs.title: 6.3 High-Fat Meals
docs.text:
Meals high in fat and protein (such as pizza or creamy pasta) can cause delayed glucose rises.

  • When Loop is on, you can use a longer absorption time in the bolus calculator to spread insulin delivery more evenly.
  • When Loop is off, you can use an extended or dual wave bolus.
  • Some people enter "extra carbs" to cover for fat and protein, especially if they are on a low-carb diet.

docs.title: 6.4 Exercise
docs.text:
Aerobic physical activity lowers glucose levels more quickly and increases insulin sensitivity.

  • Start the Workout Preset 1-2 hours before and during exercise.
  • Reduce the meal bolus with 25-30% if a meal is taken within 3 hours of exercise.
  • Take a small snack if your glucose drops below 126 mg/dl or 7 mmol/l.

docs.title: 6.5 Illness
docs.text:
Illness often causes higher glucose levels due to stress hormones.

  • Keep twiist in Auto Mode or Loop On unless advised otherwise by your healthcare provider.
  • Consider using frequent correction boluses or a lower correction range if the illness leads to hyperglycemia.
  • Check frequently for ketones if glucose remains elevated despite corrections.
  • Always hydrate well.

docs.title: 6.6 Alcohol
docs.text: When consuming alcohol, consider taking proactive steps to prevent hypoglycemia, such as activating the Workout Preset. Increasing carbohydrate intake is not an effective approach.

docs.title: 6.7 Travel
docs.text:
When traveling, always bring extra supplies of sensors, pods, chargers, and backup insulin pens.

Flying with twiist:

  • Bluetooth communication between your iPhone, Libre 3 Plus, and twiist pump can remain active during flights.
  • If airplane mode is required, keep Bluetooth on so the pump and CGM remain connected.
  • Internet access is not required for twiist to function, but Tidepool uploads will pause until you are back online.

Time zone changes: The twiist pump time and date automatically synchronize with your iPhone through the twiist app.

docs.title: Transcript
docs.text:
In this final chapter, we'd like to share a valuable video resource that highlights the practical use of the twiist AID system.

In this official overview, the twiist team demonstrates how the twiist pump and app works. The walkthrough includes examples of how twiist manages daily routines, responds to glucose changes, and supports safe diabetes management.

We encourage you to also explore real-life perspectives from people using twiist. These insights provide a practical look at how twiist fits into everyday life and can help you build confidence in using the system.

Congratulations on completing this module on the twiist AID system! You now have a solid understanding of its features, functions, and best practices.

Remember, diabetes management is a continuous journey. Stay connected to the twiist community, use available learning resources, and don't hesitate to reach out to your healthcare team for guidance.

docs.title: Continuous Glucose / Ketone Monitoring (CGM / CKM)
docs.text: Select a module from the sidebar to get started.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alarms
  • 5. Sharing data
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM! In the videos ahead, we’ll explore the key features of this sensor and how it works with an AI-enabled predictive algorithm to support proactive diabetes management.

The Accu-Chek SmartGuide solution consists of three elements: the sensor, the SmartGuide app, and the Predict app. The sensor, which has a diameter of 3 cm and weighs about 5 grams, is placed on the back of your upper arm using an all-in-one applicator and lasts for up to 14 days. After a one-hour warm-up, it sends real-time glucose readings every five minutes to the SmartGuide app on your smartphone via Bluetooth. The Predict app, with its AI-powered algorithm, then uses these glucose values and other available information to detect your glucose patterns and estimate your levels over the next 30 minutes, 2 hours, and during the night, helping you stay ahead of potential changes. The Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM solution is intended for individuals 18 years of age and older.

In our "Master Module on Glucose Sensors", we covered the essential principles of CGM use, from appropriate reliance on sensor readings and management of issues such as disconnections and allergic reactions. We now turn to in-depth training on the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM and share practical strategies for getting the most from the device in daily life.

We’ve created this module to help healthcare providers and individuals with diabetes use the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM as effectively as possible. However, please remember that these videos should not be taken as medical advice. Always consult your healthcare provider before adjusting your treatment plan.

This module was produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr. Liesbeth Van Huffel, an endocrinologist in the AZORG Hospital in Aalst in Belgium. With their expertise and insights, you can expect a well-rounded assessment of the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM.

This material is produced independently of Roche, the sensor manufacturer. Thus, you can be confident of an unbiased and comprehensive discussion of the SmartGuide sensor’s strengths and limitations.

Upon completing this module, you’ll be well-equipped with the knowledge and skills to confidently use the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM. You’ll learn how to place and start the sensor, set alarms, share glucose data, and connect the sensor with other devices. You’ll also get expert tips for safe and optimal use of the CGM. If you’re a healthcare provider, you’ll strengthen your ability to support SmartGuide sensor users. If you’re a sensor user, you’ll benefit from concrete, easy-to-follow advice for integrating the CGM into your daily life. Let’s embark on this meaningful journey together. Wishing you all the best!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (4 min)
docs.text: Welcome to the first lesson of the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM module. In this video, we’ll demonstrate how to apply and remove the SmartGuide sensor. Let’s begin.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text:
Prepare the following:

  • an Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM pack, which contains an applicator with a built-in sensor, a product leaflet;

  • and an alcohol wipe.

docs.title: 2.2 Applying the sensor
docs.text:
Start by downloading the Accu-Chek SmartGuide app from the Apple App Store or Google Play Store. Then, open the app. You will need to create an Accu-Chek account or sign in to your account if you already have one. You will need to enter some information about yourself. Next, consent to the terms to use the app. Finally, verify your email address and sign in to the app. To personalize the app, select your units of measurement. Then select your target range. Next, the app will ask you to grant it some vital permissions and to turn on certain settings. The names of these differ depending on which operating system your phone has. Enabling these features allows the app to send you alarms and notifications regardless of settings like Mute or Do Not Disturb. Now that your account and profile are set up, you can apply your first sensor and pair it to your phone.

Disinfect your skin before applying the sensor. Make sure to let it dry completely before you proceed. Flip the pull tab to open the device. Unscrew the blue twist cap. You’ll hear a sound that indicates that the device is new and has not been previously opened. If it has already been opened, discard it immediately and get a new one. Look inside of the applicator to check that the needle is pointing straight upwards, but make sure not to touch it. If you notice any damage, discard the device and use a new one. Do not put the blue twist cap back on after you have removed it. Make sure to hold on to the cap because you will need the PIN printed on it to pair the sensor with your smartphone. You are now ready to apply the sensor. Place your hand on your opposite shoulder to make it easier to apply the sensor. Reach underneath your arm and place the applicator on your skin. Firmly press the applicator onto your skin until you hear it click. Remove the applicator without turning or wiggling it. Smooth the adhesive to make sure it is applied properly.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: You can wear your CGM sensor for up to 14 days. When it is time for you to replace it, the Accu-Chek SmartGuide app will alert you. Find the flat edge of the sensor and carefully start peeling it off by pulling outwards. Make sure all sensor parts have been removed from your skin. If the sensing element has remained in your skin, contact your health care professional. To protect the environment and to safeguard others from harm, dispose of the sensor according to your local medical waste guidelines.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• Do not apply skincare or hygiene products (such as insect repellent or sunscreen) on the sensor or around the application area, as these may damage the device or loosen the adhesive.

  • If only the outer edges of the adhesive pad begin to lift from the skin, the sensor should still work correctly. If any part of the adhesive pad directly under the sensor comes away from the skin, do not try to press it back down or tape it in place. A reattached sensor may not function properly. Instead, apply a new sensor.

  • Some companies offer dedicated stickers to make your CGM look more fashionable. These are completely optional, of course. You can also find additional patches that can help improve adhesion.

In this video, you learned the correct procedure for placing and removing the Accu-Chek SmartGuide sensor. By adhering to these guidelines, you help ensure dependable monitoring over time and gain trustworthy glucose data to guide your treatment decisions.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (12.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on starting the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM. In this video, we’ll guide you through starting the sensor and navigating the SmartGuide and Predict apps. Plus, we’ll share tips for safe and effective use of the CGM as part of your daily routine. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 3.1 Starting the sensor
docs.text:
Open the app to get started. If this is the first time you are pairing, the app will prompt you to Pair Now. Otherwise, simply tap the Pair New Sensor button on the home screen to start the process. The app will then walk you through the necessary steps. Tap Search to look for your sensor device. Depending on your smartphone model, you may need to grant the app permission to use Bluetooth. Once Bluetooth permission is granted, the app will begin to look for your sensor. Make sure the sensor is within range. After completing the search, the app will display a list of available devices. On the twist cap that you saved earlier, look for the serial number of your sensor. Tap the matching serial number in the app and then tap Next. You will be asked to enter the six-digit PIN found on the blue twist cap. Enter the PIN and select Pair. The app and the sensor will take a little while to pair in order to form a secure connection. This is normal. After the sensor has warmed up to your body, you’ll need to calibrate it to ensure your glucose values are as accurate as possible.

The calibration process takes place in two stages. The first calibration prompt appears 12 hours after you place and start the sensor. The second prompt shows up 30 minutes to 3 hours after the first calibration.

When the app notifies you 12 hours after sensor application, tap Calibrate Now. Check your blood glucose with your meter and enter the result on the Calibrate screen. The value must fall between 40 and 400 mg/dL, or 2 and 22 mmol/l, and should be based on a reading that is no more than 3 minutes old. After typing in the result, tap Save and double-check that the number is correct, as it cannot be edited later. Tap Confirm to continue or Cancel to go back. If an incorrect value is confirmed, the system can no longer guarantee accurate performance; in that case, you should discard the current sensor, insert a new one, and repeat the previous steps. When calibration is accepted, a confirmation message appears; tap OK to return to the Home screen. If the calibration fails, wait at least 15 minutes, then repeat the procedure with a fresh blood glucose reading from your meter.

Calibrate the sensor whenever the app prompts you to do so. This will occur only twice per sensor, as described earlier. Enter the second calibration value approximately 30 minutes after completing the first calibration. Tap Calibrate and repeat steps 2–4.

After each successful calibration, the Home screen will reappear. Once two successful calibrations have been completed, the system automatically switches from Trend Mode to Therapy Mode for the remainder of the sensor wear period. From that point on, the CGM values can be used to support therapy-related decisions.

If the sensor is not calibrated, the system will remain in Trend Mode. Glucose values will still be displayed and can be used to follow glucose trends and as a general reference, but not for therapy decisions.

docs.title: 3.2. The Accu-Chek SmartGuide app
docs.text:
With the Accu-Chek SmartGuide app, you can view your glucose values and record insulin doses, carbohydrate intake, and daily activities.

The Home screen presents your current CGM reading in text and numbers, shows the unit of measurement, and displays a trend arrow indicating how your glucose is changing. Both the color of the glucose value and the direction of the trend arrow serve as visual cues.

The sensor’s operating mode appears beneath the CGM value. A sensor that has not been calibrated yet operates in Trend Mode, while a calibrated sensor runs in Therapy Mode.

In the top-right corner of the screen, you can see the status of alarms and notifications, as well as the icon that lets you switch to the Accu-Chek SmartGuide Predict app. We’ll talk more about the Predict app later.

The Home graph displays your recent glucose values in relation to your target range.

The message area provides a brief summary of important current information. For example, it can show the remaining warm-up time after pairing a new sensor. You can tap the buttons in this area to respond to the specific message. In the example, the message area shows the latest logbook entries.

In the lower part of the screen, you'll find navigation elements, namely the Home, Logbook, Add Entry, Graphs, and Menu icons. The icons appear in blue to show which section of the app you are currently viewing.

Record and track your insulin, meals, and other key details in the Logbook to see how these factors influence your glucose levels. You can use it to enter manual blood glucose meter readings, carbohydrate intake, insulin injections, and personal notes to add context to your data. To add a new entry, tap Add Entry in the tab bar on the Home screen. Follow the prompts to fill in the required fields, and include any extra details you want to remember in the Notes section. To edit an entry, tap Logbook, then select the item you want to modify or delete.

Tap the Graphs icon and choose from different data displays of your glucose data.

The Trend Graph offers an expanded version of the SmartGuide trend graph shown on the Home screen. This graph presents your continuous glucose readings from the last 6, 12, or 24 hours. By swiping the graph to the right, you can look back at earlier data. You can also tap the Calendar icon in the top right corner to display your glucose information for a specific day. Logbook entries can be layered onto the graph to provide additional context.

The Time in Range view shows how much time your glucose values have stayed within the target range, including your personal targets, over the last 7, 14, or 28 days. The main aim is to maximize the amount of time spent In Range, represented by the green section of the graph.

In the Statistics section, you can review your CGM usage and key data for the past 7, 14, or 28 days. This includes your active CGM time, average glucose values, and your Glucose Management Indicator (GMI).

docs.title: 3.3 The Accu-Chek SmartGuide Predict app
docs.text:
The Accu-Chek SmartGuide Predict app can help you forecast your upcoming glucose values, such as the likelihood of highs and lows, and identify glucose patterns and trends, so you can decide what action to take for your diabetes management. Installing and setting up the app only takes a few minutes. Let’s walk through the steps together. First, you will need to download the Accu-Chek SmartGuide Predict app from the Apple App Store or Google Play Store. When you open the app for the first time, you’ll see an overview of its key benefits, feature highlights, and a disclaimer. Please make sure to review this carefully. Next, sign in to the Accu-Chek account that you created for the Accu-Chek SmartGuide app. Before using the Predict app, you must enable its notifications on your smartphone and provide some details. This will allow the app to keep you informed of possible glucose changes, and notify you when you are at risk of leaving your target range. Every time you sign in, the Predict app will automatically sync with your Accu-Chek SmartGuide app and share your latest data and settings.

The Predict app Home screen is divided into several sections.

Tap the SmartGuide app icon to switch to the Accu-Chek SmartGuide app Home screen while the Predict app stays open in the background.

Tap the information icon to read more about how the graph presents your glucose predictions.

The graph displays your glucose values from the past hour, your current level, and the predicted range for the next two hours. The dotted line within the prediction area shows your projected average glucose value. The further ahead the prediction goes, the less precise it becomes, which is illustrated by the widening vertical bars. If any prediction error messages occur, they will appear in this graph area.

Your current glucose level is shown along with its unit of measurement.

Key information, such as your risk of a night-time low or a low glucose event in the next 30 minutes, appears beneath the graph or as a notification. If the app identifies patterns in your glucose levels, they are also displayed in this section.

At the bottom of the screen, the Home, Patterns, and Menu icons help you navigate the app. As in the SmartGuide app, the icon for the section you are viewing is highlighted in blue.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• After inserting a new sensor, pair it with the app within 30 minutes. If pairing is delayed beyond 30 minutes, the sensor may take longer to connect in order to conserve battery. The same 30-minute rule applies after a lost connection – reconnect the sensor to the app within this time frame.

  • The sensor can communicate with a mobile device over a distance of up to 10 meters (or 33 feet) in a clear line of sight. This range may be shorter depending on the type of mobile device and surrounding conditions (for example, other nearby electronic devices).

  • If the link to your sensor is interrupted, you will stop receiving glucose readings and alarms until the connection is restored. The sensor can store up to 8 hours of data if it cannot send readings to the app. To prevent losing information, make sure the data is transferred before the sensor battery is depleted.

  • Repeated connection drops between the sensor and the app may shorten the sensor’s battery life. To help maintain a stable connection, keep your sensor and mobile device close together.

  • Try not to calibrate in environments that are extremely hot, extremely cold, or where the temperature is changing quickly.

  • If you accidentally confirm an incorrect calibration value, it cannot be undone. In this case, you must remove the sensor and start again with a new one.

  • Calibration is only possible during the scheduled calibration process. Once this process has passed, the sensor cannot be calibrated later.

  • The sensor is water-resistant and can be worn while bathing, swimming, or showering. However, do not submerge it for more than 60 minutes or deeper than 1 meter (or 3.3 feet).

  • Do not bring portable radio frequency communication equipment (including accessories such as antenna cables and external antennas) closer than 30 cm (or 12 inches) to the sensor, as this may interfere with its performance.

  • Remove the sensor during an MRI, CT or X-ray. Some centers may allow the sensor to remain in place during CT scans or X-rays; check with your healthcare professional in advance.

  • If the app fails to show glucose values for more than 20 minutes and there is no notification or alarm recorded in the event log, contact customer support.

  • Certain substances can interfere with sensor accuracy and may falsely increase the CGM readings shown in the app, including:

  • more than 500 mg of vitamin C per day taken orally, or any amount given intravenously

  • Supplements containing gentisic acid

  • and Methyldopa

  • When your CGM reading is outside the device’s measuring range (above 400 mg/dL or 22.2 mmol/L or below 40 mg/dL or 2.2 mmol/L), the app will show “HI” or “LO” instead of a numeric value.

  • Logbook entries that include glucose values used for calibration cannot be edited or deleted.

  • Once a logbook entry is deleted, it cannot be restored.

  • The Predict app relies on a continuous stream of data from your Accu-Chek SmartGuide device to function properly.

  • Ensure you have a stable internet connection, that the CGM app is installed on your smartphone, and that it is paired with an active CGM sensor.

  • Keep your smartphone within communication range of the device and stay signed in to your Accu-Chek account in both apps.

  • Install new app updates whenever they are released.

In this video, you learned how to get the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM up and running, from pairing the sensor with your smartphone to becoming familiar with the SmartGuide and Predict apps' main features. As you guide your patients through their early days with the sensor, be mindful that technical problems can arise, including connection errors, questionable sensor readings, or glucose prediction issues. Go through the available troubleshooting options and reach out to customer support when necessary. Your expertise, patience, and dedication are key to helping your patients use their CGM to its full potential.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (12.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM. In this video, we'll discuss the various alarms the sensor can issue and show you how to customize them in the SmartGuide and Predict apps. Let’s get started.

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
The Accu-Chek SmartGuide app can alert you when your glucose goes very high, low, or very low. The Very High Glucose Alarm notifies you when your glucose value rises above your very high glucose limit. By default, this is set to 250 mg/dL or 13.9 mmol/L. The Low Glucose Alarm alerts you when your glucose value drops below your low glucose limit. The default low glucose threshold is 70 mg/dL or 3.9 mmol/L. The Very Low Glucose Alarm warns you when your glucose value falls below 54 mg/dL or 3.0 mmol/L. For safety reasons, this very low limit cannot be changed.

You will only receive one alarm when your glucose first goes above the very high limit or below the low limit. In contrast, if your glucose remains below the very low limit, the app will continue to send a glucose alarm every 5 minutes until your value rises above that level or you dismiss the alarm.

Glucose alarms are active around the clock unless you have enabled sleep alarms. Sleep alarms let you choose different alarm thresholds during the night so that you are not disturbed unnecessarily while sleeping.

Glucose alarms are turned on by default, although they can be switched off. However, disabling them is not recommended for safety reasons.

The “Do not disturb” override makes sure you still receive important glucose notifications even when your phone is silenced or set to Do Not Disturb. To enable this, tap Menu, tap “Do not disturb”, and turn them on in your phone settings so that you can respond quickly to glucose excursions.

In addition, you can receive a reminder when your CGM sensor is due to expire and should be replaced. Tap Menu, tap Sensor Expiration Reminders, and select 24 hours before, 2 hours before, or both.

You will also receive a notification if the app loses connection to the sensor. To adjust this setting, go to Menu, then tap Sensor Connection Loss. This alert is enabled by default.

The SmartGuide app also uses various other notifications to keep you informed about sensor status, issues, and required actions. These may appear as error messages, maintenance messages, warnings, informational messages, or reminders.

On the other hand, the Predict app focuses on forecasting your glucose levels. Its purpose is to give you advanced insights so you can act early and help keep your glucose within your target range, instead of responding only after a high or low has occurred.

The Predict app offers three types of glucose predictions: Glucose Predict, Low Glucose Predict, and Night Low Predict.

Glucose Predict displays an estimate of your glucose levels for the next 2 hours. To access this feature, you need at least 1 hour of CGM data, an active internet connection on your device, and a current glucose reading that is above your low glucose threshold of 70 mg/dL (or 3.9 mmol/L).

On the graph, the large green dot and the number shown beneath it both represent your current glucose value. To the left of this dot, a solid line shows your glucose readings from the previous 60 minutes. To the right, a dotted line indicates the best estimate of your future glucose values. Behind this dotted line, vertical grey bars illustrate the degree of uncertainty in the prediction. As with any prediction, the further into the future it extends, the less certain it becomes. This is shown by the length of the grey bars: longer bars mean higher uncertainty. Your real glucose values may still end up outside these grey bars. Glucose values are color-coded: readings in your target range are shown in green, while values above the range appear in yellow or orange, and low values are marked in red or dark red.

Low Glucose Predict alerts you when a low glucose event is likely within the next 30 minutes. When the system detects an increased likelihood of low glucose, it sends a push notification. You can adjust the glucose threshold that triggers this notification between 60 and 100 mg/dL (or 3.3 to 5.6 mmol/L) by changing the Low Glucose alarm setting in the Therapy Settings of the Accu-Chek SmartGuide app. The feature is switched on by default but can be disabled via Notification Settings in the Menu options of the Predict app. When you tap the notification, additional details and suggested actions are displayed.

The Low Glucose Predict function will only operate if your low glucose alarm in the Accu-Chek SmartGuide app is set between 60 mg/dL (or 3.3 mmol/L) and 100 mg/dL (or 5.5 mmol/L). If your setting is above this range, the Predict feature will not be active.

Night Low Predict shows how likely you are to experience low glucose during the night. You can receive a notification if your risk of night-time low glucose is high or very high. Set your usual bedtime for the week, and, as long as notifications are enabled, a reminder will appear on your lock screen sometime between 9 PM and 2 AM. During this time window, the Home screen displays your risk of low glucose over the next 7 hours of the night. For this feature, “low glucose” is always defined as a value below 70 mg/dL (or 3.9 mmol/L), regardless of your personal Low Glucose threshold.

If your risk is different in the first 3.5 hours of the night compared with the last 3.5 hours, the prediction will highlight the part of the night with the greater risk. Predictions are generally more reliable for the earlier hours of the night, as short-term forecasts are more accurate.

Night Low Predict uses three risk levels, each represented by a different color. Green indicates a normal or average risk of low glucose. In this case, you can get ready for bed as you usually do. Yellow signals a higher-than-normal risk, and red indicates a very high risk of low glucose. When a high or very high risk is displayed, you may choose to take action, such as eating long-acting carbohydrates combined with protein and fat and/or adjusting your basal insulin or basal rate before going to sleep. The specific steps you take should be based on your diabetes treatment plan, prior experience, and whether the low is expected in the first or second half of the night. If you are unsure, consult your healthcare provider for guidance.

Finally, the Glucose Patterns feature highlights repeating trends in your glucose levels. A pattern is defined as an event that happens more than once within the same day or during the same calendar week.

When the Predict app has sufficient data to recognize patterns in your glucose values, these patterns are displayed and can be tapped to view more detailed information. They may appear very differently depending on how often they occur and what is causing them, and are represented using various icons and colors.

Patterns from the last 24 hours are shown on the Home screen. If no patterns are identified, the Predict app will display a message to let you know. When a new pattern is detected, you’ll receive a notification; tap it to see further details.

To explore pattern graphs, tap the Graph icon in the bottom menu, select a pattern and switch the view to Week or Month. Possible causes for each pattern are listed below the graph, and you can tap these causes to see suggestions for changes you might consider.

Some patterns may be improved or resolved by adjusting your behavior. Once a pattern has been resolved, the Predict app moves it to the Past tab, where you can review all your previous patterns if you need to refer to them later.

Your Logbook entries play an important role in identifying patterns. For instance, patterns related to meals can only be detected if you consistently log your meals in the SmartGuide app.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the SmartGuide and Predict apps
docs.text:
To set up your glucose alarms, open the Menu and select Glucose Alarms. Use the toggles to switch specific alarms on or off. If you enable sleep alarms, you can also adjust your Bedtime (start time) and Wake-Up Time (end time) in 15-minute increments. Next, enter the glucose limits you want for each alarm, then tap Save to apply your settings.

To enable or adjust Critical Alerts, Sensor Expiration Reminders, or Sensor Connection Loss, tap Menu and toggle the relevant settings on or off.

In the Predict app, the Glucose Predict, Low Glucose Predict and Night Low Predict feature are enabled by default. You can disable them via the menu.

You can also set reminders, including push notifications, to help you actively work on particular patterns you want to improve. By creating a reminder for a detected pattern, the app can warn you ahead of time so that alarms may help you prevent the same event from recurring.

A summary of the previous week’s patterns is generated automatically every Monday. If you would like to be notified when this summary is ready, go again to Patterns via the bottom navigation, tap Enable on the Weekly Summary card, turn on the notification toggle, and tap Next. Then select Notify me daily at and adjust the time at which you want to receive the notification, tap Next, and confirm on the final screen.

docs.title: To ensure you receive CGM alarms, understand how your phone operates and review its settings regularly to confirm that:
The CGM app is open and running.
Power-saving or battery optimisation modes are turned OFF.
The volume is set high enough.
Do Not Disturb or Focus mode is turned OFF.
Your phone stays close to you.
The app alerts you mainly through sound. It may also use other types of notifications, such as vibration or visual signals. The alert sound volume follows the sound volume settings of your phone.

When the sensor is operating in Trend Mode, CGM readings may be less precise. This can lead to alarms sounding even if your actual glucose is within range, or no alarms being triggered even if your true glucose is too high or too low.
Make sure that both the Very Low Glucose Alarm and the Low Glucose Alarm are switched on in the Accu-Chek SmartGuide app during the night.

The Predict app bases its forecasts on your CGM readings and your logged carbohydrate and insulin entries. Any actions you take in the following two hours — such as eating, taking insulin, or exercising — are not included in the prediction. A 2-hour prediction is mainly intended to inform you when the predicted change is more than 45 minutes away, so you can prepare for possible future steps such as checking your glucose more often, planning insulin doses, or keeping carbohydrates within reach. When high or low glucose is predicted within the next 45 minutes, you should actively consider what actions you may need to take.
An active internet connection is required for the Low Glucose Predict feature. If your device is offline or in airplane mode, you will not receive a “Low glucose soon!” notification.
