You may not hear or notice notifications while you are asleep. To reduce the risk of night-time hypoglycemia, use the Night Low Predict feature and take preventive steps before going to bed. You can also enable a Low Glucose Alarm in the Accu-Chek SmartGuide app for additional safety.

Alarm notifications cannot be guaranteed in every situation because there are inherent limitations to any alarm system. Do not depend solely on alarms, as this could result in missing serious low or high glucose events. Open the app regularly to review your glucose levels as advised by your healthcare provider, or whenever you suspect your glucose may be too low or too high. Never ignore symptoms of low or high blood glucose.

In this video, we took a closer look at Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM alarms. Tailoring these alarms to your needs allows you to turn potential issues in your diabetes management into clear prompts for action, helping you feel more secure and in control of your health.
5. Sharing data (5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM. In this video, we’ll explain how you can share your sensor data with your healthcare provider and generate reports with the Predict app. Let's jump right in.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your healthcare provider
docs.text:
The Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM is fully integrated with the Accu-Chek Care platform, giving healthcare professionals continuous access to detailed and therapy-relevant CGM data. The SmartGuide app automatically syncs all readings to the Accu-Chek cloud, allowing real-time monitoring through the Accu-Chek Care platform.

As a healthcare provider, you can request access to the Accu-Chek Care platform through your local Roche Diagnostics representative. Once your access is approved, you can log in at https://care.rochediabetes.com for international users or https://hcp.myrdcp.com for users in the United States.

When you log into Accu-Chek Care as a healthcare provider, you will first see the BGM view. This default view presents a high-level summary of your patients’ data from the past 14 days. Each row displays personal and health information about a specific patient. For more details about hypo and hyper events, click the drop-down arrow at the end of the patient row. Use the search field to find patients by name or ID. Click the logo in the upper left corner to clear all filters and reset search results. Select results per page to change the number of patients displayed at a time.Click “Sort by” to change the order by name or patient results. Click “Filters” to refine results by category. Use arrows by column headings to view in ascending or descending order. Click a patient’s name to open the Patient View. Return to Patient Management by clicking the logo in the upper left corner. Click CGM in the top menu bar to view Patient Management for CGM. This view shows data for patients using a CGM, such as percentage of CGM active time and glucose management indicator. Click the patient’s name to open Patient View.

To allow your healthcare provider to access your CGM data, you must first be logged in to your Accu-Chek account. From the SmartGuide app Home screen, tap Menu, go to Account and select Manage Account; this will take you to your Accu-Chek account page. In your account, open the Sharing tab and choose Manage Shared Data, then select Generate code. The code that appears is case-sensitive and can be shared with your healthcare provider. Once the connection has been set up successfully, the link to your healthcare provider will be shown in your Accu-Chek account.

As a healthcare provider, you can add a new patient by tapping Create new patient and entering the sharing code. After you confirm the code, the patient’s information will auto-populate, and you can complete the creation of the new patient profile.

In the patient settings, you can update the patient’s account information, health and personal information and account configuration. You can adjust the meal-related time blocks, edit the glucose target ranges and see a list of all devices associated with the patient. Deactivating a patient will remove them from the current patient list.

docs.title: 5.2 Creating reports
docs.text:
To view reports as a healthcare provider, sign in to the platform. You will automatically arrive in the Patient Management view. To quickly narrow your list to patients using CGM therapy, select CGM from the menu bar. Click a patient’s name to go to Patient View. The CGM view displays graphs and statistics for the patient’s available CGM data, including: Time In Range, the standardized ambulatory glucose profile and daily profiles. Click information icons to learn more about each graph. Use arrows to expand the graph. Click legend to view graphed elements. Daily Glucose Profiles can also be expanded to show more details. Click the back arrow to return to the default CGM View.

If you don’t have access to Accu-Chek Care webplatform, the Accu-Chek SmartGuide Predict app also gives you the option of creating and sharing your Ambulatory Glucose Profile and your detected Patterns for a 14-day time period. In the app Menu, select "Export report" and choose the desired time interval. Select to export the AGP, patterns, or both. Attach the PDF to an email and send it to your healthcare provider. While you can view patterns in the app, the full AGP report is only available as a PDF.

docs.title: 5.3 Sharing data with your family or loved one
docs.text: Until now, there is no way to share Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM data in real time with a family member or caregiver. There is no follow app available for this.

docs.title: In this video, we looked at how the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM lets you share glucose data and generate reports. By using these features, you and your care team can strengthen your collaboration and make prompt, evidence-based decisions that support better health outcomes.
6. Connecting other devices (2.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM to other devices. In this video, you’ll discover how to integrate your sensor with smartwatches. We’ll also outline options involving smart insulin pens and pumps. Let’s jump right in.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatches
docs.text:
If you are using an iPhone, you can pair the app with an Apple Watch. Once your Apple Watch is connected to your iPhone, you can see your latest glucose value, the trend arrow, and a trend graph directly on the watch. You’ll also receive all error, maintenance, and warning messages, as well as reminders, straight on your Apple Watch.

On Android, you can install the third-party app Juggluco and link it with the native SmartGuide app to display your glucose data on your smartwatch. More information can be found on the website of Juggluco and in our CGM to smartwatch guide. Please be aware that Juggluco is not supported by Roche Diagnostics and lacks approval from regulatory authorities like the FDA or EMA, making its usage carry inherent risks that should be approached cautiously.

docs.title: 6.2 Smart insulin pens
docs.text: At present, the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM does not offer direct integration with most commercially available smart insulin pens.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
Currently, the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM operates as a standalone system and is not used in commercial automated insulin delivery systems. On a relevant note, the SmartGuide sensor can be linked to Juggluco, which is capable of communicating with AndroidAPS, a well-known open-source AID platform.

Keep in mind that open-source apps are not officially supported by Roche Diagnostics and have no FDA or European regulatory approval. If you decide to use them, you do so solely at your own risk. Although we don't encourage this practice, we are committed to openly sharing all existing options.

docs.title: In this video, we explored how the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM can link to smartphones, insulin pens, and insulin pumps. These integrations can help you build a seamless, coordinated system that fosters smarter and more customized diabetes care.
7. Case report (1.5 min)
docs.text:
Congratulations on finishing this module on the Accu-Chek SmartGuide sensor!

By investing the time to understand this CGM, you’re empowering yourself to manage diabetes with greater confidence and impact.

Now that you have a strong theoretical grasp of the concepts, it’s time to see them in action. We recommend watching the YouTube video below to get a concrete sense of how the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM works in real life.

https://youtu.be/tRZrkqfAJi4?si=6OQDlRaqGY_stPzj

In this Roche-sponsored video, Tom discusses his experience with the Accu-Chek SmartGuide sensor and how it stacks up against other CGMs available today.

When you pair insights from users like Tom with the concepts covered in this course, you’re better positioned to achieve optimal outcomes with the Accu-Chek SmartGuide CGM.

On a final note, remember that you’re never alone on this journey. Collaborate with your healthcare team, reach out to peers living with diabetes, and participate in supportive online communities. Take full advantage of modern technology to help you stay in control of your health and steer it in a positive direction.

Once more, congratulations on completing this module. May your growing expertise and consistent effort keep driving you toward the future you envision.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alarms
  • 5. Sharing data
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the Anytime CT3 and AT4 CGMs. In this series of videos, we'll take a closer look at the main features and functions of these split-design continuous glucose sensors that provide high-frequency glucose data.

The Anytime CT3 and AT4 sensors measure approximately 39 × 30 × 8 mm and weigh 6.5 grams. They are applied to the side or back of the upper arm or to the abdomen using an all-in-one applicator. After a 1-hour warm-up, the sensor sends real-time readings to the Yuwell Anytime app every three minutes. The disposable sensor is designed for up to 14 days of wear for the CT-3 and up to 15 days for the AT4. The rechargeable transmitter has a lifespan of up to two years. Both variants are intended for people with diabetes aged 18 years and older, although in some regions the CT3 is also approved for individuals aged 14 years and above.

A key distinction is that the AT4 (Anytime 4 Pro) includes a green safety lock rather than an orange one and supports optional calibration. In certain regions, alternative versions exist under different branding—for example, the Tracky CGM in India. This module focuses specifically on the Anytime CT3 and the comparable AT4 system.

We’ve already covered the fundamentals in the Master Module on Glucose Sensors, from interpreting sensor readings to troubleshooting common issues. This module builds on that knowledge with practical, device-specific training.

The objective is to support both healthcare professionals and people living with diabetes in using the Anytime CGMs effectively. This content is for educational purposes only and does not replace medical advice. Any therapy adjustments should be discussed with a qualified healthcare provider.

This module was created by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Lukas Trcka, a person living with type 1 diabetes from the Czech Republic who blogged about his experience with the Anytime sensor. With their combined expertise, you can expect an objective and trustworthy assessment of the Anytime CT3 and AT4 CGMs.

This material was developed independently of Yuwell, the CGM manufacturer, allowing for an unbiased evaluation of the strengths and weaknesses of the Anytime CT3 and AT4.

By the end of this module, you’ll be equipped to use the Anytime CT3 and AT4 with confidence in daily life. You’ll learn how to apply and start the sensor, configure alarms, share glucose data, and connect the sensor with compatible smart devices. You’ll also benefit from expert tips to ensure safe and reliable use. If you’re a healthcare provider, you’ll enhance your ability to guide Anytime users effectively. If you’re using the sensor yourself, you’ll walk away with practical strategies you can apply from day one. Let’s get started — best wishes as you move forward.

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (7 min)
docs.text: Welcome to the first lesson of the Anytime CT3 AT4 module. In this video, we’ll walk you through how to apply and remove the Anytime CT3 sensor, along with best practices and key precautions. The procedure is the same for the Anytime AT4. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text:
Get the following ready:

  • an Anytime CT3 sensor pack, which contains an auto-applicator with a built-in sensor;

  • an Anytime CT3 transmitter pack consisting of a transmitter, charging base, charging cable, and user manual;

  • and an alcohol wipe.

docs.title: 2.2 Applying the sensor
docs.text:
The first step is preparation. Place the transmitter in the charging stand. Observe the status of the charging indicator. A flashing indicator means charging is in progress. A constant blue light means the battery is fully charged. After charging is complete, remove the transmitter and prepare to wear it. Now, the transmitter activates Bluetooth mode.

Step 2 is to download the app and connect the transmitter. Scan the QR code on the product packaging with your phone. Download and install the Yuwell Anytime app. Log in, then enter your personal information. Turn on Bluetooth. Please do not turn off Bluetooth during the period of transmitter wear. Now, the app displays the transmitter number. Confirm that the number matches the transmitter being used. Click to confirm the connection. Scan the QR code on the sensor packaging box with the app, or manually enter the number below the QR code. Enter the sensor information. When the one-hour initialization countdown appears, it means the pairing has been successful. Please confirm that the transmitter is fully charged.

Step 3 is to select and clean the application site. To select the application site, keep away from scars, muscle tissue, and ulcerated skin areas. To wear on the arm, the user can choose the side or back of the upper arm where it is rich in fat. If the user is relatively thin or has well-developed arm muscles, they can choose to wear the transmitter on the abdomen. Avoid the ribs, the belt line, and the area within 5 centimeters around the navel. After determining the application site, disinfect the skin in this area with alcohol cotton pads. Doing so helps prevent dirt from affecting sensor adhesion. Apply the sensor once the alcohol has completely dried.

Step 4 is sensor placement. The sensor is a disposable, sterile consumable. After opening the sterile packaging of the sensor, placement should be completed immediately to avoid contamination from long-term exposure to the environment. Confirm that the packaging is intact and within its validity period before use. Open the sterile packaging and hold the orange area in the middle of the protective film. Remove the protective film from the sensor tape. While relaxed, attach the sensor to the cleaned skin. Press the insertion device so that the adhesive tape firmly adheres to the skin. Slide to open the safety lock of the insertion device, then press the gray buttons on both sides simultaneously. A “click” sound indicates that placement has been successfully completed. Remove the insertion device slowly.

docs.title: Step 5 is to install the transmitter, which we will demonstrate in the next lesson.
2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: After the wear period, the transmitter and sensor need to be removed together by slowly peeling off the sensor tape. The transmitter can be reused. After removing the sensor, the transmitter must be detached. Peel the buckle outward from the side gap, then slide the transmitter apart to separate the two. Place the transmitter into the charging stand for next time use.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• Be aware that the safety lock and activation buttons are small and relatively stiff. To deploy the sensor, both grey buttons must be pressed simultaneously using the fingertips with firm, precise pressure. This may be difficult for users with reduced hand mobility.

  • If the transmitter is not found during CGM pairing, first check your environment and device positioning.

  • If you are in a location such as an airport, subway, railway station, shopping mall, store, exhibition hall with electrical equipment, transformer substation, MRI room, or small enclosed space, move to a different area before continuing.

  • Make sure the transmitter is within 6 meters or 20 feet of your phone. For best results, both devices should be in the same room and positioned close to each other.

  • If the transmitter is still not detected, try the following troubleshooting steps.

Step 1: Check Bluetooth settings.

  • Confirm that Bluetooth is turned on.

  • Go to Settings → Bluetooth → Device List on your smartphone.

  • Even if the transmitter appears in the list, do not connect to it from the phone’s Bluetooth settings.

  • Disconnect any other connected Bluetooth devices.

  • Retry connecting the transmitter through the Anytime App.

– If that doesn’t work, move to Step 2.

Step 2: Recharge the transmitter.

  • Place the transmitter on the charging base. Charge it until the blue indicator light turns on. Keep the transmitter on the charging stand while attempting initialization again.

– If the transmitter is still not found, continue to Step 3.

Step 3: Try a different phone.

  • Try searching for the transmitter using another phone. Check the Bluetooth settings on the second phone to see if the transmitter can be found.

– If the transmitter still cannot be found, proceed to Step 4.

Step 4: Restart the phone.

  • After restarting your phone, attempt the initialization process again.

– If necessary, proceed to Step 5.

Step 5: Contact Yuwell Customer Service for after-sales support.

  • If you feel pain after the sensor is applied, take a moment to assess how strong the pain is and how you feel overall.

  • If the pain is mild, try to stay relaxed and continue using the CGM. Pay close attention to the sensor’s position and watch for any unusual sensations or signs that it may not be placed correctly. If you notice anything abnormal in how the sensor feels or sits on your skin, remove the CGM promptly.

  • If the pain is intense or becomes unbearable at any point, remove the CGM immediately. If you need to apply a new sensor, choose a different application site with a thicker fat layer, as this may help reduce discomfort.

  • If the problem continues or cannot be resolved, follow the after-sales support process and contact Yuwell Customer Service for further assistance.

In this video, you learned the correct procedure for applying and removing the Anytime CT3 and AT4 sensors. Each time you perform this process correctly, you reinforce accurate monitoring and empower confident therapy decisions.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (6 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on getting started with the Anytime CT3 and AT4 CGMs. In this video, we’ll show you how to get your Anytime sensor up and running and guide you through the Anytime app. We’ll also share practical tips to help you wear the sensor safely and correctly every day. Let’s jump right in.

docs.title: 3.1 Starting the sensor and transmitter
docs.text: Tilt the transmitter into its designated slot on the sensor. Snap the transmitter into place until a “click” sound is heard. Rotate it in reverse to confirm that it is locked. If it does not fall off, this indicates that it is installed correctly. Transmitter application is complete. Please do not remove the transmitter during the wear period. In addition, avoid scratching or squeezing the device. After one hour, sensor initialization is complete. The Yuwell Anytime app begins displaying blood glucose values and performs blood glucose monitoring.

docs.title: 3.2 The Yuwell Anytime app
docs.text:
This is the home screen of the Yuwell Anytime app. It shows your most recent CGM reading along with the time, and the reading updates every three minutes. The screen also displays a glucose trend arrow, which indicates how your glucose levels are changing. Here’s what the different trend arrows mean.

Below the current glucose reading, you can see your Time in Range. The interface also includes a glucose trend graph that can be viewed over different time ranges. In addition, the graph area highlights the target range and shows a record of events.

Tap an event icon on the home screen to open the Event Log page. Then tap the plus icon in the lower right corner to add a new event. Your CGM allows you to log four types of events: insulin, medication, diet, and exercise. Enter events as they occur to ensure accurate tracking.

docs.title: 3.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• Attaching the transmitter requires a push-and-rotate movement and the slot on the CGM may not always be easily visible, which can make this step difficult for users with limited mobility. When applying the sensor, it is advisable to orient the slot toward the front side of the body so it remains visible and easier to access when mounting the transmitter. If the slot is oriented toward the back and the CGM is placed on the back of the upper arm, the user may need to rely on tactile feedback or use a mirror to correctly attach the transmitter.

  • The Anytime CT3 and AT4 sensor and transmitter are water-resistant up to a depth of 1.5 meters or 5 feet for up to two hours. However, because adhesion strength varies from person to person, it is strongly recommended that you avoid prolonged exposure of the transmitter and sensor to water.

  • Avoid vigorous physical activity, impacts, or bumps, as these may cause the sensor to partially or completely detach and result in inaccurate readings.

  • If the adhesive tape loosens or the sensor falls off while you are wearing your sensor, first check whether the buckle is securely fastened. If it is not, remove the transmitter and reattach it to the sensor, then rotate the buckle in reverse to ensure it is properly secured. Next, inspect the adhesive tape to see if it has become loose. If the tape is loose, apply a strengthening sticker to help keep the sensor in place. You can also gently press the CGM for a short time and check whether data transmission resumes.

  • If the message “Check sensor” appears on the app home page while you are wearing your CGM, remove the transmitter and inspect it along with the sensor base. Check for steam, sweat, water, or blood on either part. If you notice any moisture or residue, clean both the transmitter and the sensor base and make sure they are completely dry. Once dry, reattach the transmitter to the sensor and ensure the buckle is securely fastened by rotating it in reverse. Gently press the sensor for a short time, then wait about 30 minutes to see whether data transmission is restored.

  • If the glucose values shown on your app seem inaccurate compared with your blood glucose meter, check how long you have been wearing the sensor. If you have been wearing it for 48 hours or less, be aware that the CGM is still in its adaptive phase. During this initial period, glucose readings may be unstable, but they usually become more accurate once the adaptation period is complete.

  • If you have been wearing the sensor for more than 48 hours, remove the transmitter and check both the transmitter and the sensor base for steam, sweat, water, or blood. If you find any moisture or residue, clean and thoroughly dry both parts. Then reattach the transmitter to the sensor, making sure the buckle is securely fastened by rotating it in reverse. Gently press the CGM and wait about 30 minutes to see whether the readings stabilize. If the glucose values return to a stable range, no further action is needed.

  • If the readings remain inaccurate despite a stable glucose trend, the sensor should be replaced. For the Anytime AT4, there is also the option to calibrate the sensor to determine whether this improves accuracy before changing it.

  • Remove both the transmitter and sensor before undergoing an MRI examination.

In this video, we explored the start-up process for the Anytime CT3 and AT4 CGMs, from pairing the sensor to navigating the app. While occasional technical issues may occur, a structured troubleshooting approach and timely support can resolve most concerns. Your expertise and reassurance will help patients build confidence and make the most of their CGM experience.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the Anytime CT3 and AT4 CGMs. In this video, we’ll examine the sensor’s alarm settings and demonstrate how to tailor them to your needs and preferences in the Anytime app. Let’s begin.

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
The Anytime CT3 and AT4 sensors provide several glucose-related alerts to help you monitor changes in your glucose levels. These alerts are delivered through audio, vibration, and message notifications.

Importantly, the default alert thresholds depend on the user profile selected during setup. The system adjusts its initial settings based on how you identify yourself in the app.

For example, if you select that you have diabetes, the default Low Alert is set at 70 milligrams per deciliter, or 3.9 millimoles per liter, and the default High Alert is set at 180 milligrams per deciliter, or 10 millimoles per liter.

If you select “not specified,” the default High Alert may be set lower — that is, at 140 milligrams per deciliter, or 7.8 millimoles per liter — while the Low Alert remains at 70 milligrams per deciliter, or 3.9 millimoles per liter.

For both the Low and High alerts, an optional snooze function is available. This allows you to temporarily silence the alert and receive a reminder after a selected interval, ranging from 3 minutes up to 3 hours, if the condition persists.

In addition to the High and Low alerts, there is an Urgent Low Glucose Alert, typically set at 56 milligrams per deciliter, or 3.1 millimoles per liter.

Besides these threshold alerts, optional rate-of-change alerts can be enabled. If glucose is rising or falling rapidly — defined as 3 milligrams per deciliter per minute, or 0.15 millimoles per liter per minute or more — the system generates a Rapid Rise or Rapid Fall alert. This allows users to respond not only to absolute glucose values, but also to the speed of change.

Optional predictive alerts are available as well. The CT3 can notify you in advance if your glucose is expected to reach the Low Alert threshold within the next 20 minutes, or the High Alert threshold within the next 30 minutes.

Finally, there is a Quiet Time option. When activated, Quiet Time temporarily silences all alerts and alarms until it is manually toggled off. This can be useful during meetings, sleep, or other situations where notifications need to be minimized.

docs.title: Aside from glucose alerts, the Anytime CT3 and AT4 CGMs also issue notifications related to the status of the sensor and transmitter. The system may send alerts and temporarily stop displaying glucose data if the sensor current falls outside the normal range. The sensor will also notify you if it is unable to establish an initial connection with the transmitter, particularly when the transmitter battery level is low.
4.2 Setting alarms on the Yuwell Anytime app
docs.text:
To set High and Low alerts, open the menu by tapping the three-dash icon in the upper left corner of the home screen or by swiping from left to right. Select High/Low Target, scroll to choose your desired alert levels, and tap Save to confirm.

From the same menu, tap Reminder Setting to customize the glucose alerts you receive. You can tap an individual alert to adjust its threshold or turn it on or off. However, the Urgent Low Alert cannot be disabled and only its sound setting can be changed. In addition, you can enable or disable Bluetooth disconnection notifications and the Do Not Disturb setting.

4.3 Tips

docs.title: To receive alerts and notifications in a timely manner:
Keep your phone with you at all times and ensure Bluetooth remains connected.
Make sure the app’s sound volume is set high enough to hear alerts.
docs.text: • Close unnecessary apps while allowing the Anytime app to continue running in the background.

docs.title: Do not clear the app cache, as this may cause serious issues such as app restarts or data loss.
docs.text: • If the CGM app is put to sleep by your phone’s system, battery management, or energy-optimization settings, this can lead to missed readings and connection errors. Ensure the CGM app is excluded from battery-saving restrictions. Guidance for different phone brands can be found at: https://dontkillmyapp.com/.

docs.title: You’ve now seen how the Anytime CT3 and AT4 alarms work. By setting them correctly and staying attentive, you can respond quickly to glucose shifts and remain in control of your health.
5. Sharing data (5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Anytime CT-3 and AT4 CGMs. In this video, you’ll learn how to share sensor data with loved ones and healthcare providers, as well as create glucose reports. Let’s get started.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your loved ones
docs.text:
You can share your real-time glucose monitoring data with your healthcare provider, guardians, or family members through the Anytime Follow app.

To begin sharing, open the menu by tapping the icon in the upper-left corner of the home screen or by swiping from left to right, then tap “Share with others”. Tap Add to invite a follower, enter the follower’s email address, and include a note if needed. You can view the list of followers you have invited and check related notifications in the Followers and Notifications sections. Tap the arrow next to a follower’s name to open their settings, where you can edit notes or stop sharing your data as needed.

Your followers will open the invitation email on the smartphone they plan to use for following you, then download, install, and set up the Anytime Follow app, or log in to an existing account to access your monitoring information. Once logged in, your follower can add you as a sharer. To do this, they tap Add, enter your email address, optionally include a note, and tap Invite. You will then receive a sharing request notification in your Anytime app.

After the connection is established, your follower will see a list of sharers on the Anytime Follow home screen. This list displays each sharer along with their glucose readings. By tapping on a specific sharer, your follower can view detailed information.

In particular, your follower will enter the real-time follow view. This screen provides access to your current glucose readings, trend graphs, event logs, and daily views. In the daily view, your follower can review glucose data for a selected date, including diagrams and recorded events. From the real-time follow screen, your follower can also open the settings menu by tapping the icon in the upper-right corner. Here, they can add notes, customize notifications, or stop following you. If they choose to stop following, a notification will be sent to your Anytime app.

Your follower will receive glucose alerts based on your CT3 readings. Except for the Urgent Low Alert, the follower can adjust alert thresholds, switch alerts on or off, and set alert intervals according to their personal preference.

docs.title: 5.2 Sharing data with healthcare provider
docs.text:
CGM data from the Anytime app can be shared with healthcare professionals in two ways.

First, through Anytime View. This software is intended for medical staff and can be linked to the Anytime app through an email-based account connection. Once connected, CGM data collected in the Anytime app is transmitted over the network to the Anytime View platform, where healthcare professionals can review and monitor the data.

Alternatively, data can be shared through the Glooko portal.\ To connect the Anytime CGM app to Glooko, open the Main Menu in the top left corner, select “Share with others,” choose Glooko, and tap Confirm. Enter your Glooko username and password, select Login, and tap Agree to authorize data sharing.

After the accounts are successfully connected, glucose data automatically syncs to your Glooko account approximately once per hour.

Please note that the Yuwell Anytime–Glooko integration may not be available in all countries.

docs.title: 5.3 Creating reports
docs.text:
You can access several report options from the Anytime app menu. For the Daily View report, tap the Menu icon in the upper-left corner of the home screen or swipe from left to right to open the menu, then select Daily Overview. This screen displays your CT3 glucose readings for the current day. You can also select a specific date to review corresponding CT3 data, including the glucose curve and key indicators.

To access more detailed summaries, open the menu and tap Data Report. Within the Data Report, you can review multiple sections, including the selected sensor session, device information, monitoring results, a blood glucose overview, a 24-hour overlay graph, a 24-hour trend graph showing daily glucose patterns, a graph highlighting daily high and low values, time-based percentages for high and low glucose levels, and the distribution of glucose readings. To share data reports or data lists, tap the Share icon in the upper-right corner of the screen.

You can also view and share the Ambulatory Glucose Profile (AGP) Report by selecting AGP Report from the menu. This section allows you to share the report as needed.

docs.title: This video covered the data-sharing capabilities of the Anytime CT3 and AT4 CGM systems. Keep in mind that collaboration with your care team is essential. Sharing your glucose data enables them to monitor patterns, intervene when needed, and support your ongoing progress.
6. Connecting other devices (2 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Anytime CT3 and AT4 CGMs to other devices. In this video, we'll take a look at how these sensors work together with smartwatches, insulin pens, and insulin pumps. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatches
docs.text:
The Anytime CT3 and AT4 connect to an Apple Watch through the iPhone, rather than pairing directly with the sensor. After installing the Anytime app (version 1.1.26.0 or later) on an iPhone, you can enable the companion app on an Apple Watch running watchOS 9.6 or later. The watch then displays real-time glucose readings, trend arrows, and graph data that are transmitted from the sensor to the phone via Bluetooth.

In contrast, the CT3 and AT4 CGMs cannot be paired directly with Android smartwatches, as no compatible Wear OS app is available.

docs.title: 6.2 Smart insulin pens
docs.text: At present, the Anytime CT3 and AT4 do not support direct, real-time pairing with smart insulin pens. Instead, data can be integrated indirectly through the Glooko cloud platform. When you link both your Yuwell account and your smart insulin pen account to Glooko, you can view your past insulin doses alongside your glucose trends in a single combined report for clinical review.

docs.title: At this time, the Anytime CT3 and AT4 do not connect directly to smart insulin pumps for automated insulin delivery. They are also not compatible with open-source automated insulin delivery systems, such as AndroidAPS.

In this video, we broke down the connectivity options of the Anytime CT3 and AT4 with various smart devices. Bringing these tools together can make your daily management smoother and help shape a care approach that works specifically for you.
Congratulations on completing this module on the Anytime CT3 and AT4 CGMs!
You now understand how the sensor is inserted, how the transmitter functions, how the app is set up, and how glucose data can be reviewed and interpreted within the platform.
In this final lesson, we shift from technical explanation to real-world user experience.

We refer to an independent report by Lukas from OpenSource Clinic, who wore the Anytime CT3 alongside several other CGM systems in daily life. His article describes hands-on, day-to-day experience. It is important to be clear: this is a personal user review. It is not a clinical trial, not a validation study, and not accuracy research.
What this type of report offers is practical insight into how the device feels and behaves in real use.

In his testing, Lukas describes the general wear experience — how the sensor fits into normal daily routines, how it behaves during regular activity, and how comfortable it is over the wear period. He notes that although the Yuwell sensor is the largest and thickest among the devices he wore, it remains comfortable because of its rounded design.
He also reflects on how the glucose curves appear during everyday life. This includes observations about trend visualization and how intuitive the data feels when reviewing patterns over time.

Another important aspect covered in the report is interaction with the software environment. This includes the setup process, navigation within the app, and the way glucose reports are structured and displayed. For educational purposes, this is relevant because usability directly affects how easily people can review and understand their glucose data.

Finally, the report includes a comparative personal impression of the CT3 when worn alongside other CGM systems. Again, this reflects subjective experience — not measured performance — but it provides context on how the device integrates into daily use.

When reviewing independent user reports like this, it is essential to separate experiential feedback from clinical evidence. User experience informs comfort, practicality, and workflow. Clinical data informs medical decisions. Both perspectives are useful, but they are not interchangeable.
You have now completed the Anytime CT3 and AT4 module.
Understanding a CGM system means understanding not only how it works technically, but also how it is experienced in everyday life.
Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alerts
  • 5. Sharing data
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Conclusion

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the CareSens Air CGM. In this series of videos, we'll examine the ins and outs of one of the newest CGMs on the market. Let's begin.

Measuring approximately 3.5 by 2 cm and weighing 4.5 grams, the CareSens Air CGM is placed on the back of your upper arm using an applicator and secured with sensor tape. After a 30-minute warm-up, the sensor monitors glucose levels in your interstitial fluid and delivers real-time readings to the CareSens Air smartphone app or the CareSens Air receiver via Bluetooth every five minutes. The sensor lasts for up to 15 days. It is intended for people with diabetes 18 years or older.

In our “Master Module on Glucose Sensors,” we covered CGM fundamentals, including when to trust sensor readings and how to manage issues such as disconnections and skin reactions. This module centers on the start-up education for the CareSens Air CGM and adds advanced tips and tricks.

This module will help both healthcare providers and individuals with diabetes get the most from the CareSens Air system. But before we start, please note that these videos do not serve as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before modifying your therapy in any way.

These videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, Belgium. The content was reviewed by Maike Henssen, a diabetes educator from Bosman in the Netherlands. With their expertise and perspectives, you are guaranteed a balanced assessment of the CareSens Air CGM.

Be assured, all the information provided here is completely independent from i-SENS, the sensor manufacturer, ensuring an unbiased evaluation of the strengths and limitations of the CareSens Air.

By the end of this module, you will have a thorough grasp of the CareSens Air CGM. We will discuss sensor placement and start-up, alarm settings, data sharing, device integrations, and best practices. As a healthcare provider, you will be equipped to confidently support users of the CareSens Air, while users themselves will learn practical strategies to optimize their daily experience with the device. Together, let’s start this empowering journey. Best of luck!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (3.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to the first lesson of the CareSens Air CGM module. In this video, we’ll walk you through placing and removing the CareSens Air sensor, along with key dos and don’ts. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text:
To start, you'll need a CareSens Air kit, which consists of:

  • An applicator with a built-in sensor;

  • Sensor tape; and

  • An alcohol swab, which may or may not be included in the box depending on regulatory approvals in your country.

docs.title: 2.2 Placing the sensor
docs.text:
Download the CareSens Air app, and create an i-SENS account and sign in. Scan the barcode on the sensor package label to enter the sensor information.

To prevent infection, wash your hands before attachment. Then open the CareSens Air sensor package. Take the applicator out of the package and set it on a flat, stable surface.

Choose the area where it will be attached. The sensor should be attached to the back of the upper arm, and the area should have enough fat to avoid muscles and bones. Clean the area thoroughly with an alcohol swab and let it dry completely.

Hold the safety cap down with one hand, and grab the applicator with the other hand to pull upward to remove the safety cap.

Place the applicator on the area. Be careful not to press the applicator onto the skin too hard or tilt it.

Press the blue release button on the applicator. Then remove the applicator. Make sure that the sensor has been properly attached. Gently press the white tape around the sensor to secure the placement.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text:
The CareSens Air sensor can be used for up to 15 days. The sensor will be automatically disconnected when it expires.

If you wish to replace the sensor before it expires, you need to manually disconnect it. Tap the Settings icon at the bottom of the app home screen to manually disconnect the sensor. Tap Disconnect on the right side of Connection Management. The pop-up window will appear, asking you to enter the four-digit code to disconnect. Enter the four-digit code.

Once the sensor is successfully disconnected, the status will be shown as not connected.

Remove the sensor slowly from the edge of the sensor tape.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are essential guidelines for placement and removal of the CareSens Air sensor.

  • CareSens Air has not been evaluated or approved for the following individuals:

  • Infants and children under 18 years of age

  • Pregnant and breastfeeding women, and

  • Individuals that are on dialysis or critically ill.

  • Do not use the sensor if the package is damaged or opened, or if the sensor itself is damaged or defective, as this may lead to infection.

  • Do not remove the safety cap of the applicator until you are ready to attach the sensor.

  • After you separate the safety cap from the applicator, be careful not to face it toward anybody.

  • If a sensor part breaks or disconnects in the process of attaching the sensor, you must check whether the part has remained under the skin. If you experience inflammation, redness, swelling, or pain due to an infection at the site where the sensor was attached, seek assistance from your healthcare provider.

You’ve now learned the steps for placing and removing the CareSens Air sensor. Following these best practices helps ensure reliable glucose readings day after day, supporting confident treatment decisions.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (8.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on starting the CareSens Air sensor. In this video, you'll learn how to start the sensor and navigate the app. Plus, we'll tackle practical tips that will allow you to make the most of your CareSens Air system. Let’s jump right in.

docs.title: 3.1 Starting the sensor
docs.text:
To connect the sensor with the app, press the power button until it clicks. After the sensor is successfully connected, you can configure your alert settings. We'll dive deeper into alerts in the next lesson.

Sensor warm-up begins automatically and takes 30 minutes. Your glucose level will appear on the home screen once the sensor is ready.

docs.title: 3.2 The CareSens Air app
docs.text:
In the CareSens Air app, instant glucose data and glucose trend arrows are displayed on the home screen. Your dynamic glucose data will appear in different colors depending on where it is in your target glucose range. It will appear green if it is within, orange if it is high, and red if it is low.

Your glucose reading will be tagged as HIGH if it is greater than 500 mg/dL (27.8 mmol/L), and LOW if it is less than 40 mg/dL (2.2 mmol/L). If no glucose data has been received within the last 25 minutes, a dash sign will appear.

The Glucose Trend Arrow indicates the direction and the rate of change in blood glucose compared to the previous reading. It is displayed as an arrow on the right side of the latest glucose reading on the home screen.

There are seven trend arrows indicating: stable, slowly increasing, increasing, rapidly increasing, slowly decreasing, decreasing, and rapidly decreasing glucose changes. If there is insufficient data to determine direction and rate, the change is considered “Unknown,” and three dots (…) are displayed.

Tap the Glucose Statistics icon at the top left corner of the home screen to view the statistics of glucose levels in the past 24 hours. Tap the Screen Mode icon on the home screen to see your glucose trend in more detail. In the horizontal mode, you can see recorded events along with your glucose trends. Each event is displayed with an icon above the timeline. Tap the event icon for more details. You can see the 24-hour glucose trends and events. Zoom in with your fingers to see 6- or 12-hour trends.

If you need to reinstall the app due to changing your smart device or other reasons, you can download the previous data uploaded in the cloud server. After reinstalling the CareSens Air app and logging in with the previous account, Data Restoration page will pop up. To restore data while using the app, tap on the arrow on the top right corner of Previous Data.

You can add a variety of events related to glucose care in the Logbook page. Tap Logbook at the bottom of the home screen. Recorded event logs are displayed from the most recent events. To record a new event, tap the plus icon at the bottom right corner of the screen. The Event Record page will appear. Choose an event you want to record from Blood Glucose, Exercise, Insulin, Ketone, Meal, Medication, or Note. Fill in the details including the event time and date. Then tap Save. The event is recorded. You can find the recorded events from Logbook or Glucose Trends in horizontal mode.

You may choose to calibrate your glucose readings if you feel the need or think it does not reflect your physical condition, particularly when the difference between the sensor reading and a finger-prick test exceeds 20%. Remember to only calibrate your sensor if your glucose is stable and in range. CareSens Air supports a widget so you can see key info on your smart device’s main screen. Use the widget to check your sensor connection status, glucose readings, and trend arrows, or to enter a calibration value. Android offers three widget layouts; iOS offers one.

You can enter a calibration value when the Calibration icon is blue. Tap the icon to open the Calibration screen. On the other hand, calibration is not available when the icon is gray.

Meanwhile, tap the Plus icon to open the "Add New Event" screen.

docs.title: 3.3 The CareSens Air receiver
docs.text:
If you cannot use the CareSens Air smartphone app or choose not to, you can use the CareSens Air Receiver instead. The receiver is operated using four buttons: a round Select/Next button, a Previous button, and Move Menu Up and Down buttons. Refer to the table for the other functions these buttons perform.

To turn on the CareSens Air Receiver, press the round button. Review the device terms, then tap Agree. Next, complete the initial setup by selecting the date format, entering the date, picking the time format, setting the time, and choosing the unit.

After reading the product description and safety information, connect the receiver to the sensor by entering the six-digit PIN found on the package label. If pairing is successful, you’ll see a confirmation message. If the connection is cancelled, a cancellation pop-up will appear instead.

Once pairing is complete, you will be prompted to set threshold values for Very Low, Low, and High glucose alerts, as well as the Rapidly Changing Alert settings. You will also select your preferred alert type.

Once the 30-minute warm-up is complete, the receiver will take you to the Home Screen where you’ll see the latest CGM value, the trend arrow, the unit, the CGM trend graph, the average blood glucose for the graph period shown, and the time remaining until the sensor expires.

To open the Menu from the Home Screen, press the round button. Menu options include Start New Sensor, Calibration, Events, Report, Information, Settings, and Shutdown. Selecting Events opens a sub-menu where you can enter event records for blood glucose, ketones, insulin, meals, oral medication, and exercise. You can also access the event log from the same section.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Now, let’s highlight some do's and don'ts for using the CareSens Air system.

  • To connect the sensor with the app, ensure Bluetooth is enabled on your smartphone.

  • For iOS users:

  • Enter your PIN code and tap "Pair" to finish connecting the sensor with the app.

  • Do not close the app after connecting the sensor. Otherwise, they will be disconnected.

  • You will not receive glucose data in real time if the app is closed in the background. Make sure to reopen the app once it is closed.

  • If the sensor fails to warm up, contact the i-SENS customer service center or refer to the company website.

  • A sensor in use with a logged-in account can be reconnected to the same device as long as the sensor remains valid. Once the sensor is connected to the app, it cannot be connected to another app. When reconnecting or changing devices, the sensor can be connected only to the app that it was first connected to.

  • The glucose value shown in the iOS widget may differ from the most recent reading. Open the app to check the latest value.

  • Keep the sensor and your smartphone within 6 meters or 20 feet of each other, without any obstacles such as walls or metal objects in between. The distance from the sensor to the smart device must be closer if there is any solid object between them. Otherwise, the connection may fail.

  • The sensor is waterproof for up to 24 hours at a depth of 1 meter. You can wear it during a shower or bath or while swimming. However, it is recommended to attach waterproof tape to ensure safe use of the sensor when exposed to water for extended periods.

  • If a skin care product such as sunscreen or insect repellent gets on the sensor, wipe it immediately with a clean cloth. Do not wash the sensor. Using an unsuitable solution could damage the device.

  • Remove the sensor before an X-ray, MRI, or CT scan.

  • Comply with your country’s regulations for handling and managing medical waste when you dispose of the sensor.

We've now covered the CareSens Air system set-up, from sensor warm-up, app navigation and glucose calibration, to important reminders for proper use. As you support your patients in their first days with the CareSens Air, acknowledge that technology may sometimes come with operational bumps, such as sensor errors or connection issues. Should they arise, stay patient and persistent. Fully explore the available troubleshooting steps, and contact CareSens customer support as needed. With your knowledge and determination, you are well positioned to help your patients realize the full benefits of their CGM.

docs.title: 4. Setting alerts (7 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on setting alerts with the CareSens Air CGM. In this video, you’ll learn about the types of alerts you can receive from the CareSens Air app and how to customize them. Let’s get started.

docs.title: 4.1 Available alerts
docs.text:
The CareSens Air app sends alerts for:

  • Glucose level (Very Low, Low, High)

  • Rapidly changing glucose level

  • Signal anomaly detection

  • Signal loss

  • Sensor replacement

  • Sensor error

  • Care provider connection

With the app running, the seven alert types appear as popup alerts. If the app is closed or your smart device screen is locked, you'll see the alerts as banners.

Glucose Level alerts occur when your readings fall below your Low or Very Low thresholds or above your High threshold. These alerts require treatment decisions.

You'll get a Rapidly Changing Glucose Level alert if your glucose changes faster than the rate you set. You may have to make treatment decisions in response to these alerts.

Signal Anomaly Detection alerts result from factors like improper sensor attachment or pressure on the sensor, which cause temporary inaccuracies in blood glucose readings. If you receive this alert, check that the sensor is properly attached, ensure nothing is pressing on it, and wait for readings to stabilize. Use a glucose meter if treatment decisions are needed during a signal anomaly.

A Signal Loss alert arises when the connection between your sensor and smart device is lost for 25 minutes or longer.

You'll receive Sensor Replacement alerts 5 days, 3 days, 1 day, and 1 hour before the sensor expires. Recall that the sensor can be used for a maximum of 15 days.

If an error in the sensor occurs during warm-up, 'Sensor Warmup Failure' will appear. If an error occurs after warmup has been completed, a 'Sensor Error' alert will occur. When there is a Sensor Error, the app disconnects from the sensor and can no longer be used. Remove the sensor from your arm as instructed.

Last but not least, you'll get a Care Provider Connection alert when a new care provider is registered in the app. We'll explore data sharing with care providers in the next lesson.

With the CareSens Air Receiver, you’ll receive the same glucose and trend alerts that are generated in the CareSens Air app. Here’s how those glucose alerts appear on the receiver.

In addition, the Receiver provides system alerts for Sensor Warmup,, Signal Loss, Sensor Expiration, Sensor Error, Abnormal Signal and Low Battery.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alerts on the CareSens Air app
docs.text:
You can configure your alert settings right after you've successfully connected the sensor to your app, and right before sensor warm-up begins.

Later on, you can customize alerts for out-of-range glucose, rapidly changing glucose levels, system alerts, and text-to-speech. Start by tapping the Settings icon on the home screen.

You can customize Glucose Level alerts by setting the VERY LOW, LOW, and HIGH threshold values; the alert type; and whether or not to activate them. Switch the alerts on and off based on your preferences.

For Rapidly Changing Glucose Level alerts, you can choose a rapid-change threshold of above 2 or 3 mg/dL/min or above 0.1 or 0.15 mmol/l/min.

Tap System Alerts to change settings for Signal Loss, Sensor Replacement, Sensor Error, and Signal Anomaly Detection.

When you enable Mute Mode, alerts are silenced. Once you turn it off, the alerts return to your previously selected alert settings.

With Text-to-Speech on, you’ll hear an additional voice notification whenever any of the seven alerts is triggered.

Finally, you can find out more details on alerts by tapping the bell-shaped icon in the upper right corner of the home screen. You can see the history of glucose alerts, sensor alerts, and care provider’s connection alerts that you’ve received while using the app.

docs.title: 4.3 Setting alerts on the CareSens Air Receiver
docs.text:
From the Main Menu, go to Settings, then Alerts. Here you can customise the Very Low, Low, High, and Rapidly Changing Glucose alerts. You can also adjust system alerts, enable Mute mode, and reset your alert settings. Use the Up or Down arrow to select the alert you want to configure, then press the round button.

In particular, you can change thresholds for Very Low, Low, High, and Rapidly Changing alerts. By default, the Receiver sends these alerts with sound cues, but the alert type can be modified as well. Save your selections by pressing the round button.

From the Alerts sub-menu, select System to view the system alerts. Use the Up and Down buttons to navigate, then press the Round button to confirm your selection. Keep in mind that for system alerts, only the alert type can be adjusted.

You can also silence notifications by selecting Mute from the Alerts sub-menu. Confirm that you want to enable Mute mode by pressing the Round button. Keep in mind that Very Low, Signal Loss, Sensor Error, Sensor Warmup, and Sensor Expiration alerts are not affected by Mute mode.

Finally, you can restore all alerts to their default sound settings by selecting Reset Alerts.

docs.title: 4.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Keep the following tips in mind when setting alerts with the CareSens Air.

  • To receive alerts from the app, you must enable notifications in your smart device’s settings. Go to Settings on your device. Tap Notifications. A list of apps that send notifications will appear. Find “CareSens Air” on the Notifications screen and tap it. In the app details, tap Notifications and turn on “Show notifications.”

  • When customizing your alert settings, you can choose from Sound, Vibrate, Sound + Vibrate, or Mute. It may be difficult to distinguish CareSens Air alerts from notifications sent by your device or other apps when the app’s alert type is set to sound or vibration. Adjust your alert settings to more easily recognizable options to help you make appropriate treatment decisions.

  • Very Low Glucose, Signal Loss, Sensor Error, Sensor Warm-up, and Sensor Expiration alerts are not included in Mute Mode. These alerts must be muted manually.

  • A pop-up alert will always be displayed, even if the smart device is in Mute or "Do not disturb" mode.

  • If multiple alerts are triggered at the same time, the alerts will be delivered in the following order of priority: Signal Anomaly Detection alert > Glucose Level alert > Rapidly Changing Glucose Level alert.

We’ve now examined how the CareSens Air alert system works. By leveraging these customizable alerts, you transform potential challenges in your diabetes management into actionable moments. Ultimately, you’re empowered to stay on top of your well-being.

docs.title: 5. Sharing data (10.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the CareSens Air CGM. This video will explain how you can share your glucose data with your healthcare provider and loved ones via the Sens365 app and Sens365 Web. We’ll also go over the details of the available reports.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your care provider via the Sens365 app
docs.text:
Care providers can install the Sens365 app on their smart devices to receive and monitor your glucose data. To do this, you must first generate a six-digit code on the CareSens Air app.

Tap Settings at the bottom of the home screen. Tap Care Providers. Tap Connect to link your account to your care provider’s account. Tap Generate Sharing Code. Choose the data you want to share with your care provider, then tap “Generate Sharing Code.” Then share the generated code shown on the screen with your care provider.

Now let’s enter the shared code on the Sens365 app. After running the Sens365 app on the care provider's smart device, tap the person icon at the bottom of the home screen. Tap Add. Enter the shared code provided by a sensor user and tap Ok. You can also enter the sharing code via the Settings at the bottom of the home screen. Tap Connections. Tap the Add Person icon at the upper right corner of the screen. Enter the shared code provided by a sensor user. Tap Ok to complete the process. Once completed, a notification pop-up will appear on your CareSens Air app.

You can also enter the code provided by your care provider on your CareSens Air app to connect with them. Open the CareSens Air app and tap Care Providers in the Settings, then tap Connect. Tap Enter Sharing Code. Enter the code from your care provider. If the code is valid, a care-provider confirmation alert will pop up on the screen. Make sure you are adding the right care provider. When the process is completed, a completion notification will appear.

You can temporarily stop sharing glucose data with your care provider or change items you share with them. At the bottom of the home screen, tap Settings, then tap Care Providers. On the Care Providers screen, tap the care provider whose sharing options you want to change.

Tap on Share Data to activate or deactivate sharing data. Tap the check boxes to stop sharing data on glucose reading and events or alerts.

If you no longer want to share data with a care provider, you can turn off sharing. On the Care Providers screen, tap the Delete icon next to the provider you want to stop sharing data with. Tap Disconnect on the pop-up to complete the process.

docs.title: 5.2 Sharing data with your care provider via the Sens365 Web
docs.text:
All data saved by the CareSens Air app on your smart device is automatically stored on the cloud server. To refresh the upload of your app data, tap Settings at the bottom of the home screen, then tap Upload.

You can view and manage your uploaded glucose data using the cloud-based platform Sens365 Web. Open your web browser and go to https://sens365.i-sens.com. Click Get Started on the start screen.

The first time you visit the site, you'll be prompted to choose your country of residence and language. Click Submit to confirm your selections.

Click create an account on the login screen if you don't have a Sens365 account yet. Enter and verify your email address. Accept the required terms, create your password and fill out all the required information. A "Registration completed successfully" pop-up will appear.

If you already have a Sens365 account, simply enter your registered email and password on the screen to log in.

On your first login to Sens365 Web, the user mode selection screen will be displayed. If you are a CareSens Air user, select Sensor User. If you are a care provider, select Care Provider.

Sensor users must click OK to confirm their country of residence and glucose reading unit. They will then be taken to the home screen.

To connect to a new care provider, click Connections in the navigation sidebar on the left. Click the Connect with Care Provider button on the right. Enter the sharing code of the care provider and click OK. Verify the name of the sharing group. After reading the agreement, check the box and click OK. Once completed, you can find the new care provider added on the list.

To disconnect from a care provider, click the Disconnect button, then Click Disconnect on the pop-up window.

Now, let’s discuss Care Provider mode. When you log in, the screen displays group names and a list of sensor users in each group. A summary of each user’s information is also displayed. You can drag a column to reposition it.

When clicking on the Dashboard button for a specific user, the Add to Dashboard pop-up appears. Assign a care provider to the user, then click OK. The dashboard will appear on the right side of the screen. You can open the dashboard at any time by clicking on the Dashboard button in the top-right corner.

Groups can be created or deleted. To create a group, click the three dots next to the group name. In the Group Settings pop-up, customize the group name and generate the group’s sharing code, then click Save.

You can also add a new sensor user or invite one to the group. If you are adding a sensor user, enter the sharing code you received from them. If you are inviting a sensor user, confirm the group name, then send your sharing code to the user. You can copy the code or send email invitations directly. Your care provider sharing code remains the same unless you change it; you can generate a new sharing code as needed.

Moreover, you can invite another care provider to manage the user groups with you. To do this, click the three-dot menu next to the group name and select Invite Care Provider. Enter the care provider's email address, then choose Admin or Coordinator Authority. Select the acknowledgment checkbox and click Send.

docs.title: 5.3 Reports
docs.text:
In Care Provider mode, click the sensor user’s name to access the Reports screen. By default, you’ll be taken to the AGP report. On the left, you’ll find Overview. On the right, you can access Comparison, Patterns, Trends, Statistics, and Events.

In the row above, you can adjust the time period you want to view.

The Overview page displays Time in Range, Average Glucose, GMI, Glucose Variability, and Standard Deviation. Daily Average Glucose, Insulin, Daily Time in Ranges, Time CGM Active, and Device Information are also shown. A customization option is available, allowing you to adjust the layout by adding, deleting, or moving blocks.

AGP is a standardized glucose report that includes Time in Range, Time CGM Active, Glucose Metrics, Ambulatory Glucose Profile, and Daily Glucose Profiles. You can check the percentage of time spent in the target glucose range using the TIR graph and estimate A1c using GMI.

On the Comparison page, two graphs are displayed based on the selected time periods. This makes it easy to see changes in glucose trends and improvements in glucose management by comparing the two graphs.

On the Patterns page, low, target, and high glucose ranges are marked on the graph for the selected time period. This helps you identify patterns by showing when low or high glucose levels occur and which events may be associated with glucose changes.

The Trends page shows daily and weekly glucose trends. You can view day-to-day fluctuations or see all daily trends together in a weekly graph.

The Statistics page shows glucose readings by day of the week and hour of the day. You can review daily and hourly average glucose statistics within the selected time period.

The Events page shows all event logs recorded in the CareSens Air app in chronological order.

Further up the screen, next to the sensor user’s name, click Target Range Settings to adjust the target blood glucose range for daytime and night-time periods and tailor management to the user’s goals.

In both Sensor User and Care Provider modes, you can download, print, and send reports, as well as export data. To download a report, click the downward-arrow icon in the top-right corner; the report will be saved as a PDF. To print, click the printer icon. To send the report, click the envelope icon; it will be sent as a PDF to the email address you specify. Finally, to export your data in CSV format, click the Export Data icon.

Meanwhile, on the CareSens Air Receiver, the Statistics option in the Main Menu lets you view reports for the past 7, 14, 30, or 90 days. Each report shows your average glucose, standard deviation (SD), and coefficient of variation (CV) for the selected period, along with your Time in Range.

docs.title: 5.4 Tips
docs.text:
Review these reminders before sharing your CareSens Air data:

  • When connecting to a care provider via the CareSens app, the time limit for entering the generated sharing code is 5 minutes. If the code expires, create a new one.

  • When you change your sharing options for a care provider, that provider is notified of the changes.

  • Sens365 is a secondary viewer for the CareSens Air CGM System and is not intended to provide treatment decisions or to be used as a substitute for professional healthcare advice. Sens365 does not guarantee the real-time reception of glucose data of the CareSens Air CGM user.

  • You cannot share data directly from the CareSens Air Receiver. To access data stored on the device, visit the Sens365 website, sign in, and upload the data from the receiver to the cloud server.

In this video, we tackled various options for sharing and uploading your CareSens Air data. With these features, you can work more closely with your care team, helping you make timely, informed decisions for better health.

docs.title: 6. Connecting other devices (4.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on connecting your CareSens Air CGM to other devices. In this video, you'll discover how to integrate your CGM with blood glucose meters and explore options with smartwatches, smart pens, and insulin pumps. Let's start.

docs.title: 6.1 Blood glucose meters
docs.text: CareSens blood glucose meters with Bluetooth functionality can be linked to the CareSens Air app to automatically send glucose measurements. Tap Settings at the bottom of the home screen. Tap Blood Glucose Meter. Tap the Blood Glucose Meter icon at the top right corner of the screen to connect with a new glucose meter. Tap the blood glucose meter you wish to pair within the list of connectable devices. Enter the PIN code shown in the glucose meter display. Be sure to follow the specific instructions for your BGM model. Once pairing is complete, data from your previous blood glucose meter can be imported. Choose data you wish to import and tap Save.

docs.title: 6.2 Smartwatches
docs.text:
You can connect the CareSens Air app to a Galaxy watch or an Apple watch, provided the minimum system requirements are met. Visit the official CareSens Air website (https://caresensair.com/content/compatibility) to find smartwatches that have passed i-SENS’s compatibility tests.

To connect your Galaxy Watch to your phone app, launch the Play Store app on your smartwatch. Search for “CareSens Air,” then tap Install.

For iOS users, Search for “CareSens Air” in the App Store and tap Install. Launch the Watch app on the iPhone where the CareSens Air app is installed.

If the watch app is already installed, close and restart it. The CareSens Air app will appear in the AVAILABLE APPS list, and the watch app will install automatically on your Apple watch. Installation may take a few minutes to complete.

On the watch app screen, you'll see the status of the Bluetooth connection between your smart device and sensor; the date and time when the blood glucose level was last received; the most recently measured glucose level; the current rate of change in your glucose level compared with the previous measurement; and a graph displaying changes in your glucose level while the sensor is in use.

You can choose from the following four types of widgets for Galaxy smartwatches:

  • Glucose trends

  • Glucose level

  • Gauge + glucose level, and

  • Reception date and time + glucose level

For Apple watches, five widget options include different combinations of gauge, glucose level, reception date and time, chart data, trend arrows, and changes.

When the app is closed or your smartwatch is locked, your watch can still display glucose and sensor alerts. Just ensure that alerts are set to be sent to both your smart device and smartwatch in your device settings.

Take note that the smartwatch communicates with the mobile phone, not the sensor. If the smartwatch is not connected to the phone and the phone is not connected to the sensor, the smartwatch will not receive alerts or glucose readings.

Finally, you can connect other smartwatches to your CareSens CGM via xDrip+. In the CareSens Air app, tap Settings, tap Manage Data & Connections, and turn on the switch next to xDrip+. Then, in xDrip+, go to Settings and Hardware Data Source, and select CareSens Air.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pens
docs.text: At this time, the CareSens Air system does not support direct connectivity with smart insulin pens, nor can it be integrated with the Glooko Health app for this purpose. However, these integrations may be considered for future versions of CareSens Air.

docs.title: 6.4 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
The CareSens Air sensor does not integrate with commercial AID systems, although it is compatible with open-source AID systems such as AndroidAPS via xDrip. iPhone-based open-source systems do not yet support CareSens Air.

Be aware that using open-source apps like xDrip+ carries inherent risks and is not supported by i-SENS or approved by regulatory authorities. We provide this information transparently, though we don't recommend engaging in this practice without understanding the risks fully.

In this video, you learned how to connect your CareSens Air CGM with compatible blood glucose meters and smart devices. By putting these integrations to work, you can create a seamless setup that empowers more informed choices and tailored diabetes management.

docs.title: 7. Conclusion (1 min)
docs.text:
Congratulations on completing this module on the CareSens Air! Learning how this CGM works is a significant step toward managing diabetes with greater confidence and effectiveness.

Before we end, we recommend that you watch this independently produced user video on placing the CareSens Air sensor:

https://www.instagram.com/reel/DAJM_47MCEY/?hl=en

It offers a practical, real-world demonstration that complements the theoretical knowledge you’ve gained in this course and can help you feel more comfortable applying what you’ve learned.

And always remember, you're not doing this alone. Seek support from your healthcare team, fellow individuals with diabetes, and online communities. Continue using technology as a tool to stay ahead and make informed decisions that support your health.

Once more, congratulations on finishing this module. May your knowledge and dedication keep moving you forward toward your goals.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alarms
  • 5. Data sharing
  • 6. Connecting other devices

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video on the Dexcom G6, a continuous glucose monitor that can greatly improve your diabetes management. In this chapter, we will explore the key features of the Dexcom G6 and how to maximize its benefits. Let's dive in!

The Dexcom G6 is primarily used in conjunction with an insulin pump and offers continuous glucose monitoring for up to 10 days. The transmitter, on the other hand, can be reused for a period of up to 3 months. The Dexcom G6 sensor measures your glycemia every 5 minutes. You can conveniently access your sensor data through your compatible mobile phone or, in some cases, via your insulin pump. Additionally, there is a Dexcom G6 receiver available for alternative data retrieval.

In the "Master Module on Glucose Sensors", we covered general information about glucose sensors, including insights on when to trust the sensor readings and tips for handling sensor disconnections and allergies. In the upcoming videos, we will focus on the start-up education for the standalone Dexcom G6 sensor and provide advanced tips and tricks for healthcare providers.

Please note that these videos are not intended as a substitute for medical advice. It is always essential to consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

The content of these videos has been developed by Dr. Inge van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas hospital in Ghent. The materials have been reviewed by prof. dr. Laurent Crenier, Head of the Diabetology Clinic at Hôpital Erasme in Brussels, and Cathy Van de Moortele, a passionate advocate for people living with diabetes and blogger about her personal journey with type 1 diabetes. Their expertise and perspectives ensure that you receive honest opinions on the pros and cons of the Dexcom G6 sensor.

By the end of these videos, you will have gained a comprehensive understanding of:

  • How to properly insert and remove the Dexcom G6 sensor

  • The process of starting the sensor using the Dexcom G6 app

  • Setting up alarms to receive timely notifications

  • Enabling real-time tracking of sensor data for your family and caregivers

  • Connecting the Dexcom G6 sensor to other devices,

  • And valuable tips and tricks to streamline your experience and enhance your daily diabetes management.

This knowledge will empower healthcare providers to guide individuals using the Dexcom G6 sensor effectively, and it will equip users of the Dexcom G6 sensor with valuable tips and tricks to enhance their experience. Good luck on your journey with the Dexcom G6!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (4 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on placing the Dexcom G6 sensor.

In this video, we will focus on correctly placing and removing the Dexcom G6 sensor to ensure accurate readings and comfortable wear. Let's walk through the process step by step.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text: To successfully place the sensor, gather the following supplies: a sensor with sensor applicator, a transmitter, and alcohol wipes for proper hygiene.

docs.title: 2.2 Inserting the sensor
docs.text:
Follow these steps to insert the sensor effectively:

  • Start by washing your hands and disinfecting the area of skin where the sensor will be placed.

  • Remove the protective film from the Dexcom G6 applicator, taking care not to touch the adhesive patch.

  • Position the applicator on the designated area of the skin.

  • Fold and break off the orange safety clip.Press the orange button to insert the sensor and then remove the applicator. This ensures the sensor is securely inserted and can remain in place for 10 days. However, remember that you still need to insert the transmitter.

  • Disinfect the transmitter connection using an alcohol wipe and let it dry.

  • Insert the transmitter, aligning the narrow side first into the sensor and snapping it into place. Two clicks will confirm that the transmitter was inserted correctly.

  • Gently rub the area around the sensor patch three times to ensure the adhesive patch sticks firmly. Now, your transmitter is also securely in place. Remember, this transmitter can be reused for the next 3 months.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: When it's time to remove the sensor after the 10-day wear period, perform the following steps for a safe removal. Remove the patch from your skin.
