Break off the sensor tab on the wide round side of the sensor holder. Slide the transmitter out of the sensor holder. Keep the transmitter for reuse in your next sensor.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few key tips to keep in mind:

  • When placing the sensor, it is recommended not to press the inserter too firmly in order to avoid bleeding. Bleeding is not automatically a reason to remove the sensor. Clean the sensor holder if there is blood before inserting the transmitter. If the bleeding persists or the insertion site is uncomfortable, remove the sensor.

  • After placing the sensor, the transmitter can be clicked into the sensor holder. Caution! First click in the transmitter tab on the narrow side and then press down the round side.

  • Keep the sensor and transmitter code as it will be required for future sensor start-ups. Consider taking a picture of it to avoid misplacing or losing the code.

  • Remove old transmitter numbers from your phone's Bluetooth settings. Failure to do so may result in the device continuously searching for the old transmitter.

  • If you encounter any technical difficulties, please reach out to your local Dexcom Technical Support for assistance. While Dexcom absolutely does not support this, some people attempt to resolve the issue by disconnecting the sensor and transmitter and repeating the setup process. To remove the transmitter from the holder, tools like a bank card, test strip, or hair clip are sometimes utilized. Instructional videos demonstrating these techniques can be found on YouTube, although these methods are not endorsed by Dexcom.

By following these guidelines and being resilient in the face of challenges, you can confidently place and remove your Dexcom G6 sensor, optimizing its performance for accurate and reliable glucose monitoring. Even if the start-up process doesn't go smoothly, remind yourself that you have options to troubleshoot and get the sensor and transmitter properly connected.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (4 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on starting the Dexcom G6 sensor.

In this video, we will cover the process of starting the Dexcom G6 sensor and scanning it using the Dexcom G6 app. Let's delve into the details.

For some automated insulin delivery systems, starting the Dexcom G6 sensor is seamlessly integrated into the automated insulin delivery system's software, eliminating the need for the Dexcom G6 app.

Although there is a Dexcom G6 receiver available, we won't focus on it extensively since it is not widely used.

docs.title: 3.1 Dexcom G6 app
docs.text:
The Dexcom G6 app can be downloaded on most mobile phones, with separate versions available for Android and iPhone devices. You can find a list of all compatible devices and software versions on this website: dexcom.com/compatibility

The Dexcom G6 app has minimal impact on battery life during regular use. If your phone's battery dies, your data will not be lost. Additionally, you can view your sensor data while your phone is charging.

To receive sensor data on your Dexcom G6 app, follow these steps:

  • When starting the Dexcom G6 app for the first time, you will be prompted to enter the serial number of your transmitter and establish a connection.

  • Enter the sensor code provided with your sensor.

  • Insert the sensor and attach the transmitter. Wait for the transmitter to pair with the app.

  • Tap "Start New Sensor" to initiate the sensor.

  • Please note that the sensor requires a 2-hour warm-up period before your glucose data becomes available. Keep the receiver within 6 meters of the transmitter for optimal data reception.

Once the sensor has completed the warm-up period, the initial screen will display the following information:

  • In the middle, your sensor's current glucose reading and the associated trend arrow. If your glucose falls below 40 mg/dL (2.2 mmol/L), the reading will display as "LOW." Conversely, if your glucose exceeds 400 mg/dL (22.2 mmol/L), it will show as "HIGH." In such cases, perform a finger prick test to verify your glucose level and take appropriate actions for hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.

  • Below the glucose reading, you can view your glucose graph.

  • Finally, you can access the settings located at either the bottom or top of the homescreen, depending on whether you're using an iPhone or an Android phone.

  • You can also go to the Share icon to set up Dexcom Share & Follow.

The events section allows you to record additional information, such as insulin dosages.

By rotating your mobile phone to landscape mode, you can view your glucose readings of the last 24 hours along with any insulin dosages you have entered.

docs.title: 3.2 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few important considerations:

  • The Dexcom G6 sensor is water-resistant up to 2.4 meters) for up to 24 hours. You can shower and swim while wearing your Dexcom G6 sensor.

  • The time and date on the Dexcom G6 app automatically synchronize with your mobile phone's settings.

  • If there is a temporary loss of connection to the transmitter, your glucose graph may display a gap in the data. Once the connection is re-established, the gap will be filled with readings from the last 3 hours.

  • You may encounter issues when a new sensor has the same code as the previous one. In such cases, the new sensor may not be accepted or recognized by the system. To prevent this, you can try not to use sensors with the same code consecutively. Instead, select a different sensor from the box to ensure proper acceptance and recognition by the system.

By adhering to these guidelines and maintaining resilience throughout your glucose monitoring journey, you can leverage the Dexcom G6 app to access accurate and valuable data for effective diabetes management.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the Dexcom G6 sensor.

Throughout this session, we'll walk you through the available alarm options and demonstrate how to set them on the Dexcom G6 app on your phone. Let's get started!

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
The Dexcom G6 system provides an extensive range of alarm options to keep you informed about specific glucose levels and sensor-related issues. You have the ability to set alarms for Low glucose, High glucose, signal loss (when values are not visible due to Bluetooth problems), and no readings (when the sensor cannot detect any values). Additionally, there are two crucial alerts: the Urgent Low alert and the Urgent Low Soon alert.

The Urgent Low alert is automatically triggered when your glucose level reaches 55 mg/dL (3.1 mmol/L) or lower. It's important to note that this alert is mandatory and cannot be turned off for your safety.

The Urgent Low Soon Alert is designed to notify you when your glucose is rapidly decreasing and is projected to reach 55 mg/dL or 3.1 mmol/L within the next 20 minutes. This early warning provides you with ample time to take necessary actions and prevent your glucose levels from dropping dangerously low.

Moreover, you can utilize the snooze function, which enables reminders for consistently high or low glucose levels. Additionally, the system offers flexibility in setting an alert schedule, allowing you to customize alerts based on specific time blocks that suit your individual needs.

You will receive alarms on the Dexcom G6 app as long as your phone and sensor are within 6 meters (20 feet) of each other without obstructions.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the Dexcom G6 app
docs.text:
Using the Dexcom G6 app, you can configure alarms via the settings menu.

You can customize the alarm experience by choosing different vibrations and melodies. The alarm volume corresponds to the sound volume settings on your mobile phone.

By utilizing these alarm settings effectively and maintaining resilience, you can stay informed and proactive in managing your glucose levels with the Dexcom G6. When managing alarms, stay vigilant and ensure you're using the settings that best suit your needs. This proactive approach empowers you to respond effectively to changing glucose levels.

docs.title: 5. Data sharing (5.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Dexcom G6 sensor.

In this video, we will discuss how to share your data with healthcare providers, create reports, and share data with your loved ones who play a significant role in supporting your diabetes management.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your healthcare provider
docs.text:
Your healthcare provider can access your data through Clarity, Dexcom's reading software. There are separate versions of Clarity available for patients and healthcare professionals. Both versions are free, and you can create your account at clarity.dexcom.eu at any time. Use the same log-in details for your Dexcom G6 app as for Dexcom Clarity. You can also download the Dexcom Clarity app and log in with your Dexcom account. Looking at your glucose trends regularly can help you to fine tune your insulin setting to achieve an even better glucose control.

In Clarity for professionals, you can add a patient by entering their name and date of birth. Once added, you can send an email invitation to the patient from Clarity, inviting them to share their data. The patient needs to log into their personal Clarity account via the Clarity app or via the Clarity website and enter the provided code via the settings or via clarity.dexcom.eu/share.

docs.title: 5.2 Creating reports
docs.text:
Once the glucose sensor data is uploaded, Clarity offers eight to nine different reports, which are found in the left side bar.

  • The OVERVIEW report provides key glucose metrics from the date range you select within the last 90 days. Patterns and the best glucose day are summarized here, which you click for more detail. There's also information about the Dexcom CGM system in use and the alerts and alarms the patient has set. Talk with your patients if they need to adjust their alerts to help them stay in range.

  • PATTERNS provides detailed graphical views and statistics regarding the patterns found, and offers some possible considerations for improved glucose control. Day and night highs and lows are potential patterns that could be found within the date range selected. Help patients stay positive with best day which shows them when they were most within their target range for the selected date range.

  • TRENDS provides a visual representation of where patients spend the majority of their time, but eliminates outliers. The black line indicates the average.

  • OVERLAY features all CGM data points to help visualize patterns and individual events. Hover over each day to view just that day.

  • The DAILY report shows every glucose value and patient-entered events for each day. Highs and lows outside the customized target range are highlighted here, and in the trends report.

  • Clarity's COMPARE report is useful to analyze how well diabetes is managed. Compare different days, weeks or even months to see the impact of a medication adjustment, or simply to see how a patient has progressed.

  • STATISTICS provides detailed metrics displayed by time and day of the week. The Ambulatory Glucose Profile, AGP, is a standardized glucose report where you can see your Time In Range, a trends graph and daily profiles.

  • PATIENT GLUCOSE RANGE is only available in Clarity for Clinics. Use this page to customize the patient target range. Changes you make on this page only apply to this Clarity Clinic view and do not change any settings on the patient's Dexcom CGM system or the patient's personal Clarity account. All the interactive reports are available to print, download or email for any date range.

Our general module on glucose sensors provides additional guidance on interpreting these reports effectively.

Sharing data with healthcare providers may seem daunting at first. Take it step by step, creating your Clarity account and exploring the options available. Remember that sharing data empowers your healthcare team to provide you with better care, making it worth the effort.

docs.title: 5.3 Sharing data with family
docs.text:
Using the Dexcom G6 app, you can share your data with up to 10 caregivers through the Dexcom Follow app.

The process involves these steps:

  • The user of the Dexcom G6 app enables the Dexcom Share feature in their app and invites a caregiver.

  • The caregiver needs to accept the invitation, install the Dexcom Follow app, and complete the setup process.

This video demonstrates how to enable the Dexcom Share feature and walks through the necessary screens to invite a caregiver. When creating the invitation, you can define specific settings for the caregiver, including data visibility and alert preferences. When the invitation is sent, the caregiver will receive an email with two options: to download the Dexcom Follow app or start following immediately if they already have the app. Upon accepting, they will see informational screens in the app, which they can accept. The caregiver can modify the suggested settings without affecting the patient's personal settings. However, the patient retains control over whether the caregiver can view glucose readings. This choice remains with the patient.

Sharing data with your healthcare provider and loved ones is a collaborative step towards better diabetes management. Remember, it takes a supportive network to navigate this journey successfully.

docs.title: 6. Connecting other devices (6 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Dexcom G6 sensor to other devices.

In this chapter, we explore the exciting possibilities of connecting your Dexcom G6 sensor with various devices, such as smartwatches, smart pens, and insulin pumps.

docs.title: 6.1 Smart watch
docs.text:
Dexcom provides extensive support for smartwatch integrations with the Dexcom G6 sensor, and we'll explore options with popular smartwatches such as Garmin, Samsung Galaxy, and Apple Watch.

To begin, you must activate your Dexcom Share account, which can be easily done by adding a follower in your Dexcom G6 app, even if you choose yourself as the follower.

Let's look at the setup process for each smartwatch.

1) Connecting the Dexcom G6 app to your Garmin smartwatch.

  • Install the Garmin Connect app and Garmin Connect IQ app on your Android or iPhone.

  • Download the Dex CGM Watch Face from the Connect IQ store on your phone.

  • Enter your Dexcom username and password in the settings of the Dex CGM Watch Face.

  • If the Dex CGM Watch Face doesn't appear automatically on your Garmin watch, you can manually select it from the available watch faces.

  • Alternatively, you can use the Dexcom Widget or Dexcom Data Field from the Connect IQ store.

  • If you have a Garmin Edge cycling computer, you can also display Dexcom data using the Dexcom Data Field feature.

  • Garmin recently introduced a new feature allowing you to place dexcom readings on the main screen of the Garmin smartwatch. You can find more information about this feature on Garmins website.

2) Connecting the Dexcom G6 app to your Samsung Galaxy smartwatch.

  • Install the Galaxy Wearable app on your Android phone.

  • Download the Blose app on your smartwatch and enter your Dexcom credentials directly.

  • Find and install the Blose watch face from the available options.

Alternatively, you can explore the following options for Samsung Galaxy Smartwatch integration:

  • G-Watch Wear app: Install the G-Watch Wear app on both your phone and smartwatch, and enter your Dexcom credentials via settings.

  • Wearable Widgets app: Combine the Wearable Widgets app with the G-Watch Wear app to display Dexcom data as a complication or a tile on your Samsung Galaxy smartwatch.

  • Dexcom G6 app via Wear Installer (2) app: If you prefer a typical Dexcom watch face on your smartwatch, you can install the Dexcom G6 app on your phone using the Wear Installer (2) app. Additional steps and customization are involved in this method.

3) Connecting the Dexcom G6 app to your Apple Watch

  • Open the Watch app on your iPhone, find the Dexcom G6 app, and install it on your Apple Watch.

  • Once installed, access the Dexcom app on your watch from the app icons, displaying your Dexcom sensor graph.

  • You can also integrate Dexcom sensor data into a complication on a modular watch face. Keep in mind that Apple Watch complications might only update information every 15 to 45 minutes, and tapping the complication will show a larger Dexcom sensor graph with the most recent blood glucose reading.

  • Alternatively, download the Sugarmate app from the App Store to display sensor data as a calendar appointment. Follow the app instructions, connecting Sugarmate to your Dexcom account and creating a new calendar in your iCloud account.

  • On some modular watch faces, you can change the middle complication to "my calendar" to view real-time Dexcom data.

Please note that the setup steps may vary based on the type of phone and smartwatch you use. To simplify the process, a downloadable PDF with tutorial links is provided below this video. It's generally easier to follow the setup with the actual devices in front of you rather than trying to understand abstract instructions.

docs.title: 6.2 Smartpens
docs.text:
Currently, the Dexcom G6 app does not have direct compatibility with any smartpens. However, there is a workaround available. You can install the Glooko app and link it to your Clarity account. Additionally, in the Glooko app, you can connect your smart insulin pen, for example a Novopen 6 or Echo Plus pen, or a Tempo Smart or SoloSmart Button.

By logging into Glooko, you'll have access to integrated data from both Dexcom and Novopen, providing a convenient solution for monitoring your glucose levels and insulin data in one place.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
Dexcom G6 is widely used in automated insulin delivery systems, including Tandem Control IQ, Omnipod 5, mylife Loop system, Diabeloop, and the iLet Bionic pancreas.

Moreover, Dexcom G6 is the preferred sensor for open-source automated insulin delivery systems. While DIY Loop, iAPS and Trio users don't need additional tools, AndroidAPS users require a third-party app like xDrip+ to transmit Dexcom G6 data to the AndroidAPS app.

It's important to note that using open-source apps is not supported by Dexcom, nor is it approved by the FDA or European authorities. Engaging in this practice is entirely at your own risk. While we don't recommend it, we believe in being transparent about all available options.

Embrace the possibilities of connecting your Dexcom G6 sensor to other devices, as they allow you to personalize your Dexcom G6 experience and optimize your diabetes management strategy.

7. Case report

Congratulations to all of you for successfully completing this comprehensive course on the Dexcom G6! Your dedication to understanding the features, functionalities, and best practices of this continuous glucose monitoring system is commendable and a significant step towards improving your diabetes management.

Throughout this course, we have delved into various aspects of the Dexcom G6, and each lesson has provided you with valuable knowledge and practical insights to optimize the use of the Dexcom G6 in your daily life.

To further enhance your understanding of using the Dexcom G6 in real-world scenarios, We highly recommend watching the YouTube video shared below this final chapter. In this video, Tom shares a wealth of practical tips and hacks based on his personal experiences with the Dexcom G6 sensor. This video provides additional perspectives and real-life insights that complement the theoretical understanding covered in this course.

To access the video, please click on the link below this video:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oJvLhvslTOM&t=11s

By observing real-world experiences and learning from the insights of others, you can gain valuable knowledge that goes beyond the theoretical lessons provided in this course. The integration of theoretical understanding with practical wisdom acquired from experienced users will help you achieve optimal results with the Dexcom G6.

In conclusion, completing this course signifies your commitment to embracing advanced diabetes management tools and techniques. The Dexcom G6 offers you the opportunity to take control of your glucose monitoring and make data-driven decisions that positively impact your health and well-being.

Once again, congratulations on your achievement! Equipped with the knowledge and skills you have acquired, you are well-prepared to embark on your Dexcom G6 journey with confidence and resilience. May this new chapter in your diabetes management be filled with improved glucose control, enhanced peace of mind, and a greater sense of empowerment.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alarms
  • 5. Data sharing
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video on the Dexcom G7, a continuous glucose monitor that can

The Dexcom G7 combines a sensor and transmitter into a single device, providing continuous glucose monitoring that lasts for 10 days with a 12 hour grace period. This advanced sensor measures your blood sugar levels every 5 minutes, and you can easily access this data on your compatible mobile phone or on a Dexcom G7 receiver. greatly improve your diabetes management. In this chapter, we will explore the key features of the Dexcom G7 and how to maximize its benefits. Let's dive in!

In some cases, the Dexcom G7 sensor can also be linked to certain automated insulin delivery systems for enhanced diabetes management.

The standard Dexcom G7 has a warm-up time of 30 minutes.

In the United States, an updated version of the Dexcom G7 is available with a wear time of up to 15 days, plus a 12-hour grace period. The standard Dexcom G7 is approved for use in people aged 2 years and older, whereas the 15-day Dexcom G7 is currently approved for adults aged 18 years and older.

In the "Master Module on Glucose Sensors", we covered general information about glucose sensors, including insights on when to trust the sensor readings and tips for handling sensor disconnections and allergies. In the upcoming videos, we will focus on the start-up education for the standalone Dexcom G7 sensor and provide advanced tips and tricks.

Please note that these videos are not intended as a substitute for medical advice. It is always essential to consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

The content of these videos has been developed by Dr. Inge van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas hospital in Ghent. The materials have been reviewed by Dr. Diana Isaacs, the Director of Education in Training in Diabetes Technology at Cleveland Clinic Diabetes Center and Justin Eastzer, an individual living with diabetes who has firsthand experience with the Dexcom G7 sensor and has published numerous tech reviews about it on his Youtube and social pages called “Diabetech”. Their expertise and personal experiences ensure that the information presented in the videos is comprehensive and unbiased.

By the end of these videos, you will have gained a comprehensive understanding of:

  • How to properly insert and remove the Dexcom G7 sensor

  • The process of starting the sensor using the Dexcom G7 receiver and app

  • Setting up alarms to receive timely notifications

  • Enabling real-time tracking of sensor data for your family and caregivers

  • Connecting the Dexcom G7 sensor to other devices,

  • And valuable tips and tricks to streamline your experience and enhance your daily diabetes management.

This knowledge will empower healthcare providers to guide individuals using the Dexcom G7 sensor effectively, and it will equip users of the Dexcom G7 sensor with valuable tips and tricks to enhance their experience. Good luck on your journey with the Dexcom G7!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on placing the Dexcom G7 sensor.

In this video, we will focus on correctly placing and removing the Dexcom G7 sensor to ensure accurate readings and comfortable wear. Let's walk through the process step by step.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text: To successfully place the sensor, gather the following supplies: a sensor applicator with a preloaded Dexcom G7 sensor, and alcohol wipes for proper hygiene.

docs.title: 2.2 Inserting the sensor
docs.text:
To insert your sensor, first choose your sensor site. In the US the Dexcom G7 sensor is approved for placement on the back of the upper arm. Children from 2 to 6 years may place the sensor on the buttocks. In Europe, it is also approved for use on the abdomen for both children and adults.

Before you begin, wash and dry your hands. Clean your site with an alcohol wipe and let it dry for at least 10 seconds. Grab your applicator and unscrew the cap. Don't touch inside the applicator. Press and hold the applicator firmly against your skin until the clear safety guard is pushed in. Now push the button and remove the applicator. To help keep the patch on your skin, rub firmly around the patch three times, then press gently on the sensor for 10 seconds. Screw the cap back on the applicator.

Your overpatch will help you keep the sensor on, and is bundled with your instructions in the box. Carefully pull off the clear liners one at a time. Please don't touch the white adhesive area. Use the colored tab to place the overpatch around the sensor. Using a mirror will make the overpatch application much easier. Rub around the over patch. Use the tab to peel off the colored liner, then rub around the overpatch. Avoid getting the adhesive patch wet, including in the shower, for 12 hours after application.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: When it's time to remove the sensor, carefully peel off the patch like a bandage. You can use body- or babyoil to help you with the removal.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few key tips to keep in mind:

  • On the box, you'll find various numbers. The one marked with "21" is the serial number, while the number prefixed with "10" is the lot number. You might need these numbers when asking for a replacement sensor. So it’s best to keep the box or take a picture of it.

  • On the applicator, you can find a 4-digit pairing code, which you'll require to link your sensor to your desired data-receiving device. It's a good practice to either keep the applicator or take a picture of it for reference. Once connected, you can also find the pairing code on your Dexcom G7 app.

  • If you ever observe the sensor wire disconnecting from the insertion needle, it indicates an issue with your applicator. This problem may arise from the applicator being dropped or damaged. In such situations, it is recommended to request a replacement sensor, as the sensor placement will not be successful otherwise. Do not use the damaged sensor.

  • When placing the sensor, do not forget to press the applicator firmly against the skin until the clear safety guard is pushed in. Otherwise it will not be possible to press the white button on the applicator.

  • If you notice the sensor filament protruding from the sensor after insertion, this is referred to as goosenecking and is a known malfunction. The sensor will not function correctly. It should be removed, and a free replacement can be requested from Dexcom.

  • If your Dexcom G7 system detects that the sensor can no longer determine your glucose reading, you may receive a Brief Sensor Issue alert or a Sensor Failed alert.

  • The Brief Sensor Issue alert means that the sensor is temporarily unable to measure glucose. If this issue occurs, it often happens during the first day of a sensor session, but it can happen anytime. It usually fixes itself within 3 hours. If it continues for more than 3 hours, it’s better to contact technical support.

  • The Brief Sensor Issue may lead to a Sensor Failed alert. This means that the sensor is unable to measure glucose. If you receive this alert, you must remove your sensor and begin a new session.

  • To help prevent future sensor failures, make sure you use a wear location with enough fat to avoid muscle tissue. Make sure you clean your sensor site with soap and an alcohol wipe and let dry completely before you insert. And make sure you use the Dexcom overpatch and keep the sensor securely adhered to your skin, so the sensor wire doesn’t come out.

  • Dexcom officially requires use of the overpatch, but it is not necessary for everyone, especially if you don't sweat a lot or plan on swimming with the sensor. If you're not a fan of the white-colored Dexcom overpatches, you can buy clear ones online or at the pharmacy. There are medical grade patch options in skin tone or colors sold by companies like Skin Grip.

By following these guidelines and being resilient in the face of challenges, you can easily and confidently place and remove your Dexcom G7 sensor. The process of placing the sensor is simple, and we anticipate no issues. However, if you encounter any technical challenges, please don't hesitate to contact your local Dexcom Technical Support for assistance.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (11.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on starting and using the Dexcom G7 sensor.

As soon as you apply the Dexcom G7 sensor, it automatically activates, and the 30-minute warm-up period begins. You may apply a new sensor while an older sensor is working and activate the new one whenever you choose. Keep in mind the 10 day wear period begins once the sensor exits the applicator.

To access the data from your Dexcom G7 sensor, you will need to make a connection between the sensor and either your smartphone or a Dexcom G7 receiver, using the provided pairing code. You can simultaneously use both the Dexcom G7 app and receiver.

If you're using the Dexcom G7 sensor in conjunction with an automated insulin delivery system, the process of connecting the sensor may be integrated into the system, eliminating the need for the Dexcom G7 app or receiver.

To ensure your phone or receiver receives the sensor data, they should always be within a 6-meter or 20-foot range of the sensor.

In this video, we will guide you through the steps to connect your Dexcom G7 sensor to your Dexcom G7 receiver and Dexcom G7 app and how to use the Dexcom G7 receiver and app.

docs.title: 3.1 Dexcom G7 receiver
docs.text:
The Dexcom G7 receiver has a coloured screen and four navigation buttons on the front: Select, Scroll down, Go back and Scroll up.

At the bottom, you will find a microUSB port for charging and data transfer. It is recommended to charge the receiver using the provided cable. Full charge can take up to 3 hours, but the battery can last for up to 7 days of use on a single battery charge.

To turn on your receiver, press and hold the select button for three seconds.

The receiver walks you through the entire setup process step by step. Select next to continue. First you will accept the Dexcom terms of use and privacy policy, and set your date and time. The receiver will then display a series of educational screens so you can learn everything you need to know about your G7 receiver. This mini tutorial will teach you all about safety, treatment decisions, trend arrows, alerts and readings on your home screen.

After learning all about your new G7 receiver, you will need to confirm your sensor is inserted. You will then enter the pairing code. You will find the four digit pairing code on the applicator you use to insert the sensor. Wait for your sensor to pair. Once pairing is complete, select finish and you will enter into your 30 minute sensor warm-up. The sensor warm-up timer lets you know when you'll start getting readings and alerts.

After your warm up you will start getting readings and alerts.

The receiver home screen will display:

  • The most recent sensor glucose reading and trend arrow.

  • If your glucose is below 40 mg/dl or 2.2 mmol/L, it will show as "LOW." If it exceeds 400 mg/dl or 22.2 mmol/L, it will display as "HIGH." In such cases, it is recommended to confirm your glucose level with a finger prick and take appropriate action for low or high glucose.

  • The sensor glucose graph is displayed below the reading.

  • You can adjust the time frame for the graph and access the menu using the buttons on the device.

Within the menu, you can explore options such as reports, events, settings, information, help, replace sensor, stop sensor session, or turn off the receiver. It is advisable to keep the receiver turned on to receive important alerts.

For a comprehensive understanding of these menu choices, we suggest downloading the menu overview provided below this video and reviewing all the available options.

docs.title: 3.2 Dexcom G7 app
docs.text:
The Dexcom G7 app can be downloaded on most mobile phones, with separate versions available for Android and iPhone devices. You can find a list of all compatible devices and software versions on this website: dexcom.com/compatibility

The Dexcom G7 app has minimal impact on battery life during regular use. However, it is recommended to keep the battery Saver Mode off, because this can cause the Dexcom G7 alerts to be delayed. It is also recommended to keep the “Adaptive Battery”, “Battery Optimizing”, and “Optimized Battery Charging” setting off, as this can prevent the Dexcom G7 app from running in the background. If the Dexcom G7 app is not running in the background, you won't get alerts.

The Dexcom G7 app uses Bluetooth to connect with your sensor. You must keep your device’s Bluetooth turned on to get sensor readings and alerts. It is also good practice to delete old Dexcom G7 sensors from your device’s Bluetooth list. Note: Dexcom G7 sensors connect, send data, and disconnect every 5 minutes. The pairing in Bluetooth for your current Dexcom G7 sensor will likely say “not connected” and is expected.

When you open the Dexcom G7 app for the first time, you will need to create a Dexcom account or log in with your existing Dexcom account. Once logged in the app walks you through the setup process step by step. First the overview section will provide you with educational videos and screens, so you can learn everything you need to know about your Dexcom G7. Then you will connect your Dexcom G7 to your phone. You will enable Bluetooth and set other required phone settings. The app will then provide you with instructions on how to insert your sensor.

Enter the pairing code. You will find the four digit pairing code on the applicator of the sensor you used. Take a photo of the QR code or enter the code manually to pair. Wait for the sensor to pair. Once complete, you will enter into your 30 minute sensor warm-up. The sensor warm-up timer lets you know when you'll start getting readings and alerts. After your warm-up you will start getting readings and alerts.

Tip: We advise you to take a separate photo of the applicator code outside of the Dexcom G7 app. This can be used in the rare case the Dexcom G7 app crashes and needs to be redownloaded and reconnected to your activated sensor.

The home screen will open to the glucose tab, where you'll find the most recent sensor glucose reading along with a trend arrow.

  • If your glucose level drops below 40 mg/dL (2.2 mmol/L), it will display as "LOW," while readings exceeding 400 mg/dL (22.2 mmol/L) will show as "HIGH."

  • In such cases, it's important to perform a finger prick test to confirm your glucose level and take appropriate actions for either hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.

Beneath the glucose reading, you can access your glucose graph and choose the desired time range for the data. The yellow line represents your high alert level, and the red line represents your low alert level.

Further down, you'll find a Clarity tab displaying your average glucose, GMI, and Time In Range. You can customize the time range for these reports.

On the bottom of the screen, there are four icons to navigate through different tabs: Glucose, History, Connections, and Profile.

  • In the history tab, you can log events such as blood glucose readings, insulin doses, meals, activity, or other relevant information.

  • The connections tab allows you to see details of the sensor, or to replace or stop the current sensor. Additionally, there is a link to access Share function and add followers. On iPhones, you'll also find an option to link with Apple Health. When activated, the G7 app will transmit data to the health app with a 3-hour delay.

  • The profile tab offers settings for configuring alerts, customizing the Glucose Tab, enabling or disabling G7 notifications, optimizing app performance through phone settings, and accessing support information.

  • You can download an overview of the menu options in the downloads below this video.

On the Dexcom G7 app home screen, when you turn your mobile phone horizontally into landscape mode, you can access a larger glucose graph, with your glucose readings from the past 3 to 24 hours, as well as events you've recorded. By sliding your finger across the graph, you'll be able to see the precise sensor glucose value for that specific moment.

Tip: At the top right corner the home screen “+” may be used to enter events. At the top right corner of the graph, there is an ellipsis (...) which can be used to adjust ranges or turn on and off high and low alerts, as well as activate Quiet Modes which we will cover shortly.

docs.title: 3.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few important considerations:

  • The Dexcom G7 sensor is water-resistant up to 2.4 meters (8 feet) for up to 24 hours. You can shower and swim while wearing your Dexcom G7 sensor.

  • The time and date on the Dexcom G7 app automatically synchronize with your mobile phone's settings, while on the Dexcom G7 receiver, you will have to manually adjust this. You can do this by navigating to the settings menu, selecting Date / Time and then adjust it.

  • Upon triggering the Dexcom G7 applicator, the sensor initiates automatically, triggering a 30-minute warm-up phase. This feature allows you to bridge the usual data gap caused by the warm-up period. When you insert the new sensor and wait 30 minutes before replacing the previous sensor in your Dexcom G7 receiver or app, your app will seamlessly acquire the sensor data from the new sensor, ensuring uninterrupted data transmission between sensors.

  • The Dexcom G7 sensor has the option to be calibrated with a finger prick value. You can do this by tapping the + icon on the homescreen or selecting the History icon, select Blood Glucose, and then the option “Use as calibration”.

  • The Dexcom G7 sensor readings may seem a bit bouncier than those from the Dexcom G6. This happens because the G7 system doesn't smooth out the numbers as much as the G6 system does. But don't worry, the Dexcom G7 sensor is still very accurate, with an overall MARD or accuracy rating of 8.2%.

  • The Dexcom G7 sensor may encounter occasional connectivity issues. If your display device fails to receive sensor readings for two consecutive attempts, a Signal Loss banner will appear. After approximately 20 minutes without sensor readings, your display device will alert you. Once the connectivity is resolved, up to 24 hours of missed data will be filled in on the trend graph. To minimize the risk of signal loss, Dexcom recommends wearing your phone or receiver closer to the sensor and in line of sight. Additionally, ensure that your phone has sufficient battery charge, as a battery level below 20% may trigger low power mode and disable Bluetooth. If you encounter issues, consider stopping and restarting the Bluetooth connection on your phone or power-cycling the phone itself. Dexcom is actively working on improving the Bluetooth connection for future updates.

By adhering to these guidelines and maintaining resilience throughout your glucose monitoring journey, you can leverage the Dexcom G7 system to access accurate and valuable data for effective diabetes management.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the Dexcom G7 sensor.

Throughout this session, we'll walk you through the available alarm options and demonstrate how to set them on the Dexcom G7 receiver and app on your phone. Let's get started!

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
The Dexcom G7 system provides a very extensive range of alarm options to keep you informed about specific glucose levels and sensor-related issues. You have the ability to set alarms for Urgent Low, Urgent Low Soon, Low glucose, High glucose, glucose Rising Fast or Falling Fast, Signal Loss and Brief Sensor Issue.

  • The Urgent Low alert is automatically triggered when your glucose level reaches 55 mg/dL (3.1 mmol/L) or lower. It's important to note that this alert cannot be turned off, however there is an option to silence all alerts for up to 6 hours.

  • The Urgent Low Soon Alert is designed to notify you when your glucose is rapidly decreasing and is projected to reach 55 mg/dL or 3.1 mmol/L within the next 20 minutes. This early warning allows you to take necessary actions and prevent your glucose levels from dropping dangerously low.

  • The default Low alert triggers at 70 mg/dL or 3.9 mmol/L, while the High alert defaults to 250 mg/dL or 14 mmol/L, but these settings are customizable. You have control over the glucose level, sound, and the snooze intervals for both Low and High alerts. For the High alert, there's an option to delay the initial alert, activating only if your sensor reading remains high for an extended period.

  • Rising Fast and Falling Fast alerts notify you when your glucose levels change rapidly. You can specify the rate of change and the threshold for these alerts.

When you receive an alert, it's important to take action, such as making a treatment decision or addressing any system issues. Afterward, you must acknowledge the alert on your receiver, phone, or compatible smartwatch by tapping "OK." Until you acknowledge the alert, it will re-alert every 5 minutes.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the Dexcom G7 receiver
docs.text:
To configure alarms on the Dexcom G7 receiver, navigate to the Settings within the Menu. From there, select the Alerts option, where you can specify the different alerts. Per alert, you can specify the desired alert level and repetition interval when the condition persists.

For customizing alert sounds, access the Alert Sounds menu, where you have several options for vibration, sound volume, and alarm melodies. Additionally, you can temporarily silence all alerts for up to 6 hours, and preview the alert sounds.

Keep in mind that the receiver must remain powered on to receive alarms. If the receiver is fully powered off, it won't receive any alerts.

docs.title: 4.3 Setting alarm on the Dexcom G7 app
docs.text:
With the Dexcom G7 app, configuring alarms is easy through the App Settings menu. You can access Alerts settings within the Profile tab. For each alert, you can customize the level, sound, and snooze preferences. Sound options include Sound, Vibrate, or matching your phone's sound settings.

Within the app, you have two optional quiet modes: "Silence All" for up to 6 hours and a "Vibrate" option. You can set all alerts to vibrate instead of making sounds for up to 6 hours or indefinitely. Keep in mind that Urgent Low and Technical Alerts will still produce sound if you don't acknowledge the first alert when using the vibrate option.

The app also features some rare Technical Alerts that cannot be disabled but for which you can choose the sound.

Furthermore, the app provides the flexibility of setting a second alert schedule, or profile, enabling you to tailor alerts to specific time blocks that align with your personal preferences.

Effectively utilizing these alarm settings empowers you to stay informed and proactive in managing your glucose levels with the Dexcom G7. When managing alarms, it's essential to configure only the alerts that serve your needs and to disable unnecessary ones. Dexcom's options for different alert sounds and vibrating alerts can help create a quieter experience. Additionally, personalizing nighttime alerts can be beneficial, such as setting earlier alerts to prevent nighttime lows and reduce overnight disturbances. Ultimately, these alerts can contribute to improving your Time In Range, offering a proactive approach to respond to changing glucose levels.

docs.title: 5. Data sharing (6 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Dexcom G7 sensor.

In this video, we will discuss how to share your data with healthcare providers, create reports, and share data with your loved ones who play a significant role in supporting your diabetes management.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your healthcare provider
docs.text:
If you are using the Dexcom G7 app, it will automatically link to your Clarity account during the start-up process.

There are separate versions of Clarity available for patients and healthcare professionals. Both versions are free, and you can create your account at clarity.dexcom.eu for Europe, or clarity.dexcom.com, at any time.

In Dexcom Clarity for professionals, you can add a patient by entering their name and date of birth. Once the patient is added, you can connect the Dexcom receiver to the computer and upload the data after installing the Dexcom uploader software.

If the patient is using the Dexcom G7 app, the easiest way to link the patient’s personal Dexcom Clarity account to the professional Clarity account is by using a Clinic Code. The clinic code can be found in the professional Clarity settings.

The patient enters this clinic code in the Dexcom G7 app by going to Connections, selecting Clarity Clinic, and confirming the link. Once completed, the healthcare provider has real-time access to that person’s glucose sensor data.

docs.title: 5.2 Creating reports
docs.text:
Once the glucose sensor data is uploaded, Clarity offers eight to nine different reports, which are found in the left side bar.

  • The OVERVIEW report provides key glucose metrics from the date range you select within the last seven to 90 days. Patterns and the best glucose day are summarized here, which you click for more detail. There's also information about the Dexcom CGM system in use and the alerts and alarms the patient has set. Talk with your patients if they need to adjust their alerts to help them stay in range.

  • PATTERNS provides detailed graphical views and statistics regarding the patterns found, and offers some possible considerations for improved glucose control. Day and night highs and lows are potential patterns that could be found within the date range selected. Help patients stay positive with best day which shows them when they were most within their target range for the selected date range.

  • TRENDS provides a visual representation of where patients spend the majority of their time, but eliminates outliers. The black line indicates the average.

  • OVERLAY features all CGM data points to help visualize patterns and individual events for an entire week. Hover over each day to view just that day. The DAILY report shows every glucose value and patient-entered events for each day. Highs and lows outside the customized target range are highlighted here, and in the trends report.

  • Clarity's COMPARE report is useful to analyze how well diabetes is managed. Compare different days, weeks or even months to see the impact of a medication adjustment, or simply to see how a patient has progressed.

  • STATISTICS provides detailed metrics displayed by time or day of the week. The Ambulatory Glucose Profile, AGP, is a standardized glucose report where you can see your Time In Range, a trends graph and daily profiles.

  • PATIENT GLUCOSE RANGE is only available in Clarity for Clinics. Use this page to customize the patient target range. Changes you make on this page only apply to this Clarity Clinic view and do not change any settings on the patient's Dexcom CGM system or the patient's personal Clarity account. All the interactive reports are available to print, download or email for any date range.

Our general module on glucose sensors provides additional guidance on interpreting these reports effectively.

Sharing data with healthcare providers may seem daunting at first. Take it step by step, creating your Clarity account and exploring the options available. Remember that sharing data empowers your healthcare team to provide you with better care, making it worth the effort.

docs.title: 5.3 Sharing data with family
docs.text:
Using the Dexcom G7 app, you can share your data with up to 10 caregivers through the Dexcom Follow app.

The process involves these steps:

  • In the Dexcom G7 app, users can navigate to the "Connections" section and select "Share" to invite a follower using their email address. Within this process, they have the option to share the current sensor reading along with the trend graph or opt to share without it. Additionally, they can make suggestions for notifications that the follower will receive.

  • The follower will then receive an email with an invitation to download the app from the app store or start following if they already downloaded the app. In the Dexcom Follow app, they can go through any information screens. They can now see the glucose information of the person wearing the Dexcom G7 sensor. The follower automatically gets the settings that were set for them, but they can go into settings and change them at any time. This only changes their settings and does not impact the alerts on the app of the person wearing the sensor. For example if they wants to be notified when the user is over 300 mg/dl, they can set a high notification. Using the Share and Follow function of the Dexcom G7 app can really offer peace of mind to the user and the follower.

Sharing data with your healthcare provider and loved ones is a collaborative step towards better diabetes management. Remember, it takes a supportive network to navigate this journey successfully. You can pause sharing or remove individual followers at any time.

docs.title: 6. Connecting other devices (7.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Dexcom G7 sensor to other devices.

In this chapter, we explore the exciting possibilities of connecting your Dexcom G7 sensor with various devices, such as smartwatches, smart pens, and insulin pumps.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatch
docs.text:
Dexcom offers native applications that enable real-time monitoring of your Dexcom G7 sensor data on your smartwatch.

Any brand of smartwatch may display some alerts, even if it isn't set up with your G7. On your smartwatch lock screen, you can tap OK to acknowledge the alert. That will also acknowledge the alert in your app.

In this guide, we will explore the setup options for popular smartwatches such as Garmin, Samsung Galaxy, and the Apple Watch.

docs.title: #1 Garmin Smartwatch
docs.text:
To view your Dexcom G7 glucose data on your Garmin watch, follow these steps:

  • Install the Garmin Connect IQ Store app on your phone.

  • In the Garmin Connect IQ Store app, search for "Dexcom", and select the "Dex CGM Watch Face" from the search results. If your Garmin watch is connected to your phone, you can proceed to install the watch face.

  • Configure the Dex CGM Watch Face by entering your Dexcom username and password in its settings. If the watch face doesn't automatically appear on your Garmin watch, you can manually select it from the available watch faces.

  • Another option is to install a Dexcom Widget from the Connect IQ store. Tapping on the Dexcom Widget will display your glucose trend graph on your watch.

  • Alternatively, you can install a Dexcom Data Field via the Connect IQ Store. This allows you to view your sensor value while engaging in activities on your watch. If you have a Garmin Edge cycling computer, you can also use the Dexcom Data Field feature to display Dexcom data on it.

Garmin JUST introduced a new feature allowing you to place dexcom readings on the main screen of the Garmin smartwatch. You can find more information about this feature on Garmins website.

docs.title: #2 Samsung Galaxy Smartwatch
docs.text:
To access your Dexcom G7 glucose data on your Samsung Galaxy Watch, follow these steps:

  • Access the App Store on your Galaxy Watch and search for the "Blose" app.

  • Download and install the Blose app on your Galaxy Watch.

  • Configure the Blose app by following the provided instructions. This entails signing in to your Dexcom Clarity account via your connected phone.

  • You can customize various settings such as glucose units, glucose target values, alert preferences, and the appearance of the watch face.

  • Search for and install the Blose watch face from the available options provided within the app.

  • Once installed, you will be able to view your Dexcom G7 sensor data directly on your Samsung Galaxy Watch.

docs.title: #3 Apple Watch
docs.text:
The Dexcom G7 app includes an Apple Watch app. If the watch app hasn't been automatically installed, you can manually install it by accessing the Watch app on your iPhone.

On your Dexcom G7 app of your Apple Watch, you can conveniently monitor your current glucose levels, observe the trend arrow, and view your glucose graph from the past 1 to 6 hours. You can zoom in and out by tapping or swiping on the screen.

To quickly access the G7 app from your watch, consider adding a watchface complication. This allows you to open the app with a simple tap on the complication.

Please note that these Dexcom G7 watchface complications are only compatible with specific watch faces. For instance, you can use the Infograph Modular watch face and place the Dexcom Complication in one of the available complication positions.

Keep in mind that Apple Watch complications update information at intervals of 15-45 minutes. Tapping the complication will display a larger Dexcom sensor graph with the most recent blood glucose reading.

With the Dexcom G7 sensor and an Apple Watch (Series 6 or later), you can also enable a Direct to Watch feature. This allows you to view your glucose readings directly on your Apple Watch, even when your iPhone is not with you. To activate this feature, open the Dexcom G7 app on your phone, navigate to Connections, and select Direct to Watch. The app will guide you through pairing your sensor with your Apple Watch. Once the setup is complete, the grey phone icon will disappear from your Dexcom G7 watch face.

Please note, however, that the Share function is not supported on smartwatches. Your Apple Watch cannot send glucose data to followers. To use the Share and Follow features, the primary user must rely on the Dexcom G7 app on their phone.

It’s clear that the process of setting up your Dexcom G7 data on your smartwatch depends heavily on the specific combination of phone and smartwatch you own. If the provided instructions appear challenging to follow, there's no need to worry. It often becomes much simpler when you have your devices with you and can follow along in real-time.

docs.title: 6.2 Smartpens
docs.text:
Currently, the Dexcom G7 app does not have direct compatibility with any smartpens. However, there is a workaround available. You can make a Glooko account and link it to your Clarity account. Additionally, you can connect your Novopen 6, Novopen Echo Plus, SoloSmart Button, Tempo Smart Button or InPen to Glooko.

By logging into Glooko, you'll have access to integrated data from both Dexcom and your smartpen or smartpen cap. This setup offers a comprehensive solution for monitoring both your glucose levels and insulin data within a single platform.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
The Dexcom G7 sensor is currently integrated with Tandem Control-IQ technology, the iLet Bionic Pancreas and Omnipod 5. Integration with the Dexcom G7 is also planned for Diabeloop, and there may be potential compatibility with other automated insulin delivery systems in the future.

Furthermore, the Dexcom G7 is a preferred sensor for open-source automated insulin delivery systems. DIY Loop, iAPS and Trio users can seamlessly integrate it without additional tools, while AndroidAPS users may require a third-party app to transmit Dexcom G7 data to the AndroidAPS app. This third-party app can either be xDrip+, Juggluco or a patched Dexcom G7 app like the DiAKEM app. For more detailed information, refer to the AndroidAPS documentation.

It's crucial to understand that using open-source apps is not supported by Dexcom, nor is it FDA or European authorities approved. Engaging in this practice carries inherent risks and is a personal choice. While we don't endorse it, we believe in providing transparency about all available options.

To conclude, we recommend embracing the flexibility of connecting your Dexcom G7 sensor to various devices, as it enables you to tailor your Dexcom G7 experience and enhance your diabetes management approach. Adaptability empowers you to make choices that align with your unique needs and preferences.

docs.title: 7. Case report (2 min)
docs.text:
Congratulations to all of you for successfully completing this comprehensive course on the Dexcom G7! Your dedication to understanding the features, functionalities, and best practices of this continuous glucose monitoring system is commendable and a significant step towards improving your diabetes management.

Throughout this course, we have delved into various aspects of the Dexcom G7, and each lesson has provided you with valuable knowledge and practical insights to optimize the use of the Dexcom G7 in your daily life.

To further enhance your understanding of using the Dexcom G7 in real-world scenarios, we highly recommend watching the YouTube video shared below this final chapter. In this video, Tom shares a wealth of practical tips and hacks based on his personal experiences with the Dexcom G7 sensor. This video provides additional perspectives and real-life insights that complement the theoretical understanding covered in this course.

To access the video, please click on the link below this video:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MxH321Gkemk

By observing real-world experiences and learning from the insights of others, you can gain valuable knowledge that goes beyond the theoretical lessons provided in this course. The integration of theoretical understanding with practical wisdom acquired from experienced users will help you achieve optimal results with the Dexcom G7.

In conclusion, completing this course signifies your commitment to embracing advanced diabetes management tools and techniques. The Dexcom G7 offers you the opportunity to take control of your glucose monitoring and make data-driven decisions that positively impact your health and well-being.

Once again, congratulations on your achievement! Equipped with the knowledge and skills you have acquired, you are well-prepared to embark on your Dexcom G7 journey with confidence and resilience. May this new chapter in your diabetes management be filled with improved glucose control, enhanced peace of mind, and a greater sense of empowerment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alarms
  • 5. Data sharing
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video on the Dexcom ONE Plus, a continuous glucose monitor that can greatly improve your diabetes management. In this chapter, we will explore the key features of the Dexcom ONE Plus and how to maximize its benefits. Let's dive in!

The Dexcom ONE Plus combines a sensor and transmitter into a single device, providing continuous glucose monitoring that lasts for 10 days with a 12 hour grace period. This advanced sensor measures your blood sugar levels every 5 minutes, and you can easily access this data on your compatible mobile phone or on a Dexcom ONE Plus receiver.

The Dexcom ONE Plus is very similar to the Dexcom G7 sensor. The main difference is that the Dexcom ONE Plus cannot be linked to an automated insulin delivery system, while the Dexcom G7 can.

In the "Master Module on Glucose Sensors", we covered general information about glucose sensors, including insights on when to trust the sensor readings and tips for handling sensor disconnections and allergies. In the upcoming videos, we will focus on the start-up education for the standalone Dexcom ONE Plus sensor and provide advanced tips and tricks.

Please note that these videos are not intended as a substitute for medical advice. It is always essential to consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

The content of these videos has been developed by Dr. Inge van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas hospital in Ghent, in Belgium. The materials have been reviewed by Dr. Liesbeth Van Huffel, endocrinologist at the AZORG Hospital in Aalst in Belgium, and Christophe Merlot, an individual with type 1 diabetes who uses the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor in combination with the iAPS automated insulin delivery system. Their expertise and personal experiences ensure that the information presented in the videos is comprehensive and unbiased.

By the end of these videos, you will have gained a comprehensive understanding of:

  • How to properly insert and remove the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor

  • The process of starting the sensor using the Dexcom ONE Plus receiver and app

  • Setting up alarms to receive timely notifications

  • Enabling real-time tracking of sensor data for your family and caregivers

  • Connecting the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor to other devices,

  • And valuable tips and tricks to streamline your experience and enhance your daily diabetes management.

This knowledge will empower healthcare providers to guide individuals using the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor effectively, and it will equip users of the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor with valuable tips and tricks to enhance their experience. Good luck on your journey with the Dexcom ONE Plus!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (4 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on placing the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor.

In this video, we will focus on correctly placing and removing the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor to ensure accurate readings and comfortable wear. Let's walk through the process step by step.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text: To successfully place the sensor, gather the following supplies: a sensor applicator with a preloaded Dexcom ONE Plus sensor, and alcohol wipes for proper hygiene.

docs.title: 2.2 Inserting the sensor
docs.text:
To insert your sensor, first choose your sensor site. The Dexcom ONE Plus sensor is approved for placement on the abdomen and the back of the upper arm. In addition, children from 2 to 6 years may also place the sensor on the upper buttocks.

Before you begin, wash and dry your hands. Clean your site with an alcohol wipe and let it air dry for at least 10 seconds. Get your applicator and unscrew the cap. Don't touch inside the applicator. Press and hold the applicator firmly against your skin until the clear safety guard is pushed in. Now push the button and remove the applicator. To help keep the patch on your skin, rub firmly around the patch three times, then press gently on the sensor for 10 seconds.

Your overpatch will help you keep the sensor on, and is bundled with your instructions in the box. Carefully pull off the clear liners one at a time. Please don't touch the white adhesive area. Use the colored tab to place the overpatch around the sensor. Using a mirror will make the overpatch application much easier. Rub around the over patch. Use the tab to peel off the colored liner, then rub around the overpatch at least 3 times.

Avoid getting the adhesive patch wet, including in the shower, for 12 hours after application. After those 12 hours, you can shower and swim with the sensor without extra protection.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: When it's time to remove the sensor, carefully peel off the patch like a bandage. You can use body- or babyoil to help you with the removal.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few key tips to keep in mind:

  • On the box, you'll find various numbers. The one marked with "21" is the serial number, while the number prefixed with "10" is the lot number. You might need these numbers when asking for a replacement sensor. So it’s best to keep the box or take a picture of it.

  • On the applicator, you can find a 4-digit pairing code, which you'll require to link your sensor to your desired data-receiving device. It's a good practice to either keep the applicator or take a picture of it for reference. Once connected, you can also find the pairing code on your Dexcom ONE Plus app.

  • If you ever observe the sensor wire disconnecting from the insertion needle, it indicates an issue with your applicator. This problem may arise from the applicator being dropped or damaged. In such situations, it is recommended to request a replacement sensor and report the issue with Dexcom Technical support, as the sensor placement will not be successful otherwise. Do not use the damaged sensor.

  • When placing the sensor, do not forget to press the applicator firmly against the skin until the clear safety guard is pushed in. Otherwise it will not be possible to press the white button on the applicator.

  • If you notice the sensor filament protruding from the sensor after insertion, this is referred to as goosenecking and is a known malfunction. The sensor will not function correctly. It should be removed, and a free replacement can be requested from Dexcom.

  • If your Dexcom ONE Plus system detects that the sensor can no longer determine your glucose reading, you may receive a Brief Sensor Issue alert or a Sensor Failed alert.

  • The Brief Sensor Issue alert means that the sensor is temporarily unable to measure glucose. If this issue occurs, it often happens during the first day of a sensor session, but it can happen anytime. It usually fixes itself within 3 hours. If it continues for more than 3 hours, it’s better to contact technical support.

  • In rare occasions, the Brief Sensor Issue may lead to a Sensor Failed alert. This means that the sensor is unable to measure glucose. If you receive this alert, you must remove your sensor and begin a new session.

  • To help prevent future sensor failures, make sure you use a wear location with enough fat to avoid muscle tissue. Make sure you clean your sensor site with water and soap and an alcohol wipe and let dry completely before you insert. And make sure you use the Dexcom overpatch and keep the sensor securely adhered to your skin, so the sensor wire doesn’t come out.

  • Dexcom officially requires use of the overpatch, but it might not be necessary for everyone, especially if you don't sweat a lot or plan on swimming with the sensor. If you're not a fan of the white-colored Dexcom overpatches, you can buy clear ones online or at the pharmacy. There are medical grade patch options in skin tone or colors sold by companies like Skin Grip.

By following these instructions, you can easily and confidently place and remove your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor. The process of placing the sensor is simple, and we anticipate no issues. However, if you encounter any technical challenges, please don't hesitate to contact your local Dexcom Technical Support for assistance.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (5.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on starting and using the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor.

As soon as you apply the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor, it automatically activates, and the 30-minute warm-up period will start. You may apply a new sensor while an older sensor is working and activate the new one whenever you choose. Keep in mind the 10 day wear period begins once the sensor exits the applicator.

To access the data from your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor, you will need to make a connection between the sensor and either your smartphone or a Dexcom ONE Plus receiver, using the provided pairing code. You can simultaneously use both the Dexcom ONE Plus app and receiver.

To ensure your phone or receiver receives the sensor data, they should always be within a 6-meter or 20-foot range of the sensor.

In this video, we will guide you through the steps to connect your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor to your Dexcom ONE Plus receiver and Dexcom ONE Plus app and how to use the Dexcom ONE Plus receiver and app.

docs.title: 3.1 Dexcom ONE Plus receiver
docs.text:
The Dexcom ONE Plus receiver has a coloured screen and four navigation buttons on the front: Select, Scroll down, Go back and Scroll up.

At the bottom, you will find a microUSB port for charging and data transfer. It is recommended to charge the receiver using the provided cable. Full charge can take up to 3 hours, but the battery can last for up to 7 days of use on a single battery charge.

To turn on your receiver, press and hold the select button for three seconds. The receiver walks you through the entire setup process step by step. Select next to continue. First you will accept the Dexcom terms of use and privacy policy, and set your date and time. The receiver will then display a series of educational screens so you can learn everything you need to know about your Dexcom ONE Plus. This mini tutorial will teach you all about safety, treatment decisions and trend arrows. Next, you will be given the option to turn alerts on or off.

After learning all about your system and choosing your alert settings, it is time to insert your sensor. You can now insert your sensor. On the receiver, you can select that the sensor is inserted. You will then enter the pairing code. You will find the four digit pairing code on the applicator you used to insert the sensor. Wait for your sensor to pair. Once pairing is complete, select finish and you will enter into your 30 minute sensor warm-up. The sensor warm-up timer lets you know when you'll start getting readings and alerts. If your sensor has been inserted for over 30 minutes, you'll immediately start receiving readings since the warm-up period initiates upon sensor insertion.

After your warm up you will start getting readings and alerts.

The receiver home screen will display:

  • The most recent sensor glucose reading and trend arrow.

  • If your glucose is below 40 mg/dl or 2.2 mmol/L, it will show as "LOW." If it exceeds 400 mg/dl or 22.2 mmol/L, it will display as "HIGH." In such cases, it is recommended to confirm your glucose level with a finger prick and take appropriate action for low or high glucose.

  • The sensor glucose graph is displayed below the reading.

  • You can adjust the time frame for the graph and access the menu using the buttons on the device.

Within the menu, you can explore options such as reports, events, settings, information, help, replace sensor, stop sensor session, or turn off the receiver. It is advisable to keep the receiver turned on to receive important alerts.

For a comprehensive understanding of these menu choices, we suggest downloading the menu overview provided below this video and reviewing all the available options.

docs.title: 3.2 Dexcom ONE Plus app
docs.text:
The Dexcom ONE Plus app can be downloaded on the most recent mobile phones, with separate versions available for Android and iPhone devices. You can find a list of all compatible devices and software versions on this website: dexcom.com/compatibility

The Dexcom ONE Plus app has minimal impact on battery life during regular use. However, it is recommended to keep the battery Saver Mode off, because this can cause the Dexcom alerts to be delayed. It is also recommended to keep the “Adaptive Battery”, “Battery Optimizing”, and “Optimized Battery Charging” setting off, as this can prevent the Dexcom ONE Plus app from running in the background. If the Dexcom ONE PLus app is not running in the background, you won't get alerts.

The Dexcom ONE Plus app uses Bluetooth to connect with your sensor. You must keep your device’s Bluetooth turned on to get sensor readings and alerts. It is also good practice to delete old Dexcom ONE Plus sensors from your device’s Bluetooth list. Note: Dexcom ONE PLus sensors connect, send data, and disconnect every 5 minutes. The pairing in Bluetooth for your current Dexcom ONE Plus sensor will likely say “not connected” and is expected.

When you open the Dexcom ONE Plus app for the first time, you will need to create a Dexcom account or log in with your existing Dexcom account. Once logged in the app walks you through the setup process step by step. First the overview section will provide you with educational videos and screens, so you can learn everything you need to know about your Dexcom ONE Plus. You will also be given the option to turn alerts on or off. Then you will connect your Dexcom ONE Plus to your phone. You will enable Bluetooth and set other required phone settings. The app will then provide you with instructions on how to insert your sensor.

Enter the pairing code. You will find the four digit pairing code on the applicator of the sensor you used. Take a photo of the QR code or enter the code manually to pair. Wait for the sensor to pair. Once complete, you will enter into your 30 minute sensor warm-up. The sensor warm-up timer lets you know when you'll start getting readings and alerts. After your warm-up you will start getting readings and alerts. If your sensor has been inserted for over 30 minutes, you'll immediately start receiving readings since the warm-up period initiates upon sensor insertion.

Tip: We advise you to take a separate photo of the applicator code outside of the Dexcom ONE Plus app. This can be used in the rare case the Dexcom ONE Plus app crashes and needs to be redownloaded and reconnected to your activated sensor.

The home screen will open to the glucose tab, where you'll find the most recent sensor glucose reading along with a trend arrow.

  • If your glucose level drops below 40 mg/dL (2.2 mmol/L), it will display as "LOW," while readings exceeding 400 mg/dL (22.2 mmol/L) will show as "HIGH."

  • In such cases, it's important to perform a finger prick test to confirm your glucose level and take appropriate actions for either hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.

Beneath the glucose reading, you can access your glucose graph and choose the desired time range for the data. The yellow line represents your high alert level, and the red line represents your low alert level.

Further down, you'll find a Clarity tab displaying your average glucose, GMI, and Time In Range. You can customize the time range for these reports.

On the bottom of the screen, there are four icons to navigate through different tabs: Glucose, History, Connections, and Profile.

  • In the history tab, you can log events such as blood glucose readings, insulin doses, meals, activity, or other relevant information.

  • The connections tab allows you to see details of the sensor, or to replace or stop the current sensor. Additionally, there is a link to access Share function and add followers. On iPhones, you'll also find an option to link with Apple Health. When activated, the ONE Plus app will transmit data to the health app with a 3-hour delay.

  • The profile tab offers settings for configuring alerts, customizing the Glucose Tab, enabling or disabling Dexcom ONE Plus notifications, optimizing app performance through phone settings, and accessing support information.

  • You can download an overview of the menu options in the downloads below this video.

On the Dexcom ONE Plus app home screen, when you turn your mobile phone horizontally into landscape mode, you can access a larger glucose graph, with your glucose readings from the past 3 to 24 hours, as well as events you've recorded. By sliding your finger across the graph, you'll be able to see the precise sensor glucose value for that specific moment.

Tip: At the top right corner the home screen “+” may be used to enter events. At the top right corner of the graph, there is an ellipsis (...) which can be used to adjust ranges or turn on and off high and low alerts, as well as activate Quiet Modes which we will cover shortly.

docs.title: 3.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few important considerations:

  • The Dexcom ONE Plus sensor is water-resistant up to 2.4 meters (8 feet) for up to 24 hours. You can shower and swim while wearing your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor, although it is best to avoid this during the first 12 hours after insertion of the sensor.

  • The time and date on the Dexcom ONE Plus app automatically synchronize with your mobile phone's settings, while on the Dexcom ONE Plus receiver, you will have to manually adjust this. You can do this by navigating to the settings menu, selecting Date / Time and then adjust it.

  • Upon triggering the Dexcom ONE Plus applicator, the sensor initiates automatically, triggering a 30-minute warm-up phase. This feature allows you to bridge the usual data gap caused by the warm-up period. When you insert the new sensor and wait 30 minutes before replacing the previous sensor in your Dexcom ONE Plus receiver or app, your app will seamlessly acquire the sensor data from the new sensor, ensuring uninterrupted data transmission between sensors.

  • The Dexcom ONE Plus sensor readings may seem a bit bouncier than those from the Dexcom G6. This happens because the ONE Plus system doesn't smooth out the numbers as much as the G6 system does. But don't worry, the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor is still very accurate, with an overall MARD or accuracy rating of 8.2%.

  • The Dexcom ONE Plus sensor has the option to be calibrated with a finger prick value. You can do this by tapping the + icon on the homescreen or selecting the History icon, select Blood Glucose, and then the option “Use as calibration”.

  • The Dexcom ONE Plus sensor may encounter occasional connectivity issues. If your display device fails to receive sensor readings for two consecutive attempts, a Signal Loss banner will appear. After approximately 20 minutes without sensor readings, your display device will alert you. Once the connectivity is resolved, up to 24 hours of missed data will be filled in on the trend graph. To minimize the risk of signal loss, Dexcom recommends wearing your phone or receiver closer to the sensor and in line of sight. Additionally, ensure that your phone has sufficient battery charge, as a battery level below 20% may trigger low power mode and disable Bluetooth. If you encounter issues, consider stopping and restarting the Bluetooth connection on your phone or power-cycling the phone itself. Dexcom is actively working on improving the Bluetooth connection for future updates.

By adhering to these guidelines and maintaining resilience throughout your glucose monitoring journey, you can leverage the Dexcom ONE Plus system to access accurate and valuable data for effective diabetes management.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor.
