Throughout this session, we'll walk you through the available alarm options and demonstrate how to set them on the Dexcom ONE Plus receiver and app on your phone. Let's get started!

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
The Dexcom ONE Plus system provides a range of alarm options to keep you informed about specific glucose levels and sensor-related issues. You have the ability to set alarms for Low glucose, High glucose, Signal Loss and Brief Sensor Issue.

The default Low alert triggers at 70 mg/dL or 3.9 mmol/L, while the High alert defaults to 250 mg/dL or 14 mmol/L, but these settings are customizable. You have control over the glucose level, sound, and the snooze intervals for both Low and High alerts. For the High alert, there's an option to delay the initial alert, activating only if your sensor reading remains high for an extended period.

When you receive an alert, it's important to take action, such as making a treatment decision or addressing any system issues. Afterward, you must acknowledge the alert on your receiver, phone, or compatible smartwatch by tapping "OK." Until you acknowledge the alert, it will re-alert every 5 minutes.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the Dexcom ONE Plus receiver
docs.text:
To configure alarms on the Dexcom ONE Plus receiver, navigate to the Settings within the Menu. From there, select the Alerts option, where you can specify the different alerts. Per alert, you can specify the desired alert level and repetition interval when the condition persists.

For customizing alert sounds, access the Alert Sounds menu, where you have several options for vibration, sound volume, and alarm melodies.

Keep in mind that the receiver must remain powered on to receive alarms. If the receiver is fully powered off, it won't receive any alerts.

docs.title: 4.3 Setting alarms on the Dexcom ONE Plus app
docs.text:
With the Dexcom ONE Plus app, you can access Alerts settings within the Profile tab. For each alert, you can customize the level, sound, and snooze preferences. For the sound you can opt for vibrate only or choose between different melodies.

The app also features some rare Technical Alerts that cannot be disabled but for which you can choose the sound.

Effectively utilizing these alarm settings empowers you to stay informed and proactive in managing your glucose levels with the Dexcom ONE Plus. When managing alarms, it's essential to configure only the alerts that serve your needs and to disable unnecessary ones. Dexcom's options for different alert sounds and vibrating alerts can help create a quieter experience. Additionally, these alerts can contribute to improving your Time In Range, offering a proactive approach to respond to changing glucose levels.

docs.title: 5. Data sharing
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor.

In this video, we will discuss how to share your data with healthcare providers, create reports, and share data with your loved ones who play a significant role in supporting your diabetes management.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your healthcare provider
docs.text:
If you are using the Dexcom ONE Plus app, it will automatically link to your Clarity account during the start-up process.

There are separate versions of Clarity available for patients and healthcare professionals. Both versions are free, and you can create your account at clarity.dexcom.eu for Europe, or clarity.dexcom.com, at any time.

In Dexcom Clarity for professionals, you can add a patient by entering their name and date of birth. Once the patient is added, you can connect the Dexcom receiver to the computer and upload the data after installing the Dexcom uploader software.

If the patient is using the Dexcom ONE plus app, the easiest way to link the patient’s personal Dexcom Clarity account to the professional Clarity account is by using a Clinic Code. The clinic code can be found in the professional Clarity settings.

The patient enters this clinic code in the Dexcom ONE Plus app by going to Connections, selecting Clarity Clinic, and confirming the link. Once completed, the healthcare provider has real-time access to that person’s glucose sensor data.

docs.title: 5.2 Creating reports
docs.text:
Once the glucose sensor data is uploaded, Clarity offers eight to nine different reports, which are found in the left side bar.

  • The OVERVIEW report provides key glucose metrics from the date range you select within the last seven to 90 days. Patterns and the best glucose day are summarized here, which you can click for more detail. There's also information about the Dexcom CGM system in use and the alerts and alarms the patient has set. Talk with your patients if they need to adjust their alerts to help them stay in range.

  • PATTERNS provides detailed graphical views and statistics regarding the patterns found, and offers some possible considerations for improved glucose control. Day and night highs and lows are potential patterns that could be found within the date range selected. Help patients stay positive with best day which shows them when they were most within their target range for the selected date range.

  • TRENDS provides a visual representation of where patients spend the majority of their time, but eliminates outliers. The black line indicates the average.

  • OVERLAY features all CGM data points to help visualize patterns and individual events for an entire week. Hover over each day to view just that day. The DAILY report shows every glucose value and patient-entered events for each day. Highs and lows outside the customized target range are highlighted here, and in the trends report.

  • Clarity's COMPARE report is useful to analyze how well diabetes is managed. Compare different days, weeks or even months to see the impact of a medication adjustment, or simply to see how a patient has progressed.

  • STATISTICS provides detailed metrics displayed by time or day of the week. The Ambulatory Glucose Profile, AGP, is a standardized glucose report where you can see your Time In Range, a trends graph and daily profiles.

  • PATIENT GLUCOSE RANGE is only available in Clarity for Clinics. Use this page to customize the patient target range. Changes you make on this page only apply to this Clarity Clinic view and do not change any settings on the patient's Dexcom CGM system or the patient's personal Clarity account. All the interactive reports are available to print, download or email for any date range.

Our general module on glucose sensors provides additional guidance on interpreting these reports effectively.

Sharing data with healthcare providers may seem daunting at first. Take it step by step, creating your Clarity account and exploring the options available. Remember that sharing data empowers your healthcare team to provide you with better care, making it worth the effort.

docs.title: 5.3 Sharing data with family
docs.text:
Using the Dexcom ONE Plus app, you can share your data with up to 10 caregivers through the Dexcom Follow app.

The process involves these steps:

  • In the Dexcom ONE Plus app, users can navigate to the "Connections" section and select "Share" to invite a follower using their email address. Within this process, they have the option to share the current sensor reading along with the trend graph or opt to share without it. Additionally, they can make suggestions for notifications that the follower will receive.

  • The follower will then receive an email with an invitation to download the app from the app store or start following if they already downloaded the app. In the Dexcom Follow app, they can go through any information screens. They can now see the glucose information of the person wearing the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor. The follower automatically gets the settings that were set for them, but they can go into settings and change them at any time. This only changes their settings and does not impact the alerts on the app of the person wearing the sensor. For example if they want to be notified when the user is over 300 mg/dl or 16.6 mmol/l, they can set a high notification. Using the Share and Follow function of the Dexcom ONE Plus app can really offer peace of mind to the user and the follower.

Sharing data with your healthcare provider and loved ones is a collaborative step towards better diabetes management. Remember, it takes a supportive network to navigate this journey successfully. You can pause sharing or remove individual followers at any time.

docs.title: 6. Connecting other devices (5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor to other devices.

In this chapter, we explore the exciting possibilities of connecting your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor with various devices, such as smartwatches, smart pens, and insulin pumps.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatch
docs.text:
Dexcom offers native applications that enable real-time monitoring of your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor data on your smartwatch. Additionally, some compatible smartwatches have the capability to replicate smartphone notifications, including alarms and other app alerts.

Any brand of smartwatch may display some alerts, even if it isn't set up with your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor. On your smartwatch lock screen, you can tap OK to acknowledge the alert. That will also acknowledge the alert in your app.

It's worth noting that while the future holds the promise of a direct connection between your smartwatch and Dexcom ONE Plus sensor, this feature is not yet available at the time of this video. Therefore, all the discussed options will still require you to have your phone with you to receive your sensor data on your smartwatch.

In this guide, we will explore the setup options for popular smartwatches such as Garmin, Samsung Galaxy, and the Apple Watch.

docs.title: #1 Garmin Smartwatch
docs.text:
To view your Dexcom ONE Plus glucose data on your Garmin watch, follow these steps:

  • Install the Garmin Connect IQ Store app on your phone.

  • In the Garmin Connect IQ Store app, search for "Dexcom", and select the "Dex CGM Watch Face" from the search results. If your Garmin watch is connected to your phone, you can proceed to install the watch face.

  • Configure the Dex CGM Watch Face by entering your Dexcom username and password in its settings. If the watch face doesn't automatically appear on your Garmin watch, you can manually select it from the available watch faces.

docs.title: #2 Samsung Galaxy Smartwatch
docs.text:
To access your Dexcom ONE Plus glucose data on your Samsung Galaxy Watch, follow these steps:

  • Access the App Store on your Galaxy Watch and search for the "Blose" app.

  • Download and install the Blose app on your Galaxy Watch.

  • Configure the Blose app by following the provided instructions. This entails signing in to your Dexcom Clarity account via your connected phone.

  • You can customize various settings such as glucose units, glucose target values, alert preferences, and the appearance of the watch face.

  • Search for and install the Blose watch face from the available options provided within the app.

  • Once installed, you will be able to view your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor data directly on your Samsung Galaxy Watch.

docs.title: #3 Apple Watch
docs.text:
The Dexcom ONE Plus app includes an Apple Watch app. If the watch app hasn't been automatically installed, you can manually install it by accessing the Watch app on your iPhone.

On your Dexcom ONE Plus app of your Apple Watch, you can conveniently monitor your current glucose levels, observe the trend arrow, and view your glucose graph from the past 1 to 6 hours. You can zoom in and out by tapping or swiping on the screen.

To quickly access the ONE Plus app from your watch, consider adding a watchface complication. This allows you to open the app with a simple tap on the complication.

Please note that these Dexcom ONE Plus watchface complications are only compatible with specific watch faces. For instance, you can use the Infograph Modular watch face and place the Dexcom Complication in one of the available complication positions.

Keep in mind that Apple Watch complications update information at intervals of 15-45 minutes. Tapping the complication will display a larger Dexcom sensor graph with the most recent blood glucose reading.

It’s clear that the process of setting up your Dexcom ONE Plus data on your smartwatch depends heavily on the specific combination of phone and smartwatch you own. If the provided instructions appear challenging to follow, there's no need to worry. It often becomes much simpler when you have your devices with you and can follow along in real-time.

docs.title: 6.2 Smartpens
docs.text:
Currently, the Dexcom ONE Plus app does not have direct compatibility with any smartpens. However, there is a workaround available. You can make a Glooko account and link it to your Clarity account. Additionally, you can connect your Novopen 6, Novopen Echo Plus, SoloSmart Button, Tempo Smart Button or InPen to Glooko.

By logging into Glooko, you'll have access to integrated data from both Dexcom and your smartpen or smartpen cap. This setup offers a comprehensive solution for monitoring both your glucose levels and insulin data within a single platform.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
The Dexcom ONE Plus sensor is not compatible with commercial automated insulin delivery systems, but it can be utilized in open-source automated insulin delivery systems.

DIY Loop, iAPS and Trio users can integrate the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor data without additional tools, while AndroidAPS users will require a third-party app like xDrip to transmit the Dexcom ONE Plus data to the AndroidAPS app.

It's crucial to understand that open-source apps are not supported by Dexcom, nor are they approved by the FDA or European authorities. Engaging in this practice carries inherent risks and is a personal choice. While we don't endorse it, we believe in providing transparency about all available options.

To conclude, we recommend embracing the flexibility of connecting your Dexcom ONE Plus sensor to various devices, as it enables you to tailor your Dexcom ONE Plus experience and enhance your diabetes management approach. Adaptability empowers you to make choices that align with your unique needs and preferences.

docs.title: 7. Case report (1.5 min)
docs.text:
Congratulations to all of you for successfully completing this comprehensive course on the Dexcom ONE Plus sensor! Your dedication to understanding the features, functionalities, and best practices of this continuous glucose monitoring system is commendable and a significant step towards improving your diabetes management.

Throughout this course, we have delved into various aspects of the Dexcom ONE Plus, and each lesson has provided you with valuable knowledge and practical insights to optimize the use of the Dexcom ONE Plus in your daily life.

To further enhance your understanding of using the Dexcom ONE Plus in real-world scenarios, we highly recommend watching the YouTube video shared below this final chapter. In this video, Justin from Diabe_tech talks to Jake Leach, the CEO of Dexcom, about the recent launch of this new sensor. Jake shares why the Dexcom ONE Plus was developed, and how they will position the sensor in Europe. This video provides additional perspectives and insights that complement the theoretical understanding covered in this course.

To access the video, please click on the link below this video:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Xmx_Z8igRbg&t=4s

In conclusion, completing this course signifies your commitment to embracing advanced diabetes management tools and techniques. The Dexcom ONE Plus offers you the opportunity to take control of your glucose monitoring and make data-driven decisions that positively impact your health and well-being.

Once again, congratulations on your achievement! Equipped with the knowledge and skills you have acquired, you are well-prepared to embark on your Dexcom ONE Plus journey with confidence and resilience. May this new chapter in your diabetes management be filled with improved glucose control, enhanced peace of mind, and a greater sense of empowerment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alerts
  • 5. Data sharing
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this course on the Eversense 365 system. In this video, we'll explore the intricacies of the world's longest-lasting CGM. Let’s get started!

Measuring approximately 3.5 mm x 18.3 mm, the Eversense 365 sensor is placed under the skin of your upper arm through a tiny incision and secured with adhesive strips. After a 24-hour Warm-Up, the sensor works with an on-body transmitter to measure your glucose real-time. You can conveniently track your glucose readings updated every five minutes using the Eversense 365 app on your smartphone. The Eversense 365 sensor uses fluorescence to measure glucose in interstitial fluid. It is intended for individuals with diabetes 18 years or older and lasts for up to 1 year.

In the "Master Module on Glucose Sensors", we covered general information about glucose sensors, including insights on when to trust the sensor readings and tips for handling sensor disconnections and allergies. In the upcoming videos, we will focus on the start-up education for the Eversense 365 sensor and provide advanced tips and tricks for healthcare providers.

This course is designed to empower both healthcare providers and individuals with diabetes to unlock the full potential of the Eversense 365. But before we dive in, remember that these videos do not serve as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

These videos have been created by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content has been reviewed by Gary Scheiner, a Certified Diabetes Care and Education Specialist from Integrated Diabetes Services in the US. Their expertise and perspectives ensure that you receive honest opinions on the pros and cons of the Eversense 365 system.

Be assured, all the information provided here is completely independent from Senseonics, the sensor manufacturing company, ensuring a genuine perspective on the advantages and drawbacks of the Eversense 365.

By the end of this course, you will have gained a comprehensive understanding of how to:

  • properly insert and remove the Eversense 365 sensor;

  • start the sensor using the smart transmitter and the mobile app;

  • set up alerts to receive timely notifications;

  • share your glucose data with your healthcare provider and loved ones;

  • connect the Eversense 365 sensor to other devices; and

  • valuable tips and tricks to streamline your experience and enhance your daily diabetes management.

As a healthcare provider, you will gain the knowledge needed to guide individuals through using the Eversense 365 effectively, while users themselves will discover a wealth of tips and tricks to enhance their experience. Good luck on your journey with the Eversense 365!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (10.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on placing the Eversense 365 sensor.

In this video, we’ll guide you through the process of inserting and removing the Eversense 365 sensor, and cover some do’s and don’ts.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text:
Let's begin by gathering the essential supplies:

  • A sensor kit, which contains the sensor within a sensor holder;

  • An insertion tools kit, where you'll find the incision template, the blunt dissector, and the insertion tool; and

  • A transmitter kit, which houses the smart transmitter.

You’ll also need:

  • Sterile, non-latex surgical gloves of your size;

  • Chlorhexidine OR Betadine solution;

  • A sterile drape, roughly 22 by 25 inches or 50 by 60 centimeters — with a hole in it;

  • A surgical skin marker;

  • 2 mL of lidocaine hydrochloride with epinephrine;

  • A sterile syringe and needle;

  • A sterile towel drape;

  • 10 mL of 0.9% sterile saline-filled syringe;

  • A disposable sterile scalpel (number 15);

  • Sterile gauze pads;

  • A sterile surgical clamp (of 10 to 16 cm);

  • Steri-Strip™ adhesive skin closure; and

  • Tegaderm™ + pad film dressing.

The sterile drapes are in the kits supplied by Senseonics.

docs.title: 2.2 Inserting the sensor
docs.text:
Eversense Placement & Removal – Training Video on Vimeo

Sensor placement is done while the patient is fully awake. The entire procedure takes about five minutes and consists of five steps.

1. Site preparation

2. Incision

3. Sensor insertion

4. Site closure

5. Sensor and smart transmitter linking

Begin by washing your hands. Next, identify the location for the sensor placement. Ideally, the insertion area is approximately at the midway point between the acromion process and the lateral epicondyle, parallel to the humerus, in the subcutaneous tissue. Ensure that the selected insertion site allows the smart transmitter to stay flat during movement and that the patient can easily reach it. Using the transmitter, mark the edges on the skin, then align the incision template inside the marked lines. Use the incision template’s slots to mark a vertical sensor pocket path and horizontal incision line. Place the patient in a reclined position preferably on their side, with the upper arm on a flat horizontal plane.

Now that the patient has been positioned, let's move to sensor preparation.

Set up a flat clean surface designated as the sterile field. Put on the surgical gloves and present the tool tray.

Remove the red locking tab from the white sensor insertion tool by pulling it back. Loosen the tools from the tray.

Cycle and test the blue slide forward and back twice. Test the lock, and leave the blue slide in the forward and lock position.

Snap the insertion tool back into the tray. Wet the cannula by filling the preformed well with approximately 10 mL of sterile saline to completely cover the cannula.

Place the sensor holder on a flat clean surface designated as the sterile field. Unsnap the insertion tool. Pick up the sensor holder and squeeze the ridges. Remove and discard the cap.

Next, keeping the insertion tool in the slightly upright position, retract the blue slide. Firmly support the tip of the sensor holder. Align slots of holder and the tool. Align the arrows and slide onto the cannula until it clicks.

Cover the sensor by slowly advancing the blue slide. Squeeze the ridges and remove the holder. Visually check the tip of the cannula for the sensor.

Snap the insertion tool into place, and hydrate for five minutes.

With the sensor prepared, you’re ready to insert it.

Start by scrubbing the antiseptic at the incision line and working outward in circles. Repeat this process two more times, for three total passes. Allow the antiseptic to dry between passes.

When dry, drape the patient. Inject lidocaine with epinephrine at 20 degrees. Bevel up, injecting 1 milliliter at the incision line and 1 milliliter up the sensor pocket path. Apply light pressure over the area. Wait 1½ to 2 minutes and check effect.

Hold the scalpel with a pencil grip. Punch through the dermal layer to a depth of 5 millimeters. Remove the scalpel. Rotate 180 degrees, reinsert, and advance for uniform edges. Apply pressure to control any bleeding.

Hold the blunt dissector tool with the index finger resting in the finger well. Avoid placing the fingers underneath.

Introduce into the midline of the incision at a 45-degree angle. When the depth guards touch the skin, drop the tool flat. The depth guards assist with proper placement in the subcutaneous tissue. Rotate the tool slightly as you advance the entire length.

Keep the tool in the pocket for 30 to 45 seconds to decrease bleeding. Remove the tool and apply pressure to control bleeding.

Hold the insertion tool with fingers on the side, not underneath. Introduce the insertion tool under the lip of the incision into the subcutaneous pocket. Using a 45- to 90-degree angle, drop the tool flat. Follow the same pocket and advance to between the marks on the cannula. Stabilize the tool with the dominant hand. Use the other hand to unlock the blue slide at the widest end and slide back. This deploys the sensor into the pocket.

Remove the tool, and check to make sure that the sensor is not in the cannula. Remember, a correctly placed sensor will be in the subcutaneous tissue parallel to the humerus.

Apply pressure for several minutes as needed to control any bleeding.

Align the edges of the incision and apply 3 to 4 parallel Steri-Strips to fully close the wound. If desired, place 2 additional Steri-Strips in a criss-cross pattern over the top. Cover with Tegaderm, ensuring the Steri-Strips are completely under the gauze pad.

Lastly, link the sensor and the transmitter. We’ll go into detail on linking these two components in the next lesson.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text:
Eversense Placement & Removal – Training Video on Vimeo

Remember that removal of the Eversense 365 sensor takes place up to one year after its placement.

Like sensor insertion, removal takes place while the patient is fully awake. The approximately 5-minute process involves four steps, namely:

1. Site preparation

2. Incision

3. Sensor removal

4. Site closure

Start with the patient in the upright position. Assess the sensor location and clean the skin. Make a reference dot on each end of the sensor.

Place the patient in a lateral recumbent position with the upper arm on a flat horizontal plane and sensor markings facing up toward the ceiling.

With the reference dots as a guide, use the placement guide to find an excellent signal. Mark the transmitter edges.

Using the reference dots, palpate and mark the sensor ends with horizontal lines. Draw a rectangle around the sensor and a horizontal line through the middle of the rectangle. Mark the incision line about 3 to 5 millimeters below the distal end of the sensor and 8 millimeters in width.

Now scrub the antiseptic at the incision line, working outward in circles. Repeat this process two more times for three total passes. Allow the antiseptic to dry between passes.

When dry, drape the patient. Inject lidocaine at 20 degrees bevel up, injecting 1 milliliter at the incision line, and 1 milliliters on each side of the sensor. Apply light pressure over the area. Wait 1½ to 2 minutes, and check effect.

Holding the scalpel with the pencil grip, pop through the dermal layer, and make an 8-millimeter-wide incision. Remove the scalpel, rotate 180 degrees, reinsert, and advance for uniform edges. Apply pressure to control any bleeding.

Stretch the skin taut using the thumb and index finger of the non-dominant hand. Push down on the lower lip of the incision as you introduce the clamp tip at 30 degrees. Open and close the clamp to the width of the incision 3 to 5 times.

Now, with the clamp open the full width of the incision, maintain gentle pressure on the lower lip and advance to the horizontal line. Close the clamp. If there is no gap in the clamp jaws, the sensor hasn’t been grasped. Maintain the clamp location and lift the wrist slightly to change the clamp angle, and close. A gap indicates you have the sensor.

Release the thumb and finger tension. Make a quarter turn of the wrist and remove the sensor.

Apply pressure to the incision for several minutes as needed to control any bleeding.

Align the edges of the incision and apply 3 to 4 parallel Steri-Strips to fully close the wound. If desired, place 2 additional Steri-Strips in a criss-cross pattern over the top. Cover With Tegaderm. Make sure the Steri-Strips are completely under the gauze pad.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Let's go over some do’s and don’ts when placing the Eversense 365 sensor.

  • Be aware that you need to be properly trained and certified by Sensionics before performing the insertion procedure.

  • Choose the insertion site such that the patient is comfortable wearing the smart transmitter 24/7.

  • Ensure the insertion area is not too far to the side to allow the patient to easily apply the adhesive patch.

  • Avoid areas with loose skin, such as the back of the arm, as well as areas with scar tissue, tattoo, nevus, or apparent or noticeable blood vessels that could be incised.

  • Before inserting the sensor, confirm that the patient does not have allergies to the antiseptic and lidocaine to be used during insertion.

  • Insert the sensor using only the insertion tools provided in the insertion tool kit. Other insertion tools may damage the sensor.

  • Pinch and tent the skin to aid in forming a small pocket in the skin for insertion.

  • Do not create a pocket more than 3-5 mm below the skin. If the sensor is placed too deep, it may be difficult to communicate with the smart transmitter or to remove later.

  • Make sure that the subcutaneous pocket is parallel to and along the same axis as the humerus bone. When you insert the sensor, it should be level in the pocket, which will facilitate communication between the sensor and the smart transmitter.

  • Link the sensor and the smart transmitter on the day of insertion. Failure to link the sensor and smart transmitter could lead to a delay in receiving glucose readings.

  • Finally, alternate arms for next insertion sites.

We’ve now covered all the steps for the Eversense 365 sensor insertion and removal, from preparing the insertion site to closing the incision, as well as do’s and don’ts. By mastering this procedure, you are building a solid foundation for reliable, long-term monitoring, which ensures accurate data that empowers you to make the most informed treatment decisions.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (14 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on starting the Eversense 365 sensor.

In this video, we will delve into linking the Eversense 365 sensor to the smart transmitter and getting started with the mobile app. We will also highlight key do’s and don’ts for using the Eversense 365 system.

docs.title: 3.1 Smart transmitter
docs.text:
The first thing to do is download the Eversense 365 CGM Mobile App from the App Store or Google Play. The Eversense 365 App can be downloaded on most mobile phones, with separate versions available for iOS and Android devices. You can find a list of all compatible devices and software versions on this webpage: https://www.eversensecgm.com/compatibility/

Open the app, then review and accept the End User License Agreement. If you already have an Eversense account, you can log in to the Eversense 365 app with your existing login credentials. Otherwise, tap "Create an account" and complete the Eversense account creation screens. Be sure to use an email address you can access. Take note of your Eversense account email and password, then tap "Submit."

Check your email for the account activation link. Click the link in your email to activate your account. Go back to the Eversense 365 CGM Mobile App, tap to log in, and enter the account email and password you just created.

Enable Notifications and Bluetooth to allow system communication and to receive system alerts. Confirm your unit of measurement and tap "Next." Review and confirm the app tutorial.

From the Welcome screen, you can pair your mobile phone with your smart transmitter. Make sure the transmitter is fully charged and powered on.

To start pairing mode, tap the soft-touch button three times on your smart transmitter until the light blinks blue. The smart transmitter serial number should match. Tap on the serial number and accept the pairing request. Once connected, tap "Next."

To begin the linking process, tap "Sensor." Hold the smart transmitter, without the adhesive patch, in place over the sensor to find the best signal. Tap "Next." Continue to hold your smart transmitter in place until the sensor linking is complete. This could take several minutes.

When the detected serial number appears, tap "Link detected sensor." You'll see the linking process progress. When the sensor is successfully linked, you can remove the smart transmitter from over the sensor. Tap "Next."

Now the 24-hour Warm-Up Phase begins; we also call this period “Day 1.” Note the date and time when the 24-hour Warm-up Phase will be complete.

Also, you won't need to wear your smart transmitter over your sensor during this phase. But it’s a great time to program your personalized glucose settings into your Eversense 365 CGM System.

After that, you can return your smart transmitter to the charger until the Warm-Up Phase is complete.

During the Warm-Up Phase, you won't be getting any glucose data. Continue to monitor your glucose levels with your blood glucose meter throughout this period and until you begin to receive glucose values from your Eversense 365 CGM.

When you receive the "Warm-Up Complete" notification, tap "Continue." Remove your smart transmitter from the charger. It will automatically reconnect with your mobile app. The System Setup screen will take you to the next step, the Initialization Phase.

To begin, get an adhesive patch and peel off the paper backing with the Eversense logo. The center of the patch is sticky, and this is where you should place your smart transmitter, as shown. Press the smart transmitter firmly onto the adhesive. Then, remove the larger piece of the remaining clear backing on the adhesive patch. Finally, position the smart transmitter over the sensor.

If you have an over wrap on your arm, you will need to remove it so that you can see the Tegaderm bandage. It’s helpful to understand where your sensor is located. Your health care provider inserted the sensor above the incision, as shown here. This means that the sensor is likely above the center of your Tegaderm bandage.

There may also be visible marks where your health care provider marked the corners of the smart transmitter before placing your sensor. Aligning the smart transmitter in this location helps to place it appropriately over the sensor. If needed, go to a mirror in order to clearly see your Tegaderm bandage.

Open the Placement Guide in your app by going to Main Menu — Placement Guide. You’ll see a black X at first.

Hold the smart transmitter with adhesive, with the sides of the adhesive patch pulled back. If visible, use the smart transmitter corner marks on your skin to position the smart transmitter toward the top half of the Tegaderm bandage. Note that the smart transmitter needs to be touching your arm. Gently lift and slightly move the smart transmitter plus adhesive to find the location with the strongest signal, somewhere between 2 to 3 bars or good to excellent. Rotating the smart transmitter slightly may help. When moving the transmitter, allow a few seconds for the signal to update before moving the transmitter again. Once you have obtained a signal of two to three bars, leave the smart transmitter on your arm there.

Remove the smaller remaining piece of clear backing and smooth out the adhesive patch. Finally, close the Placement Guide by tapping the Next icon.

Now that you’ve successfully positioned your smart transmitter, you’ll see a Calibrate Now notification appear in your mobile app within a few minutes. This means it’s time to do your first of four fingerstick calibrations.

You can complete the calibrations in as little as 6 hours, since you can calibrate as often as every 2 hours. You must enter calibrations no more than 12 hours apart. But you won’t have to remember the time for each calibration because your Eversense 365 CGM System will remind you each time with a Calibrate Now notification.

To enter a calibration, do a fingerstick blood glucose check and tap on the blue Calibrate button.

Tap on Glucose and scroll to enter the correct blood glucose value, then tap Done. Tap Submit to confirm that the correct glucose value has been selected and the fingerstick glucose test was performed no more than 10 minutes ago. You'll receive a Calibration in Progress notification which you can clear by tapping OK.

Here’s an example. Let’s say you linked your sensor and smart transmitter at 9:45 am on Monday, so your Warm-Up phase ends at 9:45 AM on Tuesday.

You use the Placement Guide to put the smart transmitter on your arm over the sensor. Around 10:00 AM on Tuesday, you receive your first "Calibrate Now" notification. You perform a fingerstick blood glucose check and enter the result into the mobile app.

Two hours later, the 2nd Calibrate Now notification appears, and once you enter that 2nd blood glucose value, sensor glucose data begins to appear on your My Glucose home screen.

Calibration prompts 3 and 4 will follow in the same fashion. But remember, you don’t have to calibrate right away – you have up to 12 hours to perform the next required calibration. But it is ideal to complete all 4 before going to bed, so that you will have the benefit of continuous data that first night

Once the initialization phase is complete, the system requires just 1 calibration per day for 13 days.

After day 13, the system requires only 1 calibration per week.

docs.title: 3.2 Eversense 365 app
docs.text:
The MY GLUCOSE screen is the main display screen for the app. The Menu icon at the top left corner provides easy navigation to other app features. The following menu items are available:

  • Calibrate;

  • Placement Guide;

  • Event Log;

  • Reports;

  • Share My Data;

  • Alert History;

  • System Setup;

  • Connect;

  • Settings; and

  • About.

If you have activated the Do Not Disturb feature or the Temporary Profile feature, these icons also appear in the top left. At the top center, the status bar provides important information about your current glucose and system status. Meanwhile, the icons at the top right indicate your smart transmitter ID, the strength of your smart connection with your sensor or mobile device, and the battery power left in your smart transmitter.

The current glucose reading is your real-time glucose level in the indicated unit of measurement. The reading is updated every 5 minutes.

The trend arrow shows the current direction of your glucose levels, and how fast they are changing. There are seven different trend arrows. The app uses the last 20 minutes of continuous glucose data for calculating glucose trends.

Below the current glucose reading and unit of measurement, you'll find the date and time, and further down, the glucose trend graph. In this graph, the red dashed lines indicate your high and low glucose alert levels, while the green dashed lines indicate your target range. Any event that you enter into your app, such as a calibration, a meal, exercise or insulin — or any alerts you receive — show up on your trend graph with an icon.

docs.title: 3.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Now, let's discuss some do's and don'ts for using the Eversense 365 System.

  • Link the sensor and the smart transmitter on the day of insertion.

  • If the smart transmitter has been turned off to avoid vibrations during the Warm-Up Phase, turn it back on at the 24th hour. It will take about 5 minutes after the smart transmitter is placed over the sensor for the first calibration prompt to be displayed.

  • If possible, start System Initialization early in the day. This will allow you to complete all four required initialization calibrations before going to bed.

  • You can remove your smart transmitter from your upper arm at any time, except during calibration. Keep your smart transmitter in place over the sensor for at least five minutes before and 15 minutes after each calibration.

  • Always wash your hands well and dry them thoroughly before calibration.

  • Avoid calibrating when glucose levels may be changing rapidly such as after meals, after taking insulin, or during or after exercise.

  • The smart transmitter comes with a 12-month warranty. The system will notify you once the transmitter has been in use for over 365 days.

  • Your package contains a USB charging kit and silicone-based adhesive patches. Use only the supplied AC adapter and USB cable. Using other power sources can damage the transmitter or cause fire risk.

  • When fully charged, the transmitter battery lasts approximately 24 hours. The transmitter must be removed from your body for charging. Charge it daily for about 15 minutes (or until the LED turns green). Reattach it only after charging is complete.

  • While the transmitter is off your body — during charging or for any other reason — no glucose data or alerts are generated, and once reattached, the app will not backfill the missing data. To limit data gaps, keep the transmitter off your body for as short a time as possible.

  • The smart transmitter has a soft-touch button. To use the smart transmitter, touch and hold or tap your finger directly on this button, similar to using a smartphone. The soft-touch button cannot detect a touch through clothing.

  • To turn the smart transmitter ON, press and hold the soft touch button for 3 seconds. You’ll feel a quick vibration, and the LED will blink once, indicating the power is ON.

  • To turn the smart transmitter OFF, touch and hold your finger on the soft-touch button for about 5 seconds. The LED will turn on, you will feel a quick vibe, and the LED will turn off, signaling the power is OFF.

  • The communication distance between the app and the smart transmitter is up to 24.9 feet or 7.6 meter. But this range will decrease in water, such as when you are in a bathtub or pool. When the transmitter and mobile device are not within range of each other, any data gathered by the transmitter is stored and sent to the app when the two devices are back within range.

  • The smart transmitter can only be in water up to 1 meter (or 3.2 feet) deep for 30 minutes max. Exposing the smart transmitter to conditions beyond this will result in damage and void your warranty.

  • It is safe for you to wear your sensor and smart transmitter when you go through metal detectors at airports. Let security know you have a medical device and show your Medical Device Notification Card.

  • While flying, the smart transmitter performs similarly to any other Bluetooth device.

  • Do not use the smart transmitter if it is damaged or cracked as this could result in electrical shock.

  • Do not wear the smart transmitter during X-rays, CT scans, or MRI scans to avoid interference with results. The sensor itself can safely remain in place during these procedures.

  • Do not exchange smart transmitters with another person. Each smart transmitter can be linked to only one sensor at a time.

  • As for the adhesive patches, you'll have to replace them daily. You can remove and replace the same adhesive patch more than once a day, but repeated removal and replacement may reduce adhesion strength.

  • You can choose from either white or clear variants of the patch. The white adhesive is perforated and may be better at wicking away moisture. The clear adhesive is best for discretion. Try both types to determine which one works best for you.

  • Only apply the adhesives to clean, dry skin. Remove any lotion or soap before placing the adhesive patch and smart transmitter.

  • Although the patches use a medical-grade, non-reactive silicone adhesive, they may still cause a reaction or skin irritation.

We've now explored the Eversense 365 system, delving into the setup and features of the smart transmitter and the app. As you guide your patients through their first days with the Eversense 365, remember that technology can occasionally present hurdles. If you or your patients encounter difficulties, don't get discouraged. Stay persistent, explore troubleshooting options, and reach out to customer support for assistance. With your foundational knowledge, patience and determination, you can confidently empower your patients to unlock the full potential of their long-term CGM.

docs.title: 4. Setting alerts (5.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on setting alerts with the Eversense 365 sensor.

In this session, we will explore available alerts and notifications with the Eversense 365 system and guide you through the process of setting them on the mobile app. Let's dive right in!

docs.title: 4.1 Available alerts
docs.text:
The Eversense 365 system gives you alerts on your glucose readings in three ways: vibrations of your smart transmitter, sounds from your mobile device, and visual notifications on the My Glucose screen. For example, if your glucose falls to, or below, your low glucose alert level, you’ll feel your smart transmitter vibrate in a unique pattern, hear a sound from your mobile device, and see the alert pop-up on your app.

In addition to low, high, and out-of-range glucose alerts, you can receive predictive and rate of change alerts with the Eversense 365. Predictive alerts allow you to receive an “early” warning 10, 20, or 30 minutes before your sensor glucose level is expected to cross the Low or High Glucose Alert threshold, based on current glucose trends. On the other hand, you can get Rate of Change Alerts when your sensor glucose level is rising or falling faster than the Rate of Change threshold you’ve set. If you are new to CGM, wait to set predictive or rate-of-change alerts until you are accustomed to wearing your system.

Another alert is the Calibrate Now alert. The Eversense 365 system maintains accuracy during the 13 days through once-daily calibrations. Then, starting at day 14, your system prompts you to calibrate once weekly. It will alert you with the Calibrate Now alert when a calibration is needed. Please note that when you do receive the Calibrate Now alert, you have a 24-hour window to complete the calibration to maintain uninterrupted access to your CGM data.

Besides alerts on your glucose levels, you may also receive system status alerts, including alerts on your sensor, smart transmitter, Eversense 365 account, and Internet connection.

With the smart transmitter, there are four vibration patterns dedicated to high-priority glucose alerts. The transmitter also uses four other patterns for lower-priority alerts and notifications. You'll keep receiving these on-body vibe alerts even when you're away from your mobile device. But keep in mind that you cannot change any of these patterns.

Also, take note that alerts are different from notifications. An alert is an important message that needs your attention. Alerts appear in yellow on your app. In contrast, a notification is a low priority message, such as a calibration reminder, that appears in blue.

If you ever need to look back and review any alert or notification that you have received, just go to the Main Menu and tap Alert History. The ALERT HISTORY screen will list ALL alerts and notifications for that day. Tap on any message to get more information. Alternatively, you can choose to review only certain messages by tapping the icons on the top of the screen.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alerts on the Eversense 365 app
docs.text:
You can choose different ringtones for low vs. high alerts and determine how often they repeat. Let's say you want to customize the tone you hear for the Low Glucose Alert. To do this, tap Menu, then, Settings, and Sound Settings. Tap the different tones that are available to listen to a preview. Tones should get your attention and be different from any other sound notifications you use with other apps.

By setting the snooze alert, you can set how often an alert repeats after you have received a Low Glucose and High Glucose alert. Tap each snooze alert to set how often the alert repeats, then tap Done when complete.

If your mobile device is running iOS 12 and above or Android 6 and above, you can optionally allow Low and Out of Range Low Glucose alerts to always override your mobile device sound settings. When turned on, the Low and Out of Range Low Glucose alerts will be played at your phone's maximum volume. You cannot change the volume level for this override setting. This allows you to still receive Low and Out of Range Low Glucose alerts on your phone, even if your phone's Silent Mode is on, or Do Not Disturb is on. This feature works similar to apps with an alarm clock feature that will always sound regardless of your phone sound settings. You must allow this in your mobile device.

For iOS devices, start by tapping the button to turn on the override. Tap Settings, then tap Allow.

For Android devices, tap the button to turn on the override. Tap settings, then tap on Eversense 365 from the Do Not Disturb access page.

On a final note, turn on only the alerts that fit your diabetes management plan and your daily life. If you're unsure where to start, talk to your healthcare provider for advice on choosing and setting the right alerts. They can help you get the most out of your Eversense 365 system. Start with the most basic alerts, like low and high glucose alerts. You can add more alerts to your routine later. This helps you slowly adjust to the system.

In this lesson, we've examined the Eversense 365 alert system. By correctly utilizing its features and staying resilient, we can set and rely on alerts to signal critical shifts in our glucose levels, enabling us to take the necessary action to stay in control.

docs.title: 5. Data sharing (5.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Eversense 365 system.

In this session, you’ll learn how to share your data with your healthcare provider and loved ones and create glucose reports.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your healthcare provider
docs.text:
The Eversense Data Management Software Program, or DMS Pro for short, is a free web-based app that enables patients, caregivers, and health care professionals to view and analyze glucose data that has been transmitted from the smart transmitter or the app.

Clinic Share is an optional feature on your app that allows you to either invite or accept an invitation from your healthcare provider's clinic using DMS Pro. To invite your healthcare provider, you'll need their DMS Pro Clinic ID number. Contact your clinic for this information.

From the Main Menu, go to "Share My Data” and “Clinic Share.” Then tap "Clinic Requests" and "Send Requests." Enter the DMS Pro clinic ID number, and tap "Add." Confirm the name of your clinic, then tap "Yes" to send the request. Tap "OK" to acknowledge the request has been sent. Your pending invitation will be displayed in red until your clinic accepts, upon which the name will change to black. To stop sharing data, tap on the clinic name and choose "Stop Sharing."

To receive a request, your clinic must use the email associated with your Eversense account. To check for pending requests, go to "Share My Data." Tap "Clinic Share," "Clinic Requests," and "Accept Requests." Tap on the clinic name in red, then tap "Accept" or "Decline" before confirming your selection. The clinic will appear on our "My Clinics" list.

docs.title: 5.2 Sharing data with your loved ones
docs.text:
Using your Eversense 365 app, you also can invite your family and friends to join your "Circle." From the Share My Data screen, tap "My Circle" and "Invite to My Circle." Enter the email of the person you'd like to invite; a nickname is optional. You can also tap the "+" sign to select an email address from your Contacts. Tap "Send" then "OK" on the pop-up.

Your family member or friend will use the Eversense NOW Remote Monitoring App to monitor your CGM data. They can accept your invitation through the Eversense NOW app, or can follow the instructions in the email they receive. In your CGM app, their name will appear in red until they accept your invitation.

To remove someone from your circle, tap on their name, then tap "Remove" and confirm the pop-up notification. Finally, tap "OK" to confirm they are no longer in your Circle.

To temporarily stop sharing data with a member without removing them from your Circle, tap the member's name in the My Circle list to open the MEMBER screen. Then tap the Share My Data button to turn on/off data share with this member.

It’s important to note that you should not use glucose information displayed on the Eversense NOW App to make treatment decisions. The Eversense NOW App does not communicate directly with your sensor and/or smart transmitter. This makes the Eversense NOW App a secondary display of your CGM data that should not be used in place of the primary Eversense 365 App display.

docs.title: 5.3 Creating reports
docs.text:
Your Eversense 365 app automatically creates four reports you can view at any time, right in the app. These reports are the:

  • Glucose Pie Chart, which shows what percent of your readings within a given time period are within, below or above your Glucose Target levels;

  • Time in Range, which displays percentage of time you have spent in pre-set glucose ranges, based on standardized guidelines;

  • Weekly Modal Summary, which provides a seven-day summary of your glucose information in a 24-hour line graph; and

  • Glucose Statistics, which shows average, low, and high glucose values based on preset times.

To view your glucose reports on the app, tap Menu then Reports, and swipe to move across the different reports. You can also email each report by tapping the email icon in the top right-hand corner.

As a reminder, be sure to set your mobile device date and time correctly. The accuracy of the graphs and reports depends upon the accuracy of the date and time.

Furthermore, you can access reports on DMS Pro. To do this, go to https://us.eversensedms.com and enter your log-in information. On the left navigation pane, click on Reports to open the list of reports.

You may print or email your reports. Click on the printer icon to view and print your report, or click on the envelope icon to email them.

Located in the settings menu, REPORT SUMMARY settings allow you to set up a regularly-scheduled automated report that will be emailed to you. You can select how often the reports are sent, and which reports you want to receive.

In this video, you've learned how to use Eversense 365’s data sharing tools. Remember, a supportive network is vital for managing this journey. By sharing your CGM data, you empower your team with the actionable insights required to foster resilience and drive optimal health outcomes.

docs.title: 6. Connecting other devices (5.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Eversense 365 sensor to other devices.

In this chapter, you'll learn how to integrate your sensor with smartwatches and explore possibilities with smartpens and insulin pumps.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatches
docs.text:
Within the Eversense 365 App, the Share My Data feature allows you to share your Eversense data with Apple Health. In the Menu options, tap Share My Data, then Application Sharing, and Apple Health. Turn on Share Data. You may also need to allow sharing via the health app.

You can view a snapshot of your CGM data on your Apple Watch. Once you’ve downloaded and installed the Eversense 365 App on your mobile device, follow the device’s instructions for adding the app to your watch.

Tap the Eversense 365 icon on your watch HOME screen to open the app. The My Glucose screen shows your current glucose with a trend arrow, and a trend graph of your last three hours of CGM data.

You can also access the MY GLUCOSE screen if you turn on notifications from the Eversense 365 App in your Apple Watch settings. When you receive a notification, you can also tap on the message to see the MY GLUCOSE screen.

Swipe left to the next screen showing a pie chart of your total time within and outside your target range for the past 24 hours. Swipe up to display the same data shown as percentages. Lastly, swipe left to the next screen showing your current glucose with trend arrow, your next calibration time, the current system calibration phase, and battery level of your smart transmitter.

Take note that the Apple Watch is a secondary display of Eversense data and should not be used in place of the Eversense 365 App display. Any alerts and notifications that appear on your Apple Watch should be confirmed within the mobile device app. Any problems with mobile devices, wireless internet, data connection, the Eversense DMS, the smart transmitter being out of range of their mobile device, or charging the smart transmitter may cause data transfer to be delayed or not to be displayed.

On the other hand, connectivity for Android watches requires the use of a third-party app like xDrip+. Please be aware that xDrip+ is not supported by Sensionics and lacks approval from regulatory authorities like the FDA or EMA, making its usage carry inherent risks that should be approached cautiously.

#1 Garmin smartwatch

For Garmin smartwatches, download the xDrip+ app from github.com/nightscoutfoundation/xdrip. Go to the settings of the xDrip+ app and set the data source to Eversense. This allows xDrip+ to receive sensor data from the Eversense 365 app. Also in the settings, adjust the inter-app settings to send the xDrip+ data to your connected Wear OS smartwatch. Then go to the Connect IQ store app on your phone, download the xDrip+/Spike/Nightscout Watch Face, and set xDrip+ as your companion app. On your watch, you can now choose the xDrip+/Spike/Nightscout watch face to see your real-time Eversense data on your Garmin smartwatch.

#2 Samsung Galaxy smartwatch

For Samsung Galaxy smartwatches, follow similar steps by downloading the xDrip+ app and setting the data source to Eversense in the settings. Adjust the xDrip+ app settings to send data to the G-Watch App by checking the Smartwatch and Inter-app settings. Next, install the G-Watch Wear app on both your phone and smartwatch and set xDrip+ as your companion app. Finally, choose the according watch face on your Galaxy watch, and customise the watch face and alerts on your watch as you prefer.

As a reminder, using open-source apps carries inherent risks and is not supported by Sensionics or approved by regulatory authorities. We are sharing this information for transparency, but we do not recommend this practice unless you fully understand the associated risks.

Remember, the setup steps may vary based on your phone and smartwatch, but you can find detailed tutorials in the provided downloads, making it easier to perform the setup with the devices on hand.

docs.title: 6.2 Smartpens
docs.text:
The Eversense 365 system currently does not offer direct integration with smart insulin pens, but there is a workaround available.

Beyond Apple Health, the Eversense 365 system integrates with Glooko . Because smart insulin pens like the Novopen 6, NovoPen Echo Plus, inPen, Tempo Smart, and SoloSmart can also connect to Glooko, you can consolidate both your glucose and insulin data on a single screen, although this integration requires manual setup.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
The Eversense 365 will soon integrate with commercial AID systems, starting with the twiist AID system. For the latest updates, you can visit Eversense's partners page: https://www.eversensecgm.com/partners/

The Eversense CGM is also compatible with open-source AID systems such as AndroidAPS. iPhone-based open-source systems do not yet support Eversense.

In this lesson, you learned how to connect the Eversense 365 CGM to compatible smart devices and diabetes management platforms. Use these integrations to build a seamless setup that supports smarter decisions and more personalized diabetes care.

docs.title: 7. Case report (1.5 min)
docs.text:
Congratulations on completing this course on the Eversense 365! Your commitment to learning how this CGM works is a meaningful step toward more confident and effective diabetes management.

You’ve built a strong foundation; now it’s time to bridge theory with real-world insights. To better understand how the Eversense system performs in daily life, we highly recommend watching this YouTube video linked below:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QI7FROStO28

In this video that has garnered nearly 90,000 views and counting, Tom shares numerous practical tips and day-to-day hacks drawn from his personal experiences with the Eversense 3 sensor. While the Eversense 3 is indicated for up to six months of use, it shares many core features and functions with the Eversense 365, making Tom's insights highly relevant.

Learning from real-world experiences, alongside the concepts we've covered here, can help you turn knowledge into optimal results with the Eversense 365.

Remember, you're not doing this alone. Seek support from your healthcare team, fellow individuals with diabetes, and online communities. Continue to embrace technology to inform your health choices.

Once again, congratulations on completing this course. May your newfound knowledge and determination propel you further to success!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting and scanning the sensor
  • 5. Sharing data
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the FreeStyle Libre 2 and FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensors! In this session, we’ll explore the details of the world’s most widely used glucose monitoring sensors.

The FreeStyle Libre 2 sensors combine a sensor and transmitter in one device, which can be easily placed on your body with a simple click.

The standard FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor functions as a flash glucose monitor. This means you can check your glucose levels by scanning the sensor with your phone or a dedicated reader. It offers a 14-day wear time, measures your glucose every minute, provides high and low glucose alerts, and is approved for people with diabetes aged 4 years and older.

In some countries, an advanced version of the FreeStyle LibreLink or Libre ~~2~~ app is available. This version doesn’t require manual scanning to view glucose readings, making it a true real-time continuous glucose monitor.

The FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus is a version of the FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor that can connect with automated insulin delivery systems like the Omnipod 5. It has a longer wear time of 15 days and is approved for use in children as young as 2 years old.

This course is designed to empower both healthcare providers and individuals with diabetes to unlock the full potential of FreeStyle Libre 2. But before we dive in, remember that these videos do not serve as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any changes to your therapy.

These videos have been created by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content has been reviewed by Dr. Sarah Siegelaar, an endocrinologist at Amsterdam UMC in the Netherlands, as well as Tim Street, an individual with type 1 diabetes who used the FreeStyle Libre 2 and blogs about diabetes technology at diabettech.com.

Be assured, all the information provided here is completely independent from Abbott, the sensor manufacturing company, ensuring a genuine perspective on the advantages and drawbacks of the FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor.

By the end of this module, you will have a comprehensive understanding of the FreeStyle Libre 2 and FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus system. We will cover topics such as sensor placement, starting and scanning the sensor, setting alarms, sharing data, connecting the sensor to other devices and some extra tips & tricks. As a healthcare provider, you will gain the knowledge needed to guide individuals using FreeStyle Libre 2 effectively, while users themselves will discover a wealth of tips and tricks to enhance their experience. Let's embark on this empowering journey together. Good luck!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (2 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson about placement of the FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor. In this video, we will guide you through the process of placing and removing a FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor, and go over some do’s and don’ts. The steps we show also work for the FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensor.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text: To begin, gather the necessary supplies: a sensor box and an alcohol swab. Inside the box, you'll find the sensor applicator and the sensor itself.

docs.title: 2.2 Inserting the sensor
docs.text:
Properly inserting the FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor involves the following steps:

  • Start by washing your hands and selecting the spot on your upper arm where the sensor will be placed. Clean the chosen area with water and soap and let it dry.

  • Open the sensor box by removing the lid and take off the cap from the sensor applicator.

  • Place the sensor applicator inside the open box, aligning the marks on the applicator and the sensor box.

  • Apply firm pressure to the sensor applicator until it stops.

  • Position the loaded sensor applicator on the cleaned and dried spot on your upper arm, then push it firmly until the sensor is securely in place.

  • After application, check the edges of the sensor patch to ensure proper adhesion.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: To remove the FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor, gently and slowly pull it away from your skin. Dispose of the sensor in the appropriate bin.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• For ease of placement on the upper arm, lift your arm and elbow to avoid excessive twisting.

  • When placing the sensor, aim for a 90-degree angle.

As you can see, placing the FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor is quite straightforward, and there’s little room for error. If you’re unsure about any step, an instructional manual is included in every box, so there’s no need to worry.

docs.title: 3. Starting and scanning the sensor (11 min)
3. Starting and scanning the sensor (11 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video, where we'll show you how to start and scan the FreeStyle Libre 2 and FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensors. You can do this using an app on your phone or with a FreeStyle Libre 2 reader.

For simplicity, we will only discuss the LibreLink app. In some regions, however, the dedicated app for the FreeStyle Libre 2 or Libre 2 Plus sensor may appear as the “FreeStyle Libre 2” app or simply the “Libre” app.

If you're using a regular FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor and have both the reader and the LibreLink app on your phone, you can choose which device to use for scanning. You can even use both devices simultaneously by first starting and scanning the sensor with the reader, then with your phone. However, alarms will only be sent to the first device you scan with.

If you're using a FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensor or have access to an updated version of the LibreLink app, you can monitor your glucose in real-time on your phone using the FreeStyle LibreLink app without needing to scan each time. Scanning is only required once to start the sensor. If you only have access to a FreeStyle Libre 2 reader, you can still use it, but it will work as a flash glucose monitor, meaning you'll need to scan the sensor each time to see your glucose levels.

docs.title: 3.1 FreeStyle Libre 2 reader
docs.text:
If you opt to use the FreeStyle Libre 2 reader, let's explore how it works.

The front of the reader features a touchscreen and a home button. On the side, you'll find a micro USB port for charging the reader and reading data. And at the bottom, there is a test strip port, allowing you to measure your blood glucose or blood ketones with a simple finger prick.

The FreeStyle Libre 2 reader is compatible with both FreeStyle Precision blood glucose test strips and Abbott's ketone strips. By inserting the test strip and applying blood to it, you can utilize the built-in blood glucose meter or ketone meter.

Don't forget to charge the reader daily, preferably using the provided cable. Many users find it convenient to charge it overnight next to their bed, ensuring uninterrupted alerts while charging. Rest assured, if you charge the reader, you will still be able to use it.

Now, let's delve into the step-by-step process of scanning the sensor using the reader:

  • Press the blue start button to power on the scanner.

  • Once powered on, the home screen displays essential information such as the current time, battery level, sensor end time, and a cogwheel icon for accessing settings at the top. In the center of the screen, you'll find two options: "Check Glucose" and "Review History." If you don’t have an active sensor, the first option will be “Start New Sensor”.

  • Tap "Start New Sensor" and hold the reader within 4 centimeters (or 1.5 inches) of the sensor to initiate the scan. If sounds are enabled, the reader emits a beep upon sensor activation.

  • Approximately 1 hour after startup, you can view your sensor glucose data everytime you scan the sensor. To scan the sensor, press on the blue button to start the reader and simply hold the reader within 4 centimeters (or 1.5 inches) of the sensor again. It can scan right through your clothes. If sounds are enabled, the scanner emits a reassuring beep once it successfully scans the sensor. As you scan, the reader will display your current sensor glucose level and a glucose trend arrow.

  • If your glucose level falls below 40 mg/dL or 2.2 mmol/L, the reader will display "LO". Conversely, if it exceeds 500 mg/dL or 27.8 mmol/L, it will show "HI". In such instances, it is important to verify your glucose level with a finger prick and take appropriate action to address low or high glucose levels.

  • The top left corner of the screen provides additional information about your glucose level.

  • By tapping the pencil icon in the top right corner, you can add important details such as insulin dose, food intake, or exercise. Remember to press "OK" to save the information. This action will be reflected by a small marker, such as an insulin syringe or an apple, on the sensor graph.

  • The sensor graph showcases your glucose levels for the past 8 hours. If you wait longer than 8 hours to scan your sensor, there will be a gap in the scan information.

To explore your history, navigate to Review History on the Home Screen. Here you find the log, daily graph, average glucose, daily trends, time within range, low glucose events, and sensor usage.

The home screen also features a settings cog, offering you further customization options. Within the settings, you can:

  • set alarms

  • adjust sound & vibration preferences

  • adjust time and date

  • set reminders

  • test the built-in blood or ketone meter using a control solution

  • change the language

  • view system status

  • change report settings

  • consult reader basics

Ultimately, there are professional options available as well. To access them, you need to confirm that you are a healthcare provider and enter the code CAA1C. Once inside, you can modify the insulin dose increment and configure a bolus calculator.

We will not go deeper into how to use the built-in bolus calculator, as the calculator is only for use with fingerstick blood glucose results from the built-in meter. You cannot use the insulin calculator with sensor glucose readings.

docs.title: 3.2 LibreLink app
docs.text:
Now, let's explore the process of starting and scanning the sensor using the LibreLink app on your mobile phone.

The first step is to download the LibreLink app, which is available for both Android and iPhone devices.

Using the LibreLink app will have no noticeable effect on your phone's battery life during normal use. Even if your phone's battery runs out, don't worry—your data will remain safe. You can confidently scan the sensor or receive glucose data while your phone charges.

Upon launching the app, you'll notice a hamburger menu icon on the top left. In some Android phones, part of the settings is available in an additional Overflow menu in the top right corner. Here you can see what features are available via the Main Menu. This overview of the homescreen and menu of the FreeStyle LibreLink app can be downloaded below this video

Starting and scanning the sensor is a little bit different between the Android and iOS version of the LibreLink app:

  • On an iPhone, you have to tap "Start New Sensor" or “Scan” before scanning the sensor

  • On an Android device, you don’t have to press any button to do this. You can just open the LibreLink app and scan the sensor to start it.

It's important to note that the location of the NFC (Near Field Communication) antenna differs between iPhone and Android devices.

  • On an iPhone, you'll typically find it on the front of the device, often in the top center.

  • For Android devices, it's usually located at the back, either in the middle or towards the top.

  • Take a moment to gently move the phone over the sensor, observing where the scanning functionality is situated. Familiarize yourself with the NFC antenna's position to ensure successful scans.

  • If you encounter any issues with scanning, it's possible that your NFC antenna may be damaged or your NFC function could be turned off. Make sure to check these settings before troubleshooting further.

  • Some phone cases block NFC, preventing you from scanning. If your phone case is made of plastic or silicone or another non-conductive material, you can probably scan the sensor without removing the case. If the case is made of aluminium, metal or another material with a conductive surface, you may need to remove the case before scanning the sensor. The thickness of the case can also make a difference. If your case is very thick, it may prevent the phone from scanning the sensor.

One hour after activating the FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor by scanning it, you'll be able to see your glucose levels in the LibreLink app.

  • For the regular FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor, you'll need to scan the sensor each time to see your glucose level.

  • If you're using the FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus sensor or have access to the updated LibreLink app, you can monitor your glucose in real-time without needing to scan.

In the LibreLink app, you can easily add additional information from the home screen, such as your insulin dose, the number of carbs you've eaten, or any exercise you've done.

The LibreLink app also allows you to add and view notes in the Logbook at any time, providing greater flexibility in recording important details. This is more convenient than the reader, where note-taking is limited to within 15 minutes of a glucose reading.

Additionally, the app offers more extensive reporting capabilities, including real-time tracking of estimated HbA1c. To use this feature, you'll need at least 5 days of sensor data.

docs.title: 3.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Now, let's discuss some do's and don'ts for using the system:

  • The sensor is waterproof up to 1 meter (3 feet) for 30 minutes. Keep in mind that swimming beyond this depth or duration may affect its functionality.

  • The reader itself is not waterproof and comes with a 2-year warranty.

  • Remember that the reader's time and date do not adjust automatically. You'll need to manually update them via the settings, especially when changing time zones. When adjusted, a watch symbol will appear on the charts. On the other hand, if you use the LibreLink app on your mobile phone, the time will synchronize automatically with your phone's time.

  • The reader may display different error messages, some with explanatory texts and others with an E followed by a number. Refer to the user manual for detailed explanations of these messages.

  • If you use the Novopen 6 for insulin injections, you can directly upload insulin data to the LibreLink app by scanning the Novopen. This feature requires scanning to upload the data.

  • Remember to scan the sensor every 8 hours to ensure continuous data collection. If you don’t, there may be gaps in your sensor data. However, if you’re using the FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus or have access to the updated LibreLink app, scanning is not required after starting the sensor.

  • The LibreLink app has text-to-speech functions, which you can turn on in the settings via the hamburger menu. In that case, you can hear the glucose readings after each scan, along with the measurement unit and direction of the trend arrow.

Remember, technology can occasionally present us with unexpected hurdles. If you encounter difficulties scanning the sensor or connecting your devices, don't be discouraged. Stay persistent, explore troubleshooting options, and reach out to customer support for assistance.

4. Setting alarms

Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor. During this session, we will guide you through the process of setting alarms and inform you about the available options. We'll cover how to set alarms on both the reader and the LibreLink app on your phone, ensuring you have a comprehensive understanding of the process. Let's get started!

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
With the FreeStyle Libre 2 system, all alarms are optional. You can set high and/or low alarms, as well as an alarm on signal loss. There is no possibility to set predictive alarms for hyper or hypoglycemia.

Whether you opt for the reader or the mobile phone as your scanning device, alarms will only work if the device is within 6 meters (or 20 feet) of your sensor.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the reader
docs.text:
On the reader’s home screen, you can access the alarms feature through the cogwheel icon. Here, you can define thresholds for the low and high alarms, as well as opt for an alarm on signal loss. Adjust sound and vibration preferences according to your preference. The reader offers a single type of sound and two levels of volume.

Remember that if an alarm is ignored, it will reappear after 5 minutes if the condition persists.

docs.title: 4.3 Setting alarms on the mobile phone
docs.text:
In the LibreLink app you can navigate to the alarms section by accessing the hamburger menu on the left. Here, you have a broader range of options to tailor your alarm experience. Choose your preferred sound and decide if you want a popup notification. The volume of the alarm will match your mobile phone's sound settings.

It's worth mentioning that if you encounter a red exclamation mark in the alarms section, it indicates an issue with your phone's settings. Simply click on it and follow the provided instructions to resolve the matter.

To ensure proper alarm functionality, make sure to adjust the following settings if you’re using the LibreLink app:

  • Enable Bluetooth for a stable connection.

  • Don’t use Battery Saving Mode to maintain a strong signal.

  • On Android devices: enable location services to locate the sensor accurately and allow access to media to ensure proper alarm sounds.

  • On iPhones: keep the LibreLink app running in the background to maintain a continuous connection.

As we conclude this lesson on setting alarms with the FreeStyle Libre 2 sensor, it's important to remember to set your high glucose alarm thoughtfully—only set it to prompt you to take action. Overloading yourself with alarms can lead to desensitization over time, reducing their effectiveness when you need them most. However, it’s best to keep the low glucose and signal loss alarms on for safety.

docs.title: 5. Sharing data (3 min)
docs.text:
