  • At the top, international guidelines on time in range, time below range, and glucose variation are displayed, along with the person's actual glycemic control. A color bar on the left further enhances clarity.

  • Below that, you'll see trends from the chosen period.

  • Further down, trends by weekday are presented, providing deeper insights into glycemic patterns.

docs.title: 5.2 Interpreting reports
docs.text:
Now, let's delve into the art of interpreting reports from a stand-alone sensor:

  • Step 1: Begin by checking if the sensor has been worn for more than 90% of the time. If not, discuss any challenges that may hinder consistent wear.

  • If there are issues with supplies or sensors not lasting the full sensor wear time, contact the manufacturer for replacement sensors.

  • For individuals experiencing skin problems or difficulty keeping the sensor in place, explore strategies like rotating insertion sites and utilizing barrier products, overtapes, or adhesive removers to protect the skin.

  • Step 2: Assess if the user is meeting their glycemic targets by examining the top panel of the AGP profile, which displays ten core parameters.

  • Remember, positive feedback plays a crucial role in supporting self-empowerment. Recognize and celebrate progress, such as improvements in time in range, to encourage individuals in their diabetes management journey.

  • Then look for any areas of concern or opportunities for improvement.

    • Time in range should ideally be above 70% for most people with diabetes. Even a 5% improvement in TIR represents a clinically relevant outcome, as this is equivalent to a 0.3% decrease in HbA1c.

    • Time below range should be less than 4%. It is better to talk about minutes instead of percentages as this is easier to understand (1% equals 15 minutes).

    • Glycemic variation is best kept below 36%, although this may be easier for individuals with type 2 diabetes compared to those with type 1 diabetes. A glycemic variation above36% signifies an unstable glycemia and a higher risk of hypoglycemia.

  • Step 3: If any issues arise, turn to the AGP profile for a visual representation of where the problem lies.

  • Prioritize the treatment of hypoglycemia, aiming for "more green and less red" in the graph.

  • Strive for a flat, narrow, and in-range curve, and analyze patterns of hypo- and hyperglycemia.

  • Before making any adjustments, review the bottom panel of the AGP report, which displays daily patterns. This analysis helps rule out whether patterns are exclusive to specific work or rest days.

  • Additionally, examine the daily reports to ensure correct timing of insulin boluses and check for systematic overcorrection of hypo- or hyperglycemia.

  • Step 4: Ensure that the used alerts align with the specific needs of the individual, to prevent alarm fatigue. Customizing alarms helps maintain a healthy balance between staying informed and avoiding unnecessary disturbances.

  • Step 5: Set a single goal collaboratively and plan the next consultation for review. Focus on improving areas of concern—whether they relate to lows, highs, or wide glycemic fluctuations.

Interpreting sensor reports can feel overwhelming due to the abundance of glucose data available. To manage this effectively, focus on addressing the most noticeable trends rather than attempting to fix everything at once. This approach helps in gradually improving glucose control step by step. Aim to provide transparent and clear instructions based on the sensor reports, empowering patients to interpret and act on the data independently. A helpful tip is to print out the AGP profile and summarize the therapy changes on paper, allowing patients to review their results and conclusions even after the consultation.

Congratulations on reaching this stage of the module! You now know how to interpret sensor reports, which will help you to provide excellent diabetes care and make a significant impact on the lives of those you care for.

docs.title: 6. Expert insights
docs.text:
Welcome to the final chapter of our comprehensive module on glucose sensors! You've come a long way, acquiring valuable knowledge and skills to enhance your expertise in diabetes care. Congratulations on reaching this milestone!

In this concluding segment, we invite you to watch an engaging YouTube video that humanizes glucose sensors and offers insights from various experts. This video explores different CGM options such as Freestyle Libre, Dexcom, Eversense, and Medtronic. The experts discuss the pros and cons of each CGM and share their personal preferences. It's important to note that there is no one-size-fits-all CGM, and it's crucial to consider the advantages and disadvantages before making a decision.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fng5McLfVUo (14.5 min)

YouTube is a fantastic platform for discovering fun and interesting tips and tricks about glucose sensors. Although these channels are not regulated, they are managed by individuals living with diabetes themselves. Their content truly adds value to the diabetes community.

Now, you've reached the end of the module on glucose sensors. You have expanded your knowledge about various sensors, learned about potential pitfalls such as when to perform a finger prick, and substances that can interfere with sensor accuracy. You've gained valuable tips on sensor adherence and managing allergies. Additionally, we explored how to interpret glucose sensor reports and highlighted a cool YouTube channel for further education and inspiration. Your expertise has undoubtedly grown, enabling you to troubleshoot effectively when using glucose sensors. We hope you found this course enlightening and inspiring.

Remember, your learning journey doesn't end here. Continue to explore the other modules on specific glucose sensors, insulin pumps and automated insulin delivery systems, and embrace the spirit of lifelong learning.

Once again, congratulations on your remarkable achievement in completing this module on glucose sensors. We wish you continued success and fulfillment in your diabetes care endeavors. You are making a real difference!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 4. Setting alarms
  • 5. Data sharing
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case Report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video module on the Guardian 4 continuous glucose monitor. Whether you are a healthcare professional or someone living with diabetes, this course will provide you with valuable insights and practical tips to make the most of the Guardian 4 system. Let's dive in!

The Guardian 4 is an advanced continuous glucose monitor primarily used in combination with the MiniMed 780G insulin pump. With a sensor lifespan of 7 days and a transmitter that can be reused for 1 year, the Guardian 4 offers an innovative solution for continuous glucose monitoring. You can conveniently access sensor data on your insulin pump or via the MiniMed Mobile app.

In previous videos, you learned about the reliability of glucose sensors, tips for handling sensor disconnection, and managing allergies. In this module, we will focus on the start-up education for the Guardian 4 sensor and share advanced tips and tricks for caregivers.

Please note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any changes to your therapy.

The videos in this course were created by Dr. Inge van Boxelaer, founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the general St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content has been reviewed by by Dr. Henk-Jan Aanstoot, a pediatric endocrinologist and co-founder of Diabeter, and Sjoerd Aerts, a dutch individual living with type 1 diabetes who uses the Guardian 4 sensor in combination with the MiniMed 780G system. Their expertise and perspectives ensure that you receive honest opinions on the pros and cons of the Guardian 4 sensor.

By the end of this course, you will have a comprehensive understanding of various aspects related to the Guardian 4 sensor. You will learn

  • how to insert and remove the sensor correctly,

  • start the sensor with the MiniMed 780G insulin pump,

  • set alarms effectively,

  • enable real-time data tracking for caregivers,

  • and even explore the possibilities of connecting the Guardian 4 sensor to other devices.

As a healthcare professional, this knowledge will empower you to better guide your patients using the Guardian 4 sensor. And if you are a user of the Guardian 4 sensor, this course will equip you with a wealth of tips and tricks to enhance your experience. Get ready to embark on this exciting journey and make your life with the Guardian 4 even easier. Good luck!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (4 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on how to place the Guardian 4 sensor.

In this chapter, we will guide you through the process of placing and removing the Guardian 4 sensor.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text: To install a sensor, you will need the following supplies: a sensor with two oval plasters, the transmitter, the charger and the sensor inserter that come in 1 box, and an alcohol wipe.

docs.title: 2.2 Inserting the sensor
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure that you have the Guardian 4 sensors in hand, as indicated on the box and packaging. Similarly, confirm that the transmitter is a Guardian 4 transmitter.

When inserting the sensor, it's crucial to follow a few steps carefully.

  • Begin by washing your hands and disinfecting the insertion site with an alcohol wipe. Allow your skin to dry completely before proceeding.

  • Take the sensor out of the package, making sure to handle it with care and keep the litz in place. Hold the inserter with your thumb on the thumb mark, ensuring your fingers do not touch the green buttons. Press the inserter onto the sensor until you hear a click. You can then remove the base and see the sensor inside the inserter. The arrow on the inserter indicates the needle's location.

  • Next, place the inserter on your skin and simultaneously push the two green buttons on either side. Release the buttons to engage the sensor automatically. Hold the inserter against your skin for an additional 5 seconds to ensure adhesive effectiveness.

  • Slowly remove the inserter without pushing the buttons, and gently pull out the top of the needle housing to detach it.

  • Remove the foil from the plaster and press it against the skin. Secure the plaster with an oval tape like this, ensuring proper adhesion.

  • Now it is time to connect the transmitter. Remove the charged transmitter from the charger and snap it onto the sensor. Upon insertion, you will notice a green light starting to flash on the transmitter. Please wait for about 15 seconds and check if it flashes 5-6 times. If you do not see the green light blinking, there might be an issue that needs to be addressed before proceeding further.

  • Then place the adhesive tag over the transmitter and take the second oval tape and place it with the wide part over the transmitter.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: To remove the sensor after the 7-day wear period, follow these steps: remove the oval tape covering the transmitter, peel back the adhesive tag, pinch the flexible arm of the sensor, pull the transmitter away, remove the oval tape covering the sensor, and finally, remove the adhesive and sensor from your body. After removing the sensor, make sure to clean your transmitter and place it back into the charger for recharging.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• To prevent sensor wire dislocation during placement, we recommend removing the foil from the plaster while it's still in the inserter. This approach is more convenient for most people as it reduces the need for manipulation of the sensor once it's on the skin. Additionally, it helps the sensor adhere better immediately after insertion. Please note that while this is not the official method, it has proven to be a more practical approach for many users.

  • Remember to take your time and be cautious while applying the sensor to avoid any failure. Refer to the short instruction manual included in the sensor box for additional guidance.

  • Before using your Guardian 4 sensor with an insulin pump, it's important to first link them together. You can find detailed instructions on how to do this in the Minimed 780G closed-loop system module.

  • Make sure that your transmitter is fully charged before attachment to the sensor. You can check this by making sure that both lights on the charger are off when the transmitter is connected to the charger.

  • Always verify that the green light on the transmitter is flashing after attaching it, as it becomes difficult to adjust the placement after applying the second oval patch.

  • In case of allergies to the oval tape, alternative patches may be more suitable.

  • One common error message with the Guardian 4 sensor is "sensor updating." This message indicates that the sensor is not functioning properly because it temporarily lost the subcutaneous signal, and the system is attempting to restore it. Without the correct signal, the sensor cannot calculate the sensor glucose value, and calibration is also not possible. There can be various reasons for this signal disruption:

  • If you receive this message right after inserting the sensor, there is a high chance that the sensor is in the wrong position. This can occur due to scar tissue (to prevent this, rotate your sensor placement), hitting a blood vessel (to prevent this, you can cool the insertion site with ice before inserting the sensor), or placement in an area with low moisture in the muscle tissue (drinking extra fluids or massaging the sensor area might help).

  • Another cause in the Sensor Updating alert right after insertion, could be that the sensor electrode came out along with the needle. This is not visible, but often felt. The electrode may have been damaged during insertion, folded or bent under the skin, or hit against something. Additionally, if the insertion device was removed too early, the sensor may have partially or completely come out of the body. It is also possible that the skin was not properly cleaned before insertion or the sensor electrode was inserted through a layer of protective spray or glue. If the sensor is not securely taped, excessive movement can occur beneath the skin. Alternative patches can be used to secure the sensor and transmitter.

  • On later days (day 2-6), potential causes of the Sensor Update alert may include:

    • the sensor being placed in a skin fold, causing it to partially emerge from the skin when sitting;

    • engaging in intense physical activity, especially strength training that involves prolonged muscle contractions;

    • experiencing the error message only in the morning, which could be due to pressure on the sensor while sleeping;

    • high external temperatures causing the adhesive patches to loosen, resulting in an insecure sensor placement;

    • experiencing irritation on the skin beneath the patches;

    • being in a sauna or hot tub, or swimming frequently;

    • having pets that jump against you or regularly lifting children/grandchildren.

  • If you frequently encounter “sensor update” alarms, it may be due to a transmitter problem. Difficulties with the transmitter's charging are also an indication of a transmitter issue. In such cases, we recommend reaching out to Medtronic's customer service for assistance. They can provide support and, if necessary, arrange for a replacement transmitter.

  • If the sensor fails to restore the signal on its own, the pump will display the message "replace sensor." It is also advised to replace the sensor if the error message appears continuously on the pump for more than 3 hours.

If your sensor fails before the expected 7-day duration, you can contact Medtronic's customer service to request a new sensor. Generally, if the sensor fails within 5 days, there is a higher likelihood of receiving a replacement sensor.

For users of the MiniMed 780G pump, Medtronic also offers a WeCare portal and app. Through registration, users gain access to Medtronic's 24/7 customer service and online services, including the ability to request replacements for defective sensors.

By applying these additional tips and tricks, you will gain the confidence to have a successful journey with the Guardian 4 sensors. Despite their known sensor update issues, most users are content and can effectively manage them with these extra insights. Embrace these helpful tips to maximize the potential of your Guardian 4 sensors and ensure a positive experience.

3. Starting the sensor

Welcome to this lesson on starting the Guardian 4 sensor.

In this module, we will walk you through the process of starting the Guardian 4 sensor using the MiniMed 780G insulin pump.

docs.title: 3.1 Guardian 4 sensor with the MiniMed 780G pump
docs.text:
To connect the Guardian 4 transmitter to the MiniMed 780G insulin pump, please refer to the module on the MiniMed 780G automated insulin delivery system.

Before connecting the Guardian 4 transmitter to the Guardian 4 sensor, it is important that the transmitter is fully charged. You can check if your transmitter is fully charged by connecting it to the charger and waiting until the blinking of the green light stops.

When you attach a fully charged, connected Guardian transmitter to your inserted Guardian 4 sensor, the transmitter will begin communicating with the pump. The insulin pump will prompt you to start the sensor, which usually takes less than a minute, but in some cases, it may take up to 10 minutes.

Upon clicking "start new sensor," a message indicating "warming up sensor in progress" will appear. The warm-up period lasts for 2 hours, during which the sensor glucose readings will be visible on your pump. Calibration is not required with the Guardian 4 sensor.

Once the sensor has completed its warm-up period, you can view your sensor glucose readings on the home screen of your MiniMed 780G pump and on the sensor graph. If you download the MiniMed Mobile app on your compatible mobile phone, you will also be able to track your sensor glucose readings there. You can find a list of compatible phones on the website of Medtronic.

docs.title: 3.2 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• The sensor is waterproof up to a depth of 1 meter (3 feet) for a duration of 30 minutes. It is important to keep this in mind and avoid swimming at depths exceeding 1 meter or 3 feet, or spending more than 30 minutes in the bath while wearing the sensor.
  • The transmitter comes with a 14 month warranty, but in reality, it often lasts around 18 months. Predicting the exact lifespan of the transmitter can be challenging, and the warranty period starts from the moment of purchase. To be prepared, it is recommended to have a new transmitter ready for replacement around 14 months after the start-up date.

  • A possible message that you may encounter with the Guardian 4 sensor is the "no sensor signal." This message indicates that there is no connection between your sensor or transmitter and your pump. To fix this, try wearing your pump close to the transmitter, on the same side, and within a distance of less than 10 cm or 4 inch. Additionally, position your mobile phone on the opposite side of the pump. It's important to note that any device or technology that operates using radio frequency waves, such as Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, microwaves, induction hobs, wireless headsets, or nearby mobile phones (especially older versions like Nokia), can potentially cause interference. If you encounter the "no sensor signal" message, you can also try going outside or away from other devices for approximately 5 minutes, as this might help restore the signal between the transmitter and the pump.

  • If there is a temporary loss of connection to the transmitter, you may notice a gap in your sensor glucose data on the graph. However, when the connection is reestablished, the gap will be filled with the readings from the last 3 hours.

By following these guidelines and understanding the behavior of the sensor and transmitter, you can effectively utilize the Guardian 4 system and make the most of its features.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the Guardian 4 sensor.

Throughout this session, we'll walk you through the available alarm options and demonstrate how to set them via your MiniMed 780G insulin pump. Let's get started!

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
The Guardian 4 sensor offers a diverse range of alarm options to keep you informed about specific glucose levels and sensor-related issues. You have the flexibility to set alarms for low glucose, high glucose, and rising or falling glucose. Additionally, there are predictive low and high alerts that activate when the system foresees your glucose reaching the user-defined threshold within 5-30 minutes.

While all alarms are optional, two alarms are mandatory and cannot be turned off. These are the Low alarm at 54 mg/dL (3 mmol/L) and the High alarm that triggers if your glucose remains above 250 mg/dL (13.9 mmol/L) for more than 3 hours.

For your convenience, the system includes an adjustable snooze function, allowing reminders for consistently high or low glucose levels. Additionally, the system offers flexibility in setting an alert schedule, allowing you to customize alerts based on specific time blocks that suit your individual needs.

To receive alarms, ensure your MiniMed 780G pump is within 1.8 meters (6 feet) of the sensor. This proximity ensures timely alerts and seamless monitoring of your glucose levels.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the MiniMed 780G pump
docs.text:
To set alarms on your pump, navigate to the alarms section in the settings menu.

  • Under the high setup, you can configure alarms for high glucose levels or rising glucose trends within specific time blocks. During the initial setup, high alarms are usually left off.

  • In the low setup, you can set alarms for low glucose levels or falling glucose trends within specific time blocks. Typically, the lower limit is set at 70 mg/dL (or 3.9 mmol/L).

  • In the alarm settings, you can also adjust the snooze function, which determines how long the alarm repeats. The default snooze time is 1 hour for high alarms and 20 minutes for low alarms.

The audio options on your pump also allow you to choose alarm silence, which temporarily disables all entered sensor alarms for up to 24 hours. However, please note that the alarm at 54 mg/dL (3 mmol/L) and longer than 3 hours above 250 mg/dL (13.9 mmol/L) cannot be turned off.

By utilizing these alarm settings effectively and maintaining resilience, you can stay informed and proactive in managing your glucose levels with the Guardian 4. When managing alarms, stay vigilant and ensure you're using the settings that best suit your needs. This proactive approach empowers you to respond effectively to changing glucose levels.

docs.title: 5. Data sharing (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Guardian sensor.

In this video, we will explore how to share data with your healthcare provider, generate reports, and share data with family members or caregivers.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your healthcare provider
docs.text:
To share your data with your healthcare provider, Medtronic provides the CareLink platform. You can create a CareLink Personal account at carelink.minimed.eu or a CareLink Clinic account at carelink.medtronic.eu.

If you are a healthcare professional, you can add your patients to CareLink Clinic by entering their name and date of birth.

  • You can then invite the patient to share their data by sending an email through CareLink.

  • The patient can accept the invitation and enter the code from the email in their personal CareLink account settings.

When using the MiniMed Mobile app, it is linked to your personal CareLink account during the initial setup. If your personal CareLink account is connected to your hospital's account, your healthcare provider can view your data in real-time.

docs.title: 5.2 Generating Reports
docs.text: Once your data is uploaded to CareLink, you can generate reports by selecting the glucose reports option. The process of creating and interpreting reports is explained in detail in the MiniMed 780G closed-loop system reports video.

docs.title: 5.3 Sharing data with loved-ones
docs.text:
To share Guardian 4 data with family members or caregivers, you have two options.

  • Firstly, you can view CareLink data on your PC by creating a CareLink Personal account as a care partner. You will need to request access to the CareLink data of the user you want to track by entering their username. The user can then approve you as a caregiver through their own CareLink platform.

  • Alternatively, if you use the MiniMed Mobile app, you can share your data with up to 5 caregivers using the CareLink Connect app. To begin, as a patient, you need to invite a follower to your MiniMed Mobile account. The follower must accept the invitation and install the CareLink Connect app to set up data sharing.

docs.title: Embracing these sharing features enhances collaboration, resilience, and informed decision-making for a more empowered journey towards better health.
6. Connecting other devices (5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Guardian 4 sensor to other devices.

In this chapter, we explore the exciting possibilities of connecting your Guardian 4 sensor with various devices, such as smartwatches, smart pens, and insulin pumps.

docs.title: 6.1 Smart watch
docs.text:
By using the MiniMed 780G in conjunction with the MiniMed Mobile app, you can enable pop-up alarms on your compatible phone and smartphone watch, providing quick access to crucial information.

This chapter delves into the process of connecting your Guardian 4 sensor with popular smartwatches like Garmin, Samsung Galaxy, and Apple Watch.

For Apple Watch integration with the MiniMed 780G, a recent integration update has been released. Make sure you have the latest version of the MiniMed Mobile app installed to enjoy this feature. However, for Android watches, connectivity requires the use of a third-party app like xDrip+. Please be aware that xDrip+ is not supported by Medtronic and lacks approval from regulatory authorities like the FDA or EMA, making its usage carry inherent risks that should be approached cautiously.

docs.title: #1 Garmin smartwatches
docs.text:
For Garmin smartwatches, download the xDrip+ app from github and set the data source to "Companion App" in the settings. This allows xDrip+ to receive sensor data from the MiniMed Mobile app. You can then choose to install the xDrip+/Spike/Nightscout Watch Face, Widget, or Data Field from the Connect IQ store to view your Guardian sensor data as needed.

If you have a Garmin Edge cycling computer, you can utilize the xDrip+/Spike/Nightscout Data Field feature to display Guardian data on it.

docs.title: #2 Samsung Galaxy smartwatch (4 or 5)
docs.text:
For Samsung Galaxy smartwatches, follow similar steps by downloading the xDrip+ app and setting the data source to "Companion App" in the settings. Adjust the xDrip+ app settings to send data to the G-Watch App and install the G-Watch Wear app on both your phone and smartwatch. You can use the Wearable Widgets app alongside G-Watch Wear to display Guardian4 data as a complication or tile on your Samsung Galaxy smartwatch.

Please remember that using open-source apps carries inherent risks and is not supported by Medtronic or approved by regulatory authorities. We provide this information transparently, though we don't recommend engaging in this practice without understanding the risks fully.

docs.title: #3 Apple Watch
docs.text:
Lastly, for Apple Watch, ensure you have the latest version of the MiniMed Mobile app on your iPhone and install the MiniMed app on your Apple Watch. Access the MiniMed app on your Apple Watch to view your glycemia within your SmartGuard shield, and swiping left will display your sensor graph and Time In Range.

To have easier access to your glucose data, you can integrate your sensor glycemia and SmartGuard Shield as a complication on compatible watch faces. These watch faces include Portrait, Nike (hybrid), Breathe, Utility, Simple, Photos, Motion, Gradient, XL, and Astronomy. Clicking on the complication will allow you to access the full MiniMed Watch app.

Remember, the setup steps may vary based on your phone and smartwatch, but you can find detailed tutorials in the provided downloads, making it easier to perform the setup with the devices on hand.

docs.title: 6.2 Smart pens
docs.text: In addition to its integration with the Minimed 780g system, the Guardian 4 sensor can also be used in conjunction with the InPen. To do this, simply download the Guardian Connect app and the InPen app and follow the steps.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
The Guardian 4 sensor is seamlessly integrated with the Minimed 780G system, working together flawlessly, thanks to both being offered by Medtronic and designed for compatibility.

Please note that the Guardian 4 sensor is not compatible with open-source automated insulin delivery systems.

In conclusion, connecting your Guardian 4 sensor to other devices opens up a world of possibilities for personalized diabetes management. From smartwatches to smart pens and insulin pumps, these connections offer valuable insights and real-time data. While utilizing open-source apps carries inherent risks, we provide transparency about all available options. Embrace the power of adaptability and empower yourself to optimize your diabetes management strategy with these innovative connections.

docs.title: 7. Case Report (2 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to the final chapter of this module! In this video, we'll explore some insights shared by Peta, a Type 1 diabetic, on how to extend the lifespan of the Guardian sensors. Please note that while Peta shares her personal experience and tips, it's important to consult with your healthcare advisor.

It's important to note that Medtronic officially recommends replacing the sensor every seven days. However, in some cases, the Guardian 4 sensors may stop working prematurely, resulting in a potential shortage of sensors. In such situations, you might try to restart a sensor that has worked for the full seven days. It's important to remember that restarting a sensor is generally possible up to two times, allowing you to reuse a sensor for a maximum of three weeks. However, please be cautious and follow the guidelines provided by your healthcare provider.

Keep in mind that if you choose to use a sensor beyond the recommended seven-day period, it's crucial to periodically verify its accuracy with a finger-prick test. Medtronic does not provide any guarantee for the accuracy or reliability of sensors used beyond their intended usage period.

We now invite you to watch the video where Peta demonstrates the process of restarting the Guardian 3 sensor. This technique can also be applied to the Guardian 4 sensor. Remember to always exercise caution and consult with your healthcare advisor.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nfe95Gv7bmw

Congratulations on completing the course! You now have a comprehensive understanding of the Guardian 4 sensor, its insertion and removal, starting the sensor with the MiniMed 780G insulin pump, setting alarms, data sharing, connecting the Guardian 4 sensor to other devices and valuable tips from Peta's experience.

Remember, diabetes management is a continuous learning process, and it's essential to stay informed about advancements in technology and best practices. Stay proactive, consult with your healthcare provider regularly, and utilize the resources available to you. Wishing you success on your journey to optimal diabetes management. Take care!

docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alarms
  • 5. Sharing data
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the iCan i6 CGM! In the upcoming videos, we’ll explore the key features of this continuous glucose sensor, designed for children and adults with diabetes.

The iCan i6 sensor is about 23 mm in diameter, 4 mm thick, and weighs around 2 g. It can be applied on the abdomen or on the back of the upper arm using an all-in-one applicator. After a two-hour warm-up, it tracks glucose levels in interstitial fluid and sends real-time readings to the iCan smartphone app or the iCan view Reader via Bluetooth every three minutes. Each sensor is designed to last up to 15 days and is intended for people with diabetes aged 2 years and older.

In the “Master Module on Glucose Sensors”, we covered the basics, including when to trust sensor readings and how to manage disconnections and allergic reactions. The next videos will focus on in-depth iCan i6 CGM training and advanced guidance for healthcare providers.

The goal of this module is to empower both healthcare providers and individuals with diabetes to unlock the full potential of the iCan i6. However, please note that these videos are for educational purposes only and do not constitute medical advice. Always consult your healthcare provider before adjusting your therapy.

This module was produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr Amit Gupta, the founder of DTech and a diabetologist and medical director of the Centre of Diabetes Care in Greater Noida, in India. Their expertise and experience make for a credible, balanced review of the iCan i6 CGM.

All the information provided in this material is completely independent of Sinocare, the sensor manufacturer, ensuring an honest perspective on the pros and cons of the iCan i6.

After completing this module, you'll be ready to use the iCan i6 CGM confidently in everyday life. You’ll learn how to apply and start the sensor, set and adjust alarms, share glucose data, and connect the sensor with compatible devices. You’ll also get expert advice on safe use and dependable CGM performance. Healthcare providers will strengthen their ability to support iCan i6 users in clinical practice, while sensor users will take away clear, step-by-step strategies they can use immediately. Let’s dive in — may you succeed every step of the way!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (3 min)
docs.text: Welcome to the first lesson of the iCan i6 CGM module. In this video, we’ll guide you through the process of applying and removing the sensor, and highlight some do’s and don’ts. Let’s begin.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text:
Prepare the following:

  • An iCan i6 CGM pack, which contains an applicator with a built-in sensor, an adhesive patch, and a user manual; and

  • an alcohol wipe.

docs.title: 2.2 Applying the sensor
docs.text:
The first thing you need to do is download the iCan CGM app from the App Store or Google Play. When you download the app for the first time, there will be a guideline to help you learn cautions and important functions of the app. Then you will need to create an iCan account with your email address. Once logged in, the app walks you through training videos step by step first, so you can follow these instructions to start your 15 days with the iCan i6. (00:15 - 00:45)

Remove the all-in-one applicator from the box and check that the tamper-evident label is intact. Open the app, ensure Bluetooth is turned on, and use the app to scan the QR code on the bottom of the applicator.

Next, wash your hands with soap and water. Then choose the sensor site based on age: for ages 2–12, use the abdomen; for ages 13 and older, use either the abdomen or the back of the upper arm. Clean the insertion site with an alcohol pad and allow it to dry completely before inserting the sensor. Unscrew the protective cap in the direction shown by the tamper-evident label on the applicator. Place the armed sensor applicator on the selected site, and press the center button until you hear the injection sound. Gently lift the applicator away from your body. Finally, press down the adhesive around the sensor so that it sticks securely to your skin.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: Moisten the edges of the adhesive tape with medical alcohol. Peel back the edge of the adhesive tape, then remove the sensor from your skin in one slow, steady motion. Dispose of the used device in accordance with local medical waste disposal requirements.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• Ensure the insertion site is clean, dry, and free from lotions, perfumes, and any topical medications. If necessary, shave the area so the adhesive tape can stick firmly and stay secure.

  • After inserting the sensor, check the site for any bleeding. If bleeding occurs and does not stop, remove the sensor and apply firm, steady pressure with sterile gauze until the bleeding stops.

  • Once the sensor is removed, you may clean any adhesive residue with alcohol or soap and water, or apply a warm compress to help soften the adhesive, then gently wipe away any remaining residue.

In this video, you learned the correct procedure for applying and removing the iCan i6 sensor. By mastering this procedure, you help maintain dependable glucose monitoring and support reliable data to guide treatment decisions.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (6 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on getting started with the iCan i6 CGM. In this video, we’ll discuss starting the iCan i6 sensor and finding your way around the iCan app and view Reader. You’ll also get practical tips for correct sensor wear and daily use. Let’s begin.

docs.title: 3.1 Starting the sensor
docs.text: After inserting your sensor, press “Start Sensor” in the iCan app to enter into your 2-hour sensor warmup. Once warm-up is complete, you will start getting readings and alerts. Your iCan i6 CGM is intended to last for 15 days. (02:58 - 03:05; 03:10-03:15)

docs.title: 3.2 The iCan CGM app
docs.text:
The number at the top displays your current glucose reading next to the trend arrow. The trend graph below shows your CGM history over the past 3 hours, plotting glucose values every three minutes. The orange and red zones indicate your low and urgent low threshold settings. The small round icons just under the graph represent events that you logged manually. In the navigation bar, you will find five icons: Data for the home screen, Events to view history and event report, a plus icon to add events, Help, and Settings.

Within the Settings menu, you can access alert history; personal settings to adjust alert thresholds; system settings to change units of measurement, language, and view the current software version; iCan ACCESS for sharing data with care partners; and the option to end the sensor session. From the Settings screen, you can also tap the profile icon in the top-right corner to change your personal details.

Be aware that in some regions the updated iCan Health app is also available. Like the iCan app, it can connect to both the iCan i3 and iCan i6 sensors, but it features an updated layout with a cartoon-like figure on the home screen and only four navigation buttons in the navigation bar.

docs.title: 3.3 The iCan view Reader
docs.text:
If you are unable or prefer not to use the iCan CGM app, you can use the sensor with the iCan view Reader instead. This black reader is supplied with a USB charging cable and is operated using four buttons: a return button, up and down buttons, and a square confirm/next button.

When you use the Reader for the first time, it will be powered off by default. To switch it on, press and hold the Square button for five seconds. Charge the Reader before your first use to avoid startup issues caused by a low battery,

After selecting your language, proceed to the next screen. Confirm the unit of measurement to continue. Set the preferred date format, then move to the time format settings. Confirm the time format and continue to set the current time. As you continue, read each on-screen guide before moving on, and press the Square button to advance through the pages. For sensor insertion steps, refer to the previous video. When scanning the QR code, aim the camera at the QR code; note that the camera is for scanning only and does not show a live image. You will then be prompted to confirm that the sensor has been inserted successfully. After confirmation, press the Square button to proceed and wait for the Reader to connect. The Reader will display a warm-up countdown showing the remaining warm-up time. Once warm-up is complete, the Reader will automatically open the monitoring Home screen and display the first glucose reading.

The Reader Home screen shows your current glucose reading, which refreshes automatically every three minutes. The font color indicates whether your reading is within range: white means it is between your high and low alert levels, yellow indicates High Glucose, orange indicates Low Glucose, and red indicates Urgent Low Glucose. The screen also displays the trend arrow, the high alert yellow line, the white trend graph, the low alert orange line, and the urgent low alert red line. You can use the up and down buttons to change how many hours are shown on the trend graph. Selecting Menu opens the menu bar, where you can access Glucose Reports, Event Records, System Settings, Share Your Glucose, Information, Help, End Monitoring, and Power Off.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• To help the CGM device and the iCan app work correctly, make sure your phone is set to the correct local time. If you travel across time zones, avoid changing the time manually; instead, enable the setting for automatic date and time so your phone updates to the new time zone and local time when you arrive.

  • If you use the iCan app, you can activate the sensor and view your glucose data only on a compatible smartphone. For a list of compatible smartphones, visit this page: iCan CGM System | Sinocare Continuous Glucose Monitoring

  • Each sensor can be paired with either the iCan app on a compatible smartphone or with the Reader only. Once it is paired, you cannot switch to a different device type during the wear period.

  • Remove the sensor before undergoing an MRI or CT scan. There is no need to remove the sensor for a regular X-ray.

  • You can wear the sensor while exercising, bathing, or swimming. It is waterproof up to a depth of 2 meters for up to 2 hours. The iCan view Reader, however, is not waterproof and should be kept dry.

  • If the ambient temperature rises above 42°C (for example, during high-heat exposure or while bathing), the sensor reading might be affected.

In this video, we detailed the iCan i6 CGM start-up process, from sensor pairing to app and Reader navigation. As you guide your patients through their first days with the iCan i6, remember that technical issues can occur from time to time. If you or your patients run into challenges, encourage a calm, step-by-step approach. Work through troubleshooting options and contact customer support when needed. With your knowledge, patience and determination, you can empower your patients to make the most of their CGM.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (5.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the iCan i6 CGM. In this video, we’ll take a close look at the sensor’s alarms and show you how to customize them in the iCan app and on the iCan view Reader. Let’s get started.

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
The iCan i6 sensor provides glucose alerts, system alerts, and abnormal alerts.

  • Glucose alerts include Urgent Low, Glucose Rising Rapidly, Glucose Falling Rapidly, High Glucose, Low Glucose, Out of Range High, and Out of Range Low. When your glucose moves from your target range into a preset alert level, your phone will notify you visually and use both vibration and sound cues. If you do not acknowledge a glucose alert, the alert screen will reappear every three minutes with another notification and vibration, and the alert message will remain on the Home screen until your readings return to the target range.

  • System alerts occur when Bluetooth on your mobile device is turned off or when the sensor cannot pair with your smartphone.

  • Meanwhile, abnormal alerts are triggered when sensor readings are not available, or when the sensor detects electrical signal problems, abnormal values, startup failure, or temperature-related issues.

The iCan View Reader shows the same glucose-related alerts as the app, along with system and device problem alerts.

  • On the Reader, glucose alerts appear as these on-screen notifications. In addition to the visual message, the Reader also provides sound and vibration cues.

  • For system alerts, the Reader notifies you about failed sensor scanning, device pairing issues, and Wi-Fi connection problems.

  • Device-problem alerts appear when the Reader or sensor battery is low, when the sensor fails to start, or when other abnormalities are detected. The Reader issues alerts for expired sensors, Bluetooth disconnection, and CGM version updates as well.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the iCan CGM app
docs.text:
By default, the app sets the High Glucose alert to 200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) and the Low Glucose alert to 70 mg/dL (3.9 mmol/L). To adjust these default alert thresholds, tap Settings at the bottom of the Home screen, open Personal Settings, and select the alert level you want to change. The High Glucose and Low Glucose alerts are switched on by default. When an alert is enabled, you can tap the slider to set the Low Glucose threshold anywhere from 60 to 110 mg/dL (or 3.3 to 5.6 mmol/L), and the High Glucose threshold from 117 to 450 mg/dL (or 6.5 to 25.0 mmol/L). You can also choose the sound and vibration settings for these alerts, noting that the actual behavior will follow your mobile device’s sound and vibration settings.

The iCan i6 app offers three alert modes: Silence All, Vibrate Only, and Override Do Not Disturb.

  • Silence All controls both sounds and vibrations. When it is turned on, notifications will not produce sound or vibration, but floating windows, the notification bar, and pop-up prompts will still appear. Silence All is off by default; when you enable it, a pop-up lets you confirm the duration of up to 8 hours and presents a warning about the associated risks.

  • Vibrate Only controls sound alerts. When enabled, it mutes notification sounds while still allowing floating windows, the notification bar, and pop-up prompts to appear as usual.

  • If you use your phone’s Do Not Disturb mode or keep your phone silenced but still want to reliably receive Urgent Low Alerts and other critical glucose alerts, ensure Override Do Not Disturb is enabled in the app. On iOS, you must grant the required system permissions, while on Android, the exact steps may vary depending on your device model and Android version.

docs.title: 4.3 Setting alarms on the iCan view Reader

To configure alerts on the Reader, select Menu on the main screen, then go to System Settings and choose Alerts. From top to bottom, the Alerts bar lists: Silence All, Vibrate Only, High, Low, Urgent Low, Rising Rapidly, Falling Rapidly, Out of Range, System Alerts, and Default Settings. By default, all notifications are enabled, along with their sound and vibration settings. If you want to restore the original settings, open the Default Settings page, then select Confirm to reset all notifications to their default values and return to the notification settings page.

Silence All turns off sound and vibration for all notifications and can be set from a minimum of 0.5 hours up to a maximum of 6 hours, adjustable in 0.5-hour increments. When Silence All is enabled, an indicator will appear on the screen, and you will still receive on-screen text notifications even with sound and vibration turned off. Vibrate Only mutes all alarm sounds while keeping vibration active. When this mode is enabled, a corresponding indicator will also be shown on the screen.

In this video, we explored how the iCan i6 alert system works. With correctly configured alarms and steady follow-through, you can catch important glucose shifts early and take timely action to stay on top of your health.
5. Sharing data (6.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the iCan i6 CGM. In this video, you’ll learn how to share sensor data and generate reports using the iCan app and the iCan View Reader. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your loved ones
docs.text:
Use the iCan CGM app’s iCan Access feature to share your glucose information with up to 10 trusted care partners, such as friends or family members. You can enable high and low glucose notifications for them, similar to the alerts you receive in your own iCan CGM app. You can manage sharing at any time by editing a care partner’s access, pausing sharing, or removing them. Care partners do not need the iCan CGM app; they only need to download the iCan REACH app. To invite someone, open Settings and select iCan Access. Tap Invite Care Partners, then enter the person’s name and email address. Tap Next, adjust the notification settings as needed, and confirm your selections by tapping Start Reaching.

The iCan Access screen shows each care partner’s status and allows you to invite additional people. You can customize what they will see in REACH by toggling items on or off before tapping Start Reaching.

Using the Reader, you can share your glucose data with caregivers or a healthcare provider through the Share Your Glucose option in the menu bar. This cloud-sharing feature requires a Wi-Fi connection, and there are two ways to set up the network. First, you can scan a QR code to automatically obtain the Wi-Fi password. Or second, you can select Connect Manually and press the Square button to search for nearby Wi-Fi networks, then choose your preferred network and enter its password. Once the Reader connects successfully, you can generate and share the QR code or the invitation code with your care partner. Care partners can then monitor your glucose using the iCan Reach app. If you no longer want to share your data with a care partner, select Stop Sharing on the iCan Access screen, and press the Square button to confirm.

docs.title: 5.2 Sharing data with healthcare provider
docs.text:
Sharing your data with your healthcare provider is done via the iCan Review platform. The ability to share data via this platform is only enabled in certain regions, possibly due to GDPR constrictions.

If you are a healthcare provider and do not yet have an iCan Review account, go to https://sg.icancgm.com/review/ and select Register on the homepage to create one. After logging in, the system opens to the Workbench page, which displays glucose cards for all patients currently authorized to the healthcare professional or care team and under active monitoring. Each glucose card shows key patient information, including basic details, the most recent glucose reading, remaining device wear time, and the latest glucose trend curve. By default, glucose data from the past 12 hours is shown, but this view can be switched to display data from the past 4 hours or 24 hours. When managing a large number of patients, the search box in the upper-left corner lets you quickly locate a patient by first name, last name, or email address.

The Patient Management tab displays a list of patients who have authorised you or your healthcare team. The list can be searched using multiple filters, including patient name, email address, diabetes type, monitoring status, duration of non-CGM use, and glucose metrics.

Via Personal Data, you can view your clinic’s personal ID. Patients who already have an iCan app account can share their glucose data by entering this personal ID. For unregistered patients, you can invite them to share their glucose data using the Invite Patients button in the system.

docs.title: 5.3 Creating reports
docs.text:
Selecting a patient in the Patient List of the iCan Review data platform opens the Patient Details page, which provides a comprehensive view of an individual patient’s information and data. At the top of the page, you can see basic patient details such as name, gender, age, and diabetes type, along with the current glucose value and device status for the sensor being used. From the Patient Details page, authorized healthcare professionals can view and edit patient information by selecting Patient Details in the top-right corner. This opens a window where basic information, notes, glucose targets, and alert thresholds can be reviewed or updated. Patients can also be removed entirely by selecting Remove Patient from the same area.

Daily glucose patterns are accessed by selecting Daily Glucose, where you can view glucose indices and the glucose curve for a chosen date, along with events recorded by the patient in the user app, such as fingerstick readings, meals, exercise, medications, insulin, and other activities. A date selector allows you to switch between days, with the calendar visually indicating dates that contain glucose data or dates where a device was worn but no data were uploaded.

If you don’t have access to the iCan Review platform, you can still access glucose reports from the iCan app and iCan View Reader itself.

In the iCan app, the Glucose Overview Report lets you generate and share a summary of your past glucose data for up to the previous 15 days. To create one, tap Events at the bottom of the screen, select History Report, and choose the date range you want to review. You can also tap Event History to view all alert information, then tap Share to send the report by email to the person you want to share it with.

On the iCan View Reader, you can access your glucose reports by opening the Menu and pressing the Square button to go to the Glucose Reports page. From there, you can choose a 3-, 7-, 15-, or 30-day report, and pressing the Square button again takes you to the corresponding Monitor Details page. Each report includes your average glucose for the selected period and also shows your Glucose Management Indicator, the Standard Deviation, along with your Time in Range.

If you connect your reader to your computer using a data cable, you can also upload reports after downloading the iCan Glucose Data Explore program from the Sinocare website.

docs.title: In this video, we explored the iCan i6 system’s data sharing tools. Remember, a strong support network matters. By sharing your CGM data, you enable your care team to see patterns, respond promptly, and help you stay on track.
6. Connecting other devices (2 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on connecting your iCan i6 CGM to other devices. In this video, we'll outline sensor integration options with smartwatches, insulin pens, and insulin pumps. Let's start.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatches
docs.text:
Right now, the iCan i6 CGM cannot connect directly to an Apple Watch or any Android smartwatch.

That said, the open-source app xDrip+ can be set up to work alongside the iCan app as a Companion App. This means if the iCan app is running on your phone, xDrip+ can capture the iCan app’s notifications or locally available data and use it to display your glucose readings on your Android smartwatch.

Please note that connecting via open-source apps is not supported by Sinocare and is not approved by the FDA or European authorities. Choosing to do so carries personal risk and responsibility. We do not recommend this approach, but we are disclosing it so you have a complete picture of what is available.

docs.title: 6.2 Smart insulin pens
docs.text: The iCan i6 CGM does not support direct pairing with any smart insulin pen at this time.

docs.title: While the iCan CGM cannot be linked directly to any smart insulin pump at present, it is possible to connect an iCan CGM to AndroidAPS. However, the use of open-source applications is not supported by Sinocare and is not approved by the FDA or European regulatory authorities. Use is therefore at your own risk.
This video reviewed ways to connect the iCan i6 sensor with smart devices. These integrations can streamline your set-up and pave the way for more personalised diabetes management.
Congratulations on finishing this module on the iCan i6 CGM!
By deepening your understanding of this sensor, you’re empowering yourself to manage diabetes with greater confidence and control.
You’ve got the fundamentals in place — it's time to see what they look like in action. This video offers a real-world demonstration of the iCan i6.
docs.text:
As the iCan i6 CGM is a very recent system, independent reviews are not yet available on YouTube. There is, however, one video from Sinocare (in Indonesian) that clearly demonstrates the differences between the commonly used iCan i3 and the newer iCan i6 CGM. You can find the link below this video.

https://www.youtube.com/shorts/-7_00uzCPb8

docs.title: As you take the next steps, keep in mind that you’re not doing this alone. Stay in touch with your healthcare providers, connect with peers living with diabetes, and participate in supportive online communities. Use modern technology to manage your health with greater assurance and consistency.
Once more, congratulations on completing this module. Here’s to your health and continued success!

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alerts
  • 5. Sharing data
  • 6. Connecting other devices
