docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the Instinct CGM! Throughout this video series, we’ll cover everything you need to know about this sensor designed exclusively for MiniMed™ systems. Let’s get started.

The Instinct sensor measures 21 by 21 by 3 millimeters and weighs 1 g. It is inserted on the back of your upper arm using an all-in-one applicator. After a one-hour warm-up, it measures your glucose levels in real time and delivers readings to your insulin pump every five minutes. You can then view your sensor and pump data on both your MiniMed™ 780G insulin pump and your smartphone via the MiniMed™ Mobile app. The sensor lasts for up to 15 days. It is intended for people with diabetes 7 and older in the US, but age indication may differ in other regions.

Our "Master Module on Glucose Sensors" established the basics of CGM use, from when to consider sensor readings are reliable, to how to address problems like disconnections and allergies. We now shift our focus to essential training on the Instinct sensor, plus strategies to help you get the most out of the device in daily life.

This module will empower healthcare providers and diabetes patients alike to optimize their experience with the Instinct CGM. Please note, however, that these videos are not a substitute for medical advice. Always talk to your healthcare provider before adjusting your treatment plan.

These videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr. Diana Isaacs, the Director of Education in Training in Diabetes Technology at Cleveland Clinic Diabetes Center. With their expertise and perspectives, you are guaranteed a balanced assessment of the Instinct CGM.

Be assured that the content presented here is completely independent of Abbott, the sensor manufacturer, and Medtronic, the sensor distributor, allowing an impartial evaluation of the Instinct sensor's capabilities and limitations.

Upon completion of this module, you will achieve a comprehensive understanding of the Instinct CGM. You will learn how to insert, remove, and start the sensor; set alerts; share your glucose data; and connect your sensor to other devices. You will also get advanced tips for safe and proper use of the sensor. As a healthcare provider, you will be equipped to confidently support Instinct users; as a sensor user, you'll gain practical tools to maximize your experience with the device. Let's begin this meaningful endeavor together. Wishing you the very best!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (3.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to the first lesson of the Instinct CGM module. In this video, we’ll guide you through inserting and removing the Instinct sensor, and highlight major dos and don’ts.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text:
Gather the following:

  • an Instinct kit, which contains an all-in-one sensor applicator; and

  • alcohol wipes.

docs.title: 2.2 Inserting the sensor
docs.text:
To apply your sensor, you’ll need the MiniMed™ Mobile app on a compatible smartphone paired with your MiniMed™ 780G pump. Visit this webpage to check device compatibility: https://www.medtronicdiabetes.com/app-check

On your phone, open the MiniMed™ Mobile app. Tap the Menu button, then select Start sensor, and tap to confirm "Yes, Instinct."

Choose your insertion site on the back of your upper arm. Select an area that is flat, free from scars, moles, or stretch marks, and at least 1 inch away from insulin injection sites. Prepare your site by washing with plain soap. Let it dry. Then, clean the site with an alcohol wipe and allow to air-dry. Next, unscrew the cap from the sensor applicator. Place the applicator over the site, then push down firmly to apply the sensor. Gently pull the applicator away and make sure the sensor is secure.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: To remove the Instinct sensor, pull up the edge of the adhesive that keeps your sensor attached to your skin. Slowly peel away from your skin in one motion. Discard the used sensor following directions from your healthcare provider.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• To avoid the risk of no results and/or infection, do not use the sensor kit package or sensor applicator if:

  • it appears to be damaged,

  • the tamper label indicates the sensor applicator has already been opened, or

  • the sensor kit contents are past expiration date.

  • For the best connection, it’s important you wear your pump and sensor on the same side of your body. Your infusion set can be worn on either side.

  • Do not push down on the sensor applicator until placed over the prepared site to prevent unintended results or injury.

  • The sensor is designed to detect certain conditions which may occur where the sensor is not working as intended and needs to shut off. This may occur if the sensor gets knocked off from the skin, or if the sensor detects that it may not be performing as intended. When informed of the condition, replace your sensor. Contact MiniMed’s Customer Service if you receive a "replace sensor" message before the end of the wear duration.

  • Intense exercise may cause your sensor to loosen due to sweat or sensor movement. If the sensor is becoming loose or if the sensor tip is coming out of your skin, you may get no readings or unreliable readings, which may not match how you feel. Check to make sure your sensor has not come loose. If it has come loose, remove it, apply a new one, and contact Customer Service.

  • Upon removal of the sensor, any remaining adhesive residue on the skin can be removed with warm soapy water or isopropyl alcohol.

You now know the correct procedure for inserting and removing your Instinct sensor. Adhering to these best practices will help you get dependable glucose readings each day, enabling you to make confident, informed choices about your treatment.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (7.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on starting the Instinct sensor. In this video, you'll learn how to start the sensor and navigate the pump and the app. In addition, we'll go over some practical tips for living with your sensor. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 3.1 Starting the sensor
docs.text:
After applying the sensor, tap Next in the app. You can follow the “How to scan a sensor” steps in the app — which adjusts automatically depending if you’re using an iOS or Android device — or scan on your own. Make sure your pump is on the Home screen, with no active alarms or alerts. Then hold your phone near the sensor. If you’re using an iOS device, hold the top of your device near the sensor. If you’re using an Android device, hold the back of your device near the sensor. Once you hear the beep or feel the vibration, this confirms that the sensor has been started and begins the warm-up period.

When the sensor is starting, keep your pump close to the sensor until pairing is complete. You’ll see a warm-up message on both the pump and app home screen. Once pairing is successful, the connection icon turns green. Once the countdown ends, your pump will begin receiving sensor glucose (SG) readings.

docs.title: 3.2 The MiniMed™ 780G pump
docs.text:
The MiniMed™ 780G pump's Sensor feature automatically turns on when your Instinct sensor is paired to the pump. When the Sensor feature is active, the Home screen with CGM in Manual Mode displays your SG reading, as well as a trend arrow and a real-time graph. Also displayed on the pump Home screen are status icons, the Suspend Icon, and Active Insulin.

When the pump is using the SmartGuard feature, the Home screen displays a shield with your current SG, if available.

In the Manual Mode sensor display, trend arrows indicate the rate at which the most recent SG readings are rising or falling. They could be single, double, or triple downward or upward arrows; the more arrows that are shown, the faster the SG value is changing. It's also possible for trend arrows to be absent, if the rate of SG change is less than 1 mg/dL per minute, or if the sensor may be outside of operating limits.

As for the sensor graph, it includes the following information:

  • The most recent SG reading;

  • Historical SG readings for the last 3-hour, 6-hour, 12-hour, or 24-hour periods;

  • High and low SG limits;

  • Carb entries;

  • Delivered boluses;

  • Suspend events; and

  • Blood Glucose (BG) entries.

The sensor graph may also show a dotted line to represent backfilled SG values, which will be recorded anytime the sensor is out of wireless range from the pump.

To view the sensor graph from the home screen, press the Graph button on the pump. A full-screen view of the 3-hour graph appears. Press the Up button to navigate to the 6-hour, 12-hour, and 24-hour graphs. Press Left to view the SG readings and event details. To exit the full-screen view, press Back or press the Graph button again.

docs.title: 3.3 The MiniMed™ Mobile app
docs.text:
After downloading the MiniMedTM Mobile app on the Apple or Google Play app store, follow the prompts on the screen that explains how the app works. Create or sign in to your CareLink™ personal account. Read the agreement and tap ‘Agree’. Follow the prompts and select ‘Get started’. Select your pump. Pair your pump by selecting ‘Allow’. Allow use of Bluetooth by selecting ‘OK’. Read the Dosing Disclaimer and tap ‘Agree’. The app is now ready to use.

The MiniMedTM Mobile app Home screen shows an overview of data received from the pump. The Home screen is divided into two main areas: the status area and the graph. There is also a Menu button at the top of the Home screen.

The status area shows the most current data that the pump has sent to the MiniMedTM Mobile app and status messages that may require your attention. It shows your current SG value, trend arrows, active insulin, basal rate, status messages, and status icons.

The graph shows glucose values and event markers based on the data the app receives from the paired pump. To navigate the graph:

  • Swipe left or right over the graph to navigate through past and current data.

  • Stretch and pinch to zoom in and out from the 1-hour view to a 24-hour view.

  • Double-tap the graph to cycle through the various graph zoom levels, namely 24 hour, 12 hours, 6 hours, and 3 hours.

If you turn on high and low SG limits on the pump, the limits appear on the graph. The high limit line appears in orange, and the low limit line appears in red. The SG target range of 70 to 180 mg/dL or 3.9 to 10 mmol/L appears as a green band across the graph.

From the Home screen, swipe left to access your Time in Range (TIR) data and graphs.

Tap on Menu on the Home screen. The Menu screen shows the status of the insulin pump system and paired devices. The Menu screen also gives you access to the Notifications settings, Display Options, Pair Device function, Sync to CareLinkTM feature, Update pump feature, and About screen.

It's important to note that the MiniMedTM Mobile app is designed as a viewing tool and does not have the functionality to administer boluses or modify settings. Its primary purpose is to provide users with access to their Instinct sensor and insulin data, allowing them to monitor their glucose levels, insulin delivery, and Time in Range. Any adjustments or actions regarding bolus administration or settings should be made directly on the insulin pump itself.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Now, we’ll outline some important tips when using the Instinct CGM.

  • The sensor transmission range is 33 feet (or 10 meters) when unobstructed.

  • The sensor is water-resistant and can be worn while bathing, showering, or swimming. However, do not take your sensor deeper than 3 feet (or 1 meter) or immerse it longer than 30 minutes in water.

  • Taking more than 1000 mg of Vitamin C per day may falsely raise your sensor readings, which could cause you to miss a severe low glucose event.

  • You can safely have an MRI exam while wearing your sensor. Sensor readings may be inaccurate during the MRI, but sensor function returns fully back to normal after 1 hour.

  • The Instinct CGM cannot be calibrated. But when the sensor is paired with the MiniMedTM 780G, the system uses BG meter readings as a BG check to verify system performance.

In this video, we walked through starting the Instinct sensor, from pairing it with your smart device to finding your glucose data in the pump and app home screens. In their early days of Instinct CGM use, your patients may occasionally encounter issues, such as sensor errors or connectivity problems. In these moments, your patience and persistence are crucial. Carefully work through the troubleshooting guidance, and reach out to Medtronic Customer Support where appropriate. With your expertise and dedication, you are well equipped to help your patients fully benefit from their CGM.

docs.title: 4. Setting alerts (9 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on setting alerts with the Instinct CGM. In this video, you’ll discover what kinds of alerts the Instinct sensor can send and how you can tailor them to your needs. Let’s jump right in.

docs.title: 4.1 Available alerts
docs.text:
The MiniMed™ 780G AID system has a sophisticated safety network. If the network detects anything unusual, it communicates this information through notifications. Notifications include alarms, alerts, and messages.

An alarm warns of a condition that requires immediate attention. Low glucose levels and stopped insulin delivery are the most common reasons for alarms. When an alarm occurs, the pump displays a notification with a red icon and instructions. The red notification light blinks twice, followed by a pause, in a continuous repeating pattern. Depending on the sound and vibration settings, the pump also emits a series of alarm tones and vibrations. The underlying problem that triggered the alarm must be resolved. In most cases, press the Select or Down arrow button and then make a selection to clear the alarm. Sometimes the underlying problem is not resolved when the alarm is cleared. The alarm repeats until the underlying problem is fixed. If the alarm condition is not resolved after 10 minutes, the alarm tone escalates to a loud emergency siren.

Alerts indicate that a situation may require attention. When an alert occurs, check the pump screen to see if any action is required. When an alert occurs, the pump displays a notification with a yellow icon and instructions. The red notification light on the pump blinks once, followed by a pause, then blinks once again in a continuous repeating pattern. Depending on the sound and vibration settings, the pump generates a series of tones and vibrations as well. To clear an alert, press Down and then make a selection. The pump beeps every 5 or 15 minutes, depending on the alert, until the alert is resolved. Some alerts will also escalate to a loud emergency siren after 10 minutes.

A message is a notification that shows the status of the pump or displays when a decision needs to be made. When a message occurs, the pump displays a notification with a blue icon and instructions; some messages show a yellow icon. The red notification light on the pump does not blink. In addition, the pump emits a tone, a one-pulse-only vibration, or a combination of both. To clear a message, press Down and then make a selection.

There are also several specific reminders that prompt a specific action. They include personal reminders on BG meter readings and medication, as well as bolus and insulin reservoir reminders.

Let's shift our focus to the alarms and alerts related to the Instinct CGM. Alarms include Alert on low, Low SG, Sensor ending soon, Suspend on low, and Medical device. Alerts can refer to SG levels, sensor status, blood glucose, and insulin delivery.

All alerts are optional, except for the Urgent Low Alert that triggers when the sensor glucose value is at or below 54 mg/dl or 3.0 mmol/l, and the High alert when your sensor glucose is above 250 mg/dl or 13.8 mmol/l for more than 3 hours.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alerts on the pump
docs.text:
On the insulin pump, you can customize high and low SG alerts, set snooze durations for each, and silence alerts according to your preferences.

With High SG settings, you can enable the Rise Alert, Alert Before High, and Alert On High. To set up these high alerts, start from the Home screen. Press the middle circle button, select Settings, then choose Alert Settings, and tap High Alert. The High Setup screen will appear.

Begin by selecting a time segment. The end time will flash. The start time of the first segment is always 12:00 AM, and you can set up to eight time segments, each with its own high limit. All segments must add up to a full 24-hour period. Set the End Time, then enter your High Limit, choosing a value from 100 to 400 mg/dL in increments of 5 mg/dL.

To customize the high alerts for that segment, select the arrow beside the End Time. A screen will appear showing the available high alerts.

  • Turn on Alert Before High to receive an alert before reaching the high limit. Set Time Before High from 5 to 30 minutes.

  • ·Turn on Alert On High to receive an alert when the high limit is reached.

  • Turn on Rise Alert to receive an alert when SG is rising quickly.

Low SG settings alert you or suspend insulin delivery when SG approaches or reaches the low limit. To change low SG settings, start from the Home screen, press the middle circle button, and select Settings. Choose Alert Settings, then Low Alert. On the Low Setup screen, select Edit, adjust the time segment, and modify any low alert setting as needed. Select Next, then Review, and finish by selecting Save.

The High Snooze feature sets how long the pump waits before repeating a high alert if the high condition continues. To adjust it, go to Settings, Alert Settings, and Snooze High & Low. On the Snooze screen, select High Snooze and enter a duration in 5-minute increments from 5 minutes to 3 hours, then select Save.

The Low Snooze feature works the same way but for low alerts. From the Snooze screen, select Low Snooze and choose a delay from 5 minutes to 1 hour in 5-minute increments. Select Save.

The Alert Silence feature silences selected sensor alerts. When active, the Alert Silence icon appears on the Home screen. Alerts will still appear visually, but sound and vibration are muted.

To silence alerts, start at the Home screen and press the middle circle button. Select the volume icon, then choose Silence Sensor Alerts. Select from High Alerts Only, High & Low Alerts, or All Sensor Alerts. Set a Duration in 15-minute increments from 30 minutes to 24 hours, then select Begin.

To cancel Alert Silence, open the volume icon again, select Alert Silence, and tap Cancel Alert Silence.

docs.title: 4.3 Setting alerts on the MiniMed<sup>TM</sup> mobile app
docs.text:
The MiniMedTM Mobile app lets you receive alarms, alerts, reminders, and messages from your pump on your compatible mobile device. All notifications from the pump appear at the top of the Home screen. Alarm notifications are displayed in red background, alerts in yellow, messages in blue, and reminders in gray.

There are two notification settings you can control from the Notifications screen. First are Notifications from the pump. When this setting is turned on, alarms, alerts, messages, and reminders from your pump will appear on both your pump and your compatible mobile device. Your mobile device must also have notifications from the MiniMed Mobile app turned on. If either setting is turned off, the notifications will appear on your pump only.

Second are Repeat notifications. When this setting is turned on, notifications repeat every minute on your compatible mobile device until they are cleared on the pump or dismissed in the app. If this setting is turned off, notifications will not repeat on the mobile device.

Tap the toggle buttons to turn desired notifications on or off. Then Tap Done at the bottom of the screen. If you have turned on Notifications, a message appears to remind you that alerts and alarms must always be cleared on the pump. Tap OK.

docs.title: 4.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few reminders about Instinct sensor notifications on the pump and the app.

  • Since the Instinct sensor is free of calibrations, additional safety checks have been put into place. As a result, the Sensor Updating alert may occur when the system is confirming the reliability of the sensor. There is no need to take action unless recommended by the system. In the event that the updating lasts for more than 3 hours, consider changing the sensor.

  • Do not rely solely on notifications from the MiniMedTM Mobile app. Notifications on your compatible mobile device or those sent to care partners may not occur as expected, and they may not always be noticeable. Always rely on your pump as the primary source for notifications.

  • Always clear notifications on your pump. Dismissing a notification in the app does not clear it on the pump, so it is important to promptly respond to and clear all notifications that appear on your pump.

docs.title: In this video, we explored the alarms, alerts, and messages related to the Instinct sensor, and how they are delivered through the MiniMed™ 780G pump and mobile app. By tailoring these alerts to your needs, you can turn potential issues in your diabetes management into timely prompts for action, empowering you to stay in control of your health.
5. Sharing data (3.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Instinct CGM. In this video, we’ll walk you through sharing your sensor glucose data with your healthcare provider and loved ones. We’ll also discuss viewing and creating your glucose reports with the CareLinkTM system. Let’s start.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your healthcare provider
docs.text:
To share your data with your healthcare provider, Medtronic provides the CareLink platform. As a user or a family member, you can create a CareLink Personal account at carelink.minimed.com. As a healthcare provider, you can create a CareLink Clinic account at carelink.medtronic.eu, or via carelink.medtronic.com if you live in the US.

If you are a healthcare professional, you can add your patients to CareLink Clinic by entering their name and date of birth.

  • You can then invite the patient to share their data by sending an email through CareLink.

  • The patient can accept the invitation and enter the code from the email in their personal CareLink account or via the MiniMed Mobile app.

When using the Instinct app or MiniMed Mobile app, these are linked to your personal CareLink account during the initial setup. If your personal CareLink account is connected to your hospital's account, your healthcare provider can view your data in real-time.

docs.title: 5.2 Generating Reports
docs.text: Once your data is uploaded to CareLink, you can generate reports by selecting the reports option. The process of creating and interpreting reports is explained in detail in the MiniMed 780G automated insulin delivery system reports video.

docs.title: 5.3 Sharing data with loved-ones
docs.text:
To share Instinct data with family members or caregivers, you have two options.

  • Firstly, you can view CareLink data on your PC by creating a CareLink Personal account as a care partner. You will need to request access to the CareLink data of the user you want to track by entering their username. The user can then approve you as a caregiver through their own CareLink platform.

  • Alternatively, if you use the MiniMed Mobile app, you can share your data with up to 5 care partners using the CareLink Connect app. To begin, care partners need to download the CareLink Connect app on their compatible mobile phone. They must then create a care partner account and send a follow request by entering the username of the patient they wish to follow. The patient must accept this request through their MiniMed Mobile app, or directly via their CareLink account on a computer.

docs.title: 5.4 Tips
docs.text:
Take note of the following guidelines on sharing your Instinct sensor data.

  • For information to be sent to the CareLink™ Connect app, the MiniMedTM Mobile app must be:

  • within 6 meters (or 20 feet) of the pump

  • have internet connection

  • be logged into CareLink™ Personal on the app, and

  • have Sync to CareLink™ turned on.

  • Both Pump User and Care Partner must be logged into Carelink™ Personal for Carelink™ Connect App to receive data.

  • One MiniMed™ Mobile app user can have their data followed by up to 5 CareLink™ Connect app users, and one CareLink™ Connect app user can follow up to 5 MiniMed™ Mobile app users.

  • If nothing happens when you try to generate reports, you may need to disable your browser’s pop-up blocker. Call Medtronic Customer Service if the problem persists.

docs.title: In this video, we looked at multiple options for sharing your Instinct CGM data. Using these features helps you collaborate more effectively with your care team, so you can make prompt, data-driven decisions for improved health outcomes.
6. Connecting other devices (4.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Instinct CGM to other devices. In this video, we’ll tackle integrating your Instinct sensor with smartwatches, and smart insulin pens and pumps. Let’s proceed.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatches
docs.text:
The MiniMed™ Mobile app is compatible with Apple Watch. You can monitor your values on your watch face or tap to enter the app. The watch communicates with the iPhone device, not the insulin pump, so if your iPhone is out of range, you will not get updated glucose information on your watch.

To install MiniMed™ Mobile on your Apple Watch, make sure your Apple Watch and iPhone device are connected. You can check this in your Bluetooth settings. Download the MiniMed™ Mobile app from the App Store on your iPhone if you haven’t done so already. Next, you need the companion app on your Apple Watch. If this does not happen automatically, there are two ways of doing it manually.

First is to get the app via your Watch. Press the digital crown button to see the home screen, then tap the App Store online store. Tap Search and enter “MiniMed Mobile.” Find the right app with the MiniMed Mobile icon and tap get.

The second option is to get the app via your iPhone. On your iPhone device, open the Watch app. Tap the My Watch tab. Tap Install next to the MiniMed™ Mobile app.

Access the MiniMed app on your Apple Watch to view your glycemia within your SmartGuard shield, and swiping left will display your sensor graph and Time In Range.

To have easier access to your glucose data, you can integrate your sensor glycemia and SmartGuard Shield as a complication on compatible watch faces. These watch faces include Portrait, Nike (hybrid), Breathe, Utility, Simple, Photos, Motion, Gradient, XL, and Astronomy. Clicking on the complication will allow you to access the full MiniMed Watch app.

On the other hand, connectivity for Android watches requires the use of a third-party app like xDrip+. Please be aware that xDrip+ is not supported by Medtronic and lacks approval from regulatory authorities like the FDA or EMA, making its usage carry inherent risks that should be approached cautiously.

#1 Garmin smartwatch

For Garmin smartwatches, download the xDrip+ app from github.com/nightscoutfoundation/xdrip. Go to the settings of the xDrip+ app and set the data source to Companion app. This allows xDrip+ to receive sensor data from the MiniMedTM Mobile app. Also in the settings, adjust the inter-app settings to send the xDrip+ data to your connected Wear OS smartwatch. Then go to the Connect IQ store app on your phone, download the xDrip+/Spike/Nightscout Watch Face, and set xDrip+ as your companion app. On your watch, you can now choose the xDrip+/Spike/Nightscout watch face to see your real-time Instinct data on your Garmin smartwatch.

#2 Samsung Galaxy smartwatch

For Samsung Galaxy smartwatches, follow similar steps by downloading the xDrip+ app and setting the data source to Companion App in the settings. Adjust the xDrip+ app settings to send data to the G-Watch App by checking the Smartwatch and Inter-app settings. Next, install the G-Watch Wear app on both your phone and smartwatch and set xDrip+ as your companion app. Finally, choose the according watch face on your Galaxy watch, and customise the watch face and alerts on your watch as you prefer.

As a reminder, using open-source apps carries inherent risks and is not supported by Medtronic or approved by regulatory authorities. We are sharing this information for transparency, but we do not recommend this practice unless you fully understand the associated risks.

docs.title: 6.2 Smart insulin pens
docs.text: The Instinct sensor is FDA-approved for use within the MiniMed™ Go Smart MDI System. At the time this course was published, the MiniMed™ Go system had not yet been launched.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
The Instinct CGM is fully integrated with the MiniMed™ 780G insulin pump, allowing for coordinated diabetes management.

In contrast, the Instinct sensor is not compatible with open-source automated insulin delivery systems.

In this video, we examined how to connect your Instinct sensor with supported smartwatches, smart pens, and insulin pumps. With valuable insights and real-time data from these integrations, you can fine-tune your diabetes management and better support your long-term health goals.

docs.title: 7. Conclusion (1.5 min)
docs.text:
Congratulations on finishing this module on the Instinct sensor!

By learning how this CGM works, you’ve taken a purposeful step toward managing your diabetes with greater confidence and control.

Now that you have a strong grasp of the theory, we suggest watching the YouTube video below for an additional expert perspective on the Instinct CGM.

https://youtu.be/ai-E5Ch7Y1c?si=eXhECUYwaoXMjP92

In this video, Justin from Diabetech speaks with Medtronic’s Laura Endres, who explains the sensor’s main features, demonstrates application live, and discusses how it integrates with the 780G system and the InPen. They also compare Instinct with Simplera and discuss what’s next for Medtronic’s diabetes technology.

Blending this expert commentary with what you learned in this module can help you see what the Instinct sensor does best technically and translate those capabilities into day-to-day practice.

One last note: Always keep in mind that you’re not alone on this journey. Your healthcare team, other people living with diabetes, and online communities are there to support you. Keep making the most of today's innovative solutions to stay ahead and guide your health in the right direction.

Once again, congratulations on completing this module. May your growing knowledge and steady commitment keep propelling you toward the future you’re working for.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alarms
  • 5. Sharing data
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the LinX CGM. In this video series, we’ll break down the essentials of this continuous glucose monitor, one of the smallest CGM sensors on the market designed for both children and adults.

The LinX sensor measures 22 mm in diameter and 4.2 mm in thickness and weighs only 2.2 grams. It is applied to the back of the upper arm or to the abdomen using an all-in-one applicator. After the sensor insertion a warm-up period of approximately one hour is required. Once the warm-up period is complete, the sensor monitors glucose levels in interstitial fluid and transmits real-time readings to the LinX smartphone app via Bluetooth every minute. The sensor lasts up to 15 days and is intended for people with diabetes aged 2 years and older.

The sensor housing can be round or droplet-shaped. The droplet- or tear-shaped version is marketed as LinX Vista. Branding varies by country: in some regions, the same sensor is sold under the distributor’s name. Examples include the DiaExpert sensor, a droplet-shaped sensor distributed by Mediq in the Netherlands and Germany, and BUZUD CGM, distributed by BUZUD in Singapore and India.

Our “Master Module on Glucose Sensors” covered CGM fundamentals, including when to rely on sensor readings and how to manage issues such as disconnections and allergic reactions. We'll now focus on in-depth LinX CGM training and practical tips for getting optimal results with the device.

This module aims to support healthcare providers and individuals with diabetes in using the LinX CGM with competence and confidence. That said, these videos are for educational purposes only and do not replace medical advice. Please speak with your healthcare provider before changing any part of your treatment plan.

This module was produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr. Nicolae Panduru, a senior consultant and expert in diabetes at the International Medical Institute - Center of Diabetes, Nutrition and Metabolism in Bucharest, in Romania, who used the sensor in his center. Their expertise and experience make for a credible, balanced review of the LinX CGM.

This material was developed independently of the sensor manufacturer, enabling a neutral evaluation of the LinX CGM’s pros and cons.

By the end of this module, you’ll be prepared to use the LinX CGM effectively in real-world settings. You’ll learn the steps for placing and starting the sensor, configuring alarms, sharing glucose data, and linking the sensor to other devices. Along the way, you’ll receive expert tips to support safe use and reliable CGM results. If you’re a healthcare provider, you’ll be better equipped to support LinX CGM users in clinical practice. If you’re using the sensor yourself, you’ll gain practical, step-by-step guidance you can apply right away. Let’s now begin this journey together. Wishing you every success!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (3.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to the first lesson of the LinX CGM module. In this video, we’ll show you how to apply and remove the LinX sensor and outline the critical dos and don’ts at each step. Let’s jump right in.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text:
Have the following ready:

  • A LinX CGM pack, which contains an applicator with a built-in sensor and a quick guide; and

  • an alcohol wipe.

docs.title: 2.2 Applying the sensor
docs.text:
To use the sensor, install the LinX app on your smartphone. First, search for the term “LinX CGM” in the Google Play or the Apple Store. Download and open the app. Create an account using your email address. Then select a password. The password must include an uppercase letter, a lowercase letter, numbers, and be in English with at least 8 characters. Afterward, press Send to receive the verification code. Check your email to retrieve the verification code, and then enter it into the app within 60 seconds. Now you can log into the app. After logging in, the app will prompt you to select the preferred measuring unit, either in millimoles per liter or milligrams per deciliter. Now the app will check the required phone permissions to ensure it functions properly. Following this, enable Do Not Disturb Mode. The app will highlight when blood glucose measurements are needed. Next, you will be guided in setting the lower and upper target ranges. You will see information about the app’s directional arrows, background colors, and recommendations for the optimal sensor insertion locations, which include the back of the upper arm for adults or the abdomen on either side of the navel for children aged 2 to 17 years.

After disinfecting the skin with alcohol, let it air dry without using a towel or tissue. Now place the sensor applicator directly on the skin without applying much pressure or stretching the skin at the application site. Press the white button on the sensor applicator. Wait for 10 seconds. Carefully lift the applicator off the skin. The insertion is now successfully completed.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: When the app indicates that it has expired or if the user experiences any irritation or discomfort at the site while wearing it, the sensor should be taken off the insertion site. To remove it, gently lift one edge of the adhesive that secures the sensor to the skin, then slowly peel it off in a single, steady motion.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• You can only be signed in to your LinX account on a single mobile device at any given time.

  • Ensure the skin is thoroughly clean and completely dry before insertion; otherwise, the sensor may not adhere properly.

  • Do not use the sensor applicator if it appears damaged or if the safety seal is broken or missing.

  • Avoid touching or holding the inside of the sensor applicator, as the introducer needle is exposed.

  • Some bruising or bleeding may occur after insertion. If bleeding continues, remove the sensor and insert a new one at an alternative recommended site.

  • When changing sensors, you do not need to remove or unpair the existing sensor connection.

In this video, you learned how to place and remove the LinX sensor properly. Following these steps helps maintain dependable glucose monitoring over time and supports reliable data to inform your treatment decisions.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on getting started with the LinX CGM. In this video, you’ll learn how to start the LinX sensor and become familiar with the key features of the LinX app. We’ll also share tips for proper sensor use and wear in everyday life. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 3.1 Starting the sensor
docs.text: Go back to the app and press the Pairing icon. The sensor’s serial number will appear in the list of nearby devices. Select it. It will pair successfully, and the system will enter the warm-up phase. After 60 minutes, the first sensor blood glucose reading will be displayed on the main screen.

docs.title: 3.2 The LinX CGM app
docs.text:
The Home dashboard of the LinX app provides an overview of your blood glucose levels. Your real-time glucose reading appears in the upper left area and is automatically updated every minute. Beside this value is the trend arrow, which shows both the direction and rate of change in your glucose level. Different arrows indicate whether your glucose is stable, slowly decreasing, decreasing, rapidly decreasing, rapidly increasing, increasing, or slowly increasing.

Next to the real-time glucose reading and trend arrow, you can see the remaining sensor life. Once the sensor reaches the end of its wear period, its status on the app will change to “expired.”

In the top-right corner of the home dashboard, two icons are displayed: a bell icon and a personal account icon. Tapping the bell allows you to mute sensor alerts for six hours or to reactivate them. By tapping the personal account icon, you can access the share/follow, help center and multiple personal settings options of the LinX CGM app.

You can adjust the display period to view glucose trends and history from the past 6, 12, or 24 hours.

The glucose trends graph shows all recorded data from the time you first registered your LinX account. You can scroll across the graph to review glucose values over different time frames. Each data point indicates the glucose reading and the exact time it was captured. Readings outside the target range are highlighted in a different color.

At the bottom of the Home dashboard, you will find icons that take you to the History, Trends, Blood Glucose (BG), and Event dashboards.

The History dashboard lists your glucose alerts, logged events, and daily glucose summaries.

The Trends dashboard presents your glucose analysis over selectable periods (such as the last 7, 14, or 30 days, or a custom time interval), allowing you to compare patterns over time.

On the BG dashboard, you can carry out sensor calibration by entering a fingerstick blood glucose result from your blood glucose meter, provided it is measured and entered within 5 minutes of testing.

The Events dashboard lets you record and monitor factors that influence your glucose levels. You can choose from event types such as “Carbs,” “Exercise,” “Medicine,” “Insulin,” and “Other,” and specify the time each event occurred. These logged events are also shown in the History dashboard and are uploaded to the cloud server, so you can review your event history via the Cloud using your LinX App account.

docs.title: 3.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• The LinX CGM is factory calibrated, which means it does not require calibration. However, calibration with a finger prick value is possible. From the app home screen, select Fingerstick BG. Enter the fingerstick BG value and recording period, then select Calibrate.

  • Selecting Record will just record the BG measurement value on the trend curve, and it will be identified by a green dot, while calibration is identified by a red mark.

  • Taking paracetamol in doses higher than the recommended maximum (for example, more than 1 gram every 6 hours in adults) can interfere with sensor accuracy and may cause glucose readings to appear falsely elevated.

  • The sensor is water-resistant and designed to function reliably during activities such as swimming and showering. It remains operational when submerged in water up to 1.5 meters deep for as long as 30 minutes.

  • Remove the sensor before undergoing any X-ray, MRI, or CT scan. After the procedure, apply a new sensor to ensure accurate glucose monitoring.

This video guided you through the LinX CGM start-up process, including sensor pairing, app navigation, and optional calibration. As you support your patients during first use of the sensor, remember that technical issues can occur, such as login errors or readings that do not match how they feel. In these moments, encourage a calm, step-by-step approach. Work through the troubleshooting options and contact customer support if required. Your expertise and reassurance can help patients build confidence and get the most from long-term CGM use.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (2.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the LinX CGM. In this video, we’ll explore the sensor’s alarm options and show you how to personalise them in the LinX app. Let’s start.

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text: The LinX CGM provides alerts for Low Glucose, Urgent Low Glucose, High Glucose, Extreme High Glucose, as well as rapid changes, namely Glucose Rising Fast and Glucose Falling Fast. In addition to these glucose-related alarms, the app also generates a Signal Loss alarm if communication with the sensor is interrupted. You can choose to receive all these notifications as Sound, Vibration, or a combination of Sound & Vibration, and set the alert repeat interval anywhere between 5 and 60 minutes. The Urgent Low Glucose alert threshold is preset at 56 mg/dL (or 3 mmol/L) and is not user-adjustable. It automatically triggers both sound and vibration and repeats every five minutes.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the app
docs.text:
To adjust your CGM alerts, tap the Personal Account icon in the top right corner of the app’s Home screen, then select Alert Settings/Reminder Settings. On this page, you can turn on or off, and set threshold values for Low Glucose, Urgent Low Glucose, High Glucose, and Extreme High Glucose. You can also enable or disable the Falling Fast and Rising Fast alerts, and change how and how often alerts are delivered. In addition, the Alert Settings page lets you switch the Signal Loss Alarm and the Sound Always On option on or off.

  • To receive alerts, ensure that:

  • the alerts are enabled;

  • your smartphone is within approximately 2 meters (6.56 feet) of you (the effective transmission range is 2 meters in an open environment);

  • the app is running in the background at all times; and

  • the app is granted the phone permissions it requests

docs.title: In this video, we examined the LinX CGM alarm features and settings. When customized thoughtfully, these alarms can help you respond promptly to emerging issues, so you can stay confident and in control of your health.
5. Sharing data (4.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with LinX CGM. In this video, we’ll discuss how to share your sensor data with your healthcare provider and loved ones and create reports with the LinX app. Let’s get started.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your healthcare provider and loved ones
docs.text:
To share your glucose data with a healthcare provider or a caregiver, they must first create a separate account on the LinX app, following the same steps for sensor application. Note that caregivers may not use a sensor themselves, but may use the LinX CGM app to remotely access your glucose data and get notified for your alarms. From your LinX app, tap the Personal Account icon. Select Share/Follow. By default, My Shares is selected. Click + Account Authorization. Here, enter the email account of the healthcare provider or the caregiver to whom you want to share your data. You can also assign a nickname to distinguish between your followers. On the caregiver’s side they should select Personal Center, Follow/Share. Here they would choose My Follows, and they find your account listed among their followers. They can assign a nickname to you, and select which alarms they wish to receive. On the caregiver’s home screen, an additional icon will appear next to the Personal Center, allowing them to switch to remote monitoring of their list of followers’ trends.

Another option is Pancares, a diabetes data management platform that integrates with the LinX CGM and can be accessed from any device via a web browser. Sensor users can create a Personal Account to share CGM data with family and loved ones, and they can also grant access to their healthcare professional for remote data viewing. Meanwhile, healthcare providers can set up a Clinician Account for their practice.

If you're a healthcare provider, you can go to the Patients tab of the Pancares dashboard, then click Add New Patient. Fill in all the required fields marked with a red asterisk; once done, click Create. Refresh the page to ensure the new patient appears in your list. After selecting the patient, click on CGMs Monitoring, then click on CGMs App Account to link the patient's Pancares account to their LinX CGM data. There are two methods to complete this link. If the patient is not physically present in your clinic, select Enter App Account and input their email address. The patient must then open the Settings menu in their LinX CGM app, navigate to Permission Management, and select Share with Pancares Account to accept the pending request. If the patient is in the clinic, select Scan OR Code. They should then open the same Share with Pancares Account screen in their app, tap Scan Pancares QR Code, and scan the QR code on your Pancares screen to establish a connection.

docs.title: 5.2 Creating reports
docs.text:
To export an AGP report directly from the LinX app, navigate to the Trends screen. From this view, you can scroll through a general overview of your data or select a specific date range. Once the appropriate dates are set, tap the Export button located in the top right corner of the screen, then tap Confirm to generate a report.

After waiting a few seconds, a PDF report will display on the phone screen. Tap the Share button in the top right corner to send the PDF directly to your healthcare provider's or loved one's email address.

docs.title: As a healthcare provider, you can opt to use the PanCares platform to view and generate reports. Once you have linked a patient’s CGM account to PanCares, you refresh the webpage to ensure the most recent data is synchronized and visible on the dashboard. Upon refreshing, you will be able to view the patient's LinX CGM serial number, Time in Range statistics, and live glucose data if the patient is currently wearing their sensor.

To generate a report, select the patient in your list, then click AGP. Choose a date range to include in the report; look for dates marked with a small dot, which indicate that data is available for a specific day. After selecting the desired timeframe, click Generate Report to produce a PDF version of the AGP report.

In this video, we tackled the LinX CGM data-sharing and reporting features. Used regularly, these options make it easier for you and your healthcare provider to review trends, align on next steps, and make timely, data-informed treatment decisions.
6. Connecting other devices (3.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on connecting your LinX CGM to other devices. In this video, you'll learn how to integrate your sensor with smartwatches and outline possibilities with smart insulin pens and insulin pumps. Let's begin.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatches
docs.text:
Your phone and a compatible smartwatch can receive data directly from the LinX sensor at the same time. This allows you to view your real-time glucose readings, trends, and alerts directly on your wrist. On Android Watch, the LinX watch app comes pre-installed. On Apple Watch, the LinX watch app is automatically installed on the paired Apple Watch when you install the LinX app on your iPhone. A reliable connection is required for pairing and data transmission. Make sure Bluetooth is turned on for both your phone and watch, and that both devices are connected to a Wi-Fi or mobile network.

Confirm that you are logged in to the LinX app on your phone, then open the LinX app on your watch. On Android Watch, you can either double-tap the LinX watch face to open the app or select “LinX” from the watch’s app list. A QR code will appear on the watch screen. To link the watch to your account, use the scanner in the LinX phone app. From the Home screen, you can access the scanner by going to “Personal Center”, then tapping “Compatible Watch”, “Android Watch”, and “Scan”.

On Apple Watch, the LinX app can be accessed from the app list. Follow the prompts in the mobile app to complete login on the watch. Once your watch is logged in and linked to your account, it will automatically show data from the active sensor.

To pair the LinX sensor with an Android Watch, tap “SENSOR SEARCHING” to display the list of nearby sensor serial numbers (SN). Select your device, verify the SN against the label on the sensor box, then tap “PAIR.” After pairing is successful and the warm-up period is complete, real-time glucose values will appear in the watch app. For Apple Watch, follow the instructions in the mobile app to complete pairing automatically.

docs.title: 6.2 Smart insulin pens
docs.text: As of now, the LinX CGM does not connect directly with most commercially available smart insulin pens.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
The LinX CGM is designed to integrate seamlessly with the Equil S Patch Insulin Pump, with the long-term goal of enabling an automated insulin delivery system.

Currently, data from the LinX sensor and the Equil pump can be viewed together in the PanCares web platform; however, the AID algorithm is not yet commercially available.

In addition, the LinX CGM is mentioned to be compatible with AndroidAPS, an open-source AID system, via xDrip+ in companion mode.

Be aware that using open-source apps like AndroidAPS and xDrip+ carries inherent risks and is not supported by MicroTech Medical or approved by regulatory authorities. We provide this information transparently, though we don't recommend engaging in this practice without understanding the risks fully.

docs.title: In this video, we described how the LinX CGM links with smartwatches, insulin pens, and insulin pumps. These integrations can help unify your devices into one coordinated system, making it easier to act on trends and adjust diabetes care with greater precision.
7. Case report (1 min)
docs.text:
Congratulations on completing this module on the LinX CGM!

By dedicating time to understand this sensor, you’re setting yourself up for more confident and impactful diabetes management.

With the theory in place, the next step is to observe these concepts in action. The YouTube videos below offer a practical look at how the LinX works in real-life settings.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JvRMktp1TGk

https://youtu.be/I_vVnvSp97g?si=NUaCjLpNyh5_MavJ

docs.title: In these videos, Snehal shares her experience using the LinX sensor and app. By combining user insights like Snehal’s with what you have learned in this module, you can refine your approach and get the most value from the LinX CGM.

As you move forward, remember you are not doing this on your own. Stay connected with your healthcare team, connect with peers living with diabetes, and engage in supportive online spaces. Use today’s technology to stay on track and manage your health with confidence.
Once again, congratulations on finishing this module. May your evolving expertise and steady drive keep turning your goals into reality.
Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Placing the sensor
  • 3. Starting the sensor
  • 4. Setting alarms
  • 5. Sharing data
  • 6. Connecting other devices
  • 7. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the Sibionics GS3 CGM. In this series of videos, we’ll unpack the essentials of using the world’s thinnest sensor.

Measuring 24.8 by 7.8 by 2.9 mm and weighing 1.5 g, the Sibionics GS3 sensor is placed on the back of your upper arm using an all-in-one applicator. After a one-hour warm-up, it measures glucose levels in your interstitial fluid and sends readings to the Sibionics GS3 app on your smartphone via Bluetooth every five minutes. The sensor lasts for up to 14 days. It is intended for people with diabetes aged 3 and above.

Our "Master Module on Glucose Sensors" introduced the key principles of CGM use, including when to trust sensor readings and how to manage issues such as disconnections and allergic reactions. We now move forward with core training on the Sibionics GS3 sensor and practical tips for achieving the best results with the device in daily life.

Our goal in this module is to support both healthcare providers and individuals with diabetes in maximizing the benefits of the Sibionics GS3. That said, please note that these videos do not constitute medical advice. Always speak with your healthcare provider before modifying your therapy in any way.

These videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr Daniela Petrache, a diabetologist in Romania with hands-on clinical experience using the Sibionics GS3, including published reflections on its real-world use. Together, their complementary perspectives ensure a balanced, clinically relevant assessment of the Sibionics GS3 CGM system.

Rest assured that this material is produced independently of Sibionics, the sensor manufacturer, enabling a neutral and balanced review of the GS3 sensor’s benefits and drawbacks.

By the end of this module, you will have a solid, practical understanding of the Sibionics GS3 CGM. You will learn how to place and start the sensor, configure alarms, share your glucose data, and link the sensor with other devices. You’ll also receive advanced tips for safe and effective use of the device. For healthcare providers, this means confidence in supporting GS3 users; for sensor users, it translates into clear, practical guidance for everyday use of the CGM. Together, let's begin this meaningful journey. Good luck!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (3 min)
docs.text: Welcome to the first lesson of the Sibionics GS3 CGM module. This video will show you how to apply and remove the GS3 sensor and point out essential dos and don’ts. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text:
To get started, have the following ready:

  • A GS3 sensor kit containing a sensor applicator and a product insert; and

  • an alcohol wipe, which may or may not be included in the box depending on regulatory approvals in your country.

docs.title: 2.2 Applying the sensor
docs.text:
Scan the QR code on the product insert to download the SIBIONICS GS3 app, or download the app from your app store. Register an account with your email address or log in with your existing SIBIONICS account. Then follow the in-app instructions to set up your profile and preferences.

Wash your hands and get prepared. Choose a soft and visible area on the upper third part of the back of your upper arm. Then clean the site with an alcohol wipe and allow it to air dry completely for secure sensor application. Twist off the cap from the sensor applicator, and set the cap aside. Rest the sensor applicator over the prepared site, and then push the button down to apply the sensor to your body. After hearing the click sound, move the sensor applicator away. Secure the sensor by rubbing the adhesive edges. The sensor is now applied.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text: Your sensor automatically stops working after 14 days and must be replaced. To remove it, rub the adhesive edges with an alcohol wipe. Gently peel off the sensor and dispose of it properly. Then apply a new sensor as needed.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• Use a smartphone running Android 8.1 or later, or iOS 13.0 or later to ensure compatibility with the SIBIONICS GS3 app.

  • Do not use the sensor applicator if:

  • the twist-to-open label has been tampered with,

  • it seems to be damaged or already opened, or

  • it is past the expiration date.

  • Push down on the sensor applicator only when it is placed over the prepared site to prevent unintended results or injury.

  • Placement is correct as long as the sensor is applied in a vertical position. The round hole can point either upward or downward without affecting accuracy.

  • Applying the sensor may cause bruising or bleeding. In case of persistent bleeding, remove the sensor and contact your healthcare provider.

In this video, you learned the steps for proper placement and removal of the Sibionics GS3 CGM. By following these best practices, you promote reliable long-term monitoring, which provides accurate data to guide your treatment decisions.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on starting the Sibionics GS3 CGM. In this video, we’ll dive into starting the sensor and navigating the GS3 app. We’ll also cover some tips for safe and proper use of the sensor in your daily life. Let’s jump right in.

docs.title: 3.1 Starting the sensor
docs.text:
To pair the sensor with the SIBIONICS GS3 app, enable Bluetooth on your phone. For NFC-enabled phones, you can tap the sensor for instant connection. If your phone doesn’t have NFC function, you can scan the QR code or enter the six-digit code from the GS3 kit.

After pairing the sensor with GS3 app, wait 60 minutes for sensor warm-up before glucose data appears. It is recommended to remain on the page for at least one minute to ensure the connection is properly established.

docs.title: 3.2 The Sibionics GS3 app
docs.text:
Here’s what the GS3 app Home screen looks like. On it, you’ll see the most recent glucose reading, the remaining sensor life, and the glucose trend graph.

Glucose data from the past 3 to 24 hours are shown as a trend line, with the most recent glucose reading displayed on the far right. Tap any point on the graph to highlight the corresponding glucose value. Switch between 3-hour, 6-hour, 12-hour, and 24-hour views of your glucose data by tapping the desired time range above the graph. View the graph in full screen by tapping the full-screen control icon above the graph. Logged events appear as icons at the time they were recorded. Tap any event icon to see detailed information about that event.

The trend arrows reflect both the speed and direction of glucose changes based on recent readings. They indicate whether your glucose is rapidly rising, slowly rising, steady, slowly falling, or rapidly falling.

The event record is designed to capture information that may influence glucose levels. Using the Event feature on the smartphone app, you can log and save events such as meals, exercise, medication, insulin delivery and corrections, blood-glucose meter readings, sleep, and mood.

To add an event, tap the Plus icon at the bottom of the Home screen. Select or enter the required information, then tap Save. You can tap the event icon on the glucose trend graph to view detailed information about that event. Alternatively, tap the icon and then tap Logbook to review all the events you have added.

When you tap the avatar icon in the top left corner, the app’s Profile screen opens with the following options: Sensor, Logbook, Alarm Settings, Target Glucose Range, Reminders, Datahelper Setting, Glucose Bubble, Health Care, Help, Desktop Widget, and Contact Us.\

Tapping the cogwheel icon in the top right corner gives you access to additional settings.

docs.title: 3.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• Pair the sensor with your smartphone immediately after application. This helps prevent the built-in lithium battery from running out of power before the end of the 14-day wear period.

  • Only one sensor can be paired at a time. Pairing a new sensor will automatically disconnect the existing one.

  • Keep the sensor and smartphone within 10 meters (or 32.8 feet) of each other, with no obstacles such as walls or metal between them. Otherwise, communication may be affected.

  • For the best user experience, keep Bluetooth turned on and allow the app to run in the background to avoid missing data. Do not force close the app.

  • The sensor is designed to function normally within a temperature range of 5°C to 40°C. When the temperature is too high or too low, the CGM may display abnormal glucose readings. Normal operation will resume once the user returns to a suitable temperature environment.

  • Taking painkillers, high-dose vitamin C, or hydroxyurea can result in falsely high CGM glucose readings. Consult your healthcare provider first if you plan to use any of these medications while wearing your sensor.

  • The sensor must not be used with automated insulin dosing systems, including low-glucose-suspend systems.

  • Remove the sensor before undergoing an MRI or CT scan.

This video walked you through starting the Sibionics GS3 CGM, including pairing the sensor with your smartphone and finding your way around the app. When guiding your patients through their initial experience with the sensor, keep in mind that technology can sometimes present challenges, such as connectivity issues, incorrect sensor readings, or a sensor that stops prematurely. When these situations arise, stay calm and persistent. Review the available troubleshooting options and contact customer support if needed. With your knowledge, patience, and commitment, you can confidently empower your patients to fully benefit from their CGM.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (4 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the Sibionics GS3 CGM. In this video, we’ll look at the different types of alarms the GS3 sensor can generate and show you how to configure them in the app. Let’s start.

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
With the GS3 CGM, alarms fall into two main categories: glucose level and trend alarms and system status alarms.

Among alarms for glucose levels, the Urgent Low Glucose Alarm lets you know when your glucose is at or below 56 mg/dl or 3.1 mmol/L. The Low Glucose Alarm warns you when your glucose is at or below the low limit you have set. Meanwhile, the High Glucose Alarm notifies you when your glucose is at or above your selected high limit.

The GS3 sensor also provides alarms for rapidly changing glucose levels. The Rising Fast Alarm is issued when your glucose is increasing faster than 2 mg/dl or 0.11 mmol/L per minute and is above your specified threshold. Meanwhile, the Falling Fast Alarm appears when your glucose is dropping faster than 2 mg/dl or 0.11 mmol/L per minute and is below your specified threshold.

For any of these glucose-related alarms, you can tap Later to acknowledge the alert, or log an event you believe may have contributed to the change.

Until you acknowledge the alarm or log an event, the alarm box will remain on your app screen. The sound and/or vibration will repeat when the system issues the next alarm. Remember that the system analyzes your glucose readings every five minutes.

Under system status, a Signal Loss Alarm indicates that your sensor has temporarily stopped communicating with the app and you are no longer receiving glucose readings or Low or High Glucose Alarms. Follow the on-screen instructions to restore the connection. If the issue persists, contact Customer Service at support@sibionics.com.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the app
docs.text:
To customize your Low and High Glucose Alarms, go to the app’s Profile screen, tap Alarm Settings, then select Low Glucose Alarm, High Glucose Alarm, or both. Here you can set the Alarm value and choose an alarm frequency between 5 minutes and 4 hours, then select Sound, Vibration, or Vibration & Sound as the alarm style. If you select Sound or Vibration & Sound, choose an alarm tone; the volume will follow your device settings. When you’re done, tap Confirm to save your settings.

You can customize the Rising Fast and Falling Alarms too. Simply follow the on-screen prompts when these alarms appear.

If you want to adjust the Signal Loss Alarm, tap Alarm Settings, then Signal Loss Alarm. Set how long the sensor can go without communicating with the app before an alert is triggered. Next, choose your alarm style, and tap confirm to save your settings.

To restore the default alarm settings, tap Reset Alarm Settings.

docs.title: Turn Override Do Not Disturb on in the app for Urgent Low Glucose, Low Glucose, High Glucose, and Signal Loss alarms to ensure you receive audible alerts even when your device is on Do Not Disturb or has silent media volume.
You must allow access to Do Not Disturb on your device to use the Override Do Not Disturb setting.
For iOS: Allow Critical Alerts when prompted, or enable Critical Alerts from the app's notification settings.
For Android: Allow Do Not Disturb access when prompted, or enable Do Not Disturb access for the app in your notification settings.
The Urgent Low Glucose alarm will still trigger a vibration, even if your device is muted or Do Not Disturb is on.

In this video, we tackled setting sensor alerts with the Sibionics GS3 app. When you set up these alerts to match your needs, you can turn possible disruptions in your diabetes management into clear cues for action, helping you stay confident and in charge of your health.
5. Sharing data (4 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Sibionics GS3 CGM. In this video, we’ll outline steps for sharing your sensor data with your healthcare provider and loved ones. We’ll also discuss generating reports with the GS3 system. Let’s get started.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your healthcare provider
docs.text:
Tap the Profile icon on the app Home Screen. Under Connections, tap Health Care to invite a healthcare provider to view your sensor glucose readings and trend graphs. Tap Connect with New Clinic and enter the Practice ID of your clinic.

As a healthcare provider you can make a free account on SIBIONICS Proview via proview.sisensing.com/login. You can register as a healthcare provider to access the SIBIONICS ProView platform, and via the menu you can create your practice. When you create a practice, you will be able to set your Practice ID that you can later share with your patients.

Tapping the SIBIONICS ProView logo brings you to the homepage where you will be able to see your patients. Via the Patient tab you can add new patients. In your patient list, you can manage your view to see only starred patients, or patients with a specific glucose pattern. On the SIBIONICS ProView homepage you can tap a specific patient to view their data.

docs.title: At the time of creating this course, real-time sharing of GS3 data with family members is not yet available, but it will be released soon. Once enabled, you will be able to select a SIBIONICS Companion through the Health Care Connections tab and invite a follower who can monitor your data remotely.
5.3 Creating reports
docs.text:
Daily data shows the average of your sensor glucose readings, plus your Time in Range. Meanwhile, Daily Trending displays daily glucose readings as a graph, with event icons shown at the times they were recorded.

To choose the date for a report, tap Previous or Next at the top of the screen to move through daily data, or tap the date button to select a specific day to review.

AGP reports summarize data over multiple days and show the pattern and variability of sensor glucose levels over a typical day. The green band in the AGP represents the target glucose range. Note that the AGP requires at least 7 days of glucose readings to ensure statistical validity.

To export an AGP report, tap Report in the GS3 app. Set the time range to 7, 14, 30, or 90 days, or tap Custom to adjust the start and end dates manually. Then tap View Report to generate the report for the selected period. You can download or share the report using the icons in the top-right corner of the screen.

In the SIBIOINCS ProView Platform, it is possible to see 3 types of reports when you click on a patient from the homepage: AGP Report, Compare Report and a Daily Glucose Report. For each report, it is possible to change the date range. In the Compare Report, you can compare the AGP details with a previous period. In the Daily Report, you can hover over the glucose curve to see the specific glucose value, and also the specific record information like pictures of the food if they were taken at that time. Finally, it is always possible to download the reports from the download icon in the top right corner.

In this video, we explored the Sibionics GS3 CGM’s tools for sharing glucose data and generating reports. By utilizing these features, you and your care team can work together more effectively, enabling timely, data-guided decisions that support better health.

docs.title: 6. Connecting other devices (2.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Sibionics GS3 CGM to other devices. In this video, you’ll learn how to link your sensor with smartwatches and discover possibilities with smart insulin pens and pumps. Let’s begin.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatches
docs.text:
SIBIONICS does not yet offer a dedicated smartwatch app. A native Apple Watch app and an Android Watch app with direct-to-watch capability are in development.

There is a dedicated SiWatch, but it appears to work only with the GS1 sensor.

On Android, you can use third-party apps to view GS3 data on a smartwatch. Juggluco can connect to the native Sibionics app. xDrip+ can connect via a patched Sibionics app, and xDrip+ Companion mode also appears to work with the native app. Both Juggluco and xDrip+ can display glucose values on Wear OS and Garmin watches.

For Apple Watch users, there is currently no working solution.

docs.title: 6.2 Smart insulin pens
docs.text: Currently, the Sibionics GS3 CGM does not connect directly to most commercially available smart insulin pens.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart insulin pumps
docs.text:
At present, the GS3 sensor is not compatible with any commercial AID systems. However, Sibionics is actively working toward integrating its sensor technology with insulin delivery. In particular, Sibionics has entered into a strategic partnership with PharmaSens to jointly develop an all-in-one insulin patch pump system that combines glucose monitoring and insulin delivery in a single device.

Separately, the GS3 can be linked to Juggluco or xDrip+, both of which can interface with AndroidAPS, a known open-source AID system. However, the GS3 is not considered a trusted glucose source for automated insulin delivery. It is not approved for use in any commercial AID system, and publicly available accuracy data remain very limited.

It's important to note that using open-source apps is not supported by Sibionics, nor is it approved by the FDA or European authorities. Engaging in this practice is entirely at your own risk. While we don't recommend it, we believe in being transparent about all available options.

docs.title: In this video, we described how the Sibionics GS3 app links with smartwatches, insulin pens, and insulin pumps. These connections could create a smooth, coordinated system that supports smarter and more personalized diabetes care.
7. Case report (1.5 min)
docs.text:
Congratulations on completing this module on the Sibionics GS3 sensor!

Your dedication to understanding this CGM paves the way for more confident and impactful diabetes management.

The next step is to connect your solid theoretical foundation with real-world use. We suggest watching the YouTube video below to get a practical sense of how the Sibionics GS3 sensor performs in everyday life.

https://youtu.be/hEsZpfyDZuw?si=zIyELzpNDRFeY_a6

In this video, Leticia shares her honest feedback and practical tips based on her personal experience with the Sibionics GS3 sensor. The video is in Spanish, but if you don’t speak the language, don’t worry — YouTube can automatically dub it into English and other languages.

Combining authentic experiences like Leticia’s with the concepts from this course can help you translate your knowledge into optimal results with the GS3 CGM.

One final reminder: You’re not on this journey alone. Work closely with your healthcare team, connect with others living with diabetes, and engage with online communities. Keep leveraging technology to make informed decisions about your health.

Once again, congratulations on finishing this module. We’re rooting for you as your new insights and skills move you toward greater success.
