docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the Syai Ultra CGM System! In the upcoming videos, we'll examine the core capabilities of this compact, AI-powered sensor that generates high-frequency glucose readings.

The Syai Ultra CGM System measures approximately 19 × 20 × 4 mm and weighs 1.2 g. The sensor and transmitter are integrated into a single unit called the monitor, which is applied to the back of the upper arm using a disposable applicator. After a 30-minute warm-up period, the device transmits real-time glucose readings to the Syai app every minute. It uses factory calibration and does not allow user-performed calibration with fingerstick glucose measurements. The system incorporates AI-based technology to generate predictive alerts, personalized dietary insights, and glucose trend reports. The Syai Ultra is cleared for up to 14 days of wear and is indicated for adults aged 18 years and older.

Having covered the fundamentals in the Master Module on Glucose Sensors — such as understanding sensor readings and resolving common issues — we’ll now shift our focus to detailed training on the Syai Ultra CGM System.

Our goal in this module is to help you use the Syai Ultra CGM effectively, whether you’re a clinician supporting patients or an individual living with diabetes. Please note that the material presented is educational in nature and should not replace medical guidance. Be sure to speak with your healthcare provider before adjusting your treatment plan.

This module was produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Lukas Trcka, a person living with type 1 diabetes from the Czech Republic who blogged about his experience with the Syai Ultra sensor. Together, their technical knowledge and clinical experience support an evidence-based, impartial analysis of the Syai Ultra CGM.

This material was created independently of Syai Health Technology, the CGM manufacturer, ensuring an objective assessment of the Syai Ultra’s benefits and drawbacks.

Upon completing this module, you will be confident in using the Syai Ultra CGM, share glucose data, and link the sensor to compatible smart devices. Also, you’ll be equipped with expert-backed tips to help you use the device safely and confidently.

Healthcare professionals will enhance their patient support skills, while Syai Ultra users will gain practical strategies they can implement right away. Let's dive in. I wish you all the best ahead!

docs.title: 2. Placing the sensor (4.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to the first lesson of the Syai Ultra CGM System module. In this video, you’ll learn the correct method for applying and removing the Syai Ultra sensor, including safety tips and best practices. Let’s get started.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text:
Prepare the following:

  • a Syai Ultra CGM pack, which includes

  • a disposable applicator with a built-in monitor composed of an integrated sensor and transmitter;

  • and an adhesive patch;

  • and an alcohol wipe.

docs.title: Download the Syai app by scanning the QR code on the product packaging or by searching for “Syai” in the App Store or Google Play Store. Follow the on-screen instructions to install the app. Open the app and tap “Sign up.” Enter your email address as your account, then tap “Send.” A verification code will be sent to your email. Enter the verification code, tap “Next,” and create a password for your account. Once registration is complete, tap the Syai app icon on your mobile device to launch the application and log in using your registered account details.

Select the unit of measurement, either mmol/L or mg/dL, to be used in the app and in your glucose reports, and confirm your selection. If you are unsure which unit to choose, consult your healthcare provider.

Follow the on-screen instructions to complete the app setup. This includes enabling Bluetooth, enabling Location Services, allowing Notifications, and entering your personal information. After entering your personal information, you will be directed to the “Apply New Monitor” page.
Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and water. Choose an area on the back of your upper arm as the application site. Clean the site with an alcohol wipe, and let it dry completely.
docs.text: Check whether the sensor packaging is damaged or compromised. Rotate the package open and remove the device. Next, rotate the applicator to prepare it for application. Ensure the applicator is positioned relatively vertically during insertion. Place the applicator over the sanitized area on your arm, and press the button to apply the sensor. Finally, gently pull the applicator away from your arm.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the sensor
docs.text:
When the monitor has been worn for 14 days, or after you tap the “End My Session” button, the session will be terminated. Before disposing of the monitor, ensure that all data has been uploaded by following the instructions provided in the app.

To remove the monitor, gently lift one corner of the adhesive at the edge of the device and slowly peel it away from the skin. If any adhesive residue remains, clean the area with warm, soapy water.

Dispose of the used monitor in accordance with local guidelines for items that have been in contact with body fluids.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• When setting up the Syai app, select the correct unit of measurement to ensure accurate interpretation of your glucose data. Once the unit is confirmed, it cannot be changed unless a new account is registered.

  • When choosing an application site, avoid areas with scars, moles, stretch marks, or lumps. The selected area should remain generally flat most of the time. Consider shaving the site to ensure proper adhesion.

  • Do not use the applicator if it has been opened or damaged before use. The needle is sterile unless the applicator has been opened or damaged. Do not put the cover back on, as this may damage the monitor. Do not touch the inside of the applicator, as it contains a needle. During application, hold the applicator flat against your arm and ensure the bottom edge is fully adhered to the skin to avoid application failure. Before removing the applicator, keep it pressed against your arm for a few seconds to help the adhesive attach properly.

  • Applying the monitor may cause minor bleeding. If bleeding occurs, wipe the area with a cotton swab. If necessary, use the cotton swab to apply gentle pressure on the small opening on the monitor, or apply an ice pack to help stop the bleeding. Remove the monitor and apply a new one at a different site only if the bleeding does not stop.

  • Replace the monitor immediately if there is irritation or discomfort at the application site, if the Syai app reports a severe problem and indicates removal, or if the monitor filament tip has completely detached from the application site.

This video demonstrated how to apply and remove the Syai Ultra CGM System. By performing this process correctly each time, you ensure accurate glucose monitoring and build confidence in your therapy decisions.

docs.title: 3. Starting the sensor (7 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on starting the Syai Ultra CGM System. In this video, we’ll show you how to get started with the Syai Ultra sensor and navigate the Syai app. In addition, we’ll share essential tips to promote safe and proper daily wear of the sensor. Let’s begin.

docs.title: Tap “Apply New Monitor” on the Syai Ultra app screen. Read the Instructions for Use carefully for important user guidance and additional information. Then scan the QR code located on the exterior of the applicator to verify the monitor. Be sure to follow the on-screen instructions for the best possible experience. Once the connection is established, you will be directed to the warm-up page. Real-time glucose readings will become available in the app once the 30-minute warm-up period is complete.
3.2 The Syai app
docs.text:
The Syai app consists of four functional pages: Dashboard, Summary, Logbook, and Profile.

The Dashboard displays your current glucose value, a trend arrow, a glucose graph, and a glucose card on the graph.

Your glucose reading updates automatically every minute. The background uses a color scale to indicate whether your glucose level is high or low.

The trend arrow shows the direction and speed of change in your glucose level. It may indicate that your glucose is rapidly rising, rising fast, gradually rising, steady, gradually falling, falling fast, or rapidly falling.

The glucose graph displays your readings over the past 3 hours, 6 hours, 12 hours, or 24 hours.

The glucose card shows the time and glucose value for a selected point on the graph. When the label remains on the right side of the graph, it displays the current glucose reading and its time stamp. The data appears in different colors depending on whether your glucose level is within your target range.

The glucose graph also shows your selected target glucose range, which is used to calculate related glucose metrics, including Time in Range (TIR) and other analytics. The default target range is set at 70–180 mg/dL or 3.9–10.0 mmol/L. This range can be adjusted to accommodate different clinical needs or usage scenarios.

Below the glucose graph, you will find Today’s TIR and Average glucose.

To view additional glucose insights, open the Summary page. Tap the bar at the top of the screen to select different time periods, including 1, 7, 14, 30, or 90 days, and review your TIR and other glucose metrics.

On the Profile page, tap the purple device card to view details about the current monitor, including the remaining session time, serial number, and other device information. You can also end the current monitoring session from this page. In addition, the Profile page allows you to connect to compatible devices and tools within the Syai Health Ecosystem. You can manage data sharing with caregivers and healthcare providers, and access your glucose reports too.

You can tap the plus icon in the upper right corner or select Logbook at the bottom of the Home screen to record events such as blood glucose test results, meals, medications, insulin injections, exercise, sleep, or other custom entries. There is also an AI logging feature that allows you to record events using voice input. The system automatically recognizes the content and converts it into the appropriate event categories. You will then be prompted to review, edit, and save the entries in the app.

Beyond event logging, AI integration is a key component of the Syai Ultra system. The app includes an LLM-based AI expert that is available 24/7 to respond to user inquiries. It continuously learns from user interactions and provides personalized responses. To access the Syai AI feature, swipe up on the Home screen.

docs.title: 3.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• If startup fails, close the app and scan the QR code again to retry monitor activation.

  • During startup, you may occasionally receive a prompt to update the system. If this occurs, follow the on-screen instructions to complete the upgrade.

  • After the 30-minute warm-up period, the monitor requires additional time to stabilize with your body. During the first hour of wearing a new monitor, use a blood glucose meter to make treatment decisions.

  • If your glucose readings do not match how you feel, check whether the monitor is loose or has become dislodged. If the filament is partially or fully out of the skin, remove the monitor immediately and apply a new one at a different site.

  • Certain medications may affect the accuracy of glucose readings measured by the monitor. These include ascorbic acid (or vitamin C), salicylic acid, aspirin, methyldopa, tolbutamide, and acetaminophen (or paracetamol).

  • To use Syai AI, ensure your device is connected to a trusted Wi-Fi network. Syai AI is enabled by default. If the portal is not responding, review your settings and confirm that the feature is turned on.

  • Strenuous physical activity may cause the monitor to loosen due to sweating, increased body temperature, or muscle movement around the application site. If the device becomes loose, glucose readings may not reflect your actual levels. For this reason, it is important to follow the application guidelines carefully and choose an appropriate site.

  • The Syai monitor is waterproof for normal activities such as showering, bathing, or swimming. However, it may not communicate properly while submerged because Bluetooth signals are weakened in water. Do not expose the device to water deeper than 1.5 meters (or 5 feet) or for longer than 30 minutes. After contact with water, dry the monitor with a clean towel.

  • Exposure to hot water may reduce the monitor’s service life. Do not use the device near heat sources, such as fireplaces or radiant heaters, where temperatures may exceed the recommended operating range.

  • Do not clean the monitor with household cleaners, chemicals, solvents, bleach, abrasive pads, or sharp objects. To clean the surface, use a small amount of rubbing alcohol only.

  • You may continue wearing the monitor when passing through walk-through metal detectors or body scanners.

  • Remove the monitor before undergoing an MRI, CT scan, or X-ray procedure.

In this video, we covered the Syai Ultra CGM system startup process, including pairing the sensor and finding your way around the app. Although technical challenges may arise from time to time, most can be resolved with a systematic troubleshooting approach and timely support. With your guidance and reassurance, you can help your patients optimize their CGM experience.

docs.title: 4. Setting alarms (6 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on setting alarms with the Syai Ultra CGM system. In this video, we’ll explore the sensor’s alarm features and show you how to customize them on the Syai app to suit your personal preferences. Let’s jump right in.

docs.title: 4.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
The system provides two main types of alerts: glucose alerts and system alerts.

Let’s start with glucose alerts.

  • You will receive an Urgent Low alert when your glucose reading is at or below 55 mg/dL, or 3.1 mmol/L. This alert cannot be adjusted.

  • A Low alert is triggered when your glucose reaches or falls below the low threshold that you have set.

  • A High alert occurs when your glucose reaches or exceeds your selected high threshold.

  • You can also enable optional Rising Fast and Falling Fast alerts. These notify you when your glucose level is increasing or decreasing rapidly, based on your chosen rate-of-change settings.

Now let’s look at how alerts are delivered.

  • All alerts include a snooze function, which can be set between 5 and 60 minutes.

  • You can also activate predictive alerts. These can warn you 20 or 30 minutes before your glucose is expected to reach your high or low threshold.

  • You can choose how you want to be notified: via normal phone sound, high-pitch sound, a beep, vibration, or silence.

There is also a “Vibrate” option and a “Silence All” option.

  • Silence All mutes every alert — including Urgent Low and system alerts — for a selected duration between 30 minutes and 6 hours.

Now let’s move to system alerts.

  • You will receive a Signal Loss alert if there is a problem with the connection between the monitor and the app, or if network issues are delaying data transmission. This alert can be turned on or off in the settings.

  • You will also be notified when your monitor is nearing the end of its wear period, and if the device is not functioning properly.

In the system notification window, you can see your current glucose value, the trend arrow, and the time of the most recent data update. The information displayed changes depending on the status of your monitor.

Finally, mobile home screen widgets are available. These widgets display different information depending on the current status of your monitor, allowing you to quickly view your glucose data without opening the app.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alarms on the Syai app
docs.text:
To configure alerts in the Syai app, go to the Profile page, tap the Settings icon, and then select Alerts.

Under the Quiet Modes section, you can choose preset options that control how alerts notify you. These modes do not override your phone’s system notification settings, but they do affect the sound settings for individual Glucose and System alerts. Read each description carefully to avoid missing important alerts.

Under the Glucose Alerts section, you can choose the specific glucose alert you want to customize.

The Urgent Low alert cannot be turned off and its threshold cannot be changed. But you can set how you want to be notified and choose from different ringtones. If the condition continues, you will be alerted repeatedly based on the interval you have set.

You can also enable Predictive Alerts for Urgent Low and Low alerts. Specifically, you can set a timeframe for Urgent Low Soon or Low Soon notifications. The app will then alert you if your glucose level is predicted to reach or fall below your selected threshold within the chosen time period.

For Rising Fast and Falling Fast alerts, use the toggle to enable or disable each alert. In addition, you can set the rate-of-change threshold so that you are notified when your glucose is rising or falling at or beyond the selected rate. Regarding Signal Loss alerts, you will only be notified if the connection issue continues beyond the time threshold you have set.

You can create an additional Alert Profile to customize how alerts behave in specific situations, such as during meetings. After setting your alert preferences, you can define a schedule by selecting the active time period for each day and choosing the days of the week for repetition. If you do not enable the Repeat option, the profile will apply only to the selected time period for that day and will not carry over to other days. The original alert configuration is saved as the Primary profile, which allows you to switch between the default and your custom profile as needed. After adding a new profile, you will have a total of two alert profiles available. Finally, If you select Reset to Default on the Alerts page, your custom alert profile will be deleted, and all alert settings will return to their original defaults. Use this option with caution.

docs.title: Enable notifications for the Syai app during the initial setup. Do not disable app notifications in your mobile device settings. If notifications are turned off, you will not receive alerts and may be at risk of missing significant glycaemic events.

Although the alert system has been clinically tested, always pay attention to how you feel. If your symptoms do not match the alert notifications, if alerts are not triggered during suspected hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemia, or if you believe your readings may be inaccurate, perform a fingerstick test using a blood glucose meter for confirmation and consult your healthcare provider if necessary.

In this video, you learned all about Syai Ultra alarm settings and configuration. When optimized and monitored consistently, these alerts allow you to act swiftly on glucose changes and maintain steady control over your health.
5. Sharing data (5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on sharing data with the Syai Ultra CGM System. This video covers how to grant sensor data access to loved ones and clinicians, as well as how to create glucose reports. Let’s start.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing data with your loved ones
docs.text:
You can share your glucose data with a family member or friend by sending an invitation through the Syai app. Go to Profile and select Home Care. Tap Invite to follow on the Home Care page. On the next screen, enter the caregiver’s email address or phone number, then set the visibility permissions for that account. Tap Confirm to send the invitation.

The caregiver must download the Syai Link mobile app and register for a Syai Link account before they can accept the invitation. They must complete the registration using the same email address that you entered in the Syai app.

Note that you can invite up to 15 caregivers per Syai account.

docs.title: 5.2 Sharing data with healthcare provider
docs.text:
On the Profile page of the Syai app, tap Doctor Care. Send an invitation to your provider using their email address or phone number. You may link up to five doctor accounts to a single Syai account.

Your healthcare provider can accept the invitation using a registered Syai Doctor account through the Syai Doctor web portal.

If you don’t have an account yet, click Create Now below the Log In button. Enter your email address, then click Register Now, accept Syai’s privacy policy and terms of use by ticking the checkbox, and click Register Now again. Enter the verification code sent to your email, then click Next.

After that, create and confirm your password. Your password must be 8 to 36 characters long and include at least one digit, one uppercase letter, and one lowercase letter. Click Confirm to proceed.

Next, provide the required account information, including your first name, last name, country, state or province, city, patient grouping phone number, and patient grouping. Review your details, then click Next. Leave the Partner Allocation option toggled on to ensure you receive optimal support from a Syai Partner or from Syai Health, depending on your location. Click Next again.

Finally, select and confirm your preferred blood glucose unit—either mg/dL or mmol/L. Once completed, you will be taken to the dashboard, also known as the Patient Management page.

Alternatively, clinicians can use the Syai Doctor mobile app. After receiving the email invitation with instructions, they must download the app, register using the same email address, and follow the steps to begin viewing your data.

docs.title: 5.3 Creating reports
docs.text:
Aside from the Profile Report, which summarizes your personal details and CGM information, the Syai app provides several types of glucose reports.

The Snapshot report presents an overview of glucose data, low glucose events, insulin, meals, and Time in Range or TIR for your selected date range.

The Daily Glucose Profiles display glucose data from midnight to midnight for each day, with the corresponding date shown in the top-left corner.

The Ambulatory Glucose Profile (AGP) presents a graph of the 10th, 25th, 50th, 75th, and 90th percentiles of your glucose readings over the selected report period, showing overall glucose variability.

The Mealtime Patterns report analyzes glucose and insulin data for typical meals based on meal logs recorded within the chosen timeframe. It identifies patterns for users who log rapid-acting insulin and meals in the app. Entries that are not tagged with a meal type are excluded from the analysis.

The Statistics report provides a daily and hourly summary of your glucose data, including Time in Range; high and low glucose percentages; average, maximum, and minimum values; as well as variability metrics such as standard deviation, coefficient of variation, MAGE, and MODD.

Lastly, The Daily Log offers a detailed view of meal entries, insulin records, other logged events, and corresponding glucose readings for each day within the selected date range.

To view your glucose reports, select Glucose Report on the Profile page. From the list of available reports, choose the report you want to open in the report viewer. From there, tap the icon in the upper-right corner to download or share the report.

docs.title: In this video, we discussed the data-sharing features of the Syai Ultra CGM System. Remember that working closely with your care team is key. By sharing your glucose data, they can track trends, intervene when needed, and guide your continued progress.
6. Connecting other devices (4.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on connecting your Syai Ultra CGM System to other devices. In this video, we'll examine how the sensor works in sync with smartwatches, insulin pens, and insulin pumps to support diabetes care. Let’s get started.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatches
docs.text:
Glucose data from the Syai app is synced to supported applications and then mirrored to compatible smartwatches.

Supported Apple devices include Apple Watch models running watchOS 9.0 or later. To install the app, search for “Syai” in the Watch App Store or download it from the App Store on your paired iPhone, which will then sync to your watch.

For Garmin devices, including selected Forerunner, Fenix, MARQ Adventurer, and other models that support the Connect IQ Store, search for “Syai” in the Connect IQ Store and download it.

For Samsung Galaxy Watch series, Galaxy Watch Active series, and other models running Google Wear OS, download the app from the Google Play Store.

Google Pixel Watch 2 is also supported and can install the app from the Google Play Store.

To connect your Syai Ultra CGM with a Garmin or Galaxy smartwatch, open the Syai app and go to the Profile tab. Tap Connect with Watch under the Connections section. On the next screen, tap Garmin under App Bridging. You will be taken to the Garmin data authorization page. Tap Connect Now, then follow the on-screen prompts.

Once you’ve installed the Syai app on your Garmin, Galaxy or Pixel watch, open the Syai app on your phone and go to the Profile page. Tap Connect with Watch under the Connections section. For Garmin, tap Garmin under App Bridging. You will be taken to the Garmin data authorization page. Tap Connect Now, then follow the on-screen prompts. For Galaxy and Pixel, under Direct to Monitor, select Wear OS watches. You will be taken to a screen with a QR code scanner. Tap Scan now, then scan the QR code on the Syai app on your watch to log in.

Note that smartwatch compatibility is based on internal testing, and availability may vary depending on the specific model, region, or regulatory requirements.

The Syai watch app displays your current glucose reading, trend arrow, and glucose graph, similar to what you see in the mobile app. You will also receive notifications on your watch for events such as Urgent Low, Low, and High alerts, and you can acknowledge these alerts directly from the notification. Moreover, the watch app shows the monitor status, including messages such as “No Active Monitor,” “Warming Up,” or “Disconnected.”

For users who prefer other smartwatch ecosystems, the Syai app can locally broadcast glucose data to xDrip, enabling display on additional devices such as Amazfit, Pebble, MiBands, and other compatible smart bands. Glucose data can also be transmitted from the Syai app to Nightscout (CGM in the Cloud) for remote monitoring and integration with other diabetes management tools.

docs.title: 6.2 Smart insulin pens
docs.text:
At this time, direct Bluetooth pairing with smart insulin pens is not available for the Syai Ultra.

However, connection with NovoPen® 6 is possible via NFC scan. By scanning the pen with a compatible smartphone, insulin dose data can be transferred and integrated within the app.

docs.title: Currently, the Syai Ultra does not offer native, built-in integration with smart insulin pumps for commercial automated insulin delivery systems. However, it can connect to insulin pumps through AndroidAPS. To set this up, install the Syai app from the Google Play Store and start your sensor session as usual. Once the sensor is active, open AndroidAPS, go to Config Builder, and select Syai as the primary BG Source. Then return to the Syai app. Open your Profile, and go to the Syai Health Ecosystem menu. Select “Connect Now” with AAPS, accept the third-party data transfer agreement, and enable the data-sharing toggle. This allows real-time glucose readings from Syai to broadcast directly into AndroidAPS.

Please be aware that open-source applications like AndroidAPS and xDrip+ are not endorsed by Syai Health and are not approved by the FDA or European regulators. If you choose to use these systems, you assume full responsibility for any associated risks. While we do not recommend this pathway, we are providing this information in the interest of transparency.

In this video, we outlined the Syai Ultra's connectivity with wearable devices and insulin delivery systems. By bringing these technologies together, you enable a more cohesive and individualized approach to diabetes management.
Congratulations on finishing this module on the Syai Ultra CGMS!
A solid understanding of how this sensor works strengthens your ability to manage diabetes with confidence and initiative.
Now that the fundamentals are in place, let’s hear directly from a clinician about their real-world experience with the device.
docs.text:
https://youtu.be/fddwPskVu_c?si=Qv_jjojGXWhrAmmC

https://youtu.be/Xa0b1ujT0sI?si=9i3iz9iRGhlWfvM6

In these videos, Eman showcases the Syai CGM’s compact design and advanced AI-driven features. She also explains how the Syai app connects with smart devices and selected third-party platforms. Although the device featured is the earlier Syai CGM model, it shares core technology with the Syai Ultra, making her review relevant to your understanding of the Ultra’s functionality and integration capabilities.

The video is in Arabic, but you can use YouTube's automatic subtitle feature to follow along in English.

docs.title: As we conclude, remember that you’re not alone in this journey. Keep in touch with your healthcare team, connect with others living with diabetes, and seek out constructive online communities. Make full use of today's technology to stay engaged and on track.
Once more, excellent job completing this module. Here’s to your continued progress and lasting well-being.
Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• Introduction
  • Placing the Sensor
  • Starting the Sensor
  • Setting Alarms
  • Data Sharing
  • 6. Connecting Other Devices
  • Conclusion

docs.title: 6. Connecting Other Devices (1.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on how to connect your TouchCare Nano sensor to other devices. In this section, we’ll explore which devices are compatible and how they can support your diabetes management.

docs.title: 6.1 Smartwatches
docs.text:
If you use your TouchCare Nano sensor with the EasyPatch or EasyTouch app connected to your PDM, you can enable pop-up alarms on your compatible phone and smartwatch, giving you quick access to important information.

For iPhone users, a native Apple Watch integration is available through a dedicated Watch App, allowing you to view your sensor data in real time on your watch. However, if you use an Android phone or another type of smartwatch, real-time glucose display on your watch is currently not supported.

docs.title: 6.2 Smartpens
docs.text: The EasyPatch and EasyTouch apps are primarily designed for users of the TouchCare Nano Pump. At this time, they do not offer direct integration with smart insulin pens.

docs.title: 6.3 Smart Insulin Pumps
docs.text:
The TouchCare Nano sensor is not compatible with third-party insulin pumps or open-source closed-loop systems.

However, it pairs seamlessly with the TouchCare Nano 200 U or 300 U patch pump. When used together with the EasyPatch app or PDM, they offer a closed-loop system, automatically adjusting insulin delivery based on your CGM readings.

This makes the TouchCare Nano sensor and pump a powerful and convenient combination for managing diabetes.

docs.title: Conclusion (1.5 min)
docs.text:
Thank you for following along with this TouchCare Nano set-up guide.

We’ve covered everything you need to get started—from placing and starting your sensor, setting up alarms, sharing data, to connecting your device with other smart systems. By following these steps and tips, you can make the most of your TouchCare Nano CGM.

Continuous glucose monitoring is a valuable tool that helps you stay informed and make proactive choices in your diabetes care. Keep your app and settings up to date, regularly check your device connections, and work closely with your healthcare team to fine-tune your management plan.

Normally, we would end this lesson with a video review from an experienced user—but unfortunately, such videos aren’t available yet. This suggests the TouchCare Nano sensor is not widely used in Western countries. In addition, while the sensor carries a CE mark, it does not have FDA approval, and no peer-reviewed accuracy data has been published so far. Studies on its accuracy are still ongoing.

Until more official data becomes available, we advise you to be extra cautious:

  • Always do a finger prick check if you don’t feel well or if the sensor value seems off.

  • Consider doing occasional finger prick checks during the sensor wear period to double-check your values.

We hope this guide has given you the knowledge and confidence to use the TouchCare Nano CGM wisely. Stay connected, stay curious, and take charge of your diabetes journey!

docs.title: Data Sharing (4 min)
docs.text:
In this lesson, we will explain how to share your glucose data with healthcare providers and family members who support your diabetes management.

If you use the EasyPatch app, your data will automatically be sent to the Medtrum cloud every 2 minutes. If you use the PDM, you can connect the PDM to your phone using the EasyTouch app, so data is uploaded automatically from your PDM to the EasyTouch app every 2 minutes. This app mirrors the information on your PDM and also sends data to the cloud.

docs.title: 5.1 Sharing Data with Your Healthcare Provider
docs.text:
Your healthcare provider can access your glucose data through the EasyView Pro platform, while patients and caregivers can use EasyView to monitor and review CGM data. Both EasyView (for users and caregivers) and EasyView Pro (for healthcare providers) accounts can be created free of charge.

To get started:

  • Ensure that your EasyPatch or EasyTouch app is connected to your sensor and transmitting data.

  • Your provider can add you as a patient using your registered email and invite you to connect.

  • Once you accept the follow request, your healthcare provider will be able to follow you.

docs.title: 5.2 Creating and Viewing Reports
docs.text:
Within the EasyPatch app, limited reports are available for the user. You can select daily sensor reports and trend analysis over 7, 30, or 90 days.

To see more comprehensive reports, you can log into the EasyView platform. Here, multiple reports are available:

  • Dashboard, which gives you an overview and averages on the patient's data.

  • Daily summary, which gives you a detailed overview of a selected date.

  • Day by day overview, which gives you either the last 7 or 14 days worth of data on one page.

  • Sensor overlay, which gives you the last seven days worth of CGM readings all on top of each other.

  • Trend analysis, which gives you details of both pump and CGM averages.

  • Events, which gives you the history of every event that has been logged through the patient's phone or PDM handset.

  • Logbook, which gives you details of the last 7 or 14 days worth of data in regards to BG levels, carbohydrate intake, bolus dosages along with further information.

  • Alert, which gives you a history of every alert that has appeared on the mobile phone or PDM handset, as well as the pump.

  • And finally Device Settings Review, which gives you details on the settings of the senssor.

Reports can be printed, downloaded, or shared electronically, helping you and your care team make informed decisions.

docs.title: 5.3 Sharing Data with Family and Caregivers
docs.text:
Family and Caregivers can use the EasyFollow app. This app allows you to share your real-time glucose data with an unlimited amount of caregivers. Here’s how:

  • To connect, caregivers can find the user account by entering their email address in the EasyFollow app or on the EasyView website.

  • To accept follow requests, the user then goes to Main Menu → Settings → Account Security → Remote View Permission in the EasyPatch app, or to Profile and Connections on the EasyView website.

  • Once connected, caregivers can view real-time glucose readings, trend arrows, and receive alerts, with the ability to adjust notification preferences while the user maintains control over shared data.

Sharing data with healthcare providers and loved ones creates a support system that enhances diabetes management. By taking this step, you ensure that those who care for you can help keep you safe and supported.

docs.title: Introduction (2 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this guide on the TouchCare Nano sensor, a continuous glucose monitor or CGM that can enhance your diabetes management. In this chapter, we will introduce the key features of the TouchCare Nano sensor and how to make the most of its benefits. Let's get started!

The TouchCare Nano CGM works alongside a PDM or dedicated mobile app and provides continuous glucose readings for up to 14 days per sensor. The reusable transmitter can be used repeatedly for up to 1 year and communicates with a PDM or smartphone via Bluetooth. The system measures your glucose levels every 2 minutes and displays the data conveniently on your smart device.

In other training materials, we have covered general glucose sensor usage, including when to rely on sensor data and tips for dealing with sensor issues or disconnections. In this guide, we will focus on the setup process for the TouchCare Nano CGM and provide practical guidance to help you use it effectively.

Please note that this guide is not a substitute for professional medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any changes to your diabetes therapy.

The content of these videos has been developed by Dr. Inge van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The materials have been reviewed by Dr. Sarah Siegelaar, an endocrinologist at Amsterdam UMC in the Netherlands and Davor Skeledzija from Croatia, who’s daughter with type 1 diabetes has used the TouchCare Nano CGM. Their expertise and perspectives ensure that you receive honest opinions on the pros and cons of the TouchCare Nano sensor.

By reaching the end of this guide, you will understand:

  • How to correctly place and start the TouchCare Nano sensor

  • The process of setting alarms for better glucose management

  • Sharing your glucose data with family, caregivers, and healthcare professionals

  • Connecting the sensor to other compatible devices

  • Useful tips and best practices for daily use

By the end of this guide, both healthcare providers and users will have the confidence and know-how to get the best results from the TouchCare Nano CGM. Enjoy your journey toward smarter glucose monitoring!

docs.title: Placing the Sensor (5.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on placing the TouchCare Nano sensor.

In this section, we will focus on correctly placing the TouchCare Nano sensor to ensure accurate glucose readings and comfortable wear. Let’s go through the process step by step.

docs.title: 2.1 Supplies
docs.text: To successfully place the sensor, gather the following supplies: a TouchCare Nano sensor package with a loaded inserter, a TouchCare Nano transmitter, and alcohol wipes for hygiene.

docs.title: 2.2 Inserting the Sensor
docs.text:
Follow these steps for proper insertion:

  • Start by washing your hands with soap and water and allow them to air dry.

  • Now choose a flat and pinchable vertical placement site on the back of the upper arm or a horizontal placement site on the abdomen.. For children you can use a horizontal placement site on the abdomen or upper buttocks. Clean it with an alcohol wipe and let it air dry completely.

  • Open the sensor package. Gently remove the sensor.

  • Use your thumb and index finger to bend off the Safety Lock by pushing it to the left or right.

  • Remove the two pieces of protective liners. Avoid touching the adhesive area.

  • Place the sensor vertically on the back of the upper arm.

  • Place one finger on the top of the inserter and apply gentle downward pressure toward the skin to ensure the adhesive is securely attached to the skin.

  • While maintaining pressure, press the gray button firmly until you hear a “click” to insert the Sensor.

  • Remove the inserter perpendicularly away from the support mount.

  • Discard the inserter into a sharps container.

Now it's time to attach the transmitter. The triangle symbol on the transmitter should align with the rounded edge that extends from the sensor base. Keep the transmitter parallel to the sensor base. Then snap it in. Make sure the transmitter is securely connected. The green indicator light on the transmitter will flash three times after a successful connection. It will flash another six times within 1 minute indicating the system check has finished. Run your finger around the edge of the adhesive pad to make sure the pad remains tightly adhered to your skin.

docs.title: 2.3 Removing the Sensor
docs.text:
When the 14-day wear period is over or if early removal is necessary, follow these steps:

  • Under normal use, the sensor session will end automatically after 14 days. After that, you can remove the sensor from your body.

  • If removing early, disconnect the sensor from the PDM or dedicated mobile app first.

  • Gently lift the edge of the adhesive tape from your skin and remove the sensor and transmitter. We recommend that you wipe around the tape with alcohol wipes or baby oil to make the tape peel off easily.

  • Bend down the rounded edge of the sensor base to break it. Pull the transmitter away from the sensor base.

  • Safely discard the sensor and reuse the transmitter. Please charge the transmitter.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & Don’ts
docs.text:
• Before using the transmitter for the first time, make sure the battery is fully charged. For the very first charge, allow about 2 hours to fully power up the transmitter. After that, it's recommended to recharge the transmitter after each sensor session—this should not take longer than 20 minutes. To charge the transmitter, use the charging cable included in the transmitter box, along with a power adapter that delivers exactly 5 volts. Using the correct adapter helps ensure safe and efficient charging. Connect the USB end of the charging cable to the adapter's USB port. Plug in the power adapter. Align the transmitter parallel to the charging dock and snap it in securely. The indicator light on the transmitter will flash green, indicating that the transmitter is being charged. If it doesn't immediately flash green, don't worry, as it may take some time. Once it's fully charged, the green indicator light will turn off. Remove the transmitter from the dock once it's fully charged, to prevent battery degradation.

  • For added security and to keep the transmitter from moving it is strongly recommended that you apply a double-sided over tape to the transmitter. Prepare the enhanced tape. Turn the white part of the tape to your face. Peel off the sticky paper on the top. Hold one side of the enhanced tape and then peel off the sticky paper on the bottom. Peel off the round part of the white paper liners. Do not remove the smaller part of the over tape just yet. Hold the blue over tape by its covered ends, and position it according to the head direction of the CGM. Now apply one side of the blue overtapes, so that it covers the center of the transmitter, and apply the other side of the tape directly on your skin. The hole in the center of the tape should fully expose the transmitter. Smooth down the tape on your skin to reduce air pockets. Now remove the smaller part of the white paper liners and press the remaining over tapes against your skin. Peel off the blue protective film with the overtape, leaving only the transparent layer on your skin. Run your fingers around the edges of the overtape to further secure the adhesion.

  • Save your transmitter serial number and sensor code for future use; taking a picture is a helpful back-up.

  • For technical assistance, refer to the official Medtrum support team.

By following these guidelines and troubleshooting steps, you can confidently place and remove your TouchCare Nano sensor, ensuring optimal performance and reliable glucose monitoring.

docs.title: Setting Alarms (5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome! In this lesson, we will discuss setting alarms with the TouchCare Nano sensor.

In this section, we will walk you through the available alarm options and demonstrate how to set them up using the PDM and the EasyPatch app. Let’s get started!

docs.title: 4.1 Available Alarms
docs.text:
The TouchCare Nano CGM provides various alarm options to keep you informed about your glucose levels and sensor conditions. These include alarms for:

  • Low glucose levels

  • High glucose levels

  • Lost Sensor, when the sensor signal is lost for more than 10 minutes due to Bluetooth issues

  • No readings, when the sensor can’t detect glucose values. If the sensor isn’t properly inserted, replace it. Otherwise, wait—it may recover. Don’t remove the sensor unless you receive a Sensor Failure alert.

Additionally, the system features predictive alerts to warn you of upcoming high or low glucose values and rate-of-change alerts for rapidly rising or falling glucose levels.

One critical alert is the Low Glucose Alert below 54 mg/dl or 3.1 mmol/L, which is mandatory and cannot be turned off for your safety.

You can also use the snooze function to manage repeated alerts for ongoing high or low glucose levels. Moreover, the alert schedule feature allows you to customize alarms based on different time segments to fit your daily routine.

To ensure you receive these alarms, keep your phone and sensor within 10 meters (or 33 feet) of each other without obstructions.

docs.title: 4.2 Setting alerts on the PDM
docs.text:
To set up your glucose alerts on your PDM, slide to unlock your PDM. Slide your finger from right to left to access the main menu. Tap on EasyLoop. Tap on glucose alerts. Now turn on your glucose alerts. Tap on glucose limits you can add up to eight different time segments for the day depending on your need for different glucose alerts at different times. Tap add time segment to add a new time segment. You can adjust the glucose limits for low and high by tapping on the blue numbers below low and high. Once you have completed this, tap the save icon in the top right hand corner of the PDM to save your glucose limits.

From the glucose alerts menu:

  • You can also set predictive alerts to be alerted of a predicted high or low glucose value between 5 and 30 minutes in advance.

  • Via the rate alerts you can set a rapid fall or rapid rise alert

  • And via the repeat function, you can set the amount of time between alerts after the first alert. You can set the Repeat time of High Alerts between 5 minutes and 3 hours, and Low Alerts between 5 minutes and 1 hour.

Alerts can be given as sounds, vibrations, or both. To adjust this setting, go to the Main Menu, tap Settings, then General, and select Audio Options.

docs.title: 4.3 Setting alerts on the EasyPatch App
docs.text:
To set up your glucose alerts on your EasyPatch app tap on the three lines in the top left hand corner to access the main menu. Tap on EasyLoop. Tap on glucose alerts. Now turn on your glucose alerts. Tap on glucose limits. You can add up to 8 different time segments for the day depending on your need for different glucose alerts at different times. Tap add time segment to add a new time segment. You can adjust the glucose limits for your low and high by tapping on the blue numbers below low and high. Once you have completed this, tap on the save icon in the top right hand corner of your smart device to save your glucose limits.

From the Glucose Alerts menu, you can customize several alert options:

  • Set predictive high and low alerts, as well as rapid rise and rapid fall alerts.

  • Choose a specific tone for the mandatory low glucose alert below 54 mg/dL or 3.1 mmol/L.

  • For other high and low alerts, you can set the tone, repeat time, and snooze time:

  • Repeat time controls how long to wait before getting another alert if you don’t clear the original one.

  • Snooze time controls how long to wait before getting another alert even if you do clear or acknowledge it.

By managing these settings thoughtfully, you can stay informed and proactive in maintaining your glucose levels with the TouchCare Nano CGM. This approach empowers you to respond quickly to changes and improve your diabetes management.

docs.title: Starting the Sensor (7.5 min)
docs.text:
In this lesson, we will show you how to start the TouchCare Nano sensor.

You can connect the sensor using either the PDM or the EasyPatch app. We’ll explain everything step by step.

If you don’t have a TouchCare Nano pump, you can also use the EasySense app. But since most people use the sensor with the pump, we will not talk about the EasySense app in this guide.

docs.title: 3.1 PDM
docs.text:
The PDM, or Personal Diabetes Manager, is a small device that connects to both the TouchCare Nano sensor and pump.

It has a touchscreen and a Home Key on the front, a power button on the top, and a charging port and sound hole on the bottom. On the upper left corner, there is also an indicator light that can flash green, yellow or blue in different situations. When charging for example it flashes blue, while a continuous green light indicates full charge.

  • To turn it off, long-press the power button for about 2 seconds, then slide to power off. Or long-press the power button for about 8 seconds.

  • When the screen goes black, press the power button and then slide to unlock.

To start the TouchCare Nano sensor using the PDM, follow these steps:

  • First, connect your transmitter to the PDM. Slide to the left from the Main Screen. Tap Sensor from the Main Menu. Tap Transmitter SN. Manually enter the serial number printed on the transmitter or on the transmitter packaging label.

  • Next, connect your sensor to the PDM. Slide to the left from the Main Screen. Tap Sensor from the Main Menu.

  • Tap Connect Sensor. Tap Enter. Enter the sensor code from the sensor package. Tap Done to connect the sensor. Tap Next.

  • Next apply your TouchCare Nano sensor and transmitter on your skin if you haven’t done so yet.

  • After the warm up, which takes up to 30 minutes, you will receive glucose readings. On your main screen, you’ll see:

  • Your current glucose level along with a trend arrow. If your reading is below 40 mg/dL (or 2.2 mmol/L), it will show “LOW,” and if it's above 400 mg/dL (or 22.2 mmol/L), it will show “HIGH.” Always double-check these extreme readings with a fingerstick and act accordingly.

  • The home screen of the PDM also shows an estimate of the insulin you still have on board, and a glucose trend graph shown below your current reading.

  • You can also:

  • Long-press the trend graph to open a larger view in landscape mode.

  • Swipe left to open the Main Menu.

  • Or swipe right to see the status screen.

  • Before you remove a sensor from your body make sure it has been disconnected. Go to the main menu of your PDM, select sensor, select disconnect sensor. If you don't see the disconnect sensor option, skip this step.

docs.title: 3.2 EasyPatch App
docs.text:
The EasyPatch app can connect to both the TouchCare Nano sensor and pump. You can download it on most iPhones and Android phones. To check if your phone works with the app, go to medtrum.com/compatibility. You can find the app by searching “Medtrum” in the App Store or Google Play.

To start the TouchCare Nano sensor using the app, follow these steps:

  • Open the Medtrum EasyPatch App, and register or log into your account.

  • First, connect the transmitter to the EasyPatch app. From the homescreen, Go to the Main Menu. Tap settings, tap CGM system. First, ensure Factory calibration is turned on, so you can receive sensor glucose readings immediately after warm up, without manual calibration. Tap add transmitter SN. You can scan the QR code for the transmitter serial number on the label of the transmitter packaging box. Tap OK. Or you can choose to manually enter the serial number printed on the transmitter or on the transmitter packaging label.

  • Next, connect your sensor to the EasyPatch app. From the home screen, tap Main Menu, tap CGM, tap connect sensor, tap enter code. You can scan the sensor QR code on the sensor package or manually enter the sensor code. If you scan the code tap OK to confirm the code is correct and the App will return to the home screen. If you manually enter the code, tap done to go back to the home screen.

  • Next apply your TouchCare Nano sensor and transmitter on your skin if you haven’t done so.

  • After the warm up, which takes up to 30 minutes, you will receive glucose readings. Your home screen will show:

  • The current glucose reading with a trend arrow. Readings below 40 mg/dl or 2.2 mmol/L will display as “LOW,” and those above 400 mg/dl or 22.2 mmol/L will show as “HIGH.” Always verify extreme readings with a fingerstick and take necessary action.

  • The home screen also shows a glucose trend graph below the current reading.

  • In landscape mode, you can view the glucose trends over the past 24 hours.

  • Before you remove a sensor from your body make sure it has been disconnected from your Medtrum app. Go to the main menu. Select CGM. Select disconnect sensor. If you don't see the disconnect sensor option, skip this step.

docs.title: 3.3 Do’s & Don’ts
docs.text:
• The sensor is water-resistant up to 2.5 meters for 60 minutes, allowing for showering and swimming.

  • If you’re using the PDM, make sure to charge it with the included charger. The initial charge typically takes around 2 hours and can power the device for up to one week. The PDM can store up to 90 days of data. Even if the battery runs out or a system error occurs, your data will remain safe. To keep everything running smoothly, ensure the date and time are set correctly. You can adjust the date and time from the Settings Menu when there is no active Patch

  • If you're using the EasyPatch app, you may notice your phone’s battery running down a bit faster. Even if your phone powers off, your data remains secure and will update once the app restarts. The app can store up to 90 days of data, so you won't lose valuable information.

  • Bluetooth communication is essential for the EasyPatch App to connect to your sensor. The App can detect and notify you about a disconnection. As soon as the problem is solved, communication will be resumed.

  • The sensor can be calibrated if needed.

  • On the PDM: go to the Main Menu, tap Sensor, then select Sensor Calibration. Here you can enter your finger prick blood glucose value, tap done and tap yes to calibrate your sensor.

  • In the EasyPatch app: tap the Enter BG shortcut button located on the bottom menu of the home screen. Here you can enter your finger prick blood glucose value, tap done and tap OK to calibrate your sensor.

  • Notes can be made in the app. From the Main Menu, tap events to add events like food intake, exercise, or insulin delivery for comprehensive data.

By following these steps and being proactive in addressing any issues, you can make the most of your TouchCare Nano sensor and achieve effective, continuous glucose monitoring.

docs.title: Smart Insulin Pen Systems
docs.text: Select a module from the sidebar to get started.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Diabetes Technology Expert Program

Smart MDI System (InPen)

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Set Up the Smart MDI System
  • 3. Use the Smart MDI System
  • 4. Report Interpretation
  • 5. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the Smart MDI system, a connected solution for people with diabetes using multiple daily injections.

This system combines the Bluetooth-enabled InPen with a powerful mobile app, allowing users to:

  • Calculate accurate insulin bolus doses by choosing one of the 3 possibilities: Carb counting, Meal estimation or Fixed Dose

  • Log insulin administration automatically

  • Integrate glucose data from the Simplera CGM

  • And share information with care teams or caregivers

Please note that while the system can be connected to the Guardian 4 sensor, this module will not cover that specific connectivity.

Also, be aware that Simplera CGM and Simplera Sync are two distinct sensors:

  • Simplera CGM is intended for use with the current Smart MDI system.

  • Simplera Sync is designed for integration with the MiniMed 780G insulin pump.

Like insulin pumps and CGM systems, this Smart MDI solution is designed to improve safety, confidence, and outcomes in diabetes care. Key advantages include:

  • Reliable tracking of insulin on board

  • An integrated bolus calculator for both mealtime and correction insulin

  • Smart actionable insulin reminders

  • And insightful data reports for both CGM and insulin data\

In this module, you will learn to:

  • Set up and configure the Smart MDI system

  • Use it effectively in daily practice

  • Interpret the dedicated InPen reports

  • And learn from a real-world case example\

This course was developed by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, endocrinologist at the general Saint-Lucas hospital in Ghent and founder of Diabetotech. It has been peer-reviewed by Dr. Liesbeth Van Huffel, an endocrinologist in the OLV Hospital in Aalst, and Christiane Segers, a person living with type 1 diabetes who actively uses this Smart MDI system.

It is important to note that the information presented is independent of device manufacturers and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult your diabetes care team before making treatment changes.

Over the next 30 minutes, you’ll gain the knowledge and skills needed to use or support people using the Smart MDI system confidently and effectively. Let’s begin.

docs.title: 2. Set Up the Smart MDI System (2.5 min)
2. Set Up the Smart MDI System (2.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson on how to set up the Smart MDI System.

docs.title: 2.1 Components of the Smart MDI System
docs.text:
In this section, we’ll review the core components that make up the Smart MDI system — a connected solution that links insulin dosing with real-time glucose monitoring.

The system consists of:

  • The Simplera CGM: A wearable continuous glucose monitor that transmits glucose data every 5 minutes to the Simplera app on the user’s smartphone.The InPen: A reusable, Bluetooth-enabled insulin pen designed for rapid-acting insulin. It connects with the InPen app, which features

  • An integrated bolus calculator and smart insulin dose reminders.

  • A standard long-acting insulin pen: Since the InPen is for rapid-acting insulin only, basal insulin must be injected using a separate pen. Users are encouraged to manually log their long-acting insulin doses in the InPen app to maintain a complete data record.

  • Data sharing tools:

  • CareLink allows healthcare teams to access insulin and CGM data remotely.

  • And the CareLink Connect app enables real-time glucose sharing with up to five care partners, such as family members or caregivers.\

The InPen is thoughtfully designed for usability and safety:

  • It includes a dose knob for selecting insulin in half-unit increments, offering precision dosing for both adults and children.

  • It accommodates a 3 mL insulin cartridge. Two versions are available:

  • The blue InPen is compatible with aspart insulin Novo Nordisk cartridges

  • The grey InPen is compatible with Lilly cartridges

  • The pen has a built-in Bluetooth transmitter, which logs each dose automatically to the app.

  • It contains a non-rechargeable battery that lasts approximately one year. No charging is required.

Together, these components bring intelligence and automation to diabetes therapy with multiple daily injections — making it easier to track insulin doses, avoid insulin stacking, and make informed insulin decisions based on real-time glucose trends.

docs.title: 2.2 Set Up the Simplera Sensor & App
docs.text:
This section explains how to set up the Simplera CGM and start using the Simplera app to view and track glucose data.

The Simplera app is available to download on specific Android and iOS devices. You can check the compatibility online at medtronic-diabetes.com.

When you open the app, one of the first things you'll need to do is create or log into your CareLink account. Once you've signed in, you'll need to accept a user agreement. Then the app will take you through some phone settings. It will ask if you want to share your data and teach you how to customize alerts. Then it is time to insert and pair your sensor. The app will take you through the onboarding education.

The Simplera sensor is approved for placement on the back of the upper arm. Children from 2 to 17 years may place the sensor on the upper thirds of the buttocks.

The sensor has a built-in-Bluetooth transmitter, so make sure Bluetooth is enabled on your smart device. Each time you insert a new sensor, it’s important to remove the old sensor from your Bluetooth list.

Begin by washing your hands and disinfecting the insertion site. Allow your skin to air dry completely before proceeding. Remove the cap and place the inserter on the back of your upper arm. You can reach this area by either lifting your arm or reaching around it. Make sure all edges of the inserter touch your skin. To insert the sensor, press the inserter against your skin in a fluent movement until you hear a click. Gently pull the inserter away from your body in a straight line. Smooth down the sensor adhesive with your finger to ensure the sensor stays on the body for the entire length of wear. You may use over the counter tape if needed to help the sensor stick better.

Dispose of the inserter in accordance with local laws and regulations, but keep the serial number and sensor code until expiration time as you may need them in case of sensor failure. In that case you can call Medtronic’s customer service for a replacement.

When you've inserted the inserter you can tap "I inserted it" on the Simplera app. Confirm the sensor serial number and enter the sensor code, and the app will connect to the sensor.

After the 2-hour warmup time, you will start getting glucose readings every 5 minutes. Note that the sensor readings within the first 12 hours are not always reliable (as in most glucose sensors from any other brand). So check with finger pricks during the warmup time and also within the first 12 hours if you don’t feel confident about the glucose shown on the screen. When necessary you can calibrate the sensor.

The homescreen shows the current glucose value and trend value, and also a sensor graph.

Below there are 4 buttons: settings, sensor, logbook and events.

  • Via the settings you can customize alert and sync data with CareLink and caregivers

  • Via sensor, you can see the lifespan of your sensor.

  • When you tap the sensor and then options, you can replace your sensor or enter an optional calibration.

  • Via events you can log different events like blood glucose for calibration, insulin, carbs and exercise. These icons can be found on the sensor graph and also sync to CareLink.

To customize alarms on your Simplera app, navigate to the settings menu.

  • Use the toggle bar in the settings menu to change the alert volume. Here, you can also opt to mute all alerts for a duration of maximum 4 hours.

  • Via Glucose Alerts, you can set your Low alerts, High alerts, fall & rise alerts and snooze time.

  • For the Low Alerts, you can choose to set them at maximum volume at night or create a Predict Low alert.

  • The Simplera app also has status alerts that provide information about actions needed to ensure the correct functioning of the system. Make sure to act on these alerts to continue to receive sensor glucose information in the future.

  • It is important to note that when power saving settings are enabled, the mobile device may delay the alerts and notifications from the Simplera app.

A separate Simplera CGM module is available for users or educators seeking more advanced configuration tips, troubleshooting support, or use-case guidance.

docs.title: 2.3 Prepare the InPen Device
docs.text:
This section explains how to prepare the InPen smart insulin pen for use, insert the insulin cartridge, attach a needle, and administer a dose safely and effectively.

Step 1: Insert the Insulin Cartridge

Before starting, wash hands thoroughly with soap and water.

  • Remove the cartridge holder by twisting it to the right.

  • Turn the internal screw counterclockwise until it stops to make space for the cartridge.

  • Insert the 3 mL insulin cartridge (small end first) into the cartridge holder.

  • Reattach the cartridge holder by twisting it to the left until it clicks.\

Step 2: Attach the Pen Needle

  • Use a new, sterile pen needle. Remove the peel tab and push the needle straight onto the pen — not at an angle.

  • Screw the needle on securely.

  • Remove the outer needle cover and save it for later disposal.

  • Remove the inner needle shield and discard it safely.

Step 3: Prime the Pen

Priming ensures proper insulin flow and removes air from the system.

  • Dial a few units.

  • Hold the pen upright.

  • Press the injection button fully to release insulin.

  • A small drop should appear at the needle tip. If not, repeat the priming step until it does.

Step 4: Select and Prepare the Injection Site

Choose an appropriate subcutaneous injection site, such as the abdomen, thighs, buttocks or back of the upper arms.

  • The skin should be clean and dry, and free of scars, bruises, tattoos, or lipodystrophy

  • Injection site rotation is essential. Divide each area into zones and rotate weekly. Space each injection at least one finger-width apart from the previous one.

  • If hands are washed and skin is visibly clean, disinfection with alcohol is usually unnecessary. However, alcohol swabs are recommended if the area is visibly dirty, sweaty, or if injections are administered in a hospital or care facility. Allow alcohol to dry completely before injecting.

Step 5: Administer the Insulin Dose

  • Dial the prescribed dose.

  • Insert the pen at a 90° angle into the skin. Avoid injecting through clothing.

  • Press the injection button all the way down and count slowly to 10 before removing the pen.\

Step 6: Remove and Dispose of the Needle

  • Place the outer needle cap back on.

  • Unscrew the needle and dispose of it in a sharps container.\

Best Practices and Clinical Tips

  • Check insulin type and expiration date before use.

  • Allow cold insulin to warm to room temperature for 30 to 60 minutes prior to injection to reduce pain and risk of lipodystrophy.

  • Never store the InPen in the refrigerator.

  • Use 4 mm needles for most patients to reduce risk of intramuscular injection. If using 6 mm needles, apply the skin-lift technique (gently pinch a 5 cm or 2 inch skin fold).

  • Prefer thin, sharp needles (32 gauge or higher) for comfort.

  • Avoid needle reuse. While occasional reuse is common and may not increase pain in the short term, repeated use increases the risk of infection, injection site trauma, lipodystrophy and injection pain due to dull or bent needles. If reuse is unavoidable, limit it to no more than 4–5 times and monitor for skin changes.

  • Avoid pressing the needle too firmly into the skin; the base should rest lightly without causing an indentation.

Recognizing Unsuccessful Injections

Watch for signs such as:

  • Insulin leakage at the site or from the needle tip

  • Intradermal injection, indicated by a small raised bump under the skin

If these occur, the insulin dose may not have been fully delivered. Log the event in the app, and if this happens repeatedly, advise follow-up with a healthcare provider.

With these preparation steps and best practices in place, users can administer rapid-acting insulin using the InPen safely and confidently.

The next section covers how to connect the InPen to the app and configure therapy settings, including the bolus calculator.

docs.title: 2.4 Set Up the InPen App
docs.text:
This section explains how to connect the InPen smart insulin pen to the mobile app, customize therapy settings, and enable integration with CGM data from the Simplera or Guardian app.

Step 1: Download and Connect the InPen App

Begin by downloading the InPen app from the App Store or Google Play Store. After installation, open the app and log in with an existing account or create a new one. To pair the InPen with the app, the system will prompt the user to deliver a recommended pairing dose, which can be different for every new InPen. This can be done without insulin in the cartridge. The app uses this step to recognize and connect to the specific InPen device via Bluetooth. After successful pairing, the app walks users through a brief onboarding tutorial.

Step 2: Enter Insulin Therapy Settings

From the home screen, tap the Settings icon and navigate to Therapy Settings. Here, several key therapy parameters can be configured:

  • Short-acting insulin dosing can be set up using one of three strategies:

  • Carbohydrate counting

  • Meal estimation

  • Or fixed insulin doses per meal\

Based on the strategy selected, users then enter:

  • Insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio (for carb counting) or units per meal (for meal estimation or fixed doses)

  • Insulin sensitivity

  • Glucose target range

  • Duration of insulin action

  • And maximum bolus dose (for safety)

  • There is also an option to enable or disable the Insulin on Board display\

For long-acting insulin, the app allows users to enter:

  • Insulin type

  • Total daily dose

  • Frequency of administration (once or twice daily)

  • And the typical time(s) of day the basal insulin is taken\

Additionally, users can define standard meal times (e.g., breakfast, lunch, dinner) so that bolus doses are linked accurately to meals for reporting purposes.

Step 3: Integrate with the Simplera App and CareLink

To enable glucose data integration, go to Settings > Connections and link the InPen app to the user’s CareLink Personal account. This connection allows for automatic syncing of CGM data from the Simplera app.

To ensure reports are comprehensive, users should also authorize InPen Reports to be shared with CareLink.

Step 4: Navigating the InPen App Interface

Once the pen and therapy settings are configured, the home screen will display:

  • A shortcut to the bolus calculator

  • The most recent CGM glucose value and trend arrow

  • The estimated active insulin on board

  • A daily insulin graph, showing glucose data over time and logged insulin doses. The graph can be adjusted to show 3, 6, 12, or 24-hour views by tapping the screen.

Throughout the day, the app may display alert icons on the home screen. These notifications inform the user about:

  • Missed mealtime or long-acting insulin doses

  • Low battery or end-of-life status for the InPen

  • Temperature warnings if the insulin has been exposed to excessive heat or cold

  • Cartridge usage beyond the recommended 28-day period\

At the bottom of the app are four main tabs:

  1. Home

  2. Logbook – a detailed record of insulin doses, glucose readings, and events

  3. Reports – visual summaries of sensor glucose, insulin use and missed boluses

  4. And Settings – access to therapy setup, alerts, and data connections\

With the InPen app connected and configured, the system is ready to support accurate insulin dosing, bolus calculations, and seamless glucose integration.

docs.title: 2.5 Share your data
docs.text:
Sharing glucose and insulin data can support collaborative diabetes care and enable timely intervention. The Smart MDI system allows users to share data with healthcare professionals and care partners through the CareLink platform.

Sharing with Healthcare Providers

To enable clinical review of CGM and insulin data, users must connect their CareLink Personal account to their provider’s CareLink Clinic account. There are several ways to establish this connection:

  • Option 1: Link Code Provided by Clinic => The clinic creates a patient profile using name and date of birth, then generates a unique link code. The user enters this code into their CareLink Personal account to complete the connection.

  • Option 2: Username-Based Follow Request => The provider sends a follow request using the patient’s CareLink username. The user.can approve this request directly in the Simplera app. To do so, they navigate to settings, CareLink, Manage Care Partners, where they can view and approve follow requests.

  • Option 3: Direct Login (In-Clinic Setup) => If the user is present at the clinic, the provider can link accounts directly by entering the user’s CareLink login credentials — with consent.

Once connected, insulin and CGM data from both the InPen app and Simplera app are available to the provider via CareLink Clinic, supporting remote monitoring and therapy adjustments.

Sharing with Family Members or Caregivers

In addition to clinical sharing, the Simplera app supports real-time glucose sharing with up to five care partners, such as parents, partners, or trusted friends.

To set this up:

  1. The care partner downloads the CareLink Connect app on their phone.

  2. They create a Care Partner account and send a follow request using the user’s CareLink username.

  3. The user accepts the request in either the Simplera app (under Manage Care Partners) or through their CareLink Personal account.\

Once approved, care partners can view real-time glucose values and trend arrows, historical data and receive alerts. This feature can enhance safety and peace of mind — particularly for children, older adults, or people with impaired hypoglycemia awareness.

With data sharing in place, users benefit from greater connectivity across their care network, helping to align support and clinical decisions with real-time insights.

The next section explores how people with diabetes can use the InPen and Simplera apps in their daily routines.

docs.title: 3. Use the Smart MDI System (7.5 min)
3. Use the Smart MDI System (7.5 min)
docs.text: In this module, we will walk you through the process of using the Smart MDI System.

docs.title: 3.1 Using the InPen App
docs.text:
The InPen app serves as the central hub for insulin management in the Smart MDI system. By integrating insulin dosing with CGM data from the Simplera app, it enables more informed decision-making, timely boluses, and reduced risk of insulin stacking.

Using the Bolus Calculator

Before a meal, users can open the bolus calculator to determine an appropriate dose of rapid-acting insulin.

  • Begin by entering the number of carbohydrates in the upcoming meal. If the user is using meal estimation or fixed-dose strategies, they can select a meal type instead.

  • The most recent CGM glucose value and trend arrow are automatically displayed.

  • Based on the user’s therapy settings the app calculates a recommended dose. The dose is rounded to the nearest 0.5 units.

To review how the dose was calculated, tap the arrow on the recommendation banner. This reveals the formula, including carb coverage and any correction.

If the glucose value is below target, the app will not recommend insulin. Instead, it will advise the user to treat the low with fast-acting carbohydrates.

Delivering and Adjusting Doses

The InPen device automatically transmits each dose to the app via Bluetooth.

  • To adjust a dose before injection, turn the dose knob forward or backward.

  • If the user decides not to inject, return the dial to zero — no insulin is delivered until the injection button is pressed.

  • Start with a prime dose (to clear air out of the needle) when placing a new needle, thereafter you inject the recommended dose.

  • After injection, the app logs the dose and classifies it as either a priming dose or a therapeutic dose. If needed, the user can edit the dose entry in the Logbook by selecting the dose and adjusting the dose type.

  • If you also use any rapid-acting insulin from a device other than the InPen system, it must be logged manually in the app. The dose calculator won't account for unlogged doses and may suggest too much insulin. So for safety, it is important to log all rapid-acting insulin taken.

Logging Long-Acting (Basal) Insulin

Since long-acting insulin is delivered with a separate pen, you need to ensure that the name of the Long-Acting Insuline, the Usual Amount and the Administration time is registered in the Therapy Settings. An administration alert will be shown in the Home screen. In Notifications you turn the Long-acting Reminder ON. By default you can still register it manually in the app:

  • Navigate to the Logbook, tap Register Dose, select Long-acting insulin, and enter the dose and administration time.

This ensures that reports and insulin-on-board estimates remain complete and clinically meaningful.

Actionable Alerts

The InPen app includes two intelligent alerts to support timely insulin delivery and prevent prolonged hyperglycemia:

  1. The Missed Dose Alert is triggered when glucose is rising quickly and no recent mealtime insulin has been logged. The alert uses a rate of change detection algorithm to detect meals. It prompts the user to open the calculator, enter the meal, and administer a bolus. Responses are tracked in the InPen therapy reports.

  2. The Correct High Glucose Alert is activated when glucose levels are elevated and the insulin on board is not enough to bring them down. It is triggered when a correction bolus exceeding the predefined threshold is needed to reach the glucose target. This alert prompts the user to open the bolus calculator and deliver an appropriate correction dose.

These alerts can be configured under Settings > Notifications, where users can:

  • Enable or disable alerts, and adapt day and night Notification Volumes

  • Adjust high glucose thresholds

  • And limit alerts to daytime hours or allow them at night

Using these actionable alerts — instead of traditional high alerts from the Simplera app — can reduce alert fatigue and improve adherence. Clinical data show that people who respond to these actionable notifications achieve better glycemic outcomes than those who do not.

InPen Reports

The InPen app generates detailed, exportable reports that summarize:

  • Glucose sensor and insulin delivery data

  • Missed doses and use of the bolus calculator

  • And basal and bolus insulin trends

These reports are explored in more detail in Section 4.

docs.title: 3.2 Using the Simplera App
docs.text:
While the InPen app focuses on insulin dosing, the Simplera app complements it by providing continuous glucose data and real-time alerts. Together, these two apps create a complete view of glucose-insulin dynamics — essential for safe and effective diabetes management.

Key Functions of the Simplera App

Users rely on the Simplera app to:

  • Start and pair new CGM sensors: The app guides users through sensor setup, pairing, and warm-up.
