  • View real-time glucose readings: Glucose values are updated every five minutes and displayed as both numeric values and trend graphs.

  • Set and respond to alerts: Users are notified of urgent lows, customizable high/low thresholds, and rapid changes in glucose levels.

  • Log events manually: Contextual data such as exercise, illness and stress can be entered into the app.

  • Accept or manage care partner connections: Through the app’s Settings > Manage Care Partners, users can approve or remove follower requests and adjust permissions.

  • Maintain a connection to CareLink: All Simplera data sync automatically to CareLink Personal, supporting remote monitoring and therapy review.

docs.title: 3.3 Apple Watch Integration (for iOS Users)
docs.text:
For users with an iPhone and Apple Watch, the Simplera app offers Apple Watch compatibility, enabling quick access to therapy information and alerts throughout the day.

When paired with the iPhone, the Simplera Apple Watch app provides:

  • A real-time glucose trend curve on the watch face

  • Current glucose value and trend arrow at a glance

  • And alerts for high/low glucose, rapid changes, and sensor issues

This is especially useful when the phone isn’t easily accessible — such as during exercise, meetings, or sleep.

This integration extends the Smart MDI experience to the wrist — offering discreet, real-time access to key diabetes data.

In the Smart MDI system, each app serves a distinct but complementary role:

  • The InPen app manages insulin therapy — offering bolus calculation, dose tracking, reminders, and reports.

  • The Simplera app manages glucose monitoring — displaying real-time sensor data, alerts, and event logging.

When used together, these two apps provide a comprehensive, real-time view of insulin–glucose interaction. This integration supports more informed self-management and more precise clinical oversight.

The next section will explore how to view and interpret this combined data using the system’s reporting tools.

docs.title: 4. Report Interpretation (7 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this lesson wherein we will discuss the report interpretation.

docs.title: 4.1 Where to Find Reports
docs.text:
One of the key advantages of the Smart MDI system is its ability to generate comprehensive, data-rich reports. These reports help both people with diabetes and their care teams identify patterns in insulin use, glucose trends, and therapy effectiveness — supporting informed clinical decisions.

Reports are accessible via the InPen app or via CareLink Personal or CareLink Clinic.

1. To access reports in the InPen app:

  • Tap the Reports tab at the bottom of the home screen

  • Select a date range (e.g., 1 week, 2 weeks, 1 month, or 3 months)

  • Export the report as a PDF if needed — for example, to send to a healthcare provider before a consultation

2. To access reports via CareLink Personal or Clinic:

  • Select the Reports section

  • Navigate to the InPen tab

  • Choose a date range

  • Click Generate Report

  • Reports can also be printed or downloaded for review with the healthcare team.

The InPen Insights Report typically consists of three pages: 1 page with a summary overview and 2 pages with daily charts

At the top of the summary page, four circular charts provide a quick snapshot of key therapy metrics:

  1. Time in Range and average glucose

  2. Missed bolus doses per day

  3. Bolus calculator usage

  4. And total daily insulin, broken down into basal and bolus insulin. Priming doses are not used for this calculation.

Other metrics displayed in this section are the Glucose Management Indicator, the glucose variability, and the average amount of rapid-acting boluses per day.\

The report then displays the Ambulatory Glucose Profile alongside the user’s current insulin therapy settings, including the glucose target, carb ratio, insulin sensitivity factor, duration of insulin action and maximum bolus setting.

The Long-Acting Insulin Assessment evaluates basal insulin coverage based on the identified fasting window — the time period with minimal or no rapid-acting insulin doses.

It includes:

  • Median bedtime and fasting glucose values

  • Days included in the assessment - as days with overnight boluses are excluded

  • The incidence of nocturnal hypoglycemia

  • And median overnight glucose trend

The Meal Assessment is divided by standard meal times: breakfast, lunch, and dinner.

Each graph displays:

  • Glucose patterns following bolus administration

  • It only includes boluses recorded during defined meal time windows

  • And excludes correction doses or boluses administered outside mealtimes

Each daily chart provides a visual timeline of insulin delivery and glucose trends, including:

  • CGM glucose values plotted over 24 hours

  • Rapid-acting insulin doses, shown as shaded blue curves

  • Long-acting insulin, shown as blue circles

  • Carb entries or meal annotations

  • And missed doses, indicated by gold-colored bands

Bolus entries include icons to indicate user behavior:

  • A tick icon for doses that match the calculator recommendation

  • An arrow up or down for doses that exceed or are below the recommendation

  • Or no icon if the calculator was not used

(Additional Reporting via CareLink)

In addition to the InPen Insights Report, CareLink also allows users or providers to download a General Summary Report, which includes:

  • Simplera CGM alert settings

  • Logged events (like exercise or illness)

  • Sensor calibration data and device status

This combined dataset supports a comprehensive review of glucose control and therapy adherence across both apps.

docs.title: 4.2 How to Interpret Reports
docs.text:
This section outlines a step-by-step framework for interpreting data from the InPen Insights Report and associated CareLink reports. The goal is to identify patterns, assess insulin effectiveness, and guide data-driven therapy adjustments.

Step 1: Assess Glucose Control

Begin with the Summary page:

  • Review Time in Range: The recommended target for most people with diabetes is more than 70% time in range and less than 4% time below range, with less than 1% below 54 mg/dl (or 3 mmol/l).

  • Evaluate bolus frequency. The number of meal boluses per day should correspond to the individual’s usual eating pattern (typically 3 times per day).\

Step 2: Optimize Insulin Settings

Use the Ambulatory Glucose Profile to spot patterns of highs or lows:

  • For daytime trends, check if highs or lows are linked to meals in the Meal Assessment and Daily Charts.

  • For overnight trends, use the Long-Acting Assessment to see the trend of the overnight glucose and the amount of nocturnal hypoglycemia before adjusting your basal insulin.

Step 3: Evaluate User Behavior and Alert Response:

Use the behavioral indicators in the report to assess therapy engagement:

  • Missed dose count: Frequent missed boluses are associated with increased glycemic variability and reduced time in range.

  • Bolus calculator usage: Ideally, more than 70% of insulin doses should be administered using the bolus calculator. Skipping the calculator may result in inconsistent dosing or incorrect corrections.

Also evaluate how users respond to actionable alerts (like missed meal alerts or correct high alerts). Addressing alert fatigue may require simplifying alert settings or reinforcing the importance of timely responses.

Step 4: Review Safety and Back-up Planning

Ensure that:

  • Insulin types and settings in the app reflect the user’s actual prescriptions.

  • A backup plan is in place, such as:

  • A manual pen for insulin administration if the InPen fails

  • Awareness of how to manually track insulin when offline or between devices

Healthcare professionals should use these reports during consultations to:

  • Support goal setting

  • Adjust therapy safely

  • Reinforce positive behaviors

  • And provide visual feedback on how glucose responds to insulin dosing decisions

Tip: If uncertain about icons, trends, or colors, refer to the legend at the bottom of each report page for clear explanations.

By using this structured approach, diabetes educators and endocrinologists can help individuals gain deeper insight into their own data and build confidence in their daily insulin management.

docs.title: 5. Case report
docs.text:
To conclude this module, we present a real-world example of how the Smart MDI system — combining the InPen smart insulin pen and Simplera CGM — is experienced by a person living with type 1 diabetes.

The review, titled “I Tried Smart Insulin Pen: Simplera Smart MDI Review,” is available on YouTube and features a person with type 1 diabetes who transitioned from an insulin pump to the Smart MDI system.

Watch the video

The reviewer shares several positive aspects of the system such as reduced mental burden, helpful reminders, improved glycemic awareness and greater confidence. While the system was generally well received, the reviewer highlighted a few areas for improvement.

  • Managing two separate apps — one for glucose (Simplera) and one for insulin (InPen) — was occasionally cumbersome.

  • And full functionality is only achieved when both the Simplera CGM and InPen are used together.

Real-world testimonials like this offer important context for both healthcare providers and people with diabetes. Hearing directly from users helps clinicians understand not just the technical capabilities of these tools, but their psychological and behavioral impact.

Thank you for completing this accredited module on the Smart MDI system. You’ve now explored:

  • How to set up and use the InPen and Simplera CGM

  • How to support users in daily decision-making

  • How to interpret therapy data and integrate it into clinical care

  • And how this technology is perceived in real-world settings

We encourage you to continue exploring how digital health tools like the Smart MDI System can personalize care, improve outcomes, and empower people living with diabetes.

docs.title: Insulin Pumps
docs.text: Select a module from the sidebar to get started.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. How the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump works
  • 3. Insulin setup
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module about the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump! Although this pump is no longer being commercialised, some people are still using it.

In previous general insulin pump videos, you learned the fundamentals of how insulin pumps work, setting basal and bolus insulin, changing insulin and infusion sets, and handling alarms.

In the following videos, we will take you through the complete start-up education for the Accu-Chek Insight pump. It's important to note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the general St-Lucas hospital in Ghent, has created these informative videos. The content has been reviewed by Professor Dr. Laurent Crenier, an endocrinologist at Erasmus Hospital in Brussels, Belgium, and Renate Groenendijk, a user of the Diabeloop-Insight system and moderator of the dutch Diabeloop FaceBook group. These individuals have no financial interests in Roche, ensuring that the information provided is completely unbiased and independent.

By the end of these videos, you will have a clear understanding of:

  • How the Accu-Chek Insight pump functions

  • Setting basal and bolus insulin levels on the pump

  • Changing the insulin PumpCart and infusion set

  • And managing alarms and responding appropriately

Whether you are a caregiver or a user, this knowledge will empower you to better guide individuals using the Accu-Chek Insight pump. As a user, you'll gain valuable tips and tricks to enhance your experience with the pump. Remember, your diabetes journey can be more manageable with the right tools and knowledge.

We wish you the best of luck as you embark on this insightful video program. Let's dive in and explore the world of the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump together!

docs.title: 2. How the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump works (5.5 min)
2. How the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump works (5.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this informative video on how the Accu-Chek Insight pump operates. In this segment, we will explore the different parts and buttons of the pump, learn how to change the battery, unlock its functions, and navigate through its screens and menus.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts & buttons on the pump
docs.text:
Let's start with the various components and buttons on the pump:

Here, you can see the different parts of the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump: the pump itself, the insulin, the battery, the battery cover, and the adapter and tubing. When the pump is inserted, it looks like this. You can connect the tube to your body via the infusion set to administer the insulin.

On the front of the pump, you'll find three buttons for navigation: two arrows and a middle OK button. Below the screen, there is a Zoom button that allows you to adjust the size of the characters displayed. At the top of the pump, you'll find two buttons that serve the same purpose in most situations, primarily used to unlock the pump. The back of the pump displays the serial number, which you may need when contacting customer service.

docs.title: 2.2 Replacing the battery
docs.text:
To replace the battery, use one AAA battery, preferably lithium. Be prepared to change your battery every 2 weeks, although they often last longer. Insert the battery with the + side first, and the - side facing out. Make sure to push the battery cover all the way into the battery compartment until you hear a click. Rotate the adapter to seal the pump securely.

To maintain a strong battery connection and prolong battery life, it is recommended to replace the battery cover every six months. This helps prevent oxidation and ensures optimal performance.

If you want to replace the battery without replacing the adapter, you can simply rotate the adapter 90° to the left. Press the latch of the battery compartment cover towards the ampoule compartment. You can use the flip-up lever to easily remove the battery compartment cover.

When inserting a new battery and closing the cover, the pump will perform a function test, indicated by a series of color screens, vibrations, and beeps. If the pump has been without a battery for more than an hour, you'll need to set the date and time. Confirm the battery type and insulin start by pressing the OK button.

docs.title: 2.3 Unlocking the pump
docs.text: To unlock the pump, press one of the top keys within one second, followed by pressing the up and down arrows or vice versa. The pump is set to key lock mode by default, and if the keys are not pressed for a certain duration, the pump goes into lockdown. This duration defaults at 10 seconds, but can be adjusted according to user preferences, with the option to extend it up to 30 seconds.

docs.title: 2.4 Home screen and pump menu
docs.text:
Upon unlocking the pump, you will typically see the status screen, which displays the time, battery status, and date at the top. An icon at the top right indicates the pump's status, with a green triangle indicating insulin delivery, a red square indicating stopped, and two blue dashes representing pause. Additional icons may include an airplane mode icon or a lock icon for an active key lock. The middle section of the screen shows information about active basals, temporary basals, or boluses.

Pressing the OK button on the home screen will take you to the pump menu, which can be navigated using the arrow keys. The OK button is used to confirm or open a menu, while one of the top buttons allows you to go back to the previous level. Each menu also has a home icon that takes you back to the home screen.

The pump menu offers options such as bolus, basal, stop pump, start pump, pump data, modes, settings, cartridge and infusion set, and home. A white arrow indicates submenus, and a scroll bar on the right suggests more menu items. If an action requires multiple steps, the top right corner shows the remaining steps. A radio button indicates whether a function is enabled or disabled.

In the settings menu, you can adjust various parameters, such as sound alarms, key lock duration, display brightness, orientation, and background color.

docs.title: 2.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Remember these do's and don'ts:

  • You can find a comprehensive menu overview in the downloads section, which can be saved for easy reference when providing remote guidance as a healthcare provider.

  • The pump is waterproof, but only for an hour until a depth of 1.3 meter (or 4.3 feet).

  • Utilize the pump clip or other carrying materials, such as a clip case, flip case, or leather case, to secure the pump to your clothing. The holes on the left side of the pump can accommodate a lanyard if needed, although this is not commonly used. Roche provides a list of carrying systems in the downloads section, and there are also other companies offering carrying solutions.

  • The Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump does not automatically adjust the time and date. So when changing between winter and summer time, or when traveling to a different time zone, you have to adjust the time yourself. You can do that via the settings.

You now have a good understanding of how to operate the Accu-Chek Insight pump, including battery replacement. In the next video, we will delve into setting the insulin delivery. Stay tuned!

docs.title: 3. Insulin setup (4 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this instructional video on how to set insulin delivery on the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump.

Before we begin, please note that if you are using this pump in conjunction with Diabeloop, insulin delivery will be controlled through the handset. You will learn more about that in the Diabeloop chapter. However, it's useful to know how to control the pump directly from the pump itself in case you forget your handset.

In this video, we will cover the following topics:

  • Programming basal insulin

  • Setting temporary basals

  • Administering an insulin bolus

  • And important do's and don'ts

docs.title: 3.1 Programming basal insulin
docs.text:
If you are using the Accu-Chek Insight pump with Diabeloop, basal insulin is typically set on the Diabeloop handset. However, you can still check the set basal profile from the pump itself. Simply navigate to the menu, select "Basal," and activate the desired basal profile. The current profile, usually set to DBLG1, corresponds to the basal security profile on your handset. Pressing it will display the profile details.

To temporarily stop insulin delivery, go to the menu, select "Stop Pump," and confirm. The status screen will then indicate "Pump stopped," and the status icon will change to a red square.

In stop mode, the pump will beep every minute. You can silence this stop warning by pressing either of the top buttons for 3 seconds.

To restart the pump, go to the menu, select "Start Pump," and press OK. The status icon will change back to a green triangle, indicating that the pump has restarted.

docs.title: 3.2 Temporary basal
docs.text:
To set a temporary basal, access the menu, choose "Basal," and select "Temporary Basal Dose." Choose the desired basal TBD (to be determined) option and use the + and - buttons to set the percentage and duration of the temporary basal insulin. Confirm with OK. The status screen will display information about the temporary basal dose.

To cancel a temporary basal, navigate to the menu, select "Basal," choose "Temporary Basal Dose," and then "Cancel TBD." Confirm with OK, and an orange warning will appear to indicate the cancellation. Press OK again to finalize.

docs.title: 3.3 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
To give a standard insulin bolus, access the menu, select "Bolus," and choose "Standard Bolus." Use the up and down arrow buttons to select the desired units and confirm with OK three times. The status screen will show the progress of the bolus.

In the Bolus submenu, you can also choose for an extended or a multiwave bolus.

Note that the Accu-Chek Insight pump does not have a bolus calculator. If you want to calculate a bolus based on carbohydrate intake, it must be done through Diabeloop's handset, the Accu-Chek Aviva Insight Diabetes Manager or the MySugr app.

To cancel a bolus, navigate to the menu, select "Bolus," and choose "Cancel Bolus." During a bolus, pressing either of the top buttons for 3 seconds will also cancel the bolus.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• The pump lacks a built-in bolus calculator. Hence, if you wish to administer an insulin bolus based on carbohydrate intake, it should be done via the Diabeloop handset, the Accu-Chek Aviva Insight Diabetes Manager or the MySugr app.

  • Ensure maximum bolus limits are set slightly above expected bolus doses. You can set this between 0 and 50 units. 

  • The Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump also has a Quick Bolus feature. This lets you discretely deliver a bolus while your pump is hidden in your clothing. You can find more information about this in the manual.

Congratulations! You now know how to set and adjust insulin delivery on the Accu-Chek Insight pump. In the next video, we will demonstrate how to change the insulin reservoir and infusion set.

docs.title: 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (6 min)
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (6 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this instructional video on how to replace the insulin reservoir and infusion set on the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump.

In this video, we will cover the available infusion sets, the 4-step process for changing the insulin and infusion set, and last but not least, some important do's and don'ts.

docs.title: 4.1 Different infusion sets
docs.text: The Accu-Chek Insight pump offers different types of infusion sets, including the Accu-Chek Insight Flex, Accu-Chek Insight Tender, and Accu-Chek Insight Rapid. Below this video you can find an overview of the available infusion sets, their tubing and cannula lengths, and filling volumes.

docs.title: 4.2 Supplies
docs.text:
To replace your infusion set and reservoir, gather the following items in addition to your Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump:

  • an insulin PumpCart ampoule (at room temperature)

  • an Accu-Chek Insight Adapter and Tube

  • an Accu-Chek Insight Flex cannula

  • an Accu-Chek Insight Flex LinkAssist Plus insertion aid (optional)

  • and some disinfectant wipes

docs.title: 4.3 Reservoir replacement
docs.text:
The Accu-Chek Insight pump uses pre-filled insulin PumpCarts, which are easy to replace. However, note that each PumpCart contains only 160 units of insulin, requiring more frequent replacements compared to tubed pumps with larger reservoirs.

To replace the PumpCart, simply open the insulin compartment, and the pump will prompt you to confirm that you want to replace the ampoule. The pump will go into the “pauze” mode for maximum 15 minutes, so you have time to change your ampoule, infusion set and cannula.

Follow the prompts to remove the existing adapter and ampoule, and dispose them properly.

Next, insert a new PumpCart in the insulin compartment while holding the pump upright. Make sure that the PumpCart fits correctly. Place a new adapter on the bayonet socket of the insulin compartment. Make sure you insert the nose of the adaptor into the notches of the pump's housing at 90 degrees. Otherwise the needle could bend. Turn the adaptor clockwise until it is aligned with the pumps housing. Then press the select button to confirm. Confirm the ampoule type, and the pump will perform a function test.

docs.title: 4.4 Filling the tubing
docs.text:
After inserting the new insulin PumpCart, the pump will prompt you to fill the tube. Confirm the filling process, and the pump will guide you through the steps. Once insulin appears at the end of the connector, press one of the buttons at the top of the pump to stop filling. By default, the pump stops filling at 14 units of insulin. If necessary, repeat the procedure to eliminate air bubbles from the tubing.

After filling the tubing, the pump suggests connecting the tubing to the infusion set. To do this, you must first insert the infusion set.

docs.title: 4.5 Infusion set placement
docs.text:
The most commonly used infusion set is the AccuCheck Insight Flex, which includes an adhesive plaster, a connector plate, an introducer needle, a needle box with a button and a tube with disconnect cover.

If you have the Accu-Chek LinkAssist Plus insertion aid, you can use it to insert the infusion set automatically.

  • First, wash your hands thoroughly.

  • Then choose an infusion site on your abdomen and disinfect it well.

  • Insert the needle holder into the filling chamber of the insertion aid, the coloured part towards the safety catch.

  • Remove the two sections of cover film from the adhesive patch.

  • Hold the base of the Accu-Chek LinkAssist Plus firmly with one hand and with the other hand, pull the clamping element out and up as far as possible. The insertion needle with the soft cannula folds out.

  • Place the applicator on the selected infusion site. Press the applicator onto the skin until the safety catch is no longer visible.

  • Push the release button to insert the infusion set.

  • Remove the insertion aid.

  • Return the clamping element to its home position and put the insertion aid away.

  • Stick the patch on the infusion site and press it to ensure good contact with the skin.

  • Remove the needle holder with the insertion needle at a right angle, while holding the plaster back. The plaster and the connecting system remain on the skin. The Insertion Needle automatically jumps back into the needle holder.

So, you have now placed your infusion set correctly. You can now proceed on the pump to fill the cannula.

docs.title: 4.6 Filling the cannula
docs.text:
To fill the cannula, navigate to the menu, select "Ampoule and Infusion Set," and choose "Fill Cannula." Connect the tube to the infusion set located on your body. Confirm the connection, and the pump will begin filling the cannula.

Check the status icon on the pump to ensure insulin delivery is active, indicated by a green triangle.

docs.title: 4.7 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• When using the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump, you have the option to use either NovoRapid or Fiasp PumpCarts.

  • Check your glucose levels two hours after inserting a new infusion set to confirm proper insertion and insulin delivery.

  • When disconnecting the tube from the infusion set, press on the side clamps of the connector and ensure a firm click when reconnecting.

Congratulations! You have successfully replaced the insulin reservoir and infusion set on the Accu-Chek Insight pump. Remember, if you encounter any difficulties, don't hesitate to try again or seek assistance. Good luck!

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms (3.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this video on the alarms in the Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump.

We will discuss 4 types of alarms: errors, maintenance messages, warnings and reminders.

All alarms are indicated by beeps and/or vibrations.

When an alarm appears, you should always confirm it by pressing OK. Failure to confirm within 60 seconds will increase the sound and vibration signals to the maximum level until confirmation is given.

Let's go over the different types of notifications, along with some do's and don'ts.

docs.title: 5.1 Errors
docs.text:
Error messages indicate a malfunction in the pump. The pump will enter STOP mode and display a red cross.

Examples are mechanical error, electronic error, rewind error, or language error.

If you can't resolve the error, contact Roche Diabetes Service.

docs.title: 5.2 Maintenance messages
docs.text:
Maintenance messages require immediate action to ensure proper pump functioning. The pump will enter PAUSE or STOP mode and display an orange lozenge.

Examples are ampoule not inserted, empty ampoule, low battery, automatic shutdown, clogging, end of term, incomplete ampoule replacement, data download failed, pause mode ended, battery type not set, or ampoule type not set.

After a maintenance message, the pump will be in STOP or PAUSE mode.

docs.title: 5.3 Warnings
docs.text:
Warning messages indicate upcoming maintenance or attention needed. The pump will continue to operate as usual and display a yellow triangle.

Possible warnings are ampoule almost empty, low battery, invalid time/date, end of guarantee, cancelled temporary basal dosage, cancelled quick bolus or loantime warning.

docs.title: 5.4 Reminders
docs.text: You can set a reminder for a bolus administration, missed bolus, alarm clock, infusion set replacement, or completed temporary basal dosage.

docs.title: 5.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• If in doubt, refer to the user manual or call customer service.

  • The Accu-Chek Insight insulin pump uses 4 signal modes that allow you to quickly set your notifications.

  • You can choose the normal setting,

  • via vibrations,

  • quieter than usual (if you are in a meeting, for example)

  • or louder than usual (if you are in a noisy environment, for example).

  • You can set the volume of the notifications and whether the notifications should be accompanied by a vibration or not via the device settings. Through these device settings, it is also possible per signal mode to delay the signal during a certain time block. You can do this once or all days in a certain time block.

  • If you will not be using your pump for an extended period, you can put the pump in storage mode. You can do this by putting the pump in STOP mode and then remove the battery to extend its usage time, remove the ampoule and replace the battery compartment cover.

Knowing the alarms and what to do next will help you navigate pump notifications confidently. If you have any doubts, refer to the user manual or contact customer service. Good luck!

docs.title: 6. Case report (1.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome back! We've reached the final chapter of our comprehensive Accu-Chek Insight Pump module. Throughout this journey, we've covered various aspects of using the pump, from understanding its features to setting insulin delivery, replacing the reservoir and infusion set, and dealing with alarms. Now, it's time to celebrate your progress and achievements!

Before we conclude, we wanted to take a moment to showcase a real-life example of someone using the Accu-Chek Insight pump.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rR4iKEIU0vE – 8 min

In this video, an expert in diabetes education from University College London Hospitals will guide you through the process of replacing an Accu-Chek Insight Flex infusion set. Even though we've addressed this procedure in an earlier video, it's worth noting that repetition plays a crucial role in learning. By observing the steps from a different perspective, you'll enhance your understanding. To access the video and acquire practical knowledge about utilizing the Accu-Chek Insight pump, simply click the link provided below the video.

By completing this course, you have acquired the knowledge and skills necessary to navigate the Accu-Chek Insight pump confidently. Remember, always consult with your healthcare provider for personalized advice and guidance.

Congratulations once again on successfully completing the Accu-Chek Insight Pump course! We commend your dedication and commitment to enhancing your understanding of this remarkable diabetes management tool. Wishing you all the best in your diabetes management journey. Keep up the great work!

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Pump operation
  • 3. Programming the pump
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the DANA-i Pump! In this series of videos, we’ll explore the workings of one of the smallest and lightest insulin pumps in the world.

Released in 2020, the DANA-i insulin pump is the successor to the DANA RS — the world’s first insulin pump to make use of a patient’s own smartphone as a fully-fledged remote control. Measuring 97 × 47 × 22 mm and weighing 86 g (including the battery), the DANA-i is about half the weight of a standard insulin pump. This compact device has a 300-unit reservoir and is indicated for subcutaneous delivery of short-acting 100 units per milliliter insulin in individuals with diabetes aged 1 year and up. The DANA-i utilizes secure Bluetooth Low Energy connectivity for full, discreet control through the AnyDANA app. As an Alternate Controller Enabled (ACE) infusion pump, it can be used on its own or with digitally connected medical devices for closed-loop therapy. It is compatible with Diabeloop and CamAPS FX, depending on your region, and several open-source AID systems.

In our Master Module on insulin pumps, we covered general information about these devices, including how to set basal and bolus rates, change reservoir and infusion sets, and configure system alarms.

This module will provide comprehensive start-up training for the DANA-i Pump, plus advanced tips and tricks for healthcare providers and diabetes patients alike. However, please note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Make it a point to consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

These videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr. Liesbeth Van Huffel, an endocrinologist in the AZORG Hospital in Aalst in Belgium. Their expertise and perspectives ensure that you receive honest opinions about the DANA-i Pump.

Be assured, all the information in this module is completely independent from the manufacturer of the DANA-i insulin pump, ensuring a genuine perspective on the pros and cons of the device.

By the end of these videos, you’ll be able to:

  • identify the parts of the DANA-i insulin pump and operate the device;

  • program the pump’s insulin settings;

  • replace the DANA-i reservoir and infusion sets; and

  • respond to DANA-i system alarms.

As a healthcare provider, you will acquire the knowledge to confidently support DANA-i users; as a user, you will gain key insights for optimizing your experience with the device. We wish you all the best as you get started with the DANA-i pump!

docs.title: 2. Pump operation (8.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on operating the DANA-i pump.

In this video, we’ll identify its parts, install the battery, and get familiar with the pump’s display screen and the AnyDANA mobile app. We'll also highlight essential do's and don'ts for using the DANA-i system. Let’s get started!

The DANA-i insulin pump kit includes:

  • 1 insulin pump

  • 1 Auto Setter

  • 2 syringe caps

  • 2 battery caps

  • 2 linking screws

  • 2 size ½ AA batteries, and

  • 1 user manual.

Please note that this reflects earlier kits. The updated Auto Setter now uses one AAA battery, and the ½ AA batteries are no longer included in current versions.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts of the pump
docs.text:
The insulin pump consists of the reservoir cap, the battery cap, the reservoir window, the control panel, and the LCD screen.

The reservoir cap is where you insert the insulin reservoir and linking screw. To open it, turn a quarter-turn clockwise.

The battery cap houses the AAA battery and opens the same way — a quarter-turn clockwise.

The reservoir window lets you check the remaining insulin.

The control panel has four buttons for navigating menus, adjusting settings, and selecting functions. Press the Plus button (+) to increase values, the Minus button (-) to decrease values or return to the previous screen, the Right (▷) button to move to the next option, and OK to select or confirm an option.

The LCD screen displays the status of the pump, as well as system options and messages. It serves as the main user interface and lights automatically when you press any control-panel button.

docs.title: 2.2 Installing the battery
docs.text: The DANA-i pump requires one AAA 1.5 V alkaline battery. To install it, open the battery cap by turning it clockwise 45 degrees. You can use a key or a coin for this purpose. Place the edge of the coin in the cap. Insert the battery with the positive (+) facing up and the negative (-) end into the pump. Replace the cap and lock it by turning 45 degrees counterclockwise. For reference, see the illustrations showing the cap in the locked and open positions.

docs.title: 2.3 The LCD home screen and the main menu
docs.text:
At the top of the LCD home screen, you'll find the current date and time. On the middle-left, a flashing B indicates basal delivery, while T indicates a temporary basal.

The current basal rate is shown with "u/h", which stands for "units per hour." Any value above or below 100% indicates a temporary basal is active; 100% reflects the normal basal delivery state.

The Battery Status appears in the lower-left.

The Button Lock icon appears at the bottom center when the LCD screen is locked. This function is especially useful for children unable to program their own pump or patients who are sleeping. Meanwhile, the remaining insulin volume in the lower right side shows the volume of insulin left in the reservoir.

Status messages that may include Extended Bolus, Dual Bolus, and No Delivery. The No Delivery status is displayed when the pump is not delivering insulin.

When a compatible mobile device is connected to the DANA-i pump, the Remote Control Mode symbol appears and the pump’s buttons are disabled. If you do not intend to connect, you can exit this mode by pressing the minus (-) button for more than 5 seconds.

In Airplane Mode, Bluetooth is disabled.

Additional symbols include Bolus Block, which prevents a repeat bolus during a preset block period; and Allowable Daily Max, which appears when the total daily dose is nearing the configured maximum. Remaining units when the total drops below 20 are displayed.

From the home screen, press OK to open the Main Menu. The Main Menu scrolls in a carousel through Review, Bolus, Suspend, Button Lock, Temporary Basal, Basal, Refill, and Options. After Options, it returns to Review. Use OK to select an item, the Right button to move to the next item, and the Minus button to go back to the previous one.

Here’s an overview of the DANA-i pump main menu and sub-menus. A full overview can be found in the download section.

docs.title: 2.4 The AnyDANA app
docs.text:
AnyDANA is the smartphone app that pairs with the DANA-i pump. AnyDANA-i is for iOS devices (with iOS 9 or later), while AnyDANA-A is for Android devices (with Android 4.3 or later). Download either from the App Store or Google Play.

Via Bluetooth, AnyDANA lets you configure and operate the pump on your phone’s larger display for added convenience. After installing and opening the app for the first time, allow all requested permissions and ensure Bluetooth is turned on. You may see an update notice before being prompted to create a password. Set one that has 9 to 12 characters and includes lowercase and uppercase letters, numbers, and at least one special character. For quicker access, enable biometric authentication.

The app will then let you search for your pump. Tap Search [Device] and wait for your serial number to appear; this may take a couple of minutes on the first connection. When it appears, tap it to begin pairing. The app will request a Pairing Key from the pump. On the pump screen, enter Pairing Key 1 first, then Pairing Key 2. (You’ll repeat this process whenever pairing with a new phone.) After a message confirms the connection is certified, tap Done to enter the AnyDANA app.

The home screen shows key details such as the connected pump’s serial number, pump battery status, remaining insulin, the previous bolus, today’s total insulin delivered, and any active insulin. Tapping the button in the top-right corner opens the main menu, where you’ll find quick access to both pump-specific and app-specific settings.

By tapping Review, you can also view your pump data history, which you can share anytime using the button in the top-right corner.

docs.title: 2.5 Do’s & don’ts
docs.text:
Keep the following important tips in mind when using the DANA-i pump:

  • The pump is indicated for short-acting insulin of 100 units per milliliter only. Other insulins have not been tested and may be incompatible with the DANA-i pump.

  • The warranty period is four years from the date of purchase and applies only to the original purchaser of the pump.

  • The pump has an IP68 rating, meaning it is dust-tight and can be immersed in water up to 1.5 meters for 30 minutes. However, drops or impacts may allow moisture to enter the display. The pump may continue to function, but the screen can fail. Swimming with the pump is therefore not recommended. Showering generally does not cause problems.

  • Avoid exposing the pump to strong electromagnetic fields or radiation sources, such as those generated by MRI, CT scans, or X-rays. The pump can emit radio-frequency energy, which may interfere with nearby medical devices.

  • Use one AAA 1.5 V alkaline battery. The manufacturer recommends Duracell Gold or Energizer Advanced alkaline AAA batteries. Do not use carbon-zinc batteries, as they are incompatible with the pump. Lithium batteries are not recommended either because the battery-level indicator may be inaccurate.

  • The battery life is approximately 3 to 4 weeks.

  • Do not attempt to change the battery while a bolus is in progress.

  • Remove the battery for long-term storage.

  • The battery cap has an O-ring that seals the battery compartment. If the O-ring is damaged or missing, replace the battery cap.

  • To conserve power, the screen blanks after one minute of inactivity. Press any button to wake the display and activate the backlight for approximately 10 seconds.

  • Press the pump buttons with your finger pad. DO NOT use fingernails or any sharp objects.

  • Clean the exterior of the insulin pump and accessories monthly. Wipe with a soft cloth moistened with water or a neutral-pH detergent, then dry with a clean cloth. Do not use thinner, alcohol, benzene, or similar solvents.

Well done! You now have a strong understanding of operating the DANA-i pump, from identifying its parts, to installing the battery, to navigating the LCD display and the AnyDANA app, to keeping the pump in optimum condition. Coming up, we'll dive into programming the pump's insulin delivery settings. See you in the next video!

docs.title: 3. Programming the pump (14.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on programming the DANA-i pump.

In this video, we’ll guide you through setting basal and temporary basal rates, delivering a bolus, suspending delivery, and programming settings through the anyDANA app. Plus, we’ll cover some best practices for adjusting the pump’s insulin settings. Let’s begin.

docs.title: 3.1 Setting a basal rate
docs.text:
The DANA-i pump lets you store adjusted basal rates in four profiles, A, B, C, and D. These profiles are useful for exercise, sick days, or other events that may affect your insulin sensitivity.

To edit basal rates, go to the MAIN MENU, select BASAL, choose EDIT BASAL, and press OK. The current Basal Profile is displayed; the default profile is A. Confirm with OK. You’ll be taken to the Edit A screen.

Let’s first identify the parts of the screen. The hourly basal-rate bar graph contains 24 bars, each representing one hour from 00 to 24 (12:00 a.m.). When you select a specific time period, that bar turns black.

The active basal profile letter appears in the title line. In the example, Profile A is selected.

Total Daily Basal, or TDB, shows the total basal insulin to be delivered over 24 hours. In the example, TDB is 5.2 units.

Start and end times are shown for each hourly segment. The first number is the start of the hour, while the second one is the end.

Last is the basal rate. In the example, the basal rate between 18 and 19 (6:00–7:00 p.m.) is 0.3 u/h.

Going back to setting basal rates, the Edit Basal screen opens with the default time 00-01. Use the plus and minus buttons to adjust the Start Time, then press Right to move to the End Time. Use Plus and Minus to adjust the End Time, then press Right again to move to the Basal Rate. Use Plus and Minus to set the Basal rate for the selected hour. Press OK to save the rate. A SETTING SAVED screen appears. Press OK to finish or Right to return to the Start Time and set the next hourly rate. When you save changes, a confirmation message indicates that the basal rate has been updated; press OK to confirm.

To view basal rates, go to Basal, select View Basal Rate, and press OK. The current Basal Profile letter is shown; confirm with OK. The cursor is positioned at the current time. Press Right to move to the next time value, or press Minus to exit.

docs.title: 3.2 Setting a temporary basal rate
docs.text:
The Temporary Basal Rate feature helps manage blood glucose during unexpected, short-term situations, such as exercise, illness, or stress. It lets you make temporary changes that automatically revert to your usual basal rates when the set period ends.

To set a Temporary Basal Rate, go to the Main Menu, select TEMPORARY BASAL, and press OK. Press Right to toggle between HR, which is the duration in hours, and % (the percentage of your usual basal). Use the Plus or Minus buttons to adjust the selected rate. For example, choosing 150% for 1 hour raises the basal to one-and-a-half times the regular rate for the next hour. Press OK to confirm.

Remember that a flashing T on the home screen indicates an active Temporary Basal Rate. To review details, open the Main Menu and select TEMPORARY BASAL. The TEMP. BASAL screen shows how long the Temporary Rate has been running, the time remaining, and the percentage selected. In the example, the Temporary Basal Rate is 150% for a total time of 1 hour, and there are 25 minutes remaining for this rate.

When a Temporary Basal Rate ends, the pump does not provide a notification. It automatically switches back to the programmed standard basal profile.

To stop a Temporary Basal Rate, select TEMPORARY BASAL from the main menu. When the TEMP. BASAL screen appears, press OK. Confirm STOP TEMP. RATE by pressing OK.

docs.title: 3.3 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
The DANA-i pump can deliver a bolus as a Quick Bolus, or with the integrated bolus calculator. The main parameters of the bolus calculator are set in BOLUS SETTING, where you can also enable dual and extended boluses and define how the Quick Bolus function operates. The Quick Bolus can deliver insulin based on entered carbohydrates or fixed units, or let the user choose between both options. The glucose target (Ideal BG) is adjusted separately under USER OPTION, while the duration of insulin action is configured in Doctor Mode, discussed later.

To use the integrated bolus calculator, go to the Main Menu, select Bolus, then BG BOLUS CALCULATOR, and press OK. In the BOLUS CALCULATOR screen, set the parameters for this bolus. BG is the current blood glucose level, CARBO the grams of carbohydrate in a meal, IDEAL the target blood glucose, CIR the carb ratio, and CF the correction factor or insulin sensitivity. Press the Right button to move through fields and Plus or Minus to adjust values; press OK to confirm. The Review screen shows the suggested bolus dose. At the top of the screen, G indicates glucose, C indicates carbohydrates, and A indicates active insulin. Press OK to start bolus delivery.

After choosing either a Quick Bolus or a bolus with the integrated bolus calculator, the DANA-i pump can deliver three bolus types: Step Bolus, Extended Bolus, and Dual Pattern Bolus.

To use the Step or Quick Bolus option, open the Main Menu, select Bolus, then select BOLUS from the BOLUS Sub menu. Press OK. Adjust the grams of carbohydrate with Plus or Minus. Move down the menu using the Right button to adjust the carb ratio, then press OK for the next step. Keep in mind that if BOLUS CALCULATION is set to “UNIT”, this carbohydrate step is skipped. Use Plus or Minus to increase or decrease bolus volume or delivery speed. Press OK to confirm and start bolus delivery.

During delivery, the INSULIN INJECT screen is shown and you can hear the motor; the pump beeps or vibrates for every 1.0 unit delivered. When the bolus completes, a DELIVERED BOLUS message displays the amount. Press OK to return to the initial screen.

To stop delivery mid-bolus, press the Minus button, and confirm STOP with OK. The DELIVERED BOLUS message then shows the amount delivered before stopping.

docs.title: 3.4 Suspending delivery
docs.text:
The Suspend function stops all insulin delivery, including Basal and Bolus. To activate it, select SUSPEND from the MAIN MENU. The initial screen will then alternate between SUSPEND and NO DELIVERY. While suspended, the pump sounds an alarm every 4 minutes to remind you that no insulin is being delivered.

To restart delivery, select SUSPEND OFF from the MAIN MENU and confirm with OK. The pump will issue an alarm, and the basal-active status will reappear on the home screen.

docs.title: 3.5 Programming settings through the AnyDANA app
docs.text:
Now that you’ve seen how to program settings on the pump itself, let’s switch to the AnyDANA app, which lets your phone act as a remote control.

In the Pump Settings, open Bolus Settings to adjust your ratios. Tap a ratio to edit the value; when you’re done, tap the checkmark in the top-right corner to save. You can also turn Extended Bolus on or off and enable Missed Bolus Alerts, which you can tailor to your schedule and preferences. Remember to tap the checkmark to save any changes.

From the same menu, open Basal Settings to view, switch, and edit your basal profiles. You can review rates hour by hour, tap Edit to change individual hours, or select multiple hour blocks to make larger adjustments at once. When finished, tap the checkmark on the upper right and Yes to save.

Back on the Home screen, select Bolus to access the two calculators and a shortcut to Bolus Settings. The Carb Calculator is ideal for snacks or top-up carbs, and you can choose among Step, Extended, or Dual Pattern delivery. In a Step Bolus, you can adjust both delivery speed and insulin amount, and you can start delivery from the pump and stop it instantly at any time. After delivery, the Home screen updates to show your pre-bolus and Active Insulin.

For corrections, use the BG Bolus Calculator to calculate a correction dose and a carb dose together. You can set custom targets and edit ratios directly within the calculator when needed. At the bottom of the screen, you’ll see the calculated dose breakdown and the app’s suggestion; tap Continue and choose your preferred delivery type. With a Dual Pattern Bolus, decide how much to give immediately and how much to extend over time for up to eight hours. You can interrupt delivery at any point, and the Home screen will indicate that a Dual Pattern bolus is in progress.

Temporary Basal is quick to set: choose a percentage from 0 to 200% and a duration up to 24 hours. The Home screen will show both the percentage and a Temporary Basal icon. To cancel, return to Temporary Basal and select Cancel. You can also Suspend the pump from within the app; the Home screen will confirm when the pump is in suspend mode.

docs.title: 3.6 Do's & don’ts
docs.text: The following guidelines and cautions apply to setting insulin delivery rates with the DANA-i pump.

docs.title: Doctor Mode or Professional Mode settings on the DANA-i insulin pump need to be configured when starting a new pump, as the device ships with factory default values for all safety parameters, including basal and bolus limits. These advanced settings are typically adjusted by a healthcare professional or certified pump trainer before the pump is handed over for patient use, to personalize the device according to the individual's insulin needs and safety requirements. Here’s how to do this:
docs.text:
First, we need to know the password. To determine what it is, we need to press OK to head into the Main Menu, OK, and Review, across to Shipping Information, and OK again. Here, number three will tell us the date of manufacture, which informs the Doctor Mode password. 16th of October 2020, in this example. Press OK to leave, Minus, and Minus again to head back to the home screen. To bring up the password selection box, you must press and hold the Right arrow and Plus and Minus all at the same time while looking at the home screen. A password box should appear. The password will be 3-0 followed by the day of manufacture, in our case 16.

Pressing OK should take you through to Doctor Mode. Much like User Options, Doctor Mode is a checklist. Right moves you down the list, Plus and Minus are your edit buttons, and OK will allow us to exit when we reach Option 15.

Number 1 is Preset Bolus. Preset Bolus should remain off for the most part. This removes the bolus calculator and instead provides you options for set times and sizes of boluses.

Melody is your “Check Glucose” alarm. You may set a time in 10-minute increments, whereby that increment post-bolus, an alarm will go off asking you to check your glucose. If you would not like any “Check Glucose” alarms, make sure your Melody is set to 0.

Number 3 is Bolus Block. This allows you to set a limit, again in 10-minute increments, on how frequently you can deliver boluses. This is designed for people that might accidentally bolus twice back to back, forgetting that they’ve just bolussed.

Number 4 is Bolus Increments, the increments that we can move in when calculating a bolus. For the most part, we’d set this to the minimum, which is 0.05.

Number 5 is Basal Increments. Again, for the most part, we’d set this to the minimum, 0.01.

Number 6 is your Ideal Blood Glucose. We can change this inside of User Options, but we can also change it inside of Doctor Mode.

Number 7 shows you your Active Insulin. This is just a value for reference so that you can see what effect changing number 8, your Decreasing Ratio, has on your active insulin.

Number 8 is Decreasing Ratio. This is how we express duration of action, and we show it as a percentage. A Decreasing Ratio of 20% means that 20% of your bolus insulin is used each hour post-bolus, giving your bolus a 5-hour duration of action. A 25% Decreasing Ratio would be equal to a 4-hour duration of action.

Number 9 is Basal Max. This is the most amount of basal insulin the pump will allow you to deliver inside of an hour. This includes temporary basal rate changes.

Number 10 is Bolus Max. This is the largest bolus the pump will allow you to deliver in a single go.

Number 11 is Daily Max. This is the daily total maximum of insulin — both bolus and basal — the pump will allow you to deliver.

Number 12, Safety Ratio, is a nuanced safety feature whereby it looks at the three-day average of bolus size, and you may set a ratio whereby should you choose to deliver a bolus, it will check to see whether it’s that ratio or higher than the average. Should it be higher than the average, it will prevent the bolus from going through. For the most part, we leave this off.

Number 13 is Block Sensitivity, or your occlusion alarm sensitivity. We have low (L), medium (M), and high (H) settings. For the most part, we’d recommend setting this to M, although certain closed-loop systems may have different requirements.

Number 14 allows you to change your UTC time zone settings.

Number 15 is Exit. Press OK on Exit, and OK to save your settings, and Doctor Mode has been programmed.

docs.title: Setting the correct date and time is essential for accurate basal insulin delivery and for maintaining accurate records of all insulin doses. The date and time themselves can only be adjusted in Doctor Mode, where Greenwich Mean Time, or UTC, must be entered. In the Main Menu under Options and then Clock Settings, you can change the time zone, for example time zone 1 or 2 for standard time and daylight saving time.
docs.text: • If you want to know your current active insulin, you can go to the Main Menu, select Review, and choose More Information.

docs.title: Great job! You’re now equipped to program insulin-delivery settings on the DANA-i pump. In the next lesson, we’ll cover replacing the reservoir and infusion set. See you again soon.
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (14.5 min)
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (14.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on replacing the DANA-i pump reservoir and infusion set.

In this video, you’ll learn the necessary supplies and step-by-step process for loading and refilling the DANA-i pump with insulin. We’ll then cover infusion-set specifics — namely connecting the set to the pump, priming the tubing, and inserting the set into the body — before wrapping up with key reminders for performing these procedures properly. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 4.1 Different infusion sets
docs.text: The DANA infusion sets are designed for subcutaneous insulin delivery with SOOIL pumps, including the DANA-i Pump. Available options include Soft Release O, Soft Release ST, Soft-Release Micro Steel, EasyRelease, and DANA Inset II. You can find an overview of the specifics of each infusion set in the download section. Availability of specific infusion sets may vary depending on your region.

docs.title: 4.2 Supplies
docs.text:
Gather the following materials and place them on a clean dry surface:

  • Your DANA-i insulin pump

  • Insulin at room temperature

  • 1 Alcohol swab

  • 1 DANA 3 ml syringe and needle

  • 1 Linking screw

  • 1 DANA Auto Setter

  • And 1 DANA infusion set

Remember to wash and dry your hands before opening any sterile packets and starting the filling process.

docs.title: 4.3 Reservoir replacement
docs.text:
Firstly, make sure your linking screw is moving up and down the thread smoothly before setting it to the top. The blue top hat should be flush with the head of the linking screw.

Next, remove the round cap at the back end of the syringe and discard it. Take off the small white reservoir cap from the plunger and keep it for later use.

There are teeth all around the base of the syringe. There are two corresponding teeth on either side of the linking screw. Clip the linking screw into the reservoir, making sure the teeth make a proper connection. Push the plunger up and down 2 to 3 times to lubricate the reservoir.

Now, decide on how much insulin you would like to fill and set your plunger to that length. Each milliliter is 100 units, and each of the short markers is 20 units.

Clean the insulin vial’s rubber lid with an alcohol swab. Carefully remove the clear protective needle cover, insert the needle into the vial, inject air from the reservoir, then draw the desired amount of insulin. Pull the plunger until the reservoir volume matches the required volume.

Afterwards, replace the needle cover. Gently tap the reservoir so any air bubbles rise to the top, then press the plunger gently to expel the air from the reservoir.

Hold the syringe as vertically as possible and gather all the air at the top. Push all the air out until you only have insulin filling the neck; you should see a smooth dome of insulin at the top. Retrieve the white reservoir cap from earlier and place it gently on top, just enough so that it grips. Avoid pressing the cap too hard. Doing so can force air back into the neck, which you’ll need to remove before priming.

With the reservoir filled with insulin, you're ready to adjust the length of the linking screw. This can be done by hand. Wind the linking screw until the black O-ring that you can see on the head of the linking screw is parallel to this point of overlap between the top and bottom half of the cartridge.

Alternatively, you can use the DANA Auto Setter to set the linking-screw length. Loosen the linking screw until the shaft-cap portion is covered by the head (blue) portion. This step is critical for accurate length adjustment. Insert the insulin-filled reservoir into the Auto Setter and turn it on by pressing the button; ensure the hexagonal end of the linking screw engages the Auto Setter’s hexagonal socket. Dashed lines leading into a “3” means the Auto Setter is ready. Press the button again to start measurement. The Auto Setter will first wind the reservoir out before withdrawing it in while counting the number of units on the screen. Have your insulin pump ready as when the Auto Setter finishes, it will send the volume in units to your pump via Bluetooth. When the Confirm Refill Volume screen appears on the pump, press OK. The pump will then prompt you to insert the reservoir, connect the infusion set, and keep the pump upright during priming.

At this point, you can unlock the reservoir cap, remove it, and insert the reservoir. Replace the cap, remove the white cover, and lock the cap — you should hear and feel a click. While inserting, rotate the reservoir 45° until the side notch slides into place, then let the reservoir and linking screw fall into place gently. Align the reservoir cap in a straight line with the guide mark and turn it 45° counterclockwise to lock.

When it’s time to reload the pump, remove the old reservoir first. Disconnect the infusion site from the pump tubing before opening the pump to prevent unintended insulin delivery. Open the pump's reservoir compartment by turning the reservoir cap a quarter-turn clockwise, then lift out the old reservoir. While holding the reservoir barrel, firmly pull the linking screw out of the reservoir.

docs.title: 4.4 Filling the tube
docs.text:
Attach the infusion-set tubing by turning counter-clockwise until firmly seated. Hold the pump upside down while connecting the tubing to prevent insulin from leaking into the pump.

On the pump, go to Refill in the main menu. Select Prime, and press OK. Once more, press OK to start. The screen will display reminders for priming.

Press OK twice to start Tube Prime. Make sure that your insulin pump is facing upright so the insulin and air bubbles rise easily to the surface. During priming, the pump may beep or vibrate after every unit of insulin primed. Press Minus to check whether insulin droplets appear at the end of the tube. Inspect the entire length of the tubing for bubbles. If you find any, press OK to re-start priming. Otherwise, press Minus again to finish the process. Keep in mind that droplets of insulin must be clearly visible at the tube or needle tip before stopping the prime. Unless you stop it earlier, priming automatically stops after 25 units.

docs.title: 4.5 Infusion set placement
docs.text:
The recommended insertion sites are shown. Rotate sites — ideally among four separate palm-sized areas of the body — to minimize skin damage and allow healing. Avoid recent insertion sites, scars, scar tissue, and bruised areas.

We will demonstrate how to insert the Inset II infusion set. For other infusion sets, follow the instructions provided in their packaging or refer to online tutorials.

The inset™ infusion set has a soft cannula inserted at a 90-degree angle, and comes with an integrated insertion device called the serter. The serter is an incorporated part of the product and contains the set and tubing.

Here you can see the parts of the infusion set: disconnect cover, sterile paper, insertion device, introducer needle, lined indentations (press here when loading), round indentations (press here when releasing), tubing, adhesive, soft cannula, cannula housing, connector needle, needle guard, lid, three raised dots (press here when lifting the lid off).

Preparation

Before you begin, check the tamperproof seal and sterile paper. Do not use if the seal or paper have been broken. Wash your hands before inserting the infusion set, and choose an insertion site as recommended by your healthcare provider. Clean the area for insertion with a disinfectant as directed by your health care provider. Be sure the area is air-dried before inserting the infusion set.

Preparing the serter

Pull the red tape to remove the tamperproof seal. Remove the sterile paper. To remove the lid, firmly press the three raised dots on each side while lifting the lid up. Be careful not to bend or touch the introducer needle when preparing the serter and infusion set. The needle should come placed securely in the serter: pointing straight, not at an angle or loose. If it is not straight or if it is loose and insertion is attempted, pain or minor injury may occur. To avoid this, please ensure that the following steps are done gently so the infusion set is not loosened or removed by accident.

Unwind the tubing by carefully unlocking the tubing from the slot. Now unwind the tubing from the serter by pulling it carefully upwards. Be careful not to touch the introducer needle while unwinding the tubing or pull too hard when the tubing is fully released, as this could accidentally remove the infusion set from the serter. Connect the infusion set to the pump. Before attaching the set to your body, fill the infusion set until insulin dispenses from the tip.

  • Ensure there are no air bubbles remaining in the tubing.

  • Never try to fill or free clogged tubing while the tubing is attached to the infusion set which has been inserted on the body. This may result in an unknown amount of insulin.

  • Consult your pump manufacturer’s instructions for use for further details about pump connection and filling of the infusion set.

When filling the infusion set tubing, hold the infusion set with the needle pointing down to ensure that insulin does not get in contact with the adhesive backing paper. Pull up gently on the white tab to remove the adhesive backing paper. Place your fingers on the lined indentations on each side. Pull the spring back firmly until you hear a click. Carefully remove the needle guard by gently twisting and then pulling it up, taking care not to touch the needle itself. Check that the soft cannula does not exceed the introducer needle.

Inserting

Before positioning the inset™ II infusion set over a clean insertion site, place the tubing in the slot to ensure that the tubing is not caught underneath the infusion set during insertion. Insert the inset™ II infusion set by positioning it on the clean insertion site. Simultaneously, press the round indentations on each side of the serter to insert the inset™ II infusion set. Push gently on the center of the serter to secure the adhesive on the skin. Remove the serter and introduce a needle by grasping the center and pulling it gently back. Gently massage the adhesive tape onto the skin. Fill the empty space in the cannula. Push the lid back in place until you hear a click.

Disconnecting

The inset™ II infusion set allows you to temporarily disconnect your pump without having to change the infusion set. Hold the adhesive tape in place by placing a finger just in front of the cannula housing and gently squeeze the sides of the connector. Pull the connector needle straight out from the cannula housing.If preferred, insert the disconnect cover into the cannula housing until you hear a click.

Reconnecting

Remove the disconnect cover from the cannula housing. Ensure the flow by filling the tubing until insulin dispenses from the needle. When filling the tubing, hold the connector needle pointing down. Place a finger on the adhesive tape just in front of the cannula housing and push the connector needles straight in until you hear a click. Now you’re ready to restart your insulin delivery.

docs.title: 4.6 Filling the cannula
docs.text:
When you fill the tubing of the Inset infusion set, the cannula is filled automatically. If you ever replace only the infusion set but keep the existing tubing, you should manually fill the cannula. This can be done through the same pump menu.

Go to the Main Menu on the pump’s LCD screen, select REFILL and press OK. Choose PRIME CANNULA from the sub menu and press OK. Verify that the PRIME CANNULA VOLUME is correct and press OK to confirm. Press OK again to start the process; the PRIME CANNULA window will display the delivery.

docs.title: Take note of these tips and guidelines for reservoir and infusion-set procedures.
docs.text:
• Always verify your glucose level 2 hours after inserting a new infusion set. This is the only way to confirm proper infusion set insertion and insulin delivery.

  • Do not push or force the reservoir into the pump, as this can damage the pump or force insulin out. If repeated insertion attempts fail, use a new reservoir.

  • Disconnect the pump from both the infusion set and your body before starting any part of the refill procedure, as remaining connected can lead to unintended insulin delivery.

  • The linking screw is part of the pump and is reused. Keep this part at every refill.

  • Do not reuse an old reservoir or infusion set.

  • Do not use an infusion set if the package is damaged, inadvertently opened, or wet.

  • PRIME is essential to ensure accurate delivery, as air in the tube commonly causes delivery problems and occlusion alarms. Users must understand how to prime correctly and why it matters.

  • Never start a tube prime while the infusion set is connected to the body.

  • If the prime amount is less than 7 U, an alarm will display because infusion sets used with the DANA-i require a minimum prime of 7 U.

  • A successful tube prime with visible insulin droplets at the tube tip confirms proper engagement of the linking screw with the motor gear. If no insulin appears after more than one prime, readjust the linking screw length. Contact your healthcare provider or pump trainer if this occurs frequently.

  • After insertion of the infusion set, the site should not feel uncomfortable to the touch.

  • The cannula fill volume is set in the USER OPTION menu and can be adjusted between 0.1 and 0.9 units. Follow the cannula instructions to determine individual fill requirements.

  • The Release infusion sets of the DANA-i pump are connected and disconnected differently than other pump infusion sets. To disconnect the Release infusion set, gently push the side ridges to release it. To reconnect, click the tubing cap onto the cannula base. Always make sure the tubing cap is securely in place by rotating the tube and pulling the cap upwards.

You're all set to manage the reservoir and infusion set for your DANA-i pump. Should you encounter difficulties, keep calm and work through the steps. And don't hesitate to reach out to your healthcare provider or pump trainer for assistance. In the next video, we'll examine the DANA-i alarm system. See you there.

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms (3.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on alarms, errors, alerts, and reminders with the DANA-i pump.

In this video, we’ll differentiate among these notifications, outline the steps for changing alarm settings, and share practical do’s and don’ts. Let’s jump right in!

The DANA-i pump uses visual, sound, and vibration cues to signal alarms, errors, and alerts.

You can choose SOUND, VIBRATION, or BOTH for pump alarms and alerts. To do this, open the Main Menu, select Option, then User Option, and scroll to ALARM. But keep in mind that for safety, alarms will sound even if VIBRATION is selected.

docs.title: 5.1 Alarms
docs.text: Alarms are safety-critical messages that require immediate attention. They will sound even if VIBRATION is selected. There are 10 alarms, including those for LOW BATTERY, EMPTY RESERVOIR, SHUTDOWN, OCCLUSION, CHECK ERROR, SYSTEM ERROR, PRIME INCOMPLETE, BASAL MAX, and DAILY MAX. To silence an alarm, press any button, then take the appropriate corrective action right away.

docs.title: 5.2 Errors
docs.text:
Errors indicate a problem with the pump but are less urgent than alarms. They include LOW RESERVOIR, SUSPEND, or DELIVERY LESS THAN BASAL SET RATE.

Regarding LOW RESERVOIR, this error depends on the Low Reservoir threshold set in User Option. Until you refill the pump, the Low Reservoir alert repeats every hour when more than 20 units remain, and every 30 minutes when 20 units or fewer are left.

docs.title: 5.3 Alerts
docs.text:
Alerts inform you about pump status or prompt a decision. They include MISSED BOLUS, PRIME AMOUNT IS NOT ENOUGH, CHECK GLUCOSE, CONFIRM PAIRING, and NO DELIVERY.

MISSED BOLUS 1 to 4 are reminders for regular meal boluses. When turned ON, you can set a time window; if no bolus is delivered in that window, an alarm reminds you of a missed bolus. To disable the MISSED BOLUS setting, set it to OFF.

For full descriptions and step-by-step responses to all alarms, errors, and alerts, please refer to the DANA-i pump user manual.

docs.title: 5.4 Do’s & don’ts
docs.text:
Let's go over some important reminders about the DANA-i pump's alarm system.

  • Always keep spare batteries on hand so you can respond immediately to a LOW BATTERY alarm.

  • In case of an EMPTY RESERVOIR or NO DELIVERY alarm, the pump cannot deliver basal or bolus insulin and you will have no access to any delivery function until the issue is fixed.

  • Even after clearing an OCCLUSION alarm, check your blood glucose to confirm that insulin delivery has fully resumed.

  • CHECK ERROR and SYSTEM ERROR stop all delivery. After restarting the pump, check if it delivers insulin. If the alarm persists, contact your local insulin-pump technical support.

  • Regularly verify that the display turns on, audible beeps can be heard, and vibration can be felt. If any of these functions are not working, discontinue pump use and contact your healthcare provider or your local insulin-pump distributor’s technical support.

Fantastic work! You’re now well versed in DANA-i pump alarms, errors, and alerts. If your pump makes a sound, don’t panic — check the screen for guidance. If any message is unclear, refer to the user manual or contact customer support. Best of luck!

docs.title: 6. Case report (1 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to the final video of this module! Thank you for investing the time to learn about the DANA-i insulin pump.

You’ve built a strong foundation; now let’s connect theory to everyday use. To see how a DANA pump performs in daily life, we recommend watching this YouTube video:

https://youtu.be/jftTbUPlLYI?si=K5X020qkH8zcyHqm

In it, Iulia shares her experience with the DANA RS, the DANA-i’s predecessor. The two devices share many core features, making Iulia’s insights relevant to your DANA-i journey.

Combining real-world insights with the concepts we’ve covered can help you turn your knowledge into confident and effective pump use.

Remember, your commitment is a meaningful step toward more consistent time-in-range and healthier diabetes management.

Once again, thank you for joining us. We wish you the very best as you continue managing your diabetes with your insulin pump. Keep up the great work and remember, your health and well-being are worth the effort.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Pump operation
  • 3. Programming insulin delivery settings
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the Equil Insulin Pump! In the upcoming videos, we’ll examine the components and functionality of this modular, tubeless insulin pump system.

Measuring 59.5 × 40 × 11.1 mm and weighing 23 g, the Equil Pump is a modular patch pump, combining a reusable pump body and pump battery with a disposable insulin reservoir and pump base. The cannula is built into the pump base which adheres directly to the skin. The pump is controlled using a wireless Personal Diabetes Assistant (or PDA). Depending on your system version, the pump capacity and PDA interface may vary slightly. There is also a physical bolus button on the pump for quick insulin delivery without the remote. The device features a reservoir capacity of 200 to 300 units of U-100 rapid-acting insulin and is designed for people with diabetes aged 3 years or older.

Our Master Module on insulin pumps provided an overview of standard pump management, including basal and bolus rate configuration, reservoir and infusion set changes, and system alarm settings.

In this module, you will receive in-depth training on the Equil Pump, along with advanced tips for healthcare providers and people living with diabetes. But please note that these videos are for educational purposes only and do not constitute medical advice. Always consult your healthcare provider before making changes to your therapy.

These videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent, in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Anna Kreczmer, a diabetes educator from Poznań in Poland, with personal experience using the Equil Pump, who trains patients in pump therapy. Together, they provide a transparent and independent evaluation of the Equil Pump.

Rest assured, the content in this module is independent of MicroTech Medical, the manufacturer of the Equil Pump. As a result, you receive a genuine, balanced assessment of the device’s strengths and limitations.

Upon completing this module, you’ll be able to:

  • identify the components of the Equil Pump and operate the device;

  • configure the pump’s insulin delivery settings;

  • replace the Equil Pump insulin reservoir and infusion sets; and

  • customize and respond to Equil system alarms.

If you are a healthcare provider, this module will equip you to support Equil Pump users with assurance; if you are using the pump yourself, you will learn practical strategies to get the most from the device. We wish you every success as you get started with the Equil Pump!

docs.title: 2. Pump operation (22 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on operating the Equil Insulin Pump.

In this video, we’ll introduce the key components of the Equil Pump system. You’ll learn how to set up and charge the Personal Diabetes Assistant (PDA), navigate its buttons, and complete the initial Settings Wizard. We’ll also walk through the PDA Home Screen and explain its main functions before outlining essential do’s and don’ts for safe and effective use of the Equil system. Let’s begin.

The Equil Pump system has evolved through different versions, with updates to the PDA, software, and reservoir capacity.

The earliest version of the PDA, which we’ll refer to as the classic PDA, is a dedicated medical device with physical buttons and a grid-style menu interface. It was designed primarily for manual pump control and includes a built-in blood glucose meter, allowing users to check their glucose levels directly on the device.

A newer version, Equil 1.5, features a touchscreen PDA similar to a smartphone. Its interface replaces the icon-based menu with a home screen that includes tappable buttons and a live CGM graph, enabling users to view key information at a glance. The Equil 1.5 pump also has an increased reservoir capacity of up to 300 units of insulin.
