Building on these developments, MicroTech Medical introduced a closed-loop version, the Equil S. Like the Equil 1.5, it has a 300-unit capacity. Note that the classic and Equil 1.5 pumps are similar in size, while the Equil S is slightly larger, weighing 31 g.

While the classic and newer Equil systems differ in interface and capacity, their core operating principles are the same. In this module, demonstrations will primarily use the classic pump and PDA, with key differences in the newer versions highlighted where relevant.

The Equil Pump system includes the following reusable, non-consumable components:

  • 1 pump body (which is covered by a 4-year warranty)

  • 1 Personal Diabetes Assistant (or PDA) with battery

  • 1 cannula inserter (also covered by a 4-year warranty)

  • 2 pump batteries

  • 1 pump battery charger

  • 1 PDA charger, and

  • 1 PDA charging cable.

These durable parts are designed for long-term use.

You will also need the following disposable, consumable components, which are not included in the system and have to be purchased separately:

  • insulin reservoirs

  • and infusion sets (made up of the cannula and the pump base).

Here’s how the reusable and consumable components fit together.

docs.title: 2.1 Setting up the PDA
docs.text:
Remove the battery cover by holding the PDA firmly in one hand and lifting the cover using your fingernail in the slot shown in the figure. Next, insert the microSD memory card into the slot, then install the battery in the orientation shown. Replace the battery cover, ensuring it is secured around the perimeter of the PDA; you may hear a snap as it locks into place.

The PDA has several buttons and ports for operating and controlling the pump. To use it effectively, it is helpful to know them well.

One, the Power button. If the PDA is off, press and hold it until the PDA vibrates. After 30 seconds, the Home Screen appears. If the screen is on, press and hold the button to lock the PDA. Generally, the PDA screen turns off after a predefined period of inactivity. In this case, you will see the Lock Screen. When the screen is off, you can either tap the power button to turn it on and bring up the Lock Screen or press and hold it to turn the device off.

Two, the Help button. This button provides information about the device's commands and functions. It is active on certain screens, such as the main screen, the Base and Bolus screens, and the Log screen.

Three, the Back button. Its function is to take you back to the previous screen, or to close dialog boxes after reading them and verifying that they are not needed.

Four, the Home button. This button will always return you to the Home Screen.

Five, Up button. Six, Down button. These buttons change the selection. Their function is to help you navigate between options without using the touchscreen.

Seven, the Enter button. Its function is to select an action by pressing it, without using the touchscreen.

Eight, Touchscreen.

Nine, the Power Charging Port. It is also used for data transfer to a personal computer or cloud service.

Ten, Headphone Output.

Eleven, the port for the blood glucose meter integrated with the PDA. Use Exactive EQ strips to activate the Blood Glucose Test menu.

Twelve, Strip Ejector. Use it to remove the glucose test strip after the measurement has been completed. This is located on the back of the device.

Thirteen, the External Speaker. It can also be found at the back.

docs.title: 2.2. Charging the PDA and the pump battery
docs.text:
Before use, fully charge both the PDA battery and the patch pump battery. The PDA battery can only be charged while it is inside the PDA. The PDA may be on or off during charging, but it will charge faster when turned off.

To charge it, connect the narrow (USB-C) end of the cable to the PDA and the wide (USB-A) end to the PDA charger. Then plug the charger into a power outlet. If the PDA is on, the battery icon will change to a charging icon; if it is off, a charging animation will appear.

To charge the patch pump battery, insert it into the pump battery charger until you hear a click, which indicates it is properly seated. Connect the narrow end of the cable to the pump battery charger and connect the wide end to the PDA charger, then plug the PDA charger into a wall outlet. The blue LED will turn on to indicate charging; if the charger is faulty, the indicator will flash. When the battery is fully charged, the blue LED will turn off. You can then disconnect the cable and chargers, and remove the pump battery by lifting it out of the charger groove.

docs.title: 2.3. The PDA Settings Wizard
docs.text:
If you are using the pump for the first time, the first step is to press and hold the Power button on the PDA. The device will automatically take you to the Settings Wizard. This is where the pump’s basic settings are set before use. The rest of the settings can be adjusted later.

At this stage, you will see five screens for operating settings. The Next button will take you to the next screen, and the Previous button will return you to the previous screen. During this stage, none of the buttons on the PDA device will be active until you’re done configuring the initial settings.

The first screen you see is where you set the time and date. When a dialog box pops up, enter the exact date using the plus and minus buttons, then click OK.

Similarly, set the time, select OK to save and exit, and then set the hour format. It is automatically set to 24 hours, but you can choose 12 hours, which will allow you to choose morning or evening. Once that’s done, click Next.

The next page will appear. Here you adjust the basic basal rate parameters, with three options. First is the Maximum Basal Rate. Selecting it brings up a dialog box where you can set the maximum basal rate. It’s helpful for putting a threshold on the basal rate injection if there’s any error caused by insufficient pump knowledge. After that, you choose the Base Basal Rate. Use the plus and minus buttons to select the appropriate value. The last option is a Temporary Basal Rate, which can be disabled or displayed in units of insulin delivered per hour. Alternatively, you can use a percentage of the current basal rate.

Usually, this screen shows all the options selected by your healthcare provider. It’s also possible to skip it at the current stage and reset it at any time from the Home Screen via Settings and Insulin Delivery Settings.

Proceed to the third screen in the Settings Wizard. Here you will find the basic bolus settings, and this contains three options. First, the Bolus Increment setting allows you to set the intervals used when increasing or decreasing bolus amounts. The second option lets you set the Maximum Bolus value. Having a maximum dose limit will prevent human errors. Last is the Extended Bolus setting. It will open a dialog box, which can be disabled or expressed as insulin units, or as a percentage of the total calculated bolus amount. This screen shows doses set by your healthcare provider, which can be skipped and reset later.

Click Next to enter the fourth screen of the Settings Wizard. This page displays advanced bolus settings, where you can adjust two main pump features. The first is a Quick Bolus function, which allows you to administer a bolus using only one button located on the pump. This feature is turned off by default. If you choose it, you can set the Quick Bolus Increment, which is how much insulin will be injected every time you press the button. Selecting OK will automatically turn it on.

The second feature is a Bolus Calculator, which you can use to determine the size of a bolus based on blood glucose levels, carbohydrate consumption, and other parameters. With the assistance of your healthcare provider, enter these parameters here once. This feature is switched off by default.

In general, MicroTech Medical recommends not activating or interacting with these features unless you have received sufficient training on the use of the pump from an authorized trainer.

Afterwards, click the Next button and enter the last screen of the pump’s basic settings. Here you specify how many insulin units remain in the insulin reservoir in order to receive an alarm when the reservoir is about to run out. This is done through an option called the Low Reservoir Threshold.

Also from the same screen, you can set the full automatic shutdown of the pump. This is an option that will not be activated by default. That said, you can choose to activate it and adjust the number of hours the pump has been operating without any human interaction, such as entering data or pressing buttons. After that time passes, the pump will stop delivering insulin and shut down completely. A 15-minute alarm will begin before the auto-off time expires, and will continue until we acknowledge it. This is to let you know that insulin delivery has stopped. In this case, you have to refill the reservoir in order for the pump to work again. Click Finish.

Once you have completed the Settings Wizard, you will be able to use all the basic functions of the pump. If you wish to modify anything afterward, from the Home Screen, select Settings, then Settings Wizard. The pump is now ready for use.

docs.title: 2.4 The PDA home screen
docs.text:
This is the main screen, or Home Screen, where you can find all the pump options. The Home Screen is divided into three sections. At the top is the Status Bar, which shows the PDA and pump status. In the middle is the Information Display Area, where you’ll find important measurements and the status of special functions. The bottom section contains Function Buttons for operation, settings, and history.

As for the status bar, it is divided into two parts, separated by the time. In the English version, the left part of the bar corresponds to the pump, is colored blue, and includes an icon representing the pump’s battery status. It will show the charge decreasing gradually unless the connection with the PDA is lost.

There’s also an icon that shows how much insulin is left in the reservoir, in different degrees. If the PDA wireless link is poor, it displays this symbol.

On the right, you can see the PDA status, which is green. This icon displays the state of the PDA battery in different degrees. When it’s charging, it looks like this.

Another icon shows the WiFi signal strength, in three different degrees. In the event of a total disconnect, you’ll see this icon.

Additionally, a yellow warning icon will appear if there are any warnings from the pump.

If a reminder for blood sugar measurement has been previously set, a clock icon will appear.

Pressing the button in the middle of the status bar displays a larger screen with a detailed representation of the information in the status bar. It contains the name of the pump user and the pump serial number. Furthermore, all the data contained in the status bar is shown in numerical format, not only as icons.

You’ll see alert notifications if there are any pump warnings. Then you can acknowledge and cancel them from here.

In the middle of the Home screen, you’ll find the three most prominent numbers related to pump operation and the user’s status, such as the most recent blood glucose measurement and the time of the test, the latest bolus — that is, when it was administered and its dose — and the current basal rate.

In certain situations, the Information Display Area changes to reflect the action taking place at the moment — for example, during bolus delivery, the temporary basal rate status, or the amount of time that insulin delivery has stopped.

The lower part of the Home Screen contains buttons for the following functions:

  • One, Bolus. Pressing this button takes you to the Bolus screen, where you can administer a normal or extended bolus. This will be explained in detail in a separate video.

  • Two, the Basal button. By clicking it, you can set the basal programs or start a temporary basal rate. You can refer to the Basal video for more information.

  • Three, the Actions button. This brings up the Actions screen, where you can perform several operations, including changing the pump battery, starting a new reservoir, or initiating a new pump. This will all be explained in an upcoming video. You can also manually enter a blood sugar measurement value and determine the user’s condition at the time of the test. The Food Library contains a list of carbohydrates found in a wide variety of known foods, allowing you to search by name and category. By clicking on the plus sign, you can add your own favorite food and its attributes to the list. The last item in Actions is the Audio Player, which is only available if the PDA is equipped with a memory card.

  • Four, the History button. This is where you can find information about the user’s therapy history and historical averages during a specific period of time. The current day is automatically displayed when you open it. You can also choose a specific day to display by using the arrows to the right and left of the current date. In portrait mode, data appears in gray, with timestamp entries such as blood glucose readings, changes in basal rate, bolus amounts, and carbohydrate consumption. Using your finger, you can scroll up and down the list. You may also filter the results by using the checkboxes beneath the list. Above all these events are the daily averages, such as average blood sugar, total daily carbohydrate intake, total insulin, and the bolus percentage of total insulin for the day.

  • When the orientation icon is shown, it means that if you hold the PDA in a horizontal, or landscape position, the information will be displayed in a graph display mode. The graph will illustrate two types of information. As you can see in the upper chart, it displays blood glucose measurements as green circles and carbohydrate intake as vertical green lines. The lower chart shows the basal rate as a blue waveform, and boluses are indicated as vertical blue lines.

  • This is the display screen for the daily record. But if you want broader averages, press the button that says Average (AVG) and you’ll get an average for a longer period of time. By default, the duration is a week. However, you can increase this duration by clicking on it and selecting the desired period from the quick keys, or select a specific number of days by using the plus and minus buttons.

  • At the bottom of the screen, you can choose different filters to show — for instance, the averages of all readings that were taken before or after exercise, or before or after a meal.

  • While you are reviewing historical averages, you can access Calendar Mode by rotating the PDA to landscape orientation. The left side of the screen shows the time, while the top of the screen shows the day. When you tap on any hourly data, detailed information is displayed. You can also change the week using the left and right arrows. By clicking the LOG button, you can return to the daily log.

  • Five, the Settings button. Clicking on it takes you to the Settings menu consisting of the following items:

  • The Settings Wizard contains all the basic settings needed to operate the pump, including the five screens explained earlier.

  • BG, or Blood Glucose, Setting, where you can set reminders for measuring blood glucose or enable the Bolus Calculator if you didn’t do so in the Settings Wizard.

  • Insulin Delivery Settings. This window contains all the settings for administering insulin, whether it is a bolus dose or a basal rate. Some of those settings were made using the Settings Wizard, and you can reset them here.

  • General Settings. This contains some menu items that are not available in the Settings Wizard, such as About System. This item shows information about the PDA model, software version, PDA serial number, and the paired pump serial number. It also includes options for changing the language, as well as memory card information, including how much of its total capacity is used and how much is available. You can also back up the history on the memory card.

  • Display settings allow you to adjust the brightness of the display and set the inactivity timeout before the screen turns off.

  • User settings, where you can enter data such as your name and password to ensure security during power-on, contact details, and restore factory settings.

  • Lastly, there are settings for Sound and Bluetooth connection, so you can export your history without having to use a data cable. This allows data and records to be transferred to the cloud, enabling healthcare providers to check your settings and data remotely.

  • Six, the Suspend/Pause button can be found in the bottom right corner of the Home Screen This function can be used, for instance, if you temporarily do not require insulin infusion or you need to remove the pump from the base. Pressing this button opens a confirmation dialog box.

  • If you choose Suspend (Pump won’t rewind) and click OK, the PDA returns to the Home Screen, and the Information Area will display the amount of time that insulin delivery has stopped for. The Pause button will become a Resume button. The PDA will flash every 15 minutes during the pump suspension period.

  • When choosing Stop (Pump will rewind), the pump will stop and the rod will return to its original position. In this case, you’ll need to follow the Change Reservoir procedure.

  • Note that the pump cannot be in Suspend Mode while a bolus is in progress, and the dose must be cancelled before the pump can be suspended.

You can turn off the PDA display using the Power button, or it will automatically turn off after the preset screen timeout. When you press the Power button again, you’ll see this Lock Screen. To unlock, pull the long bar from left to right, then enter the password. In case you forget the password, you can enter the pump serial number.

Another way to unlock the PDA is to press and hold the Down and Enter buttons at the same time.

docs.title: 2.5 Do’s & don’ts
docs.text:
• Use only accessories and disposables manufactured by MicroTech Medical or labelled under the Equil brand. Using non-standard components may compromise device safety.

  • For safety, the PDA can control only one pump at a time.

  • Each PDA battery supports at least 300 charge–discharge cycles. On a full charge, it typically lasts around 2–3 days. Heavy or frequent use will drain the battery faster, so charge as needed based on your usage.

  • Each pump battery supports approximately 200 charge–discharge cycles. The pump system package includes two pump batteries so you can alternate charging between them. One fully charged battery lasts about 5 days. Repeated plunger operation increases power consumption and may shorten battery life.

  • Insert the microSD memory card only after removing the battery. A microSD card is not included in the pump package, but you may provide your own. The microSD card is not required for the pump system’s primary functions; however, secondary features — such as data transfer and the MP3 player — will not function correctly without a memory card inserted.

  • The blood glucose testing function of the PDA works exclusively with MicroTech Exactive EQ blood glucose test strips. Other test strips are not supported and will either produce an error or no result.

  • The newer versions of the Equil Pump are water-resistant under specific conditions, meaning routine activities such as showering, sweating, and brief water exposure are generally supported within manufacturer limits. However, full submersion is not advised; it is therefore recommended to disconnect the pump before swimming. In contrast, the classic pump and the PDA are not water-resistant and must be kept dry at all times.

  • Avoid exposing the pump and PDA to temperatures above 40°C or below 0°C.

  • If you are scheduled for an X-ray, CT scan, MRI, or any procedure involving radiation, make sure to remove both the pump and the PDA before entering the room with the imaging equipment.

  • Dedicated covers are available for both the PDA and the pump.

  • If needed, clean the outer surfaces of the pump and PDA with a soft, damp cloth and mild detergent, then dry with a separate cloth. You can also disinfect them using an alcohol wipe. Do not use solvents like nail polish remover or paint thinner on the device surfaces.

  • If the pump system is not in use for a short time, keep all components in a clean, dry, cool, and well-ventilated storage space.

  • If the pump will not be used for an extended period, remove the battery and store it separately.

That concludes our discussion on operating the Equil Pump system — what’s reusable versus disposable, how to set up and charge the PDA, how to navigate the PDA main screen, and the essential do’s and don’ts to follow. Next, we’ll move on to programming insulin delivery settings so the pump aligns with the user’s therapy plan. See you in the next video.

docs.title: 3. Programming insulin delivery settings (18 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on programming insulin delivery settings with the Equil Pump.

In this video, you’ll learn how to set up and edit basal programs, apply a temporary basal rate when needed, and deliver boluses using manual, preset, extended, and quick-bolus options. We’ll also show you how to use the Bolus Calculator to support dosing decisions and how to suspend and resume insulin delivery safely. Finally, we’ll review practical do’s and don’ts to help you adjust settings correctly and avoid common errors. Let’s dive in.

docs.title: 3.1 Setting a basal rate
docs.text:
From the Home Screen, press the Basal function button. You will see three basal program tabs, out of the seven programs the pump can run. You can navigate between them using the touchscreen. Here, you are viewing data for a lighter basal program. The top row displays the total basal amount, the program status — that is, whether it is active or not — and a list of basal program periods with their corresponding values and times. Swipe down in this area to scroll and view additional entries. All values in this list will always be lower than the Maximum Basal Rate. At the bottom of the screen, you’ll find the basal program action buttons, which allow you to activate a program or change its settings.

If you rotate the PDA to landscape orientation, the basal program graph mode is activated. In this view, the vertical axis represents the basal rate, while. Because the basal rate is currently set to a fixed value and no program periods have been defined, it appears as a straight line. Once a program is configured, the different basal periods will be shown both as a list and as a graphical display. Basal rates often vary throughout the day depending on daily activities. Since activity patterns can differ across days (such as workdays versus weekends, or fasting days versus regular days), you may need to create and activate different basal programs, switching between them as needed.

The Equil Pump also offers a quick basal program setup option, making it easier and faster to edit basal programs. To begin, enter Edit Mode by selecting the top row below the program name and tapping the Edit icon. The Basal Initial Setup window will appear. Use the plus and minus buttons to adjust the total daily basal amount, then choose how many time segments you want — either 24 or 6. Based on these settings, the system will automatically assign basal time periods to the program. To modify any time period, tap its Edit icon and, in the pop-up window, set the start time, end time, and basal rate. Keep in mind that changing individual time periods may slightly affect the total daily basal amount. You can also delete a time period by selecting the Cancel icon; in this case, the period will revert to the base basal rate. Note that the PDA may automatically merge adjacent time periods that have the same basal rate.

Basal programs can also be edited in graphical mode. Tap and hold a section of the graph to select a time period; the selected area will appear darker. You can move between periods using the left and right arrows, or by using the touchscreen. As in portrait mode, this icon opens the Basal Rate Settings window. Selecting the Cancel icon removes the selected time period and returns it to the base basal value, which you’ll see reflected immediately on the chart. This icon exits Edit Mode and cancels any changes, while selecting the Save option exits Edit Mode and saves your settings.

When you are not in Edit Mode, you can use the touchscreen to move between different basal programs. Renaming a basal program is done in portrait mode. Enter Edit Mode, tap Change Basal Program Name, and enter a new name in the dialog box. After renaming, tap Save Settings. The program can then be activated by selecting Activate and confirming by tapping OK. The active basal program’s name will appear on the Home Screen. If you edit a program that is already active, an Update Pump dialog box will appear after saving. Select Cancel to discard the changes, or OK to apply the updates to the pump’s current basal program.

docs.title: 3.2 Setting a temporary basal rate
docs.text:
Before you can use a temporary basal rate, the feature must be enabled. You can activate it either from page 2 of the Settings Wizard or by going to Settings > Insulin Delivery Settings > Temporary Basal Rate.

The Temporary Basal option can be used only within the currently active basal program. After the program is activated, the same activation button changes to Temporary Basal Rate. The button appears green when the Temporary Basal option is enabled. If it is not enabled, the button will appear gray.

Press the Temporary Basal Rate button to open the settings dialog box. Enter the Temporary Basal rate value in units per hour, based on the settings you selected earlier. Then set the duration and tap OK. The PDA will send the command to the pump to begin delivering the updated rate, and the Home Screen will display the Temporary Basal status.

docs.title: 3.3 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
There are several methods to administer a bolus. The first is a manual bolus. From the Home Screen, tap the Bolus button to open the Bolus screen. In the upper-left corner, you’ll see a green Bolus button. Initially, the bolus amount is zero, and the Start button is greyed out. Tap the Bolus button to open a dialog box and enter the desired bolus amount, then press OK. The insulin amount will appear in the box, and the Start button will turn green. Tap Start to begin delivery. A “Connecting to Pump” animation will appear, followed by a confirmation window showing your last bolus amount and time. Tap OK to proceed. The PDA will return to the Home Screen and display the bolus delivery status at the previously configured rate. The Bolus button will remain inactive until the bolus has been fully delivered.

The Bolus screen also includes three programmable preset bolus buttons. Tap the Edit icon to open the Preset dialog box. Adjust the dose using the plus and minus buttons, assign a name, and tap OK. When you tap the left side of a preset button, its preset value appears in the bolus amount box on the left, along with a small arrow linking the preset to the selected dose. The Start button turns green, and you can proceed as before. Preset boluses are especially useful for fast-digesting foods, as the full dose is delivered immediately.

For meals that digest more slowly, you can use the Extended Bolus option. Extended boluses are delivered in two parts: Amount Now, which is delivered immediately, and the remaining amount, which is delivered over a set period of time. First, enter the total bolus amount as usual. Before starting, tap Extend Bolus to open the Extended Bolus window. Under Amount Now, enter the portion to be delivered immediately, using absolute units as defined in your settings. Then set the duration over which the remaining insulin will be delivered. Tap OK, then Start, and follow the same confirmation steps as before.

While delivery is in progress, the PDA returns to the Home Screen and displays the Extended Bolus status, including how much insulin has been delivered from each part. A countdown timer shows how much time remains for the extended portion. For high-fat meals, an extended bolus without an immediate dose may be appropriate. In this case, set Amount Now to zero. No insulin will be delivered immediately, and the full dose will be spread over a period ranging from 30 minutes to 8 hours.

The Equil Pump also includes a Quick Bolus feature. This allows you to administer a bolus directly using the Bolus button on the patch pump, without using the PDA. This can be useful if you are away from your PDA or prefer more privacy. Press and hold the Bolus button on the patch pump. An audible alert indicates that Quick Bolus mode is active. Each additional press increases the bolus by a fixed increment. The total bolus equals the number of button presses multiplied by the Quick Bolus Increment. For example, if the increment is 2 units and you need a 10-unit bolus, press the button five times. Each press is confirmed by a beep, with the tone increasing in pitch up to five tones to help you count presses. The total Quick Bolus dose cannot exceed the preset Maximum Bolus value. After entering the desired dose, wait three seconds. The pump will replay the tones to confirm the dose. If correct, press the button once more to start delivery. If the tones are incorrect, wait more than 10 seconds and the Quick Bolus will be cancelled automatically. You can also deliver a Quick Bolus using the Enter button on the PDA instead of the pump button.

In some situations, you may need to cancel a bolus after it has started — for example, if you entered an incorrect dose, miscalculated carbohydrates, entered the wrong blood glucose value, or changed your meal plan. While a bolus is in progress, tap Cancel on the Home Screen. A confirmation window will appear. Tap OK to confirm, and a dialog box will display the amount and time of insulin already delivered. A bolus can also be cancelled directly from the pump by pressing and holding the Quick Bolus button. You will hear an audible confirmation, and the bolus will stop immediately. A dialog box will then show the delivered dose details.

Be aware that canceling a bolus stops the Total Bolus, Amount Now, and Extended Amount. If you are manually recalculating a new dose, you must account for the insulin already delivered and subtract it from the next dose. If you are using the Bolus Calculator, the cancelled dose is automatically included as Active Insulin and deducted from the new bolus.

docs.title: 3.4 Using the Bolus Calculator
docs.text:
The Bolus Calculator can suggest a bolus dose after you test your blood glucose level and enter the amount of carbohydrates you will be eating, helping keep your blood glucose within the target range. This feature determines the amount of insulin needed to cover the rise in blood glucose associated with carbohydrate intake as the Meal Bolus. In addition, a Correction Bolus is added to help bring your current blood glucose level back to the target range. And if you have recently taken a bolus, there may still be active insulin in your body, so the Bolus Calculator will automatically subtract the active insulin from the Total Suggested Bolus dose.

The Bolus Calculator is switched off by default. To activate it from the Home screen, press Settings > Blood Glucose Settings, then turn it on. You’ll see five settings displayed; these values should be adjusted in consultation with your healthcare provider. First is the Target Blood Glucose Range, which sets the default target range. This is a user setting confirmed by the healthcare provider. The Equil Pump also allows you to set different target ranges for different time periods, and the calculator takes the average adjusted value into account. Next is the Carbohydrate Ratio (ICR), which refers to how many carbohydrates are covered per unit of insulin and is used to calculate the Meal Bolus. Because metabolism varies from person to person, set this value under the guidance of your healthcare provider. Note that different carbohydrate ratios can also be set for different time periods. The Insulin Sensitivity Factor (ISF) indicates how much your blood glucose may drop after taking one unit of insulin. Since this value also differs between individuals, consult your healthcare provider, and remember that ISF can be set for different time periods as well. Even if you have recently taken a bolus, you may still have active insulin in your body, as insulin typically remains active for four to six hours, and this value must be determined by your doctor. Finally, Reverse Correction affects how correction boluses are calculated. If it is off, the Bolus Calculator will consider a correction bolus only when your current blood glucose is higher than the target range. If it is on, a correction bolus is always calculated. The default setting is on, but your doctor can change it if necessary. Once you’ve adjusted the settings and understood the inputs, you can use the Bolus Calculator quickly and easily.

Only two inputs are needed to generate a bolus suggestion: your current blood glucose level and the amount of carbohydrates you will be eating. On the Home screen, select Actions, then click Enter BG Reading to open the manual entry screen. Using the plus and minus buttons, enter your blood glucose reading, select the appropriate tag — Before/After Exercise or Before/After Meal — and press Save. When the dialog box appears, choose OK to save the result. You’ll notice that the Bolus Calculator button changes from grey to green, indicating that it has been activated. The internal PDA blood glucose meter can also activate the Bolus Calculator. If the display is on, insert an Exactive EQ test strip and the blood glucose meter screen will appear. Apply enough blood sample, then the screen will count down five seconds. The test result will be displayed, and the Bolus Calculator button will turn green once a blood glucose reading has been recorded. Note that your blood glucose reading is valid for only ten minutes. If you do not deliver a bolus within 10 minutes of your test, please test again.

Now, press the Bolus Calculator button. It will ask you, “Will you be eating now?” If you choose Yes, a dialog box will appear asking you to enter the amount of carbohydrates. You can estimate the carbohydrates in your food by reading packaging labels, using carbohydrate-counting books or phone apps, or referring to the PDA’s Food Library. Enter the total amount of carbohydrates you intend to eat and press OK. The next page is the Suggested Bolus screen. At the top, you’ll see the suggested bolus dose. The middle section shows the calculation parameters, and the bottom contains action buttons. The suggested bolus amount appears automatically in the Bolus Amount area. Tapping the number opens a dialog box that allows you to change the value if your physician has advised adjustments, or you can use it as is. The Details button opens a window that explains how the suggested bolus was calculated. By pressing Start, you can administer the bolus dose. Alternatively, you may use the Extended Bolus option, as explained earlier. However, if you choose No in the “Will you be eating now?” dialog box, the Meal Bolus will not be included.

docs.title: 3.5 Suspending delivery
docs.text:
Occasionally, you may need to temporarily pause insulin delivery using the Suspend function — for example, if you do not need insulin infusion for a short period or you need to remove the pump from the infusion set. From the Home Screen, tap Suspend to stop insulin delivery. A confirmation dialog box will appear; select OK to suspend or Cancel to exit. You may also choose to stop delivery and allow the pump to rewind. Keep in mind that if the pump rewinds, you will need to prime the reservoir again before resuming use.

Once Suspend mode is activated, the PDA returns to the Home Screen, and the information area will show how long insulin delivery has been paused. The Suspend/Stop button will change to a Resume button.

To restart insulin delivery, tap Resume. When the confirmation dialog box appears, select OK to resume insulin delivery.

docs.title: 3.6 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• At pump start-up, specific bolus settings are configured using the Settings Wizard, in consultation with your healthcare provider. These settings can be adjusted later from the Home Screen > Settings > Insulin Delivery Settings > Bolus Rate.

  • The normal bolus delivery rate is 3.0 units per minute. This can be reduced to 1.5 units per minute if needed.

  • The Bolus Increment setting determines how much the requested bolus changes each time you press the plus or minus button. It can be set between 0.1 and 2 units.

  • The Maximum Bolus setting defines the largest single bolus dose the pump can deliver. This safety feature helps reduce the risk of overdose due to user error. It is recommended to set this value to the largest bolus you would normally administer. Setting it too high increases the risk of overdosage. You can also configure the Extended Bolus option. By default, this feature is turned off and the corresponding button appears grayed out. Once enabled, you can choose how much insulin is delivered immediately, either in insulin units or as a percentage of the total bolus dose.

  • The final setting is Quick Bolus, which is inactive by default. Select the checkbox next to Quick Bolus to enable it. The Quick Bolus Increment determines how much the bolus increases with each press of the Quick Bolus button.

  • When you open the Basal screen for the first time, no basal program is active. If you activate one of the three basal programs, the previously running basal rate will stop automatically.

  • The Initial Basal Setup provides only an estimate of daily basal needs. For individualised dosing, consult your healthcare provider.

  • You cannot switch between basal programs in Graph Display mode. Return to List Display to select a different basal program.

  • If you edit a basal program that is currently running on the pump, a dialog box will ask whether you want to send the updated program to the pump. Select OK to update and save, or Cancel to return to the Basal Edit screen.

  • The maximum number of increments for a Quick Bolus is 20.

  • To cancel a Quick Bolus using the PDA, press the Back button, or wait 10 seconds for the system to return to the Lock Screen.

  • If there is no activity for 10 seconds, the pump will exit Quick Bolus mode automatically. To cancel a Quick Bolus using the pump, simply wait 10 seconds for the system to exit Quick Bolus mode.

docs.title: Nice work! You now know how to manage the Equil Pump’s key insulin-delivery functions, including basal and temporary basal rates, bolus options, the Bolus Calculator, and suspending delivery. Coming up, we’ll cover replacing the insulin reservoir and infusion set. See you in the next lesson.
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (8 min)
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (8 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on replacing the Equil Insulin Pump reservoir and infusion set.

In this video, we’ll walk through the full process of installing the Equil Pump, starting with the necessary preparations and attaching the pump base. Next, we’ll discuss how to fill the insulin reservoir, assemble the pump, and complete reservoir and cannula priming as required. We’ll then explain how to replace an empty reservoir and finish with important do’s and don’ts. Let’s get started.

docs.title: 4.1 Preparing for pump installation
docs.text:
To replace an Equil patch pump, gather the following supplies:

  • an alcohol wipe,

  • a pump base pack,

  • a cannula pack,

  • your cannula inserter,

  • an insulin reservoir pack,

  • an insulin vial or cartridge,

  • a fully charged pump battery,

  • and your PDA.

Before beginning the pump installation process, wash your hands thoroughly with soap and water, or disinfect them using alcohol. Then select an appropriate infusion site from the recommended areas, which include the abdomen on either side of the navel, the back of the upper arm, the thighs, or the lower back. Once a suitable site has been selected, clean the area with an alcohol wipe and allow it to dry completely without using a towel or tissue.

docs.title: 4.2 Attaching the pump base to the skin
docs.text: Open the pump base pack, remove the protective covering, and place the pump base on the selected infusion site. Next, open the cannula pack as indicated on the packaging. Insert the cannula assembly into the cannula inserter until it reaches the cocked position and you hear two clicking sounds. Once the inserter is securely cocked, remove the protective sleeve. Hold the cannula inserter and align its front end with the lower protruding section of the pump base, as shown in the video. Press the back of the inserter until you hear a click, confirming that it is locked in place. To insert the cannula, press the two orange release buttons on both sides of the inserter at the same time. The cannula will be inserted into both the pump base and the infusion site. After insertion, release the inserter by pressing the button at the lower end and lifting it away. The pump base should now appear as shown, indicating successful cannula insertion. If the needle remains in the pump base, carefully remove it from the infusion site and dispose of it according to safety guidelines. Normally, however, the needle remains inside the inserter. In this case, replace the protective sleeve over the needle hub, press firmly on the top of the inserter to eject the needle hub, and dispose of it safely.

docs.title: 4.3 Filling the insulin reservoir
docs.text: Remove the insulin reservoir from its packaging. The reservoir is fitted with a plastic component called the fill adapter, which is used during filling. Before filling the reservoir, pull the pull rod fully out and then push it back in. Repeat this action three times to ensure smooth operation. Clean the insulin vial or cartridge with an alcohol wipe and attach it to the fill adapter. With the vial positioned on top, slowly pull the pull rod to draw insulin into the reservoir. Gently tap the reservoir to allow any air bubbles to rise to the top. Slowly push the pull rod to expel the air back into the vial, then pull again to draw more insulin. Repeat this process until the reservoir contains enough insulin and no visible air bubbles remain. Remove the insulin vial from the fill adapter. Pull the two release tabs outward to detach the fill adapter, and carefully unscrew the pull rod counterclockwise without pushing or pulling. Before proceeding, ensure that the pump battery is fully charged.

docs.title: 4.4 Assembling the pump and priming
docs.text: Assemble the pump and reservoir in the correct orientation, then press them together until you hear a click. If this is the first time you are installing the pump, you will need to complete the initial setup, including setting the time and date. This process is covered in Lesson 2: Pump Operation. Continue through the Settings Wizard until you reach page five, then select Finish. From the Home Screen, tap Actions, select Change Pump, and press OK to continue. Enter the pump serial number printed on the pump. The PDA will then attempt to connect to the pump, and an animation will indicate when the connection is successful. Select Continue. Hold the pump in the position shown in the video, then tap Prime Reservoir. When you see an insulin drop at the tip of the reservoir needle, confirm this by selecting OK. The Continue button will turn from gray to green; tap Continue. Attach the pump to the pump base in the correct orientation and press it into place until you hear a click. You will then be prompted to choose whether to prime the cannula or skip this step. Cannula priming is required only when changing the pump base, as a new cannula must be primed. If you are changing only the reservoir or battery, select Skip to avoid delivering unnecessary insulin. For demonstration purposes, let’s select Skip. After the pump connects, it is ready for use.

docs.title: 4.5 Replacing the insulin reservoir
docs.text: When the insulin reservoir is empty, or when replacing the battery, follow the same procedure. From the Home Screen, select Actions, then Change Reservoir/Battery. An animation will prompt you to remove the pump from its base. Remove the pump and select Continue. A confirmation message will appear; select OK. The pump will reconnect and begin retracting the push rod to its original position. Wait until the rod is fully retracted before separating the pump from the reservoir, as failure to do so may damage the pump. Hold the pump as shown, with the printed side facing upward, and carefully separate the pump and reservoir. Dispose of the used reservoir according to safety instructions. Fill a new reservoir as previously demonstrated. Ensure the battery is fully charged, and assemble the battery correctly within the reservoir. Reattach the pump and reservoir, select Continue, and hold the pump in the indicated position. Select Prime Reservoir and wait for an insulin drop to appear, then confirm by selecting OK, and tap Continue. Finally, attach the pump to the base following the same steps as before. Choose whether to prime the cannula as appropriate, then select Finish.

docs.title: 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (8 min)
4.6 Do’s & don’ts
docs.text:
• Infusion sets consist of two components: the cannula pack and the pump base. The most commonly used cannula is the HRN-S-60, a straight 6 mm Teflon cannula. Other available options include the HRN-S-90, HRN-H-45, and HRN-H-65 cannulas.

  • When pressing the release button, keep your other hand firmly pressed against the bottom of the cannula inserter. Ensure the baseplate does not lift.

  • The fill adapter contains a needle. Dispose of it properly.

  • During disassembly, pay close attention to the pump’s orientation. Incorrect positioning or disassembly may cause device damage.

  • Always use a fully charged pump battery. A low battery may force more frequent reservoir changes, which can result in wasted insulin.

  • Skip Prime Cannula only if the infusion set has not been replaced, meaning the cannula does not need to be filled.

You now have the core skills to install the Equil Pump and handle key tasks such as attaching the pump base, preparing and replacing the insulin reservoir, and completing priming as required. If anything feels unclear, take a moment to review the steps carefully and repeat the procedure if necessary. For support, contact your healthcare provider or authorized pump trainer. Up next, we’ll take a closer look at the Equil Pump alarm system. See you again soon.

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms (4.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on Equil Insulin Pump alarms.

In this video, we’ll break down how the Equil Pump and PDA work together to notify you when attention is needed. We’ll review the alarm types you may encounter and finish with practical tips for responding to alarms. Let’s jump right in.

docs.title: 5.1 Available alarms
docs.text:
When an issue is identified, the system can generate alarm notifications through sounds, LED indicators, or vibrations. It also provides on-screen details on the PDA. The system operates as a two-part setup: the pump detects events and triggers the alarms, while the PDA receives those alarms and delivers the user notifications. Medium-priority alarms occur when insulin delivery has stopped due to a technical problem, meaning you must intervene — for instance, troubleshooting, replacing the pump, or, if necessary, administering insulin manually to manage blood glucose. Meanwhile, low-priority alarms indicate that an event is likely to occur soon, but insulin delivery continues as normal; these alerts are intended to keep you informed so you can plan ahead and maintain reliable therapy.

Here’s an overview of the PDA and pump alarm priority levels, along with their corresponding visual, audio, and vibration signals. Note that the pump does not generate audio alerts, while the PDA does, at a sound pressure level of 60 to 90 dB.

Regarding pump alarms, you may receive medium-priority alerts when there is no insulin remaining, a blockage is detected, insulin delivery stops unexpectedly, or the pump battery is depleted. In these situations, you will need to take the appropriate corrective action before insulin delivery can resume. Low-priority alarms, on the other hand, occur when there is not enough insulin left to deliver a full bolus, or when the pump battery level is running low. These alerts give you time to prepare a new reservoir or a fully charged battery so that therapy can continue without interruption.

Your PDA will issue alarms for the same pump issues, using both LED and vibration signals. In addition, you may receive a low-priority alarm for a PDA failure. In that case, you should restart the device, and if the problem persists, contact your supplier for a replacement.

Because the alarm system is split between the pump and the PDA, there is a built-in delay between detection and notification. First, a safety sensor inside the pump detects a problem in less than a tenth of a second. Next, the pump generates its own alarm signal almost immediately, so the pump can alert you within about two-tenths of a second. The pump then sends the alarm information wirelessly to the PDA. This transfer can take up to four seconds. As soon as the PDA receives the information, it triggers its own alarm, again in less than a tenth of a second. In short, the pump responds almost instantly, while the PDA typically follows within four seconds.

On a final note, alarm settings on the Equil Pump — including alarm volume — are fixed and cannot be adjusted.

docs.title: 5.2 Tips
docs.text:
• The system does not provide alarms for expired/old insulin or insulin leakage. You should remain alert to these situations and respond appropriately if they occur.

  • If the pump and PDA power on normally and no alarms are triggered, this indicates the alarm system is functioning properly.

  • If the Auto-Off function is enabled, insulin infusion may stop automatically according to the preset configuration.

  • When a medium-priority alarm occurs, the pump will rewind automatically and stop all insulin delivery. A pop-up message will appear on the PDA with details and suggested actions. Tap OK to close the pop-up. Replace the reservoir to re-initialize the system and resume delivery.

  • Low-priority alarms appear once only and do not repeat.

Well done! You’re now familiar with how Equil Pump alarms work and what different alerts are designed to communicate. If an alarm occurs, stay calm and follow the instructions shown on the screen. If you need additional guidance, refer to the user manual or contact customer support. You’re well prepared to move forward.

docs.title: 6. Case report (1 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to the closing video of this module. Thank you for following along and dedicating time to understanding the Equil Insulin Pump.

Now that you’ve established the essentials, it’s time to see the Equil Pump in action. For a practical look at how it works in everyday use, we recommend this YouTube video:

https://youtu.be/BVJqXFQ-OCA?si=bnrlMukWCTcJxoWe

In the video, Maike highlights the Equil Pump’s key features and provides a step-by-step demonstration of three core functions: using the Bolus Calculator, setting a temporary basal rate, and canceling active programs.

The video is in Dutch, but YouTube provides English subtitles if you don’t speak the language.

By pairing real-world insights with the concepts covered in this module, you can apply what you’ve learned with greater clarity and consistency.

Remember that your dedication matters — it helps move you toward more consistent time-in-range and more effective diabetes management over the long term.

Congratulations on completing this module. Stay the course, and take pride in the progress you’re building.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. How the iLet Device works
  • 3. Insulin setup
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this informative video on the iLet Device! This insulin pump is used in conjunction with iLet Dosing Decision Software, which combines it with a Dexcom G6 or G7 sensor for advanced diabetes management.

In previous general insulin pump videos, you learned the fundamentals of how insulin pumps work, setting basal and bolus insulin, changing insulin and infusion sets, and handling alarms.

In the following videos, we will specifically focus on the iLet insulin pump, providing a comprehensive start-up education for this innovative pump. These explanations will benefit healthcare professionals guiding individuals using the iLet Device and offer support for those initiating the use of the iLet Device. Please note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. It is crucial to consult with your healthcare provider before making any therapy adjustments.

The videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr. Diana Isaacs, the Director of Education in Training in Diabetes Technology at Cleveland Clinic Diabetes Center and Chelsea Lugone, a certified diabetes educator living with type 1 diabetes in the US who has firsthand experience with the iLet Device. Their expertise and personal experiences ensure that the information presented in the videos is comprehensive and unbiased.

By watching these videos, you will gain a clear understanding of:

  • How the iLet Device operates

  • How to setup the pump to start dosing basal insulin and give meal boluses

  • How to change the insulin reservoir and infusion set

  • And what actions to take in case of pump alarms

As a healthcare professional, these videos will equip you with the knowledge to better guide individuals using the iLet Device. For iLet Device users, this resource will assist in preparing for a successful start with the pump, while also providing valuable tips and tricks to make life with the iLet Device even more manageable.

Wishing you the best of luck on your journey with the iLet Device, whether as a healthcare professional or an individual with diabetes.

docs.title: 2. How the iLet Device works (7.5 min)
2. How the iLet Device works (7.5 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this informative video on how the iLet Device works! In this segment, we will explore the various aspects of the pump, including its parts, buttons, charging process, unlocking mechanism, and the different screens and menus.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts & buttons on the pump
docs.text:
Let's start with an overview of the parts and buttons on the pump. The iLet pump has a sleek design. At the front of the pump, you'll find a large touchscreen that allows for easy navigation.

At the top of the pump, there's a single button for putting it to sleep or waking it up with just a light touch.

On the bottom left side, you'll find a compartment for the insulin reservoir, called the "iLet Cartridge." This cartridge connects to a plastic luer connector, known as an “iLet Connect”, ensuring a sealed connection between the pump and the insulin tubing and infusion set.

The pump works with prefilled insulin pump cartridges or you can manually fill the iLet Cartridges with insulin, using a needle and syringe.

At the back of the pump, you can find the serial number and 2 small holes designed for attaching the pump clip. With this “iLet clip” you can attach the pump to your clothes.

Additionally the pump comes with a black soft pump case, providing protection for the device.

docs.title: 2.2 Charging the pump
docs.text:
The iLet Device contains a rechargeable battery that is not replaceable. It can be charged using a dedicated inductive charger that comes with the device. Only use the power adapter and USB cable provided with the iLet Device.

Connect the charger to the power adapter with the micro-USB cable and plug it into an electrical outlet. Place your iLet Device onto the charger. If the charger status light is solid and the battery indicator animates on the touchscreen, your iLet Device is charging properly.

If the charger status light is blinking, your iLet Device is not charging properly. Remove it from the charger for at least 10 seconds and then place it back on the charger.

To optimize battery life, charge the battery daily. It typically takes around 2 hours to fully charge a depleted battery, providing 4 to 5 days of usage when fully charged. Battery life depends on factors such as usage intensity, backlight, and insulin delivery.

The iLet remains operational and keeps delivering insulin while charging, although it might not be convenient to stay connected to the pump while it is charging.

To minimize disruption to insulin therapy, consider charging during activities like bathing or showering to maintain an optimal battery level.

docs.title: 2.3 Unlocking the pump
docs.text:
To unlock the pump, simply tap the Sleep/Wake button to turn on the touchscreen and backlight. Tapping the button can also turn off the screen if desired.

Drag the Unlock slider to the right to unlock the touchscreen.

The touchscreen will automatically go to sleep after 45 seconds of inactivity. During this time, your iLet will continue dosing insulin, the display will continue to provide basic status information, but the touchscreen cannot be activated by finger taps.

docs.title: 2.4 Home screen and pump menu
docs.text:
Let's now delve into the home screen and pump menu of your iLet Device.

The home screen presents a variety of information.

  • At the top, you'll find the status bar, indicating the pump's time, paired sensor or phone status, any alerts, and the device's battery level.

  • Below this, there are three icons granting access to the main menu, insulin cartridge details and management, and notifications. If there is a number displayed in the notification bell icon, it indicates the number of alerts that acquire your attention.

  • In the center, your current glucose level and trend arrow are displayed. Below 40 mg/dl or 2.2 mmol/l, it will indicate LOW, and above 400 mg/dl or 22.2 mmol/l, it will show HIGH. If the sensor connection is lost, three dashes appear, and during warming up, the warming up time is shown. The circle around the glucose status spins when the iLet is operational.

  • Finally, at the bottom of the home screen, there's a meal announcement button where you can specify the meal type and estimated size.

Tapping the menu icon reveals seven icons for different submenus.

  • The blood glucose meter icon allows entry of fingerprick blood glucose for sensor calibration or when there's no active sensor session.

  • The pause insulin icon can be used to pause and resume insulin delivery.

  • The Dexcom sensor icon facilitates sensor pairing, alert adjustments, and viewing sensor information.

  • The phone icon enables pairing with a compatible device via the iLet Mobile app.

  • The cogwheel icon grants access to additional device information and settings.

  • The clock icon displays your history, including alerts, meal announcements, cartridge and infusion set changes, algorithm steps, and insulin history.

  • Finally, the microphone icon allows volume selection. Even when set to Off, the iLet still vibrates.

You can close the menu by tapping on the X.

Tapping on the glucose status leads to a graph screen displaying recent glucose data and insulin delivery. You can switch between 3, 6, 12, and 24-hour views using the left and right arrows. Zoom in by tapping anywhere on the graph, and tap again to zoom out.

If you tap the chart icon within the graph screen, a summary of your glucose control and total daily insulin dose appears. You can switch between 1, 7, 30, and 90-day views using the left and right arrows. To return to the Graph screen, tap the graph icon in the upper righthand corner.

docs.title: 2.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few important tips to keep in mind:

  • You can find a comprehensive menu overview in the downloads section, which can be saved for easy reference when providing remote guidance as a healthcare provider.

  • The iLet Device is waterproof for 30 minutes until a depth of 3.6 meter or 12 feet. However, it is recommended to remove the iLet Device for all water related activities like swimming.

  • The pump comes with a soft black pump case and iLet clip. If you want to charge the pump, you’ll need to remove the clip. The pump case however can stay on the device.

  • For individuals bothered by the glare on the iLet Device screen, an anti-glare screen protector offers a solution. Additionally, some users have found the iLet clip lacking in strength and have chosen to upgrade to a sturdier clip, such as the one available from EDC Solutions (https://edcsolutionstype1.com/).

  • The iLet Device does not automatically adjust the time and date. So when changing between winter and summer time, or when traveling to a different time zone, you have to adjust the time yourself. You can do that via the settings.

Congratulations! You now have a good understanding of how the iLet Device works, including its parts, charging process, unlocking mechanism, and the different screens and menus. In the next video, we will delve into setting insulin delivery, so stay tuned!

docs.title: 3. Insulin setup (5.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to our comprehensive guide on setting up insulin delivery for the iLet Device.

With the iLet Device, you won't need to configure basal delivery rates, carb ratios, or insulin sensitivity settings. The integrated iLet Dosing Decision Software handles all insulin delivery automatically. The specifics of how this algorithm calculates insulin delivery are explained in the module dedicated to the iLet System.

In this lesson, we'll cover how to set up the iLet Device using only your weight and how to administer insulin boluses for meals.

docs.title: 3.1 Initial setup of iLet Device
docs.text:
When you first use your iLet Device, it will prompt you to:

  • Set the time and date,

  • Connect your glucose sensor,

  • And install your insulin cartridge and infusion set.

The final step is to input your weight. Once done, you can activate insulin delivery and "Go Bionic".

If you need to stop insulin delivery, for example to prevent hypoglycemia during exercise, you can tap pauze insulin from the menu. First you will need to select when you want to be reminded to resume insulin delivery.

docs.title: 3.2 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
The sole method for delivering an insulin bolus is by announcing a meal. It's recommended to do this only when you're about to eat, rather than to correct high blood sugar levels, as all correction insulin is automatically delivered by the system.

Announce your meal at the start of your eating session. You can do this up to 30 minutes after you begin eating. If you forget to announce your meal and more than 30 minutes have passed since you ate, refrain from announcing your meal to prevent insulin "stacking".

To announce a meal, access the Meal Announcement button on the home screen. Select the meal type: breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Then indicate the meal size: usual, more than usual, or less than usual.

For the majority of your meals, choose "Usual." Opt for "Less" if your meal has approximately half the carbs of your usual meal, and select "More" if your meal contains around 50% more carbs than your usual meal. If the meal or snack contains less than one quarter of the carbs in your usual meal, announcing it is unnecessary. Individuals consuming very few carbohydrates in a meal or snack, for example less than 15 grams of carbs for an adult, do not need to announce the meal or snack to the iLet.

docs.title: 3.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• The iLet delivers insulin boluses at an even rate of one unit every five seconds. Therefore, a dose of 6 units will take 30 seconds to deliver. The speed of insulin delivery cannot be adjusted.

  • The maximum meal dose is 30 units. There is no limit on the number of meals that can be announced per day.

  • Maintain consistency in selecting meal sizes within each meal type. Choose "Breakfast" for breakfast, "Lunch" for lunch, and "Dinner" for dinner.

  • Announce snacks as meals if they contain as many carbs as a regular meal. If a snack matches the carb content of your usual meals for that meal type, announce it as a meal. If a snack doesn't meet the carb criteria for your "Less" meal type, refrain from announcing it as a meal.

  • Only announce meals containing carbs; do not factor in protein or fat. So although users don’t need to count carbs, they’ll need to be carbs aware to understand the portion of carbs in their meals. If you follow a very low carb diet, consider not announcing meals altogether.

  • For coursed meals, the user can announce appropriately for each course. For very large meals the user may need to announce more than one "usual for me" meal.

  • It's optimal to announce meals and snacks just before eating, or within 15 minutes prior up to 30 minutes after starting to eat. Avoid using meal announcements to correct high blood sugar levels, or to announce carbs used to treat lows.

  • To prevent over-treating post-meal lows and aid the iLet in learning, avoid over-correcting low blood sugar levels that occur after meals.

  • Initially, the "Less" meal option may not be available as the iLet learns your insulin needs for your "Usual for me" meal. Once the iLet learns your requirements for each meal type, the "Less" option becomes available. If the Less option is not yet available and you are eating less carbs than your Usual for me meal, do not announce that meal.

  • In the first few days, consume meals within the "Usual for me" carb range and wait at least 4 hours before eating and announcing again. This helps the iLet learn your insulin needs for your typical meals.

  • If your glucose is low before a meal, treat the low with rapid-acting carbohydrates before eating and announcing the meal. Don't include carbs used to treat lows in your meal announcement size and avoid announcing your meal as smaller than it actually is to receive less insulin as a "reverse correction."

Congratulations! You're now equipped with the knowledge to effectively manage insulin on the iLet Device. In our next segment, we'll walk you through changing the insulin reservoir and infusion set.

docs.title: 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (13.5 min)
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (13.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on reservoir and infusion set replacement on the iLet Device.

In this video, we will cover the different infusion set options, step-by-step instructions for replacing the insulin cartridge and infusion set, and helpful tips for success.

4.1 Different infusion sets

The Inset 1 infusion set is the most widely used infusion set for the iLet Device. It has a Teflon cannula of 6 mm in a straight angle

If you prefer a steel cannula, you can opt for the Contact Detach infusion set. A detailed overview of these infusion sets, including tubing and cannula lengths and filling volumes, can be found in the download below this video.

We’ll demonstrate changing the insulin reservoir and infusion set using the Inset 1 infusion set. For demonstration videos of the Contact Detach infusion sets, please check the link below this video.

Contact Detach => https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8xtQm11BeKk

docs.title: 4.2 Insulin cartridge and infusion set replacement
docs.text: Start by washing your hands and ensuring your workspace is clean, and gather these items: your iLet Device, an infusion set with tubing, an iLet Connect luer lock, some alcohol wipes and your insulin at room temperature. This can be a prefilled insulin cartridge for example of 160 U of Fiasp, or it can be your insulin vial with an iLet Cartridge of 180 U, syringe and needle.

docs.title: #1 Reservoir replacement
docs.text:
To fill the iLet Cartridge with insulin, gather the following supplies: an Islet Cartridge, a syringe and a needle, a vial of Humalog or Novolog or Fiasp insulin, and an alcohol wipe. If you are using prefilled Fiasp cartridges, you can skip this step.

First, wipe the top of the insulin vial with an alcohol wipe and let it dry. Next, open the iLet Cartridge package and remove the empty iLet Cartridge from the packaging. Open the syringe package and remove the syringe from the packaging. Last, open the needle package and remove the needle from the packaging.

Attach the syringe to the needle by twisting one onto the other. Remove the needle guard without touching the needle. Pull back the plunger of the syringe. Make sure the plunger is at the 1.8 milliliter mark, which is about halfway between 1.5 and 2 milliliter marks. Insert the syringe into the insulin vial while the vial is placed upright on a table or countertop. Push down the syringe plunger rod to fill the vial with the air from the syringe. Without letting go of the plunger rod, invert the insulin vial and syringe.

Carefully and slowly, pull back the syringe plunger rod to fill the syringe with the amount of insulin you require in 2 to 3 days but no more than 1.8 milliliters. Remove any air remaining in the syringe. Pull back as needed, again, to fill the syringe to about 1.8 milliliters.

To fill the iLet cartridge, place the base of the empty cartridge upright on a flat surface. Hold the cartridge with one hand. With the other hand, insert the syringe needle into the empty cartridge septum. Press down slowly on the syringe plunger rod to transfer the insulin from the syringe into the cartridge. It's important not to overfill the cartridge. The rubber plunger in the back might get pushed out if too much insulin is transferred. If this happens, dispose of the cartridge and use a new one. The cartridge does not hold more than 1.8 milliliters of insulin.

Remove as much air from the cartridge as you can by pulling back slightly on the syringe plunger rod. Once there are no air bubbles remaining inside the cartridge, remove the syringe and discard the syringe needle in a sharps disposal.

When filling the insulin cartridge, remember to keep the needle straight and do not angle the needle to try to remove bubbles on the sides of the cartridge. This can tear the septum of the cartridge, which can cause leaking of insulin. If the septum tears, discard the cartridge and restart the process. If there is still a bubble present near the top of the cartridge, slowly withdraw the syringe needle from the cartridge while maintaining pressure on the plunger. The needle tip should now be surrounded by the air bubble. Pull back on the plunger until the air bubble has been removed.

It's important to remember to be gentle when filling your insulin cartridge. Do not insert the needle too many times or wiggle the needle around too much while it is inserted in the cartridge. These things can tear the septum, which will cause leaking of insulin into the iLet. Take care to remove large bubbles from your cartridge, but lots of tiny little bubbles are okay and will be primed out once you prime the tubing.

Do not attach your filled insulin cartridge to your iLet Connect or insulin tubing yet. These should never be attached outside of the iLet.

First, you need to prepare your iLet for your cartridge and prepare your insulin tubing. Remember not to insert your filled cartridge into the iLet until it has completely rewound.

To prepare your insulin tubing, pull the red tape to remove the tamper-proof seal from the Inset infusion set. Remove the sterile paper. To remove the lid, firmly press the three raised dots on each side while lifting the lid up. Be careful not to bend or touch the introducer needle when preparing the infusion set. The needle should come placed securely in the inserter, pointing straight, not at an angle or loose. If it is not straight or if it is loose and insertion is attempted, pain or minor injury may occur. To avoid this, please ensure that the following steps are done gently so the infusion set is not loosened or removed by accident. Unwind the tubing by carefully unlocking the tubing from the slot. Now unwind the tubing from the inserter by pulling it carefully upwards. Be careful not to touch the introducer needle while unwinding the tubing or pull too hard when the tubing is fully released, as this could accidentally remove the infusion set from the inserter. Connect the iLet Connect Luer adapter and insulin infusion set flexible tubing by twisting one into the other until tight. Make sure this connection is straight and tight.

Once the insulin tubing is ready, prepare the iLet Device for the new insulin cartridge. From the home screen, select the Insulin Cartridge icon. Select Change Cartridge & Tubing. Disconnect any current insulin infusion set tubing from your body. Allow the iLet Device to complete the rewind process. Then remove the old insulin cartridge and attached tubing. Only insert the insulin cartridge once the rewind process is complete.

Insert the new insulin cartridge into the insulin chamber of the iLet Device. After the cartridge has been inserted, insert the iLet Connect into the chamber of the iLet Device to lock the filled insulin cartridge into place. While continuing to push the iLet Connect into the chamber, rotate the connector a quarter turn to the right until the iLet Connect is fitted tightly.

docs.title: #2 Filling the tubing
docs.text:
Once the new insulin cartridge is in place, tap “Go to Fill Tubing” on the iLet Device screen. Press and hold the “Press & Hold” button until the tubing is filled, clear of bubbles, and you see drops at the end of the insulin infusion set. Ensure that there are no air bubbles remaining in the tubing. When filling the infusion set tubing, hold the infusion set with the needle pointing down to ensure that insulin does not get in contact with the adhesive backing paper. Once the insulin tubing is filled and you see drops at the end, tap “Yes” on the screen to complete the process.

The pump will now ask if you also need to replace your infusion set.

  • If you do not need to insert a new insulin infusion set base, select “No” and connect the new insulin infusion set tubing to the insulin infusion set base already on your body.

  • If you do need to insert a new insulin infusion set base, select “Yes”.

docs.title: #3 Infusion set placement
docs.text: Now, we're ready to apply the infusion set. Pull up gently on the white tab to remove the adhesive backing paper. Place your fingers on the lined indentations on each side. Pull the spring back firmly until you hear a click. Carefully remove the needle guard by gently twisting and then pulling it up, taking care not to touch the needle itself. Check that the soft cannula does not exceed the introducer needle. Before positioning the inset infusion set over a clean insertion site, place the tubing in the slot to ensure that the tubing is not caught underneath the infusion set during insertion. Insert the inset infusion set by positioning it on the clean insertion site. Simultaneously, press the round indentations on each side of the inserter to insert the inset infusion set. Push gently on the center of the inserter to secure the adhesive on the skin. Remove the inserter and introduce a needle by grasping the center and pulling it gently back. Gently massage the adhesive tape onto the skin.

docs.title: #4 Cannula Filling
docs.text:
If a new insulin infusion set base is inserted, and your insulin tubing is connected to it, drag “Next” to go to the Fill Cannula screen.

Drag “Fill” to the right to fill the cannula. The user must select the kind of insulin set they are using. If a Teflon infusion set has been selected, “Fill Cannula” will deliver the appropriate volume of insulin to fill the cannula and reset the reminder to replace the infusion set to count down from three days. For a steel infusion set, “Fill Cannula” will not deliver any insulin (the steel cannula is filled during priming) but will reset the reminder to replace the infusion set to count down from two days.

When the cannula is done filling, you’ll see a screen confirming the process is complete! Nice job! You've now successfully replaced the insulin reservoir and infusion set!

4.3 Do's & don’ts

  • The insulin approved in the iLet Device is Novolog, Humalog and Fiasp.

  • The insulin reservoir or insulin cartridge needs to be replaced when it runs out of insulin or every 3 days for teflon infusion sets, or every 2 days for steel infusion sets, whichever occurs sooner. There is no hard shutdown of your iLet Device, but you’ll get a notification. The iLet Connect and infusion set tubing need to be replaced every time you replace your iLet insulin cartridge. The iLet insulin cartridge, iLet connect, and tubing should always be replaced together. None of these components should be reused, and they should not be separated once connected.

  • Your infusion set needs to be replaced every 3 days for teflon cannulas or every 2 days for steel cannulas, even if the reservoir is not empty. Always replace your iLet infusion set (as well as cartridge, tubing and connector) if you have any doubt it is not working!

  • If there is a large bubble in the iLet Connect or tubing, you can use “Fill Tubing Only” via the “Cartridge icon” to prime the bubble out and ensure consistent insulin delivery. Make sure the user disconnects from their infusion site before priming the tubing.

  • The infusion set should be placed at least 7.5 cm or 3 inches apart from a Dexcom CGM, and 2.5 cm or 1 inch apart from an Abbott CGM.

  • Avoid placing the tubing in the notch of the inserter before pulling the spring-loaded inserter, as this will create tension and may result in the cannula bending. Only insert the tubing into the notch after loading the inserter.

  • Prior to disposing of the insertion device, reattach the lid until it clicks.

  • Remember, 2 hours after inserting a new set, you should check your glucose level. Checking your glucose level is the only way to confirm your infusion set is properly inserted and that you're receiving insulin.

  • The Inset infusion set allows you to temporarily disconnect your pump without having to change the infusion set. Hold the adhesive tape in place by placing a finger just in front of the canular housing and gently squeeze the sides of the connector. Pull the connector needle straight out from the canular housing. If preferred, insert the disconnect cover into the canular housing until you hear a click. To reconnect the tubing to the infusion set, remove the disconnect cover from the canular housing. Ensure the flow by filling the tubing until insulin dispenses from the needle. When filling the tubing, hold the connector needle pointing down. Place a finger on the adhesive tape just in front of the canular housing and push the connector needle straight in until you hear a click. Now you are ready to restart your insulin delivery.
