So now, you're equipped with the knowledge to replace the insulin reservoir and infusion set on the iLet Device. If you stumble, don't worry – it's perfectly normal. Keep calm, follow the steps, and remember, if all else fails, there's always the free customer service of Beta Bionics available. Also, don't forget that you can contact your diabetes team, who also have an on-call service for medical support. Good luck!

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms (4.5 min)
docs.text:
In this video, we dive into the iLet Device's alarm system. We'll discuss the different kinds of alarms, and share some best practices.

Your iLet Device will alert you when important issues need to be addressed. Alerts are categorized into high priority, medium priority and low priority alerts, recognizable by 3, 2 or 1 exclamation mark.

When multiple alerts occur at the same time, alerts with the highest priority will be displayed first. Each one requires individual confirmation until all are acknowledged.

docs.title: 5.1 High priority alerts
docs.text: The only high priority alert is an urgent low glucose alert, for which you need to take glucose immediately. This alarm goes off when your sensor glucose is below 54 mg/dl or 3.0 mmol/l and cannot be turned off. The iLet Device will alert you every 5 minutes with 20 beeps (if volume is on) and 5 vibrations. These will escalate to the highest volume level after 5 minutes. The alert will not stop until acknowledged or resolved.

docs.title: 5.2 Medium priority alerts
docs.text:
Medium priority alerts require attention as possible. Your iLet Device may have stopped delivering insulin and you need to fix the issues that trigger the alert. You may need to contact Beta Bionics for further resolution.

These alerts are expressed every 5 minutes with 12 beeps (if volume is on) and 3 vibrations. These will escalate to the highest volume level after 15 minutes. The alert will not stop until acknowledged or resolved.

Examples are Urgent Low Soon, Low Glucose, High Glucose, Glucose Falling Quickly, Enter a BG Value, Insulin Very Low, Change Insulin, Insulin Occlusion, Recharge iLet Soon, Recharge iLet Now, Change Insulin, Insulin Fill Tubing, Dosing Stops Soon and Dosing Stopped.

You can find an overview of all alerts and what to do in the manual.

docs.title: 5.3 Low priority alerts
docs.text:
Low priority alerts are reminders about an upcoming event, or an alert that a component of your iLet System may stop functioning soon. You need to acknowledge the alert by tapping the button at the bottom of the alert message.

Depending on the level, you’ll get just an alert without beeps or vibrations, or the iLet Device will alert every 5 minutes with 2 beeps and 1 vibration. These will not escalate to the next volume level.

Possible low priority alerts are Calibration Failed, Sensor Expiring, Sensor Expired, Sensor Failed, Replace Your Sensor, Sensor Reconnecting, CGM Battery Low, Pair a New Transmitter, Transmitter Not Found, Sensor Failed, Brief Sensor Issue, Pairing Failed, Calibration Not Used, Sensor Expiring, Sensor Expired, Readings Stop, Replace Sensor New, Insulin Low, iLet Setup, Insulin Set Expired and Check Body Weight.

docs.title: 5.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• A 'malfunction' alarm indicates a system fault. Take note of the fault code and contact customer service.

  • If in doubt, refer to the user manual or call customer service.

  • If you won't be using your pump for a significant period, put it in storage mode. To do this, go into Settings and choose Other, then choose Shutdown. All dosing will stop. To turn the iLet Device back on, place the iLet Device on the charger. Upon waking up if the battery has been fully depleted, the iLet Device may require you to go through the iLet Startup Sequence again. In that case, a new treatment session will be started using a new body weight entry, but all other user settings on the iLet Device will be retained.

You're now equipped with knowledge about the pump's primary alarms and what actions to take. Therefore, there's no need to panic when you hear an alarm from your iLet Device. Simply check the display screen - it usually provides instructions. If the message isn't clear, refer to the user manual or call customer service. Best of luck!

docs.title: 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2 min)
6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2 min)
docs.text:
In this final video, we'd like to spotlight Justin from Diabetech, an individual living with type 1 diabetes who actually tested the iLet Device and made an easy to follow review. In this video, Justin shows how to program the iLet device and how it looks. Please click on the link below this video to watch his review.

The iLet Bionic Pancreas - Hands-on Demo

In addition to Justin’s insightful review, we’d like to introduce the iLet simulator app, a virtual iLet Device pump available on your desktop. This simulator allows you to experiment with navigating through the pump screens and announcing meals, providing a safe and educational environment to understand your pump's functionalities better.

http://deploy-react-simapp.s3-website.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/SimulatorEntry

To make this more engaging, we challenge everyone to perform a meal announcement on the iLet simulator app and share a screenshot of this in the comments section. This interactive exercise can help you bond with others on similar journeys and applaud your progress in learning to manage your insulin pump.

By completing this course, you have gained crucial knowledge about the iLet Device and taken a significant step toward mastering your diabetes management. Remember, integrating the pump into your lifestyle isn't just about making your life more easy—it also increases your time in range, and it can hopefully help you to prevent complications and live a healthy life with diabetes.

We extend our heartfelt gratitude to you for joining us on this journey and wish you the best as you continue managing your diabetes with your insulin pump. Keep up your exceptional work and always remember: your health and well-being deserve every ounce of effort you put into managing them!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Comparison of different insulin pumps
  • 3. Insulin settings
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 6. Case story

docs.title: 1. Introduction (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this journey into the world of insulin pumps!

Insulin pumps are revolutionizing diabetes management, allowing individuals to fine-tune their insulin dosages, mimicking the operations of a healthy pancreas. Compared to multiple daily injections, insulin pump therapy is linked to reductions in HbA1c levels, fewer episodes of hypoglycemia, tighter glucose control, and typically, an enhancement in the quality of life as insulin can be dosed more easily.

Modern medical technology has brought us automated insulin delivery systems, linking your insulin pump, a glucose sensor, and an algorithm. This marvel of innovation adjusts your insulin delivery every five minutes based on sensor-detected glucose levels. These automated insulin delivery systems are so effective, that they are recommended for all people with type 1 diabetes and those with insulin-deficient diabetes.

However, transitioning to an insulin pump isn't always straightforward. A thorough understanding of how the pump operates, its usage, and potential risks is essential. Ensuring accurate and safe insulin dosing demands precise pump settings and prompt action in case of malfunctions or blockages.

This comprehensive module on insulin pumps is here to guide you. The videos are created by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, Diabetotech's founder and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent in Belgium. All content has been reviewed by Dr. Liesbeth Van Huffel, endocrinologist at the OLV Hospital in Aalst in Belgium, and Melissa Holloway from the UK and Renate Groenendijk from the Netherlands, two individuals living with type 1 diabetes.

Be assured, all the information provided here is completely independent from the insulin pump manufacturing companies, ensuring a genuine perspective on the advantages and drawbacks of different insulin pumps.

Before we delve into the details, it is important to note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your diabetes therapy.

In the next 45 minutes, you will learn:

  • The available insulin pumps and their key differences,

  • How an insulin pump works and how to set basal rates and bolus insulin ratios,

  • What to keep in mind when changing the insulin and infusion set,

  • How to respond to an alarm,

  • And how people with diabetes experience the transition from pens to a pump.

This module will equip you with insights into the typical start-up education of an insulin pump, enabling you to have more confidence to initiate or guide others with an insulin pump.

Enjoy the journey!

docs.title: 2. Comparison of different insulin pumps (8 min)
2. Comparison of different insulin pumps (8 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson, where we will explore the various insulin pumps available in the market.

In this video, we will cover tubed pumps and patch pumps, and essential considerations to keep in mind while choosing the right insulin pump for your needs.

Insulin pumps, small yet power-packed devices, carry within them a reservoir of insulin, a pumping mechanism, a tiny computer and a battery. They work with rapid-acting insulin, delivering it at a steady, low rate as basal insulin, or in larger doses as a bolus during meals or when a correction dose is needed. While the insulin used is generally Novorapid, depending on the manufacturer’s labelling and/or a healthcare professional’s discretion, Humalog, NovoRapid, NovoLog, Apidra, Fiasp, or Lyumjev may also be used.

Let's delve into the intriguing differences among various insulin pumps.

Essentially, we have two categories - tubed pumps and patch pumps.

  • Think of tubed pumps as little command devices. They come with inbuilt software, buttons, and a screen for pump control. These devices channel insulin through a length of tubing to an infusion set attached to your body, infusing it into your subcutaneous fat tissue via a cannula. Every 2 to 7 days, you need to replace the infusion set and the cannula to avoid infection and maintain control of glucose levels. The pump has to be worn 24/7, though it can be disconnected for up to 2 hours at a time for bathing, intimacy, or some types of exercise. It can be clipped to your clothing or placed beside you while sleeping.

  • On the other hand, patch pumps are streamlined units consisting of only the insulin, the pump mechanism, and a battery. They are controlled by an external remote control or potentially a mobile phone. Affixed directly to your body, a patch pump needs to be replaced every three days just like most of the infusion sets of a tubed pump. They may offer more freedom in daily activities as there's no need to worry about the risk of tubing getting caught. However, you do need to carry a remote control or mobile phone all the time. And once you apply a patch pump, you have it on in that body site for 3 days, which may be more visible than a tubed pump, depending on what outfits you wear.

docs.title: 2.1 Tubed pumps
docs.text:
Popular tubed pumps include the MiniMed 780G, Tandem t:slim X2, Tandem Mobi, Kaleido, mylife Ypsopump, iLet Device, Dana pump and the twiist Pump.

  • The MiniMed pump is the largest tubed pump, although size is usually not that big a deal if someone needs the features of a specific pump. The mylife YpsoPump and Dana pump are smaller tubed options. The Tandem Mobi, Kaleido, and twiist pumps are tubed systems that can also be worn on the body. The Tandem Mobi and Kaleido also offer shorter tubes, allowing the pump to be placed directly next to the infusion set.

  • The majority of these tubed pumps have replaceable batteries that usually last for 1 to 4 weeks. On the other hand, the Tandem, Kaleido, and iLet pumps have a rechargeable battery, offering a more environmentally friendly option, but requiring regular charging.

  • Insulin reservoirs are typically filled by hand from a vial of insulin, but the mylife YpsoPump and iLet Device can also be used with prefilled insulin PumpCarts. These PumpCarts hold only 160 units of insulin, so they need replacing more frequently than reservoirs like the MiniMed pump, which can store 300 units.

  • Tubed pumps usually have a screen and buttons to adjust insulin delivery. The Tandem t:slim X2 pump, mylife YpsoPump, and iLet device feature a touchscreen, while most other pumps rely on physical buttons for control. The Tandem Mobi, twiist pump, and Kaleido are app-controlled via smartphone, though both the Tandem Mobi and twiist pump also include a single button on the pump itself.

  • These pumps can all be integrated with a sensor or algorithm via Bluetooth, which is why they're often used in automated insulin delivery systems today. These automated insulin delivery systems are discussed in separate modules of this Diabetes technology Expert Program.

docs.title: 2.2 Patch pumps
docs.text:
Patch pumps include the Omnipod DASH and Omnipod 5, TouchCare Nano pump, and the Equil pump.

Patch pumps are operated with a remote control or with an app on your phone.

  • Always check whether your mobile phone is compatible and if the app is available in your country. For Omnipod 5 for example the app is only available in the US.

Most patch pumps are modular, with a reusable pump base that typically lasts up to 4 years, while the insulin reservoir needs to be replaced every 3 days. The Omnipod pumps are an exception, as they are fully discarded every 3 days.

The evolution in insulin pumps goes really fast. This content is updated in November 2025. For the latest updates, please visit the company's websites. Additionally, staying updated with resources like the Diabetotech blog can help you explore the evolving diabetes tech field.

2.3 Choosing an insulin pump

When choosing a pump, it's crucial to consider a few things.

  • Check which insulin pumps are covered by your health insurance. Not all automated insulin delivery systems are reimbursed in all countries or even all regions or hospitals within the same country. Also, consult your diabetes team on what technology they can offer that best suits your needs. Important considerations include the pump's ability to function as an automated insulin delivery system, your preference for a patch or tubed pump, size and weight of the pump, and needing to carry a remote control or mobile phone to control the pump.

  • It's important to protect your pump and remote control appropriately. A silicone cover and screen protector are usually included and are recommended for use. A crack in your pump means it's no longer waterproof and needs to be replaced. Insulin pump systems typically have a warranty period of 4 years.

  • Tubed pumps offer a variety of wearing options, and there's an array of helpful accessories such as customized pouches, belts, and specially-designed clothing and underwear. Many companies typically provide a belt clip or a holder at no extra cost. Remember to keep your pump within reach as you will need frequent access. Be sure to explore the range of unique and practical options available in various online specialty stores.

  • When it comes to showering or bathing, most users choose to disconnect their tubed pump at the infusion set. For intimate activities, you have the option of either letting your pump hang if your catheter is long enough, or disconnecting it. Be mindful, however, that your pump should not be disconnected for more than two hours, as this could leave you without insulin, increasing the risk of ketoacidosis.

  • Lastly, bear in mind that the main issue with insulin pumps is making sure the insulin is getting into the body safely. Most infusion sets need to be changed every 3 days to reduce the risk of an infection and keep glucose levels in control. Going without insulin because it’s not being absorbed from the infusion site can increase the risk of diabetic ketoacidosis, which is a serious health emergency.

Now you have a general feeling on how insulin pumps work, which insulin pumps are available and what the differences are. This may seem like a lot of information, but once you really get to grips with it, you will see that each insulin pump works largely the same and is reasonably intuitive. Most people on an insulin pump will be perfectly capable of operating their pump themselves, after all, they work with it 24/7. So don't be worried about starting insulin pump therapy. Think of it like using a mobile phone, once you get the hang of it, the functionality becomes second nature.

docs.title: 3. Insulin settings (13 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this comprehensive video about insulin pump settings!

An insulin pump offers an intricate way to deliver insulin. It allows you to control both your basal and bolus insulin levels with only short acting insulin.

In this discussion, we'll explore the various settings and how to set basal and bolus insulin settings as you switch from insulin pens to a pump.

It's important to note that most guidelines about insulin pump settings are opinion-based, and that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult with your healthcare provider before changing your treatment.

docs.title: 3.1 Overview of insulin settings
docs.text:
In an insulin pump, you can adjust basal insulin delivery for each half-hour or hour-long segment throughout your day.

  • In most pumps, you can set up multiple different basic profiles to select from.

  • There's also a feature to choose a temporary basal rate, which allows you to adjust your insulin release for a specific period of time.

  • You can also stop or suspend your insulin pump at any time. However, be cautious about stopping the pump for longer than two hours, as it could lead to ketoacidosis.

As for bolus insulin, the pump gives two options:

  • a manual bolus, which lets you determine the insulin amount you want to administer,

  • and a bolus from the bolus calculator, which computes the necessary insulin based on your carbohydrate intake, blood sugar level, carbohydrate ratio, insulin sensitivity, target value, and active insulin.

Furthermore, you can choose among various types of boluses like a simple bolus, an extended bolus, or a combined bolus, that could be particularly useful for meals high in fat.

docs.title: 3.2 Basal insulin rate
docs.text:
Most individuals with type 1 diabetes using insulin pens follow a basal-bolus regimen. This consists of one or two injections of long-acting insulin and three or more injections of rapid-acting insulin for mealtime carbohydrate intake.

In transitioning to an insulin pump, we generally calculate the total daily insulin dose, by making the sum of both long and short-acting insulin, and set the pump to deliver 80% of this total daily insulin dose. This reduced dose is because the insulin seems to work more efficiently via the pump, and we want to avoid hypoglycemia.

As an example, let's consider Anne, who has type 1 diabetes. Every day, she injects 45 units of Lantus before bed and on average, she has a total of 30 units of Novorapid with her meals. That's 75 units in total. If she switches to an insulin pump, she'll need to reduce her total daily insulin dose to 60 units.

This insulin amount is then divided into doses of basal and bolus insulin to be given by the insulin pump. For most people, the starting ratio of basal to bolus is about 50/50%, but it can vary depending on individual dietary habits. This means that the basal daily insulin dose will be half of the previously calculated total daily insulin dose.

Now that you know the total basal insulin dose, you can divide that by 24 and set that as a fixed hourly rate. In our previous example, we determined that Ann's total daily insulin requirement would be 60 units when switching to an insulin pump. From this, her total basal insulin need can be calculated to be 30 units per day. By dividing this amount by 24 hours, we arrive at a calculated basal insulin rate of 1.25 units per hour. Most healthcare providers are accustomed to further fine-tuning the basal rate to align with the circadian insulin needs of their patients.

  • For manual insulin pumps, it is common practice to set a lower basal insulin rate at night, for example 20% lower, and a higher basal rate during the day to better match the body's varying insulin requirements.

  • For people with known dawn phenomenon, a higher basal rate can be set between, say, 2AM and 7AM.

To evaluate basal insulin, you can monitor blood sugar levels. This can be done either with finger sticks or a glucose sensor, but using a glucose sensor makes the process easier.

  • At night, this is relatively straightforward as no additional factors like eating or exercising affect your blood sugar.

  • During the day, it's a little more complex. One way is to occasionally skip or delay a meal for a few hours and see if there's a persistent change in blood sugar levels. A rise or fall of more than 30 mg/dl (or 1.7 mmol/l), may indicate a need for basal insulin adjustment.

Once the basal rates are set, the bolus calculator parameters can also be defined.

docs.title: 3.3 Bolus calculator parameters
docs.text: Bolus calculator parameters include the carbohydrate ratio, insulin sensitivity factor (also called correction factor), bolus calculator target value, and duration of insulin action. We’ll discuss them one by one.

docs.title: Insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio
docs.text:
The Insulin-to-Carbohydrate ratio or carb ratio is the quantity of carbohydrates that can be covered by one unit of insulin. To determine the amount of insulin to bolus for a set amount of carbs, patients need to count their carbohydrate intake during meals and then divide it by the calculated carb ratio.

For example if you're going to eat 50 grams of carbs and your carb ratio is 5 grams per unit, you will need to bolus 10 units of insulin.

The ratio can be established in a few ways:

  • by employing the 450-rule,

  • through a dietician who calculates the carbohydrate intake per meal and then divides it by the amount of insulin units injected,

  • or by taking a certain percentage of the total daily insulin dose.

The 450-rule means that you can divide 450 by the total daily insulin dose to calculate the carb ratio.

For example, if Ann’s total daily insulin dose was 60 units, and using the 450-rule, her carbohydrate ratio would be 7.5 grams per unit. Hence, for a meal of 15 grams, she would need 2 units of insulin.

Alternatively, some healthcare providers use the 300 to 500-rule to calculate the carb ratio.

Another method involves a dietician calculating the actual carbohydrate intake, which is then divided by the units of insulin injected.

Lastly, one can take a certain percentage of the total daily dose for meal boluses. In the case of fixed portion meals, it is typically recommended to use 50% of the total daily insulin dose for all meals. Given that more insulin is usually required during breakfast, it can be divided as follows: 20% for breakfast, 15% for lunch, and 15% for dinner.

To evaluate the carbohydrate ratio, check the blood sugar level 3-5 hours after an insulin bolus. It should return to pre-meal levels. If it is consistently higher or lower, consider adjusting the carbohydrate ratio by 10%.

docs.title: Insulin sensitivity
docs.text:
The second parameter for the bolus calculator is insulin sensitivity. This reflects the amount the blood sugar level drops per unit of insulin, measured in mg/dl or mmol/l. The 1800-rule (or 100-rule in mmol/l) is commonly used, but a more aggressive setting could be the 1500-rule (or 90-rule), and a less aggressive setting might be the 2000-rule (or 120 to even 130-rule).

Take Ann’s case for example: with a total daily insulin dose of 60 units, her insulin sensitivity can be calculated using the 1800-rule. 1800 divided by 60 equals 30. Therefore, setting the insulin sensitivity to 30 mg/dl per unit means that a unit of insulin will decrease the blood sugar level by 30 mg/dl.

docs.title: Glucose target
docs.text:
The bolus calculator also requires a glucose target, which could be a fixed number or a target range.

Depending on the type of insulin pump, the bolus calculator may use the target value differently.

  • Most pumps will correct to the middle of the target range.

  • Some pumps, like the Omnipod DASH, also have a 'Correct Above' value, indicating when a correction will be applied.

  • Some pumps also offer an option for reverse correction, subtracting insulin if the current blood sugar level is lower than the target.

It is generally recommended to set a fixed target value or a target range of 90-100 mg/dl (5-5.5 mmol/l). However, this should be individualized.

docs.title: Duration of insulin action
docs.text:
Lastly, you need to set the duration of insulin action. This parameter determines how long the insulin stays active, and thus how much of the previous insulin bolus is still in effect. To avoid insulin stacking, it's crucial for the pump to take this into account. Typically, this is set to 3 to 5 hours.

So now you understand how to set the basal insulin and the parameters for the bolus calculator.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• On the first day of using an insulin pump, basal insulin from the insulin pens may still be present. It is recommended to inject only half the long-acting insulin the day before starting the pump. For users of Toujeo or Tresiba, it may be best to switch to Lantus a week in advance due to its shorter action profile. If unsure, it's safer to reduce the basal insulin rate when starting the insulin pump to prevent hypoglycemia.

  • When starting on an insulin pump, it's usually necessary to set a limit for insulin delivery, i.e., the maximum basal and maximum bolus. Typically, it's recommended that the maximum basal insulin delivery should be double the highest set basal insulin delivery, and the maximum bolus should be the largest bolus expected for a large meal.

  • All insulin pumps allow users to set various profiles for different situations. The number of basal profiles that can be set varies depending on the pump. Some pumps only store the basal insulin in a profile, while others, like the Tandem Pumps, also allow adjustment of the carbohydrate ratio and insulin sensitivity per profile.

  • There are also several strategies for adjusting your insulin needs with temporary basal rates.

  • These should be evaluated individually. In some cases you could consider a temporary higher basal rate, for instance:

    • a 50% higher basal rate for 8 hours after eating a high-fat meal

    • an 80% higher basal rate for when you’re ill

    • a 50% higher basal rate 3-6 hours after receiving a corticosteroid injection

    • an 80% higher basal rate from 1-2 hours before a scheduled stressful event

    • a 40% higher basal rate during prolonged inactivity, like a long car ride

    • a 50% higher basal rate in the week before your menstruation, some people also set a 30% lower basal rate during their menstruation

  • In other cases you can consider a lower temporary basal rate, for instance:

    • a 50% lower basal rate from 1-2 hours before extensive activity like housework or yard work

    • a 30% lower basal rate for 6-10 hours after exercise to prevent late onset hypoglycemia

    • or a 40% lower basal rate after consuming alcohol for 2 hours per drink

Lastly, remember to constantly monitor your blood sugar level. The insulin pump's primary advantage is the precision and customization it offers. To make the most of it, you should adjust settings based on the observed patterns. You may also need to tweak these settings frequently, at least in the beginning, to achieve optimal glucose control.

Remember that every person is different, and while these guidelines may apply to many, there's no 'one-size-fits-all' solution in diabetes management. It requires personal fine-tuning and learning by trial and error under the supervision of your healthcare provider.

Keep in mind that while insulin pumps can make management easier and more precise, they also require a deeper understanding and commitment to managing your diabetes. In some ways, it can be more demanding because of the necessary knowledge and continual adjustments.

We hope this presentation has provided you with some valuable insights about transitioning from insulin pens to an insulin pump. We encourage you to reach out to your healthcare team for further support and guidance during this transition.

docs.title: 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (8.5 min)
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (8.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson about reservoir and infusion set replacement in insulin pumps.

This tutorial will guide you through the available infusion sets, essential steps of replacing your insulin reservoir and infusion set properly, and adverse infusion site reactions.

docs.title: 4.1 Different infusion sets
docs.text:
While patch pumps usually work with 1 fixed cannula, tubed pumps offer a wider selection of infusion sets and tube lengths:

  • Type of Cannula: You can choose between angled and straight plastic cannulas, or a straight steel cannula, each coming in various lengths. The standard choice is a straight plastic cannula that can remain in place for three days. However, for individuals frequently experiencing discomfort from a folded subcutaneous cannula or who have allergies, a steel cannula might be a more suitable alternative. Remember, steel cannulas generally need replacement every two days instead of three.

  • Length and Angle of Cannula: Usually, the shortest cannula is selected, but if you notice insulin leakage at the infusion site, a longer or angled cannula might be a better solution. An angled cannula could be particularly beneficial for those with less subcutaneous fat or those who find straight cannulas uncomfortable.

  • Tube Length: Tubed pumps allow the option to select tubes of different lengths, adding another layer of customization to your insulin delivery system.

  • MiniMed has infusion sets that offer extended wear, lasting up to 7 days.

  • Ypsopump’s mylife orbit infusion sets come with a unique feature - the tubing can rotate 360-degrees around the skin-colored adhesive patch.

An overview of all available infusion sets for tubed pumps can be found in the download section below this video. Remember, the goal is to choose an infusion set that ensures comfort, minimizes complications, and optimizes insulin delivery. Your healthcare provider can help guide your choice based on your lifestyle, physical characteristics, and personal preferences.

docs.title: 4.2 Supplies
docs.text:
Before starting, make sure you have the following items handy:

  • Insulin: depending on the pump you use, you can use a pre-filled insulin PumpCart, or draw insulin from an insulin Penfill or Vial using a needle into the pump reservoir.

  • Infusion set and tube: These might be bundled together or separate.

  • An insertion system may be used to insert the infusion set into your skin

  • And alcohol wipes or skin prep wipes for sanitization.

docs.title: 4.3 Four-step procedure
docs.text:
The process of changing an insulin reservoir and infusion set in a tube pump generally involves four stages:

  • Refilling or swapping the insulin reservoir

  • Filling the tubing

  • Placing the infusion set

  • And filling the cannula under the skin

If you're using a patch pump, there's no tubing to fill. You will have to fill the patch pump with insulin, follow the priming process, place the patch pump on your skin and insert the cannula.

After replacing your insulin and infusion set, it's crucial to check your blood sugar level about 2 hours later or keep an eye on your glucose sensor for the next few hours to make sure the change was successful. It’s best practice to avoid changing your infusion set before bedtime unless you're prepared to wake up two hours later to check your blood sugar level.

docs.title: 4.4 Infusion site management
docs.text:
Consider these guidelines when selecting and preparing an infusion site:

  • Choose an Ideal Spot:

  • Opt for the abdomen, back of the upper arms, or lower back. Avoid the upper legs due to unpredictable absorption rates, particularly during exercise.

  • On a limb, mount the patch pump vertically. The pump's window can point either direction. For the back, abdomen, or buttocks, position it horizontally or slightly angled.

  • Maintain a minimum distance of 5 cm or 2 inch from the navel and the last infusion site. Avoid placing under belts, near bones, or on scars, injuries, and skin conditions.

  • Rotate your injection sites regularly to prevent lipodystrophy, a condition that affects the fat under the skin. Creating a simple rotation schedule will help ensure consistency and reduce the risk of developing this problem.

  • Preparation Steps:

  • Clean the skin with an alcohol wipe to ensure adherence. Allow it to air dry and ensure no soap or lotion residue or excess body hair.

  • Avoid applying sunscreen or insect repellent near the site as it could affect the pump's function.

  • For sensitive skin or allergies, opt for barrier wipes like Skin Prep or Conveen wipes, instead of alcohol wipes.

  • Consider additional adhesive like Skintac if the infusion set tends to come loose.

  • Slight discomfort upon insertion may occur. Ice or anesthetic cream may help, but ongoing discomfort could indicate the cannula touching a nerve or muscle, necessitating removal and reinsertion.

  • Routine Checkups:

  • Inspect your infusion site twice daily for signs of irritation or infection such as pain, swelling, redness, warmth or insulin leakage. In these cases, reposition the infusion set: "When in doubt, change it out".

docs.title: 4.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are some key points to ensure efficient insulin delivery and prevent issues:

  • The main risk associated with insulin pumps is ketoacidosis. Those new to using an insulin pump should be educated that if they experience prolonged hyperglycemia (for example more than 2 hours above 270 mg/dl or 15 mmol/l) without a clear reason, they should always consider a problem with the infusion set and not just sleep through it. The mantra is “if in doubt, change it out”.

  • Managing Air Bubbles:

  • Let insulin reach room temperature before filling the reservoir to minimize air bubbles.

  • Ensure you remove any large air bubbles during the filling process. Small bubbles won't harm, but an air bubble over 2.5 cm length equals a 0.5 unit insulin gap, so it's best to eliminate them.

  • Timely Infusion Set Replacement:

  • Replace the plastic cannula every 3 days and steel cannula every 2 days. If you’re using an Extended Wear Infusion Set, replace it every 7 days.

  • Some pumps won't halt insulin delivery after 3 days, so set an alarm to avoid missing the replacement, or purposely put just the amount of insulin you expect to use for 3 days, plus the amount to fill the tubing, into the pump, to ensure you will change the set on time. Other pumps, like the Omnipod DASH and Omnipod 5, automatically stop after a set time.

  • Carefully remove infusion sets or pods to avoid skin irritation, and clean any leftover adhesive with soap and water or a specialized product.

  • Choosing the Right Filling Volume:

  • Each type of tubing and cannula requires a specific filling volume as per its user guide.

  • Be aware that short steel cannulas might not need filling.

  • Do not overlook filling the cannula; otherwise, you may miss the initial 0.5 unit of insulin.

  • You can check the necessary filling volume of the cannula by looking at the manual of the infusion set or at the overview in the download section.

  • In the rare event of the infusion set coming loose from your body, you can use an overpatch to secure it more firmly. It is recommended to leave an opening in the overpatch to allow for disconnecting the infusion set when needed, such as during showering.

Remember, it's not uncommon to encounter issues during infusion set insertion, like a kinked cannula or premature insertion mechanism, but it's usually a minor setback. You can always restart with a new set. If you see blood post-insertion, reinsert the set. If you're out of practice, instructional videos on YouTube can be helpful. Good luck!

5. Pump alarms

Welcome to this lesson on insulin pump alarms.

We’ll discuss alarm types and some general do’s & don’ts.

Although these alarms vary across different pumps, they share certain common characteristics.

docs.title: 5.1 Alarm types
docs.text:
Insulin pumps generally categorize alarms into two priorities:

  • High-priority alarms: These include an empty insulin reservoir, depleted battery, technical faults, etc., leading to a halt in insulin delivery.

  • And Lower-priority alerts: These are typically related to a low insulin reservoir, low battery, blockages, etc. The pump continues insulin delivery but requests problem resolution.

Each alarm requires acknowledgment and action. If ignored, alarms will progressively get louder. You can often customize alarm volume and vibration settings and can temporarily silence lower-priority alarms. The pumps usually allow setting up one or more reminders.

docs.title: 5.2 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• Familiarize yourself with potential alarms to avoid panic when one occurs. Instruction manuals outline all alarms and the appropriate actions, so consider saving a digital copy on your phone.

  • Contact the pump manufacturer's customer service department if needed, keeping in mind that not all offer 24/7 support. It’s worth creating a Contact in your mobile phone for your insulin pump company, and including their hours of operation as a note on the contact.

  • Some alarms, like the "screaming pod" from Omnipod, can be tricky to deactivate without the remote control. In such cases, use a paperclip to disable the chip card through the pod's back opening.

  • If you're temporarily not using your pump, switch it to storage mode to prevent constant alarms. Each pump has its unique procedure for this, outlined in its operating instructions.

  • It's crucial to be aware that in the event of a pump malfunction, insulin delivery remains essential. To ensure preparedness, it is recommended to have your rapid- and long-acting insulin pens easily accessible as a backup. Create an emergency plan outlining the required insulin doses in case of pump failure, and make sure to update this plan regularly based on your current insulin needs. Being prepared for unexpected situations is vital, as pump problems can arise at any time.

  • Additionally, it's wise to keep a record of all your basal rates and bolus ratios in a note on your phone, which is synced to the Cloud, or email yourself a copy whenever you make any changes. This way, if you need to set up a new pump, you will have a record of your settings readily available. Taking these precautions ensures you are well-prepared to handle any potential issues with your insulin pump effectively.

Now, you're equipped with some essential knowledge about pump alarms and how to respond. If an alarm triggers, stay calm and check the screen instructions or refer to the user manual or customer service. Good luck!

docs.title: 6. Case story (3 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to our final chapter, where we have a special video for you to watch. In this segment, we hear from Andrea, an insulin pump user with over 19 years of experience managing type 1 diabetes. Andrea shares her personal transition story from using insulin pens to embracing an insulin pump, providing valuable insights into this significant change.

In the video, Andrea discusses her decision to switch to an insulin pump due to frequent low blood sugars, her initial emotional and overwhelming impressions of the pump, and the challenges she faced in the beginning, such as dealing with a kinked cannula after just two hours of training. She emphasizes that adapting to the pump can be a substantial adjustment, and feeling overwhelmed is entirely normal. Despite the initial difficulties, Andrea speaks highly of the pump, highlighting how it has significantly improved her life.

To watch Andrea's inspiring journey, click on the link below this video.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wVr89eDEKqc – 17 min

Congratulations on completing this comprehensive introduction to insulin pump therapy! Your dedication to understanding this essential aspect of diabetes management is commendable and will undoubtedly empower you to support individuals navigating their diabetes journey.

By acquiring vital knowledge, you have equipped yourself to guide and assist those living with diabetes effectively. This is truly an outstanding achievement!

Take a moment to reflect on what you've learned. When you're ready, consider exploring more about specific insulin pumps through our additional modules.

Thank you for joining us on this enlightening journey. Your commitment is making a positive difference in transforming diabetes from a life-limiting condition to a manageable aspect of daily life. Great job!

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Pump Operation
  • 4. Replacing the insulin reservoir and infusion set
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the Kaleido insulin pump! In this module, we will explore the features and operation of the Kaleido insulin pump.

This modular insulin pump can be used as a separate standalone pump, but also in an automated insulin delivery system such as DBLG1 from Diabeloop with Kaleido. This module specifically focuses on the Kaleido insulin pump, using the dedicated Kaleido handset, providing valuable insights into its usage and functionality.

The Kaleido pump is a semi-patch pump operated using a handheld device, and known for its wide range of color options. Each user is provided with two pumps and can select from ten different color combinations for their pumps and handset, offering choices from vibrant orange to classic black.

Both the handset and pumps are rechargeable and reusable for a period of four years. However, it's important to note that the insulin reservoir, or cartridge, and infusion set should be replaced at least every three days.

One of the standout features of the Kaleido pump is its compact size, making it one of the smallest durable pumps available. It can be attached to the skin using a short tube measuring 5 cm (or 2 inches), or it can be conveniently carried in a pocket with a longer tube of 30 cm (or 12 inches).

In the general module on insulin pumps, you have already learned about the fundamentals of insulin pumps, such as their operation, setting basal and bolus insulin, changing insulin and infusion sets, and managing alarms.

In the upcoming videos, we will showcase Kaleido in action, focusing on specific aspects of the onboarding education for this semi-patch pump. Please be aware that the content presented here may change without prior notification. The User Manual for the medical device remains the primary document for ensuring the safe operation, and it’s important to note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

These videos have been created by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content has been reviewed by Dr. Sarah Siegelaar, an endocrinologist at Amsterdam UMC in the Netherlands, as well as Vanessa Buitendijk form the Netherlands, an individual living with type 1 diabetes who uses the Kaleido pump. Their contributions ensure that the information provided is unbiased and independent.

By the end of these videos, you will have a comprehensive understanding of how the Kaleido insulin pump works, how to set basal and bolus insulin, how to change the insulin reservoir and infusion set, and what actions to take in case of an alarm.

Whether you are a caregiver looking to guide individuals using Kaleido, or a user preparing for a successful start-up, these videos will equip you with valuable knowledge, along with practical tips and tricks to enhance your experience with Kaleido.

We wish you the best of luck on your journey with the Kaleido insulin pump!

docs.title: 2. Pump Operation
docs.text: Welcome to this video on how the Kaleido insulin pump works! In this segment, we will explore the parts and buttons of the pump and handset, how to charge them, how to unlock the handset, the home screen and menu of the handset, and some do's and don'ts.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts & buttons
docs.text:
The Kaleido insulin pump consists of two main components: the pump and the handset, which controls the insulin delivery of the pump.

The pump is very small, weighing only 19 grams. It consists of a durable and colorful pump base that can be recharged and reused for 4 years, and a disposable insulin cartridge that can be used for 3 days. There are no buttons on the pump, only a space for the insulin reservoir, and a groove for the tubing. The cartridge has to be manually filled with insulin. The insulin cartridge comes with the tubing attached, which can be a short tubing to place the pump on your body, or a long tubing to keep the pump in your pockets. The infusion sets come in separate packaging, and should be connected to the tubing connector.

The handset is also small and weighs only 60 grams. On the bottom there is a micro USB port and a speaker. The micro-USB port is used to charge the rechargeable lithium battery and for data upload. The handset has a power button on top, and a color screen in the front. Below there are 4 buttons to navigate up and down, and left and right. And in the center there is an “enter” button. On the back of the handset, you can find specifics of the handset like the serial number.

The handset should be kept with you at all times, as it is not only necessary for delivering boluses but also for responding to alarms. Please note that there are no buttons on the pump, so control must be managed using a separate device. The handset communicates with the pump via Bluetooth and needs to be within a range of 2 meters (or 6.5 feet) to establish a connection. Once programmed, the pump does not require continuous communication with the handset for basal insulin delivery.

docs.title: 2.2 Charging the pump base and handset
docs.text:
It is advisable to charge the handset using the provided cable and charger. The optimal practice is to charge the battery each night while you sleep, ensuring a fully charged battery for approximately two days of use. When charging for the first time or when the battery is completely depleted, it may take up to 4 hours to reach a full charge.

When the battery is fully drained, the handset will power off, but the pump will continue delivering basal insulin. You can continue to use the handset while it is charging.

For each of your Kaleido pumps, which you wear for maximum three days at a time, it's a good practice to charge them promptly after removal so they're ready for your next use. This can be done by placing an empty pump into the dedicated charging dock, using the same charging cable as for your handset. An orange light will indicate the charging process. Once your pump is fully charged, the status light on the dock will change from orange to green, signifying that your pump is ready for use. Charging the pump takes about 2 hours.

To remove your pump from the charging dock, simultaneously press the release buttons on both sides of the charging dock – there are two buttons in total. This should release the pump without requiring excessive force.

docs.title: 2.3 Unlocking the handset
docs.text:
You can send your handset to sleep mode at any time by briefly pressing the Power button. This will help you save your handset battery. You can wake it up again at any time just by pressing the Power button or the Enter button.

It is also possible to set a passcode for the lock screen and an emergency message via the Systems Settings. This message functionality allows you to display the name and phone number of your emergency contact, or you can input your own name and phone number so that individuals can reach you if your device is lost.

docs.title: 2.4 Home screen and pump menu
docs.text:
The home screen is divided into five sections, each of which leads to a different menu. At the top, you’ll also see handy details like the time and how full your insulin cartridge, pump and handset batteries are.

The 5 sections are Basal, Bolus, Resource Levels, Menu and Status.

  • Once everything is set, your Basal section will show the basal rate you are currently receiving. If you need to change or review your basal profile, just navigate to Basal and select it by pressing the Enter button.

  • Your Bolus section shows you the details of the last bolus you delivered – that’s the date, time and the amount of insulin you got. When you need to deliver your next bolus, navigate to Bolus using the buttons and press Enter.

  • If you select the Resource levels, you can see the level of your handset battery, pump battery, amount of insulin in the cartridge, insulin on board, and the maximum time until the next insulin cartridge placement.

  • Via the Menu, you can check your History, do a data upload and check and edit Personal Information and System Settings. Although the handset stores up to 3 months of data, the History only shows the data of the past 8 days.

  • To pause, stop and restart your pump, navigate to Status, press Enter and go from there.

Each menu ends with a back or home option, to navigate to the previous screen or home screen.

docs.title: 2.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are some important do's and don'ts when using the Kaleido insulin pump:

  • We recommend you to save the menu overview provided in the downloads for easy reference. As a healthcare provider, it will help you guide individuals through the pump menu remotely.

  • Kaleido pumps are waterproof up to 1.5 meters or 5 feet for up to 1 hour.

  • The handset is not waterproof, so avoid exposing it to water.

  • Take good care of the handset and pumps, as it should last for approximately 4 years.

  • The handset does not automatically adjust the time, date, and time zone. You need to manually update the time when transitioning between winter and summer time or traveling to different time zones. This can be done in the system settings under the yellow menu option.

  • It’s important to note that it is not possible to change your glucose units in the Kaleido handset. There are different handsets for mg/dl and mmol/l. The type of handset you have is marked on the back.

With this information, you now have a better understanding of the main features of the Kaleido system. One of the key advantages is the small size of the pump and handset, and the fact that you can wear it on your body, without the hassle of long tubing. The pump is waterproof, but it is also possible to disconnect the pump and temporarily remove it from your body. However, keep in mind that you will need to carry both the pump and the handset, to be able to control your pump. Knowing this in advance will help you adjust and adapt.

3. Insulin setup

In this video, we will guide you through the process of setting the insulin delivery on the Kaleido handset.

Please note that the insulin will only start running once you have successfully paired a pump with your handset.

We will cover the following topics:

  • Programming basal insulin

  • Setting up temporary basal rates

  • Giving an insulin bolus

  • and some extra do’s and don'ts.

Let's get started!

docs.title: 3.1 Programming basal insulin
docs.text:
Before you can start using the Kaleido pump, you’ll need to set a basal insulin rate on your handset.

  • To do this, go to the home screen, scroll to Basal and press Enter to select. Scroll to and select Create new profile.

  • Press the left or right buttons to scroll between each hourly segment. You have to set a basal rate for all 24 hours of the day. Press the up or down buttons to select the basal rate recommended by your healthcare provider for each hour. Below the basal profile graph, you’ll see details of the hour you’re currently setting, along with the total basal insulin dose. When you’ve entered a basal rate for each hour and your new basal profile is ready, press the Enter button. Press Enter again to select Next.

  • Next up, it’s time to enter a name for your new basal profile. Use the up or down buttons to scroll to the letter or number you want to use. Press the right button to move onto the next letter or enter a space. To scroll through the alphabet quickly, just press and hold the up or down button. When you’ve named your basal profile, press Enter and select Save.

  • Your handset will confirm that your basal profile has been saved. Press Enter to return to the Home screen a little quicker.

Many people opt to use different profiles for different situations, for example work day, weekend, sports and illness. Your handset can store up to seven basal profiles at a time.

If you need to check which basal profile your pump is using, you can go to the basal section and select view current profile. Here you can also change your profile, or edit your profile. It’s important to note that it is not possible to change a profile that is currently active. To edit the current profile, you’ll need to duplicate it first.

To pause your basal insulin delivery, navigate to the Status section and tap "pause pumping," then confirm with "Yes." Your handset will display the amount of missed insulin since pausing. To resume basal insulin delivery, tap "continue pumping." If you opt to "stop pumping," your handset disconnects from the pump. To restart, access the Status screen, where your handset will ask whether you need to fill your cannula or not. Select the appropriate option, and your pump will restart insulin delivery.

docs.title: 3.2 Temporary basal
docs.text:
If you want to temporarily change the amount of basal insulin you’re receiving, you’ll need to set a temporary basal rate.

  • To do this, go to the Basal section via the homescreen, and select Set temporary basal rate.

  • Here you can set a basal insulin rate percentage between 0% to 200%. Once you have your chosen percentage, press the Enter button.

  • Set the amount of time you’d like your temporary basal rate to run for. You can choose a minimum of 0.5 hours and a maximum of 24 hours. Once you’ve chosen the amount of time, press the Enter button again.

  • Press the Enter button to select Next.

  • Your handset will now show you details of the temporary basal rate that you’ve set. If the details are correct and you’d like to start your temporary basal rate immediately, press Enter to select Deliver.

  • A confirmation will pop up. You can press Enter to return to the Home screen a little quicker.

  • To help you keep track, the Basal section of the Home screen will show you your temporary basal rate details for as long as the temporary basal rate is running.

It’s important to note that you won't receive an alert when your preset temporary basal rate ends and your normal basal profile resumes.

To stop the temporary basal, you can go to the Basal section, and select Stop temporary basal rate.

docs.title: 3.3 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
You have several options to administer a bolus using the handset. You can utilize the Kaleidoculator, which is an integrated bolus calculator. Or you can opt for a manual Quick bolus, which is a standard bolus, or a manual Extended bolus, which is a dual-wave bolus.

Before you can use the Kaleidoculator, you have to configure it via the Personal Info in the Menu section. There you can set the Target BG, Insulin to carb ratio, Insulin sensitivity, and the Duration of insulin action. For more information about these parameters, please check the general module on insulin pumps.

To deliver a bolus with the bolus calculator, navigate to the Bolus section, and choose Kaleidoculator.

  • You will first be asked to enter the grams of carbohydrate you are going to eat. You can enter for example 25 grams and tap Next. If you’re not eating, but want to use Kaleidoculator to help correct your BG, you can leave the number boxes empty and press the Enter button to select Next.

  • You’ll need to enter your current BG reading, then press next. It is also possible to give a bolus without entering a BG-reading. Once you select Next, the BG reading and amount of carbs you’ve entered will be saved in History.

  • The Kaleidoculator will show you your current Insulin on Board, that’s how many units of insulin are still active in your body. You can accept or reject the Insulin on Board to be included in your calculation.

  • The Kaleidoculator will now show you details of its recommended bolus. You have 2 options to choose from:

  • to deliver the recommendation as a Quick Bolus

  • or to deliver the recommendation as an Extended Bolus.

  • Via the next step you can also adjust the amount of insulin to be delivered.

  • If you decide you don’t want to deliver a bolus after all, just scroll left to Cancel. If you still want to give the bolus, tap Next, and then Deliver. The bolus amount that's being delivered is visible on your home screen.

With the Kaleido handset, you can also give a manual bolus without the bolus calculator. To do that, go to the Bolus section and choose Quick bolus. Here you can choose the amount of insulin you want to give and tap Next and then Deliver.

For a manual Extended bolus, you can also choose that option. First you enter the amount of insulin you wish to receive, and the amount you wish to deliver immediately, and finally the duration over which you wish to deliver the remaining bolus.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are some do's and don'ts to keep in mind:

  • Ensure your maximum bolus limit is set slightly above your expected bolus doses. You can set this between 0 and 30 units. 

  • If the Kaleidoculator recommends a bolus that is bigger than the maximum bolus, you’ll get a popup that warns you about this. If you'd like to continue with this high bolus, don’t forget to deliver another bolus of the remaining units after your first bolus has finished.

  • Sometimes, Kaleidoculator may indicate a negative amount of insulin as its recommended bolus for you. If you’re not eating or not eating very much carbs, and if your blood glucose is already below your target blood glucose, you don’t need more insulin, you actually need less. If you select either Quick Bolus or Extended Bolus when your recommended bolus is a negative number, your handset will pop up with a quick reminder to tell you that giving a bolus isn’t the safest thing to do at this time.

In conclusion, setting the insulin delivery on the Kaleido handset is a straightforward and intuitive process. As a healthcare provider, it is crucial to familiarize yourself with the different pumps and their settings. With Kaleido, you can easily access all the necessary settings under the Basal section and you can find the settings for the bolus calculator under the Personal Info in the Menu section.

docs.title: 4. Replacing the insulin reservoir and infusion set (13.5 min)
4. Replacing the insulin reservoir and infusion set
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on how to replace the insulin reservoir, or cartridge, and infusion set of the Kaleido insulin pump.

In this video, we will cover the available infusion sets, the 4-step process for changing the insulin cartridge and infusion set, and last but not least, some important do's and don'ts.

It’s important to note that if you intend to use the Kaleido pump as an Automated Insulin Delivery system with Diabeloop, you should not pair your pump with the Kaleido handset, but instead with the DBLG handset.

docs.title: 4.1 Different infusion sets
docs.text:
The Kaleido pump has one type of infusion set with a teflon cannula in a 90 degree angle. You can choose between a cannula of 6 or 9 mm.

Additionally, Kaleido provides two lengths of tubing, which are included with the insulin cartridge. Most people use the short tubing (5 cm or 2 inches) to keep the pump close to the infusion site on the skin, but you can also opt for the longer tubing (30 cm or 15 inches) to wear the pump in your pocket.

Below this video you can find an overview and comparison of the available infusion sets of different insulin pumps, their tubing and cannula lengths, and filling volumes.

docs.title: 4.2 Insulin cartridge and infusion set replacement
docs.text:
To replace the insulin cartridge and infusion set, gather the following supplies: your Kaleido handset, a charged pump, a body and pump patch, insulin at room temperature, a new insulin cartridge, a syringe and needle, an infusion set, an infusion set inserter and alcohol wipes.

It’s important to note that if you intend to use the Kaleido pump as an Automated Insulin Delivery system with Diabeloop, you should not pair your pump with the Kaleido handset, but instead with the DBLG handset.

docs.title: #1 Step: Filling the insulin cartridge and the tubing
docs.text:
Start off by washing your hands and preparing a clean flat surface to work on. Be sure to check the expiry date and ensure the packaging and insulin cartridge are not damaged. If there is any damage present or if it's expired, discard and use another one. Start by opening the packaging from your insulin cartridge by slowly pulling from the bottom. Place your insulin cartridge so that the numbers 1 and 2 are facing towards you.

Remove the syringe from its packaging. Without moving the plunger, push the tip of the syringe firmly into Hole 1. Carefully pull the plunger all the way up to the top of the syringe. You should feel some resistance. This will remove any excess air from your insulin cartridge. Now, remove the syringe from Hole 1 and push the plunger back towards the tip of the syringe.

Next, remove your needle from its packaging. Push the needle onto the tip of the syringe until they are connected. Take your room temperature insulin vial, check the expiry date and clean the rubber stopper with an alcohol wipe before use. Remove the needle cap from the needle. Draw the plunger back to fill the syringe with 2.5ml of air. Place the insulin vial in front of you onto the flat surface. Carefully push the needle through the rubber stopper of the insulin vial. Push the plunger all the way in to put all the air from the syringe into the vial. Keep holding the plunger down. Turn the vial with the syringe upside down and slowly pull the plunger down till you’ve drawn 2.5 ml of insulin. Give the syringe a few taps on the bottom to push any air bubbles up towards the needle. Push the plunger upwards to push the air bubbles back into the insulin vial. Ensure that there is 2.5ml of insulin in your syringe. Check the syringe for air bubbles, some small champagne sized air bubbles are acceptable. Remove the syringe from the insulin vial, be careful not to touch the needle. Turn the syringe so the needle is pointing downwards and give a few firm taps on the syringe. This will ensure that any air bubbles that remained in the syringe go upwards near the plunger.

Holding your insulin cartridge, gently place the needle into Hole 2. Slowly push down the plunger, filling your cartridge with insulin. You must fill your insulin cartridge completely. Stop pushing the plunger when you see a drop of insulin coming out of Hole 1 (stop before any air bubbles get transferred into the insulin cartridge). It is normal that some excess insulin remains in the syringe after filling the insulin cartridge. Remove the syringe and dispose of the needle and syringe into a sharps bin.

Now you will need to prime and then remove your insulin cartridge from the filling cradle. First, hold the top of your insulin cartridge on both of the round edges and squeeze it further into the filling cradle. This will unhook the plastic tab. Then squeeze downwards fully into the filling cradle twice and then release the insulin cartridge. It is important that this action is done twice. You can now remove your insulin cartridge from the filling cradle. Turn your insulin cartridge over. Squeeze the tabs on either side of the insulin cartridge connector and then lift up to unclip it.

Finally, have one last check to make sure there are no large air bubbles in the reservoir. If large air bubbles are present, discard and use another insulin cartridge. If they are champagne sized air bubbles, that’s generally okay and you can use this insulin cartridge. If you see any air bubbles in your insulin cartridge, check your User Manual for what is acceptable or what is not. Your insulin cartridge is now fully filled, primed and ready to use! Now it is time to prepare and wear your pump.

docs.title: #2 Step: Infusion set placement
docs.text: You’ll need to consider where your pump will be placed in relation to your infusion set. Start by washing your hands and choose where you’d like to position your infusion set. Prepare the area by cleaning it with an alcohol wipe. Allow your skin to dry naturally. Check the expiry date and ensure the packaging and infusion set are not damaged or expired before use. Peel open the paper lid. Remove the protective circle of plastic that’s sitting on top of your infusion set. Press the button on the top of your inserter to make sure it’s ready to use. Take your inserter and line it up with the top of your infusion set. You can do this by making sure the gap in the base of the inserter sits above the tabs on the infusion set’s plastic cap. Push the inserter firmly onto the top of your infusion set. Push down until you hear the Infusion set click into your inserter. Lift your inserter out of the packaging and check if the needle is straight. Be careful not to touch the needle! It’s important that it stays sterile. Peel the paper backing off the sticky part. Try not to touch the sticky area. If possible, use one hand to hold your skin taut of the area where you want to place your infusion set. Use the gap in the base of your inserter as a guide for where your insulin cartridge connector will connect to your infusion set. Use your other hand to position your inserter on your skin. When your inserter is in the right place, firmly press the button at the top of your inserter. Keep the inserter pressed firmly onto your skin to aid the insertion. This will push the infusion set into your body. Now pull your inserter away from your body in a straight line without twisting it. Put the inserter aside for now. Be careful not to touch the needle. Gently rub the adhesive plaster around the edge of your infusion set to make sure it’s sticking to your skin properly. If the Infusion set is peeling from the skin at this stage, it’s unlikely that it will last for three full days of wear. If this is the case, you should remove it and try again with a new one. Now, you need to dispose of your needle. Don’t forget to keep your inserter safe, ready for next time!

docs.title: #3 Step: Preparing your pump
docs.text:
Turn your handset on. Have your fully filled insulin cartridge ready to go. Hold your pump with the pump’s white parts facing towards you. Hold your insulin cartridge above the space and line it up, tipping it to feed the tubing into the cartridge tubing groove. Gently press the cartridge tab to feel it click into place. Never push your insulin cartridge straight in or put excessive force on it. This can damage the pump and cartridge. When inserted correctly, you will hear 2 beeps to signal your pump is now ready to be connected to the handset. If everything’s good, the back of the insulin cartridge will sit flush with the back of the pump.

Only if this is the first time you use this pump, a message will pop up on your handset, letting you know your pump is ready to pair. Make sure your pump color and serial number matches the pump you are trying to pair. When you are ready to pair, press Enter to select Pair. The pairing process may take up to a minute. Once pairing is successful, the Handset will show a confirmation message. The pump will beep three times to let you know pairing was successful. If you have already used and paired your pump previously, your Handset will ask you to connect to your pump. Press Enter to select connect and now your pump is connected.

Now your handset will ask if you have used a new, fully filled insulin cartridge. If you just filled your insulin cartridge, press Enter to select Yes.

It is time to apply your patches! You will need your pump and body patch. Take your pump patch and peel off the paper backing. This will reveal the patch’s adhesive. Stick the pump patch to the bottom of your pump, so that it covers the insulin cartridge and the fuzzy hexagon shape is facing outwards. Now take your body patch and without removing its paper backing, stick it to your pump patch by pressing together the hexagon shapes. Make sure the body patch is aligned with your pump.

Support the weight of your pump, and clip the connector at the end of your insulin cartridge tubing into your infusion set. You’ll know it’s secure when you hear the two clicks as the right and left hand sides connect. Check whether the connector is smoothly aligned with your infusion set. You should not feel bumps or gaps. Holding onto your pump, peel off the paper backing from the top of your body patch. Now it is time to place your pump where you’d like to wear it. Be careful not to stretch or kink your insulin cartridge tubing. When you are using the 30 cm or 15 inch insulin cartridge tubing, place your pump in a secure place. If you are using a new infusion set, you need to prime it first. Select the cannula length you are using and press Enter. Press Enter again to select Yes to start pumping. Your cannula is now being primed. Once priming has completed, your handset will confirm that pumping has started. Your pump is now pumping!

docs.title: #4 Step: Removing your pump
docs.text:
If your insulin reservoir is empty or when you have used the pump for 3 days, you’ll have to stop and remove your pump. You can do that by going to the status section and tap stop pumping. You'll receive a confirmation.

Then you can remove your pump. Start by washing your hands. Gently squeeze the tabs on both sides of your insulin cartridge connector. Lift it up to unclip it from your infusion set. Lift the pump away from your body patch. Next, gently remove the body patch from your skin, as well as the patch from the back of your pump. Take your insulin cartridge out of your pump by pulling on the tab. And finally, remove your infusion set. Dispose of your infusion set, insulin cartridge, body and pump patches according to local regulations.

Place your used pump on the charging dock. It will take about 2-4 hours to fully charge your pump.

docs.title: 4.6 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are some important points to keep in mind:

  • You should replace your insulin cartridge when it's empty or after 3 days of use. This is also a good time to switch between your two pumps for recharging. The infusion set should also be changed at least every 3 days. Ideally, you can do everything together, but if needed, you can replace the parts separately. If your insulin cartridge empties before 3 days, or your pump battery is empty, you can do a separate replacement more quickly.

  • The Kaleido pump is compatible with Novorapid and Humalog with a concentration of 100 U/ml.

  • The cartridge always has to be filled with 200 units of insulin.

  • Do not reuse insulin reservoirs. Doing so may contaminate your insulin, lead to infection, and possible under or no delivery of insulin.

  • Do not reuse body and pump patches or use them longer than three days. Doing so may lose their adhesive properties, which could result in your pump becoming detached during use.

  • Make sure that the back of your pump is clean and dry before you attach a pump patch. This will ensure a better bond between the patch and pump, and prevent your pump from coming loose.

  • Do not pair your pumps to your Kaleido Handset if you intend to use the system as part of the Diabeloop System.

  • Once you’ve placed your pump where you want it, try not to play with it too much or bring it into contact with water during the first hour of wear. This will make sure it’s stuck securely.

  • Remember to check your blood glucose levels 2 hours after inserting a new infusion set. This is the only way to confirm proper insertion and insulin delivery. It's best to avoid changing your infusion set right before bedtime.

  • When temporarily removing your insulin pump, it’s best to close the protective cover on your infusion set. If you leave the protective cover open, dirt may enter into the opening which may cause infections or occlusions.

That's it! Now you're familiar with the process of replacing your Kaleido pump and infusion set. We've personally found wearing the pump quite comfortable, hardly noticing its presence. However, if the cannula hits a muscle, it could cause discomfort, in which case it's best to insert a new infusion set. If you're unsure about the replacement steps, clear explanatory videos are available on Kaleido's YouTube channel. Especially in the beginning it is helpful to use these videos when you replace your insulin cartridge and infusion set.

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson about the alarms on the Kaleido insulin pump.

The handset and or pump can alert you about various issues through beeping or vibrating. Kaleido classifies these alerts as either low or high priority alarms.

If multiple alarms happen simultaneously, the intelligent alarm system will first notify you of high priority alarms, followed by low priority ones. Each alarm must be acknowledged individually before the next one appears. The alarm sounds and vibrations will repeat until you acknowledge the message on your handset. To do so, simply press the Enter button after reading and understanding the alarm message.

Let's delve into these alarms and share some extra tips.

docs.title: 5.1 High priority alarms
docs.text:
High priority alarms demand immediate attention. They will notify you with 10 short beeps on your pump or handset and will stop your pump if it's related to the pump, or turn off your handset if it's related to it.

You’ll get a high priority alarm related to the pump if:

• Your pump battery is empty and infusion stopped

• Your insulin cartridge is empty

• The insulin cartridge you’re using has expired

• Your pump has an insulin blockage

• Your insulin cartridge has come out of your pump while it was pumping

• There’s a problem with your Kaleido pump

You’ll get a high priority alarm related to the handset if:

• There’s a problem with your Kaleido handset

docs.title: 5.2 Low priority alarms
docs.text:
Low priority alarms are important but not urgent. They notify you with 2 or 3 short beeps and won't stop your pump or handset.

You’ll get a low priority alarm related to the pump if:

• Your pump battery is getting low

• You’ve been using the same insulin cartridge for three days

• Your insulin cartridge volume is getting low

You’ll get a low priority alarm related to the handset if:

• Your handset battery is getting low

• There is a communication error between your handset and your pump

• Your Insulin on Board has been reset

• Your handset battery is empty

• Your handset and pump software versions do not match

docs.title: 5.3 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are some important points to remember:

  • You should always keep your handset within reach in case there’s an alarm and keep the handset battery sufficiently charged.

  • Old alarms can be reviewed in the History Menu. Active alarms will be visible if you wake your handset from sleep mode.

  • If you are unsure how to respond to an alarm, consult the user manual or contact customer service.

  • For some pump and handset alarms, the handset will show a 4 digit number in the bottom right corner. The alarm number can also be found in your History on the handset and in the manual. In case you are not able to resolve the issue, contact customer service and they will ask you for the 4 digit number.

  • If you temporarily won't be using your handset, you can simply switch it off. To do this, hold the power button until you can confirm to turn off the handset.

Congratulations! You have completed the final lesson on the Kaleido pump. Initial setup is usually supervised by a diabetes nurse, and if you have any further questions, don't hesitate to consult them. Well done on reaching this point!

docs.title: 6. Case report
docs.text:
Welcome to this final part of the module about the Kaleido insulin pump! You've gained valuable insights into its features and functions, showing your dedication to managing diabetes effectively. Now, let's take a look at a real-life review of the Kaleido pump.

In this video review by Sanne and Anne, they discuss their experience with the Kaleido pump. If you understand Dutch, you can watch the video by clicking on the link provided. If not, we can summarize it for you.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QCxFEdR9fuI

Sanne and Anne appreciated the pump's compact size, lack of tubing, and attractive design compared to other insulin pumps. They found it convenient for exercising and discreet bolusing via the handset.

However, they noted a few drawbacks. The handset requires daily charging, and occasionally, the pump may need to be recharged between sessions, possibly due to initial incomplete charging. They also mentioned that the pump patch can loosen, especially with frequent removal and reattachment, and that they needed to carry a lot of equipment due to the many different parts of the pump.

Once again, congratulations on completing the module! Your commitment to learning and embracing new diabetes technologies is admirable. Armed with this knowledge, you're well-prepared to support others using the Kaleido insulin pump. Remember, ongoing learning and communication with healthcare providers are key to optimizing diabetes care.

Best wishes on your journey with the Kaleido pump! We hope it brings you greater convenience and enhances your quality of life. Keep up the fantastic work, and feel free to share your experiences and questions with the community!

  • 3. Insulin set-up
  • 4. Patch Pump replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Welcome to this guide all about the Medtrum TouchCare Nano Insulin Patch Pump System.

If you’ve already checked out our earlier videos on insulin pumps, you probably have a good idea of how they work — like setting your daily insulin (basal), giving extra insulin for meals (bolus), swapping out insulin and infusion sets, and dealing with alarms.

Now, we’re going to dive into the TouchCare Nano Insulin Patch Pump. It’s a small, tubeless device that’s easy to wear and simple to use.
