In this guide, we’ll walk you through the whole set-up process and show how to use it with confidence. Whether you're a healthcare provider helping someone else or a new user getting started, we’re here to make it all clear and easy.

It is important to note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult your healthcare provider before adjusting your therapy.

The videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, an endocrinologist at general St-Lucas hospital in Ghent, Belgium, and founder of Diabetotech. The content has been reviewed by Dr. Sarah Siegelaar, an endocrinologist at Amsterdam UMC in the Netherlands, as well as Davor Skeledzija from Croatia, who’s daughter with type 1 diabetes has used the TouchCare Nano Pump. Here you can see their disclosures. Their expertise and personal experiences ensure that the information presented in the videos is comprehensive and unbiased.

By the end of this video, you’ll confidently understand:

  • How the TouchCare Nano Insulin Patch Pump works.

  • How to set up your daily and mealtime insulin using the Personal Diabetes Manager (or PDM) and the EasyPatch App.

  • How to change the insulin reservoir and infusion set

  • And what to do if your pump or sensor gives any alerts.

We’re excited to help make your experience with the Medtrum TouchCare Nano Insulin Patch Pump smooth and easy. You’ve got this!

docs.title: 2. Pump Operation
docs.text:
Welcome to the next part of your TouchCare Nano journey!

In this lesson, we'll explore the parts and buttons of the pump, walk you through the home screen and main menu of both the PDM and the EasyPatch app, and share some key tips to remember. By the end of this section, you'll have a clearer understanding of how the pump operates using either the PDM or the EasyPatch app.

There are 2 versions of the TouchCare Nano pump: 200 U and 300 U. While we’ll focus on the 200 U TouchCare Nano Pump, the 300 U patch pump looks and works very similarly.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts & Buttons of the Pump
docs.text:
The TouchCare Nano Insulin Patch Pump is made up of two main parts:

  • a disposable insulin Reservoir Patch that you’ll change about every 3 days.

  • and a small reusable Pump Base that can be reused for up to 4 years,

The Pump Base stores your insulin settings and communicates with the EasyPatch app or PDM via Bluetooth, with a range of up to 4 meters (approximately 13 feet) when there are no obstructions.

The disposable Reservoir Patch includes an insulin reservoir that can be filled through the fill port, an insulin delivery mechanism, a built-in battery, a buzzer, an adhesive patch to attach it to the skin, and a needle insertion system secured with a safety lock.

Once the Pump Base is attached to the Reservoir Patch, the 200 U TouchCare Nano insulin pump measures just 4 x 3 cm and weighs only 13.8 grams, making it the smallest and lightest insulin patch pump available.

docs.title: 2.2 PDM Home Screen and Main Menu
docs.text:
When you turn on the PDM, the Home Screen appears with a status bar at the top showing the battery level, any recent alerts, the current time, audio settings, and the status of the Bluetooth connection to the pump.

In the center of the screen, you'll see the current glucose value along with a trend arrow, the amount of insulin on board, the current insulin delivery status, current basal rate, and how much insulin remains in the reservoir. Just below that, a graph displays your glucose levels over the past 3 hours.

A colored ring surrounds the glucose value to show what kind of insulin delivery is active.

  • A grey indicates that no pump is currently connected.

  • green ring indicates basal delivery,

  • dark green shows a temporary basal rate,

  • blue means a normal bolus is being given,

  • purple represents an extended bolus,

  • and red signals that insulin delivery is paused,

If you swipe from right to left on the home screen, the Main Menu will appear. This menu includes icons for bolus, basal settings, suspending insulin delivery, patch management, sensor data, history, events, EasyLoop, and general settings.

Swiping in the opposite direction, from left to right, takes you to the pump Status Screen, where you can view more detailed information about your current insulin delivery.

docs.title: 2.3 EasyPatch App Home Screen and Main Menu
docs.text:
The EasyPatch app can be downloaded on most iPhones and Android phones. To check if your phone works with the app, go to medtrum.com/compatibility. You can find the app by searching “Medtrum” in the App Store or Google Play.

When you open the EasyPatch app for the first time, you’ll be asked to either register or log into your Medtrum account.

The Home Screen is designed to give you a clear overview of your current status. At the top, you'll see a status bar with quick access to the Main Menu, any recent alerts, and a shortcut to the detailed Status Screen. Just below that, there's a second bar with icons showing your audio settings, the Bluetooth signal strength to the pump, the amount of insulin remaining, the EasyLoop function, and the Bluetooth signal strength of the transmitter if a sensor is connected.

In the center of the screen, the app displays your current glucose value, complete with a trend arrow, the amount of insulin on board, your current insulin delivery, and the current basal rate. A glucose graph just below shows how your levels have changed over the past 3, 6 and 12 hours.

Surrounding the glucose value is a colored ring that reflects the type of insulin delivery currently active. A green ring means basal delivery is running, dark green shows a temporary basal rate, blue indicates a normal bolus is in progress, purple stands for an extended bolus, red means insulin delivery has been paused, and grey indicates that no pump is currently connected.

Just beneath the glucose graph, there are four quick-access icons: Suspend-Resume, Bolus, Enter BG, and Events.

By tapping the hamburger icon at the top of the screen, you’ll open the Main Menu. From here, you can navigate to Monitor, CGM, Insulin Delivery, Statistics, Events, EasyLoop, Reminders, Settings, or Alerts.

Tapping the information icon (the little “i” in the top bar) will take you to the Status Screen, where you can view detailed information about your insulin patch pump and CGM.

Finally, if you tap on the glucose graph, you can explore your glucose statistics for a specific day and even switch to landscape mode for a more detailed view of the data.

docs.title: 2.4 Do’s & Don’ts
docs.text:
• The TouchCare Nano pump is waterproof to a depth of 2.5 meters or 8 feet for up to 60 minutes.
  • Medtrum provides a standard spare Pump Base in case of failure, offering users extra peace of mind.

  • If you need to clean a pump base, gently wipe it with a clean, damp cloth or you can use an alcohol wipe to clean the surface. Let it air dry before connecting it to a new Patch.

  • Keep the pump and PDM or phone within 4 meters or 13 feet and remove any obstacle between them. If your PDM or phone disconnects, don’t worry — the pump keeps delivering basal insulin according to the selected program..

  • If you’re using the PDM, remember to adjust the time on the pump manually when traveling or when the clocks change — it doesn’t do it automatically. Note: You cannot adjust the time while there is an active Patch.

  • You’ll need one small extra hardware item: the Needle Eject Tool. When removing the pump, insert the tool into the needle retraction hole to safely retract the steel cannula before taking the pump off.

And there you have it! You now know all the parts and buttons of the TouchCare Nano pump, PDM and EasyPatch app. Stick around — next up, we’ll show you how to set your insulin delivery settings!

docs.title: 3. Insulin set-up
docs.text:
Welcome to the part where we set up your insulin delivery — don’t worry, it’s easier than it sounds! We’ll show you how to program your basal insulin delivery, set a temporary basal, suspending insulin, deliver an insulin bolus, and give you some simple tips to make it all go smoothly.

We’ll always first show you how to do it with the PDM, and then with the EasyPatch app.

docs.title: 3.1 Programming Your Basal Insulin
docs.text:
If you’re using the PDM, go to the Main Menu, and tap on ‘Settings,’ then ‘Insulin Pump,’ then ‘Basal Set-up.’ Tap Edit Basal,’ and Select ‘Standard’ to edit the pattern

Note: The active basal pattern is check-marked.

The edit screen appears. Tap +Add time segment to create and add a new segment. Enter the end time for this segment. Then tap Done. Your first time segment starts at midnight. The last basal segment always ends at 24:00 (or 12:00 pm).

Tap each section to type in the numbers. When you’re happy with them, tap ‘Save.’ If you need different settings at different times of the day, tap the plus icon to add more segments.

To Delete, select segment, slide to left on each and a delete button will appear. Tap Delete to delete the selected segment. Slide back to right to close Delete. The last segment cannot be deleted, and the starting time of all segments cannot be changed.

If you’re using the EasyPatch app, to set up your basal rate on your EasyPatch app,

tap on the three lines in the top left hand corner to access the main menu. Tap on settings. Tap on insulin pump. Tap on edit basal pattern. Tap on Standard. You can add different time segments for the day depending on your need for different rates of insulin at different times. Tap add time segment to add a new time segment. You can adjust the insulin rate for each time segment by tapping on the blue number next to each time segment. Now tap done. If you want to delete a time segment, simply slide your finger across the time segment from right to left and then tap delete. Once you've completed the basal set-up, tap done in the top right hand corner of your smart device to save your basal rate.

docs.title: 3.2 Setting a Temporary Basal Rate
docs.text:
Sometimes you might need a little more or less insulin — like during exercise or illness. To do this, you can set a temporary basal.

If you’re using the PDM, to deliver a temporary basal rate on your pump, slide to unlock your PDM. Slide your finger from right to left to access the main menu. Tap on basal. Tap on temp basal. Decide if you are increasing or decreasing your basal rate. Tap on the blue number next to percent. Now select how much basal insulin you wish to deliver. Tap done. Tap on the blue number next to duration. Now select the time for how long you would like the temporary basal rate to run for. Tap done. Now tap next. The PDM will show you a breakdown of how the temporary basal will be delivered. Now slide to activate the temporary basal rate. The pump will now activate the delivery of the temporary basal and this will be indicated with a dark green circle on your home screen with the temporary basal rate below.

If you’re using the EasyPatch app, to deliver a temporary basal on your pump, tap on the three lines in the top left hand corner to access the main menu. Tap on insulin delivery. Tap on temp basal. Decide if you are increasing or decreasing your basal rate. Now select how much basal insulin you wish to deliver. Tap done. Tap on the blue number next to duration. Now select the time for how long you would like your temporary basal rate to run for. Tap done. Now tap next. Your smart device will now show you a breakdown of the percentage and length of the temporary basal. Now slide to activate the temporary basal rate. The pump will now activate the delivery of the temporary basal and this will be indicated with a dark green circle on your home screen with the temporary basal rate shown below.

docs.title: 3.3 Suspending insulin
docs.text:
If you’re using the PDM, to suspend the delivery of insulin on your pump, slide to unlock your PDM. Slide your finger from right to left to access the main menu. Click on suspend. Now you can select the amount of time you wish to suspend your pump for. This can be set anywhere from 15 minutes up to 2 hours with 15 minutes increments. Select done. Select next. Now slide to suspend the delivery of insulin. The pump will now suspend the delivery of insulin and this will be indicated with a red circle on your home screen with the suspend time remaining below. To resume the delivery of insulin on your pump, slide to unlock your PDM. Slide your finger from right to left to access the main menu. Click on resume. Now slide to resume the delivery of your basal insulin. The pump will now resume the delivery of your basal insulin and this will be indicated with the green circle on your home screen with your basal delivery below.

If you’re using the EasyPatch app, to suspend the delivery of insulin on your pump, tap on suspend at the bottom of your smart device. Now you can select the amount of time you wish to suspend your pump for. This can be set anywhere from 15 minutes up to 2 hours with 15 minutes increments. Select done. Select continue. Now slide to suspend the delivery of insulin. Now tap ok. The pump will now suspend the delivery of insulin and this will be indicated with a red circle on your home screen with the suspend time remaining below. To resume the delivery of insulin on your pump, tap on resume at the bottom of your smart device. Now slide to resume the delivery of your based insulin. The pump will now resume the delivery of your basal insulin and this will be indicated with a green circle on your home screen with your basal delivery below.

docs.title: 3.4 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
With the TouchCare Nano pump, you can choose for a Normal bolus, Extended bolus, Combo bolus – whatever that works better for you. In the settings menu, you can also enable the bolus calculator. This feature allows you to set your carbohydrate ratio, insulin sensitivity, target glucose level, and the duration of insulin action, to make a more correct estimation of the insulin requirement for meals and corrections.

If you’re using the PDM, Slide to unlock your pdm. Slide your finger from right to left to access the main menu. Tap on bolus. Tap on bolus calculator enter your blood glucose by tapping the three dashes under bg. Now tap done. Enter your carbohydrates by tapping the zero on the carbs. Now tap done. Tap next. Tap next again. Now slides to deliver the bolus. To deliver a manual bonus on your pump, slide to unlock your pdm. Slide your finger from right to left to access the main menu. Tap on bolus. Tap on manual bolus. Enter the amount of insulin you wish to administer by tapping the three zeros to set bolus. Now tap done. Tap next. Now slide to deliver the bolus. The pump will now deliver your manual bolus.

If you’re using the EasyPatch app, Tap the bolus button on your app. Enter your blood glucose by tapping on the two dashes next to bg. Enter your carbohydrates by tapping on the zero next to carbs. Now tap done. Now tap next. Now tap next again. Now slide to deliver the bolus. The pump will now deliver your bolus. To deliver a manual bonus on your pump, tap on the three lines in the top left hand corner to access the main menu. Tap on insulin delivery. Tap on manual bolus. Enter the amount of insulin you wish to administer by tapping on the three zeros next to set. Now tap done. Now tap next. Now slide to deliver the bolus. The pump will now deliver your manual bolus.

docs.title: 3.5 Do’s & Don’ts
docs.text:
• When you use an insulin pump, it’s always best to set your maximum basal and bolus delivery for safety. On both the PDM and the EasyPatch app, this can be done via the settings. The maximum basal should be at least 2 times your highest current basal insulin delivery rate, and your max bolus should be set a little higher than your usual biggest dose.

  • The TouchCare Nano pump allows you to work with preset bolus amounts for Breakfast, lunch, dinner and snacks. These can be preset via the settings menu.

That's it! You may now confidently program, adjust, and administer insulin. Keep it up!

docs.title: 4. Patch Pump replacement
docs.text:
In this section, we’re going to show you how to replace your TouchCare Nano Patch Pump.

In just a few easy steps, we'll guide you through the process of changing a TouchCare Nano Pump. We'll cover everything from connecting the pump base to the PDM or EasyPatch app, filling the Reservoir Patch with insulin, priming it, and applying the pump to your body.

docs.title: 4.1 Pump Replacement with the PDM
docs.text:
If it’s your first time using the pump base, you’ll need to connect it to the PDM. Just go to the main menu, tap on Settings, then choose Insulin Pump, and tap Add Pump Base. You can use your phone’s camera to scan the QR code on the back of the pump base or the box it came in. Or, if you prefer, you can enter the serial number manually.

Once the pump base is connected, it’s time to attach it to the reservoir patch. First, wash your hands with soap and water and let them air dry. Then, open the reservoir patch package and place the patch on a clean flat surface. Take the pump base and gently press it into the patch at a 90-degree angle until you hear a click. After that, press again at a 45-degree angle to make sure it’s securely attached. You’ll hear four beeps when everything is correctly connected.

Next, it’s time to fill the patch with insulin. Start by cleaning the top of your insulin vial with an alcohol wipe. Attach the needle to the syringe and pull down the plunger to draw insulin. If you’re using an insulin vial, before drawing insulin, inject an amount of air into the vial equal to the volume of insulin you plan to withdraw. Tap the syringe gently to make air bubbles rise, then push the plunger slightly to remove the bubbles. Insert the needle straight into the fill port on the bottom of the patch. Pull the plunger back just a bit to remove any trapped air, then withdraw the needle. Remove any remaining bubbles from the syringe, then reinsert the needle and slowly fill the patch with insulin. When you’re done, remove the needle and put the protective cap back on before safely disposing of the syringe.

Now you’re ready to prime the pump. Make sure the patch pump is placed on a clean surface and kept at least 1 meter or 3.2 feet away from magnetic objects like phones or other pumps. Hold the pump with one hand and press the needle button down with the other hand until it locks halfway. In the app, tap Prime. The priming process takes a little time, and it’s important not to remove the safety lock while it’s priming.

When priming is finished, the app will show you that it’s time to attach and insert the patch. Choose a clean, flat, and pinchable spot on your body. Apply the patch horizontally on the abdomen, back, or buttocks, or vertically on the upper arm or thigh. Wipe it with an alcohol wipe and let it dry completely. Slide off the safety lock and peel off the adhesive liners starting from the side with the insulin fill port. Be careful not to press the needle button yet. Place the patch on your skin and press down firmly, especially around the edges of the adhesive and the area near the needle by pressing the center of the patch for a few seconds. Then, press the needle button in one quick motion. You’ll feel it lock in place. In the app, tap Continue — and that’s it! Your patch pump is now active and ready to deliver insulin.

When it’s time to remove the patch, start by deactivating it in the app. Go to the main menu. Select patch, then select deactivate patch. Slide to deactivate patch. Follow the instructions to remove your Reservoir patch. Insert the needle eject tool vertically into the needle withdrawal hole and press vertically to eject the infusion needle. Gently lift the edge of the adhesive tape from your skin and remove the entire patch pump. We recommend that you wipe around the tape with alcohol wipes or baby oil to make the tape peel off easily. Fold and break the tab of the reservoir patch. Remove the pump base from the old Reservoir patch. Discard the used Reservoir patch according to your local waste disposal regulations and reuse the pump base.

docs.title: 4.2 Pump Replacement with the EasyPatch app
docs.text:
The items that you need to activate a new patch pump include the EasyPatch app, a Pump Base, a packaged Reservoir patch, a vial of rapid acting insulin at room temperature, and alcohol wipes.

If it’s your first time using the pump base, you’ll need to connect it to the app. Just open the EasyPatch app, go to the main menu, tap on Settings, then choose Insulin Pump, and tap Add Pump Base. You can use your phone’s camera to scan the QR code on the back of the pump base or the box it came in. Or, if you prefer, you can enter the serial number manually.

Once the pump base is connected, it’s time to attach it to the reservoir patch. First, wash your hands with soap and water and let them air dry. Then, open the reservoir patch package and place the patch on a clean flat surface. Take the pump base and gently press it into the patch at a 90-degree angle until you hear a click. After that, press again at a 45-degree angle to make sure it’s securely attached. You’ll hear four beeps when everything is correctly connected.

Next, it’s time to fill the patch with insulin. Start by cleaning the top of your insulin vial with an alcohol wipe. Attach the needle to the syringe and pull down the plunger to draw insulin. If you’re using an insulin vial, before drawing insulin, inject an amount of air into the vial equal to the volume of insulin you plan to withdraw. Tap the syringe gently to make air bubbles rise, then push the plunger slightly to remove the bubbles. Insert the needle straight into the fill port on the bottom of the patch. Pull the plunger back just a bit to remove any trapped air, then withdraw the needle. Remove any remaining bubbles from the syringe, then reinsert the needle and slowly fill the patch with insulin. When you’re done, remove the needle and put the protective cap back on before safely disposing of the syringe.

Now you’re ready to prime the pump. Make sure the patch pump is placed on a clean surface and kept at least 1 meter or 3.2 feet away from magnetic objects like phones or other pumps. Hold the pump with one hand and press the needle button down with the other hand until it locks halfway. In the app, tap Prime. The priming process takes a little time, and it’s important not to remove the safety lock while it’s priming.

When priming is finished, the app will show you that it’s time to attach and insert the patch. Choose a clean, flat, and pinchable spot on your body. Apply the patch horizontally on the abdomen, back, or buttocks, or vertically on the upper arm or thigh. Wipe it with an alcohol wipe and let it dry completely. Slide off the safety lock and peel off the adhesive liners starting from the side with the insulin fill port. Be careful not to press the needle button yet. Place the patch on your skin and press down firmly, especially around the edges of the adhesive and the area near the needle by pressing the center of the patch for a few seconds. Then, press the needle button in one quick motion. You’ll feel it lock in place. In the app, tap Continue — and that’s it! Your patch pump is now active and ready to deliver insulin.

When it’s time to remove the patch, start by deactivating it in the app. Go to the main menu, choose Insulin Delivery, then tap Deactivate Patch and slide to confirm. Use the needle eject tool by inserting it straight into the needle retraction hole and pressing down. This safely retracts the needle. Gently lift the edge of the adhesive from your skin. If needed, you can use alcohol wipes or baby oil to help remove the tape. Break the tab on the reservoir patch, remove the pump base, and keep it for your next use. Dispose of the used patch according to your local rules — and you’re done!

docs.title: 4.3 Do’s and don’ts
docs.text:
Here are some important points to keep in mind

  • Some people use a Skin Barrier product or extra medical tapes to make the wearing experience of the patch pump more comfortable. Medtrum can provide an alternative patch for users of the 200 U patch pump who experience an allergic reaction to the adhesive layer.

  • The TouchCare Nano Patch pumps are compatible with Humalog, NovoRapid, Admelog, Apidra, Lyumjev and Fiasp with a concentration of 100 U per milliliter.

  • The TouchCare Nano Pump has a fixed 90-degree steel cannula of 5 mm or 0.2 inch.

  • The 200 units TouchCare Nano pump holds 200 U of insulin and can be worn for up to 3 days. If you require more than 200 units of insulin within 3 days, the 300 units TouchCare Nano pump will be more suitable for you. It is reassuring to know that the pump is not automatically disabled after three days or a fixed period. This flexibility allows you to choose a convenient and calm moment for a patch change.

  • Remember to check your blood glucose levels 2 hours after inserting a new pump. This is the only way to confirm proper insertion and insulin delivery. It's best to avoid changing the pump right before bedtime.

  • During a patch change, inspect the contact pins on the Pump Base for any discoloration and clean them with an alcohol wipe if needed. The pump is sensitive to moisture and dirt, and a small number of users have reported pump errors—most likely due to poor contact between the patch and the pump base. To minimize this risk, always place the pump base carefully and clean the contacts with an alcohol wipe at each change.

  • If you ever feel unsure, don’t panic! Take your time, follow the steps, and if needed, reach out to customer service or your diabetes care team.

You did a great job! Now you're familiar with the process of replacing the TouchCare Nano Pump with the PDM or EasyPatch app. Enjoy and trust the process!

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms
docs.text:
Let’s talk about alarms and notifications on your TouchCare Nano Insulin Patch Pump — these are here to keep you safe and informed.

Like in most insulin pumps, there are 3 types of notifications: alarms for high and medium priority, alerts for low priority, and reminders.

docs.title: 5.1 Alarms
docs.text:
Alarms are triggered by serious or potentially serious conditions. You must respond to the alarm by taking appropriate action in order to clear the alarm condition. For example: When Alarm “PATCH EXPIRED” occurs, the Lock Screen and Alarm screen display the following screen.

  • If it is a high priority alarm, the PDM or EasyPatch app will display an alarm message with instructions and a red triangle with three exclamation marks. The PDM or EasyPatch app will emit 10 beeps or vibrations and the patch pump will emit 3 beeps.

  • If it is a medium priority alarm, the PDM will display an alarm message with instructions and a red triangle with two exclamation marks. The PDM or EasyPatch app will emit 3 beeps or vibrations and the patch pump will emit 3 beeps.

If a Pump alarm is not cleared within 10 minutes, both your PDM or EasyPatch app and Patch Pump will make a siren sound until the alarm is cleared.

The following table lists high priority alarm messages. The following table lists medium priority alarm messages.

docs.title: 5.2 Alerts
docs.text:
Alerts are triggered by conditions that may require your attention. Alerts are less

serious than alarms. You must respond to an alert by pressing buttons and/or taking actions.

For example: When Alert “PATCH BATTERY LOW” occurs, the Lock Screen and Alert screen display the following screen.

The PDM or EasyPatch app displays an alert message with instructions and an empty triangle with an exclamation mark. The PDM or EasyPatch app can beep or vibrate 1 time every 3 minutes, and the patch pump can beep 1 time.

The following table lists alert messages for PDM or EasyPatch app. The following table lists alert messages for Patch Pump.

docs.title: 5.3 Reminders
docs.text:
Reminding messages are automatically displayed to remind you of a condition, function or event. Reminding messages include the notifications you get after setting reminders and the low-priority reminding notifications. A Message requires you to press buttons to clear it and/or to take action if necessary.

For example: When Message “BASAL RESUMED” occurs, the Lock Screen and Message screen display the following screen. Your PDM emits two beeps and/or one vibration every three minutes, three times in total.

With the TouchCare Nano PDM or the EasyPatch app, you can set a specific reminder called the Bolus Reminder. For example, if you choose a 2-hour reminder, and you forget to deliver a bolus between two time points — let’s say time A and time B — you’ll automatically get a reminder at time B. It’s a helpful way to make sure you don’t miss your insulin dose.

docs.title: 5.4 Do’s and don’ts
docs.text:
• You can customize how you receive alerts. Go to the main menu, then tap on Settings, then General Settings, and finally Audio Options. Here, you can choose between sound, vibration, both, or none. If you set both Audio and Vibrate to "Off," your PDM won’t make a sound or vibrate for most CGM alerts — except in urgent situations. For example, if your glucose drops below 56 mg/dL (or 3.1 mmol/L), or stays above 250 mg/dL (or 13.9 mmol/L), for more than 3 hours during auto mode, your PDM will vibrate once every three minutes; if you don’t clear the alert within nine minutes, a loud siren will sound until you acknowledge it. On the EasyPatch app, the urgent low or high alerts will repeat once every minute until cleared.

  • If you ever feel unsure, it’s totally okay to call customer service or check your user guide. There’s also a complete list of all alarms in the manual.

  • If you use the EasyPatch app, you can turn on push notifications so alerts pop up right on your phone. Just check your app settings and make sure notifications are enabled.

  • And if you ever need a break, the Snooze function within the CGM alerts lets you temporarily silence alerts — just make sure to check back in later.

  • If you won’t be using your pump for a while, be sure to deactivate the patch pump and disconnect the pump base from your PDM or EasyPatch app.

Remember, alarms and alerts are there to help — not to stress you out. Now that you are aware of what to do, stay calm, follow the prompts, and reach out for help if needed. You’re doing great! In the event that the message is unclear, refer to the user manual or contact customer service. Good luck!!

docs.title: 6. Case report
docs.text:
We’re so glad you’ve joined us on this journey, and we hope you continue to feel confident using your insulin pump to manage your diabetes.

As a final tip, we invite you to read a personal review by Tim Street about his experience with the TouchCare Nano insulin pump. In his blog, he shares honest feedback — noting some minor points like occasional friction against the skin — but overall, he was very positive. He especially appreciated the pump’s compact size, lower cost compared to other patch pumps, and the fact that it can connect to AndroidAPS, an open-source automated insulin delivery system.

https://www.diabettech.com/patch-pump/medtrums-nano-an-alternative-patch-pump/

One thing to keep in mind: while you may be able to download the EasyPatch app in your country and explore its features, you’ll only be able to connect a pump once a company representative activates this option for your account.

And that’s it — you’ve made it to the end! Great job.\ Always remember: your health and well-being are worth every bit of effort. Keep going, keep learning, and keep doing amazing things. You’ve got this!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. How the MiniMed 780G insulin pump works
  • 3. Insulin setup
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to our focused exploration of the MiniMed 780G pump!

This insulin tubed pump is a key component in the MiniMed 780G automated insulin delivery system, where it works in collaboration with a Guardian, Simplera Sync or Instinct sensor.

By now, you've gained a solid understanding of insulin pump functionality through our general videos. You've delved into setting basal and bolus insulin, handling infusion sites and managing alarms.

Now, we're turning our lens towards the MiniMed 780G insulin pump. You'll walk through a comprehensive setup tutorial for this widely used tubed pump, providing an accessible guide for healthcare professionals aiding MiniMed 780G users, and supportive resources for those starting their journey with this pump. It's important to note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult your healthcare provider before adjusting your therapy.

This module was developed by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, founder of Diabetotech and endocrinologist at the general St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr. Henk-Jan Aanstoot, founder of Diabeter, which are diabetes treatment centers in the Netherlands, and Sjoerd Aerts, a user of the MiniMed 780G system and administrator of the dutch FaceBook page about the MiniMed 780G. Here you can see their disclosures. Their expertise and perspectives ensure that you receive honest opinions on the pros and cons of the MiniMed 780G insulin pump.

By the end of this series, you'll have a comprehensive understanding of the MiniMed 780G's operation, setting basal and bolus insulin, changing the insulin reservoir and infusion set, and handling pump alarms. This will enhance your ability as a healthcare provider to assist MiniMed 780G users, or as a user, to prepare for a successful setup. We'll also provide you with handy tips and tricks to make your life with the MiniMed 780G easier.

Best of luck on your journey with the MiniMed 780G!

docs.title: 2. How the MiniMed 780G insulin pump works (5.5 min)
2. How the MiniMed 780G insulin pump works
docs.text: Welcome to this informative video on how the MiniMed 780G pump works! Today, we'll explore its various features, including the pump's components, battery replacement, unlocking process, and the screens and menu options you'll encounter.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts & buttons on the pump
docs.text:
Let's start by familiarizing ourselves with the parts and buttons on the pump. The front of the pump boasts a color screen that you can control using the buttons located below it. At the top right, you'll find the power button, while the back button sits at the top left. The center button, featuring a small circle, acts as the select or OK button, and the surrounding arrows allow for easy navigation through the menu.

The MiniMed 780G pump operates on a single AA battery, housed in the right-side battery compartment. On the left side, you'll notice the insulin reservoir, which connects to your body through a tube and infusion set. The backside of the pump displays the serial number, and there's also a space available for attaching a pump clip.

docs.title: 2.2 Battery replacement
docs.text: To ensure proper functioning, it's recommended to replace the AA battery every 1-2 weeks. Alkaline batteries work perfectly well. To replace the battery, simply twist open the battery cover using a penny or the pump belt clip. When inserting the new battery, ensure that the battery cover slot remains horizontal. Consider replacing the battery cover once a year to maintain its waterproof seal, especially when entering frequently in wet conditions.

docs.title: 2.3 Unlocking the pump
docs.text: Now let's move on to unlocking the pump. Pressing any button will power on the pump and bring you to the home screen.

docs.title: 2.4 Home screen and pump menu
docs.text:
On the home screen, you'll see the most recent glucose reading in the center, along with icons indicating the battery, reservoir, and sensor connection status. The active insulin amount is displayed at the bottom. By selecting the right arrow, you can access your time in range, this is the time that your blood sugar is between 70 and 180 milligram per deciliter or 3.9 and 10 millimoles per liter. Further details can be accessed by using the down arrow button. Please note that the time in range mentioned only covers the last 24 hours. Unfortunately, at the moment, the MiniMed pump does not provide the average time in range for longer periods, such as weeks.

When SmartGuard is activated, a blue shield icon appears on the home screen.

If your sensor is paired, pressing the sensor graph button will take you to the sensor graph display. By pressing the arrow at the top, you can extend the time frame to view more hours. Here, you can also observe the insulin delivery performed by the SmartGuard algorithm. The purple bars represent basal insulin micro boluses, and the blue bars signify autocorrect boluses, which will be covered in detail later in this module.

By pressing the select button, you'll be directed to the menu screen. From here, you can access various options, including History & Graph, SmartGuard, Sound & Vibration, Blood Glucose, Insulin, Reservoir & Set, Status, Paired Devices and Settings.

Take some time to explore the internal menu of the MiniMed 780G pump so you can become familiar with the different functions. The pump operates somewhat similarly to a mobile phone and intuitively guides you through its features.

docs.title: 2.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Before we conclude, let's go over a few do's and don'ts:

  • We strongly recommend downloading the menu overview for convenient access. This resource will greatly enhance your troubleshooting skills, especially when assisting someone remotely. Below this video, you will be able to download this MiniMed 780G pump overview, and this simplified menu map.

  • The pump is waterproof for up to 24 hours, with a maximum depth of 3.6 meters or 11.8 feet.

  • Ensure the battery cap is horizontally aligned to prevent cracking. Over-tightening the cap can lead to cracks.

  • The MiniMed 780G pump is susceptible to cracking, especially around the pump clip area. Periodically check the back of the pump for any cracks. If you spot a crack, the pump will still function but will no longer be waterproof. Contact Medtronic for a replacement pump in such cases.

  • The pump comes with a complimentary pump clip that can be easily attached. To remove it, press on the sides at the top.

  • Additionally, consider using silicone sleeves or other protective measures for the pump. Medtronic provides plenty compatible carrying solutions, and various other companies offer alternative options.

  • The MiniMed 780G pump does not automatically adjust the time and date. So when changing between winter and summer time, or when traveling to a different time zone, you have to adjust the time yourself. You can do that via the settings menu.

And there you have it! You now have a comprehensive understanding of how to operate the MiniMed 780G pump and replace its battery. In our next video, we'll delve into the process of configuring insulin delivery.

docs.title: 3. Insulin setup (5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this comprehensive video guide on setting insulin delivery using the MiniMed 780G pump.

In this segment, we will cover programming basal insulin, setting temporary basals, delivering a bolus with or without the bolus wizard, and important do's and don'ts to keep in mind.

docs.title: 3.1 Programming basal insulin
docs.text:
Let's begin by exploring basal insulin programming. To adjust basal insulin levels, navigate to the pump menu and select Insulin or Settings. From there, choose "Delivery Settings" and "Basal Pattern Setup" to specify the pattern you wish to modify. Under the options, you can edit the hourly blocks and insulin rates. Always begin at 0 hours and end at 24 hours. For example, you can set a basal rate of 0.9 units per hour from 0 to 8AM, 0.65 units per hour from 8AM to 6PM and 0.9 units per hour until midnight. Once you have made the desired adjustments, save the changes.

To stop basal insulin, go to the insulin bottle in the menu or use the up arrow for quicker access. Choose "Suspend All Delivery" to stop insulin delivery and click yes to confirm. The home screen will show "Delivery Suspended," and you'll receive reminders every 15 minutes. To resume, click on the up button, select "Resume Basal" and confirm to restart insulin delivery.

docs.title: 3.2 Temporary basal
docs.text: When you need to administer a temporary basal, select the insulin bottle in the menu and select "Basal" followed by "Temp Basal." Adjust the duration and percentage as needed. Alternatively you can also choose a specific basal rate. While the temporary basal is active, you will see a green banner displayed on the home screen, indicating its remaining duration. To cancel a temporary basal, return to the insulin bottle in the menu, where you will find the option to cancel the temp basal. This process is intuitive and self-explanatory.

docs.title: 3.3 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
Next, we'll delve into setting up the bolus calculator, known as the bolus wizard at Medtronic. To configure it, go to the menu, and select settings. Select "delivery Settings" and then select "Bolus Wizard Setup". Select "Bolus Wizard" to turn on the feature. Here it is shown how you adjust the necessary parameters for the bolus wizard, namely the carbohydrate ratio, the insulin sensitivity, the target glucose and the active insulin time.

Now, let's discuss giving a bolus. We will cover two methods: manual bolus and bolus using the bolus wizard.

  • For a manual bolus, access the insulin bottle in the menu and select the "bolus" option or use the down arrow as a shortcut. The down arrow serves as a shortcut to the bolus screen, where you can enter the desired insulin amount. For instance, you can give a bolus of 0.5 units using this method.

  • To give a meal bolus using the bolus wizard, select the insulin bottle, choose "give bolus," and activate the bolus wizard. The wizard will consider factors such as blood glucose levels, carbohydrate intake, and active insulin to calculate the recommended bolus amount. Simply enter the relevant information, and the wizard will present the suggested bolus. Confirm and deliver the bolus as instructed.

In the manual mode of the MiniMed 780G pump, you can choose between 3 types of bolus deliveries, including normal bolus, Square Wave bolus, and Dual Wave bolus. Square and Dual Wave boluses can be set up and delivered only after the Dual/Square Wave feature has been turned on in the Delivery Settings.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Before we conclude, let's go over some important do's and don'ts:

  • Ensure maximum bolus limits are set slightly above expected bolus doses. You can set this between 0 and 25 units. 

  • Consider adjusting the Bolus increment in the Delivery Settings to 0.025 units for optimal SmartGuard performance.

  • The bolus speed determines how quickly the pump delivers bolus insulin. You can choose a standard rate (1.5 units per minute) or a quick rate (15 units per minute). If you’re experiencing a lot of infusion set blockages with the quick rate, it might be helpful to switch to standard rate.

  • The MiniMed pump offers an Easy bolus feature, although it is not commonly utilized. This feature allows you to deliver a regular bolus using only the up button. For more information, please refer to the pump's manual.

Congratulations! You now possess the knowledge to set and adjust insulin delivery on the MiniMed 780G pump. In our next video, we will guide you through changing the insulin reservoir and infusion set.

docs.title: 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (6.5 min)
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (6.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on replacing the infusion set and insulin reservoir on the MiniMed 780G pump.

In this video, we will cover the different infusion set options, step-by-step instructions for replacing the reservoir and infusion set, and helpful tips for success.

docs.title: 4.1 Different Infusion Sets
docs.text:
Let's begin by introducing the available infusion sets for the MiniMed 780G pump, which include the Extended Wear, Mio Advance, Mio, Mio 30, Silhouette, Quickset, and Sure-T infusion sets. A detailed overview of these infusion sets, including tubing and cannula lengths and filling volumes, can be found below this video.

Now, let's dive into the process of replacing the insulin reservoir and infusion set on the MiniMed 780G pump. We will show this with the Extended Wear Infusion Set.

4.2 Supplies

Before you begin, remember to wash your hands and gather all the necessary supplies for the replacement procedure. That is an Extended Wear infusion set with the included Insertion Device, an Extended Reservoir, some alcohol wipes, and your insulin on room temperature.

docs.title: 4.3 Reservoir replacement
docs.text:
• To replace the insulin reservoir, access the "Reservoir and Set" menu on your pump, and choose for "New Reservoir".

  • Remove the used infusion set by gently loosening the adhesive and pulling it away from your body.

  • Then, turn the tubing connector counterclockwise to detach the reservoir from the pump. Dispose of the used infusion set and reservoir appropriately.

  • Next, rewind the piston in your pump.

  • Ensure that the insulin vial is at room temperature and wipe the top with an alcohol wipe, allowing it to dry.

  • Remove the new reservoir from its packaging and position the plunger according to the desired insulin amount. Hold the reservoir by the blue transfer guard and connect it to the insulin vial by pressing down. Flip the vial over and release your thumb pressure from the plunger to fill the reservoir with insulin. Tap the reservoir to remove any air bubbles.

  • To fill the tubing connector, ensure both the top of the reservoir and the inside of the connector are dry. Connect the tubing connector to the top of the reservoir, turning it clockwise until it locks with a click. Tap the reservoir to allow air bubbles to rise, then push the plunger to purge the bubbles from the reservoir into the tubing.

  • If you're not able to push insulin into the tubing, disconnect the tubing connector and then reconnect it.

  • Unscrew the plunger rod counter-clockwise until it completely separates from the reservoir.

  • Put the reservoir in the pump and turn the tubing connector clockwise until you feel the reservoir lock into place. The tubing connector should line up with the groove in the battery cap of your pump.

  • Now you will need to load the reservoir in the pump. On your pump select "Next" to go to the Load Reservoir screen. With "Load" highlighted press and keep holding the select button until the screen shows complete. When you see "Complete" and a yellow check mark on the screen, select "Next".

docs.title: 4.4 Filling the tubing
docs.text:
• Remove the cap from the infusion set and hold it with the needle facing downwards.

  • Fill the tubing by pressing the fill option, ensuring there is no air in the tubing and drops of insulin appear at the needle's end. Follow the pump's prompts to proceed.

docs.title: 4.5 Infusion set placement
docs.text:
• Clean the infusion site thoroughly with an alcohol wipe and allow it to air dry.

  • Pick up the insertion device and remove the paper backing, being careful not to touch the adhesive. Remove the disconnect cover by gently squeezing the sides and pulling it away from the insertion device. Store the disconnect cover for later use to protect the cannula housing.

  • When disconnected, stretch the skin until smooth. Place the insertion device on the site that you have prepared.

  • The raised arrows indicate the side where the tubing will be connected. To insert, firmly press the top button. Carefully pull the insertion device away from your body. Smooth the adhesive onto the skin. Hold the cannula housing steady with your finger, then push the sight connector straight into the cannula housing until you hear a click.

docs.title: 4.6 Filling the cannula
docs.text: • Now that the introducer needle is removed, the cannula is empty and will need to be filled with insulin. Using the pump, fill the cannula with insulin. Both the six millimeter and nine millimeter cannula of the Extended Wear Infusion Set, take 0.6 units of insulin to fill. Here you can see the required fill volumes of the other infusion sets.

docs.title: 4.7 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Lastly, let's go over some important do's and don'ts:

  • The insulin approved in the MiniMed 780G pump is Novorapid, Humalog, and Novolog. The use of Fiasp and Lyumjev is approved in Europe.

  • Remember, 2 hours after inserting a new set, you should check your blood glucose. Checking your blood glucose is the only way to confirm your infusion set is properly inserted and that you're receiving insulin.

  • For swimming or showering, you can disconnect your tubing from the infusion set and apply the provided protective cover. To reconnect, simply remove the cover and snap the tubing back on.

  • The extended infusion set and extended reservoir can both be worn for up to seven days. However, you may require more insulin than the extended reservoir can hold. In this case, you will need to change only the reservoir while keeping the same set.

  • Always use the Extended Wear Reservoir with the Extended Wear Infusion Sets. If you use another infusion set, you can use the regular reservoirs of Medtronic.

  • Consider setting a reminder for a set change at intervals of 2, 3 or 7 days, depending on the infusion set that you are using.

In the rare event of encountering difficulties during the replacement process, remain calm and follow the pump's instructions. If needed, reach out to the provided support number or consult user videos on platforms like YouTube for additional guidance. Also, don't forget that you can contact your diabetes team, who also have an on-call service for medical support.

Congratulations! You are now equipped with the knowledge to confidently replace the insulin reservoir and infusion set on the MiniMed 780G pump.

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms (4 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this informative video about the alarms and notifications on the MiniMed 780G pump.

The MiniMed 780G pump differentiates between alarms, alerts, and status messages. When you see a white triangle in the bottom-right corner of the screen, press the select or the down arrow button. The notification will remain until you clear it, and sometimes, responding to one notification may trigger another.

It's crucial to pay attention and respond promptly to all notifications that appear on the pump. Let's dive in!

docs.title: 5.1 Alarms
docs.text:
An alarm signifies a situation that requires immediate attention, such as a stopped insulin delivery or a hypoglycemic event.

During an alarm, a red icon and instructions will be displayed on the pump screen.

The red light will flash twice, followed by a pause, and repeat continuously.

Depending on your settings, the pump will emit beeps and/or vibrations in a pattern of three pulses followed by a pause.

To clear an alarm, press the designated button and select the appropriate action. Clearing the alarm may resolve the issue, but if not, the alarm will persist until the underlying problem is addressed. If the alarm remains unresolved after 10 minutes, it will escalate to a loud emergency siren.

docs.title: 5.2 Alerts
docs.text:
Alerts indicate a situation that requires attention but may not be as urgent as an alarm.

During an alert, a yellow icon and instructions will be displayed on the pump screen.

The red light will flash once, followed by a pause, and then flash once again in a continuous pattern.

And the pump will emit beeps and/or vibrations in a pattern of three pulses followed by a pause, depending on your settings.

To clear an alert, press the designated button and make the appropriate selection. The pump will beep at intervals of 5 or 15 minutes (depending on the alert) until the alert is cleared. Some alerts may escalate to a loud emergency siren after 10 minutes.

docs.title: 5.3 Messages
docs.text:
Messages provide information about the pump's status or require your input.

When a message appears, a blue icon and instructions will be displayed on the pump screen. The red light on the pump will not flash, but the pump will emit a single beep and/or vibration, depending on your settings.

To delete a notification, press the designated button and make the necessary selection.

docs.title: 5.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• If you are unsure about what to do, consult the user manual, contact customer service for guidance or discuss with your diabetes team.

  • A critical alarm to watch out for is a pump failure. If a critical pump failure occurs, you will see a specific screen, and an emergency siren will activate. Disconnect the pump immediately and do not use it again. Contact Medtronic's service number for assistance.

  • If you need to temporarily stop using the pump, such as during a defect, you can activate storage mode. You can do this by removing the pump's battery and pressing and holding the back arrow until the screen turns off.

  • Medtronic offers the WeCare portal and app specifically designed for users of the MiniMed 780G pump, and we highly recommend signing up for it. By registering, users gain access to their 24/7 customer service, online services, and personalized diabetes management support. It includes replacements for defective sensors and belt clips, CareLink data insights, notifications of software updates, a diabetes community, and the StartRight coaching program for the initial 3 months of therapy.

With the knowledge of this lesson, you can confidently address the main alerts and notifications on your pump. Don't be concerned when they occur—simply refer to the on-screen instructions. If you need further assistance, consult the user manual or reach out to customer service or your diabetes team. Best of luck on your journey!

docs.title: 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2 min)
6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this final video about the MiniMed 780G insulin pump, where we will showcase a case report and immerse ourselves in a virtual pump challenge.

In this chapter, we'd like you to watch a video featuring Andrea, who has been living with type 1 diabetes for over 19 years. Andrea will demonstrate how she conceals her insulin pump under various outfits, showcasing different creative methods she uses to keep her pump discreet. please click on the link below this video to take a look!

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-csDveXqR_c&t=387s – 13 min

Remember, concealing the insulin pump is not only about aesthetics but also about enhancing your confidence and comfort. Finding the best method that works for you can make a significant difference in your daily life. By utilizing these techniques, you can seamlessly integrate your insulin pump into your personal style and enjoy the freedom it provides.

Now, in addition to showcasing different ways to conceal the pump, let's explore another exciting feature: the virtual MiniMed pump. The virtual pump provides a simulated experience where you can practice setting basal delivery and giving insulin boluses. It's an excellent tool to help you become more familiar and comfortable with your pump settings. You can find a link to the virtual Minimed pump under the video.

  • For people in US: https://us.medtronicvirtualpump.com/gTl7X5011ly6IjOlU/VirtualDemoPump/MiniMed_780G_EC/5GC/MMT-1884/

  • For people outside of US: https://eu.medtronicvirtualpump.com/fxCuA839rYX97WZH/VirtualDemoPump/MiniMed_780G_OUS/gs3/mmol_l/%5B4

Now, join the fun challenge! Open the virtual insulin pump on your desktop and take a screenshot of it delivering a 5-unit insulin bolus. Share the picture in the comments of this video and show us what you've got! Let's celebrate your progress in mastering insulin pump management together in this interactive activity.

Congratulations to each one of you for completing this course and gaining valuable insights into using an insulin pump. By mastering the skills covered in this program, you have empowered yourselves to take control of diabetes management.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Diabetes Technology Expert Program

Mylife YpsoPump

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. How the mylife YpsoPump works
  • 3. Insulin setup
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
1. Introduction (2.5 min)

Welcome to our comprehensive overview of the mylife YpsoPump!

This insulin pump serves as a key component of the mylife Loop automated insulin delivery system and is typically used alongside a Dexcom G6 or FreeStyle Libre3 sensor. The mylife YpsoPump distinguishes itself from other tubed pumps with its compact size and ease of use, facilitated by its use of prefilled insulin PumpCarts. This pump also employs a unique system of icons, which, while initially may require some adjustment, are ultimately quite intuitive.

In previous general insulin pump videos, you learned the fundamentals of how insulin pumps work, setting basal and bolus insulin, changing insulin and infusion sets, and handling alarms.

In the upcoming videos, we'll walk you through the entire start-up procedure for the mylife YpsoPump, providing detailed guidance specific to this particular pump. It's important to note that these videos are not intended as medical advice, so always consult your healthcare provider before making any changes to your therapy.

These videos are brought to you by Dr. Inge van Boxelaer, founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the general St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content has been reviewed by Daniella Struijk, a diabetes educator from the Netherlands who uses the mylife Loop system herself and Tom Vesely from Germany, an individual living with type 1 diabetes who reviewed mylife Loop on his TypeOne Talks YouTube channel. Their expertise and personal experience contribute to the comprehensive and reliable information provided in the videos.

Upon completing these videos, you'll gain a comprehensive understanding of:

  • How the mylife YpsoPump functions

  • How to adjust basal and bolus insulin using the mylife YpsoPump

  • How to change the insulin PumpCart and infusion set

  • And what to do if an alarm on the pump is triggered.

This will empower you, whether you're a caregiver or a user, to better navigate the YpsoPump system and discover all the helpful tips and tricks to make life with the mylife YpsoPump smoother and simpler.

Best of luck on this journey!

docs.title: 2. How the mylife YpsoPump works (5 min)
docs.text:
2. How the mylife YpsoPump works (5 min)

Greetings and welcome to this comprehensive tutorial on navigating the mylife YpsoPump!

In this guide, we will walk you through:

  • The design elements and operational buttons on the pump
  • The process of charging your pump

  • The steps to unlock your pump

  • And how to navigate the (initial) screens and internal pump menu.

2.1 Parts & buttons on the pump

The front panel features a black and white touchscreen. The top right corner houses the battery, secured by a battery cap, while the bottom left corner is the home of the prefilled insulin PumpCart and adapter. This adapter is connected to the tubing. The cap of the tubing can be clicked on the cannula base. The sole button on the pump is the Function button, designed for multiple purposes.

2.2 Battery replacement

The mylife Ypsopump operates on a standard AAA alkaline battery. When replacing, ensure the negative side goes in first. The battery usually lasts for about a month and should be replaced accordingly. Once in place, place the battery cap on top.

It is advisable to replace the battery cap every three months to maintain the pump's waterproof attribute. You can secure the battery cap with a penny ensuring it sits flush with the pump.

Upon battery replacement, the pump will display a screen featuring the serial number and software version. Accept it by pressing the tick mark, which will then initiate the pump's self-test. Set the time and date next, and you will be directed to the status screen.

2.3 Unlocking the pump

By default, the pump is set to the key lock. Pressing the function button will activate the pump. To access the main menu, swipe left on the status screen, and enter 1-2-3.

2.4 Home screen & pump menu

The home screen displays the next items.

  • On top, you can find the time, date, lock status, and battery level.

  • In the center, the status icon represents the current state of insulin activity. This may appear as dashes with a square, indicating halted basal insulin. Alternatively, it could be dashes accompanied by the letter A or B, indicating the active basal profile. If a temporary basal is in effect, the icon will show a percentage sign. And lastly, if a normal, square, or dual bolus is being delivered, the icon will display the corresponding bolus symbol.

  • It also shows the rate of basal insulin delivery or the remaining bolus insulin to be delivered.

Swipe left on the home screen and enter 1-2-3 to access the main menu, which comprises seven options: Bolus, Basal rates, Cartridge change, Prime infusion set, Data, Stop/Run mode, and Settings. Many of these options have submenus. A complete menu map and icon overview is available for download below this video.

From the home screen, swiping right will lead you to the Last Bolus view, which displays all information related to the last fully delivered bolus.

Menu navigation is easy – just tap on the icons. A cross indicates cancel, a tick confirms, a forward arrow with a lock symbolizes forward to unlock, an arrow denotes forward, an up arrow represents going up a menu level, and a back arrow means going back one step. Occasionally, an icon may be grayed out, indicating its unavailability.

The function button can guide you back to the status screen at any point. It can also switch the screen on/off and deliver a blind insulin bolus if configured. The function button can be operated in two ways. A short press will execute most functions. For others, like activating the blind bolus, press the function key for over 2 seconds until the pump vibrates.

2.5 Do's & don’ts

  • Keep the menu overview from the downloads section easily accessible. It will help healthcare providers guide patients remotely.

  • The pump is waterproof, but only for an hour until a depth of 1 meter (or 3.3 feet).

  • To simplify function button operations, consider rotating the pump's screen 180 degrees. You can do this by going to the settings in the main menu. The icon with the three squares and arrows symbolizes screen rotation. Confirm this to rotate the screen 180 degrees.

  • Consider using the pump clip or other carrying material as needed. Also consider using a cover as the pump body can get scratched easily. A list of carrying and protective systems from Ypsomed is available in the downloads. Several other companies also provide carrying and protecting solutions.

  • The mylife YpsoPump does not automatically adjust the time and date. So when changing between winter and summer time, or when traveling to a different time zone, you have to adjust the time yourself. You can do that via the settings.

Congratulations! You now understand the basic operations of the mylife YpsoPump, including battery replacement. Our next video will guide you on setting the insulin delivery.

docs.title: 3. Insulin setup (5 min)
docs.text:
3. Insulin setup (5 min)

In this video, we're exploring how to program insulin delivery on the mylife YpsoPump.

Do bear in mind: If you plan to use this pump as a part of the mylife Loop automated insulin delivery system, insulin delivery settings will be managed via the mylife CamAPS FX app. A dedicated module covers this in detail. However, in case you forget your phone, understanding how to manually control the pump is critical, and that's our focus in this video.

We'll cover:

  • Programming basal insulin,

  • Starting and stopping a temporary basal,

  • And administering an insulin bolus.

  • Finally, we'll touch upon some useful tips and precautions.

3.1 Programming basal insulin

The mylife Ypsopump permits only two basal insulin rates - basal A and basal B. If a single basal rate suffices for you, simply set basal B to match basal A.

To program the basal rate, navigate to "basal rates" in the main menu and select Basal A. Adjust the insulin units per hour for the first hour of the day by scrolling, then confirm with the check mark. Repeat this process for all 24 hours. Swipe right to revisit the previous hour. Upon completion, the pump displays the total basal insulin dose over a day along with a graph of the scheduled basal rates. Confirm to save the settings. Under basal rates submenu, you'll now see a total daily basal insulin dose listed against Basal A.

As stated, Basal B must be set up similarly to Basal A. Select basal B in the main menu and program the basal insulin rate accordingly.

If you want to stop basal insulin delivery, this can be done via the main menu, opting for stop mode. Resuming insulin delivery involves the same steps.

A notification will pop up if the pump is in stop mode for over an hour. To receive this alert instantly, long-press the function key, and confirm.

3.2 Temporary basal

To start a temporary basal, visit the basal rates submenu and look for the icon symbolizing temporary basal. Here, you can set the rate (from 0 to 200%) and duration (15 minutes to 24 hours). Once confirmed, a percentage symbol appears next to the active basal profile (A or B) on the home screen. The basal rate alternates between the set percentage and the remaining duration of the temporary basal.

When the temporary basal expires, a message prompts for your acknowledgment. Regardless of your response, the pump defaults to the scheduled basal rate.

To cancel the temporary basal, access the basal rates submenu and select the temporary basal icon, which switches the pump back to the pre-set basal rate.

3.3 Delivering a bolus

To administer a bolus, access the main menu and click on the bolus icon represented by a knife and fork. A submenu presents three options: standard bolus, extended bolus, and combination bolus. The mylife YpsoPump lacks an inbuilt bolus calculator, so this setting isn't available here.

  • For a standard bolus, you can select an amount between 0.1 and 30 units. An icon on the status screen denotes an active bolus, with a countdown showing the remaining units to be delivered. The bolus can be canceled by pressing the cross.

  • If you opt for an extended bolus, after setting the bolus units, specify the delivery duration (ranging from 15 minutes to 12 hours). The status screen will then display not only the remaining insulin units to be delivered but also the time left.

  • For a combination bolus, input the total bolus units first, then specify the immediate units, and lastly, the duration for the remaining bolus delivery. The top-left icon indicates the active bolus settings.

3.4 Do's & don’ts

  • The pump lacks a built-in bolus calculator. Hence, if you wish to administer an insulin bolus based on carbohydrate intake, it should be done via the mylife Loop app or the mylife Dose app.

  • The maximum bolus on the mylife YpsoPump is fixed at 30 U.

  • The mylife Ypsopump also features a blind bolus function to deliver a bolus without viewing the pump. How to activate this function and the steps involved can be found in the instruction manual.

  • If you prefer a larger bolus increment, you can modify this. From the main menu, select the settings icon represented by a wrench, and choose Set bolus increment. You can adjust it to 0.1, 0.5, 1, or 2 units.

  • At times, you may not be able to deliver a combination bolus. This occurs when the remaining insulin in the reservoir is less than twice the bolus increment.

And there you have it, a guide on how to program and modify insulin settings on the mylife YpsoPump. In our next video, we'll discuss how to replace the insulin reservoir and infusion set.

docs.title: 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (8 min)
docs.text:
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (8 min)

Welcome, in this video we offer comprehensive guidance on how to change the infusion set and reservoir of your mylife YpsoPump.

Our video commences with an overview of various infusion sets, followed by a systematic, four-step procedure to replace both the insulin and the infusion set. Lastly, we address some essential do's and don'ts.

4.1 Different infusion sets

Our demonstration centers around the commonly used mylife Orbit Soft infusion set. It's worth noting that you can also utilize the mylife Orbit Micro infusion set with a steel cannula with the mylife YpsoPump.

  • Both infusion sets feature a 90-degree angle cannula, but there are no options for angled cannulas.

  • These infusion sets can be manually inserted, or you can use the mylife Orbit inserter for assistance.

  • A distinctive aspect of these infusion sets is their 360-degree rotatability, which allows for easy connection and disconnection without needing to look at the infusion site.

  • The infusion sets have a unique skin-colored patch that makes them less noticeable, but they might not stick as firmly, causing some users to require additional plaster.

  • Finally, the tubing of the mylife Orbit infusion sets are colored blue for easier detection of any air bubbles.

Below this video, we provide an overview of the different available infusion sets, including their tube and cannula lengths and filling volumes.

4.2 Supplies

To change the infusion set and insulin, you will need: your room-temperature insulin Pumpcart, your infusion set, a mylife Orbit inserter, an alcohol swab, and your mylife YpsoPump.

4.3 Reservoir replacement

Start by disconnecting the infusion set from your body. To do so, gently push the two marked sides of the infusion set, which will detach the tubing cap from the cannula base.

To replace the insulin cartridge, navigate to the insulin cartridge icon from the main menu. Here, you can retract the drive rod by pressing the double back arrow and confirming. After the drive rod has been retracted to 0%, confirm this and the pump will initiate a self-test.

Now, rotate the adapter a quarter turn counterclockwise to disconnect it and remove the insulin reservoir, adapter, and tube. Insert a new insulin Pumpcart into the cartridge holder (with the opening upwards) and position the adapter on top. The adapter should be positioned at a 90-degree angle to the pump and rotated a quarter turn towards the pump until it clicks into place.

If prefilled PumpCarts are not available, you need to fill a mylife Reservoir manually. Disinfect the insulin vial with an alcohol wipe. Take the mylife Reservoir and adapter from the package and attach the adapter to the Reservoir. Move the plunger up and down, then pull it back to fill the Reservoir with air. Attach the adapter to the insulin vial, inject the air into the vial, turn the vial and Reservoir upside down, and draw insulin into the Reservoir. If you see any air bubbles, tap the reservoir, and inject the air back into the insulin vial. Remove the adapter and unscrew the plunger from the Reservoir. You now have a filled mylife Reservoir ready for use in the mylife YpsoPump.

4.4 Filling the tubing

Next, fill the tube, a process which is quicker with the mylife YpsoPump than other insulin pumps. From the main menu, navigate to the icon with the needle and insulin droplets (representing infusion set pre-fill) and select tube filling. Set and confirm the filling volume. While the filling is in progress, hold the insulin pump upright and gently tap it against your palm to dislodge any air bubbles in the cartridge. Repeat this process until insulin exits the cannula and no air bubbles are visible in the tube. If you've filled the insulin reservoir yourself, check for any remaining air bubbles in the reservoir using the transparent cartridge holder.

4.5 Infusion set placement

You are now ready to insert the infusion set.

First, pick your infusion site, clean your hands, disinfect the site with an alcohol wipe and let it dry. You can manually insert the infusion set at a 90° angle or use the mylife Orbit inserter.

We will demonstrate the placement of a mylife Orbit Soft infusion set with the inserter:

  • Insert the infusion set into the inserter until it clicks into place.

  • Peel off the protective film from the plaster without touching the adhesive layer.

  • Gently rotate the protective cap to remove it from the cannula.

  • Turn the lower part of the inserter clockwise until it locks into place. Caution: the inserter is now activated. Avoid pointing it towards your eyes to prevent accidental injuries.

  • Position the inserter on the skin and press the button until it clicks.

  • Smooth the patch onto the skin, rubbing it gently for a few seconds to enhance adhesion.

  • While holding the plaster against your skin, use two fingers of your other hand to grasp the infusion set and remove the Insertion Needle or Insertion Cap by pressing the outer wings of the Insertion Cap and pulling it away.

  • Recap the removed Insertion Needle with the blue protective cap and dispose of it in a needle container.

  • Return the inserter to its original position.

  • Click the tubing cap directly onto the infusion set's cannula base. Turn the tube left and right, pulling up the cap to ensure it's securely engaged.

4.6 Filling the cannula

The final step is to fill the subcutaneous cannula. Navigate to the pre-fill infusion set icon from the main menu and select fill cannula. Here, set and administer the filling volume.

4.7 Do's & don’ts

  • The mylife Ypsopump is compatible with all short-acting insulins, but PumpCarts are only available for Novorapid and Fiasp. If you prefer Humalog, Lyumjev or Apidra, or you don't have access to PumpCarts, you can use mylife YpsoPump reservoirs. Always use short-acting insulin with a normal concentration of 100 E/ml.

  • Mylife Reservoirs can be refilled once within 7 days of the initial fill. Do not store a refilled reservoir. You can refill it using either a syringe and needle or the adapter. If using a syringe and needle, draw insulin from the vial and transfer it into the empty reservoir, making sure to remove any air bubbles. If using the adapter, you can first fill a new Reservoir from the package and store it in the refrigerator. Next, connect your used reservoir to the adapter, draw insulin into it, and reuse it once.

  • Always verify your glucose level 2 hours after inserting a new infusion set. This is the only way to confirm proper infusion set insertion and insulin delivery.

  • With the mylife Ypsopump, you can change the reservoir, tube and infusion set separately. So, you don't have to swap out your tube and infusion set each time you put in a new insulin reservoir. The infusion sets are also available separately from the tube.

  • The infusion sets of the mylife Ypsopump are connected and disconnected differently than other pump infusion sets. To disconnect the mylife Orbit infusion set, gently push the side ridges to release it. To reconnect, click the tubing cap onto the cannula base. Always make sure the tubing cap is securely in place by rotating the tube and pulling the cap upwards.

By now, you should feel confident in replacing both the infusion set and the insulin in your mylife YpsoPump. Thanks to pre-filled insulin vials, the process is relatively smooth. It may take a bit of practice to get used to the infusion sets and the inserter, but it's not more difficult than with other pumps. If you have any doubts, you can always refer back to this tutorial. Best of luck!

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms (4.5 min)
docs.text:
5. Pump alarms (4.5 min)

Welcome to this informative session about potential alarms on the mylife YpsoPump.

We'll delve into the different alarms and warnings that can appear on the mylife YpsoPump and offer some supplementary advice.

5.1 Alarms

Alarms are the highest priority alerts. They're signified by an exclamation mark inside a triangle icon, and always result in a cessation of insulin delivery.

Alarms given are primarily tactile and audible and there are 4 escalation levels. The levels escalate at intervals of 5 minutes. If an alarm is confirmed but not dealt with, it is given again by audible and tactile means after 30 minutes.

Alarms are arranged based on their priority, namely no battery, empty battery, battery not suitable, charging internal rechargeable battery, occlusion, no insulin, cartridge empty, automatic stop, or electronic error. What to do in these situations can be found in the manual, accessible in the downloads at the end of this video.

You can access all warnings in the alarm history. To do this, from the main menu, select the data icon depicted by a sheet of paper, and then the alert history icon represented by an exclamation mark within a triangle on a piece of paper.

5.2 Warnings

Warnings are lower priority alerts than alarms. They're symbolized by an 'i' icon but do not result in the cancellation of insulin delivery.

Possible warnings include cartridge low, battery low, drive rod not fully retracted, priming not completed, bolus canceled, temporary basal function completed or canceled, insulin pump stopped, and Bluetooth connection failure. Steps to be taken in these scenarios can be found in the manual, accessible in the downloads at the end of this video.

Like alarms, warnings can also be felt and heard in four escalating levels of intensity.

These warnings aren't displayed in the alarm history but need to be acknowledged on the status screen.

5.3 Do's & don’ts

  • If you're uncertain about what to do, always refer to the user manual or contact customer service. The manual not only covers warnings and notifications, but it also outlines other incidents. It details what actions to take if certain icons or functions are unavailable, or if there is insulin, dirt, or water in the pump. In such situations, always consult the manual.

  • A specific warning to note is the internal battery charging. The mylife YpsoPump features an internal rechargeable battery that's powered by the inserted alkaline battery. If the internal battery is heavily charged, it may deplete. In this case, the "charge internal battery" warning displays. The internal battery charges through the inserted AAA battery and is signified by arrows circling an hourglass. Charging could take up to 20 minutes. Active boluses and temporary basal will be halted during this period.

  • Another peculiar warning is the electronic error. In this situation, you'll immediately receive an audible and palpable signal, and all pump functions will be canceled. This is, thankfully, a rare occurrence. You'll be presented with two alternating screens instructing how to restart the pump. Disconnect the infusion set from your body and remove the battery. Press the function button for 2 seconds to put the pump into storage mode. The pump can then be restarted by reinserting the battery. Verify the settings and insert a new insulin reservoir and infusion set. Then, you can restart the pump.
