  • If for any reason you plan on temporarily not using the pump, it's recommended to place it in storage mode. Achieve this by stopping the pump and removing the battery. Replace the battery cover, and after 5 minutes, the pump will switch to storage mode.

Now, you're familiar with the primary alarms on the pump and what steps to take. Thus, there's no need to panic when you receive a pump alarm. If your pump is linked to the mylife Loop app, most alarms will typically display on your mobile phone, not your pump. If there's any confusion, always refer to the user manual or contact customer service. Good luck!

docs.title: 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2 min)
docs.text:
6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2 min)

In this final chapter, we’d like to showcase Tom from TypeOneTalks, an inspiring individual living with type 1 diabetes, as he shares his journey with the mylife YpsoPump. Through his personal experiences over an extended period, Tom will shed light on the pump's strengths, limitations, and the practical strategies he has developed to effectively manage his diabetes.

Don't miss this insightful look into the mylife YpsoPump and how it can make a difference in your life. Click on the link below this video to watch Tom’s review.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NyVc4u1XPiA – 12 min

Alongside Tom’s insightful video, we’d like to introduce the YpsoPump Explorer app, a virtual simulation of the mylife YpsoPump available on your smartphone. This app allows you to familiarize yourself with the pump's features, simulate basal delivery, and administer insulin boluses. It provides a safe and educational environment to better understand how the pump functions.

Now let's inject some fun into this insulin pump journey! Try out the YpsoPump Explorer app and show off your bolus simulation skills. Share a screenshot of your pump screen in the comments section and join our supportive community of pump masters. It's time to celebrate your insulin pump prowess together! Pump it up and let the simulation party begin!

Congratulations once again on finishing this module! By completing this course, you've acquired crucial knowledge about the mylife YpsoPump and taken a big leap forward in managing diabetes.

A heartfelt thank you for embarking on this journey with us! We're truly grateful for your participation and dedication. As you continue to rock diabetes management with the mylife YpsoPump, we wish you all the best. Keep up the fantastic work!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Pump Operation
  • 3. Insulin setup
  • 4. Replacing the Pod
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this module on the Omnipod DASH insulin pump! In this video, we will explore the features and operation of the Omnipod DASH. This patchpump is usually used as a separate manual pump, but can also be used in open-source automated insulin delivery systems such as AndroidAPS, DIY Loop, iAPS or Trio.

It's important to note that the Omnipod DASH is distinct from the Omnipod 5. The Omnipod 5 is Insulet’s commercial closed-loop system linked to a Dexcom or FreeStyle Libre sensor. While the external appearance of the Pods is similar between the two models, the software and functionality differ significantly. Additionally, the Omnipod 5's Controller is slightly larger and contains different software compared to the PDM of the Omnipod DASH.

This video specifically focuses on the Omnipod DASH insulin patch pump, providing valuable insights into its usage and functionality.

In the general module on insulin pumps, you have already learned about the fundamentals of insulin pumps, such as their operation, setting basal and bolus insulin, changing insulin and infusion sets, and managing alarms.

In the upcoming videos, we will showcase the Omnipod DASH in action, covering the entire onboarding education for this patch pump. However, please keep in mind that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

These videos have been created by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the St-Lucas Hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content has been reviewed by Dr. Sarah Siegelaar, an endocrinologist at Amsterdam UMC in the Netherlands, as well as Veerle Kerckvoorde, an individual living with type 1 diabetes who uses the Omnipod DASH in combination with AndroidAPS. Their contributions ensure that the information provided is unbiased and independent.

By the end of these videos, you will have a comprehensive understanding of how the Omnipod DASH insulin pump works, how to set basal and bolus insulin, how to change the insulin Pod, and what actions to take in case of an alarm.

Whether you are a caregiver looking to guide individuals using the Omnipod DASH or a user preparing for a successful start-up, these videos will equip you with valuable knowledge, along with practical tips and tricks to enhance your experience with the Omnipod DASH.

We wish you the best of luck on your journey with the Omnipod DASH insulin pump!

docs.title: 2. Pump Operation (6 min)
docs.text: Welcome to this video on how the Omnipod DASH insulin pump works! In this segment, we will explore the parts and buttons of the Pod and PDM, how to charge and unlock the PDM, the home screen and pump menu of the PDM, and some do's and don'ts.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts & buttons
docs.text:
The Omnipod DASH insulin pump consists of two main components: the Pod and the Personal Diabetes Manager (PDM). The Pod is a patch pump that needs to be replaced every 3 days, while the PDM allows you to control the Pod.

The PDM should be kept with you at all times, as it is not only used to deliver boluses but also to respond to Pod alarms. The PDM communicates with the Pod via Bluetooth and needs to be within a range of 1.5 meters (5 feet) to establish a connection. Once programmed, the Pod does not require continuous communication with the PDM for basal insulin delivery.

At the back of Omnipod DASH Pods there is a blue needle cap and a hole for insulin injection using the provided syringe and needle.

The Omnipod DASH PDM has various buttons and ports:

  • On the side, you will find the power button and a button for adjusting sound or vibration settings.

  • The front of the PDM features a large color touchscreen.

  • At the bottom, three buttons are present, with the left button serving as the previous screen button. The other buttons don’t have a function.

  • The top of the PDM has a micro USB port for charging and data transfer purposes.

  • The back of the PDM houses a rechargeable lithium battery, like a regular mobile phone.

docs.title: 2.2 Charging the PDM
docs.text:
It is recommended to charge the PDM using the cable and charger provided with the device. Charging the battery every day while you sleep is ideal, as it ensures a fully charged battery for approximately 2 days of usage. Charging the battery to 80% or more takes a maximum of 1.5 hours.

When the battery level is low, the PDM conserves power by disabling vibrations and sounds. If the battery completely drains, the PDM will turn off, but the Pod will continue administering basal insulin. You can continue using the PDM while it is charging.

docs.title: 2.3 Unlocking the PDM
docs.text: The PDM is locked with a PIN code, requiring you to enter it before accessing certain functions or delivering boluses. You can customize the security message and background on the lock screen. If you forget the PIN code, you can use the last 4 digits of the PDM's serial number as an alternative PIN code. The serial number can be found on the back of the PDM.

docs.title: 2.4 Home screen and pump menu
docs.text:
Key elements on the home screen include:

  • A status bar at the top displaying the remaining insulin, battery status, and PDM time.

  • A hamburger menu, date, settings icon, and an icon for alarms and notifications.

  • Three tabs in the center: Dashboard, Basal, and Pod Information. You can swipe or tap to navigate between the tabs.

  • The Dashboard tab shows a welcome message or displays insulin on board if the bolus calculator is enabled.

  • The Basal tab indicates the active basal profile and the total insulin dose for that profile. If a temporary basal is active, the tab turns green and displays "Temporary Basal."

  • The Pod Information tab shows the remaining insulin in the Pod and provides information on when it needs to be replaced.

  • Information about the last bolus given and the last glucose measurement is displayed below the tabs.

  • At the bottom of the screen, there is the bolus button.

When you click on the menu, you can set a Temp Basal, see Pod information, enter a Blood Glucose, Suspend your insulin, access your Basal Programs, change your PDM and Bolus Settings, and find information about your PDM version and serial number.

During operation, the PDM shows confirmations at the bottom of the screen through a green banner, which disappears after five seconds. You can swipe the banner to the right to close it earlier.

docs.title: 2.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are some important do's and don'ts when using the Omnipod DASH insulin pump:

  • Save the menu overview provided in the downloads for easy reference. As a healthcare provider, it will help you guide individuals through the pump menu remotely.

  • Omnipod DASH Pods are waterproof up to 7.6 meters or 25 feet for up to 1 hour.

  • The PDM is not waterproof, so avoid exposing it to water.

  • Take good care of the PDM, as it should last for approximately 4 years. The starter pack includes a free black silicone case for added protection, but you can also buy other colors online. It is also recommended to use a screen protector for the PDM.

  • The PDM does not automatically adjust the time, date, and time zone. You need to manually update the time when transitioning between winter and summer time or traveling to different time zones. This can be done in the settings under the PDM device.

  • Additionally, adjusting screen brightness and timeout settings can help conserve battery life.

With this information, you now have a better understanding of the main features of the Omnipod DASH system. One of the key advantages is the convenience of a patch pump without the hassle of catheters. You won't have to worry about getting stuck, and the Pods are barely noticeable when worn. However, keep in mind that you will need to carry both the PDM and the Pod, which can be a minor inconvenience. Knowing this in advance will help you adjust and adapt. Overall, the PDM's size and weight are manageable and shouldn't cause significant issues.

docs.title: 3. Insulin setup (7 min)
docs.text:
In this video, we will guide you through the process of setting the insulin delivery on the Omnipod DASH PDM.

Please note that the insulin will only start running once you have successfully paired a Pod with your PDM.

We will cover the following topics:

  • Programming basal insulin

  • Setting up temporary basal rates

  • Giving an insulin bolus

  • and some extra do’s and don'ts.

Let's get started!

docs.title: 3.1 Programming basal insulin
docs.text:
When you initially set up the PDM, you will be prompted to enter a basal insulin rate.

To edit an existing basal program, tap the Basal tab on the home screen, then select "View and edit." Pause the insulin delivery for a moment, and you can now edit the program's name and add labels if necessary. Tap Next to proceed. To modify an existing basal segment, tap that segment and adjust the end time or basal rate. To add a new basal segment, tap the row with the start time of the new segment. We recommend writing down your complete basal program before adding a new segment. Enter the end time of the previous segment as the start time of the new segment. Continue this process until you have completed the 24-hour basal program. To delete a basal segment, tap the segment preceding the one you wish to remove and enter the end time for that segment. The modified basal program will be saved, and you can tap "Resume insulin" to proceed.

To create a new basal program, tap the "Display" button in the Basal tab, then select "Create New" at the bottom. Enter a name for the program and add labels if needed. Tap Next to proceed and set the end time and basal rate for each time segment. Repeat this process until the last segment ends at midnight. Review the basal program for accuracy and tap "Save" to confirm.

To interrupt insulin delivery temporarily, go to the menu and tap "Suspend Insulin." You can set the desired duration, ranging from 30 minutes to 3 hours. A yellow banner will appear on the home screen, indicating that insulin delivery has been interrupted. During this interruption, the Pod will beep every 15 minutes. Once the duration expires, you will be prompted to resume insulin delivery. Insulin will not resume automatically, and both the Pod and PDM will continue beeping every 15 minutes until you choose to resume.

docs.title: 3.2 Temporary basal
docs.text:
You can only set a temporary basal if this option is enabled. To do this, access the menu, tap "Settings," then select "Basal" and "Temporary Basal." By default, the temporary basal rate is set as a percentage change. If you prefer an absolute speed in units per hour, you can modify this setting here.

To set a temporary basal rate, tap "Set Temp Basal" under the menu. Enter the desired basal rate and duration, ranging from 30 minutes to 12 hours. Confirm and activate the temporary basal rate.

To expedite the process, you can create presets for temporary basal rates. Up to 12 presets can be defined. To create a preset, tap "Preset Temporary Basal Speed" in the menu, then select "Create New." Enter a name and label if desired, followed by the basal rate and duration. Save the preset, and it will be accessible under the "Temporary Basal" option.

docs.title: 3.3 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
During the PDM setup, you will be required to configure the bolus calculator. These settings can be checked and changed via the menu, if you navigate to the Bolus Settings. Here you can see the settings for the Target BG, Correct Above value, Minimal BG for calculations, Insulin to Carb Ratio, Correction Factor (which is the insulin sensitivity), Reverse Correction and Duration of Insulin Action.

Specific for Omnipod is the Correct Above and Minimal BG value, which is the value above and below which a correction bolus will be calculated. If Reverse Correction is toggled on, the bolus calculator will also calculate a negative correction bolus if the blood glucose is below the target value.

To deliver a bolus, tap the bolus button on the PDM.

  • Enter the carbs you are going to eat, for example 15 grams, and enter your blood glucose, for example 100 mg/dl or 5.5 mmol/l.

  • You can also add labels to the blood glucose measurement by selecting one of the options.

  • The calculator will suggest meal and correction boluses based on the entered values. Confirm and start the bolus.

  • A progress bar will display during bolus delivery, and you have the option to cancel the bolus if needed.

To give an extended bolus, follow the same steps as for a normal bolus, but instead of confirming, choose "Extend Bolus." Enter the percentage of the meal bolus to be delivered immediately in the "Now" field. Specify the duration for the extended portion of the bolus, then confirm and start.

For a fixed bolus, just enter the desired number of units in the "Total Bolus" field, then tap on Confirm

3.4 Do's & don’ts

Here are some do's and don'ts to keep in mind:

  • Ensure maximum bolus limits are set slightly above expected bolus doses. You can set this between 0 and 30 units. 

  • In the bolus calculator settings, you can configure the minimum blood glucose for calculations and enable reverse correction.

  • The minimum blood glucose for calculations ensures that an insulin bolus is not given when the glucose level falls below a certain threshold for safety reasons. This can typically be set between 50-70 mg/dL or 2.8-3.9 mmol/l, with 60 mg/dL or 3.3 mmol/l being a common value.

  • Reverse correction determines whether the bolus calculator reduces the suggested insulin bolus when the glucose level is below the target value. It is generally recommended to have this feature enabled.

  • When setting up the PDM, you will be asked when you want to be reminded that your Pod is nearing empty and expiration. You can customize this reminder to suit your preferences, such as setting it at 10 units for the remaining insulin, and 4 hours before expiration of the Pod..

In conclusion, setting the insulin delivery on the Omnipod DASH is a straightforward and intuitive process. For a visual demonstration of each step, you can find helpful videos on YouTube. As a healthcare provider, it is crucial to familiarize yourself with the different pumps and their settings. With the Omnipod DASH, you can easily access all the necessary settings under the Basal tab and the Menu button.

docs.title: 4. Replacing the Pod (6.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on how to replace the Pod on your Omnipod DASH insulin pump!

In just 4 easy steps, we'll guide you through the process of changing a Pod. We'll cover everything from filling the Pod to linking it with the PDM, applying the Pod to your body, and finally, initiating it.

Get ready to dive into the nitty-gritty, and we'll also share some essential do's and don'ts along the way.

docs.title: 1) Pod filling and replacement
docs.text:
To insert a Pod, gather the following supplies: your PDM, a new Pod, alcohol wipes, and insulin at room temperature.

#1 Filling the Pod

On the PDM, navigate to Pod Info and select "Set New Pod." If you already have an active Pod, tap "Show Pod Data" first, and then tap "Replace Pod." Deactivation takes a few seconds. Once the green banner confirms deactivation, you can remove the Pod. The PDM is now ready to activate a new Pod.

The PDM will guide you through the process of filling a new Pod. Start by opening a sealed Pod, preferably keeping it in its holder during preparation and filling. Use an alcohol wipe to clean the top of your insulin vial. Attach the needle to the syringe and remove the protective cap. Draw an amount of air into the syringe equal to the insulin you want to fill the Pod with. Inject the air into the insulin vial and then invert the vial. Fill the syringe with the desired amount of insulin. To remove air bubbles, allow them to rise and gently push them back into the vial. Adjust the plunger to draw the desired insulin amount into the syringe and remove the needle from the vial. The filling port of the Pod is indicated by a black arrow at the bottom. Hold the syringe perpendicular and insert the needle into the fill port. Slowly push the plunger to fill the Pod with insulin. You will hear two beeps once the Pod is filled with at least 85 units of insulin. Keep pushing the plunger to empty the syringe completely even after hearing the two beeps.

docs.title: #2 Linking the Pod to the PDM
docs.text: To pair the Pod with the PDM, place the filled Pod next to the PDM, ensuring that the holder and PDM are touching. On the PDM, press "Next" and wait for the Pod to connect and perform safety checks automatically. Wait for the audible confirmation indicating that the Pod is paired and ready for application.

docs.title: #3 Pod placement
docs.text: Before applying the Pod, remove the blue needle cap. Use the pull tabs to remove and discard the white paper covering the adhesive tape. Carefully position the Pod in the desired infusion site. Ensure the Pod is applied evenly and flat on the skin to avoid adhesive wrinkles. Once applied, run your finger over the edge of the adhesive tape to secure it in place.

docs.title: #4 Starting the Pod
docs.text:
After applying the Pod, it's time to insert the cannula under the skin. If the infusion site has little body fat, consider using the squeeze method. Place your hand on the Pod and gently squeeze the skin around the infusion site. Keep the skin squeezed until the cannula is inserted. Find a comfortable position before proceeding. When ready, tap "Start" on the PDM, then confirm. Within seconds, the cannula will be inserted with minimal discomfort.

The PDM will prompt you to verify the correct insertion of the cannula. Check the pink insertion area on the surface of the Pod. A light pink square above the view window indicates that the cannula has been properly inserted. You can also check the light blue cannula in the view window. If the cannula is correctly inserted, tap "Yes" on the PDM. A green banner will indicate that the Pod is set.

docs.title: 4.2 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are some important points to keep in mind:

  • It is necessary to replace the Pod when the reservoir is empty or after 72 hours of use. The PDM will give you an alarm in advance for both scenarios. You should also replace the Pod if it detaches from the infusion site or when indicated by the PDM alarm.

  • The Omnipod DASH Pods are compatible with Novorapid, Novolog, Humalog, Apidra, Fiasp and Lyumjev. However, Apidra requires Pod replacement every 2 days according to the manual. The Pods are only approved for insulin with a concentration of 100 U/ml.

  • The Pod can hold a minimum of 85 units and a maximum of 200 units of insulin. If you require more than 200 units of insulin within 3 days, this system may not be suitable for you.

  • Some individuals experience a slight increase in blood glucose levels after Pod changes. To prevent this, consider giving a 1-unit bolus with each Pod change.

  • The Pods come with a fixed cannula length of 6.5 mm or 0.25 inch at a 45-degree angle. There is no option for a longer cannula for individuals with thicker skin. It's important to be aware that these short cannulas can sometimes get kinked like other infusion sets.

  • In some countries, PodPals are included in the starter pack. These are overtapes for the Omnipod Pods. Alternatively, you can purchase other overtapes online to ensure the Pod stays securely in place.

  • Remember to check your blood glucose levels 2 hours after inserting a new Pod. This is the only way to confirm proper insertion and insulin delivery. It's best to avoid changing the Pod right before bedtime.

  • Pods must be replaced every 72 hours or 3 days. Plan Pod changes for different days of the week since you receive exactly one Pod every 3 days. The PDM will provide an alarm at 72 hours, but the Pod will continue working for an additional 8 hours before it stops functioning.

  • If you wish to recycle the Pods, you can request a recycling box from the company, depending on the country you live in.

That's it! Now you're familiar with the process of replacing the Pod. We personally found it comfortable to wear the pump, hardly noticing its presence. If the cannula hits a muscle, it might lead to discomfort, in which case it's better to insert another Pod. The replacement process can go very fast, and experienced users report completing it in just 3.5 minutes! This is notably shorter than the procedure for catheter pumps since there's no need to fill an additional catheter. You also don't have to worry about forgetting how to change the Pod, because the PDM will guide you through the entire process!

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms (5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson about the alarms on the Omnipod DASH insulin pump.

The Pod and the PDM can alert you to various issues through beeping or vibrating. In Omnipod DASH, the alerts are categorized as hazard alarms, advisory alarms, notifications, and communication errors.

Let's explore these alarms and provide some additional tips.

docs.title: 5.1 Hazard alarms
docs.text:
Hazard alarms are high-priority alarms indicating a serious problem that has occurred.

After a hazard alarm, the Pod must be replaced.

You will hear a continuous beep from the Pod or the PDM, accompanied by a notification with a red triangle.

Possible causes include an empty reservoir, an expired Pod, an occlusion, or auto-off (an adjustable automatic shutdown of the pump after a period of inactivity).

If you hear a continuous beep, your PDM will display the specific problem.

After reading the message, the Pod will often be deactivated, and it's important to replace it as soon as possible. In certain cases, contacting customer service may be necessary.

docs.title: 5.2 Advisory alarms
docs.text:
Advisory alarms have a lower priority but require prompt action.

You will hear intermittent beeps from the Pod and or PDM and see a notification with an orange sphere.

Possible causes of a warning alarm include an expired Pod, low reservoir, low battery, resume insulin alert, and auto-off signal.

If you delay addressing a warning alarm, it can escalate into a hazard alarm.

docs.title: 5.3 Notifications
docs.text:
Notifications are customizable reminders that you can set.

Examples include reminders for an almost expired Pod, no active Pod, a blood glucose check after a bolus, or a missed bolus within a specific time segment.

You can set these reminders yourself via the menu.

docs.title: 5.4 Communication errors
docs.text:
Communication errors can occur when the PDM is too far from the Pod or if external signals disrupt the communication.

The PDM may indicate a communication error with two dots in the status bar. This is usually a common issue that can be resolved easily.

If the PDM fails to establish communication with the Pod during an action, you will receive a communication error message. In such cases, you can try repeating the action, cancel it, or replace the Pod if communication cannot be restored.

Communication failures can be caused by:

  • Your body: If administering a bolus while the Pod is on the back of your body, you may need to hold the PDM next to your body, aligned with the Pod, during delivery.

  • Your environment: Some places may have better communication than others. Certain locations, such as remote outdoor areas, may pose communication challenges. If you face difficulty in one location, try moving to another.

  • Interaction with other electronics: Rooms with high radio wave activity, Wi-Fi routers, or USB-3.0 ports may affect communication. Avoid such spaces when establishing communication.

To refresh the communication with the Pod without issuing a command, simply tap the "Pod Info" tab on your PDM. This ensures your PDM receives the latest information, such as insulin remaining in the Pod.

docs.title: 5.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are some important points to remember:

  • To properly acknowledge an alert, it is not enough to just read it. You must click on the blue bell icon, read the dark blue notification, scroll down if needed, and swipe left on the message.

  • If you are unsure how to respond to an alarm, consult the user manual or contact customer service.

  • To familiarize yourself with the alarm sounds or vibrations, you can test them through the menu settings by selecting "Check Alarms."

  • If a Pod alarm becomes persistent and cannot be turned off using the PDM, you can use the end of a paperclip to press the small hole at the back of the Pod (opposite the cannula). This will silence the alarm of the "screaming Pod."

  • The PDM can store up to 90 days of alarm history. To access previous hazard and advisory alerts, tap the bell icon in the top right corner. New alerts are displayed with a blue icon, while previously viewed notifications have a grey icon.

  • If you temporarily won't be using the PDM, you can simply switch it off.

Congratulations! You have completed the final lesson on the Omnipod DASH pump. Now you have all the necessary knowledge to successfully start using this pump. Initial setup is usually supervised by a diabetes nurse, and if you have any further questions, don't hesitate to consult them. Well done on reaching this point!

docs.title: 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2.5 min)
6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Congratulations! You have successfully completed the course on the Omnipod DASH insulin pump, gaining valuable knowledge about its features and functionalities. Your dedication to learning about this innovative pump demonstrates your commitment to effectively managing diabetes. Now, let's delve into a real-life case that showcases the practical aspects of using the Omnipod DASH pump.

In this chapter, we want to highlight a review of DiabeticDanica that discusses the Omnipod DASH insulin pump. The video provides an authentic perspective on using the pump in daily life. Dana explores the pros and cons of this tubeless insulin delivery system, shedding light on its unique features. Please watch the video by clicking on the link below this video!

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=flBKilxCrow – 11 min

Hey, here's something truly exciting to enhance your Omnipod DASH experience! Did you know there's a virtual PDM available for your desktop? That's right! You can now simulate the PDM interface and tinker with various insulin settings right from your computer. It's like having your own diabetes playground!

Get ready for some hands-on fun as you test different scenarios and explore the ins and outs of the PDM virtually. We encourage you to take up the challenge and share your adventure in the comments section below!

Here's how to join the virtual PDM challenge:

1. Click on the link below this video to open the virtual PDM.

2. Familiarize yourself with the app's cool interface and its incredible functionalities.

3. Play around with insulin settings and deliver an insulin bolus of 5 units.

4. Capture a screenshot of your virtual PDM in action, giving that epic bolus moment!

5. Share your awesome screenshot in the comments and let the celebration begin!

By taking part in this challenge, not only will you master the Omnipod DASH system, but you'll also be contributing to the community's knowledge and learning from other fellow adventurers. So, let's dive into this virtual journey together and have a blast!

Once again, congratulations on completing this module! Your commitment to learning and exploring new technologies for diabetes management is commendable. With the knowledge you have acquired, you are well-equipped to guide and support others using the Omnipod DASH pump. Remember, continuous learning and open communication with healthcare providers are vital in optimizing diabetes care.

Best of luck on your journey with the Omnipod DASH insulin pump, and we hope it brings you improved convenience and enhanced quality of life. Keep up the excellent work, and don't hesitate to share your experiences and questions with the community!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Pump Operation
  • 3. Insulin set-up
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to our focused exploration of the Tandem Mobi insulin pump!

By now, you've gained a solid understanding of insulin pump functionality through our previous general insulin pump videos. You've delved into fundamentals of how insulin pumps work, setting basal and bolus insulin, changing insulin and infusion sets, and handling alarms.

Now, we turn our attention to the Tandem Mobi Insulin Pump. In this module, you'll walk through a comprehensive set-up education for this advanced pump. These explanations will benefit healthcare professionals guiding individuals using the Tandem Mobi Insulin Pump with Control-IQ technology and offer support for those initiating the use of the Tandem Mobi Insulin pump.

It is important to note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. Always consult your healthcare provider before adjusting your therapy.

The videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the AZ St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Gary Scheiner, a Certified Diabetes Care and Education Specialist from Integrated Diabetes Services in the US. Here you can see their disclosures. Their expertise and personal experiences ensure that the information presented in the videos is comprehensive and unbiased.

By the end of this video, you'll have a comprehensive understanding of the Tandem Mobi Insulin Pump:

  • How the Tandem Mobi insulin pump operates

  • How to set basal and bolus insulin on the pump

  • How to change the insulin reservoir and infusion set

  • And what actions to take in case of pump alarms

Finally, we'll provide a link to a review from a real Mobi pump user and demonstrate the Mobi simulator for you to test the system yourself.

This will enhance your ability as a healthcare provider to assist Tandem Mobi users or, as a user, to prepare for a successful set-up. We'll also provide you with handy tips and tricks to make your life with the Tandem Mobi Insulin Pump easier.

Best of luck on your journey with the Tandem Mobi Insulin Pump!

docs.title: 2. Pump Operation
docs.text: Welcome to our comprehensive guide on the Tandem Mobi insulin pump! By the end of this module, you'll have a thorough understanding of how the Tandem Mobi insulin pump works, its various features, including the pump's components, charging the pump, unlocking process, the screens and menus on the Tandem Mobi Mobile app and do’s and don’ts.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts & buttons
docs.text:
The Tandem Mobi pump is a modular semi-patch pomp. It has around the same size and weight as an Omnipod pump. It consists of a durable pump base that has a guarantee of 4 years, and a disposable cartridge and t:lock Connector that should be replaced every 3 days.

The pump has no screen and is controlled by the Tandem Mobi Mobile app. It has 1 pump button on the bottom. This button is used to turn the pump on or off, deliver a Quick Bolus, snooze alerts and alarms and activate the pump status lights.

Above the pump button there are 2 status lights that light up in different colors and patterns to let you know what the pump is doing even if you don't have your smartphone with you. To view the lights you need to press and release the pump button. After a few seconds the pump status lights will light up and the pattern will display for 10 seconds.

Each color has a different meaning:

- RED represents an alarm or malfunction. This means that all insulin delivery has stopped. If you see this please check your pump as soon as possible.

- YELLOW represents an alert or a reminder such as a sensor expiring soon or low battery

- BLUE represents bolus delivery or the tubing being filled during the cartridge loading process.

- WHITE represents either that the pump is charging or insulin delivery has been stopped manually

- GREEN represents basal delivery or a temp basal rate is being delivered.

Refer to your user guide to learn more about the pump status lights blinking patterns. Understanding these components and indicators will ensure you can effectively manage your insulin delivery with the Tandem Mobi Insulin Pump.

docs.title: 2.2 Charging the Pump
docs.text:
When you receive the pump, you'll need to charge it first. The Tandem Mobi pump does not have a charging port. Instead it comes with an inductive charging pad that connects to a power source using a USB-A to a USB-C cable. It also includes accessories for charging from a wall outlet. You should only use accessories that have been provided by Tandem Diabetes Care and we don't recommend using adapters or extension cords.

While charging your system make sure the inductive charging pad is plugged into a functioning wall outlet. There is a logo on the back of the pump and a t- logo on the top of the charging pad. Align these logos and note that the pump will not be centered on the pad.

There are two signs to look for that your pump is charging

  • first the charging pad will briefly light up

  • second you'll see a lightning bolt icon on the top of the battery symbol in the upper left corner of the tandem Mobi mobile app.

The pump status lights can also be used to show the charge amount

  • one of the lights will blink if the charge is less than 50%

  • if the charge is more than 50% one light will be solid white while the other blinks until the pump is fully charged.

You will know charging is complete when the pump status lights above the pump button are white. A full charge will typically last between 3 and 5 days depending on your use.

If your pump power is very low, one light will blink red or it may not turn on. It is recommended to avoid fully draining the battery and charging the pump daily for a short period of time, like 10 to 15 minutes.

The pump will work normally while it is charging so you don't need to disconnect if you don't want to. However if you do disconnect while charging you may need to replace missed basal insulin. Never disconnect your pump during the delivery of an insulin bolus, and always check your blood glucose levels before disconnecting and again before reconnecting.

If you lose any accessories or need replacements, you can contact Tandem customer technical support.

docs.title: 2.3 Unlocking the pump
docs.text:
Next, we'll proceed to show you how to turn the pump on and off.

To turn the pump off, place it on the charging pad and make sure the logos on the pump and the pad align. Don't worry if it looks a little off center. Now hold the pump button for 20 seconds. The pump will beep three times before it will turn off.

To turn on the pump, place it on the charging pad. While it is on the charging pad, press and hold the pump button until you see the pump status lights on the pump light up. The pump will beep four times when it has turned on and is ready to use.

Be aware that when you first receive your pump you may be able to see the pump status lights when the pump button is pressed. This does not necessarily mean that the pump is turned on.

docs.title: 2.4 Home screen and pump menu on Tandem Mobi Mobile app
docs.text:
Now, let’s move on to learning more about the mobile app. We will begin with an overview of the Dashboard.

Once your pump has synced with the mobile app, pump data can be viewed on the mobile app’s main screen, or Dashboard. At the top, the mobile app displays the pump battery level. Please note that this icon is different from the phone battery level which is shown on the top right corner of the screen. The antenna icon shows CGM communication status and next to that, alert status is displayed when an alert is active. On the top right, you’ll find the Basal and Bolus delivery status icons as well as the amount of insulin remaining in the cartridge. Below those, Insulin on Board is shown. On the left, you will see the most recent glucose reading with a trend arrow. In the center of the Dashboard is the graph of the most recent glucose readings. This graph displays sensor readings for the last 24 hours. You can swipe left and right to see all 24 hours in the graph. The graph includes glucose values that were entered into the bolus calculator, shown as a circle on the graph. At the bottom of the Dashboard, you see the basal rates for the past 24 hours including changes in insulin delivery related to use of Control-IQ technology. The amount of insulin delivered as a bolus is shown as a droplet. This includes meal boluses, manual correction boluses, and any Automatic Correction Boluses within the timeframe displayed on the Dashboard.

If you tap on a bolus icon or a glucose value on the graph, detailed information about that event will pop up on the screen. Right beneath the graph, you see your Time in Range for the past 24 hours. This is calculated based upon the values you entered into the Target Glucose Limits found in Glucose Threshold Settings. When you swipe up, you find the Current Status of the pump. At the top, the current Basal Rate is displayed. Information on the last bolus delivered is shown here. You see the current status of Control-IQ technology. Next, you see the settings for the active Personal Profile and beneath that you see the status of the CGM system which may vary by sensor.

Within the Pump menu, you find the following:

This is where you program Personal Profiles and where you turn on Control-IQ technology. You program the Quick Bolus settings and Delivery Limits which are the Max Bolus and Basal Rate Limit values. You program the Time and Date for your pump from this menu. So, this is where you need to go after the time changes or when you travel to update the time on your pump since it is not automatically updated when the time on your phone changes. You can find Pump Info and at the bottom is where you go to update your pump software.

docs.title: 2.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few important tips to keep in mind:

  • You can find a comprehensive menu overview in the downloads section, which can be saved for easy reference when providing remote guidance as a healthcare provider.

  • The pump is water resistant, for up to 2 hours at a depth of 2,4 meters (or 8 feet).

  • The Tandem Mobi mobile app is available only on selected iPhone models and is now gradually rolling out to selected Android devices. You can check your device’s compatibility on Tandem’s website.

  • The Tandem Mobi mobile app must run in the background in order to receive and transmit data to and from your pump, as well as to the Tandem cloud. Be sure to disable battery optimization on your smartphone to ensure the Tandem Mobi mobile app can receive alerts and alarms.

  • It’s also possible that there is a Bluetooth disconnection between the Mobi pump and your smartphone.

  • In that case you will see a “Pump connection lost” notification banner at the top of the homescreen of the Tandem Mobi Mobile app, together with how much time has lapsed since your pump disconnected from your smartphone. You will also see a gray shaded area on the graph since no data can be displayed when the connection is lost.

  • The Mobi pump will continue to deliver insulin as intended, receive glucose values if paired to a CGM, and all pump alerts, alarms, reminders, and malfunctions will annunciate on your pump even when disconnected from your smartphone.

  • The Bolus screen on your Tandem Mobi Mobile app will be inaccessible when disconnected. However, if enabled previously, the Pump button on the pump may still be used to deliver a Quick Bolus.

  • Take these steps to reconnect your pump to your smartphone:

  • Make sure that your pump and smartphone are within 1.5 meter or five feet of one another and without any obstruction between the two (including body parts).

  • If you have persistent issues with the Tandem Mobi mobile app, close or force stop the Tandem Mobi mobile app to end the current session. If the issue persists, try re-pairing the pump.

  • The Tandem Mobi pump does not automatically adjust the time and date. When changing between winter and summer time, or when traveling to a different time zone, you need to adjust the time yourself. You can do that via the pump settings.

  • Finally, it’s important to note that you should avoid placing metal objects on the charging pad.

Congratulations! You now have a good understanding of how the Tandem Mobi Insulin pump works, including its parts, charging process, how to turn the pump on and off, and the different screens and menus. In the next video, we will delve into setting insulin delivery, so stay tuned!

docs.title: 3. Insulin set-up
docs.text:
Welcome to our detailed guide on insulin delivery set-up for the Tandem Mobi insulin pump.

We'll explore how to program basal insulin, set a temporary basal rate, and administer an insulin bolus. Additionally, we'll share some crucial do's and don'ts to enhance your pump experience.

docs.title: 3.1 Programming basal insulin
docs.text:
• To access your personal profile tap ‘settings’ from the navigation bar, next tap ‘Pump’, tap ‘Personal profiles’, tap ‘Add New Profile’ then tap the ‘Profile Name field’.

  • Enter a profile name up to 16 characters and tap ‘next’. Tap ‘Add Segment’ to begin setting insulin delivery settings. The first Time Segment will start at 12 AM.

  • Within each time segment, you must program a basal rate, correction factor, carb ratio and target BG. To enter values for each variable, tap the area labeled “Tap to set up”. A number keypad will appear, and once you have entered the first one, simply tap the field to enter the next value. Tap Done to close the keypad.

  • Review all of the values you just entered and tap ‘Save’. A Time Segment has been saved banner is displayed at the top of the Tandem Mobi mobile app.

  • If you need to add more time segments tap the plus icon. When adding more time segments, any settings that you entered in the previous time segment are copied and appear in the new segment. This allows you to simply adjust only the specific settings you want rather than having to enter them all over again.

  • Tap ‘Add Segment’ then tap ‘ Start Time’. Using the on-screen keypad enter the time that you want the time segment to begin and tap’ Done’. Remember that this segment will be active from the start time until 12 AM or until the next segment you enter.

  • When you have confirmed your settings and are done adding time segments, tap the back arrow icon to program your bolus settings. First tap the ‘Bolus Settings’ panel to get started. By default the insulin duration is set at 5 hours and the carbohydrates are turned on. We recommend to leave these settings on the default values.

  • To delete a time segment, tap the start time of the time segment you wish to delete and tap ‘Delete Segment’. Tap ‘Yes’ to confirm.

  • If you want to Rename an existing Profile, from navigation bar, tap ‘ setting’. Tap ‘ Pump’, then ‘Personal Profiles’. Tap the name of the Personal Profile to be renamed. Tap ‘Profile Options’ then tap ‘ Rename’.

  • When you have confirmed your settings, tap the ‘Dashboard’ to return to the home screen. If you need to add additional personal profiles repeat the steps outline in this video.

  • You may also duplicate an existing personal profile. Then select edit to change only certain settings to create a new profile.

To stop basal insulin, from the navigation bar, tap ‘Actions’ then select 'Stop insulin' and tap ‘Yes’. When insulin is stopped, the home screen will show the status “All deliveries stopped”. Restarting basal insulin can be done in the same way via ‘Actions’.

docs.title: 3.2 Temporary basal
docs.text:
To start a temporary basal, navigate to 'Actions' from the navigation bar and select 'Temp Rate'. Here, you can set the percentage and duration of your choice. Tap Dashboard on the Navigation bar to view the Dashboard screen and confirm the icon indicating a Temp Rate is active. A 'T' in a yellow box means Temp Rate is Active, while a ‘T’ in a red box means Temp Rate of 0 u/hr is active. You can always manually turn off the temporary basal, which reverts the 'T' back to the current system settings.

3.3 Delivering a bolus

Administering a bolus is straightforward. From the start screen, hit the bolus button, input the carbohydrate grams and, if necessary, a finger prick value. If paired with a glucose sensor, the last glucose value will auto-fill. The pump recommends an insulin dose, which you can override.

A handy tip is the option of extended bolus delivery. When confirming a bolus, you can opt to make it an extended bolus and adjust the percentage and time duration as needed.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• Ensure maximum bolus limits are set slightly above expected bolus doses. You can find the Max Bolus via the pump settings. Tap ‘Delivery Limits’, then tap ‘Max Bolus’. The maximum bolus defaults to 10 units, but you can set it between 1 and 25 U.

  • It's recommended to set the Target BG value at 110 mg/dl or 6.5 mmol/l, as this is consistent with the target used when using Control-IQ technology.

  • It is recommended that all users set up the Quick Bolus function which enables you to deliver a bolus by simply pressing the pump button. It is a way to deliver a bolus by following beep/vibration commands without navigating through or viewing the pump screen.

And that's it! With this knowledge, you can effectively set-up and adjust insulin on the Tandem Mobi Insulin pump. In our next video, we'll walk you through changing the insulin reservoir and infusion set.

docs.title: 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (7.5 min)
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on reservoir and infusion set replacement on the Tandem Mobi insulin pump!

In this video, we will cover the different infusion set options, step-by-step instructions for replacing the reservoir and infusion set, and helpful tips for success.

docs.title: 4.1 Different infusion sets
docs.text:
The Autosoft 90 infusion set is the most widely used infusion set for the Tandem pump. Other alternatives include the Autosoft 30, the Varisoft with an angled cannula, and the TruSteel with a steel cannula.

Additionally, Tandem provides different lengths of tubing. Most people use the short tubing of (12 cm or 5 inches) to keep the pump close to the infusion site on the skin, but you can also opt for the longer tubing options of (60 to 110 cm or 23 to 43 inches) to wear the pump in your pocket.

A detailed overview of these infusion sets, including tubing and cannula lengths and filling volumes, can be found in the download below this video.

docs.title: 4.2 Reservoir & Infusion Set Replacement
docs.text: Start by washing your hands and ensuring your workspace is clean. Gather these items: a reservoir, a cartridge, your insulin (at room temperature), an infusion set, some alcohol wipes, and your pump.

docs.title: #1 Reservoir replacement
docs.text:
Make sure your hands are clean and you've swabbed the top of the vial with alcohol first. Let's prepare the new cartridge. You do this by pulling down the fill rod and then pushing it back up to force air out of the cartridge. Next pull the fill rod down to the desired volume. You can measure this by using the top ring on the plunger to align with the desired amount of insulin. Now it's time to fill the cartridge. Start by placing the insulin vial upright on a flat surface. You'll push the vial adapter down onto the vial. Next push the fill rod down to force air from the cartridge into the vial. Be sure to maintain pressure on the fill Rod. Then turn the set with the vial still secured upside down and slowly release the fill Rod. Insulin will begin to flow from the vial into the cartridge. From here, you can slowly pull back the fill Rod to the desired amount of insulin. Keep in mind that there must be at least 30 units of insulin remaining in the cartridge once the tubing is filled. Next tap and rotate the cartridge to release any potential air bubbles. If you see air bubbles, bubbles push up on the fill Rod to force them out. Keep in mind that pushing out air bubbles may also push out some insulin. Make sure to check that you have enough insulin in the cartridge. Finish the fill process by removing the vial from the vial adapter.

Next, it's time to remove the cartridge from the set. Start by unscrewing the fill Rod counterclockwise then gently release the cartridge from the set. Now unscrew the

vial adapter counterclockwise to remove it from the t-lock connector.

You'll be prompted to remove the empty cartridge from the pump by

rotating it counterclockwise. Be sure the piston on your pump is in the down position. It should look like this, not this. Now place the newly filled cartridge on the pump and rotate it clockwise until it clicks into place then tap continue.

Be sure you're following all proper disposal methods for needles, cartridges, set components and infusion sets.

docs.title: #2 Filling the tube
docs.text: Next you'll fill the tube. Confirm that the infusion set is disconnected from your body. Attach tubing from a new infusion set to the t-lock connector on the cartridge. Then tap continue. Hold the pump with the t-lock connector facing up. This will make sure any air in the cartridge will be pushed through the tubing first. Then tap yes on the mobile app. Make sure that your infusion set is disconnected from your body. Then press and hold the pump button to fill the infusion set tubing with insulin. Hold the pump upright and keep the pump button pressed, until you see drops of insulin at the end of the infusion set. Once you see drops of insulin release the pump button. This process is much faster with tandem Mobi than with the t-slim X2 pump. So pay close attention. Keep in mind that the pump may pause at regular intervals during the process. This is normal and allows you to check for drops of insulin. Tap yes if you see drops of insulin at the end of your tubing

docs.title: #3 Infusion set placement
docs.text:
Now, we're ready to apply the infusion set. We will demonstrate the Autosoft 90 infusion set. Start by sanitizing your skin and peel away the paper backing from the infusion set patch. Be sure to keep the infusion set firmly in place on the needle during this process. Next, prep the insertion set by tugging on the white plastic until you hear a click. At this point, you can safely remove the needle guard. Locate one of the two depressions on the insertion device and insert the tube. Position the prepared device on your cleaned skin and press the needle into place by applying gentle pressure on the two circular depressions. Press the white plastic one more time. Lastly, take care to gently remove the insertion device.

Don't forget to click "Finish" when prompted to continue insulin delivery. If you forget, an alarm will sound as a reminder.

You've now successfully replaced the insulin cartridge and infusion set! Remember to do this at least once every three days, even if the cartridge is not yet empty.

docs.title: #4 Cannula Filling
docs.text:
When you fill the tubing of the Autosoft infusion set, the cannula is filled automatically. If you ever replace only the infusion set but keep the existing tubing, you should manually fill the cannula. This can be done through the same pump menu. Fill the cannula of the Autosoft 90 infusion set with 0.3 units for 6-mm needles or 0.5 units for 9-mm needles. Angled cannula's need a filling volume of 0.7 units, and steel cannula's do not need to be filled.

4.3 Do's & don’ts

  • The insulin approved in the Tandem Mobi Insulin pump is NovoLog or NovoRapid and Humalog with a concentration of 100 U/ml..

  • Remember, 2 hours after inserting a new set, you should check your blood glucose. Checking your blood glucose is the only way to confirm your infusion set is properly inserted and that you're receiving insulin.

  • Prior to disposing of the insertion device, reattach the lid until it clicks.

  • When temporarily removing your insulin pump, it’s best to cover the connect piece of your infusion set. If you leave the protective cover open, dirt may enter into the opening which may cause infections or occlusions.

  • Make sure you're inspecting the tubing for air bubbles. Air and the tubing occupy space where insulin should be. And this could affect insulin delivery. Remember that changes in temperature or weather can cause bubbles. If you find bubbles in your cartridge, consider wearing the pump with the tubing angled down or to the side to help prevent the air from getting into the tubing. If you see air bubbles in the tubing, disconnect the infusion site from your body. Then under the load cartridge menu, select fill tubing and prime the tubing until the air is purged completely out. This will push the air out of the tubing.

  • Be aware that some skin care products such as lotions, sunscreens and insect repellant can cause cracks in the plastic of the cartridge or pump. Do not allow these products to come into contact with the pump or cartridge. Remember to always wash your hands before handling your pump or cartridge after using these types of products.

So now, you're equipped with the knowledge to replace the insulin cartridge and infusion set of the Tandem Mobi Insulin pump. If you make a mistake, don't worry – it's perfectly normal. Keep calm, follow the steps. Remember, if all else fails, there's always the Customer Service and a lot of useful YouTube videos available. Also, don't forget that you can contact your diabetes team, who also have an on-call service, for medical support. Keep up the good work! Good luck!

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms
docs.text:
In this video, we dive into the Tandem Mobi Insulin pump's alarm system. We'll discuss the different kinds of alarms, alerts, and reminders, and share some best practices.

The Notifications screen informs you when information related to your pump or CGM requires attention. The outline color of the notification indicates the importance of the information.

  • Red stands for alarms, and indicate that a malfunction has occurred on the pump and insulin delivery has been stopped.

  • Yellow stands for Pump or CGM alerts.

  • Blue indicates a reminder or informational message. Insulin therapy is not impacted.

Dismiss appears if you slide an alert notification with your finger to the left. Tap this icon to dismiss the alert.

Delete appears if you slide a reminder or informational notification with your finger to the left. Tap this icon to delete the reminder or informational message

If multiple reminders, alerts, and alarms happen at the same time, alarms will be displayed first, alerts will be displayed second, and reminders will be displayed third. Each must be acknowledged separately until all have been acknowledged.

If push notifications are enabled on your smartphone, and the Tandem Mobi mobile app is open, you will get the alert notification on the lock screen of your smartphone.

docs.title: 5.1 Alarms
docs.text:
Alarms act as urgent notifications, alerting you to a current or imminent interruption in insulin delivery, such as when the insulin cartridge is empty. These require immediate attention.

These alarms are signaled with three sequences of three tones or vibrations, as per your chosen volume/vibration settings. If left unacknowledged, alarms escalate to the maximum sound and vibration level and continue to repeat until the issue is fixed. The pump status lights will light up in red.

The Auto-Off Alarm is a user settable alarm. Other alarms are Resume Insulin Alarms, Low Power Alarm, Empty Cartridge Alarm, Cartridge Error Alarm, Temperature Alarm, Occlusion Alarms, Pump Button Alarm and Pump and IOB Reset Alarm.

docs.title: 5.2 Alerts
docs.text:
Alerts inform you about safety conditions that you need to know about, like low insulin levels.

These alerts are expressed with two sequences of three tones or two vibrations, following your volume/vibration preference. They continue to repeat until they are acknowledged, but they do not escalate. The pump status lights will light up in yellow.

Enabling the Snooze function allows you to silence this beep or vibration for a set period of time in the event that you are unable to look at your Tandem Mobi mobile app.

The Low Insulin Alert and Max Basal Alert are user settable alerts.

Other pump alerts are Pump Low Power Alert, Incomplete Bolus Alert, Incomplete Load Sequence Alert, Incomplete Setting Alert, Basal Rate Required Alert, Basal and Carb Ratio Required Alert, Max Hourly Bolus Alert, Max Bolus Alert, Max Basal Alert, Min Basal Alert, Pump Button Alert, Quick Bolus Alert, Data Error Alert and Temperature Alert.

Possible CGM alerts are different Calibration Alerts and Transmitter Alerts, CGM High Alert, CGM Low Alert, CGM Fixed Low Alert, CGM (Rapid) Rise Alert, CgM (Rapid) Fall Alert, Unknown Sensor Glucose Reading, Out of Range Alert, Failed Sensor Error, and CGM Unavailable.

Possible Control-IQ technology Alerts are Out of Range Alert, Control-IQ Technology Low Alert, Control-IQ Technology High Alert and Max Insulin Alert.

5.3 Reminders

Reminders notify you about an option you've set, such as a reminder to check your blood glucose after a bolus.

Reminders notify you with a single sequence of 3 notes or 3 vibrations depending on your volume and vibrate settings. They repeat every 10 minutes until acknowledged. Reminders do not escalate.

Examples are Low BG reminder, High BG Reminder, After Bolus BG Reminder, Missed Meal Bolus Reminder, Bolus Confirmation Reminder and Site Reminder.

docs.title: 5.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• A specific alarm is the 'malfunction' alarm. This indicates a critical error and all deliveries have been stopped. You will need to switch to your backup insulin delivery method. Take note of the fault code and contact customer service.

  • If in doubt, refer to the user manual or call customer service.

  • You can enable push notifications through the tandem Mobi mobile app. Just tap settings then alerts and sounds and then app notification settings. Make sure your notification preferences are enabled and that your smartphone will allow notifications from outside of the app. To do this, go to settings, then notifications, then toggle allow notifications to the on position. It’s recommended that you turn on critical alerts and time sensitive notifications. It’s also recommended that you leave the app running in the background and do not close or force quit the app. Acknowledging and clearing alerts within the tandem Mobi app will prevent the notification from recurring and can also help save your battery life.

  • Enabling the Snooze function allows you to silence this beep or vibration for a set period of time in the event that you are unable to look at your Tandem Mobi mobile app. To enable and set up Snooze, go to the Pump Sounds in the Alerts & Sounds menu.

  • If you won't be using your pump for a significant period, put it in storage mode. Place the pump on the charging pad and then press and hold down the Pump button for 20 seconds. The pump will beep 3 times before going into storage mode. Remove the pump from the power source.

Now you know what to do in the event of the pump's primary alarm, and you are more prepared. Therefore, if you hear a sound in your pump, there’s no need to panic. Just look at the screen and instructions are typically provided there. Consult the user manual or call customer care if the message is unclear. Wishing you luck!

docs.title: 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (1 min)
6. Case report & virtual pump challenge
docs.text:
In this final video, we'd like to highlight a video tutorial of Jillian Boleau, an individual living with type 1 diabetes, as she shows how to change your insulin cartridge, tubing and infusion set with the Tandem Mobi. This is great for visual learners who want to see all the steps on a real person. During the video she also gives several practical tips based on her personal experience which makes the video even more valuable.

Please click on the link below this video to watch her review.

TANDEM MOBI: Change Your Insulin Pump Site with Me!

In addition to Jillian's insightful review, we’d like to introduce the Tandem Mobi simulator website, a virtual Tandem pump available on your desktop for people in the US and some other countries. This simulator allows you to experiment and understand your pump's functionalities better.

You can find the website link below this video.

https://www.tandemdiabetes.com/products/software-apps/virtual-pump-demos/tandem-mobi-simulator

You've learned important information about the Tandem Mobi Insulin pump and advanced your diabetes management skills by finishing this course.

We appreciate you coming along this journey with us, and we wish you the best as you continue to use your insulin pump to manage your diabetes. Keep up doing amazing things, and never forget that your health and wellbeing are worth every bit of effort you put into taking care of them! Congratulations!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. How the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump works
  • 3. Insulin setup
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this informative video on the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump! This pump is primarily used in conjunction with Tandem Control-IQ technology, which combines it with a glucose sensor like a Dexcom G6, Dexcom G7 or FreeStyle Libre sensor for advanced diabetes management.

In previous general insulin pump videos, you learned the fundamentals of how insulin pumps work, setting basal and bolus insulin, changing insulin and infusion sets, and handling alarms.

In the following videos, we will specifically focus on the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump, providing a comprehensive start-up education for this widely utilized pump. These explanations will benefit healthcare professionals guiding individuals using the Tandem pump and offer support for those initiating the use of the Tandem pump. Please note that these videos are not intended as medical advice. It is crucial to consult with your healthcare provider before making any therapy adjustments.

The videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the general St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Dr. Liesbeth Van Huffel, an endocrinologist at OLV Hospital in Aalst in Belgium, along with Rosaline Jongeneelen and Ilse Faber, both Tandem Control-IQ system users residing in the Netherlands. Here you can see their disclosures. Their disclosures are provided below the video. Their expertise and personal experiences ensure that the information presented in the videos is comprehensive and unbiased.

By watching these videos, you will gain a clear understanding of:

  • How the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump operates

  • How to set basal and bolus insulin on the pump

  • How to change the insulin reservoir and infusion set

  • And what actions to take in case of pump alarms

As a healthcare professional, these videos will equip you with the knowledge to better guide individuals using the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump. For Tandem pump users, this resource will assist in preparing for a successful start with the pump, while also providing valuable tips and tricks to make life with Tandem Control IQ even more manageable.

Wishing you the best of luck on your journey with the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump, whether as a healthcare professional or an individual with diabetes.

docs.title: 2. How the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump works (6.5 min)
2. How the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump works (6.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this informative video on how the Tandem t:slim X2 pump works! In this segment, we will explore the various aspects of the pump, including its parts, buttons, charging process, unlocking mechanism, and the different screens and menus.

We will focus on the pump with Control IQ software. It's important to note that there is another version available with the Basal IQ software, which features a slightly different menu. However, we will not delve into the details of the Basal IQ software in this module.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts & buttons on the pump
docs.text:
Let's start with an overview of the parts and buttons on the pump. The Tandem t:slim X2 pump has a sleek design. At the front of the pump, you'll find a large color touchscreen that allows for easy navigation.

The back of the pump features a slide-out reservoir connected to a tube via a luer lock. It's important to note that on the Tandem pump, the luer lock is situated 5 cm away from the pump itself. From there, a longer tube extends to the infusion set, which is attached to your skin.

At the top of the pump, you'll find one button. The color of this button indicates the pump's status:

  • A green light every 30 seconds signifies normal operation.

  • Three green lights every 30 seconds indicate the pump is charging.

  • A yellow light every 30 seconds signifies a warning or reminder.

  • A red light every 30 seconds indicates a fault or alarm.

On the side of the pump, there is a USB port used for charging the pump and accessing data.

docs.title: 2.2 Charging the pump
docs.text:
To charge the pump, you have a rechargeable lithium battery inside. It's best to charge the pump daily for 10-15 minutes using a laptop, PC, wall socket, car, or power bank. It's recommended not to charge it overnight. If left uncharged, the battery will last approximately 5 days, so it's important to charge it for at least 30-60 minutes every 5 days.

Make sure to slide the pump's USB cover aside gently to avoid damage. You will hear a few short beeps when the battery starts charging. During the charging process, you can keep the pump connected to your body.

When the battery is completely flat, the screen may not turn on immediately upon connecting the USB cable. However, the button will light up green every 2 seconds until there is enough battery power to activate the screen.

docs.title: 2.3 Unlocking the pump
docs.text: To unlock the pump, simply press the button and then enter the unlock code, which is a sequence of 1-2-3. Pressing the button can also turn off the screen if desired.

docs.title: 2.4 Home screen and pump menu
docs.text:
Now let's explore the home screen and pump menu. The home screen displays various information, depending on whether you are connected to a glucose sensor such as a Dexcom or FreeStyle Libre sensor. At the top left, you'll find the battery status, while the top right shows the remaining insulin in the pump. The bottom section displays the insulin on board, indicating the amount of active insulin from previous boluses and automatic changes of the basal insulin.

Tapping the remaining insulin on the top right takes you to a status screen, where you can view the active personal profile, basal insulin delivery, time of the last bolus, and your current settings used to calculate a bolus. Additionally, you can also see the last calibration, sensor start time, transmitter battery, and mobile connection information.

On the right side of the screen, you can adjust the number of hours displayed in the sensor graph, ranging from 1 to 24 hours. Tapping on the sensor graph enlarges the view, but please note that basal insulin delivery is not visible in this view.

By tapping the Tandem logo, you can always return to the home screen.

Through the options menu, you can access various settings, including Stopping or resuming insulin delivery, Load, Activity, My Pump, My CGM, Device Settings and History.

  • Under "My Pump," you can access different options such as Personal Profiles, Control IQ, Alerts & Reminders, and Pump Info. The alerts and reminders section allows you to set up various reminders and customize alerts, such as the low insulin level alert and the Auto-Off alarm.

  • Within "my CGM," you can start/stop the sensor, calibrate the CGM with finger prick values, set CGM alerts, and enter the transmitter ID (this can only be done when the sensor is stopped).

  • In "Device Settings," you can customize the screen timeout duration, set the time and date, adjust the sound volume for Alarms, Alerts, Reminders, Keypad, Bolus, Quick Bolus, and Fill Tubing, and even set a security PIN. The security PIN can be useful for individuals, particularly children, who may not be operating the pump themselves.

  • Lastly, the "history" option allows you to review all the pump and CGM history in a logical manner.

docs.title: 2.5 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
Here are a few important tips to keep in mind:

  • You can find a comprehensive menu overview in the downloads section, which can be saved for easy reference when providing remote guidance as a healthcare provider.

  • The pump is waterproof, but only for 30 minutes until a depth of 0.9 meter (or 3 feet).

  • The pump comes with a black cover and clip, but you can also order different covers and explore other carrying solutions. A link to a list of compatible covers and carrying systems can be found below the video.

  • The Tandem t:slim X2 pump does not automatically adjust the time and date. So when changing between winter and summer time, or when traveling to a different time zone, you have to adjust the time yourself. You can do that via the settings.

Congratulations! You now have a good understanding of how the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump works, including its parts, charging process, unlocking mechanism, and the different screens and menus. In the next video, we will delve into setting insulin delivery, so stay tuned!

docs.title: 3. Insulin setup (6 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to our detailed guide on insulin delivery setup for the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump.

We'll explore how to program basal insulin, control temporary basal rates, and administer insulin boluses. Additionally, we'll share some crucial do's and don'ts to enhance your pump experience.

docs.title: 3.1 Programming basal Insulin
docs.text:
Programming basal insulin in the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump is unique as it requires you to input both basal insulin and bolus calculator settings in the same time segments.

  • To access your personal profile settings first tap options, next tap my pump, tap personal profiles, tap the plus icon to create a new profile using the on-screen keypad.

  • Enter a profile name up to 16 characters and tap the green check mark icon. Tap press to set up to begin setting insulin delivery settings. The first settings you enter always start at 12 AM.

  • Next to basal, tap press to set up. Using the on-screen keypad, enter your basal rate and tap the blue checkmark icon.

  • Next to correction factor, tap press to set up. Using the on-screen keypad, enter your correction factor and tap the blue check mark icon.

  • Next to carb ratio tap press to set up. Using the on-screen keypad, enter your insulin to carbohydrate ratio and tap the blue check mark icon.

  • Next to target BG tap press to set up. Using the on-screen keypad enter your target BG and tap the blue checkmark icon.

  • Review all of the values you just entered and tap the green checkmark icon. To confirm your settings, tap the blue check mark icon if your data is correct or tap the x icon to make changes.

  • If you need to add more time segments tap the plus icon or tap the back arrow icon to program your bolus settings. When adding more time segments, any settings that you entered in the previous time segment are copied and appear in the new segment. This allows you to simply adjust only the specific settings you want rather than having to enter them all over again.

  • On the add segment screen next to start time tap press to setup. Using the on-screen keypad enter the time that you want the segment to begin and tap the blue check mark icon. Remember that this segment will be active from the start time until 12 AM or until the next segment you enter.

  • When you have confirmed your settings and are done adding time segments, tap the back arrow icon to program your bolus settings. First tap the bolus settings panel to get started.

  • Next tap insulin duration. Using the on-screen keypad enter the desired time for the duration of insulin action and tap the blue check mark icon.

  • Next tap max bolus. Using the on-screen keypad enter the desired amount for your maximum bolus size and tap the blue check mark icon.

  • Next tap the carbohydrate slider to turn carbs on meaning the bolus calculator will use your carb ratio when calculating boluses.

  • Now review all the values you entered and tap the green check mark icon. The pump will then ask you to confirm your settings, tap the blue check mark icon to confirm or the x icon to make changes.

  • When you have confirmed your settings tap the Tandem logo to return to the home screen. If you need to add additional personal profiles repeat the steps outlined in this video.

  • You may also duplicate an existing personal profile. Then select edit to change only certain settings to create a new profile.

To stop basal insulin, select 'Stop insulin' under Options. When insulin is stopped, the home screen will show the status “All deliveries stopped”. Restarting basal insulin can be done in the same way via Options.

docs.title: 3.2 Temporary basal
docs.text: For temporary basal control, navigate to 'Activity' under Options and select 'Temporary basal'. Here, you can set the percentage and duration of your choice. When it's on, an orange 'T' appears in the status bar. You can always manually turn off the temporary basal, which reverts the 'T' back to the current system settings and insulin delivery status icon.

docs.title: 3.3 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
Administering a bolus is straightforward. From the start screen, hit the bolus button, input the carbohydrate grams and, if necessary, a finger prick value. If paired with a Dexcom G6, the last glucose value will auto-fill. The pump recommends an insulin dose, which you can override.

A handy tip is the option of extended bolus delivery. When confirming a bolus, you can opt to make it an extended bolus and adjust the percentage and time duration as needed.

docs.title: 3.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• Ensure maximum bolus limits are set slightly above expected bolus doses. This defaults to 10 units, but you can set it between 1 and 25 E

  • It's recommended to set the Target BG value at 110 mg/dl or 6.5 mmol/l, as this is consistent with Control IQ's default value. Once Control-IQ technology is turned on, the default Target BG is set to 110 mg/dl or 6.5 mmol/l.

  • If you need more than 25 units for the meal, you will get 2 alarms. At the first alarm, confirm that you want to get the maximum bolus set. At the second alarm, you must confirm that you also want to get the rest of the bolus.

  • Advanced users can set up the Quick Bolus function which enables you to deliver a bolus by simply pressing a button. It is a way to deliver a bolus by following beep/vibration commands without navigating through or viewing the pump screen.

That's it! You're now equipped with the knowledge to configure and adjust insulin on the Tandem t:slim X2 pump efficiently. In our next video, we'll guide you through changing the insulin reservoir and infusion set.

docs.title: 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (6 min)
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (6 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on reservoir and infusion set replacement on the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump.

In this video, we will cover the different infusion set options, step-by-step instructions for replacing the reservoir and infusion set, and helpful tips for success.

docs.title: 4.1 Different infusion sets
docs.text:
The Autosoft 90 infusion set is the most widely used infusion set for the Tandem pump.

Other alternatives include the Autosoft 30, the Varisoft with an angled cannula, and the TruSteel with a steel cannula. A detailed overview of these infusion sets, including tubing and cannula lengths and filling volumes, can be found in the download below this video.

docs.title: 4.2 Supplies
