docs.text: Start by washing your hands and ensuring your workspace is clean. Gather these items: a reservoir, a pull-up syringe, a pull-up needle, an infusion set, your insulin (at room temperature), and your pump.

docs.title: 4.3 Reservoir replacement
docs.text:
To determine how much insulin to pull into the syringe, multiply your total daily dose by 3 (for 3 days) and add approximately 50 units for the tube filling. If using a vial, disinfect the port and inject air to help the insulin flow out easily.

Once filled, remove any air from the reservoir by inserting the needle into the filling port and drawing out air. Then, slowly inject insulin into the new reservoir.

Next, navigate to 'options' on your pump, choose 'charge', and then 'replace reservoir'. Disconnect the infusion set from your body and remove the old reservoir. Insert the new reservoir securely. The pump will detect the reservoir and suggest tube filling.

docs.title: 4.4 Filling the tube
docs.text:
Start with a new infusion set and its tube. Carefully open the packaging, then release the lid by applying pressure to the three points on the plastic sheath with one hand while pulling off the lid with the other. Remember, the Autosoft 90 system is quite delicate due to its soft plastic construction, so avoid bending it too much or touching the needle. Gently extract the tube from the sheath, taking care not to tug too forcefully. Now, connect the tube to the reservoir's luer lock and give it an extra quarter-turn clockwise to ensure a secure fit.

Next, activate your pump to begin the tube filling process. It is essential to keep your pump upright during this procedure so that any air bubbles can rise to the top. Pay close attention to the tube for the presence of air bubbles. Once you observe a few drops of insulin coming out from the needle, you can stop the filling process on the pump.

Important to note, you should never perform this process while the infusion set is connected to your body. Also, ensure that you fill the tube with a minimum of 10 units of insulin.

docs.title: 4.5 Infusion set placement
docs.text:
Now, we're ready to apply the infusion set. We will demonstrate the Autosoft 90 infusion set. Start by sanitizing your skin and peel away the paper backing from the infusion set patch. Be sure to keep the infusion set firmly in place on the needle during this process. Next, prep the insertion set by tugging on the white plastic until you hear a click. At this point, you can safely remove the needle guard. Locate one of the two openings on the insertion device and insert the tube. Position the prepared device on your cleaned skin and press the needle into place by applying gentle pressure on the two circular depressions. Press the white plastic one more time. Lastly, take care to gently remove the insertion device.

You've now successfully replaced the insulin reservoir and infusion set! Remember to do this every three days, even if the reservoir is not yet empty.

docs.title: 4.6 Cannula Filling
docs.text: When you fill the tubing of the Autosoft infusion set, the cannula is filled automatically. If you ever replace only the infusion set but keep the existing tubing, you should manually fill the cannula. This can be done through the same pump menu. Fill the cannula with 0.3 units for 6-mm needles or 0.5 units for 9-mm needles, then resume insulin delivery. Don't forget to click "OK" when prompted to continue insulin delivery. If you forget, an alarm will sound as a reminder.

docs.title: 4.7 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• The insulin approved in the Tandem t:slim X2 pump is Novorapid, Humalog and Novolog. Lyumjev is approved for use in Europe. Fiasp remains off-label.

  • Remember, 2 hours after inserting a new set, you should check your blood glucose. Checking your blood glucose is the only way to confirm your infusion set is properly inserted and that you're receiving insulin.

  • Prior to disposing of the insertion device, reattach the lid until it clicks.

  • For swimming or showering, you can disconnect your tubing from the infusion set and apply the provided protective cover. To reconnect, simply remove the cover and snap the tube back on.

So now, you're equipped with the knowledge to replace the insulin reservoir and infusion set on the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump. If you stumble, don't worry – it's perfectly normal. Keep calm, follow the steps, and remember, if all else fails, there's always the free customer service helpline and a multitude of useful YouTube videos available. Also, don't forget that you can contact your diabetes team, who also have an on-call service, for medical support. Good luck!

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms (3 min)
docs.text:
In this video, we dive into the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump's alarm system. We'll discuss the different kinds of alarms, alerts, and reminders, and share some best practices.

When multiple reminders, alerts, or alarms are activated concurrently, the system displays them in the order of priority – alarms first, then alerts, and finally reminders. Each one requires individual confirmation until all are acknowledged.

docs.title: 5.1 Alarms
docs.text:
Alarms act as urgent notifications, alerting you to a current or imminent interruption in insulin delivery, such as when the insulin reservoir is depleted. These require immediate attention.

These alarms are signaled with three cycles of three tones or vibrations, as per your chosen volume/vibration settings. If left unacknowledged, alarms escalate to the maximum sound and vibration level and continue to repeat until the issue is rectified.

docs.title: 5.2 Alerts
docs.text:
Alerts inform you about safety conditions that you need to know about, like low insulin levels.

These alerts are expressed with two sequences of three tones or vibrations, following your volume/vibration preference. They continue to repeat until they are acknowledged, but they do not escalate.

docs.title: 5.3 Reminders
docs.text:
Reminders notify you about an option you've set, such as a reminder to check your blood glucose after a bolus.

Reminders notify you with a single sequence of 3 notes or a single vibration depending on your volume and vibrate settings. They repeat every 10 minutes until acknowledged. Reminders do not escalate.

docs.title: 5.4 Do's & don’ts
docs.text:
• A 'malfunction' alarm indicates a system fault. Take note of the fault code and contact customer service.

  • If in doubt, refer to the user manual or call customer service.

  • If you won't be using your pump for a significant period, put it in storage mode. To do this, connect it to a power source and hold the Screen on/Quick bolus button for 30 seconds. You'll hear three beeps before it switches to storage mode. Disconnect the pump from the power source. To deactivate storage mode, reconnect the charger.

You're now equipped with knowledge about the pump's primary alarms and what actions to take. Therefore, there's no need to panic when you hear an alarm from your pump. Simply check the display screen - it usually provides instructions. If the message isn't clear, refer to the user manual or call customer service. Best of luck!

docs.title: 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2 min)
6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (2 min)
docs.text:
In this final video, we'd like to spotlight Tom from Type One Talks, an individual living with type 1 diabetes, as he embarks on a journey with the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump. Through this case study, Tom will unveil his personal experiences over a considerable period, shedding light on the pump's strengths, drawbacks, and the practical strategies he's developed for managing his diabetes more effectively.

Please click on the link below this video to watch his review.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9xwjDU3L4T4 – 17 min

In addition to Tom’s insightful review, we’d like to introduce the t:simulator app, a virtual Tandem pump available on your smartphone. This simulator allows you to experiment with setting basal delivery and administering insulin boluses, providing a safe and educational environment to understand your pump's functionalities better.

To make this more engaging, we challenge everyone to perform an insulin bolus on the t:simulator app and share a screenshot of this in the comments section. This interactive exercise can help you bond with others on similar journeys and applaud your progress in learning to manage your insulin pump.

By completing this course, you have gained crucial knowledge about the Tandem t:slim X2 insulin pump and taken a significant step toward mastering your diabetes management. Remember, integrating the pump into your lifestyle isn't just about aesthetics—it also enhances your comfort and confidence, allowing you to lead a life unrestricted by your condition.

We extend our heartfelt gratitude to you for joining us on this journey and wish you the best as you continue managing your diabetes with your insulin pump. Keep up your exceptional work and always remember: your health and well-being deserve every ounce of effort you put into managing them!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Diabetes Technology Expert Program

docs.title: twiist Insulin Pump
docs.text: This educational resource covers comprehensive operation of the twiist insulin pump system. The content is designed for both healthcare professionals and individuals managing diabetes with this device.

docs.title: Lessons
docs.text:
• 1. Introduction
  • 2. Pump Operation
  • 3. Insulin set-up
  • 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement
  • 5. Pump alarms
  • 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge

docs.title: 1. Introduction (2 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to the twiist Pump module!

In this session, you'll learn how to operate the twiist pump confidently and effectively, whether for your own diabetes management or to support others.

Before we begin, we recommend watching our general insulin pump videos. These explain the basics of basal and bolus setup, infusion site management, and alarm handling — essential knowledge for understanding any pump system.

We'll now focus on the twiist Pump, part of the twiist Automated Insulin Delivery (AID) system. This innovative pump connects seamlessly with compatible glucose sensors and can automate insulin delivery using the integrated twiist Loop algorithm.

In this module, we'll guide you through the full setup process and daily operation of the twiist pump. It's designed for both healthcare professionals supporting twiist AID users and individuals preparing to start with the system.

Please note that the information in this module is for educational purposes only and should not be taken as medical advice. Always consult your healthcare provider before making any adjustments to your therapy.

The videos were produced by Dr. Inge Van Boxelaer, the founder of Diabetotech and an endocrinologist at the AZ St-Lucas hospital in Ghent in Belgium. The content was reviewed by Gary Scheiner, a Certified Diabetes Care and Education Specialist from Integrated Diabetes Services in the US. Their expertise and personal experiences ensure that the information presented in the videos is comprehensive and unbiased.

By the end of this module, you'll know:

  • How the twiist pump works
  • How to set basal and bolus insulin delivery
  • How to replace the insulin cassette (reservoir) and infusion set
  • And what to do when the pump gives alarms or alerts

Whether you're a healthcare provider or a user, by the end of this module, you will gain practical tips, step-by-step guidance, and the confidence to manage the twiist system effectively.

docs.title: 2. Pump Operation (10.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to the twiist Pump Operation module!

By the end of this section, you'll have a thorough understanding of how the twiist pump works, its components, charging process, navigation menus, and important do's and don'ts for safe and effective use.

docs.title: 2.1 Parts & Buttons
docs.text:
The twiist pump has a reusable pump base with a lifespan of 3 to 4 years. At the bottom sits the insulin cassette, which you fill with insulin and replace every 3 days.

Both can be separated by twisting the pump base to the left, hence the name "twiist Pump".

To reattach, align the ridge on top of the pump-bump with the ridge on the cassette. When the ridges are aligned, turn the pump clockwise toward the infusion set tubing. When the ridge on the pump-bump is aligned with the infusion set tubing, the cassette has been fully attached.

  • The cassette can be filled with up to 300 units of insulin via a fill port on the side.
  • The pump base holds a rechargeable battery and a pump button.

This pump button provides the ability to: Confirm that delivery is in progress, Resume insulin delivery, Place the pump into pairing mode (when not connected to a cassette), or Deliver a One-Button Bolus if this is enabled. Press and hold the pump button for 3 seconds to resume delivery or confirm that delivery is in progress.

The pump is shipped with a pump cover, pump clip, 4 rechargeable batteries and dedicated battery chargers.

docs.title: 2.2 Charging the Pump
docs.text:
When you change the cassette, you should also replace the battery in the pump base with a fully charged one. A fully charged pump battery will power the pump for 3 days.

The pump batteries typically take 6 hours or less to charge. The pump batteries should be charged using the provided battery charger and wall power adapter.

  • A green light on the charger indicates that the pump battery is fully charged.
  • Orange means that the battery is charging.
  • Red means something is wrong — the battery is not charging as expected.

docs.title: 2.3 Home Screen & Pump Menus
docs.text:
The twiist app shows you how the system is working through the icons and charts on the Home Screen. You can see what is happening at a glance or you can tap the icons and charts for additional information. Some features of the twiist app work differently when Loop is off.

The Home Screen has three main areas: Status, Charts & a Toolbar.

Status icons

The top of the Home Screen displays the different Status icons.

  • The CGM Status icon displays your current glucose and glucose trend arrow when you are connected to a CGM. When your CGM reports HIGH or LOW, check your fingerstick glucose and treat your high or low glucose. Tap the CGM Status icon to open the CGM Status screen and view additional CGM information.
  • The twiist Loop status icon shows the status of your AID system.
    • A closed circle means Loop is on, and your basal insulin delivery is adapted to the predicted glucose value. The color of the circle indicates how much time has gone since the last successful Loop adaptation based on the last received CGM value.
    • An open circle means Loop is off, and you are receiving your scheduled basal rate. You can use the twiist system with Loop off, with or without a CGM.
    • You can tap the twiist Status icon for more information.
  • The insulin delivery status icon shows whether basal insulin is being delivered at your scheduled basal rate, or whether your basal delivery has been reduced or increased.
    • A solid line with a value of zero indicates that your basal is being delivered at your scheduled rate. It does NOT mean your basal rate is zero units per hour.
    • A dropped icon indicates that your basal is reduced by the value shown. A reduced basal rate is always indicated with a negative value such as -1.0 U/hr.
    • A raised icon indicates that your basal is increased by the value shown.
  • The twiist Pump Status icon displays an estimation of how much insulin remains in your cassette, and your pump battery level.
    • Grey icons mean more than 20% of your insulin remaining in your cassette and your pump battery has more than 20% charge. Yellow icons mean less than 20%, and red icons mean less than 10%.
    • Note that the cassette and pump battery icons are independent from each other and may show different percentages and colors.
    • When you tap the pump status icon, the pump menu will be displayed.

The twiist app may display a Temporary Status Banner below the Status icons. You will see and can interact with this banner for example when a bolus is being delivered, when a temporary basal is active, when a cannula fill is in progress or when you have turned on a Pre-Meal or Workout Preset.

Charts

The largest portion of the Home Screen has four charts. Here you can see your glucose and how your insulin and the carbs you have entered are working to impact your glucose. Touch and hold anywhere within the Charts to see more information. As you hold your finger on the chart, you can slide left and right to view data for a specific time. Rotating your phone will allow you to see additional history.

  • The Glucose Chart displays your glucose prediction, and shows your eventual glucose of 6 hours into the future. The twiist system will update this prediction every 5 minutes as it works to bring your glucose into your Correction Range. When Loop is off, no glucose predictions are made or displayed.
  • The Active Insulin Chart displays how much insulin has been delivered but has yet to take action within your body. Your current Active Insulin is displayed at the top-right corner. This can be a negative value if you have less insulin active in your body than you usually have scheduled for this time.
  • The Insulin Delivery Chart displays both your basal and bolus delivery history over the last few hours. Your current basal rate is displayed in the top-right corner.
    • Basal is displayed as a series of orange bars. Bars above the orange line indicate increased basal, while bars below the orange line indicate reduced basal.
    • Boluses that have been delivered are displayed as orange triangles.
    • Tap the Active Insulin or Insulin Delivery Charts to see your Event History. The twiist app displays up to 14 days of event history.
  • The Active Carbohydrates Chart displays the carbs that you have entered into the twiist app and how the twiist system expects them to impact your glucose over time. Your current active carbohydrates are displayed in the top-right corner.

Toolbar

The bottom of the Home Screen displays a Toolbar with buttons for some of the most common features:

  • The Carb Entry button is used to enter details about what you are eating and to get a bolus recommendation.
  • The Pre-Meal Preset temporarily lowers your Correction Range before a meal to reduce post-meal glucose spikes. The Pre-Meal Preset will be in effect for up to one hour, until canceled, or until carbs are entered.
  • The Bolus Entry button allows you to deliver a correction bolus to bring down high glucose. No bolus is recommended when it is predicted that an additional bolus will lead to hypoglycemia.
  • The Workout Preset temporarily raises your Correction Range for activity. Workout Preset will be in effect for the time you indicate when you activate it, or until you cancel it.
  • The Settings button opens the Settings Menu to turn Loop On or Off, view and edit your Therapy Settings, and other settings.

docs.title: 2.4 Do's & Don'ts
docs.text:
• Attach your twiist pump to a belt or clothing using the supplied pump clip. Slide the pump into the clip with the pump bump facing upward.
  • You can also place the pump in a pocket. It's about the size of an Omnipod or Tandem Mobi pump. Check that the infusion set tubing is not pinched.
  • Some users secure the pump directly to their body with tape or a Mobi Adhesive Pocket. Others personalize their pump and clip with stickers.
  • In order to maximize the signal between your pump and CGM, and minimize communication interruptions, your pump and CGM should be placed on the same side of your body. The twiist logo on the pump should face away from your body.
  • The twiist pump is not waterproof. It may tolerate minor splashes, but immersion in water may lead to pump malfunctions.
  • Sequel MedTech does not provide a spare pump base, so take good care of your pump.
  • If you need to clean a pump base, gently wipe it with a clean, damp cloth using mild soap and water or you can use an alcohol wipe to clean the surface. Do not submerge the pump or expose it to running water.
  • The twiist pump time and date automatically synchronizes with your iPhone through the twiist app. Make sure the time and date is set correctly on your iPhone. If the time and date is not set correctly, delivery errors may occur resulting in low or high blood glucose.
  • Note that the twiist app can be switched to dark mode at any time via your iPhone settings.

Congratulations! You now have a strong understanding of the twiist pump's core functions — including its parts, charging routine, navigation menus, and safety best practices. In the next section, we'll cover insulin set-up so you can prepare your pump for therapy with confidence.

docs.title: 3. Insulin set-up (4.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to our detailed guide on insulin delivery set-up for the twiist Pump.

In this section, you'll learn how to program basal insulin, suspend delivery, set a temporary basal rate, and give a bolus when Loop is off. Use of the pump when Loop is on is covered separately in the twiist AID System module.

We'll also outline key do's and don'ts to help you use your pump safely and efficiently.

docs.title: 3.1 Programming basal insulin
docs.text: Tap Settings on the Toolbar. Tap Therapy Settings. Tap Basal rates. Here you can edit your basal rate and add new time blocks if needed. To change your basal rate for a specific time block, just tap the value, adjust the setting, and save it.

docs.title: 3.2 Suspending insulin
docs.text:
To suspend insulin delivery, tap the pump status icon to open the pump menu, then select "Suspend Delivery." Tap "Confirm" to stop insulin. You'll hear the delivery suspended sound, unless Quiet Mode is on.

To resume insulin, tap the temporary status banner or the insulin suspended icon, then tap "Resume Delivery." The screen will show "Communicating with Pump," and you'll hear the delivery sound when insulin starts again.

docs.title: 3.3 Temporary basal
docs.text:
When Loop is off, you can use a Temporary Basal to adjust your basal rate for a short time, such as during exercise or illness.

Tap the heart-shaped button on the toolbar. Choose the duration and percentage for insulin delivery, then tap "Start Temporary Basal." Authenticate with your iPhone passcode, Face ID, or Touch ID.

While a Temporary Basal is active, a status banner shows its progress and lets you stop it if needed. The insulin delivery icon will show whether the basal rate is increased or reduced.

docs.title: 3.4 Delivering a bolus
docs.text:
When Loop mode is off, you can give a bolus using the built-in bolus calculator.

Your carb ratio, insulin sensitivity, and correction range are set when you first start the twiist app but can be changed anytime in Therapy Settings. Here you can view these settings and tap any value to adjust it.

  • For a meal bolus, tap the meal icon, enter your carbs, and enter your current glucose.
  • For a correction bolus, tap the triangle icon and enter your glucose value.

You can review and modify the suggested dose before delivering the bolus.

docs.title: 3.5 Do's & don'ts
docs.text:
• The twiist app lets you set two key delivery limits: maximum basal rate and maximum bolus.
    • Set the max basal rate to about 3–4 times your highest scheduled basal and adjust if needed.
    • Set the max bolus slightly above your largest expected meal dose (up to 25 units) to prevent overdosing while keeping flexibility.
  • The app uses a fixed insulin action duration of 6 hours, but you can adjust the peak time: Go to Settings, tap Therapy Settings, tap Insulin Model and select either:
    • Fast-Acting Adults (with a peak at 75 minutes) or
    • Fast-Acting Children (with a peak at 65 minutes).
  • Before changing settings or delivering insulin, authentication is required with your iPhone passcode, Face ID, or Touch ID.
  • The One-Button Bolus feature is off by default and should only be enabled with your healthcare provider's guidance. To turn it on, go to Settings, tap Therapy Settings and toggle on One-Button Bolus.
    • Each press of the pump button adds 1 unit to the bolus (up to 10 units total). You'll hear a confirmation sound each time.
    • When ready, press and hold the pump button until you hear the playback of the selected dose.
    • If the playback is correct, press and hold again until you hear the Delivering sound.

That's it! You now know how to set up basal insulin, program temporary adjustments, and deliver boluses on your twiist pump in manual mode. In the next section, we'll cover how to replace the reservoir and infusion set to keep your pump ready for reliable insulin delivery.

docs.title: 4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (8.5 min)
4. Reservoir and infusion set replacement (8.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on cassette and infusion set replacement with the twiist Pump!

In this guide, we'll walk through your options for infusion sets, step-by-step instructions for changing both the cassette and infusion set, and some tips to help you get the best results.

docs.title: 4.1 Different infusion sets
docs.text:
The twiist pump works with several luer-lock infusion sets, including Contact Detach, Inset, Quick-set, Silhouette, Comfort, and Cleo 90.

Currently, it is distributed only with the Cleo 90 infusion set, which has a 6 mm, 90-degree Teflon cannula and 61 cm (or 24 inch) tubing.

Replace the infusion set every 2–3 days, following the manufacturer's guidance or your healthcare provider's instructions.

docs.title: 4.2 Supplies
docs.text: To change the cassette, infusion set, and insulin, gather the following: room-temperature insulin, a Cleo infusion set with inserter, the dedicated tubing, an alcohol swab, a Skin Tac or Skin Prep wipe, a needle and syringe, a new twiist cassette, your twiist pump base with a charged battery, and your phone with the twiist app open.

docs.title: 4.3 Cassette & Infusion Set Replacement
docs.text:
If it's your first time using the pump base, you'll need to set up your twiist AID system and pair your pump.

  • Create an account at www.twiist.com/register and download the twiist app from the App Store. When you open the app, you'll be prompted to pair your CGM and pump base.
  • Insert a fully charged battery into the pump base. The charger light turns green when it's ready. Tap Pair Pump, then select your pump's serial number from the list. If it doesn't appear, press and hold the pump button for five seconds until you hear a ready beep, then release it. You can pair the pump by scanning a photo or entering the PIN manually.
  • After pairing, the pump will check for software updates. Follow the on-screen instructions if an update is available.

Once your pump base is connected, you can change the cassette. There are four steps: cassette replacement, filling the tube, infusion set placement, and cannula filling.

Before starting, wash your hands with soap and water and let them dry.

Step 1 – Cassette replacement

  • In the twiist app, open the pump menu and tap Change Cassette. The app will guide you through each step, starting with suspending insulin delivery.
  • Disconnect the infusion set from your body by pinching the connector arms inward and sliding it fully open to detach the tubing cap from the cannula base.
  • Twist off the old cassette and tubing from the pump base by turning counterclockwise. Replace the battery in your pump base with a fully charged one.
  • Attach a new empty cassette by aligning the ridges on the pump bump and cassette top. Push down and rotate the pump clockwise until the ridge aligns with the tubing.
  • Wait for the self-test to finish. Then tap Scan Cassette, position the cassette's QR code inside the green box, and tap OK.

Step 2 – Filling the tube

  • Select your Cassette Fill Volume. The twiist system remembers your previous setting. Choose a volume between 100 and 300 units, enough for two to three days, then tap Next.
  • Clean the insulin vial top with an alcohol wipe. Open a new syringe and needle, attach them securely, and draw air into the syringe equal to the fill volume. Insert the needle into the vial, and inject the air. Keeping the syringe in place, turn the vial upside down and slowly pull the plunger to draw insulin to the selected fill volume. Tap the syringe to remove air bubbles, then withdraw the needle.
  • Open new tubing and attach it to the cassette by twisting the luer connectors together.
  • Insert the needle gently into the cassette filling port and push the plunger down to fill the cassette. Hold the plunger down while removing the needle. If resistance occurs, remove and reinsert the needle gently. Dispose of the syringe and needle in a sharps container, then tap Next.
  • On the twiist app, tap Start Prime. The pump will make priming sounds as insulin moves through the tubing. Watch for drops at the tubing tip — this confirms priming is complete. Tap Stop Prime, confirm with Yes, or tap No to repeat the step.

Step 3 – Infusion set placement

Now you're ready to apply the Cleo infusion set.

Choose an insertion site where you normally inject insulin and where the pump and CGM can stay on the same side of your body. Rotate sites regularly to maintain good absorption.

When selecting an infusion site:

  • Avoid areas that may constrict the site, such as the waistline, beltline, or where the body naturally bends.
  • Avoid the area 5 cm or 2 inches around your belly button.
  • Keep the infusion site at least 2.5 cm or 1 inch from your CGM.
  • Avoid placing the infusion site on scars, moles, stretch marks, and tattoos.
  • Avoid broken skin, cuts, and scrapes.
  • Keep the infusion site at least 2 to 5 cm or 1 to 2 inches from your previous infusion site.

Wipe the skin with Skin Tac for enhanced adhesion. Twist off the cap from the inserter and check the adhesive and insertion needle.

Press the inserter firmly on the site for about 20 seconds. Remove it straight or with a rolling motion. Press to secure the adhesive.

Pinch the side connector arms inward and slide open. Attach the site connector so the tubing faces the desired direction. Push until you hear a click. Make sure the needle has retracted, replace the cap, and dispose of materials properly.

In the twiist app, confirm the Cannula Fill Volume and Cassette Fill Volume, then tap Done. Tap Start Basal to begin insulin delivery. The pump will play the "Delivering" sound as basal delivery starts.

Step 4 – Cannula Filling

The infusion set cannula is normally filled during a cassette change. If you replace your infusion site separately, you'll need to fill the cannula manually.

For the 6 mm Cleo infusion set, fill with 0.2 units of insulin.

  • In the twiist app, open the Pump Menu and tap Fill Cannula.
  • Select Cannula Fill Volume to adjust if needed, then tap Fill Cannula and Confirm to deliver the insulin.

docs.title: 4.4 Do's & don'ts
docs.text:
• Change your infusion set every 2–3 days. The app does not remind you, so track this yourself.
  • Check your blood glucose 1–2 hours after inserting a new site to confirm proper insulin delivery.
  • The cassette can be used for up to 3 days and changed separately from the infusion set.
  • Inspect tubing regularly for air bubbles. If present, perform a cassette change.
  • Some users report fewer cassette failures when they wear the cassette with the tubing pointing downward to prevent air in the tubing. Others adjust the clip to make this positioning easier.
  • For infusion site success:
    • Rotate sites to avoid irritation or scarring.
    • Avoid contact with lotions, sunscreen, or insect repellent, which can damage plastic parts.
    • Many users find Skin Tac or Skin Prep works better than alcohol wipes for adhesion.
    • If the Cleo set sticks to the applicator, loosen the seal at the small dot or use a fork to hold the set while retracting the inserter.
  • Pairing reminders:
    • Pairing a pump and iPhone requires Wi-Fi or cellular connection.
    • The twiist app can connect to only one pump at a time.
    • To pair a new pump, go to Settings → Configure Devices → Delete Pump before adding the new one.

You did it! You now know how to replace the twiist pump's cassette and infusion set from start to finish. With practice, this process will become second nature, and you'll be able to complete it quickly and confidently. Remember: if you get stuck, your twiist User Guide, customer support, and your diabetes care team are always there to help.

docs.title: 5. Pump alarms (5.5 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to this lesson on alarms and alerts for the twiist insulin pump.

The twiist app and pump play sounds to help you recognize the system's current status.

Ensure your iPhone volume is turned up, vibration is turned on, and the twiist App and CGM Notifications and Critical Alerts are enabled. Notifications are played based on your iPhone ringtone and alert volume.

The twiist app displays messages in order of priority: Alarms, Alerts, and Notices — with the Urgent Low Glucose alert taking the highest priority.

When two or more alerts occur, the alerts are displayed in the twiist app with the most recent alert first and progressing to earlier alerts as they are addressed.

To ensure twiist App notifications are displayed on your iPhone lock screen, Notifications must be enabled within your iPhone settings. Notifications inform you of conditions that require attention. Critical Alerts inform you of conditions that require immediate action.

docs.title: 5.1 Alarms
docs.text:
Alarms occur when insulin delivery is stopped and require immediate attention. Check your blood glucose and take the appropriate action to treat high or low glucose. When an Alarm occurs, the twiist App plays the Alarm sounds (3 beeps + 2 beeps, occurring 2 times, and repeating every 10 seconds) and displays a message with an Alarm symbol followed by the Alarm name.

  • If the alarm has not been resolved within 5 minutes, the alarm notification will be played regardless of your iPhone volume or Focus settings.
  • After an additional 5 minutes, the alarm notification will be repeated on your iPhone and will play on your twiist Pump every 15 seconds until silenced.
  • Silenced alarms that have not been resolved will display again after 15 minutes.
  • If the twiist App and pump are out of communication during an alarm, Alarm sounds will play on the pump immediately.
  • Alarms can frequently be resolved by changing the cassette.

Common alarms include: Cassette Empty, Cassette Error, Cassette Not Attached, Line Blocked, Pump Battery Empty, or Pump Error.

docs.title: 5.2 Alerts
docs.text:
Alerts display information about the twiist Automated Insulin Delivery system. Alerts should be addressed as soon as possible.

When an Alert occurs, the twiist App plays the Alert sounds (2 beeps) and displays a message with an alert symbol followed by the Alert name. The Alert sounds are played based on your iPhone ringtone and alert volume. If your iPhone is silenced or Focus is enabled, alert notifications may be missed. Alert sounds repeat every 15 minutes until the alert is dismissed.

Possible alerts include: Cassette Low, Pump Battery Low, Interrupted Bolus, Pump Inactive, Reattach Cassette, No Communication, and others.

CGM alerts display information about your current glucose and CGM status. CGM alerts should be addressed as soon as possible. The Urgent Low Glucose alert is displayed with higher priority than all other alerts. CGM alerts (except Urgent Low Glucose) are only played on your iPhone. Alert sounds repeat every 5 minutes until the CGM alert is dismissed.

docs.title: 5.3 Notices
docs.text:
Notices provide information about the status of the twiist Automated Insulin Delivery system. CGM and Loop notices are displayed to keep you informed about your CGM and your automated insulin delivery.

Examples:

  • Critical Alerts Disabled
  • Internet Connection Required
  • No Communication
  • Notifications Disabled
  • Phone Battery Low at Start of Software Update
  • Prime Stopped
  • Pump Battery Low
  • Security Features Disabled
  • System Time Updated
  • The twiist App is Closed
  • Workout is Still Active
  • CGM Ending Soon (3 Days / 1 Day / 1 Hour)
  • CGM Ready
  • Loop is Off / On / Waiting
  • Timer Expired

docs.title: 5.4 Do's & don'ts
docs.text:
• To understand what triggered an alarm, review the details in the Event History menu. You can tap Active Insulin or Insulin Delivery Charts to access and view past events.
  • Quiet Mode limits some sounds played by the twiist pump when in communication with the twiist app. Quiet Mode is OFF by default, but it can be enabled via the Therapy Settings.
  • 3 additional alerts can be changed via the Therapy Settings:
    • An optional low insulin alert to occur when the system estimates between 20 and 100 Units of insulin remaining in your cassette.
    • An optional Phone Out of Range Alert that notifies you when the twiist pump and app have been out of communication for 1 to 120 minutes.
    • A Pump Inactive alert is set to 60 minutes by default, but can be set to 15, 30, 60, or 90 minutes.
  • When not using the twiist pump for an extended period, remove the cassette, remove the battery and re-attach the pump cover.

Now you know what to do in the event of the pump's primary alarm, and you are more prepared. Therefore, if you hear a sound in your pump, there's no need to panic. Just look at the screen and instructions are typically provided there. Consult the user manual or call customer care if the message is unclear.

docs.title: 6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (1 min)
6. Case report & virtual pump challenge (1 min)
docs.text:
Welcome to the final part of this module. We appreciate the time you've taken to learn about the twiist insulin pump.

We invite you to watch the live site change on YouTube.

In this video, you'll see how to safely replace the infusion site and maintain consistent insulin delivery. It shows how a site change is performed in real life and gives you an idea of how long the process actually takes.

That's it for this lesson. We hope this demonstration helps you feel more confident performing your next site change. You've done great work completing this module. Keep practicing these steps, and remember that every bit of effort you put into learning your pump supports smoother and safer diabetes management.
